pratique.scenario: You run into an old friend at the supermarket. You haven't seen them for a long time. Start a short conversation — greet them, ask how they are, and invite them for a coffee.
prompt_phrases: Oh! [name]! Hey!
Long time no see!
How are you?

phrase_cle.example: — Hi Marie! Long time no see! — Hey Sophie, yeah, it's been ages!
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень распространённое дружеское приветствие, когда вы не видели человека какое-то время. Буквально — 'давно не видел'. Используется между друзьями и хорошими знакомыми, не в формальной обстановке.

content.cultural_note: In British and English-speaking cultures, small talk — short, friendly conversation about everyday things (weather, how busy you are, where you're going) — is very important when you meet someone. It's a way to show warmth before anything more serious. You don't need to share big news; 'busy, but good' is a perfect answer.

'grab' — разговорное 'взять, ухватить'

pratique.scenario: It's Saturday morning. Call a friend and suggest doing something together. Ask what they're up to, share your plans, and invite them along.
prompt_phrases: Hi! What are you up to?

phrase_cle.example: — Hi! What are you up to? — Not much, just having coffee. You?
phrase_cle.explanation: Супер-распространённая неформальная фраза у британцев. Буквально — 'до чего ты дошёл?', но значит просто 'чем занят, какие планы?'. Используется постоянно между друзьями — по телефону, в сообщениях, при встрече.

content.cultural_note: Tea is a genuine part of British daily life — not a cliché. Brits drink around 100 million cups a day. 'A cuppa' means a cup of tea (never coffee), usually with milk. If a Brit offers you tea, it's a sign of warmth and welcome. Refusing can seem cold — at least say 'maybe later!'

очень британское; 'cup of tea' в разговоре
'pop' = сбегать, на минуту
типичное британское согласие
часто используется вместо 'I need an hour'

pratique.scenario: You run into a friend in a supermarket. React with surprise, make small talk about what you're both buying, and ask for advice on something.
prompt_phrases: Fancy seeing you here!

phrase_cle.example: — Oh! Sophie! Fancy seeing you here! — I know, what are the odds?
phrase_cle.explanation: Используется, когда случайно сталкиваешься со знакомым в неожиданном месте. Дружелюбно, с лёгким удивлением. 'Fancy' здесь — старое значение 'представить себе'. Очень британская фраза.

content.cultural_note: Tesco is the biggest supermarket chain in the UK — you'll find one on nearly every high street. Brits often go shopping for 'just a few bits' rather than a big weekly shop. 'Dodgy' is an incredibly useful word: it can describe food that's past its best, a suspicious person, or a questionable area. Master it and you'll sound properly British.

UK; в США — 'the checkout'

pratique.scenario: You're on a crowded tube platform with a friend. Complain about the crowd, check how long until the next train, and say which stop you need.
prompt_phrases: It's packed!
How long till the next one?
Which stop do you need?
Let's squeeze on.

phrase_cle.example: — Please mind the gap between the train and the platform. — Every time. I love it.
phrase_cle.explanation: Знаменитое объявление в лондонском метро — его слышишь на каждой станции. 'Mind' здесь = 'be careful of'. За пределами метро фраза стала культурным символом Лондона: её печатают на футболках и сумках.

content.cultural_note: The London Underground — or 'the Tube' — opened in 1863 and is the oldest metro in the world. Rush hour (7:30–9:30am and 5–7pm) is legendary: people pack in so tightly you don't need to hold on. Oxford Circus is one of the busiest stations. 'Mind the gap' has been announced since 1969 and is now one of London's most iconic phrases.

key_vocabulary.note: про людей в транспорте

культовая фраза лондонского метро

pratique.scenario: You're going to be late for a meeting with a friend. Call them, explain why, say how late you'll be, and apologise.
prompt_phrases: I'm running late.
Sorry, [reason].
I'll be there in [time].
Thanks for waiting!

phrase_cle.example: — Sorry, I'm running late. Can we make it ten minutes later? — No worries, see you then.
phrase_cle.explanation: Самый вежливый способ сказать, что ты опаздываешь. 'Running' не значит 'бежать' — это просто устойчивое выражение. Используется в сообщениях, звонках, письмах. Звучит профессионально и дружелюбно одновременно.

content.cultural_note: Brits apologise constantly — and being late is one of the biggest sins. The unwritten rule: if you're more than 5 minutes late, you must text or call. Not warning someone is considered rude. When apologising, don't over-explain — a simple 'sorry, I'm running late' followed by how late you'll be is perfect. The response you'll almost always get: 'no worries!'

key_vocabulary.note: вежливое, нейтральное

с лёгким раздражением или смирением

phrase_cle.example: — I'm starving! What shall we get? — Pret, obviously. It's right there.
phrase_cle.explanation: Гипербола. Буквально 'я голодаю', но в обычной речи просто означает 'я очень голоден'. Используется постоянно. Не путать с медицинским 'starvation' — в повседневном английском это просто сильное 'hungry'.

content.cultural_note: Pret A Manger ('Pret' for short) is the lunchtime king of London. Founded in 1986, there are now hundreds across the city — office workers live on their sandwiches, soups and coffee. 'Meal deal' lunches (sandwich + drink + snack for a set price) are a British institution. 'Good shout' is one of those phrases that instantly makes you sound like a local.

key_vocabulary.note: преувеличение, норма в речи
'fancy' = хотеть / быть в настроении
дословно и переносно
британское одобрение предложения
еду, счёт, расходы

pratique.scenario: You and a friend are stuck somewhere because of rain. Complain about the weather, decide whether to wait it out, and make a plan.
prompt_phrases: It's chucking it down!
I forgot my brolly.
Shall we wait it out?
Maybe it'll clear up.

phrase_cle.example: — Should we go out? — Have you looked outside? It's absolutely chucking it down.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень разговорный способ сказать, что идёт сильный дождь. 'Chuck' = бросать, поэтому небо как будто 'швыряет' воду. 'Absolutely' добавляет интенсивности. Используется только в неформальной речи — с друзьями, в семье.

content.cultural_note: The British obsession with the weather is real — and rainy days are the national sport of complaining. London gets around 110 rainy days a year, so Brits have invented dozens of ways to describe rain: drizzle, spitting, pouring, chucking it down, bucketing, lashing. Always carry a 'brolly' — and expect to hear weather small talk at least three times a day.

разговорное 'umbrella'

pratique.scenario: A friend asks about your weekend plans. Suggest meeting up for an activity, agree on a time and a place.
prompt_phrases: Any plans for the weekend?
I might [activity].
Want to come along?

phrase_cle.example: — Any plans for the weekend? — Bit of a lazy one, hopefully. You?
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартный вопрос в пятницу — почти ритуал в британских офисах и между друзьями. В понедельник его зеркало: 'How was your weekend?'. Отвечать можно и подробно, и коротко ('not much, just chilling') — оба варианта норма.

content.cultural_note: Borough Market is London's most famous food market — near London Bridge, dating back to the 12th century. It's crowded, but for good reason: artisan cheese, fresh bread, street food from every corner of the world. Saturdays are busiest. 'Half eleven' confuses many learners — it's British for 11:30, not 10:30. In German it would mean half past ten, but in English 'half' comes after the hour.

pratique.scenario: Something small goes wrong while you're out with a friend (broken umbrella, spilled drink, lost glove). React, explain what happened, find a solution together.
prompt_phrases: Oh no. Look at this.
You've got to be kidding me!
Lesson learned.
Got a spare?

phrase_cle.phrase: You've got to be kidding me
phrase_cle.example: — The bus is cancelled. — You've got to be kidding me. It's freezing!
phrase_cle.explanation: Реакция на плохую/абсурдную новость. Выражает смесь удивления и раздражения. 'You've got to be' = 'you must be'. Интонация обычно падающая, с лёгким смехом. Используется повсеместно в разговоре.

content.cultural_note: Brits have a complicated relationship with umbrellas. Everyone needs one, no one can keep one. Cheap ones ('pound shop brollies') break within days. Windy London streets are especially brutal — you'll see dozens of abandoned broken umbrellas on any rainy day. 'Budge up' is a wonderfully British request to make room on a bench, sofa, or under an umbrella.

key_vocabulary.note: мягкое сочувствие

phrase_cle.explanation: Короткое мягкое предложение. Заменяет полный вопрос вроде 'Shall we go?' или 'Shall we do it?'. Используется после того, как идея уже понятна из контекста. Звучит дружелюбно и необязывающе.

content.cultural_note: Nando's is a South African-Portuguese chicken chain — but in the UK, it's something else entirely: a cultural institution. 'A cheeky Nando's' became a viral phrase around 2014 and now means an unplanned, slightly indulgent visit. The chicken comes in spice levels: plain → mild → medium → hot → extra hot. Peri-peri chips are non-negotiable. Every young Brit has an opinion about Nando's.

pratique.scenario: You and a friend are waiting for a bus that isn't coming. Check the app, express frustration, and decide together what to do — keep waiting, walk, or take a different route.
prompt_phrases: Where is this bus?
Typical TfL.
Let me check the app.
Shall we just walk?

phrase_cle.example: — The bus hasn't come for twenty minutes. — Typical TfL. Nothing new.
phrase_cle.explanation: TfL = Transport for London, компания, управляющая автобусами и метро. 'Typical TfL' говорят с лёгким раздражением, когда что-то идёт не так: задержки, отмены, забастовки. Лондонцы обожают жаловаться на транспорт — это часть городской идентичности.
phrase_cle.translation: Как всегда в транспорте / в TfL

content.cultural_note: Complaining about public transport is a core London pastime. TfL runs everything: the Tube, buses, trains, even bikes ('Santander Cycles'). The 'TfL Go' app shows live bus arrivals — but everyone knows the promised time can keep rolling back. 'Worth it for the sanity' is a phrase Londoners use to justify walking instead of waiting — long walks through central London are part of the lifestyle.

phrase_cle.example: — The pub at one, roast, pints? How does that sound? — Sounds perfect.
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартный способ предложить план и узнать мнение собеседника. Буквально — 'как это звучит?'. Используется, когда ты уже сформулировал предложение и хочешь услышать одобрение или возражение. Работает и в неформальных, и в полуформальных ситуациях.

content.cultural_note: The Sunday roast is sacred British tradition: roast meat (usually beef), roast potatoes, Yorkshire puddings, gravy, vegetables. Served in pubs between 12 and 4pm on Sundays. Pub roasts are a national obsession — people queue, book in advance, even argue over which pub does the best one. Yorkshire puddings (hollow, crispy savoury pastries) are non-negotiable. ''The Crown'' is one of the most common pub names in England.

content.cultural_note: Covent Garden is one of London''s most charming areas — old market buildings, street performers, restaurants everywhere. Italian food is massively popular in the UK. When planning a birthday, Brits often split costs (card, gift, dinner bill) between friends — this is called ''going halves'' or ''splitting it''. It''s polite to offer, weird to insist on paying entirely.

pratique.scenario: You need to cancel plans with a friend. Tell them honestly, give a reason, apologise briefly, and offer a way to make up for it (card, message, another date).
prompt_phrases: I need to tell you something.
I can''t make it to [event].
I feel awful.
I''ll [something nice] at least.

phrase_cle.example: — Coming to the party? — Sorry, I can''t make it. Got work stuff.
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартная вежливая формулировка отмены. ''Make it'' здесь — идиома ''добраться, успеть''. Звучит менее резко, чем ''I can''t come''. Хорошо работает в любой ситуации — дружеской, рабочей, формальной.

content.cultural_note: Brits cancel plans with layers of apology — it''s almost an art form. The magic phrase ''I can''t make it'' softens the blow. Family obligations are the most accepted reason to bail: ''family comes first'' is almost sacred. In return, a cancelled friend is expected to respond graciously with ''don''t worry'' or ''no problem''. Holding a grudge over a cancellation is considered childish — as long as you don''t do it too often.

pratique.scenario: You forgot a plan with a friend. Apologise properly: acknowledge what you did, take responsibility, offer to make up for it.
prompt_phrases: I owe you an apology.
I''m so sorry.
No excuse, though.

phrase_cle.example: — I forgot your birthday. — What?! — I''m so sorry, honestly.
phrase_cle.explanation: Усиленное ''sorry''. ''So'' делает извинение искренним, а не формальным. Используется после ошибки, проступка, забытого обещания. Если британец говорит ''I''m so sorry'' — это не пустая вежливость, а реальное сожаление.

content.cultural_note: Brits take apologies seriously — but also don''t like it when they drag on. The ritual: acknowledge, don''t over-explain, offer to make up for it. ''I''m so sorry'' + brief reason + ''let me buy you a coffee/lunch'' is the classic formula. Over-apologising is almost worse than under-apologising — it becomes performative. The response ''don''t worry'' is the social signal that the issue is closed — dwell on it longer and you''ll make things awkward.

phrase_cle.example: — Hey, how''s things? — Not bad, not bad. You?
phrase_cle.explanation: Более разговорный, тёплый вариант ''How are you?''. Буквально — ''как все вещи?''. Используется между близкими друзьями или семьёй. ''How''s things'' звучит естественно и уютно — как будто заходишь в гости.

content.cultural_note: Phone calls between close friends in the UK are becoming rarer (most young Brits prefer texting or voice notes) — but when they happen, they''re often warm and caring. ''Are you eating properly?'' is the classic friend/mum question. Offering to come over and cook for a stressed friend is a major gesture of care. Wine is the traditional contribution. ''Lovely'' is one of the most-used British adjectives — soft, warm, genuinely positive.

phrase_cle.translation: Ни за что не угадаешь что

content.cultural_note: London''s parks are social spaces — people actually meet there to chat, not just exercise. Hyde Park, Regent''s Park, Clapham Common — all crowded on sunny weekends with groups of friends sitting on the grass. Gossip (when it''s kind, not cruel) is a bonding ritual in British friendship. ''Deserves this'' is an important concept: friends genuinely want to see each other happy, and news about love, jobs, or successes is celebrated as if it''s their own.

phrase_cle.explanation: Вежливая прелюдия перед просьбой. Даёт собеседнику понять, что дальше будет просьба, и даёт ему пространство сразу сказать да или нет. Почти всегда после этого следует ''of course'' или ''sure'' — но привычка такая, британская.

content.cultural_note: Bristol is a lively city in South West England — often topping lists of ''best places to live''. Asking friends to pet-sit, water plants, or accept deliveries is a normal part of friendship in the UK. Giving a spare key is a sign of trust, and Brits take it seriously — if someone gives you their key, you''re officially inner-circle. When doing a favour, the correct response is always ''no problem'' or ''easy'' — never ''you owe me''.

content.cultural_note: GP stands for ''General Practitioner'' — the first doctor you see in the UK''s National Health Service (NHS). Healthcare is free, but appointments can be hard to get. The running joke: whatever you have, the GP will tell you ''rest and fluids'' and send you home with paracetamol. It''s mostly true — British medicine tends to be cautious about stronger drugs. A friend meeting you after an appointment is a common kindness, especially if you''ve been worried.

content.cultural_note: Brighton is the most famous seaside town in England — an hour south of London by train. Pebble beach (not sandy, Brits love complaining about this), the iconic Brighton Pier with old-school amusements, fish and chips, bohemian atmosphere. It''s called ''London-by-the-Sea''. Victoria Station is the main departure point from London. ''Getting the train'' for a day trip is a British institution — especially in summer.

phrase_cle.example: — Have you got anything for a sore throat? — Yes, these lozenges work well.
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартная фраза в аптеке. ''Have you got'' — британский вариант ''do you have''. После этого называешь симптом или проблему. Фармацевт порекомендует лекарство. Очень частотная конструкция в бытовом английском.

content.cultural_note: Boots is the UK''s biggest pharmacy chain — you''ll find one on almost every high street. British pharmacists can advise on minor illnesses without a doctor''s note, and often recommend paracetamol, ibuprofen, or throat lozenges for common problems. Paying by card is standard everywhere — even for small amounts. The pharmacist''s parting line ''hope you feel better soon'' is a warm, customary goodbye in medical settings.

phrase_cle.explanation: Волшебная фраза на любом рынке с едой. На британских рынках продавцы обычно с радостью дают попробовать — это часть культуры. ''A bit'' звучит скромно, не жадно. Используется для сыра, оливок, чатни, мёда, колбас — всего что можно попробовать.

content.cultural_note: Borough Market, near London Bridge, is England''s most famous food market — it''s been there in some form since the 1000s. Artisan cheese, sourdough bread, Spanish charcuterie, street food from everywhere. Stallholders calling you ''love'' or ''darling'' is friendly British retail culture, not flirting — totally normal. Trying before you buy is standard. Saturdays are crowded but magical. Budget extra time: you''ll eat your way through the market.

content.cultural_note: British banks are generally good at fraud detection and block cards quickly when suspicious activity appears — abroad purchases especially. If you''re travelling, it''s smart to tell your bank beforehand (''travel notification'') or your card may be declined even for legitimate spending. When a card is blocked, getting a replacement usually takes 3-5 working days. Major banks: HSBC, Barclays, Lloyds, NatWest, Santander — they''re all on every high street.

content.cultural_note: Royal Mail is the UK''s main postal service — though private couriers like DPD and Evri (formerly Hermes) are also popular. Post offices now sit inside newsagents and small shops — the big standalone branches are disappearing. Sending abroad from the UK isn''t cheap: a small parcel to Europe starts around £10-12. Tracked services cost more but give you peace of mind — a very British concept. Christmas time is chaos; post early if you want it to arrive on time.

pratique.scenario: You''re having an honest conversation with a close friend about work. Ask if they''re happy, share your own feelings, talk about dreams or changes you''d like to make.

phrase_cle.example: — So, what do you do? — I''m a teacher. You?
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартный британский способ спросить о профессии. ''Do'' здесь означает ''делать для работы''. Простой, универсальный — звучит на вечеринках, знакомствах, собеседованиях. В ответ не нужна длинная история — короткая формулировка работает лучше всего.

content.cultural_note: ''What do you do?'' is one of the first questions Brits ask when meeting — even more than ''where are you from?''. But it''s not judgmental: people talk openly about job dissatisfaction. ''I''m between jobs'' or ''I''m doing X while I figure things out'' are completely acceptable answers. Making a career change is common and respected — the idea that you should do one thing forever is outdated. Dreaming aloud about creative work is normal friendship conversation, not showing off.

phrase_cle.example: — Well? What did you think? — Honestly? A bit long.
phrase_cle.explanation: Универсальный вопрос после совместного опыта — кино, ресторан, концерт, встреча. Приглашение поделиться мнением. Краткий, вежливый, предполагает честный ответ. Ответы могут быть очень разные: ''loved it'', ''not for me'', ''bit slow'', ''absolute rubbish''.

content.cultural_note: Odeon and Vue are the UK''s biggest cinema chains — you''ll find them in almost every city. Cinema culture is alive in Britain: premiere nights, arthouse indies, classic re-releases. Brits love debating films afterwards — in pubs, over chips, walking home. Disagreement is part of the fun: a friend who calls you ''no heart'' is joking, not fighting. And of course — chips after the cinema is tradition. Fish and chips if you''re near the sea, just chips otherwise.

pratique.scenario: A friend has had a terrible day. Notice they look upset, offer comfort (tea!), listen to their story, and show you''re on their side.

phrase_cle.example: — How was your day? — Ugh. Rubbish, honestly.
phrase_cle.explanation: ''Rubbish'' — очень британский способ сказать ''плохо'', ''дрянь''. Звучит честно, эмоционально, но не слишком драматично. Более мягкий вариант — ''a rough day''. Используется в тёплых, дружеских разговорах — когда хочется выговориться.

content.cultural_note: When a British friend is having a bad day, the ritual is: tea first, then listening. Tea isn''t really about thirst — it''s a ceremony of care. ''That''s not on'' is a brilliantly British expression of moral disapproval — softer than ''that''s outrageous'' but stronger than ''that''s unfair''. It draws a clear line: this behaviour is not acceptable. Good friends respond with outrage on your behalf, not with ''well, maybe you did something wrong...''. Loyalty first, analysis later.

content.cultural_note: London black cabs (taxis) have a famous reputation for honesty — drivers often return lost property. Uber drivers too, though you''ll need to contact them through the app. The Find My iPhone / Find My Device apps can pinpoint lost phones — but a classic call-and-listen often works. When a stranger does you a kindness, the British response is overflow with thanks: ''amazing'', ''legend'', ''you''ve saved my life''. Never just ''thanks''. Offering to pay a little extra (like a tip) is appropriate and polite.

phrase_cle.phrase: I''ve got something to tell you
phrase_cle.example: — Listen, I''ve got something to tell you. — Oh God, is it bad?
phrase_cle.explanation: Серьёзная прелюдия перед важной новостью — хорошей или плохой. Заставляет собеседника сесть и слушать. Интонация решает всё: радостная — хорошая новость, серьёзная — плохая. В любом случае после этой фразы в воздухе появляется напряжение.

content.cultural_note: Brits celebrate career milestones seriously — but also with a self-deprecating edge. ''I''m terrified'' is the honest, humble reaction to good news; saying ''I deserve this, I''m amazing'' would be seen as arrogant. A friend''s response: genuine pride (''proud of you'') + practical comfort (''terrified is normal'') + immediate celebration (pub, dinner, champagne). The British celebration of choice: a pub. Or a fancier place if the news is really big. ''Let''s go celebrate'' is a standard, warm, non-negotiable response.

''That'' подчёркивает эмоциональную силу — не просто ''this view'', а ''что за вид''. Звучит на вершинах гор, на пляжах, в смотровых площадках. Финальная фраза A1 курса — потому что когда ты можешь просто выдохнуть и сказать это, ты уже говоришь по-английски.

content.cultural_note: Arthur''s Seat is an extinct volcano in the middle of Edinburgh — you can hike to the top in about 45 minutes and see all of the city, the sea, and the hills beyond. It''s a tradition for friends visiting Edinburgh together. ''Here''s to [something]'' is the classic British toast — raising a glass (or a takeaway coffee, or just your hand) to a person, an occasion, or a memory. Ending the A1 journey here is intentional: you''ve moved from ordering coffee in a London queue to toasting your friendship on a Scottish hilltop. That''s fluency beginning.

dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah. Didn't sleep.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: How come?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Watched a film. Then another one.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What time did you go to bed?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Two? Maybe later.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. It's Monday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. I just couldn't be bothered to sleep.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That's not a thing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is now. I invented it this week.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a recent night when you stayed up too late. What kept you awake — a film, a book, your phone, work, just thinking? Or — tell her about something you couldn't be bothered to do this week. The smaller and more honest the example, the better.
prompt_phrases: Last night I stayed up too late because...
I couldn't be bothered to...
I went to bed at...
I watched a film and then...
I'm absolutely knackered today.

phrase_cle.example: — I couldn't be bothered to cook last night, so I had cereal.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень британская конструкция — означает 'было лень', 'не было сил/желания'. Не значит 'не получилось'. Значит 'я мог, но не стал'. Используется когда говоришь о себе с лёгкой самоиронией — про дела, которые откладывал.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie didn't sleep last night. She started watching a film, finished it, and instead of going to bed she watched another one. By the time she stopped, it was around two in the morning — maybe later. Now it's Monday and she's at the bus stop with Sophie, looking destroyed. Sophie immediately notices: 'You look knackered.' Knackered is a very British word for completely exhausted. When Sophie asks how come, Marie's answer is short and honest: she watched films, she went to bed late, and she just couldn't be bothered to sleep. It's the kind of confession British friends share without drama — quietly, with a small joke at the end. Sophie pushes back gently: that's not a thing. And Marie's reply is pure British humour — flat, deadpan, slightly proud of her own nonsense.

key_vocabulary.note: Британский сленг для 'exhausted'. Сильнее чем 'tired'. После долгого дня, бессонной ночи, тяжёлой тренировки. Очень частое слово.
'Я мог, но не стал.' Не про неспособность, а про отсутствие желания. С лёгкой самоиронией.
Casual способ спросить причину. Мягче чем 'Why?'. Между друзьями — стандарт.
Современное разговорное выражение. Возражение в шутку, когда собеседник несёт чушь.
key_vocabulary.translation: Так не бывает / Это не существует

key_vocabulary.note: Здесь — уклончивый ответ про точное время. 'Около двух. Может, позже.' То есть точно не помнит, и стыдно признаться насколько.

comprehension_questions.hint: She didn't sleep — she stayed up watching films instead.

pratique.scenario: Imagine someone offers to help you with something this evening — a lift, dinner, anything. How would the conversation go? Or — tell Sophie about your last weekend. Did you make plans, or did you do absolutely nothing? Boring weekends are a perfectly valid answer.

phrase_cle.example: — Hold the door? — Cheers for that, mate.
phrase_cle.explanation: Британское 'спасибо' в коротком разговорном виде. 'Cheers' — это не только тост, в Британии это очень распространённое casual 'thanks'. 'Cheers for that' = 'thanks for that', но теплее и по-дружески. Не для формальной ситуации.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Sophie was finishing up at work on a Friday evening. Her neighbour Carlos walked past and asked if she was heading home. She said yes. Then he offered her a lift, because he had the car that day. Sophie said cheers for that — that's just a friendly British way of saying thanks — and got in the car. They asked each other about their week. Both said it was long. Tired week, basically. Then Carlos asked about the weekend. And here's the thing — Sophie's plan was to do absolutely nothing. Not 'I'm going to relax', not 'I have a quiet weekend planned'. Just nothing. And that's a perfectly fine answer in Britain.

key_vocabulary.note: Casual British 'thanks'. Использовать с друзьями, соседями, коллегами. Не для формальных писем.
Не 'у меня есть машина' (I have a car), а именно 'сегодня я ей пользуюсь'. Очень частое уточнение.
Дружеское приглашение. Casual, тёплый тон. 'Get in' — нейтральнее.
Сленг для 'лечь спать рано / отрубиться'. Не падение, не авария.
Очень частая британская реплика. Принятие чужой позиции без споров. 'Yeah, makes sense.'

comprehension_questions.hint: He has the car today and they're both leaving work at the same time. 'I've got the car' = 'I'm using the car today'.
comprehension_questions.question: Why is Carlos offering Sophie a lift?

comprehension_questions.hint: Crash — British slang for going to bed early or falling asleep heavily. Not a literal crash.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Sophie planning to do tonight?

comprehension_questions.hint: Nothing. And she says it almost proudly. Doing nothing is a respectable plan in Britain.
comprehension_questions.question: What are Sophie's plans for the weekend?

pratique.scenario: Someone invites you to something this weekend — a friend, family member, colleague — and you don't really want to go. How would you politely keep the door open without committing? Or — tell Sophie about a typical Sunday at your home growing up. Was there a special meal? A tradition?

phrase_cle.phrase: I'll see what I can do
phrase_cle.example: — Can you come on Sunday? — I'll see what I can do.
phrase_cle.explanation: Полит-вежливая фраза, которая звучит положительно, но не обещает ничего. Британское 'maybe' с открытой дверью. Когда вам так отвечают — готовьтесь, что человек скорее всего не придёт. Когда вы сами это говорите — вы оставляете себе свободу, не обижая собеседника.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie's mum called on Saturday. She invited Marie to come over for Sunday lunch. Marie didn't really want to go. She wasn't planning anything specific — she just wanted a quiet day at home. So Marie said 'I might have plans.' That's not a real no, but it's not a yes either. It's a polite way to say please don't push. Mum, of course, knew exactly what was happening. She tried again — I'm doing a roast. Your favourite. The classic mum move. Marie tried again with the magic phrase: I'll see what I can do. Mum heard it for what it was — a polite no — and said so. But she also said she'd set a place at the table. Because that's what mums do.

key_vocabulary.note: Вежливое 'maybe', чаще soft no. Звучит позитивно, не обещает ничего.
key_vocabulary.word: I'll see what I can do

key_vocabulary.note: Soft uncertainty. Полезно для мягкого отказа. 'I might have plans' — стандартная отмазка.
Игривый протест между близкими людьми. Тон шутливый, не серьёзная жалоба.
Смягчает приглашение. 'You don't have to stay' = 'останься хотя бы ненадолго'.
Звук, который означает 'я слышу тебя, но не соглашаюсь'. Очень британский. Универсальное nope без слова nope.

comprehension_questions.hint: To invite Marie to Sunday lunch. A roast is the traditional British Sunday meal — meat, potatoes, vegetables, gravy.
comprehension_questions.question: Why is Marie's mum calling?

comprehension_questions.hint: Not really. She says 'I might have plans' — that's a polite way to keep the door open without committing.
comprehension_questions.question: Does Marie want to go?

comprehension_questions.hint: Mum knows her daughter. When Marie says 'I'll see what I can do', Mum hears 'I'm not coming'. But she still sets a place at the table — because that's what mums do.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Mum mean by 'That means no, doesn't it?'

pratique.scenario: Imagine you're at a place you go to often — a coffee shop, a gym, a library — and you keep seeing the same person there. Today they smile at you. How would the conversation go? Or — tell Sophie about a book you read recently. Was it one you couldn't put down?

phrase_cle.example: — Sorry, have we met? You look familiar.
phrase_cle.explanation: Универсальная фраза для начала разговора с тем, кто кажется знакомым. Может означать: (1) реальный вопрос, узнаёшь ли вы человека; (2) вежливая отмазка, чтобы заговорить со странгером; (3) лёгкий флирт. Мягче американского 'Do I know you?'. Очень полезная.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Sophie was in a bookshop on a Saturday — her usual ritual. She was reaching for a Mankell novel. At the same moment, another woman was reaching for the same book. They both stepped back politely. Then they noticed there were two copies on the shelf, so it didn't matter — they could both have one. That could have been the end of it. But the woman — Anna, as it turned out — said cheers and asked if Sophie had read Mankell before. Sophie said yes, she'd read one last year and couldn't put it down. Anna said the same. They realised they were both regulars at the same bookshop, on the same day. So Sophie said: Have we met? You look familiar. They hadn't, actually. But by the end of the conversation, they had. They introduced themselves and joked about meeting again on the same shelf next Saturday.

key_vocabulary.note: Универсальный мягкий заход в разговор. Вежливее чем 'Do I know you?'.
Универсальная британская вежливость. У двери, у полки, в очереди.
Идиома про книгу/сериал, который не получалось остановить. Очень частая.
'End up' = оказываюсь в итоге. Casual самоописание привычки.
Чуть формальнее чем 'same'. Используется в ответ на 'Nice to meet you'.

comprehension_questions.hint: In a bookshop, both reaching for the same book — a novel by Henning Mankell, a Swedish crime writer popular in Britain.
comprehension_questions.question: Where are Sophie and Anna?

comprehension_questions.hint: A book by Mankell. She says 'Couldn't put it down' — a classic English idiom for a book you can't stop reading.
comprehension_questions.question: What did Sophie read last year?

comprehension_questions.hint: No. Sophie says 'You look familiar' but Anna says 'I don't think so'. They've probably been in the same bookshop on Saturdays for months — now they finally said hello.
comprehension_questions.question: Do Sophie and Anna actually know each other?

pratique.scenario: Imagine you have good news to share — a new job, a finished project, anything. How would you tell a close friend? Try keeping it short, like Carlos. Or — tell Sophie about a time you got good news recently. What was it? Who did you tell first?
prompt_phrases: Got the job.
Heard back from...
They called this morning.
About time, mate.
Pay alright?

phrase_cle.example: — I finished the project. — About time, mate.
phrase_cle.explanation: Британское выражение, балансирующее между поддержкой и лёгким подколом. Когда другу наконец удалось то, чего он долго ждал — ты говоришь 'about time'. Это значит: 'я рад, что наконец случилось, и заодно — ты долго возился.' Мужской регистр любит такое — поддержка через мягкое поддразнивание.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Carlos walked into the pub and told Ben straight away — he got the job. Not a long speech, not a big build-up. Just 'Got the job.' Ben knew which one — they'd both been waiting to hear. The recruitment people called Carlos that morning. Better pay than his old place. He starts in two weeks. That was pretty much the whole conversation. Job. Pay. Start date. Done. Ben said 'about time, mate.' That's how men congratulate each other in Britain — by complaining that it took too long. It's a kind of affection, just delivered sideways. Then Ben said 'drinks on you' — meaning Carlos pays for the round. Because that's the rule. Good news, you buy.

key_vocabulary.note: Поддержка через лёгкий подкол. Между близкими друзьями вместо 'Congratulations'.
Британское обращение, мужское по умолчанию. 'Cheers, mate' — стандарт.
Лёгкое удивление. Не агрессивно, не недоверчиво. Просто 'ого'.
Британский social rule. Получил хорошие новости? Покупаешь раунд.
Британская конструкция. Не 'in two weeks' а именно 'two weeks Monday' — точная дата через привязку к дню.

comprehension_questions.hint: He got a new job. He says it in three words: 'Got the job.' That's male British register at full speed.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Carlos's news?

comprehension_questions.hint: Hackney — a district in East London. Ben says 'The Hackney one?' — between friends, the context is already shared.
comprehension_questions.question: Where is the new job?

comprehension_questions.hint: You're paying for the drinks tonight. British tradition — got good news, you buy the round.
comprehension_questions.question: What does 'Drinks on you, then' mean?

pratique.scenario: Imagine you're at your usual café, restaurant, or shop — somewhere you go often. Someone there suggests you try something new. How would the conversation go? Or — tell Sophie about a place you go to regularly. What do you always order?
prompt_phrases: I usually get...

Sounds posh.
Go on, then.

phrase_cle.example: — Want a biscuit with that? — Go on, then.
phrase_cle.explanation: Британская фраза-капитуляция. Буквально 'продолжай, в таком случае', но реальное значение — 'ОК, ты меня уговорил, я попробую/возьму/сделаю'. Звучит когда сдаёшься на маленький соблазн — еду, напиток, что-то приятное. Тёплая, с самоиронией: 'я не должен, но я согласен'.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie walked into her usual café on a Tuesday morning. She's a regular — the barista knows her order without asking. The usual? Flat white, please. But then the barista mentioned something new. They were trying a honey lavender latte that week. Marie's first reaction was suspicion — sounds posh. Posh is a British word for fancy in a slightly silly way. The barista pushed back gently — 'It's actually quite nice. First one's on me.' That's the magic phrase. Free coffee, because she's a regular. Marie pretended to think about it, then said the magic answer: 'Go on, then.' But with a condition. If it's weird, I'm coming back for the flat white. Fair enough, said the barista. Cheers, said Marie. Whole exchange took about a minute. That's how British morning conversations go.

key_vocabulary.note: Британская капитуляция перед маленьким соблазном. С самоиронией.
Когда тебя так спрашивают в кафе/баре — ты regular. Социальная победа.
Я плачу / Я угощаю. 'First one's on me' — первый бесплатно.
Британское слово для 'fancy' с лёгким подколом. Не всегда отрицательно, но всегда с дистанцией.
Универсальное британское принятие чужой позиции. 'Yeah, that makes sense.'

comprehension_questions.hint: A flat white — popular British coffee, espresso with milk foam. The barista says 'The usual?' — when they ask you that, you're officially a regular.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Marie usually order?

comprehension_questions.hint: A honey lavender latte. Marie's first reaction is 'Sounds posh' — British people are suspicious of fancy things, often joking before trying.
comprehension_questions.question: What is the new drink?

comprehension_questions.hint: Two reasons. First, the barista offers it free because she's a regular. Second, she keeps her right of return: if it's weird, she can come back for her usual.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Marie agree to try it?

pratique.scenario: Imagine a friend invites you somewhere this weekend — a restaurant, a film, a walk. Have a short conversation about it. Use 'What are you up to?' to ask, and 'going to' or 'I'm thinking of' to talk about plans. Or — tell Sophie about a place you've been wanting to try. Have you booked it yet? Who would you go with?
prompt_phrases: What are you up to this weekend?
I'm thinking of going to...
Yeah, I'm in.
Should I book?
Around seven / half seven.

phrase_cle.example: — Hey! What are you up to tomorrow? — Probably just the gym.
phrase_cle.explanation: Британский разговорный способ спросить о планах или текущих занятиях. Очень распространённое приветствие. 'What are you up to this weekend?' = 'Какие планы на выходные?'. 'What have you been up to?' = 'Чем занималась?'. Casual, дружеский тон.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie was planning to try a new Italian restaurant on Saturday evening. She asked Sophie what she was up to that weekend — that's the standard British way to ask about plans. Sophie said 'not much', which is half-true and half-British: even if you have plans, 'not much' keeps things casual. Then Marie invited her — Saturday at half seven. Half seven is the British way of saying 7:30. Not 'seven-thirty'. Not 'half past seven' (though that's also fine). Just 'half seven'. It sounds odd at first but you'll hear it everywhere. Sophie said yes immediately. She offered to book the table — but Marie had already done it. Table for three, in case Anna joined. Sophie's reaction was teasing: 'You're organised'. Marie is famously not organised, so this was a small joke. Marie agreed it was terrifying.

key_vocabulary.note: Универсальный британский опросник. Подходит и для 'сейчас', и для 'на выходные'.
Стандартный ответ. Может быть правдой, может — британской вежливостью. Сохраняет casual тон.
Короткое подтверждение участия. Casual, тёплый тон. 'Count me in' — длинная версия.
ВНИМАНИЕ: 'half seven' = 7:30 (как 'half past seven'), а не 6:30. Очень частая британская конструкция времени.
Вежливое предложение помочь. 'Should I book?' = 'Хочешь, я забронирую?'.

comprehension_questions.hint: Going to a new Italian restaurant on Saturday. She's already booked a table.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Marie planning for the weekend?

comprehension_questions.hint: Half seven — that's British for 7:30 PM. 'Half seven' = 'half past seven' = 19:30.
comprehension_questions.question: What time is the booking?

comprehension_questions.hint: Marie already booked the table before even asking — that's unusually well-prepared for her. Sophie is teasing her gently.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie say 'You're organised'?

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about something you keep meaning to do but haven't yet. Could be exercise, a hobby, a phone call, a book. Why haven't you started? Or — tell her about a plan a friend keeps making but never doing. Without naming names!

I'm going to start...
I said the same thing in [month]...
This time it's different.

phrase_cle.example: — Have you been to the new café? — No, I keep meaning to.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень полезная конструкция, чтобы признаться что давно хотел что-то сделать, но всё откладываешь. 'I keep meaning to call her' = 'Всё собираюсь ей позвонить, но не получается'. Настоящее continuous + 'meaning to' + verb. Звучит честно, без оправданий.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Sophie announced she was going to start going to the gym. Marie's first word was 'Again?' — and that one word said everything. Sophie has had this idea before. In January. In March. Now it's a running joke between them. Sophie tried to defend herself — this time it's different — but Marie wasn't buying it. Sophie used the magic phrase 'I keep meaning to', which is a very honest British way to say 'I've been planning this for ages but never actually doing it'. Marie pointed out that thinking about something isn't the same as doing it. Sophie said 'Watch me' — confident, slightly defensive. And Marie's final reply was peak British understatement: 'Will I, though?'. Two doubts in five words.

key_vocabulary.note: Честное признание давнего намерения. Без оправданий. Очень частая конструкция.
Одно слово, полное смысла. Подразумевает: 'мы уже это слышали'. Британский поддразнивающий тон.
Going to для будущих планов. 'I'm going to start' — более определённо чем 'I will start'.
Уверенное обещание. Лёгкий вызов. Часто звучит немного защитно.
Скептический ответ на 'watch me'. Британский understatement в чистом виде.

comprehension_questions.hint: Sophie has said the same thing before — in January and in March. The pattern is familiar.
comprehension_questions.question: Why is Marie sceptical about Sophie's plan?

comprehension_questions.hint: She's been thinking about going to the gym for months but never actually started. It's an honest admission.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Sophie mean by 'I keep meaning to'?

comprehension_questions.hint: Sarcastic. Sophie said 'Watch me' — meaning 'observe me succeed'. Marie's reply suggests she doubts there will be anything to watch.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Marie mean with 'Will I, though?'?

It's a bit boring, but...
The best gift I ever got was...

phrase_cle.example: — Where's the meeting? — I haven't got a clue.
phrase_cle.explanation: Британский эмфатический способ сказать 'не знаю'. Сильнее, чем 'I don't know'. Используется когда вы действительно теряетесь и хотите это подчеркнуть. Без 'a' тоже встречается: 'I haven't a clue'. В американском чаще 'I have no clue'.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Sophie was trying to figure out what to get Marie for her birthday. She asked Anna for help. When Anna asked what Sophie was getting, Sophie said the magic British phrase: 'I haven't got a clue.' That's the strong version of 'I don't know'. Useful when you really, properly don't know. Anna asked what Marie liked. Sophie listed three things: books, wine, quiet evenings. Anna pointed out — that's already three gifts. But Sophie had a problem: Marie already owned every book she wanted. Anna suggested a bookshop voucher. Sophie called it boring. Anna called it useful. And then there was that tiny British exchange — 'Can it be both?' 'It already is.' That's the whole national philosophy in two lines.

key_vocabulary.note: Эмфатическое 'I don't know'. Британский вариант. В US чаще 'I have no clue'.
Стандартное мягкое предложение. Casual, без давления.
Британское слово. Американцы скажут 'gift card'. 'Bookshop voucher' = подарочная карта в книжный.
Простое слово, но слышно: Sophie шутит. Тон делает 'boring' лёгким, не серьёзной критикой.
Британский ответ-подтверждение чего-то очевидного. Сухо, лаконично, с улыбкой.

comprehension_questions.hint: Books, wine, and quiet evenings. Anna immediately points out — that's three potential gifts.
comprehension_questions.question: What three things does Marie like, according to Sophie?

comprehension_questions.hint: A bookshop voucher. Sophie's first reaction is 'boring' — but Anna defends it as useful.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Anna suggest Sophie buy?

comprehension_questions.hint: Sophie asks if a gift can be both boring and useful. Anna confirms — it already is. Light, dry humour.
comprehension_questions.question: How does the conversation end?

pratique.scenario: Imagine you went somewhere recently — a date, an interview, an event. A friend asks 'How did it go?'. Try answering in short, dry, British male style. Or — ask Sophie about something she did recently. Use 'How did it go?' and follow up with short questions.
prompt_phrases: How did it go?
Yeah. Good.

phrase_cle.example: — I had my interview yesterday. — How did it go?
phrase_cle.explanation: Универсальный британский способ спросить про результат события — собеседования, свидания, экзамена, выступления. Casual и универсальный. Можно про прошлое (How did it go?) или будущее (How's it going to go?). После события — самый частый вопрос между друзьями.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Carlos went on a first date and Ben wanted to know how it went. The whole conversation is what male British register sounds like — short answers, no big emotions on the surface, but lots of meaning underneath. Carlos's first answer was just 'Good.' Ben pushed: 'Just good?' Carlos upgraded: 'Better than good.' That's actually high praise. Then he gave the basics: her name's Anna. They're seeing each other again on Friday. She picked the place — a Thai restaurant in Soho. Ben's verdict: 'She picked. Good sign.' This is how British men give relationship advice. Two words. Both true. The whole conversation is forty-three words long but it tells you everything: Carlos is excited, the date went well, Ben is happy for him, and they're both pretending it's not a big deal.

key_vocabulary.note: Универсальный британский опросник про любое событие. Casual.
Британский upgrade. Признание что было отлично, но без восторгов.
Settled middle stage между 'we went on a date' и 'we're together'. Present continuous для текущих отношений.
Past simple. Намеренно короткое. В мужском регистре — оба слова важны.
Британский мужской комплимент в чистом виде. Два слова — высокая похвала.

comprehension_questions.hint: Better than good. He doesn't say much, but the upgrade from 'good' to 'better than good' tells the story.

pratique.scenario: Suggest a day trip to a friend — somewhere by the sea, a city, the countryside. Why should they come? What will you do there? Or — tell Sophie about a place near where you live that's worth a day trip. What's special about it?
prompt_phrases: We should go to...

phrase_cle.example: — Want to go for a walk? — Why not?
phrase_cle.explanation: Двусмысленная фраза. (1) Согласие в ответ на предложение: 'Want to go?' — 'Why not?' = 'Давай'. (2) Реальный вопрос: 'I can't come.' — 'Why not?' = 'Почему?'. Контекст и интонация решают всё. В первом значении — лёгкое, тёплое согласие.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Sophie suggested a day trip to Brighton on Saturday. Brighton is a seaside town about an hour from London by train, famous for its pier, its pebble beach, and its unpredictable weather. Marie's first reaction was practical: 'It's going to rain.' Sophie's reply is pure British acceptance: 'It's Brighton. It always rains.' And Marie agreed — 'True.' Sophie sold the trip with the classics: fish and chips on the pier, walking on the pebbles. Marie said 'why not?' — that's the warm British yes. Quick decision. Train at nine. 'Sorted.' Sorted is one of the most useful British words you'll learn. It means 'decided, done, taken care of'. Conversation lasted about twenty seconds, decision made.

key_vocabulary.note: Тёплое согласие. Не вопрос — а лёгкая капитуляция перед хорошей идеей.
Туда и обратно за один день. Без ночёвки. Британцы ездят на day trips к морю, в другие города, в countryside.
Длинная конструкция, уходящая в море. У Брайтона знаменитый Brighton Palace Pier — с аттракционами, fish and chips, чайками.
Brighton beach — не песок, а pebbles. Камешки. Британцы называют это beach без иронии.
Универсальное британское согласие. 'Sorted' = 'дело сделано, договорились'. Очень частое.

comprehension_questions.hint: She mentions the rain. Brighton — a seaside town in southern England — is famous for unpredictable weather.

comprehension_questions.hint: Fish and chips on the pier and walking on the pebbles. Brighton beach is famously stones, not sand.

comprehension_questions.hint: British word meaning 'done, decided, taken care of'. Useful and very common in agreement.

pratique.scenario: Imagine you're late to meet a friend. What's your excuse? Practise the apology + reason. Or — tell Sophie about the worst time you were late somewhere. What happened? Were you forgiven?
prompt_phrases: I'm so sorry, I got held up.

phrase_cle.phrase: I'm so sorry, I got held up
phrase_cle.example: — You're late. — Sorry, I got held up at work.
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартная вежливая отмазка за опоздание. 'Got held up' = 'меня задержало что-то' (transport, work, etc.). Безличная форма — не ваша вина, обстоятельства. 'I'm so sorry' с растягиванием 'so' — британская мягкая интенсификация. Universal phrase, очень полезная.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Sophie was late to dinner. She arrived flustered and apologetic. Her excuse: the Northern line. The Northern line is part of the London Underground, and it's notoriously unreliable. Twenty minutes stuck between stations is a thing that genuinely happens. Marie's reaction was one word: 'Classic.' That single word covered everything — yes, this is exactly what the Northern line does, no, you don't have to explain further, I get it. Sophie asked if Marie had ordered. Marie said just drinks — she'd waited. Sophie said 'you didn't have to'. Marie said 'I wanted to.' That's the British friendship script: nobody owes anything, but everyone shows up. Sophie called Marie 'a star' — that's a very British compliment, warm but not over the top. And Marie said she was starving. Order time.

key_vocabulary.note: Универсальная отмазка за опоздание. Безличная — не ваша вина, обстоятельства задержали.
Одно слово — 'это типично для этой ситуации'. Обычно про что-то предсказуемо плохое.
Stuck in traffic, stuck on the line, stuck between stations. Универсальное слово для 'не могу двигаться дальше'.
Полу-благодарность, полу-протест. 'Ты сделал больше, чем должен был, спасибо.'
key_vocabulary.translation: Тебе не нужно было / Зря ты

key_vocabulary.note: Тёплый британский комплимент. За то, что человек был добр или помог. Casual.

comprehension_questions.hint: She got stuck on the Northern line — one of the London Underground lines — for twenty minutes between stations.

comprehension_questions.hint: No, just drinks. She waited so they could order together.

comprehension_questions.hint: British way to say 'you're so kind / wonderful'. Casual, warm. Said to thank someone for going out of their way.

pratique.scenario: Help a friend who has lost something. Use 'Have you tried...?' to suggest places to check. Or — tell Sophie about the strangest place you ever found something you'd lost. What was it? Why was it there?
prompt_phrases: Have you tried [the fridge / your pocket / the car]?
When did you last have it?
I've tried everywhere.
Just check.
I once found my [thing] in [strange place].

phrase_cle.example: — My phone won't turn on. — Have you tried charging it?
phrase_cle.explanation: Вежливый способ предложить решение, не звуча как nag. 'Have you tried looking in your bag?' мягче чем 'Look in your bag.' В present perfect — потому что вы спрашиваете про опыт ('Делали ли вы это уже?'). Универсально для советов, troubleshooting, разговора с людьми в стрессе.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie couldn't find her keys. Sophie went into help mode — calm, methodical, asking the obvious questions. When did you last have them? Have you tried the coat pockets? The bag? The kitchen counter? Marie was getting frustrated — yes, yes, yes, I've tried everywhere. Then Sophie said the strange thing: 'Have you tried the fridge?' Marie's reaction was understandable: why would they be in the fridge? But Sophie said 'just check'. And — they were in the fridge. This happens more often than people admit. You walk in with shopping, you put things down somewhere, and your brain confuses 'where keys go' with 'where milk goes'. The dialogue is funny because it's true. The 'have you tried...?' construction is endlessly useful — it's how British people offer help without sounding bossy.

key_vocabulary.note: Вежливое предложение проверить/попробовать. Не звучит как nag.
Универсальное вступление в любое расследование. Past simple.
Present perfect — действие до момента речи. 'Я пробовал, и сейчас всё ещё нет результата.'
Casual императив без давления. 'Я не настаиваю, но всё-таки.'
Конструкция возмущения. 'Why would' + verb выражает недоверие к идее.
key_vocabulary.translation: С чего бы им быть в...?

comprehension_questions.hint: This morning. She's sure of that. The mystery is what happened after.

comprehension_questions.hint: Coat pockets twice, bag three times. She insists she's checked everywhere.
comprehension_questions.question: Where did Marie already check?

comprehension_questions.hint: Half a joke, half experience. People put things in strange places when distracted. Sometimes weird ideas work.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie suggest the fridge?

pratique.scenario: Imagine you receive the wrong order at a restaurant or shop. Practise the polite correction — using 'I think there's been a mix-up' or similar phrases. Or — tell Sophie about a time you got the wrong order somewhere. How did you handle it?
prompt_phrases: I think there's been a mix-up.
I had the [salmon/something else].
It's no problem.
No worries.
Last time I went to...

phrase_cle.phrase: I think there's been a mix-up
phrase_cle.example: — Sorry, I think there's been a mix-up — I didn't order this.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень вежливая британская формулировка для 'произошла ошибка'. Безличная — никого конкретно не обвиняем. 'There's been' (present perfect) звучит мягче, чем 'There is'. Используется в ресторанах, магазинах, на работе — везде, где надо указать на чужую ошибку, не задевая.

content.cultural_note: So basically, the waiter brought the wrong dish. Mushroom risotto instead of salmon. Marie corrected him politely — 'I think there's been a mix-up' — and the whole exchange stayed warm. No raised voices, no drama. British restaurants run on this kind of low-key politeness. Marie said 'it's no problem, take your time' even though it was a problem and she was hungry. The waiter apologised. Marie said 'no worries'. And then — this is the British bit — instead of letting the wrong dish go to waste, Marie asked if anyone wanted the risotto. Sophie said she'd have it. Save him the trip. The waiter was genuinely grateful — 'cheers, that's kind'. The whole conversation is a masterclass in how to handle a small problem while keeping everyone's evening pleasant.

key_vocabulary.note: Безличное и вежливое указание на ошибку. Никого не обвиняет.
key_vocabulary.word: I think there's been a mix-up

key_vocabulary.note: Расслабляющая фраза. Снимает давление. Особенно в обслуживании.
Универсальное британское принятие извинения. Casual, тёплый тон.
Стандартное professional извинение. Короче чем 'I'm so sorry'. Без драматизма.
Идиома про экономию усилий другого человека. Очень тёплая, считается хорошим тоном.

comprehension_questions.hint: The salmon, not the mushroom risotto. The waiter brought the wrong dish.

pratique.scenario: Imagine you're having a rough day. A friend asks how you are. Try the British male register — short answers, understatement, no drama. Or — tell Sophie about a small disaster at home or work. Use 'could be worse' even if it really couldn't.
prompt_phrases: Alright, mate?
Could be worse.
What's wrong?
[Something]'s gone.
Brutal.

phrase_cle.example: — How's work? — Could be worse. Could be raining.
phrase_cle.explanation: Эталонный британский understatement. Используется чтобы признать, что что-то плохо, но удержать стоицизм. Когда у вас всё разваливается, и кто-то спрашивает 'как дела?' — 'could be worse' оставляет дверь приоткрытой. Может быть и истинно, и иронично — тон решает всё.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Ben's boiler had broken. In Britain, that means no hot water until someone fixes it. He had three days to wait — a plumber was coming on Friday. Cold showers in the meantime. But this isn't a complaint conversation. This is a male British conversation. Luca asked 'alright, mate?' — that's the standard British 'how are you'. Ben said 'could be worse'. That's the standard British 'I'm not great but I'm coping'. Luca pushed gently: what's wrong? Ben gave the facts in short bursts: boiler's gone, three days, no hot water, plumber on Friday. Luca said 'brutal' — that's the male equivalent of sympathy. And Ben repeated 'could be worse' at the end — said with a small smile, because he knows it's a stretch, but stoicism is the rule. Notice the pacing. Notice how much is left unsaid.

key_vocabulary.note: Эталонный британский understatement. Признание плохого + удержание стоицизма.
Британское мужское приветствие. Это не вопрос про самочувствие — это hello.
'Gone' в этом смысле = 'broken, dead, finished'. Boiler's gone, laptop's gone, kettle's gone. Очень частая структура.
Британский casual для мужчины. 'The bloke from the company' = 'тот мужик из компании'. Без негатива.
Сленг для 'это тяжело'. Современное и универсальное. Часто заменяет 'awful' или 'terrible'.

comprehension_questions.hint: His boiler has broken — 'boiler's gone' = 'the boiler is broken'. So no hot water.

comprehension_questions.hint: Three days — until Friday. He's taking cold showers in the meantime.

comprehension_questions.hint: British male stoicism. Things are clearly bad — three days of cold showers — but he refuses to fully complain. Acknowledging the problem without dramatising it.

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie you're not feeling great. What's wrong? Has anything helped? Or — describe a time when a friend looked after you when you weren't well. What did they do?
prompt_phrases: I'm not feeling great.
Headache since this morning.
Have you taken anything?
When did you last eat?
Sit down. I'll make you [tea/toast].

phrase_cle.example: — Are you coming tonight? — I might pass. I'm not feeling great.
phrase_cle.explanation: Мягкий универсальный способ сказать что плохо себя чувствуешь. Не настолько серьёзно, как 'I'm ill'. Полезно в ситуациях, когда не уверен насколько сильно болеешь, или просто устал. 'Not great' — британский understatement в чистом виде, может означать что угодно от лёгкого недомогания до жуткого состояния.
phrase_cle.translation: Мне нехорошо / Я не в форме

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie had a headache and looked pale. Sophie noticed immediately. The conversation moved fast through diagnostic questions — have you taken anything, when did you last eat? Marie's answers told the story. Paracetamol, no help. Last meal probably yesterday. Sophie just said her name — 'Marie.' One word, full of soft reproach. That's a friend who knows you. Marie said 'I know' — admitting she'd been careless. Sophie's solution was simple British care: toast. No drama, no lecture. Just bread, butter, sit down. Marie tried the polite refusal — 'you don't have to' — and Sophie ignored it. 'I'm making toast.' That's how British friends look after each other. Quietly. Practically. Without making a fuss.

key_vocabulary.note: Мягкое универсальное недомогание. Британский understatement.
Стандартное наблюдение про самочувствие. Не комплимент — beginning заботы.
Про лекарства. Universal opener после жалобы на боль.
Британское универсальное обезболивающее. В США — Tylenol. То же самое лекарство.
Полу-вежливый отказ от помощи. На который британский друг отвечает 'I'm making toast' и игнорирует.

comprehension_questions.hint: She has a headache since this morning. She took paracetamol but it didn't help.

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a time you were late to work or to a meeting. What happened? How did your boss react? Or — describe a typical morning when transport goes wrong in your city. What do you do?
prompt_phrases: I was [number] minutes late.
I had to wait for...
[Transport] was down.
She gave me the look.
I'm going to keep my head down.

phrase_cle.example: — Why are you late? — I had to drop my sister at school.
phrase_cle.explanation: Past simple от 'have to'. Используется для прошлой необходимости — что-то заставило вас сделать что-то. 'I had to wait for the bus.' = 'Мне пришлось ждать автобус, не было выбора.' Объяснение причины опоздания, отсутствия, изменения планов. В A2 это базовая структура.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie was twenty minutes late to work. The Tube — the London Underground — had problems, so she had to wait for an Uber. By the time she got in, her boss Sandra was already there. Sandra gave her 'the look' — that wordless boss expression that says 'I noticed'. Marie tried to explain. Sandra wasn't interested in the explanation. She said 'leave earlier next time'. Sophie's reaction was perfect: 'Of course she did.' Sandra is clearly that kind of boss. Marie's plan for the rest of the day was to 'keep my head down' — that's a British idiom for staying quiet and invisible at work, especially when you're already in trouble. Sophie agreed: 'Wise.' This is the language of office friendship — short, knowing, full of unspoken understanding.

key_vocabulary.note: Past simple of 'have to'. Объяснение прошлой необходимости. Базовая A2 структура.
Британское прозвище London Underground. Не 'metro', не 'subway' — Tube. 'I take the Tube to work.'
'Down' = не работает, отключено. Применимо к транспорту, сайтам, серверам.
'The look' с the — особый взгляд, понятный без слов. Часто от босса, мамы, партнёра.
Идиома для 'быть тихим, не привлекать внимание'. Особенно на работе, когда ты в неудачном положении.

comprehension_questions.hint: The Tube — London Underground — was down. She had to wait for an Uber instead.
comprehension_questions.question: Why was Marie late?

comprehension_questions.hint: She gave Marie 'the look' and said 'leave earlier next time'. Not sympathetic.
comprehension_questions.question: How did her boss Sandra react?

comprehension_questions.hint: Stay quiet, do your work, don't draw attention. British idiom for surviving a bad day at work.
comprehension_questions.question: What does 'keep my head down' mean?

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a time you said something you didn't mean. What happened? Did you fix it? Or — describe a small argument you had recently. What started it? How did it end?
prompt_phrases: I had the worst phone call with...

phrase_cle.phrase: I didn't mean it like that
phrase_cle.example: — That sounded harsh. — Sorry, I didn't mean it like that.
phrase_cle.explanation: Универсальная фраза для починки разговора, где вас не так поняли. 'I didn't mean it like that' = 'мои слова прозвучали не так, как я хотел(а)'. Очень полезно после того, как фраза вышла резкой. Мягкая попытка отступить, не извиняясь полностью.
phrase_cle.translation: Я не это имел(а) в виду

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie had an argument with her mum on the phone. The kind of argument that starts about nothing — they were just talking — and then someone says something slightly wrong, and someone else snaps back. Marie's mum said something about Marie's flat being too small. Marie said 'maybe stop visiting then'. That's a sharp thing to say to your mum. Marie immediately knew it. She told Sophie 'I didn't mean it like that' — a phrase that exists for exactly these moments. Sophie didn't lecture. She just asked the practical question: did you call her back? Marie said not yet. Then she asked, 'I should, shouldn't I.' That's not really a question — it's Marie asking Sophie to confirm what she already knows. Sophie said 'you should.' This is what good friendship sounds like in Britain. Quiet, supportive, slightly stern when needed.

key_vocabulary.note: Мягкое отступление после резкой фразы. Универсальный peace-maker.
key_vocabulary.word: I didn't mean it like that
key_vocabulary.translation: Я не это имел(а) в виду

key_vocabulary.note: Стандартное вступление в рассказ о ссоре. Подразумевает: причина была мелкой.
key_vocabulary.word: I don't even know how it started

key_vocabulary.note: Сарказм с 'maybe' и 'then'. Звучит хуже, чем хотел сказать говорящий.
Стандартный follow-up на любую ссору в семье/с друзьями. Самый практичный вопрос.
Question tag. Не вопрос — поиск подтверждения. Когда сам знаешь ответ, но хочешь услышать.

comprehension_questions.hint: Something about Marie's flat being too small. Marie took it as criticism.

comprehension_questions.question: What did Marie say back?

comprehension_questions.hint: Call her mum back. Sophie agrees she should. The 'shouldn't I' tag is Marie asking for confirmation.

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a recent thing that went well — an interview, a test, a presentation, a difficult conversation. What did people ask? What did you say? Or — wish a friend luck for something they have coming up.
prompt_phrases: It went really well.
They asked about...
I had a really good answer.
Fingers crossed.
When do you hear?

phrase_cle.example: — How was the interview? — It went really well, actually.
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартный британский способ описать удачное событие — собеседование, экзамен, презентацию, свидание. 'It went well' нейтрально, 'really well' с энтузиазмом. Past simple. Универсальная конструкция для рассказа о пройденном опыте.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie had a job interview and it went really well. She came home buzzing — that's the British word for the kind of nervous good energy you have after a thing has gone better than you hoped. Sophie wanted details. Marie gave them: standard questions about why this job, why now. The kind every interviewer asks. Marie had a good answer ready. She thought the interviewer liked her. Sophie's response was the perfect British congratulation: 'Look at you.' Three words, full of warmth and pride. Then Sophie asked the practical question: when do you hear? End of the week. 'Fingers crossed' — that's the British way to wish luck. From the gesture: literally crossing your fingers for hope. The conversation ends with Sophie being a good friend: 'You'll get it.' She doesn't know that, but she says it anyway, because that's what friends do.

key_vocabulary.note: Стандартное описание удачного события. Past simple.
Универсальная фраза заинтересованного слушателя. Casual, тёплая.
Британский тёплый комплимент. Признание чужого успеха без пафоса.
Стандартное британское пожелание удачи. От жеста: crossing fingers.
Поддержка через future simple. 'I believe you will succeed.'

comprehension_questions.hint: Really well. She felt the interviewer liked her, and she had a good answer to the standard questions.
comprehension_questions.question: How did Marie's interview go?

comprehension_questions.hint: End of the week. They told her at the interview.
comprehension_questions.question: When will Marie hear back?

comprehension_questions.hint: British way to wish luck. Literally crossing your fingers — gesture for hoping something happens. Used as a phrase even without the gesture.
comprehension_questions.question: What does 'fingers crossed' mean?

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about how you met someone — a friend, a partner, a colleague. Where did you meet? What started the conversation? Or — describe an interesting stranger you met recently. What did you talk about?
prompt_phrases: I met him/her at...
We just got chatting.

Did you exchange numbers?

phrase_cle.example: — How did you meet? — We were waiting in line and we just got chatting.
phrase_cle.explanation: Британский разговорный способ описать случайное знакомство-через-разговор. 'Got chatting' = started a conversation naturally, без плана. Используется когда вы хотите подчеркнуть, что не флиртовали специально и не подстраивали ситуацию — просто получилось.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Tom is new in Marie's life. She met him at her usual coffee shop the day before, and somehow Sophie picked up on it — probably from Marie mentioning him casually. Sophie immediately interrogated. Marie tried to play it down — 'just a guy', 'we just got chatting' — but Sophie wasn't having it. The detail that gave it away: he was reading the same book Marie's reading. That's a coincidence in a coffee shop, and the kind of thing that starts a conversation. Marie defended him — 'he was there before me' — meaning he wasn't placing himself near her on purpose. Sophie pushed for the real news: did you exchange numbers? Marie said maybe. Sophie said 'Marie!' — one word, full of accusation. And Marie admitted: yes, and he texted this morning. This is how friends share early-stage romantic news in Britain. Quietly. Reluctantly. With the friend dragging it out.

key_vocabulary.note: Случайно, без плана, естественно. Очень частая британская конструкция о новых знакомствах.
Полу-отмазка от расспросов. 'Just' тут смягчает — пытается уменьшить значение.
Шуточно. Sophie дразнит — слишком удачное совпадение с книгой.
Стандартный британский вопрос про новое знакомство. Прямой, casual.
Past simple. 'Texted' = отправил SMS/сообщение. Универсальный глагол.

comprehension_questions.hint: A guy Marie met at the coffee shop yesterday. He was reading the same book she's reading.
comprehension_questions.question: Who is Tom?

comprehension_questions.hint: They 'just got chatting' — a casual conversation that happened naturally. He was already there when she arrived.
comprehension_questions.question: How did they start talking?

comprehension_questions.hint: Yes — this morning. Marie tries to play it cool with 'maybe', but Sophie gets it out of her.

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about your first day at a new job, school, or course. What did you do? Did you remember everyone's names? Or — give advice to a friend starting somewhere new this week.
prompt_phrases: How was your first day?
Long. But good.
Mostly meetings.
I remembered [number] names.
Just call him 'mate'.

phrase_cle.example: — So! How was your first day? — Long. But good.
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартный британский опросник для важных первых дней — на работе, в школе, в новом городе, на курсах. Безопасный, тёплый. Можно ответить коротко ('fine') или развёрнуто. Подходит для любого человека, который только что начал что-то новое.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie had her first day at the new job. Sophie asked the standard 'how was your first day' — the kind of question every new starter gets. Marie's answer was honest and short: long, but good. The day was mostly meetings — that's typical of British office onboarding. By the end, she'd met everyone twice but only remembered three names out of fifteen. She was worried about that, but Sophie reassured her: that's day one. Nobody remembers. Then came the practical problem — there's a guy and Marie can't remember if he's called Mark, Matt, or Mike. Sophie's solution is genuinely useful in British offices: just call him 'mate'. Universal, casual, gets you out of the situation. Marie called this 'genius'. It is. This is the kind of small advice that makes a friendship — practical, slightly absurd, said with affection.

key_vocabulary.note: Универсальный британский опросник для важного первого дня.
Идеальный британский ответ. Признание усталости + позитив. Без драматизма.
Useful adverb. Описывает что было главным содержанием дня/события.
Британская конструкция дробей с 'out of'. Простая и универсальная.
Универсальное обращение когда не помнишь имя. В офисе работает с любым мужчиной.

comprehension_questions.hint: Mostly meetings. She met everyone twice — that's the chaos of a first day.
comprehension_questions.question: What did Marie do all day?

comprehension_questions.hint: Three out of fifteen. She's worried but Sophie says that's normal for day one.
comprehension_questions.question: How many names did Marie remember?

comprehension_questions.hint: Just call him 'mate'. Universal British workaround for forgotten names.

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about the last time you visited family. What did you bring? How was it? Or — talk about how often you visit family. Is there guilt? Are you good or bad at it?
prompt_phrases: I haven't been home in ages.
I should probably visit.
Mum's been hinting.
I'll go this weekend.
Take cake.

phrase_cle.example: — I haven't seen my parents in three months. I should probably visit.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень британское самоуговаривание. 'Should probably' — мягкое признание долга, без строгого 'must'. Используется когда вы откладывали что-то и наконец решили. Перед визитом к родителям, доктору, друзьям — типичная конструкция.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie hadn't been home to her mum's since Christmas. That's months. Sophie made her face it — 'define ages' — and Marie had to admit Christmas, which Sophie agreed was 'actually ages'. Marie said the British self-talk: 'I should probably visit.' Sophie agreed — 'yes you should'. Then came the funny detail about Marie's mum's hinting. She sent Marie a photo of her kitchen, with no caption. No 'hi sweetheart, miss you' — just a photo. Sophie diagnosed it correctly: 'classic mum guilt'. That's a real British mother move. The silent emotional message. Marie decided to go that weekend. Sophie's parting advice was perfect: 'Take cake.' Two words of peace-offering wisdom. Bring food, smooth the visit, everyone wins.

key_vocabulary.note: Мягкое самоуговаривание. Без строгого долга.
Идиома. 'Haven't seen him in ages' = очень давно. Более casual чем 'a long time'.
Подавать сигналы без прямого вопроса. Очень британский способ давления.
Универсальный термин. Можно про любого parent. Often used wryly.
Британская traditions — приходить с food. Cake — нейтрально, всегда работает.

comprehension_questions.hint: Christmas — months ago. She admits 'that's actually ages'.

comprehension_questions.hint: 'Take cake.' Two words — bring something nice as a peace offering. Very British solution.

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a time you had to ask someone something important — a serious question, a favour, a change. How did you start the conversation? Or — describe how relationships become 'official' where you're from. Is there a moment? A conversation?
prompt_phrases: I've been meaning to ask you something.
Do you want to make this official?
Took you long enough.
Is that a yes?
Yes. Obviously yes.

phrase_cle.phrase: I've been meaning to ask you something
phrase_cle.example: — Anna, I've been meaning to ask you something. Are you free Saturday?
phrase_cle.explanation: Серьёзная вступительная фраза перед важным вопросом. Present perfect continuous — 'я планировал это какое-то время'. Сигнализирует собеседнику: то, что сейчас будет, не случайный вопрос, я думал об этом. Используется перед предложениями, важными разговорами, неловкими темами.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Carlos and Anna had been dating for months. They knew it was good. But neither had said the word 'official' until Carlos finally did. The conversation is short but the stakes are real. Carlos started with 'I've been meaning to ask you something' — the British signal that this isn't small talk. Anna said 'okay...' with the trailing dots, which means she was preparing herself. Carlos asked vaguely — 'do you want to make this official?' — leaving Anna to decode it. She decoded it: 'are you asking me to be your girlfriend?' Carlos confirmed. Anna's reply was perfectly British: 'took you long enough.' Loving teasing. Then 'yes, obviously yes'. And Carlos's reply was the warm version of 'brilliant' — not sarcastic, just genuinely happy in one word. This is how British people say important things. With a bit of indirection, a bit of teasing, and as few words as possible.

key_vocabulary.note: Серьёзная вступительная фраза. Present perfect continuous.
key_vocabulary.word: I've been meaning to ask you something

key_vocabulary.note: Идиома про переход отношений на следующий уровень. Универсально для пар.
Британское слово-усилитель. 'Properly together' = действительно вместе, не просто видимся.
Тёплое поддразнивание. Ждали этого момента, наконец дождались.
Ясное подтверждение с лёгкой иронией — 'это же очевидно'.

comprehension_questions.hint: To make their relationship official — to be his girlfriend. After several months of dating.

comprehension_questions.hint: Friendly teasing. She's been waiting for him to ask. The reply means 'finally!' but with affection.

comprehension_questions.hint: Genuine happiness. Here it's not sarcastic — it's the warm version. Just one word, but the relief and joy are clear.
