phrase_cle.phrase: I've got something to tell you
phrase_cle.example: — Sit down. I've got something to tell you.
phrase_cle.explanation: Сигнальная фраза перед важной новостью — хорошей или плохой. Present perfect 'have got' = британское 'I have'. Создаёт паузу перед объявлением, готовит собеседника. После этой фразы все замолкают и слушают. Универсальная конструкция для серьёзных моментов.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Sophie had been quietly applying for a promotion at work. She hadn't told Marie — not because she was hiding it, but because of the British superstition about jinxing. If you talk about good things in advance, you'll lose them. So she waited until the news was real. When she had it, she told Marie with the magic phrase: 'I've got something to tell you.' Marie's first instinct was panic — 'now I'm panicking' is a great British line for that moment when someone says they have news. Sophie said: 'I got the promotion.' Marie was floored. Then a little hurt — Sophie hadn't told her about applying! Sophie explained the jinx logic, which Marie accepted. Then came the proud moment: 'I'm so proud of you.' And Marie's verdict was perfect: 'We're celebrating. Tonight. No arguments.' British friendship insists on celebration when celebration is due.

key_vocabulary.note: Сигнальная фраза перед важной новостью. Заставляет слушателя замолчать и слушать.
key_vocabulary.word: I've got something to tell you

key_vocabulary.note: Часто говорят перед хорошей новостью — чтобы не ждали плохого. Британский юмор.
Британское суеверие. Если говорить о хорошем заранее — не сбудется.
key_vocabulary.word: I didn't want to jinx it

key_vocabulary.note: Тёплое признание чужого успеха. Без иронии. Между близкими — частая фраза.
'Я решил(а), ты не отвертишься.' Дружеская команда без агрессии.

comprehension_questions.hint: She got a promotion at work. She'd applied for it without telling Marie.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Sophie's news?

comprehension_questions.hint: She didn't want to 'jinx it' — superstition that talking about good things in advance brings bad luck.
comprehension_questions.question: Why didn't Sophie tell Marie she'd applied?

comprehension_questions.hint: Celebrating tonight. 'No arguments' means: I've decided, you're not getting out of it.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Marie suggest?

dialogue.line: Right. I'd like to make a toast.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh god.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Be nice, Sof.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I'm always nice.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Disputed.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: To Marie. Best friend, terrible cook, slightly chaotic — and the funniest person I know.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sof.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Happy birthday, Mar. We love you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Cheers!
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: To Marie!
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Stop. I'm going to cry.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Don't. Drink your wine.

pratique.scenario: Imagine you're making a toast at a friend's birthday. What would you say? Try teasing + warm — Sophie's recipe. Or — describe the last birthday celebration you went to. What was special?
prompt_phrases: I'd like to make a toast.
To [name] — best friend, [funny detail]...
Happy birthday.
We love you.
Cheers!

phrase_cle.example: — I'd like to make a toast. To Marie — happy birthday.
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартная конструкция для тостов — 'make a toast' (произнести), 'raise a toast' (поднять бокал), 'cheers!' (сам тост). На дне рождения, свадьбе, важном событии. После 'I'd like to make a toast' все замолкают и слушают. Универсальная конструкция.

content.cultural_note: So basically, it was Marie's birthday and the whole gang had gathered for dinner. Sophie, Carlos, Anna, Marie. Sophie decided to make a toast. Carlos's first reaction was 'oh god' — affectionate dread, because Sophie's toasts are unpredictable. Anna told Sophie to be nice. Sophie defended herself: 'I'm always nice.' Marie said 'disputed.' Standard banter. Then Sophie delivered the toast. The first half was teasing — terrible cook, slightly chaotic. Marie's expression probably softened. Then the soft landing: 'best friend' and 'funniest person I know'. And the closer: 'Happy birthday, Mar. We love you.' Marie said 'Sof' the way people do when they're trying not to react too strongly. Carlos said 'Cheers!', Anna said 'To Marie!' Glasses up. Marie said she was going to cry. Sophie's reply: 'Don't. Drink your wine.' British emotional management at its finest.

key_vocabulary.note: Стандартная вступительная фраза. Все замолкают и слушают.
key_vocabulary.word: I'd like to make a toast

key_vocabulary.note: Шутливое 'я не согласен'. Используется для тёплого контраргумента.
Любящее описание чьего-то характера. Не оскорбление — affectionate teasing.
Британский тост, универсальный. Поднимая бокалы. Тоже = 'thanks'.
Practical British emotional management. 'Не плачь — занимайся вином.'

comprehension_questions.hint: Marie's. The whole gang is at dinner together — Sophie, Carlos, Anna, and Marie.
comprehension_questions.question: Whose birthday is it?

comprehension_questions.hint: Best friend, terrible cook, slightly chaotic, and the funniest person she knows. Affectionate teasing.
comprehension_questions.question: How does Sophie describe Marie in the toast?

comprehension_questions.hint: Sophie's toast was unexpectedly warm. The 'we love you' got her.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Marie say 'I'm going to cry'?

dialogue.line: Tom?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Hey. Sorry, can you hear me?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Just about. Where are you?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Train. Heading back.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: What time will you be in?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Eleven? Maybe later.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I'll wait up.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You don't have to.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I want to.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mar. I miss you.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Hurry home.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm trying.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

pratique.scenario: Imagine a short phone call with someone you love who's away. Keep it British — short, warm, practical. Or — describe a time you waited up for someone. Did they make it? Was it worth it?
prompt_phrases: Where are you?
What time will you be in?
I'll wait up.
I miss you.
Hurry home.

phrase_cle.example: — I'll be home tomorrow. — Good. I miss you.
phrase_cle.explanation: Простая, но эмоционально нагруженная фраза. В Британии говорится тише, мягче, чем в американских фильмах. После 'I miss you' британцы часто не делают паузу — продолжают разговор, чтобы не перегружать момент. В разговоре с партнёром, семьёй, близким другом — звучит редко и значит много.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie was at home and Tom called from a train. He'd been away for a few days — work, probably — and was on his way back. The signal was bad: 'just about' = barely. Tom would be home around eleven, maybe later. Marie said she'd wait up — that means stay awake until he arrives. Tom said 'you don't have to' — the standard British protest. Marie said 'I want to' — equally standard reply. Then came the small moment: Tom said 'Mar. I miss you.' One name, three words. Marie didn't say 'I miss you too' — she said 'Hurry home.' Same meaning, different texture. That's how British couples handle these moments. Direct enough to feel real, indirect enough to keep emotional pressure low. Tom said 'I'm trying' and the call probably ended there. Phone calls between people who love each other don't have to be long. Sometimes a minute is enough.

key_vocabulary.note: Эмоциональная фраза. В британском контексте — тише, реже, значимее.
Идиома. 'Can you hear me?' — 'Just about' = слышу плохо но слышу.
Casual способ сказать 'on my way home'. Универсальное.
Stay awake until someone arrives. Конкретная конструкция, не просто 'wait'.
Тёплое прощание. Не команда — пожелание. Часто между близкими.

comprehension_questions.hint: On a train, heading back home. Marie can barely hear him.
comprehension_questions.question: Where is Tom?

comprehension_questions.hint: Wait up for him — stay awake until he gets home, even though it'll be 11 PM or later.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Marie plan to do?

comprehension_questions.hint: He's been away. The phrase is short and warm — British emotional language doesn't go bigger than this on a train call.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Tom say 'I miss you'?

dialogue.line: Marie. Sit down.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Why? What's wrong?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Nothing. Just sit down.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom, you're scaring me.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I've been carrying this around for a week.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Carrying what?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: This.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Oh.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mar. Will you marry me?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Yes. Yes, of course yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a marriage proposal you've heard about — yours, a friend's, a famous one. What was special? Or — talk about traditions around proposals where you live. Are they big public events, or quiet private moments?
prompt_phrases: Sit down.
I've been carrying this around for a week.
Will you marry me?
Yes. Of course yes.
It happened on...

phrase_cle.example: — Mar. Will you marry me?
phrase_cle.explanation: Самый известный вопрос в любом языке. На английском — простой и прямой. Will + you + marry + me. Никаких украшений. В моменте предложения часто говорится тише, чем ожидаешь — потому что эмоций больше, чем слов. Универсальная фраза, никаких британских вариаций.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Tom proposed to Marie. He didn't make a big speech. He didn't take her to a fancy restaurant or hire a string quartet. He just asked her to sit down, told her he'd been carrying something around for a week, showed her the ring, and asked the question. That's a very British proposal. Quiet. Direct. The whole thing is in the small details — Marie thinks something's wrong, Tom reassures her, the build-up is short, and the moment lands when it lands. Marie's response was perfect: 'Yes. Yes, of course yes.' Three yeses, each one more sure than the last. The first 'yes' is the answer. The second 'yes' is the relief. The third 'of course yes' is the emotion catching up. There's a reason this scene exists in every romantic story — it's the moment two lives officially join. In this one, no music, no fireworks. Just two people in a room, and a yes.

key_vocabulary.note: Самый известный вопрос на английском. Прямой, без украшений.
Командная фраза, но мягкая. Готовит собеседника к чему-то важному.
Реакция на серьёзный тон. Перед хорошей новостью часто говорится.
Present perfect continuous. Действие, начатое в прошлом, продолжающееся до сейчас.
Уверенное подтверждение с эмоциональным усилением. После простого 'yes'.

comprehension_questions.hint: Because he's about to ask her to marry him. He doesn't say it directly — just creates the moment.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Tom ask Marie to sit down?

comprehension_questions.hint: A week — he says 'I've been carrying this around for a week'. Probably means the ring.
comprehension_questions.question: How long has Tom been waiting?

comprehension_questions.hint: 'Yes. Yes, of course yes.' Three yeses, increasing in confidence. The emotion overflows.
comprehension_questions.question: How does Marie respond?

dialogue.line: Mum, hi. Sit down.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Why?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I've got news.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. What is it?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Tom asked me to marry him.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Oh. Oh!
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I said yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Of course you did. Show me the ring.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Here.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Oh, Marie. It's beautiful.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Are you crying?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes I am. Don't say a word.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a piece of important news you shared with family. How did they react? Or — imagine telling your parent a big piece of good news. What would you say? How would they react?
prompt_phrases: I've got news.
[Name] asked me to...
I said yes.
Show me the ring.
Are you crying?

phrase_cle.example: — Mum, sit down. I've got news.
phrase_cle.explanation: Самое короткое объявление о важной новости. Без 'something to tell you' — просто 'I've got news'. Используется для важных событий: помолвка, беременность, переезд, повышение. После этой фразы все замирают. Британский стандарт лаконичности.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Marie went to her mum's house to tell her about the engagement. The conversation is short and perfect. 'Sit down.' 'Why?' 'I've got news.' That sequence — sit down, news — is enough to make any mum's heart stop. Then Marie said it: 'Tom asked me to marry him.' Mum's reaction is real — 'Oh. Oh!' Two ohs because the first one is processing and the second one is realising. Marie said 'I said yes.' Mum said 'Of course you did' — meaning 'I knew you would, who wouldn't'. Then the practical British follow-up: 'show me the ring.' Marie showed her. Mum said 'oh, Marie. It's beautiful.' Then the moment Marie noticed: are you crying? Mum admitted it — 'yes I am' — and immediately deflected with humour: 'don't say a word.' That's a real mother. Emotional, practical, slightly afraid to look soft. Marie tells her mum first because that's what daughters do.

key_vocabulary.note: Короткое объявление важной новости. Без украшений.
Подтверждение очевидного. Тёплое, без иронии.
Прямой императив. После хороших новостей часто звучит — про кольцо, фото, документ.
Стандартная похвала о вещи. Без интенсификации — британский tonе.
Защитная фраза, чтобы не дразнили из-за эмоций. Очень частая в семьях.

comprehension_questions.hint: Tom proposed and she said yes. She's engaged.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Marie's news?

comprehension_questions.hint: Two 'oh's that capture the shift from confusion to understanding. Then immediately: show me the ring.
comprehension_questions.question: How does Mum react?

comprehension_questions.hint: She's crying — and doesn't want Marie teasing her about it. British emotional management.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Mum say 'Don't say a word' at the end?

dialogue.line: Right. Hen do planning. Two weeks to go.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Where are we doing it?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Nothing wild. Please.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Define wild.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: No matching t-shirts. No strippers.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Bare minimum, honestly.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Okay. Cocktails first. Dinner. Then dancing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sounds perfect.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Who's doing the booking?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I'll sort it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sof, you're a lifesaver.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I know. Now leave it with me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Imagine you're planning an event with friends. Who's doing what? Who's saying 'I'll sort it'? Or — describe a celebration you helped plan. What did it look like?
prompt_phrases: [Number] weeks to go.
Nothing wild, please.
Cocktails first. Dinner. Then dancing.
I'll sort it.
You're a lifesaver.

phrase_cle.example: — Who's booking the venue? — I'll sort it.
phrase_cle.explanation: Британское обещание взять что-то на себя. 'Sort' = устроить, разобраться, организовать. 'I'll sort the booking', 'I'll sort the cake' — универсальная конструкция для распределения задач. Звучит уверенно, без обещаний 'I'll definitely make it perfect'. Просто — я этим займусь.

content.cultural_note: So basically, the wedding was approaching and the hen do needed planning. Sophie took charge. Anna and Marie were there for the strategy session. Marie's main request was: nothing wild. She specifically banned matching t-shirts (a classic hen do trope in Britain) and strippers (another classic). Anna joked that this was 'bare minimum' — meaning even with those banned, the night could still be wild. The plan they landed on was traditional and calm: cocktails, dinner, dancing. Marie approved. Then the practical moment — who's doing the booking? Sophie said 'I'll sort it.' That's the magic phrase. 'Sort' is one of the most useful British verbs. It means organise, fix, take care of, arrange. Marie called Sophie a 'lifesaver' — affectionate, exaggerated, true. Sophie's reply was perfect: 'I know. Now leave it with me.' British project management in a sentence. Confidence + boundaries.

key_vocabulary.note: Универсальное британское обещание организовать что-то.
Британское слово для bachelorette party. От 'hen' (курица) — невеста и подружки.
Шутливое уточнение. Sophie использует это вместо 'tell me more'.
Без чего нельзя. Anna шутит, что это уже минимальные требования.
Уверенная финальная фраза. Я беру управление, ты не вмешиваешься.

comprehension_questions.hint: Nothing wild — specifically no matching t-shirts and no strippers. Calm version of a hen do.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Marie not want for her hen do?

comprehension_questions.hint: Cocktails first, then dinner, then dancing. Classic British hen do format.
comprehension_questions.question: What's the plan?

comprehension_questions.hint: Sophie. She says 'I'll sort it' — and that's that. Marie calls her a lifesaver.
comprehension_questions.question: Who's organising it?

dialogue.line: Mar. Look at this view.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Wow. Just wow.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Worth the climb?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Definitely worth the climb.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Last weekend before the wedding.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I can't believe it. Honestly.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You ready?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I think so. Are you?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: For your wedding? Yeah. Cried at the rehearsal already.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sof.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Don't. We came up here to celebrate. Not cry.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Same time next year?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Same time. Always.

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a place you would climb or travel to with your best friend. Why this place? What would you say at the top? Or — describe a friendship tradition you have. Something you do every year, every month, every Sunday.
prompt_phrases: Look at this view.
Worth the climb.
I can't believe it.
Are you ready?
Same time next year?

phrase_cle.example: — You're getting married next month. — I know. I can't believe it.
phrase_cle.explanation: Универсальная эмоциональная реакция на что-то большое — хорошее или плохое. В контексте конца пути по уровню — мягкое признание чего-то, что трудно осмыслить полностью. Без 'I' тоже встречается: 'Can't believe it'. Подходит для финальных моментов, неожиданных новостей, важных переходов.

content.cultural_note: So basically, Sophie and Marie went to Edinburgh for the weekend. Just the two of them, before Marie's wedding. They climbed Arthur's Seat — a hill that overlooks the whole city, one of the most famous walks in Scotland. From the top, you can see Edinburgh laid out below, the sea in the distance. They stood there together. Marie said 'wow. Just wow.' Sophie asked if it was worth the climb. Marie said yes. And then the moment landed: 'Last weekend before the wedding.' Marie said 'I can't believe it. Honestly.' That word — honestly — does a lot of work. It signals real emotion, not the polite kind. Sophie admitted she'd already cried at the rehearsal. Marie said 'Sof' — which here means 'don't make me cry'. Sophie said 'don't' back — which means the same thing. They didn't cry. They made a promise instead: same time next year. Same time, always. This is what friendship sounds like at its best. Two friends, one hill, one promise. The whole A2 course has been building to this moment — and you've made it. End of A2.

key_vocabulary.note: Универсальная эмоциональная реакция на большое событие. Тёплая, тихая.
Конструкция 'оправдало затраченные усилия'. 'Worth the climb', 'worth the wait', 'worth the money'.
Усилитель эмоциональной правды. Сигналит: дальше реальное чувство.
Past simple. 'Rehearsal' = репетиция (свадьбы, спектакля). Британцы делают rehearsal перед свадьбой.
Question proposing tradition. Универсально для совместных встреч/поездок.
key_vocabulary.translation: В то же время в следующем году?

comprehension_questions.hint: On Arthur's Seat — a hill in Edinburgh. They climbed up for the view. The trip is just before Marie's wedding.
comprehension_questions.question: Where are Sophie and Marie?

comprehension_questions.hint: Emotional. She admits she cried at the rehearsal already. But she doesn't want to cry on this trip.
comprehension_questions.question: How does Sophie feel about the wedding?

comprehension_questions.hint: She's suggesting this becomes a tradition — coming back to Edinburgh together. Sophie agrees: same time, always.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Marie mean by 'Same time next year?'

content.title: The Manchester Offer

dialogue.line: So... I got that email today.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Which email? You've been getting about a hundred.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: The Manchester one. Greenfield Partners.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...And?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: They offered me the role. Senior associate. Starting in six weeks.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Wow. Okay. That's... that's huge, Soph. Congratulations.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Thanks. I think.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What do you mean, you think?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I mean — it's Manchester. I'd have to move. Properly move. Find a flat, leave this one, leave...
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Leave me. You can say it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I wasn't going to put it like that.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: But that's what it is, isn't it? You'd be three hours away. Minimum.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I know. That's why I haven't said yes yet. I wanted to talk to you first. Properly.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie, I... look. You should take it. If it's the right move, you should take it. I'm not going to be the friend who tells you to stay small.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm not asking you to tell me to stay. I'm asking what you think.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I think... I think I need a minute. Is that okay?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

content.situation: Sophie has just received a job offer from a Manchester firm — bigger role, better pay, but it means leaving London. She comes home and tells Marie over a late dinner. It's the first time they're not on the same page about something important.

expressions.meaning: softening device used when someone is uncertain or hesitant about what they're saying — buying time to find the right words
to phrase something in a particular way — often used to soften a harsh truth that has just been said
to hold yourself back, not take a bigger opportunity — emotional, slightly literary phrasing
I need a moment to process — used when overwhelmed, especially emotionally; not literal time
permission-asking phrase used at the end of a request — softens, shows respect for the other person's feelings

content.grammar_focus: Second conditional for hypothetical futures ("I'd have to move", "You'd be three hours away"). The whole conversation is built on conditionals because nothing is decided yet — they're testing the future by speaking it. Notice that the conditional softens the reality: "I'd have to move" is gentler than "I'm moving". Marie also uses it as protection ("You'd be three hours away") — she's not stating a fact, she's holding the possibility at arm's length.

lessons.title_ru: А ты сам что бы сделал?

content.title: What Would You Do?

dialogue.line: I haven't told anyone except Marie. Not even Mum yet.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Okay. So what's the problem?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: The problem is I don't know what I want.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's not a problem. That's where you start.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Easy for you to say.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. If money wasn't a factor — if Marie wasn't a factor — what would you do?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: That's not realistic.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Just answer the question.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: ...I'd take it. Probably. The role is what I've been saying I want for two years.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. So now we know what you want. The rest is logistics.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Marie isn't logistics, Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I didn't say she was. I said the rest is logistics. The job, the flat, the move. Marie is something else. But you can't make a decision about her until you know what you'd do without her in the picture.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: When you put it like that, it sounds cold.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's not cold. It's clear. There's a difference.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: How would you know? You and Anna have been figuring everything out together for years.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah. And that only works because we each know what we'd want on our own first. Otherwise you spend your whole life adjusting to someone else's decisions.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

content.situation: Day after the Manchester offer. Sophie meets Carlos for lunch at a café near her office. He's the friend you go to when you need clear thinking, not comfort. He listens, then makes her actually answer the question she's been avoiding.

expressions.meaning: you can say that, but you're not the one in this situation — gentle pushback, not aggressive
if [X] didn't matter — used to strip a decision down to its essentials and force honest thinking
everything else is just practical details, not the real question — separates the hard decision from the surrounding mechanics
involved in the situation; part of what you're considering — "without her in the picture" = if she weren't part of this
the way you've phrased it makes me see it differently — often used when the other person has reframed something uncomfortable
expressions.expression: When you put it like that

content.grammar_focus: Second conditional applied to hypothetical decision-making ("If money wasn't a factor, what would you do?", "what you'd do without her in the picture"). Notice Carlos uses it as a thinking tool — he forces Sophie to imagine a counterfactual world to discover her real preferences. This is one of the most useful adult applications of the structure: not as a grammar exercise but as a way to clear emotional fog. Also notice the contracted forms — "I'd", "you'd", "we'd" — which native speakers use almost exclusively in spoken English.

content.title: Telling Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, hi. Sorry, I know it's late.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's fine, love. I was up. What's wrong?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Nothing's wrong. I just — I've got some news. Greenfield Partners offered me the senior role. The Manchester one.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh, darling. That's wonderful. Isn't that what you've been working towards?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: It is. They told me they'd been impressed with the second interview. Said I was their first choice.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Well, of course you were. When do you start?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Six weeks. If I take it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: If? Sophie, what is there to think about?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Marie. The flat. London. My whole life is here, Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Your whole life is also ahead of you. Have you talked to Marie?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Yes. She said I should take it. Said she wouldn't be the friend who tells me to stay small.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And you believed her?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...What do you mean?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. Marie loves you. You don't share a flat with someone for four years and not feel something when they say they're moving away. She might have meant what she said. But she meant it through tears, didn't she?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: She didn't cry, Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's not what I asked.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...She said she needed a minute.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: There you are, then.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

content.situation: Evening. Sophie is alone in her flat, finally calling her mother to tell her about the offer. Mum is in her sixties, lives in a small town near Cambridge, and has the gift of asking the question Sophie was hoping to avoid. The conversation moves through reported speech because Sophie is summarising what's already been said by other people.

expressions.meaning: older relatives often default to this when called late at night — assumes problem unless proven otherwise; not panicked, just attentive
the goal you've been moving towards over time — present perfect continuous, used to acknowledge sustained effort
rhetorical question implying the answer is obvious — gentle pressure, not dismissive
expressions.expression: what is there to think about?

expressions.meaning: what someone says when emotional may be true, but the emotion underneath is also true — wisdom about how people speak
expressions.register: literary, but used in intimate speech

expressions.meaning: exactly — your last answer just confirmed what I was suggesting; quiet triumph, not gloating

content.grammar_focus: Reported speech in lived context ("They told me they'd been impressed", "Said I was their first choice", "She said I should take it", "She said she wouldn't be the friend..."). Notice that Sophie uses reported speech because she's summarising past conversations for someone who wasn't there — this is the actual reason the structure exists, not a textbook drill. Also notice the natural dropping of "that" ("She said I was" not "She said that I was") — this is closer to how native speakers actually speak. Backshift of tenses: "are" → "were", "will" → "would", "have been" → "had been".

content.title: Marie's Side

dialogue.line: So how are you, really? Not the version you tell people at work.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I'm... okay. I'm fine, mostly.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mostly.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I mean, I'm pleased for her. She deserves it. It's a brilliant role.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm-hm.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: It's just... I suppose I thought we'd have more warning. She's been doing those interviews for weeks, but I don't know, I think part of me assumed nothing would actually come of it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You assumed she'd stay.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I didn't think about it that directly. It was more like — Manchester felt theoretical. And then suddenly it wasn't.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Have you told her any of this?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Sort of. Not really. I told her she should take the job.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: What was I supposed to say? "Please don't go, I'll be lonely"? She'd have stayed and resented me forever.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Or she'd have known what you actually felt, and you'd have figured something out together. Which is what people do.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: It's not the same as you and Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It's exactly the same. The fact that you live with someone instead of dating them doesn't change the rule. You still have to say the difficult thing.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I'll think about it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That's what people say when they don't want to do it.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

content.situation: Two days after Sophie told her. Marie is having coffee with Anna — Anna is calm, pregnant, and one of the few people Marie can be honest with. Marie tries to stay reasonable but the hedging gives her away. The lesson is about how British English uses softening devices to say difficult things without admitting how much they hurt.

expressions.meaning: the polished, social-appropriate response — distinct from your real feelings; acknowledges that we curate ourselves
expressions.expression: the version you tell people at work

expressions.meaning: hedging cluster — softeners stacked together when someone is approaching a difficult truth slowly; classic B1+ British speech
expressions.expression: I suppose / I mean / sort of / not really

expressions.meaning: no real consequence would follow — "come of" as a phrasal verb meaning "result from"
expressions.expression: nothing would actually come of it

expressions.meaning: to work out a solution through conversation — implies negotiation between two people, not unilateral problem-solving
to express what's actually felt, even when it's uncomfortable — used when calling someone out on avoidance

content.grammar_focus: Hedging devices as core B1 vocabulary ("I suppose", "I mean", "sort of", "not really", "I think part of me"). Notice how Marie stacks them when she's not ready to say the truth — and how the stacking itself is the message. A native English speaker hearing "I'm fine, mostly" understands that the person is not fine. Also notice mixed conditionals: "She'd have stayed and resented me" (third conditional consequence) — used to argue against an alternative reality. This is how educated adult speakers reason about choices not made.

content.title: The Counter-Offer

dialogue.line: David, thanks for making time. I won't keep you long.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Of course. What's on your mind?
dialogue.speaker: David

dialogue.line: I wanted to be straightforward with you. I've been approached by another firm — Greenfield Partners, in Manchester. They've made me an offer.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Ah. Right. Senior associate?
dialogue.speaker: David

dialogue.line: Yes. How did you guess?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's the role I would have moved you into in eighteen months. You're not the first person they've tried to take from me.
dialogue.speaker: David

dialogue.line: I haven't said yes yet. I wanted to talk to you before I made any decision. I'd rather not move if there's a real path here.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I appreciate that. Genuinely. Most people in your position wouldn't bother having this conversation.
dialogue.speaker: David

dialogue.line: Could I ask — would there be any flexibility on the timeline? For the senior role here?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'd need to think about it. And I'd need to talk to the partners. I can't promise anything tonight.
dialogue.speaker: David

dialogue.line: I understand. I wasn't expecting an answer in the room. I just thought you should know what I'm weighing up.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: How long do you have?
dialogue.speaker: David

dialogue.line: I told them I'd respond by end of next week.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Then let's talk again on Wednesday. By then I'll have a clearer picture of what we can offer. Whatever happens, Sophie — this was the right way to handle it.
dialogue.speaker: David

dialogue.line: Thank you. That means a lot.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

content.situation: End of the day. Sophie has booked a fifteen-minute slot in David's calendar. He's a fair man, mid-fifties, used to people leaving. She's nervous because she's never had a conversation like this before — telling a current employer about another offer is a delicate professional move, and she has to balance honesty with strategy.

expressions.meaning: what do you want to talk about — open, neutral, used by managers to invite a conversation without pressure
to speak honestly and directly, especially when the topic is sensitive — signals respect for the listener
preference framed conditionally — softer than "I don't want to"; common in negotiation and diplomatic speech
what I'm considering carefully, balancing options — phrasal verb "weigh up" means to assess the relative merits
you behaved correctly in this situation — closing acknowledgement; preserves the relationship regardless of outcome
expressions.expression: this was the right way to handle it

content.grammar_focus: Modal verbs for diplomatic register, especially in professional negotiation: "I'd rather not", "Could I ask", "would there be any flexibility", "I'd need to think", "I can't promise". Notice that almost every important sentence in this dialogue is hedged with a modal — this is not weakness, it's professionalism. Direct statements ("I want", "I need") would sound demanding. Modals create a respectful distance that lets both sides negotiate without losing face. Also notice "I would have moved you into" — past conditional perfect, used by David to acknowledge a future that now might not happen.

content.title: The Decision

dialogue.line: I'm taking it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Okay.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I told them this morning.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You didn't say anything when you got home.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I needed to sit with it for a few hours. Make sure it still felt right after I'd actually done it. It does.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Then I'm pleased for you.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. Look at me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm looking at you.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I had a long talk with Mum. She said — she said you'd told me to take it through tears. Not literal tears. But she said you wouldn't have meant it the same way if it didn't cost you something.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Your mum is annoyingly perceptive.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah, she is. So I'm not going to pretend I don't know what this is. I'm moving to another city. We've lived together for four years. I know what I'm doing to us, and I'm doing it anyway, and I need you to know that I know.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's quite a sentence.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I rehearsed it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Sophie. I'm going to miss you so much I can hardly think about it. But I'd rather miss you than watch you not take this.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Six weeks.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Six weeks. So we'd better make them count.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

content.situation: A week has passed. Sophie has talked to Carlos, to Mum, to David. David's counter-offer came through, but it wasn't enough. Sophie has decided. Tonight she's telling Marie. Same kitchen, same late dinner as Lesson 1 — but everything has changed in between. The lesson closes Block 1 and uses the full range of what's been built so far: conditionals, reported speech, hedging, and one direct statement at the centre.

expressions.meaning: to spend time with a feeling or decision before responding — implies emotional patience, not avoidance
to require an emotional or personal sacrifice — "if it didn't cost you something" = if you weren't giving up anything by saying it
frustratingly accurate at reading people — affectionate complaint, common British phrasing
to be aware of the impact my actions have on our relationship — full responsibility statement
expressions.expression: to know what I'm doing to us

expressions.meaning: to use them well, to make them matter — said about limited time, opportunities, or chances

content.grammar_focus: Block 1 closes by combining everything built so far. Reported speech inside emotional disclosure ("She said you'd told me to take it through tears" — Sophie reporting Mum's report of Marie's words). Mixed conditional ("you wouldn't have meant it the same way if it didn't cost you something"). Future contractions ("we'd better make them count"). And critically, in the middle of all this hedged language, two direct statements: "I'm taking it" and "I rehearsed it". Notice how the directness lands harder because it's surrounded by softened speech. This is the actual rhythm of intimate adult English — not constant directness, not constant hedging, but the tactical alternation of both.

content.title: Looking for a Flat

dialogue.line: Right, this is the one I think you'll like. South-facing, second floor, recently refurbished. The previous tenant only just moved out.
dialogue.speaker: Daniel

dialogue.line: How long was she here?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: About two and a half years. She's been transferred to Bristol with work, otherwise she'd have stayed. Always a good sign when people don't want to leave.
dialogue.speaker: Daniel

dialogue.line: Mm. Could I see the kitchen?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Through here. It's compact, but it's been well thought out. Decent fridge, dishwasher, gas hob. And the boiler was replaced last winter.
dialogue.speaker: Daniel

dialogue.line: What's the area like? For someone who doesn't know the city.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Chorlton's lovely. Bit of a village feel — independent shops, good pubs, easy access into town. You've got the tram five minutes that way. I'd say it suits you.
dialogue.speaker: Daniel

dialogue.line: What makes you say that?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Just a guess. The first two flats you saw were in city centre — too noisy for someone who's used to actually being able to hear themselves think. You're more of a Chorlton person.
dialogue.speaker: Daniel

dialogue.line: I haven't lived alone in eight years. I don't know what kind of person I am yet.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Fair enough. Well, you've got time to find out. Six-month tenancy minimum, but that's standard.
dialogue.speaker: Daniel

dialogue.line: How quickly would I need to decide?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: There's interest, but nothing locked in. I'd give it two or three days realistically. If you want to think about it overnight and ring me tomorrow, that's fine.
dialogue.speaker: Daniel

dialogue.line: I think I want to take it. But I want to sleep on it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Best way. Ring me before midday tomorrow and I'll hold it for you.
dialogue.speaker: Daniel

content.situation: Sophie's first weekend in Manchester. She's seeing the third flat of the day — a one-bedroom in Chorlton, fifteen minutes from the office. Daniel is in his thirties, cheerful, professional. The conversation is transactional but warm. Sophie has to figure out what she actually wants in a flat when she doesn't know yet what her life here will look like.

expressions.meaning: the windows face south — important in the UK because it means sunlight; estate agents always mention it
designed sensibly, carefully — used about kitchens, layouts, anything where someone made good choices
an area that feels small and community-like, even within a big city — common British neighbourhood vocabulary
I accept what you're saying without arguing — common British neutral acknowledgement, neither agreement nor disagreement
to wait until tomorrow before deciding — implies the decision benefits from time, not avoidance

content.grammar_focus: Present perfect continuous and past simple in service contexts. Notice Daniel's mix: "the previous tenant only just moved out" (past simple, completed), "she's been transferred to Bristol" (present perfect, recent), "the boiler was replaced last winter" (past simple, specific time). Native speakers move between these without thinking — the choice depends on whether the time is specified and whether the action's relevance continues. Also notice the conditional in Daniel's polite suggestion: "I'd give it two or three days" — modal hedging in professional advice.

content.title: The First Week

dialogue.line: Hello, you. Show me the flat. Properly this time, not just the corner of it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Hold on, let me move. Right — kitchen. Living room, which is also where I've been working from this week. Bedroom through there, you can see the corner of the bed. That's the whole tour.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's nice, Soph. It really is. How's it been?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Honestly? A blur. I've been at the office until eight every night this week. Haven't really had time to feel anything yet.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's probably for the best.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Maybe. The work is good though. They've been throwing me into actual cases from day one, which I wasn't expecting.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I told you they wanted you for the work, not for paperwork.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah. There's a woman called Eleanor in my team. She's been there about three years, she's been showing me the ropes. She's quite serious. I can't tell yet if I like her.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Give it time. What about the rest of the building?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Friendly enough. I've been doing the awkward thing where you nod at people in lifts and don't know their names. Which I'd been doing for two years in London too, so nothing new there.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Have you eaten today?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...I had toast at lunch.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. You're going to live there for at least a year. You can't survive on toast for a year.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm going to a Tesco tomorrow. I haven't had time. Don't start.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm not starting. I'm asking. There's a difference.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah. Sorry. I'm tired.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. I'll let you go. But Soph — eat actual food this weekend.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I will. Promise.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

content.situation: Friday evening of Sophie's first week in Manchester. She's calling Marie on FaceTime from her new sofa. The flat is half-unpacked. Sophie is trying to sound cheerful and Marie can hear that she's trying. Present perfect continuous appears throughout because the week is recent and ongoing in feeling.

expressions.meaning: unclear in memory because it happened too fast — used about busy periods you can't remember in detail
to give them difficult work immediately, without easing them in — usually positive in a workplace context
to teach them how things work in a new place, especially at work — idiomatic phrasal expression
don't begin the lecture I know is coming — said affectionately when someone is about to give well-meaning advice
polite phrase to end a phone call without it sounding abrupt — implies care, not dismissal

content.grammar_focus: Present perfect continuous as the tense of the recent ongoing experience: "I've been at the office until eight every night", "They've been throwing me into actual cases", "she's been showing me the ropes", "I've been doing the awkward thing". Notice the contrast with simple past for finished actions: "I had toast at lunch". The continuous emphasises duration and ongoing relevance — Sophie is describing how the week has felt, not what specifically happened in it. Also notice past perfect continuous: "which I'd been doing for two years in London too" — used to compare a current habit to a previous one.

content.title: At the New Office

dialogue.line: Have you had a chance to look at clause 4.2?
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Yes. I think it's drafted too narrowly. The definition of 'commercial use' wouldn't cover the licensing scenario they described in the meeting.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. I noticed the same thing this morning. Did you flag it to anyone?
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Not yet. I wasn't sure if it was my place.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's absolutely your place. If you've spotted something, say something. People here would rather hear it from you than find it in three months when it's a problem.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Right. Noted.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Don't take that the wrong way. I'm not telling you off. I'm telling you the rule because no one else will.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I appreciate it, actually. Most places, you have to figure that out by getting it wrong.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh, you'll still get things wrong. Just not that thing.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Comforting.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I do what I can. Right. Other than 4.2 — anything else jumping out at you?
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: The indemnity in section 9 is unusual. They're capping liability at the value of the contract, but excluding consequential losses entirely. I'd want to push back on that.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Agreed. Draft a counter-proposal and I'll review it before we send. Use the template from the Hatfield matter — that one was structured similarly.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Will do. By tomorrow morning all right?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: End of day Friday is fine. You don't need to kill yourself for it.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: That's a refreshing thing to hear from a senior associate.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm trying to be the kind of senior I wish I'd had. Mixed results so far, but I'm trying.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

content.situation: Wednesday afternoon, week three. Eleanor and Sophie are reviewing a contract together in a small meeting room. Eleanor is methodical, slightly older than Sophie, married, lives in Didsbury. She has the precise, slightly dry energy of someone who's good at her job and doesn't need to advertise it. This is the lesson where workplace English meets personality.

expressions.meaning: to bring something to someone's attention, especially a potential problem — workplace standard
it's appropriate or expected for you to do this — used about responsibility and authority
to interpret a comment as more critical than intended — used to defuse possible offence in advance
expressions.expression: to take something the wrong way

expressions.meaning: to formally disagree or negotiate against a position — workplace English for diplomatic resistance
to overwork dramatically for something — figurative, casual

content.grammar_focus: Workplace register at B1: precise vocabulary, hedging modals, professional idioms. Notice how Eleanor uses "would rather" twice in different functions — "would rather hear it from you" (preference about reality), "I'd want to push back" (Sophie's hedged opinion). The conditional perfect appears at the end: "the kind of senior I wish I'd had" — Eleanor is describing a counterfactual past version of her own career. This is how educated speakers refer to formative experiences they didn't have. Also: contracted past forms in fast speech ("I'd want", "you'd have", "she'd been there") that learners often miss.

content.title: Missing Things

dialogue.line: Hi Mum, are you free for a chat?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Always for you, love. Where are you?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Walking in the park near my flat. It's actually nice today. Sun's out.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's been miserable here. Hasn't stopped raining since Wednesday.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Manchester gets a bad rep but it's not been too bad, actually.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: How's the flat? Settled in?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: More or less. Most of the boxes are unpacked. There's still one I haven't opened — books I think. I keep meaning to do it and then not doing it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: How's work?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Good. Genuinely good. The cases are interesting and the team is decent. I think I made the right call.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: But?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Why does there have to be a but?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because you've been speaking very steadily, like you're reading from a script. You only do that when you're holding something back.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Take your time.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I think I miss London more than I thought I would. Not London exactly. The flat. Marie. Knowing where everything is. The walk to the bus stop. Stupid things.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: They're not stupid things.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I had to ask three different people where the chemist was last week. I felt about ten years old.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie, you've been there four weeks. It's allowed to feel strange. It would be strange if it didn't feel strange.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I just thought I'd be better at it by now.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Better at moving cities entirely on your own at thirty? Darling, no one is good at that. Not really. They just get used to it.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: How long does that take?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Longer than four weeks. Less than you think. Be patient with yourself.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

content.situation: Sunday afternoon, week four. Sophie is on a walk in Chorlton Park and rings her mum. The conversation slowly moves from cheerful catch-up to something harder. Mum doesn't push — she just leaves the right amount of silence. Sophie ends up admitting things she hadn't planned to admit.

expressions.meaning: to have a bad reputation that may not be deserved — common about cities, foods, types of people
to repeatedly intend to do something but not actually do it — extremely common British construction for procrastination
expressions.expression: to keep meaning to do something

expressions.meaning: to make the correct decision — slightly idiomatic, casual register; "call" here means decision
to not say everything you're feeling — used about emotional self-restraint
give yourself time and grace — common reassurance phrase, often from older to younger speakers

content.grammar_focus: Tense layering for emotional disclosure. Notice the shift between present perfect ("It's been miserable here", "You've been there four weeks") and past simple ("I had to ask three different people"). Mum uses present perfect to describe Sophie's state across the four weeks — the ongoing, accumulated experience. Sophie uses past simple for one specific embarrassing moment (the chemist). This is how speakers separate "how things have been" from "what specifically happened". Also: "It would be strange if it didn't feel strange" — second conditional used for emotional logic, not just hypothetical events.

content.title: The Visit

dialogue.line: Okay. I owe you an apology, and I want to get it out of the way before I drink any more wine.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What for?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: When you told me about the offer. I was a bit useless about it. I told you to take the job because I thought that was the noble thing to say, but I didn't actually engage with what was happening.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie, that was months ago. You don't have to —
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Let me finish. I've been thinking about it for weeks and I'd rather just say it. I should have asked you what you wanted instead of telling you what you should do. You deserved a friend who actually listened, and I was performing.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Okay. Apology accepted. Sort of. Although for the record, I didn't entirely listen to you either. I'd already mostly decided. I was looking for permission, not advice.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: We were both being a bit useless then.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Apparently.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Can I ask you something honestly?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Are you happy here? Not 'is it going well'. Are you actually happy?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Sometimes. Some days. The work is the best it's ever been. The flat is starting to feel like mine. But I haven't made any real friends yet — there's a woman at work I might one day be friends with, but it's not there yet. So evenings can be quiet.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What do you do when it's quiet?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Read. Run. Watch things I shouldn't watch. Ring you sometimes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You've been ringing me less, actually.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I know. I'm trying not to use you as a comfort blanket. I have to actually live here, and ringing London every evening doesn't help.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I noticed. I appreciated it. And I missed it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Both can be true.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Both are true.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

content.situation: Saturday evening, six weeks since Sophie moved. Marie arrived this morning. They've spent the day walking around — Northern Quarter, Whitworth Gallery, lunch in Ancoats. Now they're back at Sophie's flat with takeaway and a bottle of wine, sat cross-legged on the sofa. They're both relieved that it still works between them. They're also both noticing how it's different now.

expressions.meaning: to deal with something difficult before relaxing, so it doesn't hang over you — common British construction
expressions.expression: to get something out of the way

expressions.meaning: to handle something poorly through emotional avoidance, not malice — affectionate self-criticism
expressions.expression: to be a bit useless about something

expressions.meaning: saying what sounds correct rather than what you feel — used for self-criticism about being inauthentic
expressions.expression: to be performing (instead of being honest)

expressions.meaning: to lean on someone emotionally to avoid building your own life — psychologically literate phrasing common in British speech
expressions.expression: to use someone as a comfort blanket

expressions.meaning: two seemingly contradictory things can both be real — emotional adult shorthand for accepting complexity

content.grammar_focus: The full conditional perfect range in real apology. Marie's apology uses "should have" + past participle ("I should have asked") — the standard form for regret about a past action. Sophie's reciprocal admission also uses it ("I should have... actually no, I'd already decided"). Notice the past perfect for layered timeline ("I'd already mostly decided"). Also notice the simple but powerful repetition: "Both can be true" / "Both are true" — moving from hypothetical possibility to acknowledged reality is a single grammatical move (modal to indicative) but a huge emotional one.

content.title: A Difficult Email

dialogue.line: Eleanor, have you got a minute? I need a second pair of eyes on something.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Of course. What is it?
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Email from Patel. About the deal that collapsed.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Right. Let me read it. Hmm. Yes. He's quite cross.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: He's not just cross, he's accusing us of negligence. Which we weren't. The deal collapsed because the other side withdrew, not because of anything we did.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. The question is how we respond. You can't ignore it, but you also can't match his tone.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: What's the rule here? I haven't had to write one of these before.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Three things. One: acknowledge his frustration without conceding the substance. Two: state our position clearly, with reference to facts. Three: leave a door open for further discussion.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Don't apologise for what we didn't do.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Exactly. Apologising sounds like accepting blame. But ignoring his feelings entirely will make him angrier. So you might start with something like: 'I appreciate this has been a frustrating outcome.' That's true, and it's not a concession.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: And then what — 'However, I would like to clarify the sequence of events'?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Better: 'It may be helpful to set out the timeline as we understood it.' Less defensive. More inviting.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Right. That's better.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Then list the facts, briefly. No adjectives. Just: this happened on Tuesday, the other side notified us on Thursday, we informed him on Friday. Let the facts do the work.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: And the closing?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Something like: 'I would welcome the opportunity to discuss this further if it would be helpful.' Polite. Open. But also implies the email itself wasn't quite the right venue.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: It's like learning a different language.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is a different language. The point isn't to win the argument in writing. The point is to make it easy for both of you to behave like adults next time you speak.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

content.situation: Tuesday morning, week eight. Sophie has just received an angry email from a client whose deal fell through last week — the client is blaming her firm, and the email crosses the line from frustrated to accusatory. Sophie shows it to Eleanor. Together they draft a response. The lesson is about how formal written register sounds inside a live conversation — and how to push back firmly without escalating.

expressions.meaning: another person to review something — common workplace phrase for asking colleagues to check your work
to be angry — distinctly British register, slightly understated; "cross" is gentler than "angry"
without admitting fault on the actual issue — professional negotiation vocabulary
to present information neutrally and trust it to be persuasive — written-register principle
expressions.expression: to let the facts do the work

expressions.meaning: polite professional opening for offering something while remaining diplomatic — formal email register
expressions.expression: I would welcome the opportunity to [verb]

content.grammar_focus: Two registers in one scene. The spoken English between Sophie and Eleanor is casual-professional ("have you got a minute", "he's quite cross"). The drafted email phrases are formal written register ("I appreciate this has been a frustrating outcome", "It may be helpful to set out the timeline as we understood it", "I would welcome the opportunity to discuss this further"). Notice the modal verbs in formal writing: "may", "would", "could" — these create the diplomatic tone. Also notice the use of conditional perfect for hypothetical formality ("as we understood it" — a deflection from absolute claim). This contrast shows learners that the same person uses very different English depending on medium and audience.

content.title: Tom and Marie

dialogue.line: Right. I want to ask you something, and I've been rehearsing it all week, so just let me say it.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Oh god. What?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Don't 'oh god' me before I've even said it. It's not a marriage proposal. Calm down.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Sorry. Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I've been thinking. Sophie's been gone two months. You're paying full rent on a two-bed flat for one person. I'm in a one-bed I don't really like. And we spend most weekends together anyway.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...Are you asking me to move in with you?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm asking if you'd consider it. There's a difference. You don't have to answer now. I just wanted to put it on the table.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I don't know what to say.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That's all right. What's coming up?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I'm not sure. It's not a no. But I don't think it's a yes either, and I don't want to say yes because I'm flattered or because the timing makes financial sense.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Fair.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Can I think about it for a bit?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Take however long you need. Just — tell me when you've decided. Don't leave me wondering for months.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I won't. That's a promise.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: And if it is a no, I'd rather hear it sooner than later. So I can stop hoping.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Okay. That's fair too.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

content.situation: Late October. Tom and Marie have been dating since they met in A2. They're at his place after dinner. He's been thinking about asking her to move in for weeks. He finally does. Marie isn't unhappy about the question — she's just genuinely uncertain, which is harder to talk about than yes or no.

expressions.meaning: to formally introduce a proposal for discussion — borrowed from negotiation, used in personal contexts too
expressions.expression: to put something on the table

expressions.meaning: what are you thinking or feeling — gentle probing question used to invite someone to open up
to feel pleased and slightly proud someone wanted you, separate from whether you actually want the thing they're offering
to be the rational decision based on money — used as a reason to do something, but here Marie is rejecting it as the right reason
to keep them uncertain about your answer for a long time — usually unkind

content.grammar_focus: Indirect requests and conditional preferences in negotiation. Tom's distinction is grammatically precise: "Are you asking me to move in?" → "I'm asking if you'd consider it. There's a difference." The shift from direct request to hypothetical inquiry creates emotional safety. Marie's rejection-but-not-rejection is built entirely from negative conditionals: "It's not a no, but I don't think it's a yes", "I don't want to say yes because". Tom's "if it is a no, I'd rather hear it sooner than later" is a first conditional with embedded preference — common adult relationship grammar.

content.title: The Fight

dialogue.line: Are you ever going to make up your mind, or are we doing this dance forever?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Excuse me?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I asked you three weeks ago. You said you'd think about it. I haven't asked again. I'm not pushing. But you've been weird about it since, and I'd rather just know.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I haven't been weird. I've been thinking. Which is what you asked me to do.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You've been deflecting. There's a difference.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Don't tell me what I've been doing. You're not in my head.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm in this relationship. That gives me some right to notice.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Okay, then notice this — I don't appreciate being interrogated about it on a Tuesday night when I've had a long day and I'm tired.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Right. Fine.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Don't 'fine' me.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What do you want me to say, Marie?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...Hold on. Let's stop.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Stop what?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Stop this. I don't want to keep going. I'm about to say something I'll regret tomorrow, and you are too. Can we — pause?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Yeah. Okay. Pause.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I'm sorry I snapped at you. I have been deflecting. You were right about that. I just didn't want you to be right at that exact moment.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: And I'm sorry I led with 'doing this dance forever'. That wasn't fair. I was annoyed before I came home and I took it out on you.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Can we talk about it properly tomorrow? Not now?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah. Tomorrow.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

content.situation: Three weeks after Lesson 13. Marie is still thinking about Tom's question. Tonight at his flat, something small turns into something bigger — a misplaced comment about her not having decided yet. They both say things they'll regret. Then they catch themselves. The lesson is about how adults repair, not how they avoid arguments.

expressions.meaning: to decide — basic phrasal verb but central to B1; often appears in conditional or future contexts
to circle around an issue without resolving it — figurative, slightly accusatory
to redirect attention away from something difficult — common psychological-literate vocabulary
construction for rejecting someone's tone or response — "Don't 'fine' me", "Don't sigh at me" — shows the listener heard the subtext
to express frustration on the wrong target — common apology vocabulary in adult relationships
expressions.expression: to take something out on someone

content.grammar_focus: Repair language and meta-conversation. Notice that the actual fight uses present continuous and present perfect ("You've been weird", "I'm not pushing", "I'm about to say") — these tenses create immediacy and accusation. The repair language switches to past simple and conditional ("I'm sorry I snapped", "That wasn't fair", "I took it out on you") — past simple closes the action, accepting responsibility for completed behaviour. Also notice "can we — pause?" with the dash showing hesitation: real-time rephrasing as someone catches themselves mid-sentence. This rhythm is hard to teach from a textbook because it's about what doesn't get said.

content.title: Anna's News

dialogue.line: Carlos. Sit down a minute.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I'm making coffee. Can it wait?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Sit down. The coffee can wait.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: ...Are you ill?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: No. The opposite. I'm pregnant.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: ...Oh. Oh.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: How long have you known?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Since Friday. I wanted to be sure before I said anything.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: How sure are we talking?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Three tests. Doctor's appointment yesterday. Twelve weeks.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Twelve. So... we'd been —
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Around the wedding. Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Anna.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I know. I keep almost laughing about it. We were so careful about everything else.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Is the baby okay?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: From what they can tell, yes. Heartbeat's strong. They want me back in four weeks for the next scan.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Are you okay?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I think so. I've been sick about three times this week. Mostly mornings. I thought I had a stomach bug and then I worked it out.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I should have noticed.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: You couldn't have. I didn't even tell you I was throwing up, because I didn't want to worry you over a stomach bug.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Anna. Come here.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Are you happy?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I'm — yes. I think so. I'm a lot of things at once. But happy is one of them. Definitely.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

content.situation: Anna and Carlos's flat. Sunday morning, mid-November. Anna has known for two days. She's twelve weeks along. She's been waiting for the right moment but realised there isn't one — there's just deciding to say it. Carlos is making coffee. The lesson is about how big news lands quietly when the people are ready for it.

expressions.meaning: what level of certainty are you describing — common British construction for asking about degrees
to figure out the truth of a situation — phrasal verb meaning to deduce, distinct from "to figure something out" (solve)
to feel multiple, sometimes contradictory emotions simultaneously — emotionally honest adult phrasing
expressions.expression: to be a lot of things at once

expressions.meaning: based on the information available so far — used about preliminary medical, technical, or factual reports
I regret not paying enough attention — modal of regret, very common in close relationships

content.grammar_focus: Modal perfect for regret, possibility, and reconstruction of past events. Carlos uses three different ones in close succession: "I should have noticed" (regret), "You couldn't have" (impossibility — Anna's reassurance), "we'd been —" (past perfect continuous, calculating backwards). This is how speakers reason about events whose meaning has just changed. Also notice the broken sentences and self-corrections: Carlos can't finish "so we'd been —" because he's calculating in his head. Anna finishes it for him: "Around the wedding. Yeah." This is intimate dialogue — sentences shared between two minds, not performed monologues.

content.title: Mum's Doctor

dialogue.line: Mum, hello. I was just about to ring you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Were you? Telepathy. Listen, I'm ringing because I went to the doctor today and I want to tell you about it before you hear it from anyone else.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Hear what from anyone? What's going on?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Nothing dramatic. Don't panic. I've been having a bit of fatigue and some odd dizziness, on and off, for a few weeks. The GP wants me to see a specialist.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: What kind of specialist?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: A haematologist. Just for blood tests. They want to rule a few things out.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum. What kind of things?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. The doctor said specifically not to start googling. So please don't.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I'm going to google. You know I'm going to google.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. Google sensibly then. The point is — at this stage, we don't know anything. The appointment is in three weeks. They wouldn't have given me three weeks if it was urgent. Try to hold on to that.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: When do you find out?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: About a week after the appointment, probably. So mid-December.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Do you want me to come down for the appointment?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie, no. It's blood tests. I'm not doing surgery. Pat next door is going to drive me. It's not a big thing.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, I want to. It's not about whether you need me, it's about whether I want to be there.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Then come down for Christmas a few days early. We'll have the news by then either way. I'd rather have you here when there's actually something to talk about.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Okay. Deal.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Yeah?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Don't tell Marie. Don't tell anyone. There's nothing to tell yet, and I'd rather not have everyone fussing about something that might be nothing.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...Okay.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

content.situation: Late November, Wednesday evening. Mum rings Sophie. She's been to the GP about some recent symptoms — nothing dramatic, but a referral to a specialist for blood tests. The conversation is about how older parents share medical news with adult children: practically, but with a quiet awareness that something has shifted.

expressions.meaning: intermittent, not continuous — used about symptoms, weather, behaviour patterns
to medically or systematically eliminate a possibility — standard healthcare vocabulary
to keep believing or remembering something for emotional support — figurative, common in difficult conversations
it's a small matter, not worth worrying about — minimising phrase, often used by older speakers downplaying their own situation
to attract excessive worried attention, especially from family — common British phrasing, slightly affectionate-irritated

content.grammar_focus: Modal verbs of medical likelihood and reassurance. Mum uses negative modals as comfort: "They wouldn't have given me three weeks if it was urgent" (reasoning from absence of action). "They want me to see a specialist" — "want" + object + infinitive, common reporting structure. Notice how Mum keeps using understated language — "a bit of fatigue", "some odd dizziness", "a few things". This is British medical understatement: the more concerning something is, the more euphemistic the language. Also notice the use of "would rather" twice for preference: "I'd rather not have everyone fussing", "I'd rather have you here".

content.title: A Family Lunch

dialogue.line: Tom, more potatoes? You haven't had nearly enough.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I'm honestly fine, thank you. Three was already pushing it.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: You're a guest, you have to take more than three.
dialogue.speaker: Mum
