dialogue.line: She's serious, Tom. I learned this the hard way.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: All right, two more. But I'm protesting.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Marie, how's the new project going?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Slowly. The client keeps changing what they want. Last week they decided the entire brand needed to feel 'more honest'. Whatever that means.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It means they don't know what they want.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Exactly.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Sophie, you're not eating. Are you all right?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I'm fine. Just thinking about something at work.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph, it's Sunday. Stop thinking about work.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Easier said than done.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: How's the team?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Good. Eleanor — the woman I told you about — she invited me round for dinner next week. So that's progress.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Wait, dinner? At her house?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's a different level of friend, Soph. That's not a 'we go for drinks after work' friend. That's a 'come and meet my husband and the dog' friend.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Yeah, I suppose it is.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Trust Marie to identify the precise category.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I have a system.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Right. Anyone for trifle?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Yes please.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Yes please.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Mum. Can I help in the kitchen for a minute?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Of course, love. Tom, Marie — back in two minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

content.situation: Sunday lunch at Mum's house, early December. Sophie has come down from Manchester for the weekend. Mum's appointment is on Tuesday. Marie has come along — partly to support Sophie, partly because Tom drove her down. The four of them are eating roast chicken. Sophie hasn't told anyone about Mum's news. The lesson is about group dialogue: short overlapping turns, multiple speakers, real conversational rhythm.

expressions.meaning: three was already too many — "pushing it" means going beyond a limit politely
yes in theory, but not in practice — universal acknowledgement phrase
to invite someone to your home — "round" is British, distinct from American "over"
a more advanced or significant version of something — used to upgrade categorisation
I'm not surprised that [name] did this — affectionate observation about someone's predictable trait

content.grammar_focus: Group dialogue rhythm at B1. Notice that turns are shorter and more overlapping than in two-person scenes. Speakers interrupt each other, agree with each other, and bounce topics around. Tense use is mostly present continuous and present simple — group conversation is almost always about now. The exception is past simple for anecdotes ("Last week they decided"). Notice the use of "I suppose" and other hedges as the rhythm of polite participation rather than actual uncertainty. Also: idiom density increases in group talk, because speakers are showing personality through phrasing, not just exchanging information.

content.title: The Promotion

dialogue.line: Partner. I still can't quite believe they did it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Neither can I, really. I keep waiting for someone to come back and say it was a mistake.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: It wasn't a mistake. Everyone knew it was coming.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I didn't. I genuinely didn't. I'd convinced myself I was a year off.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: That's because you don't tell yourself flattering things. Most people do.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Hmm. That's a very generous reading of what is probably just impostor syndrome.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Both can be true.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Both can be true. I'm going to start using that. It's a useful sentence.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: It is, isn't it? A friend taught me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Marie?
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You talk about her quite a lot, you know.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: ...Do I?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's not a criticism. It's just an observation.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: We lived together for four years. We're close.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: ...What does 'yes' mean?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Nothing. It means I heard you.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. I'm not asking anything. I'm just enjoying my drink and watching you not entirely answer a question I didn't actually ask.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: ...Okay. That's fair. I think I'm going to need another glass of wine.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'll get it. My round anyway. I'm a partner now. I can afford it.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: You absolutely can.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

content.situation: Friday evening, second week of December. Eleanor was promoted to partner today — the youngest in the firm's history. She insisted on going for drinks just with Sophie, not the whole team. They're at a pub in the Northern Quarter, two glasses of wine in. The conversation moves from celebratory to personal. Something between them shifts that wasn't there before.

expressions.meaning: I had persuaded myself, often wrongly — past perfect for self-deception that's now visible
a kind interpretation of something that could be seen less favourably — common British self-deprecation
the feeling that you don't deserve your success and will be found out — modern psychology vocabulary
diplomatic phrase used to flag something without making it confrontational — common in adult conversation
expressions.expression: It's not a criticism, it's an observation

expressions.meaning: my turn to buy the drinks — British pub culture vocabulary, important for social register

content.grammar_focus: Subtext through what isn't said. The grammar is mostly simple — present tense, casual contractions — but the meaning is built through what's withheld. Eleanor's "yes" as a complete sentence is grammatically minimal but pragmatically loaded. Notice the questions that don't get answered directly: "Do I?", "What does 'yes' mean?". Also notice past perfect for self-revelation: "I'd convinced myself I was a year off". This is the tense of someone realising they've been wrong about their own situation. Also: "watching you not entirely answer a question" — gerund phrase used to describe behaviour with affectionate precision.

content.title: The Difficult Client

dialogue.line: Right. I'm here. What did you want to say in person that you couldn't put in writing?
dialogue.speaker: Mr Patel

dialogue.line: Mr Patel, thank you for coming in. I wanted to have this conversation properly because I think email was making it harder, not easier.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on then.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Patel

dialogue.line: I want to address what happened with the deal directly. I've reviewed everything from our side, and I want to walk you through it. Not to defend us — but so we're working from the same facts.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know what happened. I lost a deal.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Patel

dialogue.line: Yes. That's the outcome. I'd like to talk about why, because I don't think it's been fully explained. May I?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Patel

dialogue.line: The other side withdrew on the Thursday. We were notified at 4pm. We rang you at 4.20pm and left a message. We rang again at 9am Friday. You returned the call at 11. That's the timeline.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You should have rung me sooner.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Patel

dialogue.line: We rang you within twenty minutes of being notified ourselves. There was no faster path.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm not saying this to win an argument. I'm saying it because I think you've been carrying around the idea that we sat on the news, and I want you to know that we didn't.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Hmm. I had assumed it was longer than that.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Patel

dialogue.line: I understand. When something bad happens, it's natural to look for where the delay was. In this case, the delay was on the other side, not ours.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Why didn't anyone tell me this before?
dialogue.speaker: Mr Patel

dialogue.line: We did, in writing. But I think it didn't land in writing the way it might have in person. That's why I asked you in.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Well. I appreciate you doing this, Sophie. I'm still annoyed about losing the deal. But I'm less annoyed at you specifically.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Patel

dialogue.line: That's a reasonable place to land.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

content.situation: Mid-January. Patel — the angry client from Lesson 12 — has come into the office for a meeting Sophie scheduled with him. He's still annoyed. Sophie has prepared. The lesson is about how to hold your ground professionally with someone who's trying to intimidate you, without becoming aggressive yourself.

expressions.meaning: to explain step by step, in detail — common professional vocabulary
to share an agreed factual basis before arguing further — diplomatic precondition
expressions.expression: to work from the same facts

expressions.meaning: to delay sharing news, especially negative news — used as accusation
the message didn't have the intended effect — "land" used about emotional or rhetorical impact
an acceptable ending point for a difficult conversation — implies neither party got everything they wanted

content.grammar_focus: Professional assertiveness through structured language. Notice Sophie's pattern: declarative sentences with no hedging when stating facts ("We rang you within twenty minutes"), but careful modals when interpreting ("I think you've been carrying around the idea"). This is the structural backbone of holding your ground. Past simple for the timeline ("The other side withdrew on the Thursday, we were notified at 4pm") creates an unarguable factual record. Compare to Patel's "You should have rung me sooner" — modal of regret used as accusation, which Sophie deflects with another factual statement, not another modal.

content.title: Saying No

dialogue.line: Sophie, have you got a minute? Bryant just asked me — he wants you in tomorrow and Sunday for the Halberd matter. Just a heads up.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: ...Tomorrow and Sunday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah. They're behind on the disclosure review and he wants someone to power through it.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor, can I be honest with you?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Always.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I'm going to say no. Or at least — I'm not going to say yes automatically.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Okay. Tell me why, and let's see how to handle it.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I've worked the last three weekends. The filing on Bridges took both Saturdays in December. I haven't seen friends, I haven't been home to my mum, and I'm starting to make small mistakes because I'm tired. If I work this weekend, I'll come back Monday worse, not better.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: All of that is true.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Is there a way to say no without it being a mark against me?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. But how you frame it matters. Don't say 'I'm tired' — that's true but it doesn't help him. Say: 'I've worked the last three weekends and I want to make sure I'm at full capacity for the Greenfield filing on Wednesday. I can either be on Halberd this weekend or sharp on Wednesday — not both.'
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: ...That's good. Make it about the work, not me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Exactly. He's trying to optimise the firm's output. Give him an answer in those terms and he'll respect it. Not happily. But he'll respect it.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Okay. I'm going to ring him.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. One more thing. Don't apologise. The minute you apologise, you're admitting you're doing something wrong. You're not. You're managing your workload like an adult.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Got it. Thank you, Eleanor. Genuinely.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's what I'm here for. Now go ring him before you talk yourself out of it.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

content.situation: Friday afternoon, end of January. Sophie has been working long days on a major filing. Eleanor stops by her desk and casually mentions that one of the senior partners wants Sophie to come in over the weekend to help with a different matter. It's framed as a request, but it's the kind of request you don't really say no to. Sophie is going to say no anyway. The lesson is about how to refuse upward in a workplace without burning the relationship.

expressions.meaning: advance warning about something coming — workplace standard
to complete demanding work by sustained effort — usually implies more energy than time
to return from extra work in worse shape than before — argument against overwork
expressions.expression: to come back worse, not better

expressions.meaning: a negative note in someone's professional reputation — common workplace concern phrasing
to convince yourself not to do something you should do — common reflexive phrasing
expressions.expression: to talk yourself out of something

content.grammar_focus: First conditional for stating consequences: "If I work this weekend, I'll come back Monday worse, not better." This is the structure for arguing your case at work — present + future. Notice also Eleanor's diplomatic conditional script: "I can either be on Halberd this weekend or sharp on Wednesday — not both." The structure "either X or Y, not both" forces a choice without sounding combative. Also the imperative for advice: "Don't say...", "Don't apologise" — direct instructions softened only by context, not by hedging. This is how senior colleagues mentor without being soft.

content.title: Mum Falls

dialogue.line: Soph, hi — give me one second, I'm just at the door, going inside now. Right. What's going on?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mum fell. She's at A&E. She's okay — broken wrist, that's all. Pat next door found her. She'd been outside for about half an hour before he saw her.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh my god. Half an hour in this weather?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah. She's mildly hypothermic. They're warming her up. The wrist is going in a cast tomorrow.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. Are you breathing? Sit down somewhere.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm fine. I am. I just — I need to talk to someone for two minutes who isn't a doctor or my mum being brave.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. Of course. Talk.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: She kept apologising on the phone. Saying she was sorry for being a bother. She slipped on ice in her own garden, Marie. There's nothing to apologise for. But she does that — she defaults to sorry when she's frightened.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. Some women of that generation do. Shouldn't, but do.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: And the blood tests came back inconclusive. They want to do another round. So that's been hanging over the last six weeks anyway. And now this.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Sophie. You haven't told me about the blood tests.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: She asked me not to. But — I think this morning has changed what's reasonable. I'm telling you now.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Are you going down to her?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Tonight. I've already booked the train. I'll work from her place this week. I rang Eleanor on the way home and she was — completely fine about it. She actually said 'go now, don't wait.'
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Right. Listen to me. Whatever else you do today, you eat something and you put on a coat. I've heard you not eating in your voice. Don't argue.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes, Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Don't be cheeky. I'm being serious. And ring me when you get to her.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I will. Marie — thank you for being there. You don't always have to fix things. Sometimes I just need someone to be there.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know, love. I'm here.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

content.situation: Sunday morning, late January. Sophie's mum has had a fall — she slipped on ice in her garden and the neighbour found her. She's at A&E with a broken wrist, but otherwise fine. Sophie just got off the phone with the hospital and rings Marie straight away. The blood test results from December came back unclear, so this fall lands on top of an already worried mind. Marie is in London, can't be there in person.

expressions.meaning: to be a source of inconvenience to others — older British register, common in self-deprecating apology
to fall back on as automatic response — psychological-literate phrasing
to be present as ongoing worry — figurative, used about unresolved problems
to shift the rules of what's acceptable behaviour given new circumstances — adult ethical phrasing
don't be playfully impudent — affectionate British rebuke, especially mother-to-child or close-friend register

content.grammar_focus: Crisis phone call register. Notice how the grammar simplifies under stress — short sentences, present continuous ("I'm just at the door, going inside now"), imperatives ("sit down", "don't argue"). Past perfect for prior context: "She'd been outside for about half an hour". Past simple for sequential events. This is real-time emergency English — speakers prioritise clarity over elegance. Notice also "I've heard you not eating in your voice" — gerund construction, advanced but commonly used by people close enough to read each other's bodies through speech.

content.title: The Wedding Stress

dialogue.line: Has Diego replied?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Not yet.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos. The deadline was two weeks ago.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I know. He's been busy. I'll chase him.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: He's been busy for two weeks?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Apparently.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos, the caterer needs final numbers by Friday. The seating plan is built around guesses for half your family. I've been making this up.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Anna, I said I'd chase him. What more do you want me to do?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I want you to have chased him already. I want this not to be the third thing this week I've had to remind you about.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Right. Okay. So this isn't about Diego.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: ...No. I don't suppose it is.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Tell me what it's actually about.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: It's about the fact that I am five months pregnant, planning a wedding for ninety people, working four days a week, and somehow also project-managing your family's RSVPs. And I'm tired, Carlos. I'm so tired I cried in the bathroom at work yesterday because someone moved my mug.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Anna —
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I don't want to be the woman who keeps a list. I'm becoming the woman who keeps a list. And I hate it.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I haven't been pulling my weight on the wedding. You're right. I've been treating it like your project that I'm helping with. Which is rubbish, because it's our wedding.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: ...Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Give me the list. Properly. Everything you've been carrying. I'll take half of it tonight and the other half tomorrow. And I'll ring Diego now while you watch.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: You don't have to do it while I watch.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I want to do it while you watch. Otherwise you'll spend the next three days wondering if I actually rang him. Hand me my phone.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

content.situation: Mid-February. Anna is now five months pregnant and visibly so. The wedding is four weeks away. They're at home, surrounded by paperwork — RSVPs, venue layouts, seating charts. The argument that's about to happen is theoretically about Carlos's cousin who hasn't responded to the invitation. It's actually about everything else.

expressions.meaning: to follow up persistently, especially for a response or payment — common workplace and life vocabulary
to invent the answer in the absence of real information — used about improvising under pressure
expressions.expression: to make something up (as you go)

expressions.meaning: to do your fair share of the work — used about partnerships, teams, marriages
to track resentments or unfinished tasks — used here as a metaphor for emotional bookkeeping
to bear emotional weight or responsibility — figurative, common in adult conversation about burdens

content.grammar_focus: Argument that becomes resolution. Notice the present perfect dominant in Anna's complaint: "He's been busy for two weeks", "I've been making this up", "I've been carrying". This tense emphasises duration of frustration. Carlos's apology shifts to present perfect continuous ("I haven't been pulling my weight") and then future commitment ("I'll take half tonight"). The grammatical move from accumulated frustration → acknowledged behaviour → forward action is the actual structure of resolution. Notice also Carlos's first conditional: "Otherwise you'll spend the next three days wondering" — naming the consequence to justify the action.

content.title: The Old Friend

dialogue.line: Sophie. You've not aged a day. How is that fair.
dialogue.speaker: Jess

dialogue.line: You're flattering me. I've definitely aged. I've just had better lighting in this pub.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Three years. I cannot believe it's been three years.
dialogue.speaker: Jess

dialogue.line: How was Lisbon?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh, it was the best decision I ever made. And the hardest. Mostly the best though. I'd do it again in a heartbeat.
dialogue.speaker: Jess

dialogue.line: Are you still — at the same firm?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: No, I left in November. I'm freelance now. Which is terrifying and amazing in roughly equal measure.
dialogue.speaker: Jess

dialogue.line: You sound happy though.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am. Properly happy, in a way I hadn't been for years and didn't know I wasn't. You know that feeling — when something lifts and you realise it had been there the whole time?
dialogue.speaker: Jess

dialogue.line: I do, yeah. I had a version of that when I moved up here, actually. Not as dramatic as Lisbon — but I hadn't realised how much I'd been performing in London.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Performing — that's a good word for it.
dialogue.speaker: Jess

dialogue.line: It's Marie's word, actually. She accused me of it once and I borrowed it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: How is Marie?
dialogue.speaker: Jess

dialogue.line: Good. She and Tom — you remember Tom — they're talking about moving in together. Which is huge for her.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Wow. Marie. Moving in with someone.
dialogue.speaker: Jess

dialogue.line: I know. I was the same when she told me. But she seems — not certain, but ready.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: There's a difference between certain and ready, isn't there.
dialogue.speaker: Jess

dialogue.line: There's a huge difference. I think I confused them for years.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Are we getting deep already? We're three sips in.
dialogue.speaker: Jess

dialogue.line: Apparently. I missed this.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Me too. Properly missed it. Even when I was glad I left, I missed having someone who'd known me for ten years.
dialogue.speaker: Jess

dialogue.line: Eleven now.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Eleven. God.
dialogue.speaker: Jess

content.situation: End of February. Jess — Sophie's friend from her first job in London — is in Manchester for two days for a conference. They haven't seen each other in over three years. Drinks at Jess's hotel bar. Slow conversation. The lesson is about how reunions actually go: half catching up, half sensing how much each person has changed.

expressions.meaning: without hesitation, immediately — emphatic phrase about how quickly you would do something again
in approximately balanced amounts — used to acknowledge two contrasting feelings at once
when an unidentified weight or sadness goes — figurative, used about emotional clarity
to mistake one quality for another — used about emotional misjudgements over time
casual phrase for early in the drink/conversation — implies surprise that the conversation has gone deep so fast

content.grammar_focus: Past perfect for re-evaluating one's own past. Sophie: "I hadn't realised how much I'd been performing". Jess: "in a way I hadn't been for years and didn't know I wasn't". This double layered past is how speakers describe insight that arrived after the fact. Notice the contracted forms throughout ("I'd been", "I'd do", "I hadn't", "didn't know I wasn't"). Native speakers use these constantly in self-reflection. Also: tag questions for invited agreement ("There's a difference between certain and ready, isn't there") — a B1 social grammar marker that softens insight into shared observation.

content.title: The Honest Conversation

dialogue.line: Eleanor. I want to say something, and I don't know how to say it gracefully, so I'm just going to say it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Okay.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Last week, on the walk back to your car. Something happened between us, or almost happened. I'm not imagining it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You're not imagining it.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I've been thinking about it all week. And the thing is — I'm not in a place where I could do anything with that. With you specifically, or with anyone else. It's not about you. It's that I have only just started to feel like I live here, and I don't have the bandwidth to start something new and complicated on top of that.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. Stop.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Sorry. I prepared too much of this in the bath.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I can tell. Listen. I appreciate you being honest with me, and I want to say something back honestly. I wasn't going to push anything. I wouldn't have. I'm not — that's not how I do this.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I know. I'm not accusing you of anything. I just didn't want it to sit between us getting bigger by not being said.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's mature of you, actually. Most people would have let it sit and then it would have got weird.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: It was already getting weird. For me, anyway.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Are we okay?
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I think we're more okay than we'd be if I hadn't said anything.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Then can I be slightly selfish for a moment?
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'd really like to keep being your friend. Even if there's a small awkward edge to it for a while. I don't have a lot of friends I trust. And I trust you.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: ...Yeah. Same. Friends. Genuine ones.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Now drink your wine. We're going to watch something rubbish on television and pretend this conversation never got this serious.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Best plan I've heard all week.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

content.situation: Early March. Eleanor invited Sophie for dinner two weeks ago. After dinner, on the walk back to Eleanor's car, the conversation became briefly something else. Eleanor didn't say anything explicit, but Sophie felt it. Now it's a week later, late on a Friday, and Eleanor has come by Sophie's flat with a bottle of wine — not for anything in particular, she said. Sophie has decided to be honest before the situation becomes something neither of them can talk about.

expressions.meaning: to phrase a difficult thing well — usually used in the negative, as confession of struggling
to have the emotional or mental capacity to handle something more — borrowed from technology, now common
to remain unaddressed and grow more difficult — used about unresolved tensions in relationships
I rehearsed this in advance and it shows — self-deprecating British acknowledgement of over-preparation
expressions.expression: I prepared too much of this in the bath

expressions.meaning: a slight residual discomfort — used about post-difficult-conversation atmosphere

content.grammar_focus: Mixed conditionals for emotional honesty. "I wouldn't have. I'm not — that's not how I do this" — third conditional implied ("I wouldn't have pushed anything"). "We're more okay than we'd be if I hadn't said anything" — mixed conditional comparing actual present to hypothetical alternative present. "Most people would have let it sit and then it would have got weird" — third conditional describing the alternate path. These are the actual structures adults use to think out loud about emotional choices. Also notice the abandoned sentence: "I'm not — that's not how I do this" — interrupted self-correction is part of intimate speech.

content.title: The Wedding Day — Morning

dialogue.line: I can't do my eyeliner. Look at my hands. Look. They won't stop shaking.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Sit down. Marie, can you do hers?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I can. Anna, give me the eyeliner. Look up.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What if I cry it off in the ceremony? What if I'm doing this in three hours and my face just falls off?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: It's waterproof. I checked. Look up. Don't blink.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Anna. Breathe out. Slowly. Yes. Again.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Why am I like this? It's not even a big wedding. It's ninety people. I've sat in audiences bigger than this for work.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Other side. Look up.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It's different because it's about you. Speeches are different from weddings. Stop comparing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I don't even know what I'm scared of. That's the worst bit. I don't have a specific fear. It's just a sort of — fizzing.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Done. Look at me. Yes. You look beautiful and you have not cried any of it off.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Where's Carlos?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Downstairs. Probably also having a fizzing moment, knowing Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I love him.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: We know. That's why you're doing this.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I also love both of you. Right now particularly.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: We know that too.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Marie. Are you and Tom going to be okay? After all this?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Anna, you are not allowed to worry about anyone else's relationship today. We're fine. Better than fine. Tell you about it tomorrow.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Promise?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Promise. Now stand up. We're putting you in the dress.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Anna — one more thing before you do.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: When we walk you to the doors and they open — just look at him. Don't look at the people. Just him. Everything else will sort itself out.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Yeah. Okay. Just him.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

content.situation: Saturday morning of Anna and Carlos's wedding. Anna's six months pregnant and panicking — about everything and nothing. Sophie and Marie are with her in the hotel suite, helping her get ready. The lesson is about how friends manage someone in a small panic — not by fixing it, but by being practical and steady around it.

expressions.meaning: to ruin make-up by crying — wedding-day specific concern
a wave of nervous energy without specific cause — invented but plausible casual phrasing for low-grade panic
given what we know about [name]'s personality — affectionate predictive phrase
to resolve without your direct effort — common British reassurance
at this specific moment more than usual — qualifier added to declarations of love or gratitude

content.grammar_focus: Imperatives in care. Notice the dense clusters of imperatives: "Sit down", "Look up", "Don't blink", "Breathe out", "Stand up". When friends manage someone in panic, they speak in imperatives because they're providing structure the panicking person can't provide for themselves. This isn't bossy — it's protective. Also notice the present continuous and present simple alternation in Anna's panic: "Why am I like this?", "I don't even know what I'm scared of". The shifting tenses mirror her unstable focus. Marie's responses use modal verbs of certainty ("It's waterproof", "You look beautiful") — short, firm, declarative.

content.title: The Wedding Speech

dialogue.line: Right. So. They tell you to keep these short. And I will try. But I have known Anna for nine years, and ninety seconds for nine years feels like a lot of compression.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I want to start with something I don't think I've ever told her properly. The first time I met Anna — properly met her, not the time before that where neither of us was paying attention — I went home that evening and rang my mother. And I said, I think I've just met someone I'm going to know for a long time.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I didn't say I'd marry her. I wasn't being dramatic. I just had a very clear feeling that this person was going to be in my life, in some shape, for a long time. And my mother asked me what she was like. And I said — and I remember this — I said: she's the calmest person in any room she walks into.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I was not calm this morning, Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: You were not calm this morning. We are excluding this morning. This morning was a day off.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Anna is the calmest person in any room she walks into. Not because she's quiet — although she is. But because she's already done the worrying somewhere else, before she got there. By the time she's with you, she's worked out what she thinks. And then she listens to you, properly. Which I think might be the most generous thing one human being can do for another.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I have been listened to by Anna for nine years. I have made some quite stupid decisions in those nine years, and she has listened to all of them, and she has very rarely told me they were stupid. She's let me work it out. Which has made me a better man, slowly. And I am — I am properly grateful for that.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I want to say one more thing, and then I'll sit down before I start crying, because nobody wants that.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: We're having a baby. Most of you know. The baby is due in June, and right now the baby is — I'm reliably informed — about the size of an aubergine.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: An aubergine, really?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: An aubergine. I have been carrying around a printed picture of a fetal aubergine for two weeks.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: And I just want to say to that aubergine, who I hope will one day hear a recording of this speech, that you are coming into a family with the calmest woman I have ever met as your mother. And you will, I promise, be all right. Whatever happens — you will be all right. Because she will have already done the worrying.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Anna. Thank you for marrying me. I love you. To Anna.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

content.situation: Reception, after dinner. Ninety people. Carlos stands up to give his groom's speech. He has notes but he's mostly going off them. The lesson is a single extended monologue — useful for shadowing, intonation practice, and listening to one speaker hold the floor. Anna has two short responses from her seat. The English is warm, informal but careful, and has the rhythms of public speaking that nonetheless feels personal.

expressions.meaning: feels like too much information packed into too little time — borrowed from data/file vocabulary
expressions.expression: feels like a lot of compression

expressions.meaning: to process anxieties privately before social interaction — describes a quality, not literal action
expressions.expression: to do your worrying somewhere else

expressions.meaning: to give someone space to reach their own conclusions, without instructing — relationship vocabulary
expressions.expression: to let someone work it out

expressions.meaning: I have it on good authority — slightly mock-formal phrase used in jokes or lightly humorous claims
everything will be fine for you — declarative reassurance, often used to babies, children, and people facing change

content.grammar_focus: Extended monologue: present perfect for accumulated experience ("I have known Anna for nine years", "I have been listened to", "I have made some quite stupid decisions"). Notice that Carlos uses present perfect not just to state facts but to claim a long shared history — this is the rhetorical work of a wedding speech. Past simple for the anchor anecdote: "The first time I met Anna... I went home... I rang my mother". Then back to present perfect for ongoing meaning. The future tense at the end is declarative reassurance: "you will be all right" — full form, not contracted, for emphasis. This monologue is ideal for shadowing because it has the rhythm of intimate public speech: pauses, parentheticals, self-corrections.

content.title: The Reception

dialogue.line: Soph, where's your wine? You're not drinking.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I am, slowly. I made the mistake of having a glass of champagne on an empty stomach earlier and now I'm being careful.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Wise. There's about three more hours of this. Pace yourself.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: How's Tom?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Tom is currently being told a long story about fishing by Carlos's uncle. He's nodding very politely. I should rescue him.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Go. Save him.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie, isn't it? I've been wanting to come and say hello. Anna talks about you.
dialogue.speaker: Anna's Mum

dialogue.line: Oh — that's lovely to hear. She talks about you too. You must be very proud today.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am. I keep trying not to cry and failing at it. She looks beautiful, doesn't she?
dialogue.speaker: Anna's Mum

dialogue.line: She looks like herself, just — with extra light on her. Which is the right way to look at your wedding.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh, that's a kind thing to say. Yes. Just like herself.
dialogue.speaker: Anna's Mum

dialogue.line: Sophie. I've just been freed. I want to thank you for your wife's intervention.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Marie isn't my wife, Tom. We just lived together.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sorry, force of habit. The way you two talk to each other on the phone, I genuinely forget sometimes.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...Tom, that's quite a sentence.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I mean it kindly. I love watching her on the phone with you. She becomes a slightly different version of herself. I like that version.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...Thank you. That's actually one of the nicest things anyone's said to me in months.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: There you are. I've been looking for you. Are you all right?
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I'm fine. Bit emotional, actually. Tom just said something quite kind and I'm sitting with it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. I'll go away then.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: No, stay. Sit with me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Okay.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor — thank you for coming up for this. It was a long way to come.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. You invited me to your closest friend's wedding. I would have come from anywhere.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

content.situation: Two hours into the wedding reception. Speeches are done. People are dancing or talking or sitting at tables eating cake. Sophie has been moving through small conversations all evening. The lesson captures four micro-conversations she has in different parts of the room. Each is short, light, and entirely social — but together they show how a thoughtful person navigates a wedding.
content.characters: Sophie, Marie, Tom, Eleanor, Anna's Mum

expressions.meaning: to manage your consumption or energy across a long event — drinking, eating, or social effort
passive construction emphasising your captive position — common at social events
expressions.expression: to be told a long story about [topic] by [person]

expressions.meaning: I did/said that automatically because of past patterns — common excuse for small social slips
to take time with an emotion before acting on it — psychologically literate phrase, also appeared in B1-06
the distance was irrelevant compared to the importance of being there — emotional declarative
expressions.expression: I would have come from anywhere

content.grammar_focus: Light social English with emotional grace notes. Notice the pattern: most exchanges use present simple and present continuous — short, light, transactional. The emotional moments arrive as small grammatical shifts: third conditional ("I would have come from anywhere"), present perfect for warmth ("I've been wanting to come and say hello"), past simple as quiet declaration ("It was a long way to come"). This is how warmth lives inside a long social evening — not in extended speeches, but in single sentences that land. Also notice the British self-deprecating humour with passive voice: "Tom is currently being told a long story".

content.title: The News

dialogue.line: Sophie, I've made a decision. I want to tell you about it before I tell anyone else.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Okay. Should I sit down?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's not bad news. But yes, sit down anyway.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I'm sat. Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm going to sell the house. I've put a deposit down on a flat at Cedar View. It's the place I drove past with you at Christmas.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...Cedar View. The sheltered housing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. Sophie, before you say anything — this isn't because of the fall, and it isn't because of the diagnosis. It's because I've been thinking about it for years, and the last six months have helped me see I'd rather move now, while I'm still myself, than wait until I have to.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...You've been thinking about it for years?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. I haven't said much. There wasn't anything to say until I'd decided.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, that house is — that's our house. That's my whole childhood.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know, love. And it always will be, in your head. But it's also a four-bedroom house with a long garden that I haven't really used in fifteen years. The stairs are getting harder. The boiler will need replacing. I rattle around in it. And every time I have to do something about it, I feel my age more, not less.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: When?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'd like to be in by July. The house will probably take longer to sell, but I can move first.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...Okay.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie, are you all right?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I'm — I'm processing. Not because I think it's wrong. I think it's right. I'm just realising I'd been imagining you in that house forever, and that's been quietly anchoring me. I didn't know I was doing it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know, love. I had a similar thing when my mother sold their house. Took me about six months to stop expecting to walk into the kitchen and find her there.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: You never told me that.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm telling you now. So you'll know what's coming. It's not bad. It just takes a while to settle.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Can I help? With the move, with sorting things, with anything?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes please. There are forty years of stuff in that loft and I am too old to pretend otherwise. Come down for a long weekend in May and we'll start.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Deal.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

content.situation: Late April. The blood test results from January eventually came back: a manageable but chronic condition that requires regular monitoring. Mum has been fine but tired. Today she rings Sophie with news she's already decided. She's selling the house and moving into Cedar View — a sheltered housing complex twenty minutes away from where she lives now. Sophie wasn't expecting this. Mum is calm. The lesson is about accepting decisions made by people we love that we don't entirely agree with.

expressions.meaning: to feel small in a space too big for current life — used about empty houses, empty offices
to become aware of the limitations of getting older — used by older speakers about themselves
to give psychological stability or grounding — figurative, common in adult emotional vocabulary
to need time before becoming emotionally normal — used about life changes
expressions.expression: to take a while to settle

expressions.meaning: I had been picturing [X] without realising it — past perfect continuous for unconscious assumptions

content.grammar_focus: Past perfect continuous for unconscious assumptions revealed by change. Sophie: "I'd been imagining you in that house forever, and that's been quietly anchoring me. I didn't know I was doing it." This is the tense of insight — realising you were doing something psychological that you weren't aware of. Mum's parallel: "Took me about six months to stop expecting to walk into the kitchen." Also notice the present perfect for accumulated thinking: "I've been thinking about it for years". Critically, the conversation does not resist or argue — Mum's grammar is declarative throughout ("I'm going to sell", "I've put a deposit down"). Sophie's role is processing, not negotiating.

content.title: The Job in London

dialogue.line: So. London. The full senior role. With the team you wanted.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And you've not said yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I've not said anything yet. I told David I'd take a week.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What's the actual question, Soph? Six months ago you'd have said yes to this in a heartbeat. The version of you who left would have rung David crying with gratitude.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I know. That's the thing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Tell me what's going on.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I think I might not want it. Or rather — I want bits of it. I want you, and Mum closer, and the city I know. But I don't want to leave Manchester yet. I've only just started living there. And I think I'd be coming back for the wrong reasons.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Which would be?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Comfort. Old friends. Knowing where the chemist is. Not because the work is better — actually it isn't. The Manchester role has more autonomy. The London role is more prestigious, but I'd be one of fifteen people. In Manchester I'm in a team of four.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: So what's the dilemma?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...There isn't one, when I say it like that.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: No, there isn't. Sophie. You've worked it out. You wanted me to listen to you say it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes. Sorry.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Don't apologise. That's literally what friends are for. What I'm noticing is — six months ago I'd have been quietly hoping you'd take it. Tonight I'm not hoping anything. I want you to do what's right for you. That's a change for me.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I noticed. While we were talking. You weren't leading me anywhere.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm trying not to. I think I performed too much last time.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: We both did. But — I want to tell you something. The fact that you live with Tom now, and I have my flat, and we ring each other twice a week instead of every night — I thought it would feel like a loss. It doesn't. It feels like — both of us actually being adults. With our own lives. That meet up, but aren't the same life.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. I felt that recently too. It's not what I thought it would be.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Better.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Better.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm going to ring David tomorrow. Tell him I'm staying.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Now stop being a serious adult. I have a bottle of cheap wine and Tom is out for two more hours. Come and complain about something with me.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

content.situation: Mid-May. Sophie has been in Manchester nearly six months. Yesterday she received an unexpected email — David, her old boss in London, offering her a senior role at the London office. Better than the one she turned down. She's flown down for the day to talk to David in person. Tonight she's at Marie and Tom's flat — Marie moved in last month — telling Marie about it. The lesson mirrors Lesson 1, but the conversation is different. They've both grown.

expressions.meaning: without hesitation — repeats from B1-23, signalling vocabulary spiraling back
motivations that don't reflect what you actually want — used about avoidance, comfort, fear
to subtly push them toward a particular conclusion — used about manipulation, even gentle
intersecting independence — phrasing for adult friendship that survived geography
expressions.expression: lives that meet up but aren't the same life

expressions.meaning: playful instruction to break out of heavy emotional mode — affectionate friend phrasing

content.grammar_focus: Mirrored conversation with grown grammar. Compare to Lesson 1: there, both characters used conditional as protection. Here, conditional appears for self-aware comparison: "Six months ago you'd have said yes", "I'd have been quietly hoping you'd take it". Both speakers can now hold their past selves at distance grammatically. Notice the past simple for closure: "You've worked it out. You wanted me to listen to you say it" — short declarative sentences claiming what just happened. Also the use of present continuous as observation: "What I'm noticing is..." — meta-conversation about the conversation itself, a B1+ skill.

content.title: Edinburgh Again

dialogue.line: We made it up Arthur's Seat in fifteen minutes faster than last time.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Are you sure? It felt about the same.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I timed it. We've been training without realising.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Living harder, you mean.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Possibly that too.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It's nice being back here. I wasn't sure if it would be — the version of us who came up last time was a different version.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Different in good ways though, I think. Last time we were panicking about you leaving. Now you've left, and we're still — this. Whatever this is.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Friends, Marie. The word is friends.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes, smart-arse. I know the word. I was reaching for something more specific.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Try.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Friends who became adults more or less in parallel, and didn't accidentally lose each other in the process. That's the longer version.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That's good. Use that.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I will. Anna — when she had Mateo last week — she sent a photo and the caption said: 'meet the small loud reason for everything'. And I kept thinking: that's how I'd describe you, but for the loud part.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...That's quite the line, Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I've had a bit of wine and a long walk. I'm not at full editorial capacity.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'll allow it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What about you? You staying?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah. I'm staying. London will still be there. The Manchester thing isn't done yet — and I think I'd resent the rest of my career if I didn't see it through.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And Mum? Cedar View?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: She moves in two weeks. I'm going down for the weekend.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: How do you feel about it?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Honestly? Less bad than I thought I would. I've been visiting the new flat with her. It suits her. She's pleased about it. And I think — I think part of becoming an adult is letting your parents make their own decisions and not turning it into a story about losing them.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's a lot of thinking for someone who just came up a hill.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I think on hills.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. I've watched you do it for ten years.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Thanks for not making me feel bad about staying in Manchester. You could have. You didn't.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph. We did the hard version of this conversation in November. We don't need to do it again. I'm pleased you're staying. I'm pleased we're both — I don't know. Properly settled into our actual lives. Not waiting for something else to start.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Pub?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Pub.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

content.situation: Late June. Sophie and Marie are in Edinburgh for a long weekend. Same hotel as the A2 finale. Saturday evening — they've climbed Arthur's Seat, eaten somewhere small, and are now walking back through the New Town in the long northern dusk. The conversation closes B1 by acknowledging what's changed and what hasn't. The English is mature, easy, lightly funny. They're both on the other side of something now.

expressions.meaning: to have a more demanding period of life — used affectionately about hard but worthwhile time
developing alongside each other — used about people changing at the same time but separately
to complete something started, especially when it gets hard — moral commitment phrasing
an emotional narrative we tell ourselves about decline — used here as something to resist
living the life we have rather than waiting for one we imagined — adult acceptance phrasing

content.grammar_focus: Closing balance of registers. The dialogue moves easily between humour ("smart-arse", "I'm not at full editorial capacity"), reflection (third conditional "I'd resent the rest of my career if I didn't see it through"), and quiet declaratives ("I'm staying", "I think on hills"). This is the actual range of B1: speakers shift register inside a single conversation depending on whether they're joking, processing, or settling something. Notice the closing rhythm — short questions and shorter answers ("Right. Pub?" / "Pub.") — which contrasts deliberately with the longer reflective passages earlier. This is how warm relationships sound: easy to slip into shorthand because the meaning is shared. End of B1.

content.title: The Equity Track

dialogue.line: Shut the door. Sit down.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: That's an ominous opener.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's not ominous. It's confidential. There's a difference.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Both can be true.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Both can be true. Right. The partners had their quarterly meeting on Monday. Your name came up.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: ...In what context?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Equity track. Two-year window. Not promised, not guaranteed — but on the table.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I've been here ten months.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Eleven, by the time the window opens. And not once in those eleven months have you missed a deadline or mishandled a client. The partners notice that sort of thing, even when they don't say so.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Why are you telling me now? If it's two years away.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because the next twenty-four months will be different from the last eleven. They'll start giving you the cases that test you. The clients other associates would rather not handle. The hours will be — let's say, more visible. I wanted you to understand that before it starts happening, rather than wonder what was going on.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Right. That's — I appreciate that. Genuinely.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: There's something else. They're bringing someone up from London. Helena Marsh. Senior partner. She'll be running the commercial team alongside me from September.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Alongside you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's the official phrasing.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: And the unofficial?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I made partner six months ago. Helena has been a partner for nine years. You can do the maths.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: ...I see.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's fine. Or — it will be fine. I'm telling you because she'll have opinions about who gets put on the equity track. Strong ones. So the next twenty-four months are not just about your work. They're about her reading of your work.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: That's a lot to take in on a Wednesday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. Sorry. Go and have a coffee. We'll talk again on Friday.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a moment when someone told you something important about your future — at work, at school, in your family. How did they phrase it? Were they careful about the words they chose? Or — describe a time when you had to give someone news that wasn't quite news yet. How did you balance honesty with discretion?
prompt_phrases: Not once have I [verb]...
It's on the table, but it's not promised.
I wanted you to understand that before it starts happening.
You can do the maths.
It's a lot to take in.

content.situation: Two weeks after Sophie returned from her Edinburgh weekend with Marie. Wednesday afternoon. Eleanor calls Sophie into her new office — bigger than the old one, partner-grade. Eleanor closes the door, which she rarely does. She has news that isn't quite news yet, and she wants Sophie to hear it before anyone else does.

phrase_cle.example: — Not once have I asked you for anything in return. — I know. That's why I'm offering it now.
phrase_cle.explanation: Эмфатическая инверсия — структура B2-уровня. Когда говорящий начинает с отрицательного наречия (not once, never, rarely, seldom, hardly), глагол и подлежащее меняются местами: "Not once have I missed a deadline" вместо "I haven't missed a deadline once". Это даёт фразе вес. Используется в формальной речи, в эмоционально нагруженных моментах, в письменной речи. Звучит как взрослый, продуманный английский.

expressions.meaning: an emphatic negative statement — places the negative element first to give it weight; common in formal or emotionally charged speech
available for discussion or consideration, but not yet decided — borrowed from negotiation; common in professional contexts
to work out an implication for yourself, especially when the speaker doesn't want to say it explicitly — diplomatic shorthand
her interpretation or assessment — "reading" used metaphorically about how someone judges a situation or person
a lot of information to process emotionally and cognitively at once — common phrase for overwhelming news

content.cultural_note: British law firms use "equity partner" to mean a partner who owns a share of the firm and takes a percentage of profits — as opposed to a "salaried partner" who has the title without the ownership. The path to equity partnership is usually 8-12 years from qualification, and being told you're "on the track" is a serious moment in someone's career — it doesn't promise anything, but it signals that the partners have started watching you specifically. Eleanor telling Sophie in advance is unusual; most firms only tell people during the formal review process. The fact that she does it privately is a sign of friendship — and also a small political move, because Helena hasn't arrived yet. By telling Sophie now, Eleanor is establishing herself as Sophie's mentor before someone else can.
content.grammar_focus: Inversion for emphasis appears for the first time in the course: "Not once in those eleven months have you missed a deadline." Notice the structure — the negative adverbial "not once" forces subject-verb inversion (have you missed, not you have missed). This is a B2 marker because it requires the speaker to plan the sentence in advance — you can't drop into inversion mid-thought. Eleanor uses it deliberately to emphasise Sophie's reliability without sounding like she's reading a performance review. Compare a non-inverted version: "You haven't missed a deadline once in those eleven months." Same information, less weight. The inversion does the emotional work. Also notice future continuous ("They'll start giving you the cases that test you", "The hours will be more visible") used to describe a foreseeable but not yet active future — this is a B2-specific use of the form.

key_vocabulary.note: Партнёр, владеющий долей фирмы. В отличие от salaried partner (партнёр на зарплате).
Звучащий как предвестник чего-то плохого. Sophie иронизирует в первой реплике.
Не для распространения. В корпоративной речи — стандартный сигнал что разговор должен остаться в комнате.
Используется в профессиональном контексте о клиентах, делах, кризисах. Mishandle a client = плохо обработать клиента.
Phrasal verb. "A lot to take in" — частая фраза о сложной новости.

comprehension_questions.hint: She wants Sophie to understand the changes that are coming, so she doesn't wonder what's happening when the harder cases start arriving. Forewarned is forearmed.
comprehension_questions.question: Why is Eleanor telling Sophie about the equity track now, two years before the window opens?

comprehension_questions.hint: A senior partner being brought up from London to run the commercial team alongside Eleanor. Eleanor mentions her because Helena will have strong opinions about who gets equity track — and she's been a partner nine years longer than Eleanor.
comprehension_questions.question: Who is Helena Marsh, and why does Eleanor mention her?

comprehension_questions.hint: She's catching herself mid-sentence. The first instinct is to say it's fine. The correction reveals it's not fine yet, but she trusts it will become so. Honest self-edit in real time.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Eleanor mean by "It's fine. Or — it will be fine"?

content.title: Telling Marie

dialogue.line: Hi! Hold on, let me find the kettle. Tom's reorganised the kitchen and I don't recognise it anymore.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: He reorganised your kitchen?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: He reorganised our kitchen, which is the politically correct phrasing. I'm allowed to be cross about it for forty-eight hours and then I have to accept the new system.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That's a remarkably specific marriage rule.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: We're not married. But yes. Anyway. How are you? You sounded a bit weird on the message.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Eleanor told me something today. I've been turning it over for about six hours and I don't know what to do with it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: They've got me on the equity track. Two-year window. Not promised — but the partners are watching. And someone's coming up from London. Helena. Senior. Nine years more partner than Eleanor. Going to run the team alongside her, in inverted commas.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Right. Okay. That's a lot.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It's a lot.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What's the question?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I don't think I have a question yet. I have a feeling I'm trying to name.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: All right. Try.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It's not — I'm not panicking. It's that I came up here ten months ago to do interesting work in a calmer setting. And the work has been good. Genuinely good. But the moment they say "equity track", everything I came up here to escape starts coming back. The visibility. The watching. The being-talked-about-in-meetings-I'm-not-in.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Hmm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What's hmm?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm trying to work out if you're describing a problem or a feature. Because — Soph, you're brilliant. That sort of work has been coming for you regardless. Manchester just delayed it by a year.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...I hadn't put it like that.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm not saying you should take it. I'm saying don't pretend it's a betrayal of why you came. It's not. It's the next bit of the same thing.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That's actually clarifying.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. What's Helena like?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Don't know yet. She doesn't start until September.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Then you've got six weeks of not worrying about her. Use them.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: How's Tom's kitchen?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Functional. Annoying. Better than mine was, which is the worst part.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a piece of news that you needed to talk through with someone before you knew what you thought about it. Not a decision yet — a feeling you were trying to name. Or — describe the moment when a friend asked you "what's the question?" and you realised you hadn't quite formed one yet.
prompt_phrases: I've been turning it over for [time].
I don't have a question yet. I have a feeling I'm trying to name.
It's that [explanation].
I hadn't put it like that.
That's the next bit of the same thing.

content.situation: Wednesday evening, the same day as the conversation with Eleanor. Sophie is in her flat in Chorlton. Marie is at her place in Stoke Newington — Tom is out at five-a-side football. Sophie rings Marie. They haven't spoken properly since Edinburgh, and now there's news that isn't quite news. Sophie wants to think out loud at someone she trusts.

phrase_cle.example: — What's bothering you? — I don't know exactly. I'm trying to name it.
phrase_cle.explanation: Психологически грамотная фраза, которая описывает раннюю стадию обработки эмоции — когда чувство есть, а слова к нему ещё нет. Часто используется в разговорах между близкими людьми, в терапии, в письменной рефлексии. На B2 уровне это переход от языка готовых формул к языку поиска точности. "To name" здесь не буквальное "назвать имя", а "найти точное слово для".

expressions.meaning: to think about something repeatedly from different angles — used about decisions or news that haven't yet resolved
expressions.expression: to turn something over (in your head)

expressions.meaning: British way of marking a phrase as quoted, sceptical, or not to be taken at face value — equivalent to American "in scare quotes" or finger-quotes gesture
is this a flaw or actually a defining quality — borrowed from software development, now common in everyday reflection
a continuation, not a contradiction — used to reframe what feels like a deviation as natural progression
expressions.expression: the next bit of the same thing

expressions.meaning: make active use of available time before pressure begins — imperative as practical advice

content.cultural_note: "In inverted commas" is one of the most useful British verbal habits to know. Inverted commas are quotation marks (Brits call them inverted commas, Americans call them quotation marks). When said aloud — "alongside her, in inverted commas" — it signals that the speaker is quoting a phrase they don't entirely believe. It's the verbal equivalent of finger-quotes, but more elegant. American English borrows this less; the American equivalent is "so-called" or "in scare quotes". Using "in inverted commas" in conversation is a small marker of British register — and it's also a way of expressing scepticism diplomatically without committing to the criticism. Eleanor saying "alongside her" was already careful; Sophie quoting it back "in inverted commas" makes the political situation visible without naming it.
content.grammar_focus: Cleft sentences begin appearing. Sophie says: "It's that I came up here ten months ago..." — this is an it-cleft. The standard sentence would be "I came up here ten months ago to do calmer work." The it-cleft ("It's that I came up here...") shifts emphasis onto the explanation itself, marking it as the answer to a previous question. Marie also uses one: "It's not. It's the next bit of the same thing" — the second clause is a stripped-down it-cleft (it is the next bit). Cleft sentences are a B2 hallmark because they require the speaker to identify what part of the sentence carries the new or important information. Also notice present perfect continuous for ongoing reflection ("I've been turning it over for about six hours") and the contracted modal of past habit ("That sort of work has been coming for you regardless").

key_vocabulary.note: Phrasal verb. О мыслях, решениях, новостях.
Adverb of degree. "Remarkably specific", "remarkably calm". Звучит чуть формальнее чем "surprisingly".
Сильное слово. Sophie использует его про себя — "a betrayal of why you came" = я предала свою причину переезда. Marie возражает.
Adjective from "to clarify". Удобная категория для оценки чужой реплики, которая помогла подумать.
Marie описывает кухню Tom'а — не "красивая", не "плохая", а просто "работает". Слегка ироничное.

comprehension_questions.hint: She has an emotional reaction to Eleanor's news, but she hasn't yet identified what it is. The feeling exists before the words for it. She's using Marie as a sounding board to find the right word.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Sophie mean by "a feeling I'm trying to name"?

comprehension_questions.hint: Sophie is treating the equity track as a betrayal of why she came to Manchester. Marie reframes it as the natural continuation — "the next bit of the same thing". Same trajectory, different city.
comprehension_questions.question: How does Marie reframe what Sophie has been worrying about?

comprehension_questions.hint: The serious part of the conversation is done. Marie signals it's over by returning to the small domestic complaint they started with. British conversational rhythm — wrap heavy topics with light ones.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Marie change the subject to Tom's kitchen at the end?

content.title: Helena Arrives

dialogue.line: Sophie. Thank you for making time. Sit. Coffee?
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I'm fine, thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I've read your file. Eleanor speaks very highly of you, which on its own would be interesting — Eleanor doesn't speak highly of many people. She also speaks highly of you accurately, which is rarer. Your work on the Halberd matter was strong.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Thank you. That was largely Eleanor's structure. I was implementing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm. Don't do that.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Don't do what?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Pre-emptively credit your superiors when you're being praised. It's a habit female associates pick up to seem unthreatening, and it costs you. Eleanor structured the strategy. You implemented it well, but you also caught two errors in the pleadings that weren't in the structure. I read them. Take credit for the things you actually did.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: ...Right. Noted.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm not telling you off. I'm telling you a rule, because no one will. Eleanor probably thinks she's helping you by not mentioning it. She isn't.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I appreciate you being direct.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Direct is the only register that works at this level. So — direct: I've been told you're being considered for the equity track. I don't know yet whether I agree with that assessment. You've been here under a year. The fact that the partners are already discussing it is unusual.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I understand.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What I'd like is to spend the next six months forming my own view. Not Eleanor's view, not the existing partners' view. Mine. I'll put you on a couple of matters that I'm leading personally. Hard ones. The point isn't to trip you up. It's to see how you think when there's no one above you to defer to.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: All right.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: First one starts Thursday. Energy client. Their public position and their actual position differ in ways that may make you uncomfortable. I'd like you to read the file before then and tell me on Thursday morning whether you want to be on the matter or not. If the answer is no, I'll respect that. If the answer is yes, I'll expect you to do the work without flinching.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: What if I don't know yet on Thursday morning?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Then you'll have to decide by Thursday morning. That's part of the test. Not the work — the deciding.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a time someone gave you direct, unsoftened feedback at work or in your studies. How did it land? Did you take it in immediately or did you need time? Or — describe a meeting where you realised the person across from you was assessing you, not asking your opinion.
prompt_phrases: I've been told [news] about you.
I'd like to spend [time] forming my own view.
Take credit for the things you actually did.
The point isn't to [verb]. It's to [verb].
If the answer is yes, I'll expect [outcome].

content.situation: First Tuesday of September. Helena Marsh started yesterday. The whole office had a brief introduction in the meeting room — formal handshakes, polite nodding. Today, she's asked to see Sophie individually. Twenty minutes in her office. Helena is in her late forties, immaculately dressed, calm in a way that doesn't telegraph friendliness. Sophie has prepared by re-reading three of her recent case notes. She has not prepared for what the conversation actually turns out to be.

phrase_cle.phrase: Direct is the only register that works at this level
phrase_cle.example: — Sorry to be blunt. — Don't apologise. Direct is the only register that works at this level.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень B2 концепт — речь как уровневый инструмент. "Register" здесь означает не музыкальный регистр, а стилистический уровень речи: разговорный, профессиональный, формальный, дипломатический. Helena говорит, что на её уровне (старший партнёр) другие регистры — мягкость, обходные пути, дипломатия — не работают, потому что их слишком много вокруг. Прямота становится редкостью, а значит ценностью. Это взрослое наблюдение о коммуникации, которое Sophie ещё не делала.
phrase_cle.translation: На этом уровне работает только прямой регистр

expressions.meaning: to praise someone in conversations with others — formal register, common in professional reference contexts
to give credit to others before being praised yourself, as a defensive social move — psychologically literate observation
to deliberately set them up to fail — used when reassuring someone it's NOT the intent
to yield decision-making authority to someone more senior — formal professional vocabulary
to do difficult or uncomfortable work without visible reluctance — figurative, slightly dramatic
expressions.expression: to do the work without flinching

content.cultural_note: Helena's comment about "a habit female associates pick up to seem unthreatening" references a real and well-documented pattern in professional environments — particularly in law, finance, and academia.
