Research on workplace communication has shown that women, especially early-career women, are statistically more likely to deflect praise to senior colleagues, even when the work was their own. The reasons are layered: socialisation, fear of being seen as arrogant, the calculation that visibility brings risk. Senior women like Helena often see this in younger associates and call it out — sometimes harshly. The mentorship is real, but the framing ("costs you") is also strategic. Helena is teaching Sophie how to be seen — partly because she remembers being unseen herself.
content.grammar_focus: Modal verbs at full B2 strength, particularly in professional register. Helena uses "would like", "may make", "will respect", "will expect" — each modal carrying a precise weight. "What I'd like is..." is a cleft with embedded modal preference, common in management speech. "That may make you uncomfortable" — modal of possibility, hedging the warning. "I'll respect that" / "I'll expect you to" — future as commitment, not prediction. The contrast between conditional (would, should, could) and future-as-commitment (will) is what makes Helena sound authoritative without being aggressive. Also notice the imperative "Don't do that" used as a teaching moment — terse, but immediately followed by explanation, which is the B2 pattern for direct mentorship.

key_vocabulary.note: Adverb. Часто про одежду, внешний вид, презентацию. "Immaculately dressed" — стандартное описание чьего-то идеального вида.
Изначально про телеграф. В современном употреблении — о невербальных сигналах. "Doesn't telegraph friendliness" = не подаёт сигналов дружелюбия.
Юридический термин. Состязательные документы в деле. Sophie работает с ними как associate.
Formal noun for evaluation. "My assessment of the situation" — стандартная профессиональная фраза.
Buквально — физическая реакция на удар. В переносном — отступить от трудного. "Without flinching" = не отступая.

comprehension_questions.hint: Because Sophie was deflecting praise — a habit Helena identifies as common among female associates. Helena thinks it costs Sophie professionally, and she's making it visible so Sophie can choose to stop.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Helena tell Sophie not to credit Eleanor for the Halberd work?

comprehension_questions.hint: Whether Sophie can decide by Thursday morning whether to take on the energy client matter — knowing the client's public and actual positions differ. Helena says "the test isn't the work — it's the deciding."
comprehension_questions.question: What is the test Helena is setting for Sophie?

comprehension_questions.hint: Eleanor mentors gently, with humour and patience. Helena mentors directly, naming behaviours and habits Sophie isn't aware of. Both are valid; both are the way they were probably mentored themselves.
comprehension_questions.question: How is Helena's mentorship different from Eleanor's?

content.title: Reading the File

dialogue.line: Soph. Hold on, I'm putting Mateo down. Two minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Take your time.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. He's down. Probably for thirty-five minutes if I'm lucky. What's going on?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I have to make a decision by tomorrow morning and I want to think out loud at someone.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You've called the right person. Talk.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: New senior partner. Helena. Wants me on a matter. Energy client. Their public line is one thing. The internal correspondence is another. They knew about contamination. They sat on the data. Not criminally — there's no clear breach of disclosure law from what I can see — but morally, it's grim.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And Helena's asking you to defend them.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Defend isn't quite the right word. The case is largely about the procedural framework — how the lawsuit is being constructed, whether the environmental groups have standing, what disclosure obligations apply retrospectively. We're not arguing they didn't know. We're arguing the legal structure of the claim.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm. So it's the technical defence, not the moral one.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Yes. But the technical defence is what allows them to keep operating. So in practice it's the same thing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. What's Helena's read on it?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: She didn't say. She told me to read the file and decide by tomorrow morning whether I want to be on the matter. She said if I say no, she'll respect it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Do you believe that?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I believe she'll respect it on the surface. I think it'll mark me, whether she means it to or not. "Wouldn't take the energy file" becomes part of how she sees me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Probably. So what's the actual question?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: The question is whether the moral discomfort of doing the work outweighs the professional cost of not doing it. And I don't think those things are commensurable. I keep trying to put them on the same scale and they don't go on the same scale.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph. Can I ask you something difficult?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: If this client were a smaller, less politically charged business — same legal structure, same suppressed report, just less spectacular — would you be agonising? Or would you take it without thinking?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: ...I'd take it without thinking. Because that kind of case is what commercial law is. People hide things, then get sued, then call us.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: So the question isn't about commercial law. It's about whether you can do commercial law for a company whose product you object to in principle.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Yes. That's it exactly.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Then that's the question you should answer. Not whether the case is winnable. Not whether Helena is testing you. Whether you can do this work for this kind of client, knowing what they make.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: ...Carlos. Thank you. That actually clears it up.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What's the answer?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I think — yes. But I want to sleep on it before I commit.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Don't decide tonight. Decide tomorrow at 7am with coffee. The morning version of you is more honest than the evening version.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a decision you made that involved weighing moral discomfort against practical consequence. Use conditionals to test your reasoning — "If the situation were slightly different, would I still feel this way?" Or — describe a time when someone helped you isolate the real question by asking a hypothetical version of it.
prompt_phrases: If this were [different scenario], would you still [verb]?
I keep trying to put them on the same scale.
That's the question you should answer.
I want to sleep on it before I commit.
The morning version of me is more honest.

content.situation: Wednesday evening. Sophie has spent six hours with the file Helena gave her. The client is North Sea Petroleum — a mid-sized energy company defending itself against a coordinated lawsuit from environmental groups about historical undisclosed leaks. Public position: full cooperation with regulators, sustainability transition. Internal position revealed in disclosure: knew about the leaks for years, suppressed reports. Sophie rings Carlos because Marie would tell her to follow her conscience and she doesn't want that — she wants someone who'll think with her, not for her.

phrase_cle.example: — What weighs more — your career or your peace of mind? — They aren't commensurable. That's why the question is hard.
phrase_cle.explanation: Слово из философии и экономики, которое в B2 английском начинает появляться в обычной речи — особенно в разговорах об этике, ценностях, выборе. "Commensurable" значит "измеримый по одной шкале". Когда Sophie говорит "those things are not commensurable", она признаёт: моральный дискомфорт и профессиональная цена — разные категории, нет единой формулы для их сравнения. Это взрослое признание, что некоторые решения нельзя свести к арифметике.
phrase_cle.translation: вещи, которые нельзя сопоставить по одной шкале

expressions.meaning: to deliberately not disclose information that should be made public — accusatory phrasing
expressions.expression: to sit on data / a report

expressions.meaning: morally bleak, distasteful, unpleasant in a serious way — distinctly British in this register
Helena's interpretation or position — "read" used as a noun, common in professional contexts
able to be measured by the same standard — formal philosophical word, used here in real adult conversation
your better-rested, clearer-thinking self — informal psychological phrasing

content.cultural_note: British commercial law firms have a particular relationship with energy clients. The largest London firms — "Magic Circle" and similar — have represented oil, gas, and mining companies for over a century, and many lawyers go through their careers without questioning the arrangement. In the last fifteen years, this has become more visible: junior associates increasingly raise concerns, some partners refuse certain matters, and a few firms have introduced informal opt-out systems for ethically contentious work. Helena's offer to Sophie — read it, decide, no consequence — reflects this newer tolerance, but the older logic still operates underneath: "declined the energy file" becomes part of how a partner reads an associate, even if no one says so out loud. Carlos is right that the choice may have a small invisible cost regardless of the words around it.
content.grammar_focus: Mixed conditionals for hypothetical moral reasoning. Carlos asks: "If this client were a smaller, less politically charged business — would you be agonising?" — second conditional with "were" subjunctive (more formal than "was"), used to construct a counterfactual that strips the moral question down to its essence. Sophie's reply uses the same structure: "I'd take it without thinking." This is the actual adult use of conditionals — not grammar exercises, but a thinking tool to isolate which factor in a complex situation is doing the moral work. Notice also the embedded conditionals in Sophie's analysis ("whether the moral discomfort outweighs the professional cost") — these are noun clauses that compress argument into a single grammatical frame. Also: "would be agonising" — past continuous in conditional consequence, describing not just the action but its emotional texture.

key_vocabulary.note: В контексте — экологическое загрязнение от утечек. Стандартный термин в энергетических исках.
Юридический термин. Обязательство раскрыть документы или факты в ходе процесса.
Adverb. "Disclosure obligations apply retrospectively" = обязательства применимы к прошлому.
Phrasal-like verb. О балансе аргументов или последствий.
British spelling (US: agonize). Сильнее чем "to worry". О мучительном выборе.

comprehension_questions.hint: Not whether commercial law is ethical in general, and not whether this case is winnable — but whether she can represent a company whose product (oil, gas) she objects to in principle. Carlos helps her isolate it.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Sophie's actual moral question?

comprehension_questions.hint: Marie would tell her to follow her conscience. Sophie wants someone to think with her, not for her — and Carlos is the friend who asks the harder questions instead of giving comforting answers.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie call Carlos rather than Marie?

comprehension_questions.hint: Late-night decisions are influenced by tiredness, anxiety, and emotional weight. Morning decisions, made with rest, are more likely to reflect actual values rather than the noise around them. Adult practical wisdom.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Carlos mean by "the morning version of you is more honest than the evening version"?

content.title: The Decision in the Morning

dialogue.line: Sophie. You're early.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: You said by Thursday morning. I thought I should err on the side of being early rather than late.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sit. What's your answer?
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Yes. I'll be on the matter. I want to make a small condition first, and I'd rather we talk about it now than later.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I read the file twice. The legal structure of the case is solid — there's a real argument about standing and a real argument about retrospective application. I can do that work without compromising anything. What I won't do is help craft the public communications. The press strategy. The lines for the CEO. If that's part of what you're asking, I'd rather know now.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Hmm.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: It's not because I think the public communications are wrong in themselves. It's because I can't sit in a room and help shape sentences that I know don't reflect what's in the disclosure. The legal arguments I can make in good conscience. The PR I can't.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's a thoughtful distinction. It's also one I've drawn myself, on different files.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Then we agree?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: We agree on the substance. I want to push back on something else, though, while we're being honest with each other.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Please.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You came in here with a condition. That's good. But you wrapped it in three softening sentences before you said it. "I want to make a small condition". "I'd rather we talk about it now". That preamble cost you about ten seconds and a small amount of authority. The condition itself was fine. State it. Then explain it if asked.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: ...Right.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Try it again. From the top. "My answer is yes". What comes next?
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: My answer is yes. I'll do the legal work. I won't be involved in the public communications. The reasons are obvious from the file.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Better. Less explanatory. Did that feel different to say?
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Slightly aggressive, if I'm being honest.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's not aggressive. It feels aggressive because you're not used to it. Direct statements feel sharp the first hundred times you make them. After that they just feel like statements. Welcome to the case. First meeting is at eleven.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a moment when you stated a condition or boundary and then noticed afterwards how many softening words you used. Or — practise saying the same thing twice: once with hedging, once without. Which feels more honest? Which feels more aggressive?
prompt_phrases: I'll err on the side of [verb]ing.
I can do that work in good conscience.
What I won't do is [verb].
State it. Then explain it if asked.
Direct statements feel sharp the first hundred times you make them.

content.situation: Thursday morning, 8.45am. Sophie slept on it. The morning version of her — Carlos was right — gave a clearer answer than the evening version had. She arrived at the office at 8.20, made coffee, sat with the file open for twenty minutes without reading it, and then walked up to Helena's office at 8.45. Helena's door is open. Sophie knocks anyway.

phrase_cle.example: — So you're agreeing? — I'm agreeing to the legal work. What I won't do is help with the press strategy.
phrase_cle.explanation: Cleft structure для определения границы. "What I won't do is help with the press strategy" вместо "I won't help with the press strategy". Cleft (it- или wh-) выделяет ключевую часть фразы и заставляет слушателя её услышать. На B2 эта структура — рабочий инструмент в переговорах, профессиональных границах, политических разговорах. Звучит твёрже чем простая отрицательная форма, потому что отрицание становится темой, а не функциональной деталью.
phrase_cle.translation: Чего я делать не буду — это [глагол]

expressions.meaning: to choose the cautious option, accepting a slight cost on that side rather than risk the alternative — formal but common
expressions.expression: to err on the side of [something]

expressions.meaning: without moral discomfort, with internal alignment — slightly formal phrase used in ethical contexts
to disagree or negotiate against a position — workplace English, also appeared in B1; here used with more weight
to surround a direct statement with polite hedging — Helena uses it as a critique of communication style
expressions.expression: to wrap something in softening sentences

expressions.meaning: the perceived sharpness of direct speech fades with practice — Helena's mentorship aphorism
expressions.expression: after that they just feel like statements

content.cultural_note: Helena's lesson about "softening sentences" reflects a real coaching pattern in British law firms, especially around female associates moving toward partnership. The behaviour Helena identifies — preceding a clear statement with three layers of polite preface — is statistically more common in women trained to seem accommodating. The cost, in environments where speed of speech and authority of speech are conflated, is real but often invisible. Senior women like Helena often teach this directly because no one taught it to them, and they remember the years they wasted not knowing. The "first hundred times" line is a specific coaching trope — the idea that direct speech feels aggressive only because it's unfamiliar, and the only way through is repetition until it becomes neutral.
content.grammar_focus: Direct vs. indirect speech as a register choice. The lesson explicitly teaches the difference, with Helena making Sophie say the same thing twice — once with hedging, once without. Notice the sentence-level mechanics: Sophie's first version uses "I want to make a small condition first" — the "want to" + "first" stack two softeners. The revised version is "I'll do the legal work. I won't be involved in the public communications." Pure declarative — subject + modal + verb, no apologies, no hedges. This is one of the hardest things to teach because the grammar itself isn't difficult — it's the willingness to use it that's the skill. Also notice Helena's use of "would rather" twice ("I'd rather we talk about it now", "I'd rather know now") — preference framed conditionally; here it sits inside an otherwise direct sentence and softens just the right amount, not too much.

key_vocabulary.note: В выражении "err on the side of" = выбирать осторожную сторону. Не современное слово — звучит чуть формально.
Юридический термин. Право истца обращаться в суд. Один из ключевых аргументов в защите по делу.
Helena использует это про лишние смягчающие предложения. Изначально — формальная преамбула документа.
Helena говорит "less explanatory" = меньше объяснений. Смысл: не надо оправдываться, просто заявляй.
Похвала чьему-то рассуждению. "That's a thoughtful distinction" — Helena признаёт качество мысли Sophie.

comprehension_questions.hint: She'll do the legal work but won't help craft public communications. The legal arguments she can make in good conscience; the PR she can't.
comprehension_questions.question: What condition does Sophie put on her acceptance?

comprehension_questions.hint: Sophie wrapped the condition in three softening sentences before saying it. The softening cost her authority. Helena makes her say it again, more directly, to feel the difference.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Helena's critique of how Sophie stated her condition?

comprehension_questions.hint: Because she's not used to it. Helena's principle: direct statements feel sharp the first hundred times. After that, they just feel like statements. Discomfort fades with practice.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does the direct version "feel aggressive" to Sophie?

content.title: Eleanor's Question

dialogue.line: Got a minute? I'll be quick.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Always.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I heard you took the energy file with Helena.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Yes. I had until Thursday morning to decide. I decided yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: On what basis?
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: On the basis that the legal work is real legal work, and I drew a clear line about what I would and wouldn't do. Helena was fine with the line. So — I took it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Hmm.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: What's hmm, Eleanor?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'd have liked you to talk to me before you decided.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: ...Right. Okay. Honest answer?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Always.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I deliberately didn't. Not because I don't trust you. I do. But because if I'd talked to you about whether to take a Helena file, the conversation wouldn't have been about the file. It would have been about Helena and you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I wasn't ready to have that conversation. I'm still not. And I didn't want my decision about my work to be filtered through a politics I don't yet understand and can't yet take a position on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's — actually, that's fair. I'd not put it to myself like that, but it's fair.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I'm not picking her over you. I want to be clear about that. I'm trying not to pick. Which I realise might be its own kind of picking. But — for now — that's where I am.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's its own kind of picking. You're right about that part. But I'd rather you be honest about it than pretend you don't see what's happening.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I see what's happening.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. I can tell. Listen — I'm not going to make this difficult for you. I'm not going to be cool with you. I'm just going to be — slightly less in your business for a bit. Until I work out what to do with this.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's not a punishment. It's information about myself. I don't know how to be your mentor and not feel slightly betrayed by your taking her file, and I'd rather notice that than pretend not to.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: ...That's actually mature.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. I hate it too.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor — for what it's worth. The work I learned to do well, I learned from you. Helena hasn't taught me anything yet, except how to ask for what I want without apologising. Which is also from you, just packaged differently.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Thank you for that. Right. Have a good weekend, Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: You too.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a friendship that cooled because of a choice you made — not a fight, but a decision that changed how a friend saw you. How did the conversation go? Did anyone name what was happening? Or — describe a time when you realised that not picking a side was itself picking a side.
prompt_phrases: I deliberately didn't [verb].
It would have been about [topic], not [other topic].
I'm trying not to pick.
I'm not going to make this difficult for you.
It's information about myself.

content.situation: Friday afternoon, two days after Sophie joined the energy matter. Eleanor has heard about it through the grapevine — she's not on the case, Helena's running it directly. Eleanor stops by Sophie's desk at the end of the day. Casual posture, hand on the doorframe, but the question that comes is not casual. The end of Block 1: the friendship between Sophie and Eleanor cools, not because of conflict, but because Sophie has chosen not to take a side in the slow politics that Helena's arrival has set in motion.

phrase_cle.phrase: It's its own kind of picking
phrase_cle.example: — I'm trying to stay neutral. — Staying neutral is its own kind of picking, isn't it.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень B2 наблюдение — что нейтральность сама по себе позиция. Структура "its own kind of [noun]" используется когда говорящий хочет сказать: то, что ты считаешь не-X, на самом деле другая форма X. Звучит зрело и честно. Eleanor не нападает на Sophie этой фразой — она признаёт её правоту и одновременно усложняет её картину. Это уровень разговора, недоступный в B1, потому что требует одновременной валидации и противоречия.
phrase_cle.translation: Это само по себе разновидность выбора

expressions.meaning: hypothetical past preference — softer than "you should have", but carrying the same regret; common in adult conflict
to establish a clear boundary about what you will and won't do — common in professional and personal contexts
to interpret or process one thing via the lens of another — used here about decisions being shaped by office politics
neutrality is itself a position — a sophisticated adult observation that refusing to choose is a form of choice
qualifier introducing an opinion or appreciation that may or may not matter — humble framing

content.cultural_note: British professional culture has a particular idiom for relationships that cool without breaking: "slightly less in your business". It signals withdrawal from the closer, mentor-style relationship while preserving the working surface. Eleanor saying it explicitly — rather than just doing it — is a generous act, even though it sounds cold. The honest version: most office friendships don't survive promotions, restructurings, or new senior hires. The mature version: name the change, explain its source, leave a door open. Eleanor's line "I don't know how to be your mentor and not feel slightly betrayed" is unusually direct. It's the kind of thing said between two women who respect each other enough to risk awkwardness. Many friendships in this position simply drift; this one is being managed, carefully, by both sides.
content.grammar_focus: Third conditional and conditional perfect for arguing about an alternate past. Sophie: "If I'd talked to you about whether to take a Helena file, the conversation wouldn't have been about the file." Two layers of past: the past act she didn't do, and the past consequence that would have followed. Eleanor uses the same pattern: "I'd have liked you to talk to me before you decided" — would have liked + infinitive, expressing past preference for an action that didn't happen. This is the grammar of sophisticated regret — not blaming the present, but identifying the missed branch of the past. Also notice the gerund constructions Eleanor uses near the end: "feel slightly betrayed by your taking her file" — possessive + gerund, formal but precise. And the future continuous Eleanor uses to describe her own coming behaviour: "I'm just going to be slightly less in your business for a bit" — present continuous as future, used for planned interpersonal change.

key_vocabulary.note: "I heard through the grapevine" — стандартная фраза о новостях, дошедших не напрямую.
Идиома о профессиональных или личных границах. "I drew a clear line" — ключевая фраза этого урока.
В переносном смысле — пропускать одно через призму другого. "Filtered through office politics" = искажённый политикой.
Сильное слово. Eleanor смягчает его "slightly" — "slightly betrayed". Признание чувства без драматизации.
В переносном смысле — преподнесённый определённым образом. "Packaged differently" = тот же урок, но в другой форме.

comprehension_questions.hint: Because the conversation would have been about Eleanor and Helena's politics, not about the file itself. Sophie wanted to make a decision about her own work, not about a political alignment she doesn't yet fully understand.
comprehension_questions.question: Why did Sophie deliberately not consult Eleanor before taking the Helena file?

comprehension_questions.hint: She's pulling back from the close mentor role. Not punishing Sophie — managing her own feelings. The friendship survives but at a different temperature, until Eleanor works out what to do with the situation.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Eleanor mean by "slightly less in your business"?

comprehension_questions.hint: Sophie is acknowledging that her direct-speech lesson from Helena traces back to Eleanor's earlier mentorship — same skill, different teacher. It's a generous closing gesture, repairing the conversation without erasing what's changed.
comprehension_questions.question: What does the closing exchange about "how to ask for what I want without apologising" reveal?

content.title: The Question Before the Question

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I've been thinking about something for a while.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...How long is a while?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Few months. Maybe longer. I haven't been keeping count, which is probably the answer to your question.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: What's the thing?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I think we should — I mean, I'd like us to —
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom. Wait. Sorry — wait.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...All right.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I can hear what you're about to say. And I'm not saying don't say it. I'm saying I need a minute before you say it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Okay.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: That's not a no. That's a — I want to be present for this, and I'm currently not.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Where are you?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: About six steps ahead of where you are. Trying to work out my answer before you've finished asking.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That's not great.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I know it's not great. That's why I asked you to wait.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Can I ask you something first?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Is there a reason you're asking now? Like — has something happened? Did your mum say something, or —
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: No. Nothing's happened. I just — I've been carrying it around and it started feeling stupid not to say it.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Okay.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. Can I ask you the thing?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Not tonight. Is that all right?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Yes. That's all right.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I'm sorry. I know that's not what you wanted from this evening.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It's fine. I'd rather you be honest about it than say yes because the wine's open.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Thank you for not finishing the sentence when I asked.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Course. It'll keep.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a time someone started to ask you a big question and you needed to slow them down before they finished. How did you do it without saying no? Or — describe a moment when you realised you were already running ahead of a conversation, working out your answer before the question had fully arrived. What does that feeling cost?
prompt_phrases: I can hear what you're about to say.
I need a minute before you say it.
I'm about [X] steps ahead of where you are.
Is there a reason you're asking now?
It'll keep.

content.situation: Friday evening at Marie and Tom's flat in Stoke Newington. They've been living together for fourteen months. Tom has cooked — pasta, nothing elaborate. The wine is half-finished. Tom has been quieter than usual all evening, carrying a sentence he hasn't said yet. Marie has noticed and decided not to ask. Then Tom starts the sentence, and Marie — without fully meaning to — turns it sideways before it can land.

phrase_cle.phrase: That's not a no. That's a [explanation]
phrase_cle.example: — So you don't want to come? — That's not a no. That's a — I'm tired and I need to think about it tomorrow.
phrase_cle.explanation: Cleft-структура для размыкания категорий. Marie говорит "That's not a no" — отделяет реакцию от отказа. Потом "That's a —" с паузой — заявляет, что реакция требует имени, и сейчас она это имя найдёт. Это типично взрослый ход в эмоциональном разговоре: не дать собеседнику услышать твою паузу как отрицание. На B2 уровне эта структура работает как буфер между импульсом и формулировкой. Полезно в личных и рабочих разговорах, где тишина воспринимается как ответ.

expressions.meaning: to track something numerically over time — used here metaphorically about how long Tom has been thinking; not keeping count signals the thought has lived past the point of measurement
to redirect a conversation just before it reaches its intended point — interpersonal, slightly literary; here Marie does it semi-consciously
to be emotionally and cognitively available for a moment that matters — therapy-adjacent vocabulary now common in adult relationship speech
the matter can wait — British idiom of patient deferral, often used to release pressure from a conversation without abandoning the topic
because the moment is dressed up to demand a yes — Tom naming the social pressure of a romantic setting, declining to use it as leverage

content.cultural_note: The British rhythm around proposals — and around any high-stakes interpersonal question — tends to favour deferral over rupture. "It'll keep" is one of the most quietly important phrases in adult British conversation: it acknowledges a topic exists, accepts that the moment isn't right, and promises continuity without demanding immediate resolution. Tom's use of it at the end of this scene is a small generosity — he could have said "fine" with hurt underneath, but "it'll keep" carries no resentment. It's the language of someone who trusts the relationship enough to wait. Note also the specifically British pattern of declining the pressure of the moment itself — Tom doesn't say "I understand," he says "I'd rather you be honest about it than say yes because the wine's open." Naming the pressure dissolves it.
content.grammar_focus: Cleft sentences in emotionally charged speech, introduced for the first time at full B2 weight. Marie says: "That's not a no. That's a — I want to be present for this, and I'm currently not." The structure is essentially a stripped it-cleft — "That's a [pause] [explanation]" — where the subject of the explanation is being constructed in real time. Compare a non-cleft version: "I'm not saying no. I want to be present." Same content, less emotional precision. The cleft does the work of separating refusal from absence. Notice also the present continuous used for an internal location: "I'm about six steps ahead of where you are." Continuous tense to describe mental positioning, not physical action. And Tom's restart pattern — "I think we should — I mean, I'd like us to — " — two layers of hedging on a sentence that never finishes. The grammar of someone preparing to say something difficult, captured exactly. Finally, the conditional "I'd rather you be honest about it than say yes" — would rather + bare infinitive, the adult preference structure that came in at the end of B1 and now sits at full strength.

key_vocabulary.note: Phrasal verb. О мысли, чувстве, вопросе, который не отпускает. "Carrying it around" — один из самых британских способов сказать "я об этом думаю давно".
В выражении "started feeling stupid not to say it" — неловко, нелепо, как будто молчание само становится ошибкой.
Не появляется в диалоге, но описывает структуру разговора. "To defer a question" — отложить вопрос, не закрыв его.
В переносном смысле. Tom не использует романтическую обстановку как leverage — не делает её аргументом за "да".
Не в диалоге, но в культурной заметке. О резком разрыве разговора или отношений.

comprehension_questions.hint: She's already running ahead, working out her answer before the question has fully arrived. She doesn't want to be six steps ahead of him at the moment that matters most. She asks for time so she can be present rather than pre-emptive.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Marie stop Tom before he finishes the sentence?

comprehension_questions.hint: He's naming the social pressure of a romantic dinner — the way the setting itself can push someone toward yes. By naming it, he releases Marie from it. He wants her real answer, not the answer the moment is asking for.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Tom mean by "I'd rather you be honest about it than say yes because the wine's open"?

comprehension_questions.hint: The British idiom for patient deferral. He's saying the question doesn't expire — he's not hurt, he's not withdrawing it. The moment passed, but the thought remains, and he trusts the relationship enough to wait.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Tom say "It'll keep" at the end?

lessons.title_en: Why It Felt Like a Trap

content.title: Why It Felt Like a Trap

dialogue.line: Hello, you're up early.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I never went to sleep properly. Tom nearly proposed last night.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Nearly?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I stopped him mid-sentence.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. I know how it sounds.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: How does it sound to you?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Like I sabotaged a moment that two grown adults had been heading towards for a year. Which I did. But also — Sophie, the second I realised what was about to happen, I felt cornered. Not by Tom. By the shape of the moment.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm. Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And I keep trying to work out what the cornered feeling was about, because Tom is the kindest, most patient — he's literally Tom. There's no version of this where he's the problem.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: So what's the version where he isn't?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I think it's that — yes. That. It's that the question, the way it usually arrives, has a yes-shape and a no-shape and no third shape. And I don't want either of those shapes. I want a fourth shape that doesn't have a name yet.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What would the fourth shape say?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Something like — yes, eventually, but not because you asked tonight. Yes because I chose the moment, not because the moment chose me.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Hmm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What's hmm?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Nothing wrong with it. I'm just thinking about how that's a very specific thing to want. And it might be hard to ask Tom for, because what you're saying is — let me be the one to bring this up, in a year or two, on a Tuesday, when nothing in particular is happening.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Which is a real preference. But it requires Tom to wait without knowing he's waiting.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: He can know he's waiting. I'd tell him.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: All right. Then it's not a trap question. It's a timing question.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's the most clarifying thing anyone has said to me since I woke up at half past five.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I've had practice this year. Eleanor reframed something for me three weeks ago and I haven't stopped thinking about it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Eleanor is good for you.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Eleanor is currently being slightly less in my business. But yes. She's good for me when she chooses to be.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...That's a story for another phone call.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It is. Yours first. What are you going to say to Tom?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm going to ask him if we can talk on Sunday evening, after he's had a day to sit with the fact that I stopped him. And then I'm going to tell him about the fourth shape.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Good plan.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. Thank you. Genuinely.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Always.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a question that was asked of you in a way that made you feel cornered — not by the person asking, but by the shape of the moment itself. What was the fourth shape you wanted? Or — describe a time when you reframed someone's problem for them and the reframe actually landed. What did they call it before, and what did you call it instead?
prompt_phrases: It felt like a trap, but not because of [person].
It was the shape of the moment.
I want a fourth shape that doesn't have a name yet.
It's not a [X] question. It's a [Y] question.
Yes because I chose the moment, not because the moment chose me.

content.situation: Saturday morning. Marie has been awake since six. She made coffee, sat with it, didn't drink it, made fresh coffee. Tom is at football. At nine, Marie rings Sophie. Sophie is in Manchester, in pyjamas, looking at the rain. The call lasts forty minutes. This is the first half — the part where Marie tries to explain why a question she'd been expecting for months felt, when it arrived, like a trap she had to escape rather than an invitation she could accept.

phrase_cle.phrase: It's not a [X] question. It's a [Y] question
phrase_cle.example: — I keep trying to decide if I should take the job. — It's not a job question. It's a question about whether you trust your own restlessness.
phrase_cle.explanation: Двойной cleft-приём, который используется когда говорящий хочет переименовать чужую проблему. Sophie делает это для Marie: "It's not a trap question. It's a timing question." Первый cleft снимает неверную рамку. Второй устанавливает верную. Это инструмент взрослой беседы, особенно психотерапевтической или менторской. На B2 эта структура работает потому, что cleft заставляет слушателя одновременно отвергнуть одно описание и принять другое — это сильнее, чем просто сказать "на самом деле дело в другом".
phrase_cle.translation: Это не вопрос про [X]. Это вопрос про [Y]

expressions.meaning: to feel pushed into a position with no acceptable exit — figurative, often used about emotional situations where the geometry of the moment forecloses options
the implicit structure of a social situation — what it permits, what it forecloses; reflective, slightly literary
the binary structure of a question that admits only two answers — Marie inventing her own metaphor, the kind of phrase B2 speakers create on the fly
to introduce a topic into conversation — phrasal verb, here used about who initiates the proposal moment
to spend time with a difficult fact or feeling rather than reacting to it — therapy-adjacent, common in adult conversation about emotional processing

content.cultural_note: The British conversational mode of working out a feeling out loud — what therapists call "co-regulation" and what friends just call "talking it through" — is a specific verbal genre. It has its own grammar: incomplete sentences, tentative connectives ("I think it's that..."), self-interruptions, and crucially, a willingness from the listener to ask short questions rather than offer long answers. Sophie's role in this call is almost entirely interrogative — "Go on." "What's the fourth shape?" "What would it say?" She offers exactly one reframe, near the end, and only after Marie has done the work of finding her own language. This is the British inheritance of a particular kind of friendship: not advice-giving, not fixing, but accompanying. The reframe lands because it has been earned by twenty minutes of patient questioning. "Eleanor is good for you when she chooses to be" is its own small line — Sophie acknowledging, without explaining, that the cooling Eleanor announced last week is real.
content.grammar_focus: Cleft sentences as emotional precision tools — Marie uses one to find her own meaning. "I think it's that — yes. That. It's that the question, the way it usually arrives, has a yes-shape and a no-shape and no third shape." The repeated "It's that..." structure is Marie locating her own thought in real time. The cleft isn't decorative — it's the grammar of self-clarification. Compare a flatter version: "The question only allows two answers and I don't want either." Same content, less emotional truth. The cleft makes the thought arrive as a discovery rather than a statement. Notice also Sophie's reframe at the turning point: "All right. Then it's not a trap question. It's a timing question." Two it-clefts in sequence — the first negates the wrong frame, the second installs the right one. This is the structure of adult conversational repair. Also: "Yes because I chose the moment, not because the moment chose me" — parallel because-clauses that swap subject and object, expressing agency in compressed form. Finally, modal of preference "would tell him" used as future commitment in conditional context.

key_vocabulary.note: Marie использует про себя — "I sabotaged a moment". Сильное слово, признание ответственности без оправданий.
Прошедшее причастие как прилагательное. "To feel cornered" — стандартная коллокация.
Современное слово из терапии и менеджмента. Marie говорит, что Eleanor "reframed something" для Sophie.
Sophie говорит "a real preference" — признаёт, что желание Marie не каприз, а легитимная позиция.
Та же фраза, что в L2 — Marie повторяет приём Sophie. Сюжетная перекличка между блоками.

comprehension_questions.hint: She's describing the binary structure of the proposal moment — the question seems to permit only acceptance or refusal. She wants a different geometry: a yes that comes later, on her own initiative, not as a response. She invents the metaphor as she's speaking.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Marie mean by "a yes-shape and a no-shape and no third shape"?

comprehension_questions.hint: Marie has been treating it as a trap question — something inherently coercive. Sophie reframes it as a timing question — Marie wants the same answer Tom does, just not on his schedule. The reframe converts the conflict from values to logistics.
comprehension_questions.question: How does Sophie reframe Marie's problem?

comprehension_questions.hint: She wants to give him a full day to sit with the fact that she stopped him — to absorb the surprise rather than react to it. It's a small generosity that protects the conversation she actually wants to have. Marie is not avoiding; she's pacing.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Marie not want to talk to Tom until Sunday evening?

content.title: Anna in the Hallway

dialogue.line: Anna.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I'm fine. I'm going.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I am. I just — give me a second.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Take it.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Anna.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: He's not going to know I'm gone.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: ...He's three months old.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I know how old he is.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I know you do. Sorry. Bad answer.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: It's not that I think he should miss me. He shouldn't. He's a baby. It's that — Carlos. I think I've been preparing for the wrong thing.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: What did you prepare for?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Missing him. Crying in the loo at lunchtime. The whole — script of it. I prepared for all of that. I didn't prepare for the part where he might not need me back the way I need to come back.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: And I know what I sound like. I sound like a woman who wants to be more important to her own baby.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: You don't sound like that.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I do a bit.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: All right. A bit. But also — Anna. You have been the entire shape of his world for three months. And in two weeks I'm taking three months off and he'll be the entire shape of my world. None of that means he picks one of us. It means he gets two parents who keep moving in and out of his foreground.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: That's a kind way to say it.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: It's also true.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos. Can I ask you something and you tell me honestly?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: When I'm gone today — does some part of you feel relieved that you'll be alone with him?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: ...Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Thank you for not lying about that.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Anna. I love you. And I love him. And I have been waiting for the version of him that's just mine for a bit. That's not against you. It's parallel to you.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Parallel to me.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I think I needed to hear someone use that word.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Right. Go to work. He'll be here when you get back. So will I.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: ...Okay. Carlos?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: If I cry in the loo at lunchtime, it'll be because of this conversation, not because of him.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Fair enough.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a moment when you returned to something — work, a relationship, a place — and discovered you had been preparing for the wrong version of it. What had you imagined? What did you find? Or — describe a time when someone gave you a single word that reorganised how you felt about a difficult situation. What was the word, and what had you been calling the thing before?
prompt_phrases: I think I've been preparing for the wrong thing.
I didn't prepare for the part where [unexpected thing].
It's not against you. It's parallel to you.
I needed to hear someone use that word.
Fair enough.

content.situation: Monday morning, 7.40am. Anna's first day back at work after maternity leave. Carlos has Mateo, who is three months old and currently asleep against his shoulder. Anna is fully dressed, holding her bag, halfway between the bedroom and the front door. She has been there for four minutes without moving. Carlos notices but doesn't speak first. When she does speak, what comes out is not what either of them was expecting.

phrase_cle.phrase: It's not against you. It's parallel to you
phrase_cle.example: — You're spending a lot of time on the new project. — It's not against you. It's parallel to you. I want both.
phrase_cle.explanation: Структура снимающая ложную бинарность. Carlos использует её, чтобы отделить своё чувство — облегчение, что он будет один с Mateo — от любого предательства Anna. "Against" предполагает противопоставление; "parallel" предполагает сосуществование. На B2 эта геометрическая метафора работает в личных и рабочих разговорах, где люди ошибочно читают чужое "я хочу X" как "я не хочу тебя". Anna прямо называет: ей нужно было это слово. Это сценическое признание силы правильного слова в правильный момент.
phrase_cle.translation: Это не против тебя. Это рядом с тобой

expressions.meaning: the entire imagined sequence of an event — used here for the emotional choreography Anna had rehearsed in her head and is now finding inadequate
to alternate between being central and peripheral in someone's attention — figurative, gentle, parental
expressions.expression: to move in and out of someone's foreground

expressions.meaning: occurring alongside you rather than in opposition to you — the precise word Anna needed; it reframes Carlos's feelings as compatible with hers
expression of acceptance or mild concession — quintessentially British male register, used by Carlos throughout the course
to be the totality of their experience — figurative, used about parents and small babies
expressions.expression: to be the entire shape of someone's world

content.cultural_note: British paternity leave has shifted in the last decade. The standard statutory paternity leave is two weeks, but Shared Parental Leave (introduced in 2015) allows couples to divide up to fifty weeks of leave between them after the first two weeks. Carlos and Anna have chosen this route — Anna takes the first three months, then Carlos takes three. The arrangement is still relatively uncommon in practice (about 2% of eligible couples actually use it), partly because of pay structures and partly because of cultural inertia. For couples who do use it, the moment Anna is in here — handing the baby over and going back to work — is statistically more likely to surface complicated feelings on both sides than the more conventional arrangement. Carlos's relief at having Mateo to himself isn't a private failing; it's a fairly common pattern that simply doesn't get spoken because most fathers never get the chance to feel it. Naming it, parallel to Anna's grief, is the small and specific kindness this scene asks of him.
content.grammar_focus: Modal perfect for retrospective re-evaluation, central to B2 emotional register. Anna says: "I think I've been preparing for the wrong thing." Present perfect continuous looking back over a period that is now ending. Then: "I didn't prepare for the part where he might not need me back the way I need to come back." The construction "might not need" — modal of possibility about the present, embedded in a past clause about preparation. This is the grammar of someone realising, mid-sentence, that the model she'd built doesn't fit reality. Notice also Carlos's careful sentence construction in his longest reply: "You have been the entire shape of his world for three months. And in two weeks I'm taking three months off and he'll be the entire shape of my world." Past perfect continuous, present continuous as future, then simple future — three time horizons in three sentences, each one meeting Anna where she is. The male register stays throughout: short replies, single-syllable acknowledgements ("Mm." "Yeah." "Fair enough."), and one long sentence where the emotional work happens. Carlos uses paragraphs sparingly, which is what makes them carry weight when he does.

key_vocabulary.note: British casual for toilet. Anna говорит "crying in the loo at lunchtime" — стандартная фраза о трудном рабочем дне.
Не в диалоге, но описывает что Anna делала. "Rehearsing the script" — готовить эмоциональный сценарий заранее.
Из живописи и фотографии. Carlos использует переносно: "in and out of his foreground" — то в центре внимания, то на периферии.
Прошедшее причастие как прилагательное. Anna прямо спрашивает Carlos о его relief — и ценит честность ответа.
Геометрический термин в эмоциональном использовании. Ключевое слово урока — без него фраза Carlos не работает.

comprehension_questions.hint: She'd prepared for missing Mateo — the conventional script of a mother returning to work. She wasn't prepared for the asymmetry: that her need to come back to him might be larger than his need to have her back. The mismatch is what's freezing her in the hallway.
comprehension_questions.question: What had Anna been preparing for, and what was she not prepared for?

comprehension_questions.hint: Anna asks for honesty and he respects the question. Lying would protect her from a small ache but cost the relationship a real conversation. Carlos's male register usually shortens replies, but here a single word — "Yes" — does the work of a paragraph.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Carlos admit he feels relieved?

comprehension_questions.hint: It separates Carlos's wanting time alone with Mateo from any rejection of her. Anna had been holding his feeling as opposed to hers; the word lets her hold it as alongside hers. Same room, different needs, both legitimate.
comprehension_questions.question: What does the word "parallel" do for Anna?

content.title: Anna's Story, Then Mine

dialogue.line: How was Sunday with Tom?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Better than I deserved. We agreed on something. I'll get to that. Can I tell you about Anna first?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Anna?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Carlos rang me Monday. Anna had a hard morning going back to work. Properly hard.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Oh.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: She told Carlos she'd prepared for missing Mateo, but not for the possibility that Mateo wouldn't really notice she was gone. Which Carlos said wrecked her for about four minutes in the hallway.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...That's a real feeling.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's a real feeling. And Carlos handled it well, apparently. He told her he's been quietly waiting for the version of Mateo that's just his for a bit. That his wanting time alone with the baby isn't against her, it's parallel to her.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That's a good word.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's an excellent word. And Anna said she needed to hear someone use it. And then she went to work.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: How is she now?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Carlos says she's fine. Better than fine, actually — once the first morning was done she said the office felt like a holiday.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie, I keep thinking about that word. Parallel.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Why?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because — okay. Sunday evening. Tom and I sat down. I told him about the fourth shape. The yes that's not now. And I was braced for him to be hurt. He wasn't. He was — interested. Like, intellectually interested. He said, "so you want to be the one who says it, when you're the one who's ready." I said yes. He said, "how long?" I said two years. He said, "all right."
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Just like that?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Just like that. And Sophie, the relief I felt was so big it scared me a little. Because the relief was — he's not asking me to absorb his pace. We're allowed to be on different timelines and still be us. We're parallel.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. You started with Anna's story for a reason.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What's the reason?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because Anna's relief and my relief are the same shape. And I needed to tell you about hers before mine, because mine still feels — embarrassing? Ridiculous? Something. Hers I can describe properly. Mine I can only describe by sliding it into hers.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Why is yours embarrassing?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because Anna had a baby. The stakes are obvious. I just didn't want to get married this winter. The stakes are — are they stakes?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: They're stakes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...All right.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: And you don't have to talk about your relief through Anna's relief. You can talk about it directly. I'm here for both.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. When did you become someone who says things like that?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Helena. Indirectly. She made me stop softening things, so now when I want to say something kind I can't smuggle it in past three apologies. I have to just say it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Helena sounds frightening.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: She is. She's also — useful. Two things can be true.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...All right. Direct version. I'm relieved that I'm not getting married this winter. I'm relieved that Tom wasn't hurt. I'm relieved that being with him doesn't require us to be on the same timeline. And I'm a little embarrassed about how much relief there is.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That's a lot of relief.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You're allowed it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a feeling you've only been able to describe by sliding it into someone else's story first. Why was the indirect route safer? What did saying it directly cost you? Or — describe a moment when a friend named the thing you were doing without naming it as a criticism. How did you feel about being seen?
prompt_phrases: Can I tell you about [other person] first?
Their relief and mine are the same shape.
I can only describe mine by sliding it into theirs.
Two things can be true.
I'm a little embarrassed about how much relief there is.

content.situation: Wednesday evening. Marie called Sophie on Saturday morning about Tom; she talked to Tom on Sunday evening; they agreed on a timeline; she has been carrying the relief of that agreement for three days. Tonight Marie rings Sophie again — but starts with a different story. Carlos rang Marie two days ago about Anna's first day back. Marie tells Sophie about Anna first, and only halfway through the call does Sophie notice that Marie is using Anna's story to say something about her own.

phrase_cle.example: — Helena sounds frightening. — She is. She's also useful. Two things can be true.
phrase_cle.explanation: Современная взрослая фраза, отвергающая бинарную логику. Используется когда говорящий хочет удержать два, на первый взгляд, противоречащих описания одного человека или ситуации. "He's kind. He's also exhausting. Two things can be true." На B2 это инструмент защиты сложности против упрощения. Часто появляется в разговорах про отношения, работу, политику. Пришло из терапевтического словаря, но теперь используется широко.
phrase_cle.translation: Две вещи могут быть правдой одновременно

expressions.meaning: to express something hard to say directly by routing it through a more legitimate-seeming version — Marie's metaphor for using Anna's story to talk about her own
expressions.expression: to slide one thing into another

expressions.meaning: to take on another person's tempo at the cost of your own — common in adult relationship discourse
to deliver a direct message indirectly, hiding it behind softening — Sophie's wry self-description
the seriousness of the situation doesn't need to be argued for — used by Marie about Anna's situation, then questioned about her own
an acceptance that contradictory descriptions of the same person or situation can coexist — modern adult shorthand for non-binary thinking

content.cultural_note: Marie's habit of routing her own difficult feeling through someone else's story first — what she calls "sliding it in" — is a recognisable conversational pattern, especially common among women who feel their own stakes are insufficiently legitimate. It has a name in psychotherapy ("affective displacement") but Marie doesn't need the term to recognise the pattern. Sophie's intervention is small but precise: she names the move, asks why, accepts the embarrassment as legitimate, and offers permission to describe directly. The line "You can talk about it directly. I'm here for both" is doing two jobs at once — granting legitimacy to Marie's relief, and gently calling out the smuggling. Note how Sophie attributes her own directness to Helena ("indirectly"), and how Marie immediately registers Helena as frightening. The two women are tracking each other's growth — Sophie has gained something from a difficult mentor, and Marie can see the gain even before she has met the source.
content.grammar_focus: Reported speech for narrative compression, used here as the structural backbone of half the dialogue. Marie reports Carlos reporting Anna: "Carlos says she's fine." "She told Carlos she'd prepared for missing Mateo." Notice the backshift — present "I'm fine" becomes past "she's fine"; past perfect "she had prepared" becomes "she'd prepared." The sentence "so you want to be the one who says it, when you're the one who's ready" is direct quotation inside Marie's report — Marie chooses to keep Tom's exact words rather than backshift them, because the wording carries the meaning. This switching between reported speech (for summary) and direct quotation (for emotional precision) is a B2 narrative skill. Notice also the parallel structure of Marie's final list: "I'm relieved that... I'm relieved that... I'm relieved that... And I'm a little embarrassed about how much relief there is." Anaphoric repetition of "I'm relieved" builds to the small twist in the last clause. This is Marie obeying Sophie's request to describe her relief directly — and Marie does it by stacking, which is how someone talks when they've been holding a feeling for three days.

key_vocabulary.note: Marie говорит "wrecked her for about four minutes" — сильное слово, разговорное. О временной эмоциональной потере контроля.
Прошедшее причастие. "I was braced for him to be hurt" — стандартная конструкция о готовности к плохому.
Marie применяет к собственному облегчению — "my relief still feels embarrassing". Признание стыда без оправдания.
Sophie использует переносно: "smuggle a kind thing in past three apologies". Очень B2 переносное использование.
Из азартных игр в общий язык. "The stakes are obvious" — серьёзность ситуации очевидна. Marie сомневается в собственных ставках, Sophie подтверждает их.

comprehension_questions.hint: Anna's stakes feel legitimate — a baby, a return to work. Marie's stakes feel less so to her — just a delayed wedding. Routing her own relief through Anna's makes it more describable. Sophie notices the move and names it.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Marie tell Sophie about Anna before talking about herself?

comprehension_questions.hint: He doesn't argue, doesn't negotiate, doesn't ask Marie to justify the timeline. He treats her preference as legitimate without requiring her to defend it. The brevity of his reply is the substance of his agreement — no haggling, just acceptance.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Tom's response "all right" reveal about him?

comprehension_questions.hint: Sophie has stopped smuggling kind things in past apologies. Helena's directness, absorbed over weeks of mentorship, has changed how Sophie speaks — including how she speaks to Marie. The change is visible enough that Marie comments on it. Marie reads Helena as frightening; Sophie acknowledges that and adds "useful". Two things can be true.
comprehension_questions.question: How has Sophie changed, according to Marie's question "when did you become someone who says things like that"?

content.title: The Sunday Conversation

dialogue.line: Tom. Thank you for waiting. I owe you a sentence.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Take your time.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I've thought about it. I want to marry you. I don't want to get married this winter.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...All right.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: That's not an evasion. It's an actual answer. I want to be the one who says when, and I'm not ready to say when yet. I'd like to be ready in about two years.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Hmm.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: What's hmm?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm working out whether I'm hurt. I don't think I am. But I want to check.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Take a minute. I owe you a minute back.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Okay. I'm not hurt. I want to ask one question.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Two years from now is two years from now. Is the date for the date, or is the date the placeholder for working out whether you'll ever say yes?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...That's a sharper question than I was expecting.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I know it is. Sorry.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Don't apologise. It's a fair question. The honest answer is — I don't know yet. I think it's the date for the date. But I can't promise that. What I can promise is that I'll tell you the moment I know it isn't.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: All right. That's what I needed.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: It is?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. I don't need a guaranteed wedding in two years. I need to know that if you change your mind, you'll say so before I find out by accident.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...That's a low bar.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It's not a low bar. It's the bar. People who don't tell each other things ruin themselves over a couple of years. I've watched it happen.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: So you want to be the one who says it, when you're the one who's ready.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I — what?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Sorry. I had it the other way round in my head. So — I want to be the one who says it, when I'm the one who's ready. And you want to know if I'm changing my mind, in real time, not later.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Then that's what we agree.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: All right.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom. One more thing.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I love you. I should have said that before any of the other sentences.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You said it by sitting down.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...You're a soft touch.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I am. Pour the tea, it's getting cold.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a conversation where you and another person agreed to something difficult and the wording of the agreement mattered more than the content. What was the question that pinned the agreement down? Or — describe a time when you tried to summarise what someone wanted and got the pronouns wrong. How did you correct yourself?
prompt_phrases: I owe you a sentence.
Is the [date/decision] for the [date/decision], or is it a placeholder for [other thing]?
I want to be the one who [verb], when I'm the one who's ready.
What I can promise is [X].
I'll tell you the moment I know it isn't.

content.situation: Sunday evening of the week before the L10 phone call. This lesson is the scene Marie summarised to Sophie — given here in full because the language of how an agreement is reached matters as much as the agreement itself. Marie made tea. Tom came in from the garden. They sat at the kitchen table, the same table where the wine had been open four nights ago. Marie speaks first.

phrase_cle.phrase: I want to be the one who [verb]
phrase_cle.example: — I'll book the holiday. — No — I want to be the one who books it. I've been thinking about it for weeks.
phrase_cle.explanation: Wh-cleft, который заявляет агентство (право быть субъектом действия). Разница между "I'll do it" и "I want to be the one who does it" — большая. Первое — функциональное согласие. Второе — требование роли. Marie использует эту структуру дважды: один раз с ошибкой в местоимениях, потом с правильной. На B2 это инструмент переговоров о ролях в отношениях, в работе, в семье. Часто появляется в обсуждениях родительства ("I want to be the one who picks her up from school"), в карьере ("I want to be the one who tells the client"), в дружбе ("I want to be the one who says we should stop").

expressions.meaning: I have something specific I need to say to you — Marie framing the conversation as a debt she's come to repay; quietly literary
the actual date as opposed to a placeholder — Tom's compact way of asking whether the timeline is real or symbolic
to slowly destroy a relationship through accumulated unsaid things — long-time-frame phrasing common in mature adult speech
expressions.expression: to ruin yourselves over a couple of years

expressions.meaning: someone easily swayed by affection — affectionate British insult, used here as a tease
the action contained the message before any words were said — the kind of thing Tom says rather than Marie
expressions.expression: you said it by sitting down

content.cultural_note: Tom's question — "Is the date for the date, or is the date the placeholder for working out whether you'll ever say yes?" — is the kind of sentence that sounds calmer than it is. He's asking whether the two-year timeline is genuine or a graceful way of breaking up over twenty-four months. The directness is unusual: most people in this position would let the question hang. Tom asks it because he understands that ambiguous timelines do more damage than honest disagreements — "people who don't tell each other things ruin themselves over a couple of years." This is the British male register at its most useful: short sentences, watchful patience, then one piercing question delivered without ceremony. The agreement they reach isn't romantic. It isn't even reassuring. What it is, is workable. And the closing line — "Pour the tea, it's getting cold" — is how British couples seal a serious conversation: by returning immediately to the small domestic task that was waiting underneath.
content.grammar_focus: Cleft sentences for negotiated agreement. Marie attempts to summarise their understanding by inverting subject and object — "so you want to be the one who says it, when you're the one who's ready" — and then realises mid-sentence she's flipped the pronouns. The correction "I want to be the one who says it, when I'm the one who's ready" is the actual cleft. Notice the structure: "I want to be the one who [verb]" — wh-cleft with relative clause, used to claim agency over a specific role. This is the grammar of negotiating positions in a relationship: not "I'll do it" but "I want to be the one who does it." The two phrases mean different things. Notice also Tom's question structure — "Is the date for the date, or is the date the placeholder for [X]?" — a parallel-construction either-or, where both halves use the same pattern but ask very different questions. This is precision under emotional weight. Finally, Marie's promise structure: "What I can promise is that I'll tell you the moment I know it isn't." Wh-cleft + future commitment + "the moment" as conjunction (synonymous with "as soon as," but more emotionally specific). The grammar of a contract being drafted in real time.

key_vocabulary.note: Formal noun. Marie заявляет: "That's not an evasion" — её ответ не способ избежать вопроса.
Из IT и документов. Tom использует переносно: дата может быть placeholder для несостоявшегося решения.
Tom говорит "I don't need a guaranteed wedding" — выбирает не требовать твёрдых обещаний.
Сильный глагол. "To ruin yourselves" — saturated word, медленное разрушение через несказанное.
British idiom. Marie дразнит Tom — "You're a soft touch". Affectionate insult.

comprehension_questions.hint: Whether the two-year timeline is genuine or a slow way of saying no. Marie can't promise the date is real — but she can promise she'll tell him in real time if her mind changes. Tom accepts that as enough.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Tom actually checking when he asks "is the date for the date, or is it a placeholder?"

comprehension_questions.hint: She gets the pronouns the wrong way round — "so you want to be the one who says it, when you're the one who's ready." The flip reveals she was rehearsing Tom's role rather than her own. Once she corrects to "I want to be the one who says it," the agreement is genuinely Marie's.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Marie correct herself when summarising the agreement?

comprehension_questions.hint: Marie apologised for not saying "I love you" before the practical sentences. Tom is pointing out that the act of bringing the conversation, with tea, on a Sunday evening, was already a declaration. Words follow actions; the actions were enough.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Tom mean by "You said it by sitting down"?

content.title: Telling the Family

dialogue.line: Mum. You've gone quiet.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I have. Sorry.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Are you disappointed?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...No. I'm working out how to say something I haven't said before, and I'm a bit slow at it.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Take your time. Tom and Dad will be at the fence for at least twenty minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. When I married your father, I was twenty-two. He'd asked me twice. The first time I said no. The second time my mother — your nan — sat me down at the kitchen table for forty minutes. By the end of it I'd said yes.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...You've never told me this.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I've never told anyone. Your father knows the broad outline, not the forty-minute version. He thinks I came round to the idea on my own.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm not saying I regret marrying him. I want to be clear about that. He is — he's been good. The marriage worked out. But I have always — always — wondered what would have happened if I'd held the no for one more month.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: And when you said just now that you and Tom have decided to wait — I felt something I wasn't expecting to feel. Not disappointment. The opposite of disappointment. Like — finally. Someone in this family is going to say when, instead of having when said to her.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I know.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: How long have you been carrying this?
dialogue.speaker: Marie
