Industry conferences favour talks that arrive at their substance through a slow descent rather than a vertical drop — a short anecdote, a small concession to the audience, a re-establishment of why anyone should listen. Sophie's draft opening is doing the wrong job: it is a written-essay opening, not a spoken-talk opening. Marie's instinct is that of a designer trained on user attention rather than legal precision. The two skills overlap less than people assume. Note also Marie's pragmatic advice about "send it Monday." Hiding work until it is perfect is one of the most common professional failure modes, and Marie names it without softening. Her authority on this point is real — she runs a design team, she has seen the pattern repeatedly. "I'm not your peer here" earlier in the call was honest about her lack of legal authority; her authority on the editing question is unqualified, and she uses it directly. This is the texture of long British female friendship: each woman knows precisely what the other is good at, and feels no need to hedge around the boundary.
content.grammar_focus: Hedging at the upper register, introduced for the first time in the course. Sophie's draft sentence demonstrates almost every device available: parenthetical clause ("which is to say..."), nested definition ("in the middle of being asked by law to become something it has not yet finished becoming"), and the resultative "none of which fully replace each other." This is a B2-to-C1 register being modelled — and then dismantled by Marie's response. The grammatical lesson is the contrast: Sophie's revised sentences are short, paratactic, present continuous ("is being asked") with deliberate omission of the qualifying clauses she trained herself to include. Notice the difference between "is in the middle of being asked by law to become something it has not yet finished becoming" (passive + present continuous + present perfect, three tenses stacked) and "is being asked to change" (passive present continuous, single move). Both are B2 grammar; the skill is knowing which to deploy when. Notice also Marie's structural advice: "the big sentence can come at minute four, when I trust you. Right now I don't trust you, because I just walked in." Modal of permission (can come) + temporal subordinate clause (when I trust you) + present simple with adverbial (just walked in). The grammar of trust as a function of time.

key_vocabulary.note: Альпинистская метафора. "To have a foothold" о понимании — место, с которого можно двигаться дальше.
Анатомическая метафора в редактуре. "The spine of a sentence" — несущая конструкция фразы.
В переносном смысле о работе. "Hiding it from her" — Sophie прячет черновик от Helena. Marie это диагностирует.
В рабочем контексте. "You have a week to fix things" — стандартная фраза о редактуре.
Marie советует Sophie "reread the wedding speech and ask the same instinct to the conference talk." Глагол "ask" к неодушевлённому — не "use", не "apply", а "ask" — слегка литературное.

comprehension_questions.hint: It is structurally beautiful but functionally inaccessible — ninety words before the listener has anywhere to land. As a written sentence in an essay it might work; as the opening of a talk to a person who has just walked into a room, it has no foothold.
comprehension_questions.question: What is wrong with Sophie's first sentence, according to Marie?

comprehension_questions.hint: The big sentence can come at minute four, when the listener trusts the speaker. The first three minutes have to earn trust through accessibility. Sophie has the right material but the wrong order: she has put the most ornate thing first.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Marie's structural advice about big sentences?

comprehension_questions.hint: Hiding work until it is perfect is incompatible with deadlines. "Good enough is what gets done by Friday afternoon." Sending it Monday gives Helena two days to give notes and Sophie the rest of the week to act on them. The maths of revision.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Marie tell Sophie to send the draft to Helena on Monday?

content.title: Five Minutes Before

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Helena. I didn't know you were coming.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I wasn't going to. Then I decided I should. Two minutes. One observation.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You're going to want to apologise once at the start. "Thank you for having me, sorry about the rain, I know everyone's tired after lunch." Don't.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: ...That was actually in my opening.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know it was. I read the draft you sent me on Monday. The sorry-about-the-rain version is in line three.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I cut it on Wednesday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Did you. Show me your card.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: It's gone. Good. Don't put it back when your nerves arrive in the next four minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Why does it matter? It's three sentences. The audience will forget.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The audience will not remember the words. They will remember whether you began as someone who is going to tell them something, or as someone who is grateful to have been allowed in. Begin as the first thing.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: ...Right.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'll be at the back. Don't look for me. Look at the third row, slightly to the left. There's a man in a green jumper. Tell the talk to him.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Why him?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because he is taking notes already and the talk hasn't started. He's going to listen. Use him as your floor.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Helena.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Don't thank me yet. Thank me at four o'clock if it goes well.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: [The talk happens. Fifteen minutes. The man in the green jumper takes notes throughout. Sophie does not apologise at the start. Q&A begins.]
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Thank you, that was very useful. My question is — how do you advise clients who genuinely cannot tell, during a transition, what their disclosure obligations actually are?
dialogue.speaker: Audience 1

dialogue.line: It would perhaps be more accurate to say that during a transition almost no client can tell with full confidence. The honest answer is that you advise them to over-disclose to the regulator and under-disclose to the public, in that order, and to assume the legal definition of "material" will be litigated against them within five years.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That sounds quite cynical.
dialogue.speaker: Audience 2

dialogue.line: I'd say it sounds clinical rather than cynical. There is, I think, a case to be made that clinical advice is what clients are paying for. The cynicism would be telling them comfort instead of accuracy.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: How do you square that with — sorry, this is a more general question — with public interest?
dialogue.speaker: Audience 3

dialogue.line: I don't square it. I think the public interest case lives outside the lawyer's office and is fought by other instruments — journalists, regulators, parliamentary committees. My job is to advise my client accurately. The public interest is served by the existence of the other instruments, not by my pretending to be one of them.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...That's an unusually direct answer.
dialogue.speaker: Audience 3

dialogue.line: I should be cautious to suggest it's the only direct answer available. It's the one I happen to hold.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: We have time for one more — yes, at the back.
dialogue.speaker: Chair

dialogue.line: Could you say a word about your own — is it firm policy not to advise on PR alongside the legal work?
dialogue.speaker: Audience 4

dialogue.line: It is not firm policy. It is my policy. I make the distinction in cases where I think the legal argument and the public communications strategy ask different things of me. My firm has been generous about this. I am not speaking for them today; I'm speaking as a practitioner who has drawn a particular line.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Thank you, Sophie. We'll break for coffee.
dialogue.speaker: Chair

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a moment when you were given a single, specific piece of last-minute advice before something high-stakes — a presentation, an interview, a difficult conversation. What was the advice? Did you follow it? Or — describe an answer you gave in front of an audience or a panel where you decided to be direct rather than diplomatic. What did the directness cost? What did it earn?
prompt_phrases: Two minutes. One observation.
Use [person] as your floor.
It would perhaps be more accurate to say [X].
There is, I think, a case to be made that [argument].
It is not [firm/general] policy. It is my policy.

content.situation: Twelve days later. Manchester Central Conference Centre. Day one of the energy transition conference. Sophie's session is at 2.45pm. At 2.40pm she is in the corridor outside the auditorium, holding a glass of water, wearing the navy suit. Helena finds her. Helena is not chairing this session and was not, strictly speaking, expected to come. The first half of this lesson is the corridor. The second half is the Q&A after Sophie's talk — the talk itself happens in the gap between the two halves and is left to the listener's imagination.

phrase_cle.phrase: It would perhaps be more accurate to say
phrase_cle.example: — Are you saying the policy was a failure? — It would perhaps be more accurate to say it was overtaken by events.
phrase_cle.explanation: Тройной хедж формального публичного регистра. "Would" — модальное условие; "perhaps" — наречие неуверенности; "more accurate" — компаратив, который не настаивает на полной точности. Вместе они создают пространство, в котором говорящий может предложить новую формулировку без обвинения предыдущей в ошибке. На B2 эта структура — рабочий инструмент профессиональной речи: интервью, дебаты, академические дискуссии, публичные ответы. Звучит как речь взрослого, который уверен в своей мысли, но знает, что аудитория не должна чувствовать давление. Sophie использует это в Q&A — это её новый голос.

expressions.meaning: rest your speaking weight on a single attentive person in the audience — Helena's compact stage advice
high-register hedging used to introduce a precise reframing — formal, slightly academic, deployed in professional Q&A
expressions.expression: It would perhaps be more accurate to say

expressions.meaning: interpolated hedge that holds an argument open without committing to it absolutely — the upper register of professional argument
expressions.expression: There is, I think, a case to be made that

expressions.meaning: I don't reconcile the apparent tension; I leave it as it is — direct response that refuses the framing of the question
the position I personally take, without claiming it as the universal one — modest qualifier on a strong statement
expressions.expression: the one I happen to hold

content.cultural_note: British professional Q&A is its own genre. The hedging structures Sophie uses — "it would perhaps be more accurate to say", "there is, I think, a case to be made" — are not decorative; they are the literal grammar of how positions are taken in public legal, academic, and policy discourse. The function of the hedge is to hold the speaker's claim without forcing the audience to take an opposite position. A flat declarative ("that's wrong") collapses the argumentative space; a hedged claim ("I should be cautious to suggest the opposite is self-evident") keeps the room open. This is B2 listening territory and C1 production territory. Helena's stage advice — "begin as the person who is going to tell them something, not as the person who is grateful to have been allowed in" — is structural feedback that shapes the entire register of the talk. Note also the man in the green jumper: a real piece of public-speaking wisdom. Speakers who try to address the whole room often address none of it; locating one attentive face and speaking to that person allows the voice to settle. Helena, of course, has scouted the room before finding Sophie. The corridor scene takes ninety seconds. The preparation behind it took fifteen minutes.
content.grammar_focus: Hedging at the upper register — the grammar of public professional argument. Each device deserves naming: "It would perhaps be more accurate to say..." — modal of probability + adverb of qualification + comparative adjective. Three layers of softening, holding the speaker's claim without surrendering it. "There is, I think, a case to be made that..." — existential there + interpolated I think + passive infinitive. The interpolation is the move: "I think" sits inside the sentence rather than at its start, which makes it modify the assertion without weakening its grammatical spine. "I should be cautious to suggest..." — modal should + infinitive of caution. This is C1-adjacent grammar; B2 learners encounter it here as comprehension target, not yet as a production target. Notice also Sophie's deliberate directness in the last answer: "It is not firm policy. It is my policy." Two it-clefts, second negating, second installing — the same structure she has been using since L8. The structure is now her professional voice. Helena's corridor coaching uses imperative + modal of prediction: "Don't put it back when your nerves arrive." Future continuous of physical sensation framed as inevitable but resistible.

key_vocabulary.note: Helena диагностирует частую ошибку: благодарность-извинение в первой минуте. Снимает авторитет.
key_vocabulary.word: to apologise once at the start

key_vocabulary.note: Helena: "Show me your card." Карточки с тезисами для выступления. Британское разговорное.
Метафорическое использование. "Use him as your floor" — Helena's оригинальное выражение, вероятно её собственное.
Юридический глагол. Sophie: "will be litigated against them" — passive, будущее.
В переносном смысле. "How do you square that with [X]" — стандартная фраза в дебатах. Sophie отказывается от рамки: "I don't square it."

comprehension_questions.hint: Because the audience will not remember the words but will remember whether the speaker began as someone with something to say or as someone grateful to have been allowed in. The opening sets the register of authority for the whole talk. Beginning small structurally undercuts the rest of the fifteen minutes.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Helena tell Sophie not to apologise at the start?

comprehension_questions.hint: She doesn't see the lawyer's role as serving public interest directly. Public interest is served by other instruments — journalists, regulators, parliamentary committees. Her job is to advise her client accurately. Pretending to be a public-interest instrument while serving as a lawyer would be the dishonest position.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Sophie's position on public interest, in her answer to Audience 3?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is being precise. Her firm has not made it a policy that lawyers refuse PR work; she has made it her own decision case by case, and the firm has tolerated that decision. Conflating the two would over-claim institutional support and would be inaccurate. Sophie is now someone who guards the line between personal practice and firm position publicly.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie distinguish between firm policy and her own policy in the final answer?

content.title: The Long Pause

dialogue.line: Tom. Are you going to say anything?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Yes. Sorry. I'm thinking.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: About what?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: About what you told me.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Could you think out loud, please? The silence is making me feel worse, not better.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Right. Okay. The reason I was quiet is that I don't believe in the version of comfort where I say "I'm sure it'll be fine" thirty seconds after you've told me you think you've lost a client. I don't think that's comfort. I think it's tidying up.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: But I should have said that out loud, instead of just doing it. Sorry.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: No — Tom — that's actually — I didn't know that's what the silence was.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm. What did you think it was?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I thought you were — disappointed. Or working out whether I was over-reacting. Or trying to find a kind way to point out what I should have done differently.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: None of those.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Yeah. I see that now.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Tell me what happened in more than three sentences. I'd rather hear it slowly.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...All right. The client is the museum, the new wing. They asked me three weeks ago to put together a pitch for the wayfinding system and the gallery signage. I had four other things on. I did the pitch. The pitch was — fine. Not great. The slides were tighter than they needed to be. And the client's marketing director, Thora, asked me — about twenty minutes in — what the system would do for visitors who couldn't read English at all, and I had thought about it but I hadn't built it into the proposal because the brief didn't mention it, and so I improvised, and the improvisation was — visible. Thora knew I hadn't prepared it. And once she knew, the rest of the questions had a different texture.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: And the worst part is I knew, walking in, that I hadn't fully prepared. I knew it. I just didn't have time to fix it, and I told myself the brief didn't ask for it, and I let myself believe that was a good enough reason.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: You don't have to do anything with that. I'm just — saying it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm not doing anything with it. I'm listening.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...Okay.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. One question.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Have you ever lost a pitch before?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Of course I have. Lots.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: And did the previous ones change anything that mattered?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: That's a kinder version of what I was trying not to say to myself.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I know. That's why I waited a bit before I said it.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I was angry at you for the first thirty seconds of the silence. I should tell you that, in case it leaks out later.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'd already noticed.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...Of course you had.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a silence in a conversation that felt like the wrong thing, until you found out what it actually was. Or — describe a failure you tracked retrospectively, sentence by sentence, identifying the small choices that produced it. How did saying it out loud change your relationship to it?
prompt_phrases: Could you think out loud, please? The silence is making me feel worse.
I knew, walking in, that I hadn't fully prepared.
I let myself believe that was a good enough reason.
I'd rather hear it slowly.
I'd already noticed.

content.situation: Wednesday evening, the same week as Sophie's conference. Marie has had a difficult day at work. She gave a forty-five-minute pitch to a major client this afternoon. The pitch did not go well — not catastrophically, but the client asked questions she should have anticipated and didn't, and the decision will be made next week and Marie thinks they have lost it. She has come home, taken her shoes off, told Tom what happened in three sentences, and then waited for him to say something. He has not said anything for about ninety seconds. Marie is starting to find the silence unbearable.

phrase_cle.phrase: I let myself believe that was a good enough reason
phrase_cle.example: — Why didn't you mention the deadline? — I told myself it wasn't relevant. I let myself believe that was a good enough reason. It wasn't.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень B2 структура для саморефлексии о небольшой нечестности. "To let oneself believe" — признание, что вера была не пассивной (не "I believed"), а активным разрешением себе поверить. Marie использует это про свой пресс-релиз: она знала, что не подготовилась, сказала себе, что бриф не просил, и разрешила себе принять это как оправдание. Структура часто появляется в зрелых разговорах о провалах, ошибках, выборах под давлением. Не самообвинение, а точная диагностика. "Let myself" — рефлексивное использование глагола разрешения.
phrase_cle.translation: Я позволила себе поверить, что это было достаточным основанием

expressions.meaning: the false comfort of premature reassurance — Tom's diagnostic phrase for the kind of comforting that serves the comforter
the tone and quality of subsequent questions changed, in ways the speaker felt but couldn't articulate at the time — sensory metaphor for social register shift
expressions.expression: the questions had a different texture

expressions.meaning: self-diagnostic phrase about a small dishonesty made plausible by time pressure — common in adult retrospection on failure
expressions.expression: I let myself believe that was a good enough reason

expressions.meaning: preemptive disclosure of a feeling that might otherwise surface unhelpfully — adult relationship hygiene
expressions.expression: in case it leaks out later

expressions.meaning: compact acknowledgement that the speaker had registered the other person's emotion before it was named — Tom's male-register variant of "I see you"

content.cultural_note: Tom's silence is a genuine British male register feature, and Marie's misreading of it is equally common. Many British men of Tom's generation have been trained — by older men, by the rhythm of male friendship — to think before responding to emotional disclosures, and to treat premature reassurance as a small kind of dishonesty. "Tidying up" is Tom's word for this; the same instinct shows up across the male characters in this course (Carlos, Ben, Luca, Dan) in slightly different forms. The cost of the register is that women living with men trained this way often experience the silence as withdrawal, disappointment, or judgement. The labour of the conversation in this lesson is bilateral: Tom learns that the silence should be named ("I should have said that out loud, instead of just doing it"), and Marie learns that what she was reading as judgement was, in fact, respect. The closing exchange — Marie pre-emptively disclosing that she had been angry, Tom dryly noting he had already registered it — is the texture of long British couples, who have stopped pretending to surprise each other and started using accuracy as the form of intimacy.
content.grammar_focus: Past perfect for layered self-diagnosis — Marie's central retelling is grammatically dense. "I had thought about it but I hadn't built it into the proposal because the brief didn't mention it, and so I improvised." Three layers: past perfect (had thought, hadn't built — the prior internal state), past simple (didn't mention, improvised — the action sequence). Then the moment of self-recognition: "I knew, walking in, that I hadn't fully prepared. I knew it. I just didn't have time to fix it, and I told myself the brief didn't ask for it, and I let myself believe that was a good enough reason." Past simple narrative interleaved with past perfect (hadn't fully prepared) and the unusual reflexive structure "told myself" / "let myself believe." These are the grammatical hallmarks of someone tracking their own complicity in a small failure. Notice also Tom's question structure: "Have you ever lost a pitch before?" "And did the previous ones change anything that mattered?" Present perfect for cumulative experience, past simple for outcome — the grammar of perspective. Tom uses tense to do what comfort would otherwise do.

key_vocabulary.note: Phrasal verb. Tom использует переносно — преждевременное утешение как способ избавиться от неудобной эмоции.
Дизайнерский термин. Marie работает над wayfinding system для музея — навигация для посетителей.
Marie импровизировала ответ на вопрос, к которому не подготовилась. "The improvisation was visible" — заметна.
Marie: "the questions had a different texture." Сенсорная метафора о изменении тона.
Marie использует про эмоции: "in case it leaks out later" — чтобы злость не выплыла позже непредсказуемо.

comprehension_questions.hint: He doesn't believe in premature comfort — what he calls "tidying up." Saying "I'm sure it'll be fine" thirty seconds after Marie's disclosure would, in his view, serve his discomfort, not hers. He was thinking about what would actually be useful to say. He admits afterwards that he should have said this out loud rather than just doing it.
comprehension_questions.question: Why was Tom silent for ninety seconds?

comprehension_questions.hint: She knew walking in that she hadn't prepared the multilingual question. She told herself the brief didn't ask for it. She let herself believe that was a good enough reason. The improvisation was visible because she had set herself up for it. The grammar of "let myself believe" is the diagnostic.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Marie identify as her own complicity in the failure?

comprehension_questions.hint: "Have you ever lost a pitch before?" "Did the previous ones change anything that mattered?" Present perfect for experience, past simple for outcome — perspective without flattening the current feeling. The questions are kinder than reassurance because they invite Marie to recall her own evidence.
comprehension_questions.question: How does Tom use questions instead of comfort?

lessons.title_en: The Other Side of the Desk

content.title: The Other Side of the Desk

dialogue.line: I want to ask you something that isn't in the formal questions. Is that all right?
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: Of course.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: You've been on shared parental leave the last three months.
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: I have. Came back in January.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: How's it been? Coming back.
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: Honestly?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I'm asking honestly. I came back myself eight months ago, three months off, second baby. So this isn't an HR question.
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: ...Right. In that case. Coming back's been — strange. The work hasn't moved much. The me that came back is slightly different, and I keep noticing it in small things.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Such as?
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: Such as — I'm faster at deciding which emails don't need a reply. I'm slower at the social part of the office. I don't know yet whether either of those is good or bad.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Mm. Both, in my experience.
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: Yeah?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Yeah. The faster decisions are good, mostly. The social slowness is — it's not bad. It's just expensive. People notice it before you do, and you spend a few months working out what they noticed.
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: ...That's a useful framing.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: It's why I asked. Most of the work-related stuff in your file is fine — strong, actually. I wanted to know how you're handling the bit that isn't in the file.
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: Can I ask you something back?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: Is the question — does the company want to know whether I'm still going to be the same employee, post-paternity?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: ...Partly. Yes. Be straight with me — what's your honest answer?
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: I'll be a more efficient employee with slightly less spare capacity. I'll do the work, I'll do it well, I will probably stop staying after six unless something is genuinely on fire. If "the same employee" means the version of me who used to drift home at quarter to seven, no, you won't be getting that one back.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: And I assume — although I can't be sure — that the company would rather have a more efficient employee at six than the previous version at quarter to seven.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: The company, in the abstract, would rather have someone who stays till seven and is also efficient. That's not on offer. So yes, between the two real options, the new version is preferable. I'm telling you that because I'd rather you knew where the realism sits.
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: I appreciate that.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: One more thing. Off the record, but worth saying — when I came back the first time, four years ago, my boss handled it badly. He kept asking me whether I was "all right" in a tone that made it clear he meant something else. I won't do that to you. If there's a thing you need to say, say it. If there isn't, we can move on.
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: ...There isn't a thing I need to say. But I want to acknowledge the offer. Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Right. Back to the formal questions. You've been editing primarily fiction. Tell me how you'd handle the cookery list if we asked you to take it over for six months.
dialogue.speaker: James

dialogue.line: Differently from the way it's currently being run, but I'd want to be careful about saying that out loud before I knew exactly what I disagreed with.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Smart answer. Go on.
dialogue.speaker: James

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about an interview or a high-stakes conversation that veered off the formal script and became more honest as a result. What did the unexpected turn permit? Or — describe a moment when you had to project a future version of yourself in front of someone deciding whether to invest in that version. What grammar did you use to do it?
prompt_phrases: I want to ask you something that isn't in the formal questions.
The me that came back is slightly different.
Be straight with me — what's your honest answer?
Where the realism sits.
I'd rather you knew.

content.situation: Friday morning. Carlos has a final-stage interview for a permanent position at a publishing house — he has been on a fixed-term contract there for fifteen months and the role is being made permanent next quarter. The interviewer is James, the head of the editorial division. Carlos has met James briefly twice before. The interview is scheduled for forty-five minutes. About eighteen minutes in, the interview takes a turn neither of them was planning.

phrase_cle.example: — So the company is open to flexible hours? — In principle, yes. In practice, probably not. I'm telling you that so you know where the realism sits.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень B2 фраза для разделения декларируемой и фактической позиции. "Realism" здесь — не "реалистичность", а "реальное положение дел". Структура "where the [abstract noun] sits" описывает, на каком уровне находится определённое качество — реализм, истина, честность, согласие. James говорит Carlos: "я говорю тебе это, чтобы ты знал, где находится реализм" — то есть на каком уровне ты можешь рассчитывать на честный разговор. Часто появляется в рабочих контекстах, переговорах, профессиональных оценках ситуаций.

expressions.meaning: costly in a non-financial sense — used here about social slowness; figurative, metropolitan British
give me the unvarnished version, not the diplomatic one — male-register direct request
the level at which honest assessment becomes possible — "sits" used about positions in argument or thought
not for formal documentation; said in confidence — borrowed from journalism into office speech
the actual options as opposed to the wished-for ones — useful adult phrase for accepting that ideal alternatives are unavailable

content.cultural_note: Shared Parental Leave in the UK was introduced in 2015, allowing eligible parents to share up to fifty weeks of leave between them. Uptake remains low — around two to three percent of eligible fathers — but among those who do take it, a generational and cultural shift is visible. James and Carlos belong to that small cohort of fathers who have taken substantial leave and returned. The conversation between them is unusual in two ways. First, James openly says "I won't do that to you" about the way his own boss handled his return — a small piece of professional repair across generations. Second, Carlos's honest framing of the trade-off (more efficient, less spare capacity, no quarter-to-seven version) is the kind of negotiation that British workplace culture has, in the last decade, slowly become able to host. Whether the new version of Carlos is what James actually wants is the real question; that the question can be asked directly, without mutual pretence, is what makes the scene possible. The closing return to formal questions — about the cookery list — is the British workplace marker that the honesty interlude is over and the formal evaluation resumes. Carlos's answer (smart, deliberately under-committal) shows he has read the register correctly.
content.grammar_focus: Future continuous and conditional for projecting professional identity — "I'll be a more efficient employee with slightly less spare capacity. I'll do the work, I'll do it well, I will probably stop staying after six unless something is genuinely on fire." Three uses of "will" with different functions: prediction (will be), commitment (will do), and probability (will probably stop). Carlos is constructing his future self in real time, in front of the person who will decide whether to hire that future self. Notice also the parallel construction: "If 'the same employee' means [X], no, you won't be getting that one back." Conditional clause + flat denial. James's hypothetical: "The company, in the abstract, would rather have someone who stays till seven and is also efficient. That's not on offer." Modal of preference (would rather) + abstract qualifier + present simple correction. The grammar of two adults agreeing that an ideal is unavailable. Finally, Carlos's evasive-but-honest answer about cookery: "Differently from the way it's currently being run, but I'd want to be careful about saying that out loud before I knew exactly what I disagreed with." Comparative + present passive continuous + conditional + past simple — five clauses, four time-frames, beautifully calibrated. James calls it smart because the grammar itself does the smart work.

key_vocabulary.note: Корпоративная фраза. Carlos признаёт у себя меньше spare capacity после декрета — меньше избыточного времени и энергии.
Carlos: "the version of me who used to drift home at quarter to seven". Slightly self-deprecating о пред-родительском темпе.
Идиоматическое: "unless something is genuinely on fire" — если только что-то не горит. Британское разговорное о настоящих чрезвычайных ситуациях.
Журналистский термин в общем употреблении. James говорит "off the record" перед личным признанием.
Carlos: "I want to acknowledge the offer." Признать жест, не принимая его. Профессиональное.

comprehension_questions.hint: Because James has been through the same return himself eight months earlier, with a second baby. He knows the formal HR-style version of the question is useless. He wants the real conversation, between two men who have done the same thing, before returning to the formal interview structure.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does James ask Carlos about coming back from leave outside the formal questions?

comprehension_questions.hint: More efficient, less spare capacity, won't stay past six unless genuinely necessary. The pre-paternity version who drifted home at quarter to seven is gone. Carlos doesn't dress this up — he names the trade-off and asks James to be honest about whether the new version is preferable. James says it is, with caveats.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Carlos honestly project about his post-paternity self?

comprehension_questions.hint: His own boss handled his return badly four years ago — kept asking "are you all right" in a way that meant something else. James is doing repair across the generation: refusing to repeat the pattern. The acknowledgement is small but significant; Carlos receives it and thanks him without needing more.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does James say "I won't do that to you"?

content.title: The Essay

dialogue.line: Sophie. I've written something for the trade journal. They want it by Friday. Would you read it before I send it? I'd value the eyes of someone who knows the firm but is — at a slight angle to it. No obligation. Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor (email 1)

dialogue.line: Eleanor. Yes, of course. Send the draft. I'll get to it today. Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (email 2)

dialogue.line: Eleanor. Read it twice. It's strong. The argument is strong, the structure is strong, the tone is — measured, which I think is right for the journal. Two notes, neither of them about substance. First — the third paragraph spends a sentence on the statistics that I think you don't need. The reader of this journal already knows the statistics; you can cut that sentence and the paragraph lands harder. Second — there's a phrase in section four, "the slow politics of partnership", that does a lot of work in five words and I'd recommend you don't surround it with hedging on either side. Let it sit by itself, the way it does in your head. Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (email 3 — sent five hours later)

dialogue.line: Sophie. Both notes correct. Cut the sentence, freed the phrase. The piece is much better for it. Thank you. The journal will run it on the 14th. I'll send you the link when it's up. Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor (email 4 — next morning)

dialogue.line: Eleanor. Glad to. I should say — I read the essay before I read your covering line, and I noticed I was reading it as a stranger would. It works as a piece of writing for someone who has never met you. That's worth knowing, I think. Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (email 5 — twenty minutes later)

dialogue.line: Sophie. That's a useful observation. It also explains why I asked you specifically and not someone closer to me at the firm. The eyes of someone who has slightly stepped back are different from the eyes of someone who hasn't. I should probably name that, since we're emailing — these last three months I've been working out what to do with the fact that we cooled. I haven't worked it out. I've decided to just keep behaving like a sensible adult and trust that the rest will sort itself. Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor (email 6 — three hours later)

dialogue.line: Eleanor. I appreciate the directness. I've been doing the same thing on my side. I don't have a clean answer either. I think the answer, if there is one, will arrive sideways — not through a conversation but through small useful exchanges of the kind we've just had. I'd rather we did the small exchanges than try to engineer the big conversation. Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (email 7 — eleven minutes later)

dialogue.line: Sophie. Agreed. Have a good evening. E.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor (email 8 — six hours later, end of working day)

dialogue.line: You too. S.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (email 9 — two minutes later)

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about an email exchange that did the slow work of repair without anyone naming it as such. What was the small task that allowed the larger relational work to happen underneath? Or — describe a piece of writing you sent to someone whose opinion mattered, and the response that surprised you with what it noticed.
prompt_phrases: Would you read it before I send it?
I'd value the eyes of someone who is at a slight angle to it.
Let [the phrase] sit by itself, the way it does in your head.
I've been working out what to do with the fact that we cooled.
The answer will arrive sideways.

content.situation: Two days after the conference. Sophie's inbox at 9.17am, an email from Eleanor with no subject line, three lines of body text and a Word attachment. The first contact between them, beyond the strictly professional, since L6. Sophie reads the email. Reads the attachment — a draft essay Eleanor has written for a legal trade journal, on gender in commercial law partnership. Sophie reads the essay twice. Then, around 10.45am, she replies. The bulk of this lesson is the email exchange that follows over the next twenty-six hours — not in real time, the way a phone call would be, but in the slower rhythm of considered emails between two women who used to talk every day.

phrase_cle.example: — Are you and Eleanor going to talk about it? — No. The answer will arrive sideways, through small exchanges. I'd rather not engineer it.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень B2 фраза о природе долгого восстановления отношений. "Sideways" в этом контексте означает "не прямо", "через побочные действия". Sophie использует это, чтобы отказаться от "big conversation" с Eleanor — не потому что не хочет, а потому что верит: ремонт произойдёт через накопленные мелкие полезные обмены, а не через одну запланированную беседу. Структура часто появляется в зрелых разговорах об отношениях, восстановлении после конфликта, разрешении напряжений на работе. Звучит как заявление о доверии к медленному процессу.

expressions.meaning: not fully inside, not fully outside — a position of useful partial detachment, here used by one professional to characterise another's perspective
expressions.expression: at a slight angle to [the firm]

expressions.meaning: the rhetorical effect of the paragraph is greater when something is removed — editorial vocabulary about impact
remove the surrounding hedging and trust the phrase to carry its own weight — editorial advice about strong sentences
Eleanor's compact phrase for the multi-year navigation of seniority structures, equity tracks, and shifting alliances — the kind of phrase a senior partner crafts after years inside the system
resolution will come not through direct address but through accumulated small acts — adult phrasing about long repair

content.cultural_note: British professional email is its own register, distinct from spoken conversation and from social writing. The compactness, the elided connectors, the single-letter sign-off — all of these are markers of mutual professional fluency. The exchange between Eleanor and Sophie follows the form perfectly while doing relational work that ordinarily belongs to phone calls or face-to-face conversations. The choice to do the work in email is significant: it allows both women time to compose, time to delete, time to reread before sending. Eleanor's confession in email 6 — "these last three months I've been working out what to do with the fact that we cooled" — is the kind of sentence that takes minutes to write and seconds to read. Sophie's response in email 7 declines the larger conversation in a way that respects both Eleanor's offer and the rhythm they have established. "The answer will arrive sideways" is the working principle of repair in long professional friendships. Note also that the exchange ends not with a resolution but with "have a good evening" / "you too" — the British way of acknowledging that something has happened without underlining it. The repair is real. The repair is also continuing tomorrow.
content.grammar_focus: Email register at the upper professional level — short sentences, deliberate parataxis, no decorative connectors. Note how each email is structured: subject (often elided after the first), salutation by name, immediate substance, sign-off by name. The grammar of the body is doing considerable work without showing it. Sophie's editorial note: "the third paragraph spends a sentence on the statistics that I think you don't need." Relative clause with embedded I think — interpolated hedge, same device as Sophie used in the conference Q&A. Eleanor's confession: "these last three months I've been working out what to do with the fact that we cooled." Present perfect continuous (have been working out) for ongoing process + complement clause introduced by "the fact that" + simple past inside the embedded clause (we cooled). The grammar of someone reporting on her own quiet thinking. Sophie's response: "the answer, if there is one, will arrive sideways — not through a conversation but through small useful exchanges of the kind we've just had." Conditional clause embedded in subject position + future simple + manner adverb + negative-positive parallel structure. The grammar of polite refusal of the big conversation in favour of the small one. Notice also the closing pattern: "E." / "S." — single-letter sign-offs, the email register's compact form for two people who don't need to perform thoroughness.

key_vocabulary.note: Eleanor: "I'd value the eyes of someone". Slightly formal use — "value" вместо "want" повышает регистр.
Sophie использует про email-сообщение, сопровождающее вложение. Британский редакторский язык.
Eleanor: "someone who has slightly stepped back". Метафорическое использование о профессиональной дистанции.
Sophie: "engineer the big conversation". Слегка ироничное о попытках организовать спонтанные разговоры.
Sophie использует переносно. "The answer will arrive sideways" — основная метафора урока.

comprehension_questions.hint: Sophie is "at a slight angle" to the firm — close enough to know the context, far enough to read like a stranger. Eleanor wanted that doubled perspective. The choice was also, Eleanor admits, an oblique way of opening a conversation about the cooling of their relationship — not the conversation itself, but the conditions for one.
comprehension_questions.question: Why did Eleanor ask Sophie specifically to read the essay?

comprehension_questions.hint: It tells Eleanor the essay does what it needs to do for its actual readers — people who have never met her — and not just for people who already trust her voice. Sophie's distance becomes useful evidence. It also confirms why Eleanor asked her: the angle is real.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Sophie's notice that she read the essay "as a stranger would" tell Eleanor?

comprehension_questions.hint: She trusts that repair between long professional friends arrives through accumulated small useful exchanges rather than through engineered set-piece conversations. "The answer will arrive sideways." Both women agree without needing to debate the principle. The exchange ends with mutual sign-offs that close the day, not the matter.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie decline the "big conversation" Eleanor implicitly offers?

content.title: The Podcast and the Text

dialogue.line: Last question, Sophie, and you may decline. I'd like to ask about the line you've drawn between legal advice and public communications. It came up in your conference paper and I'd like to put it on record here too.
dialogue.speaker: Adrian

dialogue.line: I'd rather it were on record, actually. It's not something I want to be coy about.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Then please — describe the line, and why you draw it.
dialogue.speaker: Adrian

dialogue.line: The line is between giving a client legal advice — which is to say, telling them honestly what the law allows, requires, and forbids — and shaping the language they use to defend their conduct in public. The first I do as a duty. The second I have decided not to do, because I find it asks something of me that the first does not.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What does it ask?
dialogue.speaker: Adrian

dialogue.line: It asks me to help them be persuasive about a version of events that I know, from the disclosure, to be selective. Legal advice is given on the same selective record — but the legal argument is structured to be tested by another lawyer in front of a judge. The public communications are structured to win an audience that has no equivalent test. Those two activities sit on different ground.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Some of your colleagues would say that's a distinction without a real difference.
dialogue.speaker: Adrian

dialogue.line: I'd say it's a distinction with a difference I can feel. I won't pretend it has the architecture of a legal principle. It has the architecture of a personal limit. It would perhaps be more accurate to call it a working ethic than a doctrine.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Has it cost you anything professionally?
dialogue.speaker: Adrian

dialogue.line: Probably. I can't measure what it has cost because I can't see the matters I haven't been assigned. I'd be naïve to imagine the cost is zero. I'd also be naïve to imagine it's catastrophic, because I am still here, still working, still on the equity track, and the firm has been generous about the line.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: If a junior associate came to you and said "I want to draw the same line" — what would you tell her?
dialogue.speaker: Adrian

dialogue.line: I would tell her that the line is not free, and not universally honoured, and that the person to ask about it is someone senior enough at her firm to make the line workable rather than punitive. I would also tell her that I drew the line later than I should have, and that drawing it earlier would not have been more virtuous — it would simply have spared me three years of small private compromises.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...That's a remarkable answer. Sophie, thank you very much.
dialogue.speaker: Adrian

dialogue.line: Thank you, Adrian. It's been a pleasure.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: [Recording ends.]
dialogue.speaker: Adrian

dialogue.line: [Half an hour later. Walking down John Dalton Street. Phone out of her bag. One message.]
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Just listened to the podcast. You said it well.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor (text)

dialogue.line: Eleanor. Thank you. That means a great deal coming from you. I owe a lot of how I said it to you, although I think you know that.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (text — typed, not sent)

dialogue.line: Thank you. Means a lot.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (text — typed, deleted, retyped)

dialogue.line: Thank you. Coming from you, that lands.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (text — sent)

dialogue.line: It was meant to.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor (text — three minutes later)

dialogue.line: Eleanor. Drink at some point?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (text — sent)

dialogue.line: Yes. Next week. I'll text you Monday.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor (text)

dialogue.line: Okay.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (text)

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor (text)

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (text)

dialogue.line: Well done.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor (text)

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (text — sent)

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a moment when you said something publicly that you had only ever said privately before, and someone whose opinion mattered registered it in a single short message. How did the message arrive? How did you reply? Or — describe the difference between a thing you would say in a formal interview and the same thing as you would text it to someone who already understood.
prompt_phrases: It's not something I want to be coy about.
It's a distinction with a difference I can feel.
It's a working ethic rather than a doctrine.
I'd be naïve to imagine [extreme position]. I'd also be naïve to imagine [opposite extreme].
Coming from you, that lands.

content.situation: Eleven days after the email exchange. Sophie is recording a forty-minute interview for The Practical Lawyer, a well-respected industry podcast. The interviewer is Adrian — sixties, retired barrister, asks careful questions. The recording is in a small studio in central Manchester. The first part of this lesson is the closing six minutes of the interview, in which Sophie discusses something she had not planned to discuss publicly. The second part is half an hour later — Sophie is leaving the building, picking up her phone. She has one new message. From Eleanor.

phrase_cle.example: — You're a fantastic writer. — Coming from you, that lands. From most people I would just say thank you.
phrase_cle.explanation: Современная взрослая фраза, признающая что ценность похвалы зависит от того, кто хвалит. Структура: "Coming from [person], [statement] [verb of impact]." Глагол "to land" — ключевой: похвала не просто получена, она долетает и достигает цели, потому что источник правильный. Sophie использует это с Eleanor вместо обычного "thank you" — потому что от Eleanor "You said it well" значит больше, чем от любого случайного слушателя. На B2 эта структура работает в моментах, где обычная вежливость недостаточна и нужно признать иерархию источников.
phrase_cle.translation: Из ваших уст это попадает в цель

expressions.meaning: to handle a topic with deliberate evasion or affected modesty — Sophie declining to do that with the line
a verbal separation that doesn't track an actual difference in substance — common professional rhetorical move, here named in order to be answered
expressions.expression: a distinction without a real difference

expressions.meaning: Sophie's reframe — the distinction is real to her even if it cannot be argued from first principles; modern adult shorthand for trusting personal judgment without claiming it as universal
expressions.expression: a distinction with a difference I can feel

expressions.meaning: a personal practice that guides action without claiming the status of a principle — useful adult phrasing about the limits of one's own moral authority
expressions.expression: a working ethic rather than a doctrine

expressions.meaning: praise from this particular person carries weight that praise from elsewhere would not — modern adult phrasing about source-specific significance

content.cultural_note: British professional podcast culture — particularly in law, finance, and media — has its own register: long-form, careful, comfortable with silence, willing to give guests the option of declining a question. Adrian's framing ("you may decline") is the adult version of "feel free to skip." Sophie's response ("I'd rather it were on record") is a deliberate rejection of coyness — she has decided that the line she draws is not a private quirk but a public position, and she is making that public for the first time. The grammatical tension between the formal interview register and the text-message exchange that follows is the lesson's structural centre. Eleanor's three-word text — "You said it well" — does what a long phone call would not: it acknowledges the public statement without re-opening the cooling between them. "It was meant to" — three more words — completes the acknowledgement. The repair that Sophie predicted in L23 ("the answer will arrive sideways") arrives exactly sideways: not through a conversation but through a podcast, a text, a deleted draft, a sent reply, and a drink scheduled for next week. The texture is unmistakably British. The two women have managed something difficult in the way they would have always managed it, if they were going to manage it.
content.grammar_focus: Two registers in one lesson — formal interview prose, then text-message compact. The interview section showcases hedging at full strength: "It would perhaps be more accurate to call it a working ethic than a doctrine." Modal of probability + adverb of qualification + comparative + comparative noun. "I'd be naïve to imagine the cost is zero. I'd also be naïve to imagine it's catastrophic." Parallel conditional structure, both halves negating extreme positions to install a middle one. "I drew the line later than I should have, and drawing it earlier would not have been more virtuous — it would simply have spared me three years of small private compromises." Modal perfect (should have) + gerund subject (drawing it) + modal perfect comparative (would not have been more virtuous). Then the text-message section operates in the opposite register: elliptical, no salutations after the first, dropping subjects ("Means a lot."), single syllables ("Yes." "Okay."). The pause-bubbles "..." function as syntactic placeholders for feelings that don't get typed. The grammar lesson is the contrast: B2 fluency means being able to deploy both registers, knowing when each does work the other can't. Sophie's polished podcast answer would be wrong as a text. Sophie's compact text would be inadequate as a podcast answer. Same speaker, two grammars.

key_vocabulary.note: Повтор из L5 и L6. Теперь Sophie говорит публично о черте, которую тогда провела приватно.
Slightly formal. Sophie не хочет быть coy — не хочет уклоняться от вопроса.
Высокий регистр. Sophie противопоставляет doctrine (общий принцип) и working ethic (личная практика).
Sophie использует про младшего сотрудника — "making the line workable rather than punitive". Линия не должна наказывать саму себя.
Sophie: "that lands". Современное использование — о словах, которые достигают цели, попадают в человека.

comprehension_questions.hint: She is no longer treating it as a private quirk. The conference talk and now the podcast convert it from a personal practice into a publicly stated position. "It's not something I want to be coy about" is the choice to own it openly, with all the professional consequences that may follow.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie agree to put her line on the record publicly?

comprehension_questions.hint: She reframes it: "a distinction with a difference I can feel." She doesn't claim it has the architecture of a legal principle — she names it as a personal limit, a working ethic rather than a doctrine. The reframe is honest about what kind of authority her line does and doesn't claim.
comprehension_questions.question: How does Sophie answer the "distinction without a real difference" challenge?

comprehension_questions.hint: Texts are the right register for the kind of repair Sophie and Eleanor are doing. A phone call would force a bigger conversation than either wants. An email would be too formal. The text exchange — three words, three more, a deleted draft, a final reply — does the work without overstating it. "The answer will arrive sideways" was Sophie's prediction in L23. It arrives, sideways, here.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does the lesson use text messages for the closing exchange with Eleanor?

content.title: Arriving

dialogue.line: He didn't even murmur.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I keep checking on him in case he's stopped breathing.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I assume that's standard.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is. Doesn't mean it's pleasant.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Anna, sit down. Wine?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'll have half a glass.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I shouldn't be drinking at all, technically. But I'd like to be in this kitchen with you and not in the bedroom alone wondering if he's asleep.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: What's the technicality?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm still feeding. Half a glass three hours before the next feed is — fine, in the literature.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Anna, here's your half glass.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I thought he'd cry. The unfamiliar room, the new noises. We'd genuinely prepared to be up at one. I asked Mum what we should do if he wouldn't settle, and she said "hand him to me, I haven't held a settled baby in twenty years."
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: She did say that. I heard her.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And what does it mean that he didn't cry?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Probably nothing.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Or it means our anxiety has stopped being about him and started being about us.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: ...Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Sorry. Late observation.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: It's not a wrong observation. It's a Thursday-night observation that I'd rather have on Saturday.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Fair enough.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Can I say something?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: We're not going to be able to stop you.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I think we've all brought our default frequencies down here, and we're going to have to consciously turn some of them off, or this whole week is going to be Manchester-on-the-coast.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Yes. That's exactly the thing I haven't been letting myself say.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: What's your default frequency, Soph?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Slight permanent professional alertness. Like there's an email I haven't replied to yet, even when there isn't.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mine is — wondering whether I've forgotten something I was supposed to remember. Constantly. About anything.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mine is — checking whether Mateo is fine, in some part of my head, all the time. It runs in the background even when I'm doing other things.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Mine is — trying not to seem like I'm not pulling my weight.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: ...You feel like that?
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: All the time. About work, about the house, about Mateo. It's a whole tone.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I had no idea.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Well. Now you do.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: ...Okay. So we have four frequencies in this kitchen, and we've named them, and that's enough for one night.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Agreed.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Agreed.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Agreed. Half a glass each more, and then bed.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Half a glass each more, and then bed.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about your own default frequency — the background state you have been running on for so long you don't notice it. What is it made of? Or — describe a moment when four people in a kitchen named the same kind of thing about themselves, in parallel, and the naming was the relief itself.
prompt_phrases: I keep checking on [him/it] in case [worst-case].
We'd genuinely prepared to be [worse outcome].
We've all brought our default frequencies down here.
Mine is — [internal action] about [thing].
It runs in the background even when I'm doing other things.

content.situation: Late August. The house is in St Mawes — five bedrooms, a long wooden table, a garden that runs down to a stone wall and then to the sea. They have been there four hours. Bags are still in the hall. Mum is in the garden with Tom. Mateo, ten months old now and walking with conviction, is asleep upstairs. Carlos and Anna come down to the kitchen at half past ten — they had expected him to wake the house at least once between his first sleep and theirs, and he hasn't. Sophie and Marie are in the kitchen, opening a second bottle. The conversation that follows is the kind that happens when four tired adults notice, separately and simultaneously, that the holiday they have been imagining for weeks has actually started.

phrase_cle.phrase: It runs in the background even when I'm doing other things
phrase_cle.example: — Do you ever stop worrying about him? — No. It runs in the background even when I'm doing other things.
phrase_cle.explanation: Современная фраза, заимствованная из компьютерного языка в эмоциональную речь. "In the background" — фоновый процесс. Anna использует это, чтобы описать постоянную проверку, что Mateo в порядке — это работает как фоновое приложение, не закрывается, потребляет ресурсы. На B2 эта структура часто появляется в зрелых разговорах о тревоге, бдительности, хронической озабоченности. Звучит современно и точно. Не "я всегда", а "это работает в фоне" — переносит описание из абсолютного в техническое.
phrase_cle.translation: Это работает в фоне, даже когда я занят другим

expressions.meaning: the background mental state someone has been running on for so long they no longer notice it — Sophie's compact metaphor for unconscious operating modes
the failure mode of taking your unrelaxed home self with you on holiday — Sophie's improvised compound naming the risk
a true insight badly timed — Anna's adult acknowledgement that timing is itself part of kindness
expressions.expression: a Thursday-night observation that I'd rather have on Saturday

expressions.meaning: to do your fair share of shared work — used here by Carlos about a constant background self-monitoring
modern adult phrasing for low-level chronic vigilance — borrowed from computer language into emotional self-description
expressions.expression: It runs in the background even when I'm doing other things

content.cultural_note: British holiday weeks at rented houses follow a specific arrival pattern. The first evening is almost never relaxed — it is the evening of locating cupboards, finding light switches, remembering you forgot to bring olive oil. The relaxation, when it comes, comes on Saturday morning at the earliest. Sophie's diagnosis — "Manchester-on-the-coast" — names the risk of the entire week becoming an extended first evening if no one decides to actively turn off their default frequencies. The four-way confession in the kitchen is unusual in real life and deliberately staged here as the lesson's structural device. Note that no one apologises for their frequency. No one tries to fix anyone else's. The British rule of the scene is naming without solving — letting four different anxieties be present in the same room without the social pressure to convert them into action items. The shared sign-off — "agreed" repeated four times, then the half-glass agreement — closes the conversation through ritual repetition. It is the verbal equivalent of stacking glasses by the sink before going up to bed.
content.grammar_focus: Mixed conditionals and modal calibration in shared self-disclosure. Carlos: "I thought he'd cry. The unfamiliar room, the new noises. We'd genuinely prepared to be up at one." Past simple (thought) + reported future (he'd cry) + past perfect of preparation (we'd genuinely prepared). Three time-frames in three sentences, each describing the version of tonight that didn't happen. Anna: "Half a glass three hours before the next feed is — fine, in the literature." The qualifier "in the literature" does specific work — Anna invokes external authority to give herself permission while acknowledging the permission isn't automatic. Notice the structure of the four-way disclosure. Each speaker uses the same grammatical frame for self-description: "Mine is — [present continuous gerund of internal action]." Sophie: "slight permanent professional alertness." Marie: "wondering whether I've forgotten." Anna: "checking whether Mateo is fine." Carlos: "trying not to seem like I'm not pulling my weight." The shared frame lets four different anxieties become legible to each other in parallel. The grammar is doing the social work — by using the same structure, they place themselves alongside each other rather than competitively.

key_vocabulary.note: Anna: "He didn't even murmur." О младенце — даже не пикнул. Тише, чем cry или whimper.
Британский родительский термин. "If he wouldn't settle" — если бы он не успокоился ко сну.
Sophie: "slight permanent professional alertness". Постоянное состояние готовности отвечать.
Идиома о справедливом распределении работы. Carlos признаёт, что постоянно проверяет, не отстаёт ли он.
Sophie использует переносно — внутренний "радиоприёмник" каждого человека настроен на свою частоту. Метафора блока.

comprehension_questions.hint: Not because it's wrong — Anna agrees it's accurate. Because it's a true insight badly timed. "It's a Thursday-night observation that I'd rather have on Saturday." Adult acknowledgement that timing is part of kindness; the right thing said at the wrong moment carries a small cost even when accurate.
comprehension_questions.question: Why is Carlos's late observation unwelcome to Anna?

comprehension_questions.hint: That all four of them have brought their default frequencies — their unconscious background mental states — and that without consciously turning some of them off, the holiday will become "Manchester-on-the-coast" — the unrelaxed home version transported to a different setting. Naming the risk is the start of countering it.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Sophie's diagnosis of the risk for the week?

comprehension_questions.hint: The repetition functions as ritual closure. Each "agreed" is shorthand for accepting the diagnosis, accepting the disclosures, accepting the half-glass-and-bed plan. British understated acknowledgement that something has happened in the kitchen — without the need to spell out what it was.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does the conversation end with everyone saying "agreed" four times?

content.title: On the Beach

dialogue.line: Mum. How long have you been here?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: An hour and a half. The heron arrived twenty minutes ago. He has been deciding about a fish since then.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...Are we waiting for him to make up his mind?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: We're free to do otherwise. I've quite enjoyed watching him not catch it.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Tea?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: There's a second cup in my bag. The flask is good for one more pour each.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: How are you finding the week so far?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Better than I'd expected, actually. The kitchen conversation on Friday night helped. I think we got some things out of the way that would have lurked otherwise.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I gathered. Tom told me bits.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Did he.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: He didn't tell tales. He said you'd all named your default frequencies and that it had been useful. I thought that was a good phrase.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: It was Marie's. Or — no. It was mine, actually. I didn't think about that until just now.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Hmm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: What's hmm?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'd like to tell you something that's been on my mind for a few months, and I'd rather tell you on a beach than in my flat. It's not bad news. I want to say that first because you've gone slightly tense.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...Okay.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I've been thinking about the next ten years of my life.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Stop. I am not ill. I am seventy-three and I have had a good check-up in May. What I want to talk about is a different thing, and I'd be grateful if you let me get the sentence out before deciding what it is.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Yes. Sorry. Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have noticed, in the last eighteen months, that the difference between time-that-feels-interesting and time-that-feels-like-waiting has been getting sharper. And I have decided that for whatever amount of time-that-feels-interesting I have left — it could be eight years, could be twenty — I'd rather not spend any of it being polite to people I find tiring.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I told you it wasn't bad news.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: It isn't. It is — extraordinary. Why are you telling me on a beach in Cornwall?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because the sentence has been in my head for months, and I wanted to say it once out loud to someone whose opinion I wanted, and then move on. It's not a question. It's a — a settling.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Are you going to do anything about it?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I already have. I told Maureen with the marmalade last week that I'd rather not have lunch with her on Wednesdays. I told her in the kindest way I could. She wasn't happy. I wasn't deeply upset that she wasn't happy.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. The word "settling" is a useful one. There's no decision left to make. There's only a decision left to live with.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: What's that face?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I'm noticing that the version of you that says these things to me is not a version I have to be afraid of, and I'm rearranging some old worries.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. The heron has caught something. Watch.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: He'll be insufferable about it for the rest of the morning.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Thank you for telling me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Of course. Go and swim. The water won't be warm again until June.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a decision you have already made but have been unable to articulate to anyone — a settling, in Mum's word. What is the daily shape of living with it? Or — describe a conversation you had outdoors with a parent or older relative that did work an indoor conversation could not have done. What did the setting permit?
prompt_phrases: Things would have lurked otherwise.
I have noticed the difference between [time-X] and [time-Y] has been getting sharper.
It's not a question. It's a settling.
There's no decision left to make. There's only a decision left to live with.
I'm rearranging some old worries.

content.situation: Sunday morning. Half past eight. The tide has been out since six and is starting to come back. Sophie has walked down to the beach in her swimming costume, intending to swim. Mum is already there, sitting on a rock with a flask of tea, watching a heron at the water's edge. She has been awake since five. Sophie sits down next to her and decides not to swim quite yet.

phrase_cle.phrase: There's no decision left to make. There's only a decision left to live with
phrase_cle.example: — Have you decided about the move? — There's no decision left to make. There's only a decision left to live with.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень B2 структура для разделения двух фаз решения. Обычно люди говорят "я решил" — но Mum проводит более тонкое различие: после того как решение принято, остаётся другая работа — жить с ним каждый день. Существительные одинаковы ("a decision"), глаголы разные ("to make" vs "to live with"), и это весь смысл. На B2 эта структура часто появляется в зрелых разговорах о крупных жизненных выборах — переезд, профессия, разрыв, отказ от чего-то. Сама фраза признаёт, что у решений есть жизнь после принятия.
phrase_cle.translation: Решение принимать уже не нужно. Нужно только с ним жить

expressions.meaning: to be present but undeclared, hovering at the edge of consciousness or conversation — used about feelings or topics not yet named
Mum's compound for years still active and chosen rather than passively endured — coined phrase used as a single conceptual unit
Mum's noun for a decision that has already been made and is now being lived with rather than considered — distinct from "a deliberation"
the second phase of a choice, after the choosing is done, when daily action gives the choice its real shape
expressions.expression: a decision left to live with

expressions.meaning: I am updating my mental map in light of new information about what does and doesn't actually need anxiety — Sophie's compact phrasing for cognitive recalibration; echoes L14

content.cultural_note: Mum's announcement on the beach is a particular kind of late-life British self-disclosure: small, precise, deliberately undramatic, delivered while watching a bird. The setting matters. She specifies it: "I'd rather tell you on a beach than in my flat." Outdoor conversations between British family members tend to go better than indoor ones for difficult disclosures, partly because the speaker has somewhere to look that isn't the listener's face, and partly because the surrounding action — a heron, the tide — provides a rhythm that domesticates the subject. Mum's invented compounds — "time-that-feels-interesting" and "time-that-feels-like-waiting" — are the kind of phrasing she had to coin because no off-the-shelf word fit. The concept is not new (philosophers and novelists have explored it for centuries), but the grandmotherly British register of saying it across half a flask of tea is its own form. Note the closing pivot — Mum delivers the sentence, Sophie acknowledges, and Mum returns immediately to the heron. "He'll be insufferable about it for the rest of the morning" is the small joke that closes the serious aperture. It is also a deliberate kindness: leaving Sophie space to swim rather than space to feel obliged to respond at length.
content.grammar_focus: Future-in-the-past and conditional speculation. Sophie: "I think we got some things out of the way that would have lurked otherwise." Past simple + relative clause + modal perfect (would have lurked) + adverb (otherwise) — describing what the alternative version of the evening would have produced. The grammar of unactualised consequences. Mum's central paragraph is grammatically rich: "the difference between time-that-feels-interesting and time-that-feels-like-waiting has been getting sharper. And I have decided that for whatever amount of time-that-feels-interesting I have left — it could be eight years, could be twenty — I'd rather not spend any of it being polite to people I find tiring." Present perfect continuous (has been getting) for ongoing observation, present perfect (have decided) for a completed mental act, modal of possibility (could be) twice for honest acknowledgement of the unknown range, would rather + negative gerund for stated preference. Notice also Mum's "There's no decision left to make. There's only a decision left to live with."
