Two existential there-constructions in parallel negation-affirmation, with passive infinitives (to make / to live with). The grammar of completed deliberation.

key_vocabulary.note: О чём-то невысказанном, что присутствует и не названо. Sophie: "things would have lurked otherwise".
Mum: "I gathered." — "я поняла" из услышанного. Британское разговорное.
"He didn't tell tales" — он не сплетничал, не выдавал. Британская идиома, пришедшая из школьных лет.
Существительное от "to settle". Mum использует как термин для второй фазы решения.
Slightly literary. Mum шутит про цаплю — "he'll be insufferable". Преувеличение для юмора.

comprehension_questions.hint: The first is years still active and chosen — engaged, curious, deliberately occupied. The second is years passing through her rather than being lived. Mum has noticed the gap getting sharper in the last eighteen months and decided to spend whatever interesting time she has left more selectively. The compound phrasing is hers because no existing word fit.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Mum's distinction between "time-that-feels-interesting" and "time-that-feels-like-waiting"?

comprehension_questions.hint: A decision is a verb of choosing. A settling is a noun for the state after the choosing is done. The work has shifted from deliberation to daily living-with. There is no decision left to make; there is only a decision left to live with. Two grammatical phases of the same word.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Mum mean by "a settling" rather than "a decision"?

comprehension_questions.hint: Outdoor settings let the speaker have somewhere to look that isn't the listener's face, and the surrounding action — a heron, the tide — provides a rhythm that softens the disclosure. Mum has been carrying the sentence for months and wanted to deliver it once, out loud, in conditions that wouldn't make it heavier than it was.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Mum tell Sophie this on the beach rather than in her flat?

content.title: Seven Weeks

dialogue.line: I want to tell you all something. While we're all here together.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Don't make a face yet. Let me get the sentence out.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Sorry.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm seven weeks pregnant. We found out three days before we drove down. We were trying. It was — early. Earlier than we'd planned.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Congratulations. Both of you.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: How are you feeling?
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...Mum. That is the best question anyone could have asked.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I am asking it because it's the question I'd want.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I'm — I don't have a clean answer. I'm pleased. I'm tired in a way I don't recognise yet. I'm slightly disoriented because we'd agreed on a timeline back in March and the timeline has just been rearranged for us.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What was the timeline?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Two more years. Marriage first, possibly. Then trying. The plan was that I would be the one who said when. I told Tom that explicitly. He agreed.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I remember the conversation. You told me about it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. So. I am now in a position where I'm the one who said when, and yet not actually the one who said when, because the biology said when first. I'm working out what to do with that.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You can keep working that out. I'm not asking you to have finished.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...I know.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie, can I say something?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Anna and I planned Mateo for eighteen months and he arrived three weeks late. The thing about babies is they appear to negotiate with the calendar fairly heavily. The plan-version and the actual-version of any of this are different things by the time they happen.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I'd add something to that.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Please.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: The first month I felt like — like I was carrying news that didn't fit the shape of how I'd told myself the news would feel. I wasn't sad. I wasn't ecstatic. I was something I didn't have a word for, and I felt slightly guilty about not having a word, because everyone seemed to expect a word. So if your version is — "I'm working out what to do with that" — that is the actual version. Anyone giving you a cleaner version is selling you a postcard.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Sorry. I get long when I'm tired.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Anna, you can be as long as you like. That helped.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I would suggest you stop trying to work out what to do with the timeline thing this week. The timeline rearranged itself. You don't owe it a reaction yet.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...Right.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Sophie. Pour Marie another half-glass of whatever isn't wine.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: On it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Thank you for telling them tonight. You weren't sure you wanted to.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I know. They needed to know on a Monday by a fire pit, not on a Wednesday by text.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Yes. Yes we did.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a piece of news that arrived earlier than you had planned for, and the way you described it to people who needed to know. What did you say in the first sentence? What did you not say? Or — describe a moment when an experienced person gave you permission to not have figured out your feelings yet, and the permission itself was the useful gift.
prompt_phrases: I'm pleased. I'm tired in a way I don't recognise yet.
The timeline has just been rearranged for us.
I'm the one who said when, and yet not actually the one who said when.
Anyone giving you a cleaner version is selling you a postcard.
You don't owe it a reaction yet.

content.situation: Monday evening. Marie and Tom have been carrying news for three days. They had agreed in the car down to Cornwall that they would tell everyone on the second night, around the fire pit in the garden, after dinner. Mateo is asleep upstairs again. The fire pit is going, the bottles are open, the air is starting to cool. Marie looks at Tom. Tom looks at Marie. Marie speaks first.
content.characters: Marie, Tom, Sophie, Carlos, Anna & Mum

phrase_cle.phrase: Anyone giving you a cleaner version is selling you a postcard
phrase_cle.example: — Did you absolutely love being pregnant? — Anyone giving you a cleaner version of pregnancy is selling you a postcard. It was complicated.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень B2 фраза, отвергающая упрощённые нарративы об осложнённых жизненных событиях. "A postcard" — образ красиво обрезанной картинки места, не показывающей запах рыбы и ветер со стороны парковки. Anna использует это, чтобы дать Marie разрешение чувствовать сложно. Структура: "Anyone [doing X] is [doing simplified Y]" — обвинение упрощений в продажном характере. Часто появляется в зрелых разговорах о материнстве, отношениях, карьерных переходах — везде, где культурный нарратив гладкий, а реальность шероховата.
phrase_cle.translation: Кто бы ни предлагал тебе версию попроще — он продаёт тебе открытку

expressions.meaning: compact British shorthand for actively attempting conception — used here without further explanation, signalling that the listeners will fill in the meaning
Marie's compact image for the body intervening in a planned timeline — agentive metaphor giving biology its own voice
to refuse to fit a planned schedule — Carlos's affectionate phrasing about babies and pregnancies
offering a tidied-up version of an experience that does not match the messy real one — Anna's compact phrase for false neat narratives
the event has happened but does not require an immediate emotional response — Mum's permission to defer
expressions.expression: You don't owe it a reaction yet

content.cultural_note: British announcements of pregnancy at family gatherings tend to be small, deliberately undramatic, and front-loaded with practical information rather than performed emotion. Marie's opening — "I'm seven weeks pregnant. We found out three days before we drove down. We were trying. It was early" — is four flat sentences, and that flatness is the British texture. The lesson's structural achievement is that each subsequent character offers something different: Carlos offers congratulation and then a perspective from his own experience; Anna offers permission to feel complicated; Mum offers permission to defer. None of them tries to make Marie's announcement into anyone else's. Note Mum's instruction at the end — "Pour Marie another half-glass of whatever isn't wine" — which is doing two jobs at once: signalling that Marie shouldn't drink, and giving Sophie a small physical task that ends the seriousness of the moment without abandoning it. "Sophie. On it." The verb-less compact reply is the British marker of useful action requested and accepted. Tom's closing exchange with Marie acknowledges that she had been uncertain about telling tonight; the fact that she did tell, and the fact that it went well, becomes the lesson's small victory. "They needed to know on a Monday by a fire pit, not on a Wednesday by text." Setting and timing are themselves part of the message.
content.grammar_focus: Cleft sentences as the structural backbone of the disclosure. Marie: "I am now in a position where I'm the one who said when, and yet not actually the one who said when, because the biology said when first." Three nested cleft structures with subtle subject changes — "the one who said when" appears three times, with shifting truth-values in each instance. Marie is using grammar to think out loud about a paradox she hasn't fully resolved. The repetition is the precision. Notice also the conditional perfects in Marie's emotional self-description: "It was early. Earlier than we'd planned." Past simple + comparative + past perfect — the grammar of an event preceding its expected version. Anna's contribution uses a structurally rich phrase: "I was something I didn't have a word for, and I felt slightly guilty about not having a word." Embedded relative clause + gerund of absence (not having a word). The grammar of an emotion that has not yet been named, by a speaker who is also naming the unnaming. Mum's advice uses the rare reflexive structure "the timeline rearranged itself" — an event described as self-acting, removing agency from any of the people involved. "You don't owe it a reaction yet" — modal of obligation in the negative, with "yet" performing the temporal permission to defer.

key_vocabulary.note: Anna: "I wasn't ecstatic." Признание, что радость не пришла в стандартной форме.
Британский садовый объект. Marie выбрала это место для объявления — не за столом, не в гостиной, а у огня в темноте.
Carlos использует переносно — младенцы "договариваются" с расписанием, отказываясь приходить вовремя.
Anna использует переносно — упрощённая, красивая, неточная версия опыта.
Не в диалоге, но в культурной заметке. Mum даёт Marie разрешение defer реакцию.

comprehension_questions.hint: Most reactions to a pregnancy announcement skip directly to congratulation. Mum asks about the present internal state instead — "the question I'd want," she says. By asking about the feeling rather than offering a script, she leaves Marie space to give an honest, complicated answer rather than a tidied one.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Mum's question "How are you feeling?" land so well for Marie?

comprehension_questions.hint: She had agreed with Tom in March that she would be the one who said when. Biology has rearranged the timeline without her permission. She is now both the person who said when (because she had wanted children eventually) and not the person who said when (because the timing wasn't her active choice). The paradox is the thing she doesn't know what to do with.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Marie working out about her timeline?

comprehension_questions.hint: Permission to feel something there isn't a word for. Anna names her own first-month experience: not sad, not ecstatic, something without a vocabulary, and the guilt of not having the vocabulary. The story tells Marie that complicated feelings about a planned-for thing are normal, and that anyone offering a cleaner version is selling a postcard.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Anna's gift to Marie?

content.title: The Long Table

dialogue.line: Tom, the lamb is genuinely the best thing I've eaten this year.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Eight hours at one-twenty. The trick is not to do anything to it.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Helena would say something Helena-shaped about that.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Such as?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: "The hardest skill in cooking, as in law, is leaving things alone."
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'd like Helena.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: [at the other end] Carlos, I want to ask you something while Anna is in earshot.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: When Anna told you she was pregnant — what was the first thing you actually thought, before the thing you said?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Oh, I want to hear this too.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: ...Honestly?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Honestly.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I thought "oh, this is real now" before I thought anything happy. The happy bit came in about thirty seconds. The first thirty seconds were just — recalibration.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I had no idea.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I never told you because I thought it would sound like ambivalence. It wasn't. It was just — the size of the thing arriving.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: That's actually useful for me to hear.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: [at the other end, to Tom] Are they having a real conversation down there?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Looks like it. I'd let it run.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Marie's going to need you over the next eight months in a way she hasn't needed you before.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: ...I know.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm saying it out loud because I'd rather we both knew that we knew.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom, that's actually moving.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'd been holding it for two days. The lamb gave me cover.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: [at the other end] Carlos, ask me what I thought.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: What did you think?
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: When I saw the test, I thought — "I am about to become someone whose body is going to do something I have no control over for nine months." Which was correct. Then I thought "I want to ring my mother." Which I did. Then I thought "oh, Carlos."
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I came third.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: You came third in the order. You came first in the importance.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: I don't think that's how priority works.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos, I am not going to fight you on the chronology.
dialogue.speaker: Anna

dialogue.line: Fair.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: [appearing at the centre of the table with the tart] If everyone could stop telling each other things and remember to eat the pudding, it would be appreciated.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, your timing is supernatural.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Your generation is exhausting. Tart, please. There's clotted cream.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I will eat that tart and ask for seconds.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. Come here for a second.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Just — I'm glad you're here. That's all.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum. Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: [quietly, to Tom] My mother just called Marie "Mum".
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: She did.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I think Marie called her Mum back.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: She did.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Should we say something?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Absolutely not. Have some tart.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a long dinner where two conversations ran in parallel and one of them tilted into something more honest than anyone had planned. What was the surface conversation? What did the deeper one permit? Or — describe a moment when you slipped a serious sentence inside a casual one because the casual one gave you cover.
prompt_phrases: [Person] would say something [Person]-shaped about that.
What was the first thing you actually thought, before the thing you said?
It was just — the size of the thing arriving.
I'd rather we both knew that we knew.
Your timing is supernatural.

content.situation: Wednesday evening. The middle of the week. Tom has cooked — slow-roast shoulder of lamb, two kinds of potatoes, a salad, a tart that Anna made this afternoon while Mateo napped. The long wooden table seats seven; Mateo is in the high chair at one end. The doors to the garden are open. It is light until almost nine. The conversations at the table run in parallel — sometimes converging, sometimes splitting. The lesson follows two of them, alternating, with brief moments where they overlap. The speakers at one end are Sophie, Tom, and at one point Mum; the speakers at the other end are Marie, Carlos, Anna. The challenge of the lesson — and the skill being modelled — is following two threads at once.
content.characters: Sophie, Marie, Tom, Carlos, Anna & Mum

phrase_cle.phrase: I'd rather we both knew that we knew
phrase_cle.example: — Why did you say that out loud? It's obvious. — Because I'd rather we both knew that we knew. Obvious is a category that breaks down at three in the morning.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень B2 структура для разделения имплицитного и эксплицитного знания. "Both knew that we knew" — двойной мета-уровень: не просто знаем одно и то же, но знаем, что другой знает, что мы знаем. Tom использует эту фразу, чтобы превратить молчаливое согласие в проговорённое — потому что молчаливое согласие легко разрушается, когда станет тяжело. На B2 эта структура часто появляется в зрелых разговорах между близкими людьми о взаимных обязательствах, рассчитываемой поддержке, нерасспрашиваемом понимании. Звучит по-взрослому, потому что признаёт, что молчаливость — недостаточная гарантия.
phrase_cle.translation: Я бы предпочёл, чтобы мы оба знали, что мы знаем

expressions.meaning: in the manner characteristic of Helena — adjectival use of a person's name to denote a recognisable register or thinking style
compact phrase for the cognitive impact of major news, registered as scale rather than emotion — Carlos's diagnostic about his own thirty-second pause
expressions.expression: the size of the thing arriving

expressions.meaning: I'd rather we make our shared knowledge explicit than rely on assuming it — Tom's compact statement of preference for stated over implied agreements
expressions.expression: I'd rather we both knew that we knew

expressions.meaning: the food-talk surface conversation provided enough rhythm to slip a serious sentence inside without ceremony — Tom's wry self-description
Anna's distinction between chronological position and emotional weight — direct reframing using parallel structure
expressions.expression: You came third in the order. You came first in the importance

content.cultural_note: British long-table dinners — particularly in rented holiday houses — have a specific acoustic. The conversations at each end of the table are loud enough to be private, soft enough to be partially overheard, and the social rule is that overhearing without joining-in is permitted as long as you don't act on what you've heard until invited. Sophie and Tom hearing the Marie/Carlos/Anna conversation and choosing not to lean in ("I'd let it run") is the application of this rule. Mum's interjection from the centre of the table — appearing with the tart — is itself a recognised British dinner-host move: physically interrupting two simultaneous private conversations by being the centre point that requires both to pause. Her line "if everyone could stop telling each other things and remember to eat the pudding" is mock-imperious, affectionate, age-licensed. The closing micro-exchange between Mum and Marie — "come here for a second" / "I'm glad you're here. That's all" — is one of the lesson's quietest moments, deliberately small. The choice of "that's all" as a sentence-ender is British understatement at its most precise: the smallness signals the size of the feeling. Sophie noticing afterwards that Mum called Marie "Mum" and Marie called her back is a gentle structural joke about pregnancy, generations, and the absorption of one mother into another's role for thirty seconds. Tom's instruction — "absolutely not. Have some tart." — is the right British response: notice, don't comment, eat.
content.grammar_focus: The lesson teaches a B2 listening skill: tracking two parallel conversations across a long table. Speaker tags include positional cues — "[at the other end]", "[at the other end, to Tom]" — to help the listener locate each thread. Notice the grammatical contrast between the two threads. Sophie and Tom run on short, witty exchanges with embedded quotation: "Helena would say something Helena-shaped about that." Modal of habitual prediction + invented adjective + relative pronoun. Marie, Carlos, Anna run on longer self-disclosures with embedded reported thought: "I thought 'oh, this is real now' before I thought anything happy. The happy bit came in about thirty seconds." Past simple of cognition + direct quoted thought + adverbial phrase. The two registers are simultaneous because that is how long British dinner tables actually work. Notice also the running joke embedded in tense: Anna says "I came third in the order. I came first in the importance." Carlos's reply — "I don't think that's how priority works" — is a syntax joke about category confusion. Anna's response, "Carlos, I am not going to fight you on the chronology," closes the exchange with a future continuous declarative. The grammar of intimate teasing. Mum's interjection uses second-conditional polite imperative: "If everyone could stop telling each other things and remember to eat the pudding, it would be appreciated." The grammar of mock-formal authority. Finally, Sophie's closing exchange with Tom uses one-word past simple confirmations — "She did." "She did." — twice, as the grammar of two people watching the same thing happen and agreeing without elaboration.

key_vocabulary.note: Британский воскресный стандарт. "Slow-roast shoulder" — стандартный способ готовки.
Marie специально задаёт вопрос Carlos "while Anna is in earshot" — не за её спиной, а так, чтобы Anna слышала.
Carlos использует современное слово из техники в эмоциональном контексте — "the first thirty seconds were just recalibration".
Британский продукт, особенно ассоциируется с Cornwall. Mum добавляет к тарту — географически уместно.
Sophie шутит про timing Mum. Преувеличение для лёгкого эффекта.

comprehension_questions.hint: He had wanted to tell Sophie that Marie would need her over the next eight months, and had been carrying the sentence for two days. The food-talk surface conversation provided enough rhythm to deliver the serious sentence without ceremony. The casual subject was a wrapper for the serious one.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Tom say "the lamb gave me cover"?

comprehension_questions.hint: Chronology versus weight. Carlos was the third thing she thought of after the test, but he was the most important of the three. Anna refuses to let chronological order determine emotional ranking. Carlos's tease ("I don't think that's how priority works") is a syntax joke; her reply ("I am not going to fight you on the chronology") is the closing punchline.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Anna distinguishing when she says "You came third in the order. You came first in the importance"?

comprehension_questions.hint: The slip — if it was a slip — folds Marie momentarily into the maternal category that Marie is now joining biologically. The fact that Marie returns the address is the small mutual recognition. Sophie wants to comment; Tom's instruction ("absolutely not. Have some tart") is the British rule of noticing without underlining.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie notice that Mum called Marie "Mum" and Marie called her back?

content.title: The Phone Call

dialogue.line: Sophie. Apologies for the hour. Two minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Helena. Go ahead.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The petroleum file. The other side filed an amended particulars at six this morning. Their case has narrowed considerably. Have you seen them?
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I haven't. I'm not on email.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Send me the headline. I'll read it now.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Material non-disclosure from 2018 forward. They've dropped the historical claim entirely. The rest is procedural.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: That's actually good. It cuts the discovery cost in half and the case becomes about whether 2018 was a transition year, which we can win.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Quite. I wanted you to know before the team Monday. Are you back Tuesday?
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Tuesday morning.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Read the amended particulars on the train Tuesday and we'll take it from there. Anything else?
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Helena.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I'd like to be clear about something for the rest of today and tomorrow. I'm not on email. I'd rather not take more calls about the file, even good news ones, until Tuesday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Right.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Is that all right?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. I should not need to be told that, and I'm slightly cross with myself for ringing you on a beach in Cornwall. The answer is yes, it's all right. It's better than all right. It's correct.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Have a good last day.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: You too.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: [End call. Sophie holds the phone for about ten seconds. Then she switches it off entirely and puts it in the inside pocket of her jacket. She stands. She walks down to the wet sand and stops at the edge of the water. About ten minutes later, Mum walks down from the house and reaches her.]
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Marie's making tea. I came to find you.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum. I just turned my phone off.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Did you.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Helena rang. About work. I told her I'd rather not be available for the rest of today and tomorrow, and she — apologised. She said it was correct.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, you've never done that. In your whole career, have you?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have not. I learned how to switch the phone off in the late nineties, when you were at university. I am about to be seventy-four. I have done it perhaps thirty times in twenty-five years. It still feels slightly illegal.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: ...I know what you mean.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: But I am proud of you for doing it forty years earlier than I did, and I am annoyed with myself for not having taught you, although I notice that you have learned it without my teaching, which is, I suspect, the only way.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum. That sentence is going to come back to me at strange moments for years.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. That's what they're for. Tea, Sophie. Marie has made it strong.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a moment when you turned your phone off, deliberately, in a setting where you could have justified being available. What did you say to the person who would have called? Or — describe a small adult act of independence you performed earlier than a previous generation could have, and the recognition you got for it from someone of that previous generation.
prompt_phrases: I'm not on email.
I'd rather not take more calls until [time].
It's better than all right. It's correct.
It still feels slightly illegal.
You have learned it without my teaching, which is, I suspect, the only way.

content.situation: Friday morning. The second-to-last day. Sophie has walked to the beach alone at half past seven. The tide is out a long way; the wet sand stretches almost to the horizon. She has brought her phone because she always brings her phone. At seven thirty-five, the phone rings. It is Helena. Sophie almost doesn't answer. She does answer.

phrase_cle.phrase: you have learned it without my teaching, which is, I suspect, the only way
phrase_cle.example: — You always wanted me to learn this from you. — You learned it without my teaching, which is, I suspect, the only way. The teaching version doesn't take.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень B2-к-C1 структура для философского наблюдения о пределах педагогики. Mum признаёт одновременно своё желание научить и невозможность этому научить. Структура: "[verb] without [my/your/her] [verb-ing], which is, I suspect, the only way." Interpolated "I suspect" — мягкий хедж, не настаивающий на универсальности наблюдения. На этом уровне B2 встречает структуры, которые он узнаёт, но ещё не может производить свободно — это правильно. Фраза часто появляется в зрелых разговорах о родительстве, наставничестве, обучении взрослых тому, что никто не может сообщить через инструкцию.
phrase_cle.translation: ты научилась этому без моего обучения, что, подозреваю, и есть единственный способ

expressions.meaning: compact modern shorthand for being deliberately unreachable through work channels — used here as a brief explanation that requires no further qualification
professional shorthand using verbal economy to deliver legal-procedural news — "discovery" is the formal pre-trial document exchange
expressions.expression: It cuts the discovery cost in half

expressions.meaning: Helena's compact reframing — Sophie's request is not merely tolerable but actively the right thing; "correct" carries moral and practical weight
expressions.expression: It's better than all right. It's correct

expressions.meaning: Mum's wry phrase for the persistent sense that switching off feels wrong even decades after learning it — the residual conditioning of a working life
Mum's compact philosophy — some things cannot be taught and can only be learned independently; the absence of teaching is the precondition for the learning

content.cultural_note: British professional culture has been changing slowly around the question of availability outside working hours. Lawyers, in particular, have long operated on a model where partners and senior associates were assumed to be reachable on holidays. The shift toward explicit boundaries is recent — visible in the last ten years — and uneven across firms. Sophie's act in this lesson is small and significant: she does not refuse the call, she takes the headline, she gives a useful response, and then she names the boundary. Helena's reaction is the marker of a senior partner who has internalised the new norm even if her own habits haven't fully caught up. "I'm slightly cross with myself for ringing you on a beach in Cornwall" is the precise British phrasing for self-correction — small, direct, irrevocable. Mum's exchange afterwards is the quiet centre of the lesson: a generation that learned to switch the phone off perhaps thirty times in twenty-five years recognising that her daughter has done it without instruction. The line "some things can only be learned independently" is not new philosophically, but its application here — to the small act of putting a phone in an inside pocket — is precisely sized for the moment. Mum's closing instruction ("Tea, Sophie. Marie has made it strong.") returns the conversation to the ordinary register of the holiday. The grammar of caring about one's daughter, in this register, is the temperature of the tea.
content.grammar_focus: Modal verbs of professional preference and personal boundary, layered. Sophie's central sentence: "I'd like to be clear about something for the rest of today and tomorrow. I'm not on email. I'd rather not take more calls about the file, even good news ones, until Tuesday." Modal of preference (would like) + adjective complement + temporal frame + negative present (not on email) + would rather + negative gerund + concessive clause + temporal end-point. The grammar is doing precision work — Sophie isn't refusing to talk to Helena ever, she is specifying duration, channel, exception, and resumption. Helena's response uses unusual modal stacking: "I should not need to be told that, and I'm slightly cross with myself for ringing you on a beach in Cornwall." Modal of obligation negated (should not need) + passive infinitive (to be told) + present continuous of state (am cross) + reflexive (with myself) + gerund of action (for ringing). Helena is grammatically locating her own failure with precision. Mum's long sentence near the end is one of the lesson's grammatical centrepieces: "I am proud of you for doing it forty years earlier than I did, and I am annoyed with myself for not having taught you, although I notice that you have learned it without my teaching, which is, I suspect, the only way." Two parallel emotion + reflexive + gerund clauses, then a concessive clause with embedded interpolated I suspect, then a final complement. Six grammatical moves in one sentence. The precision is the point — Mum is thinking through her own teaching, her own failure to teach, and the philosophical observation that some things are unteachable, all in one breath.

key_vocabulary.note: Юридический термин. Particulars — детали иска. Helena сообщает, что противная сторона подала изменённую версию.
Юридический термин. Pre-trial обмен документами между сторонами. Sophie говорит, что новая версия cuts the discovery cost in half.
Sophie: "send me the headline." Метафорическое использование — газетный заголовок как способ компрессии.
Helena использует сильнее, чем "all right" или "fine". Не просто разрешение, а признание правильности действия.
Phrasal verb. О телефоне, об устройстве, в переносном смысле — об отключении внимания. "To switch the phone off" — стандартное использование.

comprehension_questions.hint: She doesn't refuse it. She takes the headline, gives a useful professional response ("that's actually good — it cuts the discovery cost in half"), and then names her boundary for the rest of the holiday. The grammar of professional precision combined with personal limit. Helena recognises the shape immediately.
comprehension_questions.question: How does Sophie handle Helena's call?

comprehension_questions.hint: "All right" would be permission. "Correct" is recognition that Sophie's boundary is the right thing — not merely tolerated but actively appropriate. Helena's correction of her own first instinct ("better than all right") signals that she is updating her own register in real time.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Helena's "It's correct" do that "It's all right" wouldn't?

comprehension_questions.hint: Some independence cannot be transmitted through instruction. Mum acknowledges both her wish to have taught Sophie this and her observation that the teachable version of the lesson would not have taken. The lesson had to be learned independently for it to land. A philosophical aside delivered in the register of practical pride.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Mum say "you have learned it without my teaching, which is, I suspect, the only way"?

content.title: Driving Back

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I haven't been able to say something all week, and I want to say it now while we're driving, because I don't want to look at you while I say it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'll keep my eyes on the road.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am not unequivocally pleased about being pregnant.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I am pleased. And I am not unequivocally pleased. The two are not the same. I have been carrying both since Friday before we drove down, and I have not been able to put them in front of anyone, including Tom, in this combination, because I think it would sound like I'm not pleased, and I am. But there's a — there's a part of me that is mourning the next eighteen months I had imagined, and I notice that, and I haven't told anyone.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Don't say anything kind yet. Let me finish.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Okay.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I told Anna it was complicated. I told my mother. I told you in pieces. But I haven't said the whole sentence to anyone, including myself, until just now. The sentence is — "I am pleased and I am also slightly grieving, and the two coexist."
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You can speak now.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. Thank you for telling me without dressing it up.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...That's the right thing to say.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I knew because the week was wrong. You were — you were performing pleased. I noticed but I didn't push because it wasn't my push to make.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I knew you knew. I think that's why I could say it now. The car gave me cover.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm. Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I want to ask one thing, and you don't have to answer.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Are you going to tell Tom?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Yes. Tonight, probably. He won't be hurt. He's been doing his own version of this — being unequivocally happy in front of me because he thinks one of us has to be. We have been quietly allowing each other to perform, and it isn't going to work for the next eight months.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That's a hard conversation.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's a necessary one. Mum told me — your Mum told me, on Wednesday — that the timeline rearranged itself, and I don't owe it a reaction. I have been thinking about that all week. The thing I do owe Tom is to stop performing in his direction, because the performing is its own kind of distance.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: ...That's a sentence.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have been working on it since Wednesday.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. Can I say something?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You are about to be a mother. You are also still you. The first does not erase the second, and anyone who tells you it should is selling you a postcard.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...You took Anna's phrase.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm using it back at you. It's a good phrase.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is a good phrase.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Thank you for the year. I don't know how else to say it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I don't need it said any other way. Same to you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: ...Pull off at the next services. I want a coffee that isn't from a flask.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: On it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a moment in a car or on a long journey when someone said something they had not been able to say at home. Why did the moving setting permit it? Or — describe a feeling you have been carrying in two parts that you couldn't name in front of anyone. What was the whole sentence, when you finally found it?
prompt_phrases: I am pleased. And I am not unequivocally pleased.
Thank you for telling me without dressing it up.
You were performing pleased.
The performing is its own kind of distance.
Thank you for the year.

content.situation: Sunday morning. The week is over. Carlos and Anna left at seven with Mateo. Tom drove Mum to the station at eight-fifteen. Sophie and Marie are driving back to London together — Marie's car, Marie driving for the first leg, Sophie taking over at the services in Devon. They have been on the road forty minutes. Cornwall is behind them. They have not yet spoken much. The conversation that follows is the lesson's quiet centre and the course's structural close. It is the last conversation between Sophie and Marie in B2.

phrase_cle.phrase: The first does not erase the second
phrase_cle.example: — You're about to become a parent. — Yes, and I'm also still me. The first does not erase the second.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень B2 структура для отказа от ложной логической импликации — что одно становление автоматически отменяет предыдущую идентичность. Sophie использует это в финале B2 как структурный итог уровня: люди не заменяются своими новыми ролями, они их добавляют. Глагол "to erase" — точный, физический; альтернатива "replace" была бы слабее. Структура часто появляется в зрелых разговорах о материнстве, профессиональных переходах, потерях, переездах — везде, где культурный нарратив ожидает замены, а реальность даёт сложение. Финальный B2 урок ставит этот принцип явно — Sophie теперь может произнести то, что Mum говорила ей в L15, своими словами.

expressions.meaning: without any ambiguity or reservation — formal adverb used by Marie about an emotional state, naming exactly what she is not
to present a thing in a more flattering or simplified form than it actually has — Sophie's compact praise for Marie's directness
putting on the face of pleasure as a social act, even in private — Sophie's diagnostic phrase, gerund + adjective construction
the labour of presenting an emotion you don't fully feel becomes a barrier to the person you're presenting it to — Marie's diagnostic about her marriage
expressions.expression: the performing is its own kind of distance

expressions.meaning: Marie's compressed acknowledgement of what twelve months of friendship through change have meant — deliberately small phrasing for a deliberately large gratitude

content.cultural_note: British car conversations are their own genre, particularly long-distance ones with two people. The driver has somewhere to look that isn't the passenger's face. The passenger has somewhere to look that isn't the driver's face. The forward motion provides rhythm. Things get said in cars that don't get said at kitchen tables. Marie's choice to wait until they are on the motorway rather than say her sentence in the house is deliberate; she names it ("I don't want to look at you while I say it"), which is itself unusual British directness. The lesson's structural achievement is the way it draws together the entire B2 vocabulary into one compact final scene. Mum's phrase from L27 ("the timeline rearranged itself") returns in Marie's reasoning. Anna's phrase from L27 ("selling you a postcard") returns in Sophie's reframe. Helena's structural lesson from Block 1 (direct over softened) appears in Sophie's response ("thank you for telling me without dressing it up"). Eleanor's principle from L23 (the answer arriving sideways) is enacted: this is the answer to everything that has happened in B2, arriving sideways in a car driving north on the M5. The course's emotional anchor — Edinburgh in A1, A2, B1 — is replaced for B2 by Cornwall, and the final conversation does not happen at the destination. It happens in motion, between places, on the way home. "Pull off at the next services. I want a coffee that isn't from a flask" is the right closing register: small, practical, ordinary. The course's last word is "on it" — the British two-syllable verb-of-acceptance. The B2 student is now someone who can hear, produce, and live inside that compactness.
content.grammar_focus: The lesson deploys every grammatical tool the course has built, and uses irony as structural device — direct meaning ≠ actual meaning, and both speakers know it. Marie's central self-disclosure: "I am pleased. And I am not unequivocally pleased. The two are not the same." Three short declarative sentences, parallel structure, with the negation "not unequivocally" doing precise work. "Pleased" is true; "unequivocally pleased" is not; the difference is the entire grammar. Sophie's response uses gerund + adjective: "You were performing pleased." The construction is unusual — performing is normally followed by a noun ("performing the role of pleased") but here is followed directly by the adjective, creating a compressed, modern phrasing. Marie's later sentence: "We have been quietly allowing each other to perform, and it isn't going to work for the next eight months." Present perfect continuous (have been allowing) + infinitive (to perform) + future negative (isn't going to work) + temporal frame. The grammar of two adults updating a previously functional arrangement that has stopped functioning. Sophie's closing reframe: "You are about to be a mother. You are also still you. The first does not erase the second." Two be-verbs in parallel + a third sentence deploying "erase" as the precise verb of false logical implication. The grammar of identity as additive rather than substitutive — the structural lesson of the entire B2 level. Notice also the closing exchange: Marie's "Thank you for the year" is grammatically minimal — noun phrase + prepositional phrase. Sophie's reply — "I don't need it said any other way. Same to you." — uses passive past participle (it said) + same-construction. The compressed British register of two people who have been speaking grammatically together for so long that they no longer need full sentences.

key_vocabulary.note: Formal adverb. Marie использует в самой важной фразе: "not unequivocally pleased". Сила слова в его точности.
Marie признаёт, что mourning the next eighteen months she had imagined. Сильное слово в применении к воображаемой жизни.
Sophie благодарит Marie за то, что она не "dressed up" свою новость. Phrasal verb метафорически — про приукрашивание правды.
Театральный глагол в эмоциональном использовании. "Performing pleased" — играть радость. Современное.
Британский термин для motorway services — заправка, кафе, туалеты у трассы. "Pull off at the next services" — стандартная фраза.

comprehension_questions.hint: She wants to deliver it without being looked at — "I don't want to look at you while I say it." The forward motion of the car provides somewhere to direct her gaze that isn't Sophie's face. The setting is part of the message: the things you can say in a moving car are the things you can't say at a kitchen table.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Marie wait until the car to say her sentence?

comprehension_questions.hint: "I am pleased and I am also slightly grieving, and the two coexist." The grief is for the eighteen months she had imagined — the planned-for version of her life that has been quietly displaced. Marie has been working on the sentence since Wednesday; the precision is the result of the working.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Marie's full sentence about her pregnancy, the one she hadn't said to anyone before?

comprehension_questions.hint: It applies to motherhood the structural principle Mum gave Sophie in L15: identities accumulate, they don't substitute. Marie is becoming a mother and is also still herself. The B2 course's final formulation of its central lesson, delivered between two friends in a car, in their own voice, having earned it across thirty lessons.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Sophie's closing reframe — "the first does not erase the second" — do for Marie?

content.title: The Omission

dialogue.line: You're standing oddly.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm standing the way I always stand.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You're standing the way you stand when you're holding something. You're holding something.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm holding a glass of water.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Don't 'mm' me. You sound like Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'll take that, frankly. How's Manchester.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Cold. Productive. The usual.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And how's Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Fine.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Fine.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Fine, Marie. She's fine.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's just that you've been here for forty minutes and you've talked about three colleagues, two clients, and a man on the train who, I think you said, was eating a pie wrong. And not Eleanor. Which is — by your standards — loud.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I don't have a standard for how often I mention Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You absolutely do. The standard is: constantly. So when it drops to zero, I notice.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: There it is.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: She texted me on the train.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: And the partnership review is in March. Mid-March. Which she knew before I did, because of course she did, and which she chose to tell me before the firm did, which is — and I want to be careful here — either generous or strategic, and I haven't decided which.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Could it be both.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It could be both. That's part of the problem.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Why is that a problem.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Because if it's both, I have to thank her for the generous part and stay alert about the strategic part, and doing both at the same time is — exhausting. And I'm tired before I've even got to the actual review.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: So why didn't you mention it earlier.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Because I wanted forty minutes of not being someone who's about to be reviewed for partnership. I wanted to be the person who tells you about the man on the train.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Fair.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I know it's a luxury.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's not a luxury. It's a strategy. Don't dignify it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Tell me about the pie.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a recent moment when you held back a piece of news from someone close to you for a stretch of time before mentioning it. What was the omission about? What did the not-mentioning do for you? When you finally said it, how did the other person respond?
prompt_phrases: I didn't say it for the first half hour because…
It wasn't that I was hiding it. It was that…
When I finally mentioned it, she/he said…

content.situation: Sophie has come down from Manchester for the weekend. Late February, Marie thirty-eight weeks pregnant. They are in the kitchen of the flat Marie shares with Tom in Hackney; Tom is at his sister's. Eleanor sent Sophie a text on the train that has not, between Sophie and Marie, been mentioned, although Sophie is the kind of person who, when she has not mentioned something, leaves the unmentioned thing audibly hovering. Marie is the kind of person who, when something is hovering, does not reach for it immediately but waits to see whether it lands of its own accord.

phrase_cle.phrase: Either generous or strategic, and I haven't decided which.
phrase_cle.example: He sent flowers the day before the review. Either generous or strategic, and I haven't decided which.
phrase_cle.explanation: Ключевая для C1 конструкция: говорящий удерживает в подвешенном состоянии две возможные интерпретации одного действия и отказывается выбирать между ними. Это не нерешительность, а намеренная позиция — приём, который Sophie усвоила за два года под Helena. Обрати внимание на структуру: 'either X or Y, and I haven't decided which' — последняя часть превращает дилемму из риторической в живую. Собеседник, услышавший такую фразу, обычно понимает, что встревать с однозначным мнением — бестактно.
phrase_cle.translation: Либо щедро, либо расчётливо, и я ещё не решила, что именно.

expressions.meaning: Treats the listener's habitual baseline as a measuring instrument; what would be quiet for someone else is loud for them.
Signals the speaker is about to make a finely-judged distinction and wants the listener to read it precisely, not generously.
expressions.register: neutral-formal, common in legal and diplomatic English
expressions.expression: I want to be careful here

expressions.meaning: Refuse to give a thing a more flattering name than it deserves; usually said of self-deception.
Holds two possible motives in suspension without resolving them — a hallmark of how Sophie now thinks aloud.
expressions.expression: Either generous or strategic, and I haven't decided which

expressions.meaning: Non-committal vocalisation that, depending on context, can mean: 'I heard you', 'I don't believe you', or 'go on'. Marie uses it to invite, not to judge.

content.cultural_note: British conversational omission deserves a note of its own. In many cultures, what is left unsaid is simply absent; in British middle-class speech, what is left unsaid is often the most important thing in the room, and both speakers know it. The technique on display here — Marie noticing the omission rather than asking the question, Sophie acknowledging the omission with a pause rather than a confession — is a register that is acquired late and is one of the markers that separates B2 from C1. A learner can know every word in this dialogue and still miss what is happening in it. The lesson is that what is happening, in this case, is not the words.
content.grammar_focus: This dialogue rests on three C1 patterns. First, the cleft with 'is' as a delayed predicate — 'which is, and I want to be careful here, either generous or strategic' — where Sophie defers the predicate to insert a reflexive comment about her own reasoning. Second, the modal hedge 'could it be both' as a question that proposes rather than asks; Marie is offering an answer in interrogative form, which is more characteristic of advanced spoken English than the bare statement. Third, the omission as syntactic act: Sophie says nothing ('...') and that nothing carries a propositional load. Marie's response — 'There it is' — names the omission as if it were an object that has just appeared.

key_vocabulary.note: Используется метафорически про невысказанные темы, которые остаются в воздухе между собеседниками.
Чаще встречается в негативной форме: 'don't dignify it with a name', 'don't dignify it with a response'. Идиома на грани формального и разговорного.
Конструкция 'by [someone's] standards' — устоявшаяся; работает с любым именем или местоимением. Может быть как комплиментом, так и упрёком, в зависимости от интонации.
Здесь используется метафорически: частота упоминаний упала до нуля. Marie применяет статистический язык к разговорному поведению — приём, характерный для иронического C1.

В британских юридических фирмах partnership review — формальная процедура оценки кандидата на повышение до партнёра-собственника. Происходит обычно раз в год; решение принимает существующее партнёрство.

comprehension_questions.hint: Think about what Marie is measuring. The loudness is not in what Sophie said but in what she didn't say.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Marie mean when she says 'which is — by your standards — loud'?

comprehension_questions.hint: Consider what role she is trying to step out of, and what role she is trying to step into — even temporarily.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie say she wanted forty minutes of being 'the person who tells you about the man on the train'?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is not changing the subject. She is making a small, deliberate gesture about what kind of friend she intends to be in this moment. What is the gesture?
comprehension_questions.question: When Marie says 'Tell me about the pie' at the end, what is she doing?

content.title: Tom

dialogue.line: There's coffee.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Thanks.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sleep all right?
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Patchy. Your spare room has views on whether I should be there.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It does that. We meant to fix the radiator. We didn't fix the radiator.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Three weeks until a baby and you're still saying you meant to fix things.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah. That's the problem.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Can I ask you something.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Did your dad — sorry, I shouldn't lead with your dad. Forget that. Let me start again.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Take your time.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: When men talk about being scared of being a father, what they usually mean is they're scared of being a bad one. That's the script. I've heard it. I've said it.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: And.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And that isn't quite what I'm scared of.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm scared of being competent. I'm scared that I'll do all the things — nappies, bottles, school run, the lot — and Marie will look at me one Tuesday in 2031 and realise I've been operating the role rather than living it. And by then it'll be late.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Hm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I haven't said this to her.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I gathered.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm not asking you to. I'm asking — I don't know what I'm asking. Whether it's a normal fear. Whether saying it out loud makes it real.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: It's a normal fear. Saying it out loud doesn't make it real. Saying it out loud is what people do when they suspect a fear isn't quite the right shape, and they're trying to feel its actual edges.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'd say something else, but I don't want to.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Say it.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: If you've already got the sentence 'I've been operating the role rather than living it' available to you in February, you're not going to be the man who realises it in 2031. The men who realise it in 2031 don't have that sentence. That's why they don't realise it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: More coffee?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Thanks.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a fear of yours — about a role you've taken on or are about to take on (work, family, friendship, a creative project). Don't describe the surface of the fear. Try to find what Tom calls 'the actual edges' — the precise version of the worry that doesn't fit the standard script.
prompt_phrases: The script would say I'm scared of…
But what I'm actually scared of is…
I haven't said it out loud before because…

content.situation: Sunday morning. Marie is asleep — eight and a half months pregnant, exhausted, taking the chance. Tom is at the kitchen table with the Saturday paper folded at the cricket page he hasn't read. Sophie comes down for coffee. Tom doesn't usually start conversations of this weight, and Sophie does not usually receive them. The fact of Marie being upstairs and unconscious changes both of them. This is the first central scene in the course where Tom speaks for himself, not as Marie's partner. The British male register here works at full strength: short sentences, sideways admissions, humour as load-bearing wall.

phrase_cle.phrase: I've been operating the role rather than living it.
phrase_cle.example: I've been operating the role of senior editor for three years. Living it is a different question.
phrase_cle.explanation: Конструкция, которую Tom произносит как самодиагноз, — пример того, как современный английский язык впитывает терапевтическую лексику и возвращает её обратно в обыденную речь. 'To operate' здесь стоит против 'to live' как механическое против органического. Грамматически это present perfect continuous, который переносит точку наблюдения в гипотетическое будущее (2031) и оттуда оглядывается на годы, ещё не прожитые. Это очень C1: время как геометрия, не как линия. Конструкция 'X rather than Y' с двумя глаголами в одной форме — основа: she's tolerating it rather than enjoying it; we were managing the situation rather than addressing it. Запоминается легко, работает в десятках контекстов.
phrase_cle.translation: Я не жил в этой роли — я её выполнял.

expressions.meaning: To perform the actions of a part — parent, partner, professional — competently while remaining emotionally outside it. A specifically modern, slightly therapeutic-sounding metaphor that Tom has clearly thought about before saying.
expressions.expression: To operate the role rather than live it

expressions.meaning: I worked it out from context without being told. Drier and more economical than 'I figured' or 'I guessed'; a marker of British understatement.
An invitation to slow down — given without urgency or anxiety. In this context Sophie is offering Tom permission to construct a difficult sentence at his own pace.
The precise contours of a feeling, as distinct from its rough shape. Sophie's metaphor implies that fears have geometry, and that talking is a way of running one's hand along it.
There is a conventional, expected version of what a person says in this situation, and the speaker is naming it before stepping outside it.

content.cultural_note: The British male register has a specific way of handling emotional disclosure that is not absence of emotion but its sideways delivery. Tom's confession is structurally indirect: he begins by referencing 'the script' (the conventional worry), names what he is *not* afraid of, then pivots to what he is. The pauses are load-bearing — '...' carries as much weight as the sentences. The closing exchange — 'Sophie.' / 'Mm.' / 'Thanks.' — would, in a more demonstrative register, expand to a full thank-you; here it is compressed to three words, and that compression is itself the gratitude. Sophie reads the register correctly and replies in kind. Notice she doesn't say 'you're welcome' or anything resembling closure; she absorbs the thanks and the conversation moves on. In this register, naming a moment too explicitly destroys it.
content.grammar_focus: Tom's central admission uses a structure that rewards study. 'I'm scared that I'll do all the things — nappies, bottles, school run, the lot — and Marie will look at me one Tuesday in 2031 and realise I've been operating the role rather than living it.' The sentence is one syntactic spine with a parenthetical list, a future projection set on a specific day ('one Tuesday in 2031' — a C1 device that converts an abstract worry into a scene), and a present perfect continuous ('I've been operating') that retroactively colours years not yet lived. Sophie's reply uses a different C1 device: the conditional reframe. 'If you've already got the sentence available to you in February, you're not going to be the man who realises it in 2031.' She uses Tom's own metaphor (the sentence as a thing one possesses) and inverts the temporal logic of his fear. Notice also Tom's repeated use of '...' — the ellipsis that, in this male register, performs the function of a long exhalation.

key_vocabulary.note: О сне, погоде, информации, успехе. 'Patchy sleep' — сон, который то был, то нет.
Британское разговорное окончание перечислений. 'Nappies, bottles, school run, the lot.' Эквивалент американского 'the whole nine yards', но короче и без литературщины.
key_vocabulary.translation: и всё прочее, и всё такое

key_vocabulary.note: Из журналистики ('to lead with a story') в обыденную речь. Tom использует это самокритично: он понимает, что упоминание отца Sophie — слишком тяжёлый старт, и отыгрывает назад.
Концептуальное использование: Tom боится не неудачи, а успешного выполнения. C1-уровневый перенос: слово, обычно положительное, превращается в источник тревоги.
Из строительной лексики. 'Humour as load-bearing wall' — юмор, на котором держится разговор. Метафора, характерная для C1.
key_vocabulary.translation: несущая (о стене); метафорически: то, на чём держится конструкция

comprehension_questions.hint: Think about why someone would name the conventional version of a fear before stepping past it. What does naming the script do?
comprehension_questions.question: What does Tom mean by 'the script', and why does he name it before saying what he actually fears?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is making a claim about the relationship between language and self-awareness. Try to state it in your own words.
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie says: 'The men who realise it in 2031 don't have that sentence.' What is her argument here?

comprehension_questions.hint: Consider the cultural note. What does saying less, in this register, actually do?
comprehension_questions.question: At the end Tom says only 'Sophie. / Mm. / Thanks.' — three words. Why doesn't he say more?

content.title: The Routine Appointment

dialogue.line: She said the head's engaged. I think that's good.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It's good.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is good. It also means you'll feel like you're carrying a bowling ball between your knees for two weeks. I am sorry to be the one to tell you.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Thank you, that's exactly the encouragement I needed.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I should have led with the reassurance. Force of habit.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: What habit.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Telling Marie's mother what was actually happening, when she was being polite about it. We had a sort of system, the two of us. She gave the optimistic version to Marie. I gave the operational version.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I have never heard about this system in twenty-eight years.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: No, well. The system worked partly because you didn't know about it.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Were you like this with Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: About what.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: About — having her. Operational.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I was almost entirely operational, with Sophie. I should say, in my defence, that this was not by temperament.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: What was it by.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Necessity. Your father was — present, but not to be relied upon for the operational side of things. He was good at the optimistic version. The optimistic version was where he lived.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: That came out sharper than I meant.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Did it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Possibly not. I don't know. The truth is I haven't talked about your birth in this room before, and the room is making me sound like a witness.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: What's the operational version. Of having Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Long. Twenty-six hours. They sent me home twice. The second time I told them I wasn't going home, and a midwife I had never met before said 'right' and put me in a room. I owe that midwife my entire favourable view of the NHS, which has survived several governments since.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Why are you telling us this now.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because Marie asked. And because I've been carrying this story around with the optimistic version on top of it for thirty-three years, and the optimistic version has done its job, and the operational version is — also true. Both true. I'm too old to keep them sorted into separate drawers.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I should add — none of this is meant as warning. You are not me. Tom is not your father. The room you give birth in will not be the room I gave birth in. This is information, not weather.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Information, not weather. I'm taking that one.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's yours. With my compliments.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a story from your own family that was always told in 'the optimistic version' — and what the operational version might be, if you were to tell it. Or: tell her about a phrase someone older than you once gave you that you have kept.
prompt_phrases: The version I always heard was…
But the operational version, I think, is…
She/he said it once and I've kept it. The phrase was…

content.situation: Wednesday afternoon. Sophie has stayed in London — taken the days off. Mum has come down from Cedar View on the train; Marie wanted her at the thirty-eight-week appointment, and Mum, who does not often travel south anymore, said yes without commentary. Sophie joined them in the waiting room. The midwife has just gone to fetch a chart. The three women are alone in a small NHS consultation room — pale-blue walls, a poster about iron supplements, a chair that wheels too easily. Mum is in the chair in the corner. This is the first time in the course Mum talks, in the present, about giving birth to Sophie. The register she chooses is unusual.

phrase_cle.example: I'm telling you what the contract actually says. Information, not weather.
phrase_cle.explanation: Авторская формула Mum, которую Sophie немедленно опознаёт как ценную и забирает себе. Принцип такой: говорящий передаёт нечто, что слушатель может использовать (information), и при этом не хочет, чтобы это нечто давило на слушателя как атмосферный фон, на который реагировать невозможно (weather). На C1 ты учишься различать эти два режима как при получении информации, так и при её передаче. Конструкция 'X, not Y' через запятую — короткая, рабочая, прекрасно встраивается в письменный и устный регистр: 'a question, not an accusation'; 'a description, not a verdict'; 'a habit, not a decision'. Освой схему — и заметишь, что многие сложные фразы структурно сводятся к ней.

expressions.meaning: The version of an event that records what actually happened, mechanically and without softening. Mum opposes this to 'the optimistic version', which is reassuring but partial. The pair of terms is hers; Sophie immediately recognises the value of having them.
An automatic behaviour acquired over years that surfaces unbidden. Used here as a quiet, slightly self-mocking apology.

A self-coined distinction: facts that the listener can act on (information) versus atmospheric conditions that simply press on the listener and produce no useful response (weather). Mum makes this distinction so that her story doesn't oppress Marie. Sophie keeps the phrase.

A precisely judged criticism — the person was physically there but could not be counted on for the function under discussion. The phrasing is gentle enough to be repeated in front of the absent person's daughter.
expressions.expression: Present, but not to be relied upon

expressions.meaning: An age-justified admission that the speaker no longer has the energy or the wish to maintain a tidy separation between two truths. The drawer metaphor turns memory into furniture.
expressions.expression: I'm too old to keep them sorted into separate drawers

content.cultural_note: The phrase 'with my compliments' — used by Mum to hand Sophie a sentence — comes from a particular British register where small social gifts are wrapped in mock-formal language, and the mock-formality is the affection. A waiter saying 'a glass of champagne, with the compliments of the house' uses the same construction. Mum, by transposing it from material to verbal gifts, signals tenderness through indirection. This is a register that British people of a certain background grow up hearing and wielding without thinking; for learners, hearing it deployed in this way is itself a lesson in what English can do at its higher registers. The closing exchange between Mum and Sophie — short, formal, layered with private affection — is a small masterpiece of British understatement.
content.grammar_focus: Mum's speech in this lesson uses a register that is rare in spoken English and central to C1: the formal-narrative voice deployed in a casual setting. 'I owe that midwife my entire favourable view of the NHS, which has survived several governments since' — the syntax (subject + verb + object + non-restrictive relative clause with present perfect) belongs to written prose, but Mum delivers it in conversation. This is not pretension; it is a generation and a class for whom certain registers are habitual. Notice the inversion implicit in 'The room you give birth in will not be the room I gave birth in' — a rhythmic parallelism with quiet emphatic contrast. And the closing phrase 'with my compliments' — a fixed expression normally found on restaurant bills (compliments of the chef) — here transposed into the gift of a sentence. C1 students should hear this register as available to them, not as decorative.

key_vocabulary.note: Медицинский термин: голова ребёнка вошла в таз матери, готовясь к родам. Британский медицинский английский.
Терминологическая пара Mum, не общеупотребительная. Запоминай как авторскую конструкцию: контраст между смягчённым рассказом и фактическим.
Параллельная конструкция, объясняющая мотив поведения. 'Not by temperament — by necessity' — формальная структура, противопоставляющая внутреннее свойство и внешнее обстоятельство.
Mum использует это для частичного отыгрывания собственного тона. На C1 такие фразы — инструмент тонкой регулировки регистра в реальном времени.
key_vocabulary.word: to come out sharper than I meant

key_vocabulary.note: Метафорическое: организовать в памяти или в жизни как мебельные ящики. Британский разговорный образ; характерен для людей, читавших много художественной прозы.
key_vocabulary.word: to keep (things) sorted into separate drawers

key_vocabulary.note: Формальная фраза, обычно от ресторана клиенту. Mum переносит её на устный подарок (фразу). Это очень характерный для английского приём — взять формализм и применить его в неожиданном контексте.
key_vocabulary.translation: с моими комплиментами / от меня в подарок

comprehension_questions.hint: Consider why someone might maintain two parallel accounts of the same event for thirty years — and what changes that makes maintaining them feel unnecessary.
comprehension_questions.question: What is the difference Mum draws between 'the optimistic version' and 'the operational version'? Why does she suggest she's no longer keeping them apart?

comprehension_questions.hint: Think about how a story about birth could land on someone who is about to give birth. Mum is making sure her story doesn't function in a particular way.
comprehension_questions.question: Mum says: 'This is information, not weather.' What is she trying to do for Marie with this distinction?

comprehension_questions.hint: Sophie is acknowledging something specific about the phrase. Why does this phrase, in particular, register as worth keeping?
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie say 'I'm taking that one' at the end?

content.title: Helena Calls

dialogue.line: Where are you.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: On the Pendolino, somewhere north of Crewe. Why.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I wanted to know whether you could speak. You can speak.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I can speak.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I won't keep you long. I heard about the review date this afternoon. Mid-March, fourteenth, in case nobody has told you. Which I assume nobody has.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Eleanor told me on Monday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Did she. Well done, Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I'm not being arch. I mean it. She told you before the firm did. Make a note of it.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I have.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The reason I'm ringing — not to congratulate you, you're not in yet, and besides I never congratulate anyone in advance, it's bad luck and it's also conceptually incoherent. The reason I'm ringing is to ask you a question, and I'd like you to consider the question for a few days before you answer it. Don't answer it tonight. Don't answer it on the train. Are you somewhere quiet.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I'm somewhere with strangers, but they have headphones.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That'll do. Here's the question. What are you going to do with it.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I'll repeat it because trains have echoes. What are you going to do with it. Assume you get partnership in March. Assume you get the equity track. Assume the firm hands it to you with a ribbon. What are you going to do with it. The question is not whether you want it. I take that as read. The question is what you intend to use it for.
