Because the people I have watched fail at partnership in twenty-eight years of doing this — and I've watched a number — failed because they answered that question after they got the role, not before. And by then the role had answered the question for them.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Don't say anything now.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I wasn't going to.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. The other thing — and this is the bit you'll want to think about more carefully — there is a permitted answer here, which is 'I want it because I want it'. That is a perfectly defensible answer. People build whole careers on it. I am not telling you it's the wrong answer. I am telling you that if it is your answer, you should know that it is your answer, rather than discovering it is your answer by accident in 2030.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Helena.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Why are you giving me this.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because nobody gave it to me, and I had to spend the first eight years of partnership working it out under fire. I'm fond of you. Don't make a thing of it.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I won't.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Get off the train. Talk to me on Monday. Or don't. Up to you.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

pratique.scenario: Imagine Helena has rung you with her question — but about a different thing in your life. A promotion. A move. A relationship. A purchase. A creative project. What is the thing, what would Helena's question be in your version, and what is your honest first attempt at an answer? Don't aim for a polished answer; aim for an honest one.
prompt_phrases: The thing in question is…
Helena's question, translated into my situation, would be…
My first honest answer is…

content.situation: Thursday evening. Sophie is on the train back to Manchester after a long week in London — she's spent two days in chambers in the morning and the afternoons with Marie. The carriage is half empty, the lights are bright, and the train is somewhere between Crewe and Stockport. Helena rings, which Helena rarely does at this hour. Sophie picks up because Helena rarely rings at this hour. The conversation lasts seven minutes and contains, in Helena's tradition, exactly one question that matters and several decoys.

phrase_cle.phrase: What are you going to do with it.
phrase_cle.example: Assume you get the funding. Assume you get the team. What are you going to do with it.
phrase_cle.explanation: Helena произносит эту фразу как констатацию — без вопросительной интонации (это и графически отражено: точка, не вопросительный знак). На C1 ты учишься различать вопросы, заданные ради ответа сейчас, и вопросы, заданные ради ответа потом — последние не требуют немедленной реакции и часто формально не отмечены как вопросительные. Helena намеренно лишает фразу вопросительной мелодики, чтобы Sophie не отвечала. Конструкция 'What are you going to do with X' — рабочий каркас для любого C1-уровня размышления о ресурсе или возможности: отношения, время, деньги, должность, репутация. Освой схему: hypothesis → question, без подушек.
phrase_cle.translation: Что ты собираешься с этим делать.

expressions.meaning: Telephone-call opener that signals brevity and respect for the listener's time. A British convention; Americans more often plunge in.

I am not being knowingly clever or ironically distant. Speakers use this when they suspect their delivery has sounded wry, and they want to disclaim that reading. 'Arch' here means deliberately, performatively wry.

An idea that contradicts itself at the level of definition rather than fact. Helena uses it about congratulating-in-advance: you can't congratulate someone for something not yet achieved. The phrase belongs to the register of sharp legal or academic minds in casual mode.

I assume that as established without further discussion. From the parliamentary and committee tradition where a document is 'taken as read' (treated as if read aloud and entered into the record). Common in British professional speech.
Don't dwell on this; don't return to it; don't elevate it. Used after a small piece of warmth or vulnerability to immediately deflect attention from it. A British register marker.
expressions.expression: Don't make a thing of it

expressions.meaning: Figuring something out while the consequences of getting it wrong are already arriving. Military metaphor used in professional contexts; signals self-awareness without self-pity.

content.cultural_note: Helena's call is an example of British professional mentorship in its harder, more useful form. There is no validation here. There is no encouragement. There is one carefully placed question and a single sentence of warmth — 'I'm fond of you' — followed immediately by 'don't make a thing of it', which both prevents Sophie from over-investing in the warmth and protects Helena from having performed it. This combination — direct generosity followed by an immediate disclaimer — is characteristic of a generation of senior British professionals (especially women in male-dominated fields) who learned to give care without exposing it. The phone call itself, ending in 'Up to you', leaves Sophie with no instruction other than to think. C1 learners encountering this register should notice that what looks cool from the outside is, in this milieu, a more demanding form of attention than enthusiasm would be.
content.grammar_focus: Helena's speech in this dialogue showcases two C1 patterns at full strength. First, the triple parallel hypothetical — 'Assume you get partnership. Assume you get the equity track. Assume the firm hands it to you with a ribbon.' — three imperatives in sequence, each more specific than the last, building toward the question. This rhetorical structure is courtroom-derived; lawyers use it to lock in concessions before posing the central question. Second, the construction 'rather than discovering it is your answer by accident in 2030' — a gerund phrase ('discovering') stacked with an embedded clause ('it is your answer'), modified by a manner adverb ('by accident'), modified by a future date ('in 2030'). Each layer adds precision. C1 speakers can construct and parse these stacks in real time. Note also the modal-conditional combination: 'if it is your answer, you should know that it is your answer' — the repeated noun phrase enforces a focus that pronouns would weaken. C1 prose often resists the pull toward pronoun substitution.

key_vocabulary.note: Британское бытовое название скоростных поездов на маршруте Лондон–Манчестер–Глазго. Использование без артикля или с 'the' маркирует регулярного пользователя.
key_vocabulary.translation: Pendolino (тип поезда West Coast Main Line)

key_vocabulary.note: Helena известна тем, что в одной фразе помещает один настоящий вопрос и несколько отвлекающих. Decoy — буквально приманка для дичи; метафорически — то, что отвлекает внимание от настоящей цели.

В юридических и консалтинговых фирмах два типа партнёров: salaried (зарплатные) и equity (с долей в собственности фирмы). Equity track — карьерный путь ко второму типу. Решение принимается голосованием существующих equity partners.
Юридическая лексика, проникшая в обыденную речь. 'Defensible' — то, что можно защитить аргументами; 'perfectly' усиливает без преувеличения.

'Trains have echoes' — Helena говорит это как объяснение, почему она повторяет вопрос: акустика плохая. Реальная или ироническая причина — неясно. Это очень C1: причина может быть и буквальной, и риторической одновременно.
key_vocabulary.word: to have an echo (of a place)

key_vocabulary.note: Британское понижение градуса любви. 'I love you' — слишком много для коллег. 'I'm fond of you' — точно по мере. Helena произносит это как краткое признание и сразу закрывает тему.

comprehension_questions.hint: Consider what she explicitly tells Sophie about when to answer, and what that suggests about her purpose in asking.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Helena ask 'What are you going to do with it' in a way that doesn't sound like a question?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is removing a particular trap from Sophie's path. What trap?
comprehension_questions.question: Helena says: 'There is a permitted answer here, which is I want it because I want it.' Why is she taking the trouble to defend that answer?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the immediate next sentence and the cultural note.
comprehension_questions.question: How would you describe the way Helena handles the moment of warmth ('I'm fond of you')? What does the phrase that follows it do?

content.title: Carlos and the Pram

dialogue.line: He went out at the gate. He'll be down for forty minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: That's the long version of a nap.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah. Don't waste it.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I won't.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Can I ask you something — about the first weeks. Marie. Tom. What I should know.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You want advice.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I want — perspective.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Different thing. Easier.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The thing about advice — I don't have any that would survive contact with their actual baby. Every baby rearranges the advice. What I can give you is a frame. You want the frame.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I want the frame.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Frame is this. You're going to think one thing in week one. You're going to think a different thing in week three. Probably the opposite thing. By week six you'll have thought four contradictory things and you'll be tired of having thoughts.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: So when you talk to Marie, don't trust your own diagnosis. Whatever you're sure of about her in week one, hold it lightly. It's probably wrong. Or right, but about the wrong week.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: That's useful.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Second thing. Don't perform competence. New parents can smell rehearsed reassurance at fifty paces. If you don't know what to say, say so.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: What did people say to you that worked.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Almost nothing. The thing that worked was a friend who came round on day eleven and washed the kitchen. Didn't ask. Just washed it. Left.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Hm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Third thing — and this one's harder. Tom's going to be a different man for six months. Don't measure him against the version of him you know. Measure him against the version of him at hour eighty without sleep. That's the relevant comparison.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Same goes for Marie. Same goes for you, for that matter.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Why does the same go for me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because if you've come down from Manchester for a long weekend in your sister-of-the-mother capacity, with a partnership review coming and a decision somewhere underneath that you haven't fully named yet, you are also in an unusual chemistry. Don't trust your week-one diagnosis of yourself either.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: How did you know about the partnership review.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Marie mentioned it. I'm not supposed to know about the decision underneath it, but I made it up because of the way you're walking.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: How am I walking.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Like someone carrying a glass of water and trying not to spill it.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Christ, Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sorry. He's stirring. Walk a bit faster.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

pratique.scenario: Imagine you are about to go through a major life event yourself, or supporting someone close to you through one. Borrow Carlos's frame and write your own three-point version — what you should remember about week one, what you should remember about week three, and what 'the relevant comparison' would be. Don't aim for universal advice. Aim for the specific frame that fits your situation.
prompt_phrases: First thing — in week one I should remember that…
Second thing — in week three I should expect…
The relevant comparison is not X, it's Y.

content.situation: Saturday morning. Sophie has come back down to London for the weekend; she is determined to be present without being centrally so. Carlos has Mateo for the morning while Anna is at the lab. Sophie meets him in Victoria Park; the pram is one of those tank-like models that occupies a lot of pavement. Mateo, fifteen months old, is asleep. They walk. The British male register is here in a different mode from Tom's: Carlos has been a father for over a year and is, in his unspectacular way, an authority on the early version of what Marie is about to enter.

phrase_cle.example: I think she's angry with me, but it's only Tuesday. I'm holding it lightly.
phrase_cle.explanation: Императив, пришедший из созерцательной и психотерапевтической лексики, но прочно укоренившийся в современном разговорном английском среди людей, читающих non-fiction. Грамматически — простой императив с местоимением it и наречием. Содержательно — целая позиция: ты не отбрасываешь свою интерпретацию, но и не закрываешь её на замок. Carlos использует это как инструкцию по обращению с собственными суждениями в нестабильное время. На C1 ты учишься распознавать такие 'позиционные' императивы — фразы, которые описывают отношение к мысли, а не саму мысль. Аналогичные конструкции: 'sit with it', 'leave it open', 'let it land'. Запоминай связкой.

expressions.meaning: He fell asleep at the moment of departure (or boundary). British colloquial; 'gate' here is the threshold of the park, but 'go out' (= fall asleep, lose consciousness) would work in any threshold context.
expressions.expression: He went out at the gate

expressions.meaning: Withstand encounter with the actual thing rather than the imagined version of it. Military origin: 'no plan survives contact with the enemy'. Commonly used to describe how generic advice fails on specific cases.
Carry an opinion or interpretation without gripping it; remain open to revising. From contemplative and therapeutic vocabulary; now widely used.
Detect insincere comfort from a distance. The 'at fifty paces' is from the duelling vocabulary; 'rehearsed' is the sharp word. The whole expression mocks the well-meaning comforter.
expressions.expression: Smell rehearsed reassurance at fifty paces

expressions.meaning: In the specific role of X. From legal English ('in his capacity as trustee'); used here ironically by Carlos — 'sister-of-the-mother capacity' is not a thing, which is the joke.
The comparison that actually applies, as distinct from the obvious or default one. Carlos uses this as a thinking tool: he reframes which baseline to use when judging Tom's behaviour.

content.cultural_note: Carlos's parenting register here is recognisably British and recognisably male — not because women cannot speak this way, but because this particular blend of professional analytic frame ('the relevant comparison') with under-the-radar tenderness is a register you hear often from British fathers of his generation in the company of close friends. It is not the demonstrative warmth of some other cultures. It is precision deployed as a form of care. The closing exchange — Mateo stirring, the conversation broken off mid-thought — is also typical: in the parental male register, sustained emotional discussion is constantly interrupted by the small operational realities of a child, and the interruption is welcomed. It is a way of preventing any conversation from becoming heavy. The seriousness gets in via the cracks.
content.grammar_focus: Carlos uses two C1 structures characteristic of professional thinking made conversational. First: 'You're going to think X in week one. You're going to think Y in week three.' — repeated future progressive forms, each anchored to a specific time, building a temporal map for the listener. This is a teaching device: by predicting variation, he immunises Sophie against trusting any single moment's reading. Second, the structure of his three-point frame: 'Frame is this. First thing... Second thing... Third thing' — explicit numbering used in casual male British speech to organise a piece of advice without the formality of bullet points. Note also Carlos's signature grammatical move: dropping the article and the auxiliary. 'Frame is this' instead of 'The frame is this'; 'Different thing. Easier.' instead of 'That's a different thing, and it's easier.' The omissions compress the speech and mark register. C1 students should hear the ellipsis as a positive choice, not a mistake.

key_vocabulary.note: Sophie перефразирует 'forty minutes' через определение длины. C1 разговорный приём — характеризовать продолжительность через сравнительную меру.
key_vocabulary.word: the long version of a nap

key_vocabulary.note: Sophie различает два типа помощи. Совет — конкретное предписание; перспектива — рамка для собственного думания. Sophie запрашивает второе. Carlos одобрительно отмечает разницу.
key_vocabulary.translation: взгляд со стороны (в отличие от совета)

key_vocabulary.note: Carlos использует это о том, как каждый ребёнок 'переставляет' любой совет. Метафора: совет как мебель в комнате, ребёнок как хозяин, который её передвигает.

Британский разговорный фразовый глагол — посетить кого-либо в их доме. 'A friend who came round on day eleven and washed the kitchen.' Очень британская конструкция; американцы скорее скажут 'came over'.
О пробуждении от сна. 'He's stirring' — он начинает просыпаться. Парентское наблюдение — за минимальными признаками изменения состояния.
Carlos говорит про 'a decision somewhere underneath' partnership review. Метафорическая пространственность: одно решение существует над другим, более глубоким. Британский интеллектуальный регистр.

comprehension_questions.hint: Think about why advice fails on a specific case, and what a frame is designed to survive.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Carlos draw a distinction between 'advice' and 'a frame'? What does the frame do that advice can't?

comprehension_questions.hint: What does he know about Sophie's current chemistry that makes her also subject to the rule?
comprehension_questions.question: Carlos says: 'Don't trust your week-one diagnosis of yourself either.' Why does he extend his framework to Sophie?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the final paragraph of the cultural note. Why is this interruption not just an interruption?
comprehension_questions.question: At the very end Carlos breaks off the conversation because Mateo is stirring. What does the cultural note say about this kind of interruption?

content.title: Olivia

dialogue.line: She's here. Both well. More later. T.
dialogue.speaker: Tom (text)

dialogue.line: Oh, Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You're up.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm. They want me to sleep but I'd rather hear your voice. Five minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Five minutes. Tell me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: She came at four-twelve. Olivia.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Olivia.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Olivia is a good name.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: She made the room a different room.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Tom is — he's better than I thought he'd be. He's worse at it than I thought he'd be. Both are true. He held her like he was holding the wrong end of something for thirty seconds and then he held her like he'd been doing it for ten years.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That's the right order.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mum is on her way down tomorrow.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'll come at the weekend.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Come whenever. She won't mind. She doesn't have a calendar yet.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I don't have anything else to say. Or — I do, but I think it'll wait.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It'll wait.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sleep.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You sleep.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a moment in your life when someone very close to you became, briefly, a different person — through an event, an arrival, a loss, an illness, a passage. Use Marie's tools: the parallel comparison ('better than I thought / worse than I thought'), the suspended contradiction ('both are true'), the figurative image of the room or object that changed.
prompt_phrases: He/she was better than I thought, and worse than I thought…
Both were true.
It made the room a different room — meaning…

content.situation: Tuesday evening, ten days after the appointment. Sophie is on the train back to Manchester after another working week split between two cities — the rhythm of these last weeks. Her phone buzzes with a text from Tom: short, telegraphic. The lesson sits at the hinge of Block 1. The birth itself is not on stage; what is on stage is the way information travels and the way two friends speak across it. The dialogue is shorter than usual on purpose: at this register, length is a choice, and the choice here is concision. Sophie does not turn around. She rings Marie from a station bench at Manchester Piccadilly an hour after arriving home.

phrase_cle.example: He's the best father I know. He doesn't always know what he's doing. Both are true.
phrase_cle.explanation: Эта фраза — небольшое грамматическое и философское событие, и она проходит через весь курс начиная с A2. На C1 она становится автоматическим инструментом мышления. Говорящий помещает рядом два утверждения, которые формально кажутся противоречивыми, и заявляет, что оба истинны. Это не уклонение от позиции и не парадокс ради парадокса — это признание, что реальность часто требует двух одновременных описаний, и попытка свести их к одному обедняет картину. Структура простая: 'Both are true' с плюрализованным are (а не 'both is true'). Можно расширять: 'both can be true', 'both will be true at the same time'. На C1 ты учишься не только использовать эту фразу, но и распознавать ситуации, в которых она применима, — это и есть взросление в языке.

expressions.meaning: An entrance changed the nature of the space — not its content, its quality. A figure of speech that turns the new arrival into an architectural event. Marie reaches for it because the literal description ('she's beautiful', 'she's small') is not what she means.
expressions.expression: She made the room a different room

expressions.meaning: A sentence that has done quiet but substantial work in the courses since A2 and now appears as an automatic move. Marie holds two contradictory observations of Tom in suspension without needing to resolve them.

A newborn doesn't operate in human time. Marie says this as the practical reason Sophie can come whenever, but the phrase carries more — it names the temporary suspension of normal scheduling that the first weeks of a baby's life impose.
expressions.expression: She doesn't have a calendar yet

expressions.meaning: What I want to say can be deferred without loss. Said quietly between people who trust that the deferral is genuine, not avoidance. The phrase appears twice in the closing exchange, becoming a small ritual.
Handling an object — physical or figurative — without competence, in the brief moment before the body figures it out. Marie's image of Tom with Olivia for the first thirty seconds.
expressions.expression: Holding the wrong end of something

content.cultural_note: British conversation around momentous events often works by understatement. A close friend giving birth would, in some cultures, generate immediate florid expression — 'Congratulations! How wonderful! I'm so happy for you!' In British close-friendship register, especially between two women who have known each other for many years, the response can be 'Olivia is a good name', followed by a long silence. The silence is not coldness; it is the recognition that there is nothing to add. Marie, in turn, doesn't need congratulation. She needs Sophie's voice. The closing exchange — 'Sleep.' / 'You sleep.' / 'Yeah.' — is a mutual gentle command, an acknowledgement that words have done what they can do for tonight. C1 learners should hear this register as fluent, not impoverished. To speak more here would be to spoil the moment.
content.grammar_focus: This dialogue is built on the principle of compression: at C1, what is left out matters as much as what is said. Marie's central description of Tom — 'he's better than I thought he'd be. He's worse at it than I thought he'd be. Both are true.' — uses two parallel comparative constructions ('better than I thought he'd be' / 'worse at it than I thought he'd be') with slightly different scopes (the first about him as a whole, the second about a specific competence). The final 'Both are true' resolves the apparent contradiction by refusing to resolve it, which is the C1 move. Notice also the final exchange — six short turns, each one or two words — and how the rhythm carries the tenderness without naming it. In Russian or other languages this register often translates into longer, more explicit forms; the lesson here is that English can compress affection into 'It'll wait.'

key_vocabulary.note: Стандартная британская формула в текстовом уведомлении о рождении ребёнка. Минимальная информация, максимальная плотность. 'Both well' — следующая стандартная составляющая (мать и ребёнок здоровы).
'She came at four-twelve.' Глагол 'come' для рождения — мягкий, физиологически точный. Британское разговорное.
В Британии 'down' = на юг (Лондон), 'up' = на север. 'Mum is on her way down tomorrow' — Mum едет с севера. C1 регистр — географическая ориентация без объяснений.
Метафорическое использование: ребёнок ещё не вписан в человеческое время. Marie использует это лёгко, и фраза работает как ласкающая шутка о новой реальности.
key_vocabulary.word: to have a calendar (of a person)

key_vocabulary.note: 'It'll wait' — оно подождёт. Конструкция, в которой тема разговора получает свойство ожидания. Очень британский разговорный приём.

Sophie реагирует на описание Tom-с-ребёнком фразой 'That's the right order' — то есть: сначала неловкость, потом компетентность, и это именно правильная последовательность. C1 признак — оценка не самого факта, а его временной формы.

comprehension_questions.hint: Consider what the phrase actually claims about a newborn, and what it claims about the suspension of ordinary time in the household.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Marie say 'She doesn't have a calendar yet' as the reason Sophie can come whenever?

comprehension_questions.hint: Marie has described two consecutive states. Sophie is approving the sequence, not just the states. What is the sequence?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie responds to Marie's description of Tom with 'That's the right order.' What does she mean?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the closing paragraph of the cultural note. What does saying more here cost?
comprehension_questions.question: The dialogue ends with 'Sleep.' / 'You sleep.' / 'Yeah.' — three words and a goodnight. What does the cultural note say about why neither friend says more?

content.title: Eleven Days

dialogue.line: She's asleep, hold on, I'm moving.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Take your time.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Kitchen. Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: How are you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm too tired to lie about that, so. The honest answer is: feral.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Feral.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah. The animal version of me is in charge. The polite version is asleep on the sofa. The polite version may not return.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That sounds about right for day eleven.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Anyway. When are you coming.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Saturday. Possibly Friday evening.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Friday evening, please. I want a person who knew me before I had a baby in the house overnight, and Tom doesn't count, and Mum is here on Friday but Mum sleeps.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Friday evening it is.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Can I say a thing without you hearing it as a verdict.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Try me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You haven't told me what you're going to do about Helena's question.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm not asking you to decide on the phone. I'm telling you that I have noticed, and that not telling me what you're going to do about Helena's question is not the same as not having a position on Helena's question, and you and I both know that.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: And I'm saying this in feral voice so you don't have to be polite back.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I think I'm going to say no.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And I haven't said that out loud before, and I don't know what to do with the sentence now that it's in the room.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Leave it where it is. You don't have to do anything with it. It's allowed to sit there.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It's a sentence, Sophie. Not a decision. You said it; you didn't sign it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I might have signed it a bit.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: All right. Then you've signed it in pencil, and pencil can be erased. Friday evening. Bring wine. Don't bring a position. Bring you and the pencil version of the sentence, and we'll see what's left of it on Sunday.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah. Olivia agrees. She's making a face. Go to bed.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a sentence you've recently said out loud — to a friend, a partner, a colleague — that you hadn't said before, and that, once said, you weren't sure what to do with. Use Marie's tools: was it a position or a sentence? Was it signed in pencil or in pen? What's left of it now?
prompt_phrases: I said it for the first time when…
I think I signed it in pencil, because…
What's left of it now is…

content.situation: Eleven days after Olivia was born. Late Tuesday evening, Manchester. Sophie has been to London once, briefly, on day three — a half hour at the flat with Olivia asleep on Marie's chest, neither of them saying very much. Now she's at her kitchen table with takeaway noodles and a draft document for tomorrow's hearing. Marie rings at twenty to ten. The premise of the call is logistical — when will Sophie come down again — but Marie is at the stage of new motherhood where, as Carlos put it, the version of her speaking on day eleven is operating at hour eighty without sleep, and the relevant comparison is not the Marie of last month. Sophie can hear it in the first three words.

phrase_cle.phrase: You said it; you didn't sign it.
phrase_cle.example: I said I might leave him. I didn't sign it. I'm allowed to walk it back to a thought.
phrase_cle.explanation: Marie создаёт работающую метафору: между произнесённым предложением и принятым решением есть зазор, и в этом зазоре можно жить. Грамматически — два независимых утверждения через точку с запятой (или точку), без 'but', без 'however' — потому что Marie не противопоставляет, а различает. Произнесение и подписание — две разные операции; путать их — частый ход в моменты тревоги. На C1 ты учишься не только использовать такие фразы, но и **держать различие**, которое они вводят: между мыслью, сказанной мыслью и взятым обязательством. Конструкция расширяется: 'you suggested it, you didn't propose it'; 'I considered it, I didn't promise it'; 'we discussed it, we didn't decide it'.
phrase_cle.translation: Ты это сказала, а не подписала.

expressions.meaning: Reverted to a wild, unsocialised state. Used here by Marie about herself in early motherhood — not as complaint, as classification. The polite, considered version of the self has gone offline; what's left is operating on instinct.

Receiving a remark as a final judgment about oneself rather than as a piece of information offered for thinking with. Marie names the risk before saying her thing, which is a C1-level move — pre-empting the listener's emotional reception.

Marie's double negative carries a precise argument: silence about a question is not equivalent to absence of an answer to it. The construction is rhetorically tight — the kind of thing you'd say to a lawyer, which is what Sophie is.
expressions.expression: Not the same as not having a position

expressions.meaning: A sentence, once said aloud, has a presence that an unspoken thought does not. The metaphor — the sentence as a third party in the kitchen — is a recurring C1 figure. Sophie reaches for it because the moment requires it.
expressions.expression: Now that it's in the room

expressions.meaning: Marie's metaphor for a tentative commitment: a signature exists, but it is reversible. This compounds with 'pencil can be erased', which makes the metaphor working rather than ornamental.
An offer to let the sentence be tested by time without forcing a verdict. The phrasing — 'what's left' — implicitly admits that some sentences shrink under examination and others grow.
expressions.expression: We'll see what's left of it on Sunday

content.cultural_note: The closing exchange — Sophie says 'Thank you', Marie says 'Yeah. Olivia agrees. She's making a face. Go to bed.' — is a textbook example of British close-friendship register handling a moment of vulnerability. Marie does not absorb the thanks (which would risk turning the moment heavy); she immediately introduces a third party (Olivia, with a face) and gives Sophie a small command ('Go to bed'). The command is the affection. In this register, instructions are gifts: telling someone what to do for their own care is a way of caring for them without saying so. Sophie, who reads this register fluently, doesn't argue with the instruction. She'll go to bed. The conversation has done its work and the friendship has been reaffirmed without either of them having to handle the words 'I love you' or anything in the same family.
content.grammar_focus: Marie's central move in this dialogue is the negation stack: 'not telling me what you're going to do about Helena's question is not the same as not having a position on Helena's question'. Three negatives, parsed in real time by both speakers — a sentence that would feel laboured in writing but lands clean in speech because the rhythm holds it. C1 students should hear this as a permitted construction; double and triple negatives are unsentimental tools when they carry distinctions that simpler phrasings would lose. Notice also Marie's deployment of the present perfect for emotional positioning: 'I have noticed' — used with focal stress on 'noticed' — does the work of a several-day observation in one verb form. And the closing exchange uses the pencil/sign metaphor as a working argument: 'You said it; you didn't sign it.' The semicolon-stacked clauses (without explicit conjunction) compress the contrast. Reading them aloud, you hear that English at C1 can carry quite formal syntax in domestic contexts when the speaker is doing precision work.

key_vocabulary.note: Раньше — только про животных, перебежавших обратно в природу. В современном разговорном английском — самохарактеристика человека в состоянии истощения или социального коллапса. C1 регистр; B2 студент это слово знает, но не использует так.
Из политической и юридической лексики, перенесённой в обыденную речь. 'Having a position' противопоставляется 'having a feeling' или 'having a thought' — позиция предполагает готовность защитить.

Очень характерный C1-разговорный приём. 'The sentence is in the room' — сказанная фраза присутствует как объект. 'The elephant in the room' — английская идиома (проблема, которую все видят, но никто не называет); 'in the room' выросло из неё в более широкое использование.
key_vocabulary.translation: в комнате (метафорически: в наличии, на виду)

key_vocabulary.note: Метафорическая пара: pencil = обратимое обязательство, pen = окончательное. Marie использует её как инструмент мягкого расторжения только что произнесённого решения.
key_vocabulary.word: to sign in pencil / in pen

key_vocabulary.note: Параллельная конструкция в инструкции: что привезти, чего не привозить. C1 разговорный приём — использовать одну и ту же грамматику для двух противоположных установок, чтобы рифма донесла различие.
key_vocabulary.word: Bring wine. Don't bring a position.

key_vocabulary.note: Из политической журналистики ('the senator walked back her remarks'). Сейчас — общеразговорное. Полезный фразовый глагол на C1, описывающий именно мягкое отступление от позиции, не отказ.

comprehension_questions.hint: Marie is making a kind of trade. What does she offer, and what does she ask for in return?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie says she's speaking in 'feral voice' and adds: 'so you don't have to be polite back.' What does she mean by giving Sophie this permission?

comprehension_questions.hint: Consider what changes between thinking a sentence and saying it. Sophie is naming a specific moment of transition.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie say: 'I haven't said that out loud before, and I don't know what to do with the sentence now that it's in the room'?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the paragraph about commands as gifts. What is being given?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie's closing instruction is 'Go to bed.' How does the cultural note explain why this counts as affection rather than dismissal?

content.title: The Offer

dialogue.line: Thank you for coming up. Tea or coffee, Ms Bennett.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Aldridge

dialogue.line: Coffee, please. Black.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. I won't keep you in suspense. We're offering you the equity track. The vote was unanimous, which I mention not as flattery but as information.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Aldridge

dialogue.line: I'm aware that 'unanimous' is the word every firm uses when they want a candidate to feel good. In our case it happens to be true and the alternative would have been the partnership not making the offer at all. Mrs Carson abstained from the vote, which technically renders the unanimity rhetorical, but I would not characterise her abstention as a no.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Aldridge

dialogue.line: It was a procedural abstention. I wanted to read your North Sea Petroleum file in full before voting on you. I have now read it. Had I voted, I would have voted yes.
dialogue.speaker: Mrs Carson

dialogue.line: Thank you, Mrs Carson.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Don't thank me. The file did the work. I voted on the file.
dialogue.speaker: Mrs Carson

dialogue.line: I'd like to say something, if I may.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Go ahead.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Aldridge

dialogue.line: Sophie. You don't need a speech from me, you've had several already. I'll say only this. The offer is on the table. The decision is not. I have asked you a question some weeks ago which I will not repeat in this room, but I expect you to have heard it underneath this offer, not separately from it.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I've heard it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Helena, would you mind unpacking that for the rest of us.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Aldridge

dialogue.line: I'd rather not. With respect — Sophie knows what I mean and the offer is unaffected by it.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Very well. Ms Bennett, I won't press you for an answer today. We typically expect a response within a week. In your case I am authorised to extend that to ten days, in light of — well, in light of the fact that you do excellent work and we are willing to wait a few extra days for it. Is ten days sufficient.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Aldridge

dialogue.line: Mr Aldridge, may I ask one question.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Of course.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Aldridge

dialogue.line: If I were to say I needed longer than ten days — say, three weeks — would that be possible.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It would be unusual.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Aldridge

dialogue.line: I'm not asking whether it would be usual. I'm asking whether it would be possible.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'd take the question at face value, Frederick. She's not negotiating; she's asking.
dialogue.speaker: Mrs Carson

dialogue.line: Yes. It would be possible. Three weeks would not be a problem.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Aldridge

dialogue.line: Thank you. I'd like ten days, in fact. I wanted to know what the ceiling was before I committed to a floor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Ah.
dialogue.speaker: Mrs Carson

dialogue.line: Right. Ten days it is. Friday week. Helena will remind you of the address.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Aldridge

dialogue.line: I will.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Thank you, Ms Bennett. Welcome — provisionally.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Aldridge

dialogue.line: Thank you, Mr Aldridge. Provisionally received.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Imagine you are in a formal professional meeting where an offer has been put to you — a job, a role, a contract, a place on a team. The other side wants a decision quickly. You want time. Use Sophie's move: ask first what the maximum is, then commit to the minimum you actually need. Tell me how the conversation would go.
prompt_phrases: If I were to say I needed longer than X, would that be possible…
I'm not asking whether it would be usual — I'm asking whether it would be possible…
I wanted to know what the ceiling was before I committed to a floor.

content.situation: Wednesday morning. The week before the official review date — Helena has, as Sophie suspected she might, accelerated the process. Sophie has been called to the senior partner's office at ten. Around the table: Helena (whom Sophie has known three years), Mr Aldridge (managing partner, sixty-two, courteous, formidable), Mrs Carson (head of the energy and infrastructure group, fifty-eight, precise, opaque). The room has Edwardian moulding, a window onto Quay Street, and a tea tray. The conversation is structured but not scripted; this is how partnership offers are made at this firm. Sophie sits at the end of the table opposite Mr Aldridge.
content.characters: Helena, Mr Aldridge, Mrs Carson & Sophie

phrase_cle.phrase: I wanted to know what the ceiling was before I committed to a floor.
phrase_cle.example: I asked them whether they could pay forty thousand. They said yes. I told them I'd take thirty-two, in fact. I wanted to know what the ceiling was before I committed to a floor.
phrase_cle.explanation: Sophie произносит это после короткого манёвра, который мог показаться нелогичным: она спросила про три недели, потом взяла десять дней. Фраза-объяснение, которая превращает странность в стратегию. Архитектурная метафора — ceiling/floor — превращает абстрактное переговорное действие в пространственное и поэтому понятное. На C1 такие метафоры — рабочий инструмент: они не украшают речь, они делают аргумент доступным. Грамматически: главное предложение в past simple ('I wanted to know'), embedded clause через 'what + was' (вопросительная форма в утверждении), временная подчинённая 'before I committed' с verb 'commit to' + abstract noun. Запомни конструкцию 'X before Y' для последовательных решений: 'I checked the cost before I committed to the date'; 'I read the contract before I signed the appendix'.
phrase_cle.translation: Я хотела узнать потолок, прежде чем фиксировать пол.

expressions.meaning: A formal non-vote made for technical reasons rather than as expression of dissent. Mrs Carson uses this to clarify that she wasn't voting against Sophie; she was withholding her vote until a process was complete. The phrase belongs to committee and corporate governance vocabulary; using it conversationally is itself a register marker.

Helena's instruction to Sophie about how to hear her earlier question now: as embedded within the offer, not as a parallel matter. The preposition pair 'underneath / separately from' does precise work — Sophie's decision and Helena's question are layered, not adjacent.
expressions.expression: Underneath this offer, not separately from it

expressions.meaning: Explaining what was just said in more detail. From management consultancy and academic vocabulary; now widely used in British professional speech. Mr Aldridge's request is itself a small move — he is asking Helena, in front of Sophie, to make her opacity less opaque, and Helena declines.

I would accept the question as straightforwardly meaning what it says, rather than reading it as strategic. Mrs Carson uses this to defend Sophie from a misreading. The phrase is a recommendation for a specific kind of attention.
expressions.expression: I'd take the question at face value

expressions.meaning: Sophie's lawyerly metaphor: she wanted to know the maximum the firm could offer in time before deciding what minimum she actually needed. The architectural pair (ceiling/floor) does the work of explaining a negotiation move that would otherwise sound presumptuous.
expressions.expression: What the ceiling was before I committed to a floor

expressions.meaning: An echo of 'welcome — provisionally', echoing the structure back. Sophie matches the partner's register move-for-move. The repetition is recognised as a small piece of skill by everyone in the room.

content.cultural_note: British senior professional registers like this one are increasingly rare and worth describing carefully. Mr Aldridge's mode — courteous, dry, occasionally self-aware ('I am authorised to extend that to ten days, in light of — well, in light of the fact that you do excellent work') — is a kind of speech that depends on shared codes: you and I both know what the firm is doing, you and I both know what the offer is worth, and so the actual words can be lighter than the substance underneath them. Mrs Carson's intervention ('I'd take the question at face value, Frederick') uses Mr Aldridge's first name — which signals her seniority, her familiarity with him, and her willingness to redirect the chair without overruling him. Helena's refusal to 'unpack' her remark in front of Sophie protects something private between them and is permitted in this register precisely because she has earned the right to it. Sophie reads all of these moves correctly and matches the register without strain. Welcome, provisionally, to a kind of room very few learners will ever enter and quite a few will work in.
content.grammar_focus: This dialogue is built on the formal register of English professional speech and rewards close attention. Mr Aldridge uses the second conditional throughout: 'If I were to say I needed longer' (Sophie), 'Would that be possible' (Sophie), 'Had I voted, I would have voted yes' (Mrs Carson). The third — Mrs Carson's — is a third conditional with inversion ('Had I voted' instead of 'If I had voted'), which is the formal-literary form. C1 students should recognise that inverted conditionals are not decorative; they are how senior British professionals speak in formal settings without sounding stuffy. Note also Helena's hedge: 'I'd like to say something, if I may.' — modal 'would' + 'if I may' — a doubled politeness construction that signals both the speaker's deference to the chair and the speaker's intention to use her time well. And the closing exchange: 'Welcome — provisionally.' / 'Thank you, Mr Aldridge. Provisionally received.' — Sophie picks up the adverb 'provisionally' from Aldridge and reuses it as a participle modifier. This is a register-matching skill that takes years; in this room, it is being noticed.

key_vocabulary.note: В юридических фирмах два уровня партнёрства: salaried (зарплатные) и equity (с долей в фирме). Equity track — карьерная линия ко второму. Голосование принимают существующие equity partners.
key_vocabulary.translation: путь к equity-партнёрству (с долей в собственности)

key_vocabulary.note: 'Thank you for coming up' — Aldridge на верхнем этаже. В британском офисном языке 'come up' означает прийти к старшему сотруднику в его офис. Регистр: вежливо, иерархически осознанно.

'Renders the unanimity rhetorical' — Aldridge признаёт, что технически голосование не было единогласным. Использование 'rhetorical' для описания формальной фигуры речи в юридическом контексте — высокий C1.
'I would not characterise her abstention as a no.' Юридическая лексика: квалификация действия. Вошла в обыденный британский профессиональный регистр.

Британская формальная фраза, обозначающая мягкое несогласие или отказ выполнить просьбу. 'With respect' звучит вежливо, но используется именно тогда, когда говорящий собирается возразить. В американском контексте часто читается как сарказм.
Британская идиома: 'Friday week' = пятница, отстоящая от сегодня на одну неделю плюс. Не путать с 'next Friday' (ближайшая пятница). Регистр: разговорный британский, не американский.

Юридическое наречие, переносимое в разговор. 'Welcome — provisionally' значит: ты принят, при условии, что окончательное решение за тобой. Sophie ловит регистр и отвечает 'provisionally received' — мастерство переноса adverb на receipt acknowledgement.

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at what Helena says about Sophie having heard the question 'underneath this offer, not separately from it', and at the cultural note's discussion of Helena's earned right.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Helena refuse to 'unpack' her remark when Mr Aldridge asks her to? What is she protecting?

comprehension_questions.hint: Her own explanation — the ceiling-and-floor metaphor — is the answer. State it in your own words: what does she gain from asking the larger question first?
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie ask about three weeks and then take ten days? What is the strategic structure of her question?

comprehension_questions.hint: There is a misreading available in the room — that Sophie is negotiating. Mrs Carson is preventing that misreading. How?
comprehension_questions.question: When Mrs Carson says 'I'd take the question at face value, Frederick', what is she doing on Sophie's behalf?

content.title: Naming It

dialogue.line: Hold on, I'm going outside. Mateo is committed to a song about — I think a snail. Right. Tell me.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I got the offer. Wednesday. Equity track. Ten days to decide.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Congratulations.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I haven't accepted.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Then I'll save the congratulations.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: What are you ringing me for, Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I'm — I want to think out loud. With somebody who isn't Marie, isn't Helena, isn't Mum, isn't Eleanor. The list of people who aren't in this is short.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: All right. Talk.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I think I'm going to say no.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: And every time I get close to giving a reason, the reason comes out wrong. I sound like I'm performing humility, or pretending the offer isn't great when it is, or claiming a moral high ground when there isn't one available. The reasons that I have are real, and they keep coming out as poses.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What are the reasons.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: The work I want to do for the next ten years isn't the work the role would let me do. The clients I'd inherit are not the clients I'd choose. The hours are — well, the hours are the hours, but I've watched what they do over time. And under all of that there's a smaller thing, which is that I don't think I want to spend my thirties becoming someone whose job description requires her to be a particular kind of fluent in a particular kind of room. I'd rather be slightly less fluent in a wider range of rooms.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I notice you just gave me the reasons quite cleanly.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Did I.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Sophie, I'm going to give you one sentence and then I'm going back inside, because Mateo has gone quiet and that means something is on fire.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The sentence is: the fact that your reasons sound like poses to you is mostly a sign that you haven't said them out loud enough times. Reasons sound like poses on the first three sayings. By the seventh saying, they sound like reasons. The work this week is to say them seven times to seven different people, in seven slightly different ways, until they stop sounding like a press release.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: And then say them once more to yourself, late at night, with no audience, and see if they hold.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: That's eight.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The eighth one is the one that counts. Right. Going. Don't do anything until you've said it seven times.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah. The snail probably escaped.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

pratique.scenario: Imagine you have a decision you've been carrying — small or large — and the reasons you give for it keep coming out as poses (performing humility, claiming a high ground, sounding like a press release). Borrow Carlos's frame: try saying the reasons three different times in three different ways, to three different audiences (a friend, a colleague, a stranger). Tell Sophie how the reasons changed across the three sayings.
prompt_phrases: On the first saying, the reason came out as…
By the third, it had shifted to…
What the late-night version, with no audience, sounds like is…

content.situation: Saturday morning, three days after the offer. Sophie has come down to London again — Friday evening at Marie's as promised, Saturday at her own. She rings Carlos from a bench in Russell Square. The plane trees are bare; it's the cold dry kind of March that makes London look architectural. Carlos is at home with Mateo and Anna; he steps out into the small garden with the phone. The conversation is fifteen minutes long. Carlos's main contribution is a single sentence; everything before it is him refusing to give Sophie what she's come to him for.

phrase_cle.phrase: Reasons sound like poses on the first three sayings.
phrase_cle.example: I've been trying to explain why I'm leaving the project, and every version I try sounds rehearsed. Reasons sound like poses on the first three sayings. By the seventh, they start sounding like reasons.
phrase_cle.explanation: Carlos формулирует наблюдение, которое работает как практический инструмент. Грамматически: subject (reasons) + verb (sound like) + noun + temporal adverbial (on the first three sayings). 'Saying' здесь — герундий в значении 'произнесение, акт говорения', что не самое частое употребление. Сама идея: артикуляция требует повторения, чтобы прозрачнеть, и до определённого числа повторений говорящий звучит более отрепетированно, чем чувствует себя. На C1 ты учишься распознавать эту фазу собственной речи и не торопиться оценивать содержание решения по форме, в которой оно впервые прозвучало. Конструкция расширяется: 'apologies sound like excuses on the first attempt'; 'plans sound like fantasies on the first telling'.
phrase_cle.translation: Аргументы звучат как позы на первых трёх произнесениях.

expressions.meaning: Acting modest in a way that reads as a performance rather than as actual modesty. Sophie is naming a specific failure mode in her own attempts to explain her decision: the language of refusal, in this milieu, has stock postures, and stepping into one of them is what she's afraid of.

Asserting ethical superiority in a situation where the ethics are not actually clean enough to justify it. Sophie's anxiety is not about whether her reasons are right; it's about whether stating them puts her in a posture she hasn't earned.
expressions.expression: Claiming a moral high ground when there isn't one available

expressions.meaning: Sophie's image for what partnership would specialise her into: a high-level fluency that operates inside a narrow category of social and professional spaces. The repetition ('a particular kind of … a particular kind of …') compresses a worry about over-specialisation.
expressions.expression: A particular kind of fluent in a particular kind of room

expressions.meaning: Have the polished, impersonal quality of an officially issued statement. Carlos's diagnosis: until reasons are said many times, they sound corporate; only repetition reveals whether they are actually personal.

Test whether the reasons retain their structure when subjected to a particular kind of attention — in this case, late-night solitude with no listener. The verb 'hold' (intransitive) is the key — it carries the sense of structural integrity.

I will defer the congratulations until the situation actually warrants them. Carlos's signature dryness — he refuses to perform an emotional move that would be premature, and his refusal is itself a kind of help.

content.cultural_note: Carlos's refusal to give Sophie what she's come for — emotional validation, or a verdict, or a direction — is itself the help. He gives her one sentence, names the work she needs to do, and exits the conversation before she can extend it. This is a particular British male mode of friendship, and it depends on a shared assumption: the listener is competent and adult, and treating them otherwise would be patronising. Carlos's small jokes (Mateo's snail song, the snail escaping, 'something is on fire') do double duty — they relieve the seriousness and they demonstrate that he is not making this conversation more important than it is. By the time he says 'Going. Don't do anything until you've said it seven times' he has converted a counselling session into a brief instruction, which is what Sophie needed. The dignity, in this register, is in the brevity.
content.grammar_focus: Carlos's central sentence — 'the fact that your reasons sound like poses to you is mostly a sign that you haven't said them out loud enough times' — uses a structure C1 students should master: the noun-clause subject ('the fact that X') paired with the noun-clause complement ('a sign that Y'). The verb 'is' carries the whole weight; everything else is two embedded propositions, one diagnostic, one prescriptive. Note also the precision in the modifiers: 'mostly a sign' (not entirely), 'enough times' (a quantitative threshold rather than an absolute condition). The sentence is doing a lot in one breath. Sophie's earlier triple — 'I sound like I'm performing humility, or pretending the offer isn't great when it is, or claiming a moral high ground when there isn't one available' — uses three parallel '-ing' clauses, each with a subordinate concession ('when it is', 'when there isn't one available'). C1 speakers can maintain parallel structure across long stretches without losing the syntax. The reading is in the rhythm.

key_vocabulary.note: Глагольная конструкция: использование речи как процесса осмысления, не как сообщения готовых выводов. На C1 это сознательная активность, и говорящий часто запрашивает у слушателя право на неё.

От буквального значения позы тела к фигуративному значению риторической стойки. 'Performing humility', 'claiming a moral high ground' — стандартные позы. Распознавать их в собственной речи — C1-уровневое умение.
'The reason comes out wrong' — попытка артикулировать что-либо, при которой результат не соответствует намерению. Очень частая C1 конструкция для описания неудачной речи.
Британское профессиональное: 'the hours' с определённым артиклем = режим рабочего времени. 'The hours are the hours' — труизм, признающий, что часы не меняются, разговор не о них.

В юридических фирмах партнёр приходит с портфелем клиентов от предыдущего партнёра или из общего пула. Inherit — глагол выбора; alternative — 'take over'. Inherit предполагает наследие как объект, который имеет историю и характер.
'See if they hold' — выдержат ли они проверку. Из инженерной и архитектурной лексики: о конструкции, выдерживающей нагрузку. На C1 переносится на аргументы, отношения, обещания.

Жанр официального коммуникативного документа, характеризующийся отполированностью и обезличенностью. Использование как метафоры собственной речи ('sound like a press release') — современный британский разговорный приём.

comprehension_questions.hint: What would the congratulations be premature about? Consider what Carlos understands about Sophie's situation that allows him to defer the conventional response.
comprehension_questions.question: Carlos says: 'I'll save the congratulations.' Why does he refuse to congratulate Sophie now?

comprehension_questions.hint: His central sentence makes a claim about repetition. State it in your own words: what is the relationship between how many times something is said and how it sounds?
comprehension_questions.question: What is Carlos's explanation for why Sophie's reasons sound like 'poses' to her? What is the prescription?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the paragraph about Carlos's small jokes. What two functions do they serve?
comprehension_questions.question: Carlos closes the conversation with 'The snail probably escaped.' How does the cultural note explain what this kind of small joke does in his register?

content.title: Helena Knows

dialogue.line: Close the door.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Sit down. Drink the wine. The wine is not a tactic, it is wine. Although it is also very good wine, so. Drink it.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Helena, I —
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Wait. Before you tell me. I want to say one thing first, and I want to say it because if you tell me first, anything I say afterwards will sound like a reaction, and I don't want it to sound like a reaction.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Go ahead.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Whatever you have come in here to say, the answer from me is: fine. I have been your supervisor for three years, I am fond of you, and I do not believe I have any standing to be disappointed by an adult woman's professional decision. If you accept, fine. If you decline, fine. If you ask for a sabbatical and a job in publishing, fine. I have said this in advance because I know my own face.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I'm going to decline.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I've thought about it for —
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I don't need the thinking. I know how you think. I'm asking, more curiously than as a supervisor, what you're going to do instead.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I don't have a fully formed answer. I have — directions. There's a small chambers in Manchester, criminal and family, that's been wanting to bring on someone with my background. I'd take a salary cut and a kind of working life that's wider and less elevated. I might do some pro bono work in the housing space; there's a referral team I've been speaking to. I might — Helena, the truthful answer is, I haven't drawn the map yet. I've decided I'm not going where the offer points, and I haven't decided where I am going.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's a bigger answer than the small one I was hoping you'd give.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: What were you hoping I'd say.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I was hoping you'd tell me you had a clear plan, so that I could approve of the plan and pretend that approving of the plan was the same as approving of you leaving.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Don't look at me like that. I told you my face wasn't reliable. I am — Sophie, I am a senior lawyer and I am also a person, and the person is mildly thrown by your not having a plan. The lawyer is fine.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Helena.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I want to thank you for three years and I don't know how to do it without making it heavier than you'd like.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Then don't do it now. Send me an email at the end of the year. Two paragraphs. The first paragraph thanks me. The second paragraph tells me what you've made of yourself by then. I will read both paragraphs once and reply 'noted', and then I will reread the email approximately every six months for ten years. That is how I would like to be thanked.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: It is the only mode of thanks I am structurally equipped to receive.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: Fine.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Drink the wine.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

dialogue.line: I am drinking the wine.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Drink it more.
dialogue.speaker: Helena

pratique.scenario: Imagine telling someone who has been your mentor or supervisor that you are going to do something other than what they prepared you for. Use Helena's frame: pre-empt your own face. Then borrow her instruction about thanks — invent the email-at-the-end-of-the-year that Sophie will eventually send, in two paragraphs.
prompt_phrases: Whatever you have come to tell me, my answer is: fine.
The first paragraph thanks you. The second paragraph tells you what I've made of myself by then.
I will read both paragraphs once and reply…

content.situation: Tuesday evening of the second week. Sophie has said it seven times to seven different people, as instructed. Marie. Carlos. Mum (briefly, on the phone, who said only 'right'). Anna (over coffee). A barrister Sophie sometimes works with named Devraj. A college friend who lives in Bristol. The seventh saying, late at night, alone — held. Now she has to say it to Helena. They are in Helena's office at the firm, after hours, the lights low; Helena has a glass of red wine and has poured one for Sophie. Helena knows already, in the way Helena knows things: not because she's been told, but because she has a working model of Sophie and the model has converged.

phrase_cle.example: Whatever you've come to tell me, my answer is fine. I'm saying that in advance because I know my own face.
phrase_cle.explanation: Метакогнитивная фраза высокого C1 регистра: говорящий заявляет, что у него есть рабочая модель собственного непроизвольного выражения, и поэтому он заранее объявляет свою позицию, чтобы реакция лица не была прочитана собеседником как настоящий ответ. Это очень характерное для зрелого британского интеллектуального регистра признание того, что внутреннее и внешнее иногда расходятся, и хороший коммуникатор знает это про себя. Грамматически — простое утверждение present simple с притяжательным 'own' для усиления. Конструкция 'I know my own X' расширяется: 'I know my own voice' (когда подделываю); 'I know my own handwriting' (когда не уверен, что подписал); 'I know my own pattern' (когда замечаю, что собираюсь повторить ошибку). Все эти варианты — сигнал зрелой саморефлексии.

expressions.meaning: I am aware that my expression will betray me when I'm given news I don't want, so I'm pre-empting the moment by stating my position before I receive the news. Helena's metacognition is itself part of how she operates: she knows the shape of her own affective leaks.

Legal-derived: the right or eligibility to feel a particular emotion, framed as if it were a procedural entitlement. Helena uses 'standing' (a strict concept in law about who may bring a case) to make a quiet, disciplined admission that her disappointment, even if real, is not legally valid here.

Slightly destabilised emotionally, in a way that is being immediately acknowledged and managed. The adverb 'mildly' downgrades the disclosure; the verb 'thrown' (passive, equestrian-derived) carries the right shade of sudden involuntary movement.
Marker that the speaker is about to abandon a more polished version they had prepared and offer the actual content instead. Sophie uses this to step out of the narrative she had been building.

Helena's wry phrasing for what she can emotionally accommodate. The architectural metaphor — being 'structurally equipped' — converts personality into building structure. C1 students should recognise the move: applying technical language to interior life.

Helena's confession that she had hoped a competent plan would let her perform approval of the departure without having to feel it. The construction — equating two different acts of approval — is exact, and Helena's willingness to name it is the lesson.
expressions.expression: Approving of the plan was the same as approving of you leaving

content.cultural_note: Helena's instruction about the email-of-thanks deserves close attention: it is a piece of British professional-personal philosophy condensed into operational terms. She is allergic to thanks delivered in the present moment because the present moment cannot bear them — they would force her to perform an emotion she does not wish to perform in front of a colleague. So she displaces the gratitude into the future and converts it into a structured artefact (two paragraphs, end of year, single 'noted' reply, decade of rereading). The displacement is not avoidance; it is engineering. She is making a vessel that can carry the feeling without leaking. This kind of structured emotional handling — a willingness to design rituals around feelings rather than express them in real time — is a recognisable strain of British formal-personal culture. C1 students should understand that 'I will read both paragraphs once and reply noted' is not coldness; it is the most committed engagement Helena can stage.
content.grammar_focus: Helena's central speech opens with a structure C1 speakers should study: 'Whatever you have come in here to say, the answer from me is: fine.' The 'whatever' clause is a free relative; the colon-fronted 'fine' delivers the predicate with rhythmic punch; the construction collapses what could have been three sentences into one. Note also her chained second conditionals: 'If you accept, fine. If you decline, fine. If you ask for a sabbatical and a job in publishing, fine.' Three parallel conditionals with elliptical predicates — the predicate ('the answer is fine') is omitted because rhythm and parallelism carry it. C1 speakers can construct ellipsis like this and also receive it without confusion. And Helena's email-of-thanks instruction is a sustained future-conditional architecture: 'I will read both paragraphs once and reply noted, and then I will reread the email approximately every six months for ten years.' The mixture of future simple ('I will read'), participial ('reply noted' — note: nonstandard but characteristic), and quantitative adverbials ('approximately every six months for ten years') compresses a small theory of how she handles emotional weight: in calibrated repetitions across time. Worth dwelling on.

key_vocabulary.note: Helena упоминает, что вино — не тактика. Использование 'tactic' для собственной возможной манипуляции — характерный приём C1: называть стратегический ход прежде, чем собеседник заподозрит его.
В юридическом и академическом контексте — формальная роль более старшего сотрудника, отвечающего за развитие младшего. Не 'manager' и не 'boss'; specifically 'supervisor'.
Британский разговорный фразовый глагол. 'Bring on' = recruit. 'A chambers wanting to bring on someone with my background' — chambers (адвокатская контора) хочет взять кого-то с её опытом.
key_vocabulary.word: to bring on (someone with X background)
key_vocabulary.translation: взять на работу (кого-либо с X опытом)

key_vocabulary.note: Латинская фраза, нормализованная в юридическом английском. Обозначает работу адвоката без оплаты, в общественных интересах. C1 студент знает её и использует.
Метафорическая конструкция: процесс ясного планирования как картография. 'I haven't drawn the map yet' — у меня пока нет ясного плана. C1 разговорная фраза.
Из конной лексики: 'to be thrown' — быть сброшенным с лошади. В переносном смысле — внезапно выведенным из эмоционального равновесия. 'Mildly thrown' — слегка выбит. Очень C1 регистр.

Краткая формальная реакция в письменной деловой переписке. Не 'thanks', не 'understood' — именно 'noted'. Helena указывает, что её ответ на длинное письмо будет одно слово. Регистр: бюрократический минимализм, превращённый в личный жест.

comprehension_questions.hint: Helena gives her own reason. State it in your own words. What is she protecting Sophie from, and what is she protecting herself from?
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Helena make her speech ('whatever you have come in here to say, the answer is fine') BEFORE Sophie tells her the news?

comprehension_questions.hint: Helena is naming a substitution she had hoped to make — between two different kinds of approval. Why does naming the substitution matter?
comprehension_questions.question: Helena admits: 'I was hoping you'd tell me you had a clear plan, so that I could approve of the plan and pretend that approving of the plan was the same as approving of you leaving.' What does this confession reveal about her actual feeling?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the cultural note's image of the vessel. What is being designed, and what is it designed to carry?
comprehension_questions.question: How does the cultural note explain Helena's email-of-thanks instruction? Why is the displacement 'engineering' rather than 'avoidance'?

content.title: Mum

dialogue.line: Pass me that cloth.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Here.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: How is Olivia.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Small. Vocal at three a.m. Otherwise as advertised.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Tired. Sharper than usual. She told me on day eleven that the polite version of her was asleep on the sofa and might not return.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It returns. Slightly altered. They keep what they need from the polite version and discard the rest.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: That's reassuring.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Pass me the small one.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I'm not asking, Sophie. I'm waiting.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I know.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Take your time. There's another saucepan.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I declined the partnership.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Yesterday evening. To Helena.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: How was that.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Better than I expected. She's stranger than I thought. She gave me an instruction about how to thank her properly which involves a two-paragraph email at the end of the year.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That sounds like Helena.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: It does.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Are you going to ask me anything.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: No.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I asked you to come up. You came up. You washed the dishes. You said the sentence. There's nothing left for me to ask. If you want me to say something I will say it, but I don't think there's anything for me to say either, so I'd rather not invent one. The kettle is on if you want tea.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Tea, please.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Thank you for not asking.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's a small skill. I've been working on it for thirty-three years.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a person in your life who knows how to wait without asking — who creates a space inside which you can say what you need to say without being prompted. Or, if you can't think of such a person, tell her about a moment when someone did ask you when you needed them to wait, and what difference it made.
prompt_phrases: She/he is the kind of person who waits rather than asks…
What this looks like, in practice, is…
The difference between being asked and being waited for is…

content.situation: Wednesday morning. Sophie has come up to Cedar View on the train — Mum's place near Lancaster, the flat she has been in since selling the house in B1. Sophie has not announced what she's coming for and Mum has not asked. The kitchen is small, warm, with a kettle that whistles and a window onto a square of garden. Sophie is washing up; Mum is drying. They have been doing this in silence for about three minutes. The silence is not heavy. It is the silence of a long-established rhythm between two people who do not need to fill it.

phrase_cle.phrase: I'm not asking, Sophie. I'm waiting.
phrase_cle.example: I haven't said anything for the last ten minutes. I'm not pretending nothing happened. I'm waiting.
phrase_cle.explanation: Различие, которое Mum производит словами, — практическое и трудно достижимое. Asking — активное побуждение собеседника говорить; waiting — удержание пространства, в котором собеседник может говорить, когда сам захочет. Грамматически фраза устроена через прогрессив (am asking / am waiting) с явным противопоставлением через 'not / I'm'. На C1 ты учишься различать эти два режима в собственном поведении и распознавать их в чужом. Многие люди, считающие, что они ждут, на самом деле тихо спрашивают — и собеседник это слышит. Конструкция 'I'm not X, I'm Y' с прогрессивом — рабочий инструмент для уточнения собственной активности в моменте: 'I'm not arguing, I'm clarifying'; 'I'm not complaining, I'm describing'; 'I'm not delaying, I'm pacing.'
