expressions.meaning: A version of Sophie that had been specifically composed for his viewing — for example, by being directed to look at the camera. Henry rejects the arranged version in favour of the unaware version. The verb 'arrange' here implies social staging, and his preference for unstaged data is part of how he thinks.
expressions.expression: A version that had been arranged for me

content.cultural_note: Henry's handling of the photograph is a piece of British emotional engineering at C1 register, and it deserves study. He performs three structural operations. First, he pre-classifies the possible refusals — leaving the photograph would be a 'postponement, not a refusal' — which removes the binary pressure. Second, he lists permitted uses with deliberately escalating extremity, finishing with destruction at Manchester Piccadilly, which liberates Sophie from the assumption that the gift must be preserved. Third, he offers to take the photograph back ('I have managed it for thirty years. Another decade is not a strain') — a reverse-gift that gives Sophie the symmetrical exit. This three-part architecture is fathering by syntax, and it is recognisably the kind of British emotional handling that depends on technical precision rather than effusion. The closing exchange — 'More coffee.' / 'There is more coffee.' — is the standard British shift back to operational reality once the difficult work has been done. Notice that Sophie initiates the shift; she has registered the gift and chosen to close the moment. Henry confirms by stating the simple fact rather than performing hospitality. C1 students should hear the closing as the relief it is.
content.grammar_focus: Henry's offering speech is a sustained exercise in conditional permission. 'If you want to leave it on the table, leave it. If you want to take it and not look at it for a year, take it. If you want to take it and tear it up at Manchester Piccadilly, that is a permitted use of the photograph.' Three first conditionals with imperative consequents, each more extreme than the last, the third concluding with a noun-clause complement instead of an imperative — a slight escalation of formality that brings the structure to a close. C1 students should mark this as a usable rhetorical pattern: a list of three conditionals, with the third varying its predicate to signal closure. Note also Henry's defensive present perfect: 'I never displayed it. I would not have been comfortable explaining it to Hilary, who wouldn't have minded but would have asked, and I would not have known how to answer.' The mixed conditional structure (would not have been + wouldn't have minded + would have asked + would not have known) maps a hypothetical past in granular detail, and the embedded relative clause ('who wouldn't have minded but would have asked') performs character description in two clauses. C1 prose can carry this much hypothetical layering when the speaker has actually thought about it. And Sophie's closing question — 'Why this photograph. There must have been others.' — uses the modal of inferred past existence ('there must have been'), which C1 speakers reach for when they want to assert probability without claiming knowledge.

key_vocabulary.note: Британская писательница (1916–2000), известная компактными, плотными романами, многие из которых получили Booker Prize. Её книги в библиотеке Henry с pencil annotations — характеристика образованного северного интеллектуала. C1 культурный маркер.
Британский кухонный шкаф с открытыми полками сверху и закрытыми ящиками снизу. Не американское 'dresser' (комод). Регистр: домашняя бытовая лексика, специфическая британская.
Старомодная британская кличка для собаки, чаще для лабрадоров или колли. Знак времени (90-е) и класса.
В пленочную эпоху — отдельная сумка для оборудования. Henry уточняет: 'I was holding the camera bag' — он не фотографировал, он был ассистентом. Маленькая деталь о его роли в семье.
Глагол: разместить вещь так, чтобы её было видно. Henry никогда не displayed фотографию — она была всегда спрятана. C1 регистр: precision verb для бытового действия.
Главная железнодорожная станция Манчестера. Henry упоминает её как место, в котором Sophie могла бы порвать фотографию — иронически экстремальный вариант.
Грамматически — passive gerund после to be unaware of. C1 студент использует такие конструкции свободно.
key_vocabulary.word: to be unaware of being photographed

key_vocabulary.note: Британское использование 'keep' как непереходного глагола: 'the bread will keep for two days' = хлеб не испортится. Очень характерное британское разговорное.

comprehension_questions.hint: He is removing a specific psychological pressure. What pressure, and how does the distinction remove it?
comprehension_questions.question: Henry says: 'You can leave it here if you'd rather; it would not be a refusal, it would be a postponement, and it would be fine.' Why does he distinguish between refusal and postponement? What does the distinction do for Sophie?

comprehension_questions.hint: His preference is for the unarranged version. What does he say about access, and what does he say about wanting to remember the version he had?
comprehension_questions.question: Why did Henry choose to keep the photograph 'in which you were not looking at the camera'? What does his explanation reveal about how he has thought about Sophie over the years?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the cultural note's analysis of the closing shift. Sophie initiates; Henry confirms by stating a fact. Why does this count as relief?
comprehension_questions.question: The dialogue ends with Sophie saying 'More coffee' and Henry replying 'There is more coffee.' How does the cultural note explain this exchange as 'the relief it is'?

content.title: The Train Back

dialogue.line: Tell me you survived.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I survived.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Tell me one true thing.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: He is a careful man. More careful than I had been prepared for.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Tell me one false thing you went in expecting.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I went in expecting to come back in two skins. The Sophie who had been there, and the Sophie who was reporting. I came back in one skin.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That's the right outcome.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Is it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You went in expecting to dissociate. You didn't. The fact that you didn't tells you something about the trip. Whatever happened was — small enough to fit in one Sophie. That isn't a failure of significance. That's a success of integration.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Did he behave.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: He behaved. He told me about a woman called Hilary, who lived with him for eleven years and died in 2019. He gave me a photograph of myself at three. He did not over-claim. He did not under-claim. He fed me three times and walked me up a hill.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And the photograph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: In a paperback Penelope Fitzgerald in my bag.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Are you going to look at it tonight.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: No. Henry suggested six months. I'll honour the suggestion. The photograph will keep.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm going to ask one thing and then I'm going to let you sit on the train.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Are you going to go again.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes. In autumn. Not soon. He didn't ask. I haven't said. But yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Stoke is coming up. Buy a tea.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm glad you went.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah. So am I.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a difficult or charged trip you have made — to a place, a person, a situation — and how you returned. Use Marie's frame: did you come back in one skin or two? What did the integration or dissociation tell you about what actually happened? And — answering Marie's closing question — would you go again?
prompt_phrases: I went in expecting to [dissociate / brace / collapse / perform]…
I came back in [one skin / two skins / a different skin]…
He/she didn't ask. I haven't said. But yes.

content.situation: Sunday afternoon. The Bakewell-Sheffield-Manchester train, slow stopping service, late spring landscape outside the windows. Sophie has the photograph in a paperback in her bag and has not looked at it again. She rings Marie from the train. Marie is at home with Olivia, who is six weeks old now and has acquired a routine sufficient for Marie to take a phone call without negotiating. The carriage is half empty. The conversation is the quietest in the block — Sophie has come back from Bakewell with less than she might have expected and more than she can easily say, and Marie's job is to receive the not-quite-shaped material and reflect it back without forcing it into a shape it does not yet have.

phrase_cle.phrase: I came back in one skin.
phrase_cle.example: I expected to be split between the daughter who went and the lawyer who reported. I came back in one skin.
phrase_cle.explanation: Sophie создаёт собственную метафору, в которой целостность переживания описывается через единство тела. 'Skin' здесь — метонимия для 'self' или 'state of being'. Конструкция 'come back in X skin(s)' — авторская, не идиоматическая, но Marie её мгновенно понимает, что и есть признак сильной C1 коммуникации: метафора, которой не обязательно быть в словаре, чтобы работать. Грамматически — past simple + предлог 'in' + numeral + noun. На C1 ты учишься создавать собственные метафоры, опираясь на телесные или пространственные образы, и доверять собеседнику в их прочтении. Конструкция расширяется по контексту: 'I went in two skins' (когда нужно дистанцироваться); 'I'd lost one skin by the end' (когда ослаб защитный слой); 'we came back in different skins' (когда поездка изменила обоих по-разному).

expressions.meaning: Sophie's image for the absence of dissociation. She had expected to return as two parallel selves — the one who had experienced the weekend and the one reporting on it — and instead came back integrated. The skin metaphor is bodily and immediate; Marie reads it instantly.
expressions.expression: I came back in one skin

expressions.meaning: Marie's reframe: what looked like absence of significance was actually presence of wholeness. The construction — 'X of Y' converting an outcome into a category — is psychological vocabulary used at conversational ease. C1 speakers can deploy abstract nouns ('integration', 'differentiation', 'individuation') in close-friendship register without sounding clinical.

Echo of Devraj's diagnostic in L13 — Sophie has internalised the legal-emotional verb pair and now uses it about Henry's whole weekend behaviour. The repetition across the block is an intra-text marker: vocabulary that began as analysis has become an evaluative tool.
expressions.expression: He did not over-claim. He did not under-claim.

expressions.meaning: Marie's small operational instruction, precisely the kind Mum gave to the saucepan in L11 and Henry gave to the suitcase in L15. The form has now been performed by three different characters in this block, each at the closing of a difficult conversation. C1 students should hear it as a recurring British close-relationship move.
expressions.expression: Stoke is coming up. Buy a tea.

expressions.meaning: Sophie's three-part compression of a future plan. The first clause states what Henry didn't do; the second states what Sophie hasn't done; the third — 'But yes' — asserts the intention regardless. The structure is C1 register at full economy: three short propositions, the third overriding the first two through sheer adversative force.
expressions.expression: He didn't ask. I haven't said. But yes.

expressions.meaning: Treat the suggestion as a commitment worth keeping. The verb 'honour' (formal, slightly archaic) used about a casual remark elevates the remark into a near-promise without naming it as such. Sophie's grammar acknowledges that Henry's 'six months' was advice, not instruction, and that she has chosen to accept it as the latter.

content.cultural_note: Marie's role in this closing dialogue is the reflection-without-direction that British close friendship at its best provides. She does not tell Sophie how to feel about the weekend; she gives her two diagnostic prompts ('one true thing', 'one false thing you went in expecting'), receives the answers, and then offers a single reframe that converts Sophie's own description into a usable interpretation: 'That's a success of integration.' Notice that Marie does not ask any of the questions one might expect — what was he like, did you cry, are you all right. She trusts Sophie to volunteer what matters and trusts herself to hear what is offered. The closing exchange — 'I'm glad you went.' / 'Yeah. So am I.' — is one of the most economical pieces of close-friendship affirmation the course has staged. Marie names her position; Sophie aligns with it; the conversation ends. The verb 'glad' is doing precise work — not 'happy' (too much), not 'pleased' (too formal), not 'relieved' (would imply prior worry that needed resolving). 'Glad' is the right register, and Sophie's 'So am I' is the right echo. C1 students should hear that learning when to stop talking is part of fluency, and that the lesson ends here because there is nothing more this conversation needs.
content.grammar_focus: Marie's diagnostic in this dialogue uses a grammatical move worth dwelling on. 'You went in expecting to dissociate. You didn't. The fact that you didn't tells you something about the trip.' Three sentences moving from past simple ('went in expecting') to past simple negative ('didn't') to a present simple anchored on the noun-clause subject ('the fact that you didn't tells you'). The temporal sequence — past expectation, past actuality, present meaning — maps a small piece of psychological work in syntax. C1 students should mark the phrase 'the fact that X tells you Y', which is a diagnostic frame for converting an observation into evidence. Note also Sophie's closing compression: 'He didn't ask. I haven't said. But yes.' — past simple negative, present perfect negative, the elliptical 'But yes' (no verb, no subject). The shift in tense from past to present perfect is doing work: 'didn't ask' is closed (the weekend is over), 'haven't said' is open (Sophie still could). The 'But yes' is grammatically minimal and rhetorically maximal — it is the proposition the whole conversation has been building toward, and it arrives without embellishment. Marie's response — 'Stoke is coming up. Buy a tea.' — performs the British close-relationship closure-by-operation, returning the conversation to the next concrete next step.

key_vocabulary.note: Психологический термин: защитный механизм, при котором сознание отделяет себя от тяжёлого опыта. На C1 — рабочий разговорный глагол среди образованных британцев.
Marie спрашивает 'Did he behave' — разговорный британский для 'did he act in an acceptable way'. Слегка ироническое использование детского глагола применительно к взрослому.

Британский город между Manchester и London на main rail line. Marie упоминает его как маркер, что Sophie проедет через большую станцию и сможет купить чай. Знание британской железнодорожной географии.
Британское разговорное использование: 'a tea' с неопределённым артиклем = чашка чая на станции. C1 регистр: функциональный, не лиричный.
Британская железнодорожная терминология: stopping service vs express. Stopping service делает все остановки на маршруте; медленнее, но проходит через маленькие станции.
Конструкция Marie для извлечения сжатого ответа из перегруженного опыта. Не просьба о полном отчёте — просьба об одной точной формулировке. C1 разговорный приём.
key_vocabulary.word: one true thing / one false thing
key_vocabulary.translation: одна истинная вещь / одна ложная вещь

key_vocabulary.note: 'Stoke is coming up' — Сток уже близко. Британский разговорный: 'something is coming up' = something is approaching in time or space. C1 регистр.

comprehension_questions.hint: Sophie's expectation was a kind of psychological self-protection (going as two parallel selves). Why is the absence of that protection a positive outcome rather than a missed signal?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie says: 'I went in expecting to come back in two skins. I came back in one skin.' What does she mean, and why does Marie respond by calling this 'a success of integration'?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at what the first two sentences establish about the absence of mutual commitment, and what the third sentence asserts despite that absence.
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie's three-part answer to whether she'll go again — 'He didn't ask. I haven't said. But yes.' — is grammatically minimal but rhetorically definitive. What is the 'But yes' overriding?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the analysis of the verb 'glad' and the echo 'So am I'. Why this register rather than something warmer or more elaborate?
comprehension_questions.question: The dialogue ends with 'I'm glad you went.' / 'Yeah. So am I.' How does the cultural note explain this as 'one of the most economical pieces of close-friendship affirmation'?

content.title: First Day

dialogue.line: May I come in.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Calderwood

dialogue.line: Of course.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Welcome to Wellgate. Coffee, no coffee, the kitchen is at the end of the corridor and the milk is on its third life. Don't ask about the kettle.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Calderwood

dialogue.line: Noted.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm not going to give you the speech. I assume someone has given you the speech, and if they haven't, the speech is generally that we are small, we are honest, and we do not have the resources of the place you have come from. Most people who join us from the magic circle find this either liberating or disorienting; some find both. You will let me know which one you are at month three.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Calderwood

dialogue.line: I'd like to say one practical thing. You have, in the corner of your CV, a great deal that is irrelevant to what we do. International commercial arbitration is irrelevant to what we do. Project finance is irrelevant. Energy regulation is — well. Let us say it is at most tangentially relevant on a Tuesday afternoon every three years. Most of what you know is not what we need. I tell you this not to diminish you but so that you stop carrying it as an asset.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Calderwood

dialogue.line: What we need from you is the skill underneath the irrelevant content. The capacity for sustained attention to a brief. The willingness to read a witness statement four times. The instinct for when a client is lying without holding it against them. The patience to sit through a directions hearing with a magistrate who has had a difficult morning. None of this requires international commercial arbitration.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Calderwood

dialogue.line: I assumed something like that.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Then we agree. The second practical thing — and forgive me, I tell every new joiner this and the older juniors find it tedious — is that you will be wrong, here, in the first six months, about things you have been right about for years elsewhere. The wrongness will be small and tactical, not professional. You will phrase a question to a witness in the way you phrased it in mediation, and it will be the wrong question for cross-examination. You will write to a defendant's solicitor in the register you used for your transactional clients, and it will be the wrong register. None of this will be a crisis. It will, however, accumulate, and there is a risk that you will start to feel diminished by the accumulation. I want to vaccinate you against that feeling now. The accumulation is normal. It clears in roughly four months.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Calderwood

dialogue.line: Mr Calderwood, may I ask you something.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Edward, please. We use first names except in court.
dialogue.speaker: Mr Calderwood

dialogue.line: Edward. Why did you take me on. I came in for two interviews and I was not — I was not the most criminal-experienced candidate, and I assume you had others.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Three reasons. The first is that you wrote to us, rather than the other way round; the people who write first know what they are walking into. The second is that you turned down equity, which we noticed. We do not romanticise it; we know that some refusals are crises and some refusals are decisions. Yours read as a decision, and the people in this building tend to be people who made one of those at some point. The third — and forgive the candour — is that I read a memorandum you wrote in 2023 about a client of yours where you advised them not to do a thing they wanted to do, and the memorandum was thirty-four pages long and it persuaded me even though I was not the audience. I would like to have access to that brain on the cases we run here. The fact that the brain has, in the last six months, decided to point itself at our work rather than at someone else's is, frankly, a piece of luck for us.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Was that more than you wanted.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Less, actually. I had been told you didn't explain hires.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I do not, generally. I am explaining this one because you asked, and because I would rather you started the work knowing why you were here than spending eight weeks privately wondering.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Thank you, Edward.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Your first matter is in the green folder on your desk. It is a contested contact case in family court — child of three, separated parents, neither of them ideal. You are second to me. Read the papers. Form your view. Tell me on Wednesday afternoon whether my view, which I will give you tomorrow, is the same as your view. If it is not, I want to hear why, before I decide whose view we proceed on.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Understood.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And get the milk on your way back from lunch. The current milk is medical waste.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

pratique.scenario: Imagine starting a new role in an environment where much of what you previously knew is no longer relevant. Use Edward's frame on yourself: identify the irrelevant assets you might be tempted to keep carrying; identify the underlying skills that are actually transferable; predict the small wrongnesses you will accumulate in the first months and decide how you will resist feeling diminished by them.
prompt_phrases: Most of what I know from [old context] is not what we need here. The skill underneath, however, is…
I will be wrong, in the first months, about [specific small thing] in a way I have not been wrong elsewhere…
I want to vaccinate myself against the feeling that the accumulation will produce.

content.situation: Mid-June, Monday morning. Sophie's first day at Wellgate Chambers, a small criminal and family practice in Manchester occupying the upper two floors of a converted Victorian merchant's house in Castlefield. The transition out of Brindley Carter took three weeks longer than planned; she has been off for two weeks between the firms, which she spent at Cornwall again with Marie and Tom and Olivia. Now she is in a small office on the second floor whose previous occupant left the bookshelves and most of the books. Mr Calderwood — Edward Calderwood KC, head of chambers, sixty-one, criminal silk, once a public defender in Glasgow — knocks on her open door at twenty past nine.

phrase_cle.phrase: Some refusals are crises and some refusals are decisions.
phrase_cle.example: She left the partnership track at thirty-three. People assumed she had had a breakdown; in fact she had reached a conclusion. Some refusals are crises and some refusals are decisions.
phrase_cle.explanation: Конструкция-классификатор, которой Edward отделяет два типа одного и того же действия. Грамматически — параллельная структура 'some X are Y and some X are Z', где X — категория действий, Y и Z — два разных диагноза этой категории. Эта структура — рабочий инструмент C1 регистра для тех случаев, когда внешне идентичные действия имеют принципиально разные внутренние механизмы. Refusal — отказ; crisis — реактивное состояние, при котором решение продиктовано распадом, а не выбором; decision — обдуманное действие, продиктованное направлением. На C1 ты учишься не только использовать такие классификаторы, но и распознавать, к какой категории относится твой собственный недавний выбор. Конструкция расширяется на любые поведенческие категории: 'some apologies are confessions and some apologies are tactics'; 'some silences are restraint and some silences are absence'; 'some yes-es are agreement and some yes-es are surrender'.
phrase_cle.translation: Одни отказы — это кризисы, другие — решения.

expressions.meaning: Past its useful state but still in use, in this case the office milk. The metaphor (lives, as in cats) is dry and operational; Edward is telegraphing both that the chambers is small and that he does not intend to apologise for it. C1 register marker: domestic informality from a senior figure as the opening note.

British shorthand for the five most prestigious commercial law firms in London (Allen & Overy, Clifford Chance, Freshfields, Linklaters, Slaughter and May). Sophie's previous firm Brindley Carter is fictional but adjacent in tier. The phrase is used inside the profession with a mixture of recognition and slight irony.

Edward's mock-precision for 'almost never relevant'. The temporal specification — Tuesday afternoon, every three years — is the joke; it converts a vague low frequency into an absurdly exact one. C1 students should mark this as a recurrent British humour move: hyper-specific exaggeration in service of dry understatement.
expressions.expression: Tangentially relevant on a Tuesday afternoon every three years

expressions.meaning: Cease treating a piece of one's professional history as a strength when in the new context it is not. The verb 'carrying' (rather than 'treating' or 'considering') makes the asset bodily — something you put down rather than mentally reclassify.
expressions.expression: Stop carrying it as an asset

expressions.meaning: Pre-emptively warn you about a feeling you are about to experience so that when it arrives, it cannot do its full damage. Edward's medical metaphor for psychological pre-immunisation. The construction is precise — vaccination implies a small dose of what one is being protected against — and in his speech the dose is the warning itself.

Edward's classification of the act of declining an offer. A crisis-refusal is reactive and reveals instability; a decision-refusal is considered and reveals direction. He places Sophie's in the second category. The construction — 'some X are Y and some X are Z' — is a clean diagnostic tool.
expressions.expression: Some refusals are crises and some refusals are decisions

expressions.meaning: Apologise in advance for being more direct than the social register normally permits. A British formal-personal hedge that signals self-awareness about register-shift. Edward uses it to deliver a piece of substantive flattery without performing flattery.

content.cultural_note: Edward Calderwood's manner in this dialogue is the recognisable register of a senior British barrister at his less-formal best, and several markers deserve attention. He uses first names ('Edward, please'), which signals horizontal collegiality in chambers (lawyers are not 'staff'); he speaks with hyper-precise irony about the office milk; he delivers a substantive piece of welcome (the why-we-hired-you speech) only because Sophie has asked, and prefaces it with a brief disclosure of his own habit ('I do not, generally'). The whole register is calibrated to convey: this is a serious place, but not a self-important one; you are welcome, but not coddled. Note the closing instruction — 'And get the milk on your way back from lunch. The current milk is medical waste.' — which performs the British senior-junior hand-off through a domestic operational task. It is the same register move Mum, Carlos, and Henry have made across previous lessons: closing a difficult or weighted conversation by returning to a small concrete next step. Sophie should leave the office knowing that her hire has been explained, her work has been assigned, and the milk is her responsibility. The hierarchy and the kindness are inseparable.
content.grammar_focus: Edward's speech in this dialogue is sustained C1 register at the upper end of professional spoken English, and rewards study at three points. First, his use of the future tense as predictive narrative: 'You will phrase a question to a witness in the way you phrased it in mediation, and it will be the wrong question for cross-examination. You will write to a defendant's solicitor in the register you used for your transactional clients, and it will be the wrong register.' Two parallel future-simple constructions with embedded past simple ('the way you phrased it', 'the register you used'), each making a confident prediction about specific small failures. C1 speakers can deploy the future tense not as forecast but as quasi-instruction, mapping events that have not happened in such specific terms that the prediction itself becomes a kind of training. Second, Edward's three-reasons structure is built on parallel finite clauses with rising specificity: 'The first is that you wrote to us, rather than the other way round; the people who write first know what they are walking into. The second is that you turned down equity. The third — and forgive the candour — is that I read a memorandum you wrote.' Each reason is followed by a generalising or particularising follow-up; the pattern creates rhythm. Third, his closing instruction sequence — 'Read the papers. Form your view. Tell me on Wednesday afternoon whether my view, which I will give you tomorrow, is the same as your view. If it is not, I want to hear why, before I decide whose view we proceed on.' — uses three imperatives, then a conditional, then an embedded purpose-clause. The whole thing is a small piece of professional architecture.

key_vocabulary.note: В английской правовой системе barristers (адвокаты, представляющие в суде) объединяются в chambers — группы независимых практиков, делящих помещение и административные ресурсы. Не firm. Wellgate Chambers — название места.
Высшее звание в английской правовой системе для barristers, назначается монархом по рекомендации. До 2022 — QC (Queen's Counsel). Edward Calderwood KC — silk, старший адвокат.
Разговорный британский термин для KC: они носят шёлковую мантию вместо шерстяной (junior barrister). 'Criminal silk' — KC, специализирующийся на уголовном праве.
Викторианский индустриальный район Манчестера, теперь деловой и культурный квартал с конвертированными зданиями. Подходящее место для маленького chambers.
Слушание в магистратском или семейном суде, на котором решаются процедурные вопросы. Магистраты в Англии часто непрофессиональные судьи. Edward упоминает терпение к ним как профессиональный навык.
Семейное право: дело, в котором разведённые родители не соглашаются о порядке общения с ребёнком. Sophie получает такое как первое дело — типичное для family junior.
Профессиональный британский термин: Allen & Overy, Clifford Chance, Freshfields, Linklaters, Slaughter and May. Регистр: insider-британский. Sophie — из adjacent tier.
key_vocabulary.translation: the magic circle (пять ведущих лондонских юрфирм)

key_vocabulary.note: 'I do not, generally, explain hires.' — я обычно не объясняю свои решения о найме. Использование существительного 'hire' для конкретного нанятого человека или решения — современный британский корпоративный регистр.

comprehension_questions.hint: He is signalling that this disclosure is exceptional. Why does that matter to Sophie's reading of the disclosure?
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Edward deliver his three-reasons speech only because Sophie asks, and prefaces it with 'I do not, generally'? What does the prefacing do?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the implication that crisis-refusals and decision-refusals select for different kinds of subsequent careers.
comprehension_questions.question: Edward distinguishes between 'crises' and 'decisions' in the category of refusals. Where does he place Sophie's refusal of equity, and why does he say the chambers tends to be staffed by people who made one of those?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the cultural note's closing analysis. The instruction is doing two things at once. What are they?
comprehension_questions.question: The dialogue ends with 'Get the milk on your way back from lunch. The current milk is medical waste.' How does the cultural note explain the milk instruction as inseparable from the hierarchy?

content.title: Marie at the Drawing Board

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm. I'm eating a bad sandwich. Hold on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have been at the drawing board for forty minutes and I have not made a mark.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What kind of mark are you trying to make.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: A logotype, in principle. In practice, anything. A line. A blot. Evidence of recent occupancy.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Soph, the worst thing is — and I want to say this aloud and then we can decide if it's worth taking seriously — the worst thing is that I'm not blocked. I am perfectly capable of drawing. The hand is fine. The eye is fine. What's not fine is that I cannot remember why I would draw. The activity has lost its prior. Do you know what I mean.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie, I want to be careful here, because I don't draw and I'm not your therapist and the bad sandwich is in my left hand. So I'm going to say one thing, and you tell me whether it's relevant, and if it isn't, ignore it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: When I came back to a desk after Cornwall — three weeks off, just the holiday, not anything bigger — I sat at the desk on the Monday and I could not remember what a brief was for. Not how to read one. What it was for. The instruction had emptied out. It came back over about three days, and the way it came back was not by me sitting harder at the desk. The way it came back was by me writing one paragraph of a memo to a colleague about something else entirely, and somewhere inside the paragraph the function of legal writing returned. Not as an idea. As a feeling in the wrist.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: A feeling in the wrist.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes. The body remembers what the brain has temporarily lost. So my one thought is — and you can ignore it — don't try to make the mark you're meant to make. Make a different mark. Doodle. Write your shopping list on the page. Trace your hand. Anything that lets the wrist do the work without the brain pre-screening it. The function will probably re-arrive sideways.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Olivia was up at three. I have not slept more than four hours in a row since March. I had not, until now, made the connection between that fact and the fact that the activity has lost its prior. I had been treating the activity-problem as a creative issue.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It might be both.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It might be both. But it is at least the sleep, which is the one I can do something about, in principle. Although in practice not much.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What if the whole question of returning to work this week is the wrong question, and the right question is some other shape of returning that doesn't yet have a name.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That is — Soph, that is exactly the kind of question I cannot afford to entertain, because if I entertain it I will not deliver the logotype, and if I do not deliver the logotype the client will quietly find someone else, and the small ladder I am trying to climb back onto will quietly disappear. So the answer I have to give you, while privately agreeing with you, is: not this week. Maybe in October. We can entertain it in October.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: All right. October it is.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: But the wrist thing. The wrist thing I will try.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Try the wrist thing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm going. Eat the bad sandwich.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Eating it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a time when an activity you knew well lost its prior — when you could perform the actions but could not remember what they were for. What was the activity, what was the underlying state (sleep, grief, transition, distraction), and what brought the function back, if anything did?
prompt_phrases: I was perfectly capable of [doing the thing]. The hand was fine. What was not fine was…
The activity had lost its prior.
It came back, eventually, sideways — through [unexpected route].

content.situation: Late June, Tuesday afternoon. Olivia is four months old, asleep in the basket Marie has carried into the studio at the end of the garden. Marie has not opened her sketchpad since January. She has, today, a deadline from a client she likes — a small architectural practice that wants brand work for a new project — and she has the studio door closed and the kettle on and the light is good. She has been at the drawing board for forty minutes and she has not made a mark. She rings Sophie, who is on her lunch break in the small kitchen at Wellgate.

phrase_cle.phrase: The activity has lost its prior.
phrase_cle.example: I sat down to work on the dissertation and I could write any sentence in any direction, but I could not remember why the dissertation was worth writing. The activity had lost its prior.
phrase_cle.explanation: Технически — 'prior' это статистический термин: априорное распределение или предположение, с которым приходишь к данным. В разговорном применении Marie использует его для описания ситуации, когда деятельность утратила свою презумпцию смысла. На C1 такие переносы из специализированного регистра в обыденный — рабочий приём; они дают точность, которой нет в стандартной разговорной лексике. Грамматически: present perfect (has lost) + possessive (its). 'Its' здесь относится к самой деятельности — деятельность владела своим основанием, и теперь его лишилась. Конструкция расширяется: 'the conversation has lost its purpose'; 'the relationship has lost its centre'; 'the project has lost its premise'. Каждый раз говорящий сообщает не о провале действия, а о провале того, что делало действие осмысленным. Это очень C1-уровневая диагностика: уметь различать 'я не могу делать X' и 'я не помню, для чего X'.

expressions.meaning: Marie's mock-formal phrase for any mark on the page that would prove she had been there. The estate-agent register (occupancy) applied to her own studio is the joke; she is converting the absence of work into a property-inspection metaphor.

Marie's compressed phrase for a state in which the underlying motivation or assumption that makes an activity meaningful has temporarily evaporated. 'Prior' here is borrowed from probability and statistics ('prior probability', the assumption you bring to evidence) and applied to creative work. C1 students should hear the transposition as characteristic of educated British speech.
expressions.expression: The activity has lost its prior

expressions.meaning: Sophie's image for the bodily memory of a professional skill. Not 'a feeling in the head' or 'a feeling in the hand' — the wrist is the joint that does the work for both writing and drawing, and locating the memory there is a deliberate physical specificity. The body knows when the mind has paused.

Filtering or vetting before something proceeds. From security and broadcasting vocabulary, redeployed for cognitive process. Sophie's instruction to Marie is to bypass the brain's editorial function and let the body lead. C1 register: technical metaphor for psychological process.

Return through an unexpected route rather than directly. Sophie's image for how function and meaning are restored to a temporarily emptied activity — not by pressing harder on the activity itself, but by a peripheral movement that brings it back. The phrase echoes the recurring B2 motif 'the answer arrives sideways'.

Marie's metaphor for her freelance practice as a thing she had to step off temporarily and now must regain. The ladder is small (modest practice, not a corporate one) and her current position is below the bottom rung. The image carries the precariousness of self-employed creative work returning after a pause.
expressions.expression: The small ladder I am trying to climb back onto

expressions.meaning: Marie's two-tier acknowledgement: she agrees with Sophie's larger question but cannot afford to act on the agreement now. The construction — admitting one thing in private while doing another in public — is a precision tool of adult life, and Marie's willingness to name the doubleness is part of why the conversation works.

content.cultural_note: Marie's call to Sophie is a small but precise instance of British close-friendship support that does not perform support. Sophie does not say 'oh poor you', does not commiserate, does not ask follow-up emotional questions. She offers one practical observation from her own recent experience (the desk after Cornwall), translates it into a usable instruction (the wrist thing), and lets Marie do the diagnostic work herself (Olivia at three a.m., the sleep). Marie does the diagnosis aloud, accepts the partial reframing, and politely refuses the larger question Sophie offers. The entire conversation is conducted while Sophie is eating a sandwich — the mundane interruption is part of the register. C1 students should note that the British close-friendship register often refuses the special-occasion mode that other cultures might invoke for such a call. Marie has rung in distress; Sophie has helped; nobody has solemnised the moment, and that is itself the help. The closing exchange — 'I'm going. Eat the bad sandwich.' / 'Eating it.' — performs the same closure-by-operation that has now been used six or seven times in the course. C1 students should hear this as a recurring deep-friendship grammar.
content.grammar_focus: Sophie's central piece of advice is built on a particular C1 grammatical structure: the corrective imperative followed by a permissive triple. 'Don't try to make the mark you're meant to make. Make a different mark. Doodle. Write your shopping list on the page. Trace your hand.' Negative imperative, then four positive imperatives in increasing strangeness — the strangeness is the point, because it widens Marie's permission. C1 speakers can deploy short imperatives in cascading sequences without losing rhythm; the trick is the diminishing modifier (the first imperative has 'a different mark', the next three have no qualifier at all). Note also Sophie's earlier hedging: 'I want to be careful here, because I don't draw and I'm not your therapist and the bad sandwich is in my left hand.' Three reasons stacked in present-tense statements, the third deliberately absurd. The absurd third reason is a deliberate register-shift to keep the seriousness from collapsing into pretension. And Marie's two-tier speech — 'the answer I have to give you, while privately agreeing with you, is: not this week. Maybe in October. We can entertain it in October.' — uses an embedded participial concession ('while privately agreeing with you') to maintain the doubleness in syntax. Reading this aloud, the participle is a subordinate movement and the main clause is the operational refusal. C1 students should mark how the participle holds the agreement while the main clause holds the timing.

key_vocabulary.note: Архитектурно-дизайнерский термин: графический знак или textmark for a brand. Marie работает над logotype для small architectural practice. Профессиональная лексика.
Большой наклонный стол, традиционно используемый дизайнерами и архитекторами. Идиома 'back to the drawing board' (вернуться к началу) происходит отсюда. Marie сидит за реальной drawing board.
Чернильная клякса. Marie упоминает кляксу как минимально приемлемое 'evidence of occupancy' — что угодно лучше, чем ничего.
Из байесовской статистики: prior probability, предположение до получения данных. Marie использует его метафорически. Знание термина — маркер образованного британского регистра.
Британский интеллектуальный глагол: 'I'll entertain that idea' = я готов её обдумать. Marie говорит: 'I cannot afford to entertain it' — у меня нет возможности её всерьёз обдумывать.
Профессиональный глагол: завершить и передать клиенту. 'Deliver the logotype' = сдать логотип в срок. Маркер фриланс-регистра.
Метафора Marie: small ladder = небольшая профессиональная репутация, которую легко потерять и трудно восстановить. Британский разговорный приём.
Sophie предлагает Marie обвести собственную руку как displacement activity. Базовое детское действие, переосмысленное как разблокирующая техника.

comprehension_questions.hint: She is separating two kinds of creative difficulty. One is technical; the other is motivational. Which would be solved by sitting at the board harder, and which would not?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie says: 'I'm not blocked. I am perfectly capable of drawing. The hand is fine. The eye is fine. What's not fine is that I cannot remember why I would draw.' What is the distinction she is drawing, and why does it matter that the problem is the second kind, not the first?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at her phrase 'the body remembers what the brain has temporarily lost'. What is she suggesting about the route by which professional fluency comes back?
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie locate the recovered function 'in the wrist' rather than in the mind? What does her metaphor claim about how skills return after a pause?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is acknowledging that Sophie's reframing of her return-to-work question is correct, but she cannot afford to act on its correctness now. What would acting on it cost her?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie says: 'The answer I have to give you, while privately agreeing with you, is: not this week. Maybe in October.' What does she mean by 'privately agreeing', and why does she postpone entertaining Sophie's larger question?

content.title: Tom's Father

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have eleven minutes and I want to ask you something before Marie comes back.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Will you stay through Sunday.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Don't answer immediately. I'll explain. Marie is — she is fine. She is functioning. Olivia is fine. The household will not collapse if I am not here. I am asking because I do not want Marie to be the only adult in this flat for two nights with a four-month-old, while she is also being the wife of a man whose father is in a hospital that he is travelling to. The arithmetic of that is too much for one person, and I would rather walk out the door knowing that the arithmetic is being done by two people.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I have not asked Marie's permission to ask you. I will ask her permission once I have your answer. If you say no, the question never happened.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Yes. Of course.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom, can I ask you something.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Do you have a sense of how bad it is.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have my mother's account, which has been calibrated to not panic me, and I have my own reading underneath the calibration, which is that she is more frightened than she is letting on. I think it is somewhere between minor and major. I will know in three hours.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Sophie. One more thing.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have spent — twenty minutes thinking about what kind of son I am about to be in a hospital corridor, and the answer is, I do not know, because my father and I have not had the kind of relationship that pre-equips you for those corridors. We have had the kind of relationship that pre-equips you for going to a cricket match together once every three years and not saying very much. I am — I am going up there to find out what I am, and I do not know what I am going to find.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Tom.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: You don't need to know in advance. The corridor will give you a draft.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The corridor will give me a draft.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Yes. And nobody will hold the draft against you. Drafts are draft.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Cab in four minutes. Marie will be back from the bedroom in two. I have to ask her permission for the question I have already asked you, which is the wrong order, but the order I had time for. Forgive me.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: Forgiven.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. The cricket match thing — I know it sounded glib. It wasn't. That is the relationship. I would have liked another one. I did not have the materials for one.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

dialogue.line: I know.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Tom

pratique.scenario: Imagine you are about to enter a difficult situation — a hospital, a funeral, a confrontation, a reunion — and you don't know in advance what kind of person you will be once you are there. Borrow Sophie's frame: the corridor (the actual location) will give you a draft. What corridor are you walking into, and what kind of draft might it produce?
prompt_phrases: I do not know what kind of [son / sister / friend / colleague] I am about to be…
We have had the kind of relationship that pre-equips you for [trivial thing], not for [hard thing]…
The corridor will give me a draft. Drafts are draft.

content.situation: Friday evening, the same week. Sophie has come down to London — half because she had a directions hearing in the Royal Courts on Thursday afternoon and stayed over, half because she wanted to see Olivia. She is at the flat with Marie when Tom comes in at six holding his coat already. His phone rang twenty minutes earlier. His father, Geoffrey, in Newcastle: a heart event, possibly a small stroke, possibly both, the picture not yet clear. Tom is going up tonight on the eight-fifteen from King's Cross. Marie is in the bedroom changing Olivia. Tom and Sophie are alone in the kitchen for the eleven minutes before the cab arrives. This is the first sustained scene with Tom alone since L2, and the first in which Tom is not in the strong position.

phrase_cle.phrase: The corridor will give you a draft.
phrase_cle.example: You don't need to know in advance how you'll feel at the funeral. The room will give you a draft. You can rewrite later.
phrase_cle.explanation: Sophie создаёт метафору в реальном времени, чтобы освободить Tom от тревоги о неизвестности своей будущей реакции. Грамматически — future simple с дополнением, в котором 'a draft' выступает как чернóвый, предварительный вариант чего-либо. Идея в том, что среда сама произведёт первую версию нас в этой среде, и нам не нужно её продумывать заранее. Это очень C1-уровневое освобождение: позволить пространству сделать часть работы за тебя. Конструкция расширяется: 'the room will give you a feeling'; 'the conversation will give you a starting point'; 'the moment will give you a sentence'. Каждый раз говорящий передаёт активность от субъекта к ситуации, что снижает тревогу о готовности. Sophie добавляет: 'Drafts are draft' — почти тавтологическое утверждение, что черновики обладают свойством быть черновиками, и поэтому их можно переписать. На C1 ты учишься использовать такие почти-тавтологии как точные психологические инструменты.

expressions.meaning: Tom's image for the cumulative weight of being simultaneously a wife, a mother to a small infant, and the partner of a man absent on a family emergency. The 'arithmetic' framing converts an emotional load into a calculation, which Tom can articulate and Marie can validate; it is more useful than 'it is a lot for her'.
expressions.expression: The arithmetic of that is too much for one person

expressions.meaning: Tom's apology for asking Sophie before asking Marie's permission to ask Sophie. The reverse-engineered explanation — 'I had to ask her permission for the question I have already asked you, which is the wrong order' — names the procedural irregularity and asks for forgiveness for it without inflating the issue.
expressions.expression: The order I had time for

expressions.meaning: Tom's verb for what his mother has done with her account of his father's condition: tuned the language so as to manage his reaction rather than to convey accurate state. The military-engineering register applied to a mother-son phone call is C1 humour with substance.

The interpretation Tom has formed by hearing past the deliberately steady tone of his mother's report. The metaphor — reading underneath calibration — assumes that two layers of meaning can coexist in a single message and that a competent listener can access both.
expressions.expression: My own reading underneath the calibration

expressions.meaning: Tom's compressed image for the kind of father-son closeness that would have made hospital-corridor moments easier. The verb 'pre-equip' (technical, normally used of materiel or training) does precise work — Tom is naming an absence of training he never had.

Sophie's reply to Tom's anxiety about not knowing in advance what kind of son he will be. The corridor (the actual hospital geography) will produce a first version of him — a draft, not a final form. Sophie's gift to Tom is the metaphor of the draft, which converts an unknowable identity into a writable one.
expressions.expression: The corridor will give you a draft

expressions.meaning: Sophie's tautological reassurance — drafts have the property of being provisional. The form is short, almost gnomic, and the repetition of 'draft' as adjective and noun does the work. C1 students should hear this as a register-marker: the willingness to use a near-tautology to deliver a precise reassurance.

content.cultural_note: Tom's eleven-minute scene with Sophie is a piece of British male-register vulnerability handled with extreme care, and the cultural note to attend to is the procedural ethics of his ask. Tom asks Sophie to stay before he asks Marie's permission to ask her. He names this as 'the wrong order' and apologises for it. In a more procedural culture this might be a minor breach; in this register, the explicit naming of the breach is part of how Tom protects the arrangement from later resentment. He is signalling: I know I have done this in the wrong order; I will rectify by asking Marie next; if she objects, the question is withdrawn. Sophie's 'Forgiven' is the appropriate brief release. Note also the cricket-match remark and Tom's immediate self-correction: 'I know it sounded glib. It wasn't. That is the relationship. I would have liked another one. I did not have the materials for one.' Tom is doing a particular C1 register move — he hears how a previous sentence of his might have sounded, returns to it, names the misreading, and provides the corrected version. The four short sentences after the self-correction perform the precision: 'That is the relationship' (description), 'I would have liked another one' (preference), 'I did not have the materials for one' (causal explanation). Sophie's 'I know' is the only response the register can carry.
content.grammar_focus: Tom's central speech in this dialogue uses a sustained construction that C1 students should master: the conditional explanation that opens with reasons before reaching the request. 'I am asking because I do not want Marie to be the only adult in this flat for two nights with a four-month-old, while she is also being the wife of a man whose father is in a hospital that he is travelling to.' One main clause ('I am asking'), one because-clause, one embedded while-clause, two embedded relative clauses ('a man whose father', 'a hospital that he is travelling to'). Five clauses, four nestings, and the entire structure holds because Tom is keeping the temporal axis straight (now: I am asking; now: Marie would be alone; near future: he is in a hospital). C1 prose can sustain this kind of layering when the speaker has actually thought about the situation, which Tom has — he has had twenty minutes. Note also his triple about his mother's account: 'I have my mother's account, which has been calibrated to not panic me, and I have my own reading underneath the calibration, which is that she is more frightened than she is letting on.' Two parallel possessives ('my mother's account', 'my own reading'), each with a relative clause that does diagnostic work, and a final 'than'-comparative ('more frightened than she is letting on') that compresses the whole into a single judgment. And Sophie's reassurance — 'You don't need to know in advance. The corridor will give you a draft.' — uses the future tense as a piece of psychological provision: what the corridor will do is something Tom can rely on without having to provide it himself. C1 register often does this: deploying the future as a kind of structural assurance.

key_vocabulary.note: Главный лондонский вокзал для поездов на север-восток, включая Newcastle. Tom едет на 8:15 — последний скоростной East Coast service вечером. Британская географическая норма.

Современная медицинская формулировка, охватывающая несколько диагнозов (инфаркт, аритмия, сердечная недостаточность). Используется в медицинской и журналистской лексике, когда точный диагноз ещё неизвестен.
См. L19. Sophie говорит, что у неё было такое слушание в Royal Courts of Justice (London) — что объясняет её присутствие в Лондоне в четверг.
Гражданский и Апелляционный суды в Лондоне на Strand. Викторианская готическая архитектура. Профессиональный британский маркер.
Британское разговорное: уйти из дома, физически. Tom использует это про свой неизбежный отъезд: 'walk out the door knowing that the arithmetic is being done by two people'.
Британский фразовый глагол: 'she is more frightened than she is letting on' = она боится больше, чем показывает. Очень частая C1 разговорная конструкция.
Британское прилагательное о высказывании, которое звучит легко и недостаточно серьёзно. Tom боится, что cricket-match comment прозвучал glib, и сразу это поправляет. C1 регистр самонаблюдения.
Tom's метафора: 'I did not have the materials for one' = у меня не было того, из чего можно было построить такие отношения. Строительная лексика, применённая к человеческим отношениям. C1 регистр.

comprehension_questions.hint: He had limited time and a procedural choice. By naming the wrong order, he is doing two things simultaneously. What are they?
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Tom ask Sophie before asking Marie's permission, and why does he immediately name the irregularity?

comprehension_questions.hint: He assumes that the surface message and the actual state can differ, and that competent family members read both. Why is this assumption mature rather than cynical?
comprehension_questions.question: Tom describes his mother's account of his father's condition as 'calibrated to not panic me' and his own reading 'underneath the calibration'. What does this two-layer model assume about how families communicate in moments of crisis?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is delegating part of the work of becoming-a-son to the location itself. What is being asked of Tom, and what is being asked of the corridor?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie tells Tom: 'The corridor will give you a draft.' How does this metaphor release Tom from the anxiety of not knowing in advance what kind of son he will be?

content.title: Carlos in the Garden

dialogue.line: How's the new place.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Five weeks in. I am wrong about something small every two days.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Two days is good. When I started at the new role, I was wrong about something small every four hours for the first month. It tapered.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Did it taper because the wrongness reduced or because you stopped noticing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Both, I think. Mostly the first. The wrongness genuinely reduced. The not-noticing was a smaller share. You can tell the difference at month four.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: How.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: If you stop being wrong, you also stop catching new failure modes. You don't get better; you stabilise. If the wrongness genuinely reduced, you start noticing finer-grained errors that you couldn't have noticed at month one. The error scale shrinks. That is a good signal.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Hm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph, can I tell you something I have been told myself by someone older and wiser, which I am going to repurpose, because the original recipient has graduated.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: When I started the new role, my old boss said: you are still doing year zero. Don't measure yourself against year four. The mistake people make is to compare their own visible incompetence at month two to the apparent competence of their colleagues at year four, and conclude that they are not cut out for the work. They are. They are simply at a different point on the curve. The colleagues at year four were also incompetent at month two, but you weren't there to watch.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I am giving you this because you are about to enter a phase, around month three, where you will start comparing yourself to Edward.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Don't.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: It hadn't occurred to me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It will. The day will be a Wednesday in late August, the file will be a particularly opaque one, and you will sit at your desk at half-past four and wonder what Edward would have done with it in your time. The answer is: not what you are about to do, because Edward has thirty-two years on the curve. Don't conclude that you are doing the wrong work. Conclude that the work has a curve.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: How are you and Anna.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: We are — recalibrating. Not in trouble. Recalibrating. Mateo has shifted from a household event to a household member, which is a different relational unit. Anna's lab is going through the kind of half-year that lab people go through. I have a permanent role with a quiet brief that I am trying not to over-fill. We are negotiating who is allowed to be tired on which evenings, which is the actual marriage these days. There is no crisis.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Negotiating who is allowed to be tired.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. There is a finite amount of tiredness available to a household, and once one person uses theirs, the other has to defer. We are getting better at the deferral. We were not initially good at it.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Do you tell Anna you are tired, or do you wait.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have learned to tell her, because the alternative — performing energy I do not have — is a worse marital position than the honest position, which is to say, I cannot do bedtime tonight. She will say either fine or not fine. If she says not fine, we negotiate. If she says fine, I owe her bedtime tomorrow. The accounting is not a romance. The accounting is the thing that lets the romance survive.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I am giving you this because in the chambers, somewhere in year one, you will start to need an accounting system with the people in your life. Friends, family, lovers. Don't pretend you don't. Make the accounting visible. It does not have to be elegant. Mine is a series of recriminations and small kindnesses, and it works.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Hm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The bottle is empty. Marie is back. Mateo is awake and is making the noise that means he is about to be upset.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah. Refill the kettle on the way in.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a relationship in your life — partner, family, close friend — that has an 'accounting system'. What is in the accounting? How does it work? Has it changed in the last year, and if so, in which direction (more visible, less visible, more elegant, more efficient)?
prompt_phrases: The accounting between us is mostly about [what]…
It is not elegant. It works.
When I am tired, I have learned to tell her/him, because the alternative…

content.situation: Saturday afternoon, mid-July, Carlos and Anna's small Greenwich garden. Tom's father stabilised — minor stroke, recovering, Tom returned to London five days ago after a weekend in Newcastle. Sophie has come down for the weekend to see Olivia and Marie, and Carlos and Anna have hosted a Saturday lunch — Marie, Tom, Olivia, Sophie, Mateo (now nearly two), and Anna's sister Naomi who has just left. The garden has the particular Saturday-late-afternoon quiet that follows a big lunch. Marie is upstairs with Olivia. Anna and Tom are in the kitchen washing up and not entirely talking. Carlos and Sophie are on the bench at the back of the garden with the last of a bottle of Chablis and the sun on the wall opposite.

phrase_cle.phrase: Don't measure yourself against year four.
phrase_cle.example: I'm three months into the new role and feel incompetent next to colleagues who have been here for years. That's the wrong comparison. Don't measure yourself against year four.
phrase_cle.explanation: Конструкция-предостережение, в которой 'year four' — это собирательный образ опытного коллеги, чья компетентность стала невидимой как компетентность и выглядит как лёгкость. Сравнивать своё видимое year-zero затруднение с чужой невидимой year-four лёгкостью — типичная когнитивная ошибка начала любой новой деятельности. Грамматически — отрицательный императив с возвратным местоимением 'yourself' и предлогом 'against'. Глагол 'measure' здесь — оценочное сравнение через какую-то шкалу. На C1 ты учишься распознавать эту ошибку в своей собственной работе и применять конструкцию как ментальную защиту. Конструкция расширяется: 'don't measure yourself against last year's version of you'; 'don't measure your draft against their finished version'; 'don't measure your beginning against their middle'. Все варианты — заявления о том, что временные шкалы должны быть выровнены прежде, чем сравнение будет осмысленным.
phrase_cle.translation: Не сравнивай себя с четвёртым годом.

expressions.meaning: Carlos's quoted instruction from his own former boss, repurposed for Sophie. The two ordinal numbers do compressed work — 'year zero' is the developmental phase before competence becomes legible; 'year four' is the phase by which competence has been forgotten as competence and looks like ease. The whole thing is a calibration tool against unfair self-comparison.
expressions.expression: You are still doing year zero. Don't measure yourself against year four

expressions.meaning: Carlos's diagnostic for genuine improvement: as you get better, the errors you catch become smaller and more specific. The phrase belongs to engineering and quality-control vocabulary; Carlos transposes it to professional development. C1 students should hear this kind of technical-personal language as a register marker.

Anna and Carlos's term for what their family has become since Mateo became a participating member rather than a managed object. The phrase is psychologically informed without being clinical, and Carlos uses it to describe the structural rather than affective shift.

Carlos's compressed account of the central daily question of a working couple with a small child. The phrase is literal — there is finite tiredness in a household — and metaphorical, because the negotiation is the marriage operating in real time. The construction has been quoted by readers of similar British marital novels (Anne Enright, Maggie O'Farrell) but Carlos's version is direct.
expressions.expression: Negotiating who is allowed to be tired

expressions.meaning: Carlos's two-step argument that domestic logistics are not the opposite of love but the substrate that makes love sustainable. The repetition of 'the accounting' and the contrast 'is not / is the thing that' — short clauses, parallel structure — produces a sententious form, which Carlos delivers without sentimentality.
expressions.expression: The accounting is not a romance. The accounting is the thing that lets the romance survive

expressions.meaning: Carlos's verb for pretending to be capable of a thing one is no longer capable of. The verb 'perform' is theatrical; the noun 'energy' is biological. The transposition makes the dishonesty visible — performing biology is a category error, and Carlos has learned to refuse it.
expressions.expression: Performing energy I do not have

expressions.meaning: Carlos's honest description of his own marital accounting system — alternations of grievance and gesture, neither idealised. The phrase 'recriminations and small kindnesses' is a calibrated pair: the first noun is heavy and Latinate; the second noun is light and Anglo-Saxon. The pairing is C1 register at its most precise.
expressions.expression: A series of recriminations and small kindnesses

content.cultural_note: Carlos's mode in this dialogue is the British male mentor-friend register at full strength: he gives Sophie two pieces of professional advice, both of which are repurposed from his own mentors, and one piece of marital advice that is unembellished and original. The cultural marker to attend to is the transmission chain. Carlos has been told 'don't measure yourself against year four' by his old boss; he passes it to Sophie because the original recipient — Carlos himself — has graduated. The phrase 'has graduated' is doing dry work: he means he no longer needs the instruction. C1 students should hear this as part of how British male professional culture organises mentorship: instructions are passed forward when they have done their work for the receiver, and the form of the instruction is preserved with a small acknowledgement of source. Carlos's marital disclosures — 'recriminations and small kindnesses', 'negotiating who is allowed to be tired' — are unusual in British male register for their specificity, and Sophie's receipt of them without comment is part of why Carlos is willing to make them. The closing exchange — 'Refill the kettle on the way in' — is once again the operational closure: a small task that signals the conversation is complete and the next phase of the household has begun.
content.grammar_focus: Carlos's speech is a sustained example of professional-personal register at C1, and his most intricate construction is the predicted-day instruction: 'The day will be a Wednesday in late August, the file will be a particularly opaque one, and you will sit at your desk at half-past four and wonder what Edward would have done with it in your time.' Three future-simple clauses chained, with the third introducing a subordinate question containing a modal-conditional ('what Edward would have done'). C1 speakers can predict not just events but their precise texture — Wednesday, late August, half-past four — and the precision is the rhetorical force. Note also Carlos's use of present perfect as continuing relevance: 'I have learned to tell her, because the alternative — performing energy I do not have — is a worse marital position than the honest position, which is to say, I cannot do bedtime tonight.' The dash-bracketed parenthetical contains a participial phrase ('performing energy I do not have') which functions as the subject of a comparative ('is a worse marital position than'). Carlos can stack this much without losing the listener because the comparison is genuinely useful. And his closing aphorism — 'The accounting is not a romance. The accounting is the thing that lets the romance survive.' — uses repetition with variation ('is not X / is the thing that lets X survive') as a small piece of structural rhetoric. C1 register tolerates this kind of sententiousness from a speaker who has earned it.

key_vocabulary.note: Глагол из медицинской и военной лексики (the dose tapered, the offensive tapered). Carlos применяет его к ошибкам: 'It tapered.' Британский разговорный профессиональный регистр.
Из инженерной и системной лексики: разные способы, которыми система может отказать. Carlos переносит это в профессиональное обучение. C1 регистр.
Метафора: учебная кривая (learning curve) или кривая опыта. 'Edward has thirty-two years on the curve' = у Edward тридцать два года опыта. Британское разговорное.
Юго-восточный район Лондона на Темзе. Carlos и Anna живут там — типичный для middle-class профессиональных пар с детьми. Британская географическая норма.
Сухое белое вино из Бургундии. Британский middle-class маркер: умение выбрать конкретный регион, не общий 'white wine'. Sophie и Carlos пьют его в саду в субботу днём.
Из метрологии: восстановить точность измерительного прибора. Carlos применяет к своим отношениям: 'we are recalibrating'. Современный британский разговорный приём.
Профессиональный британский: 'brief' = задание, ответственность, портфель. 'A quiet brief' — без публичности, без давления. Carlos описывает свою новую permanent role.
Существительное, которое Carlos использует метафорически: система баланса между супругами по поводу обязанностей и усилий. C1 разговорный приём — применять корпоративный термин к семейной жизни.

comprehension_questions.hint: He treats useful instructions as portable across people. Why does the phrase 'has graduated' do important work in this account?
comprehension_questions.question: Carlos describes his old boss's advice as something he is 'repurposing, because the original recipient has graduated.' What does this transmission chain reveal about how Carlos thinks about mentorship?

comprehension_questions.hint: Generic warnings are easy to dismiss. Specific predictions land differently. What does the texture of the prediction do for Sophie's future self?
comprehension_questions.question: Carlos predicts that Sophie will compare herself to Edward on a Wednesday in late August at half-past four. Why does the precision of the prediction make the warning more effective?

comprehension_questions.hint: Many cultures oppose 'love' to 'logistics'. Carlos refuses the opposition. What does he claim about the relationship between the two?
comprehension_questions.question: Carlos says: 'The accounting is not a romance. The accounting is the thing that lets the romance survive.' How does this two-step claim challenge a more conventional view of long-term love?

content.title: Eleanor Stays

dialogue.line: I ordered for both of us. The pho here is excellent and the alternative is the spring rolls which are mediocre.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Pho is fine. Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. I have made a decision and I would like to tell you about it before we eat, because if I tell you while eating I will dribble pho on my shirt and the moment will be undignified.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am staying.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: At the firm. Equity. I have accepted the partnership offer they made me three weeks ago. The signing-on goes through in October.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I want to say several things and I want to say them in an order. The order is partly for me and partly for you, so bear with me.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: First. I am not telling you this because I think you'll be hurt by it. I am telling you because I know you, and you will read significance into the silence if I do not tell you, and I would rather we both have the actual information.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Second. The decision is mine. It is not a reaction to your decision. It is also not a reaction-against-your-decision-having-destabilised-me, which is what I was afraid of in May. I have spent the last three months — much of it lying awake at small hours — asking whether I was staying because I had reached a conclusion, or staying because I could not face a departure. The answer, when it came, was that I had reached a conclusion. The conclusion is mine.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Third. The conclusion was approximately this. I like, more than I had been admitting, the fact that the firm gives me complex problems with high stakes and competent colleagues. I do not like, but I do not require liberation from, the things I do not like. The complaints I would make if you asked me to make them are real, but they are not the architecture of my life. The architecture of my life turns out to include the firm. I had been confused about that.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Fourth. I am, having reached this conclusion, a slightly different version of myself than I was three months ago. The version that was destabilised by your decision is gone. The version I am now is a person who has chosen the firm, rather than a person who is in the firm by inertia. The choice has done something to the relationship.
