The relationship is no longer the relationship that was running in the background while I told myself stories. It is a relationship I am paying for.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: That is the harder thing to do.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I think it might be. I had not let myself frame it that way until you just said it. I am — Sophie, I am going to take a moment with the pho before I respond to that, because if I respond now I will say something I have to walk back.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Take your time.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The thing I want to say back is this. What you did in March was right for you. What I am doing in July is right for me. The two of us did different things and both things were correct, and the test of that is that neither decision feels like a refutation of the other. If your decision had made mine impossible, or mine had made yours impossible, one of us would be wrong. We are not. The fact that we can sit at this table and eat pho is the test.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: And one more thing, which I will say quickly because the pho is going cold.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Thank you for leaving. The leaving made the staying possible. I do not know how to say that without it sounding instrumental, because I do not mean it instrumentally. I mean it factually. If you had stayed, I would have stayed, and the staying would have been the staying-by-default I have been doing for eight years. Your departure made my staying a thing I had to choose. I have now chosen it. The thing you gave me, in March, by leaving, was the chance to do that. I owe you for it.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor, you don't owe me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. The owing isn't an obligation. The owing is the accurate description. I am — I am noting the accurate description, and now I am going to eat the pho.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Congratulations.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Thank you. That is the right word.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a decision you have made — or are making — to stay in something rather than leave it. Use Eleanor's frame: distinguish between staying-by-default and staying-by-choice. What was the decision actually about? What did you have to face about your own situation in order to convert default into choice?
prompt_phrases: I had been confused about whether I was [in this thing] because I had reached a conclusion, or because I could not face a departure…
The architecture of my life turns out to include…
It is now a [thing] I am paying for, rather than a [thing] I am inhabiting by inertia.

content.situation: Late July, Manchester. Eleanor has rung Sophie a week earlier and asked whether they could have lunch — not at the Italian place near Spinningfields where they last sat together (in L12), but at a Vietnamese place near Sophie's new chambers, which Eleanor specifically asked Sophie to choose. The choice of place — Eleanor coming to Sophie's territory rather than vice versa — was Sophie's first signal that something had shifted. The lunch is mid-week. Sophie has come down at half-past twelve from a hearing; Eleanor is already at the table. The restaurant is small, busy, kind. Eleanor has news.

phrase_cle.phrase: The leaving made the staying possible.
phrase_cle.example: She quit the band. I had been sitting on my own departure for two years. Her quitting forced me to ask whether I was staying because I wanted to or because I was afraid. The leaving made the staying possible.
phrase_cle.explanation: Хиазм — риторическая фигура, при которой два понятия меняются местами в параллельной структуре. Здесь: leaving / staying переставлены так, что уход одного человека стал условием для осознанного оставания другого. Грамматически — простое утверждение past simple с двумя герундиями в качестве подлежащего и дополнения. Формула 'X made Y possible' — рабочая для описания причинной связи, в которой одно событие создаёт условия для другого. Eleanor использует её, чтобы выразить парадокс: уход Sophie был не тем, что разрушило Eleanor's жизнь, а тем, что позволило её жизни стать выбранной. На C1 ты учишься распознавать и создавать такие чиазмы — они компрессируют сложную причинно-следственную ситуацию в одно запоминающееся предложение. Конструкция расширяется: 'the silence made the answer possible'; 'the failure made the rebuilding possible'; 'the loss made the gratitude possible'. Каждый раз говорящий утверждает, что то, что выглядит негативным, было условием для чего-то ценного.
phrase_cle.translation: Твой уход сделал моё оставание возможным.

expressions.meaning: Eleanor's hyphenated noun-phrase for the specific failure mode she was afraid of: choosing the opposite of Sophie's choice purely as a corrective overreaction. The hyphenation compresses what would otherwise be a long clause and signals self-aware diagnosis. C1 students should mark the hyphenation as a written-text technique entering speech.

Eleanor's metaphor for the structural arrangement of the things she values, as opposed to the surface complaints she might make. The contrast — architecture (load-bearing) versus complaints (decorative) — does diagnostic work. The phrase echoes Mum's 'geometry of my life' from L14; Eleanor reaches for the same kind of figure.

Eleanor's phrase for the firm-as-relationship now that she has chosen it consciously. The verb 'paying for' is precise — she is bearing the cost of a thing she has selected, as opposed to receiving it by default. The accounting metaphor (which Carlos used in L22) returns here in a different application.
expressions.expression: A relationship I am paying for

expressions.meaning: Eleanor's compressed thanks to Sophie. The chiasmus — leaving / staying as inverted gerunds — turns a complex causal relationship into a memorable sentence. C1 students should hear the chiasmus as a rhetorical device that compresses an argument that would otherwise need three sentences.
expressions.expression: The leaving made the staying possible

expressions.meaning: The hyphenated noun-phrase Eleanor coins for the kind of staying that is not chosen but inhabited. The hyphenation makes the phrase a concept; the concept lets her name what she had been doing for eight years and is no longer doing.

Eleanor's verbal manoeuvre when Sophie deflects her thanks. She refuses to convert 'I owe you' into 'I am obligated to you' (which would be inaccurate); instead she insists on the descriptive accuracy of 'owing' as a word, while disclaiming its operational implications. The move is precise, lawyerly, and characteristic of her.
expressions.expression: I am noting the accurate description

expressions.meaning: Eleanor's response to Sophie's 'Congratulations'. By calling it 'the right word', Eleanor is doing two things: she is accepting the congratulation, and she is noting that the register of the moment requires that specific lexical choice rather than another (e.g. 'thank you', 'I appreciate it'). C1 register awareness in two syllables.

content.cultural_note: Eleanor's restaurant choice is itself the opening move of the conversation, and a C1 close-friendship register marker. By coming to Sophie's territory rather than asking Sophie to come to hers, Eleanor is signalling: I am the one with news, I am the one moving toward you, I am respecting that you are now a different professional creature in a different part of the city. Sophie reads this signal silently and arrives prepared to receive whatever Eleanor has come to say. The Vietnamese pho is a small additional move: Eleanor has chosen a register lower than the Italian place at Spinningfields, which is the slightly-formal lawyer-lunch register; pho is more casual, a lunch one eats with chopsticks, and the casualness is part of the news-delivery framing. Note Eleanor's deliberate ordering of her four points, with each one beginning 'First, Second, Third, Fourth' — this is professional-speech clarity used in a personal moment, and Eleanor is using the register she trusts (lawyer-organised speech) to deliver the personal content (a friendship-and-career disclosure). The closing exchange — Sophie says 'Congratulations', Eleanor says 'That is the right word' — performs the precision the whole lunch has been operating at. The British close-friendship register can carry this much architectural care, and C1 students should hear that the architecture is the affection.
content.grammar_focus: Eleanor's central speech is structured by an explicit numbered architecture — 'First. Second. Third. Fourth.' — each followed by a thesis statement and an explanation. C1 speakers can deploy explicit numbering in spoken English when the material genuinely has discrete components that need separation; the technique signals that the speaker has thought carefully and that the listener can follow at the speaker's pace. Within each numbered point, Eleanor uses precise grammatical machinery. In the second point: 'I have spent the last three months — much of it lying awake at small hours — asking whether I was staying because I had reached a conclusion, or staying because I could not face a departure.' Present perfect main clause ('I have spent'), parenthetical participial ('lying awake at small hours'), continuous gerund ('asking'), embedded indirect question ('whether I was staying because…'), three different past simples ('had reached', 'could not face') doing distinct semantic work. This is C1 register at its most layered. Note also Eleanor's use of chiasmus in the closing section: 'The leaving made the staying possible.' Two gerunds in inverted positions (leaving / staying), with the verb 'made possible' as the connector. C1 prose can use this device for rhetorical compression. And Sophie's small, perfectly-placed observation — 'That is the harder thing to do.' — uses the comparative without specifying the comparison; both speakers know what is being compared (Sophie's leaving and Eleanor's staying), and naming it would be excessive.

key_vocabulary.note: Вьетнамский лапша-суп с говядиной или курицей. Британское разговорное произношение приближено к 'fer'. Современный британский ресторанный лексикон.
Британский разговорный глагол: пролить жидкую еду неаккуратно. Eleanor использует его иронически про себя ('I will dribble pho on my shirt').
Британский разговорный: финальное оформление трудового договора или партнёрского соглашения. Eleanor говорит про signing-on в октябре — partnership agreement.
Британское поэтическое выражение для 1-3 часов ночи. 'Lying awake at small hours' — типичный образ человека, который не может спать из-за трудных размышлений. C1 регистр.
Параллельная конструкция Eleanor: два возможных мотива для одного и того же поведения. Reach — активное приближение к выводу; face — пассивное столкновение с неизбежностью.
key_vocabulary.word: to reach a conclusion (vs. to face a departure)
key_vocabulary.translation: прийти к заключению (vs. решиться на уход)

key_vocabulary.note: Метафора: структурные несущие элементы. Echo Mum's 'geometry of my life' (L14). Eleanor использует своё слово, но в той же категории.

Из политической журналистики: 'walk back' = частично отказаться от ранее сказанного. Eleanor говорит, что хочет 'take a moment' прежде чем ответить, чтобы не сказать что-то, что придётся walk back. C1 разговорный.
Философский термин: ценимый только как средство для другой цели. Eleanor говорит 'I do not mean it instrumentally' — она не сводит благодарность к функциональной полезности Sophie. C1 регистр.

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at her admission that the version of herself destabilised by Sophie's departure is now gone. What was the labour of those three months actually doing?
comprehension_questions.question: Eleanor distinguishes between 'staying because I had reached a conclusion' and 'staying because I could not face a departure'. Why does this distinction matter to her, and what did three months of asking the question give her?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is making a precise causal claim. Without Sophie's leaving, what would Eleanor's staying have been? With it, what does her staying become?
comprehension_questions.question: Eleanor says: 'The leaving made the staying possible.' What does this chiasmus claim about the relationship between Sophie's decision in March and Eleanor's decision in July?

comprehension_questions.hint: Eleanor has not done a small thing. She has done a hard thing. The cultural note discusses this. What does 'congratulations' acknowledge that 'thank you' would not?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie says only 'Congratulations' and Eleanor replies 'That is the right word.' Why is 'congratulations' the right word here, when in many contexts thanks would be the conventional reply?

content.title: Marie at the Drawing Board (Again)

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm. I'm walking back to chambers. Hold on, I'm crossing Liverpool Road.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Crossed.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am at the drawing board. I have been at the drawing board for two hours. I have made — I have made a great many marks, most of them wrong, but the fact that there are marks at all is what I am ringing about.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That is excellent.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I tried the wrist thing. It worked, eventually. Not in June. In late July, when I was working on the actual logotype after I had given up trying to do it directly. I had been doodling crosshatches on the corner of the page and the shape of the cross-stroke for the letterform suddenly arrived. It arrived sideways, as you predicted. I owe you the wrist thing.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You don't owe me. The wrist thing wasn't mine. It was something the body knew.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph. Two more things and then I will let you walk back to chambers.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: First. Olivia is sleeping through, mostly. Five and a half hours at a stretch, then a feed, then another two. I am — I am sleeping, by the standards of the past four months. I am not the woman I was at day eleven. I am a different woman, and I would not be able to tell you with certainty whether the new woman is more or less polite than the original. The polite version did return. It returned slightly altered, as Mum predicted.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Second. Tom is back from Newcastle as a different version of himself too. His father is alive, and stable, and has been moved to the rehabilitation ward, and Tom and his father had — Tom told me this on Sunday, which I had not been allowed to ask about — Tom and his father had three conversations in the hospital that they had not had in thirty-five years. I am not going to tell you what they were about. They were Tom's. But Tom came back with material he did not have when he left. Material in his hands. The way he holds Olivia is different. I do not have the language for it yet.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What I'm hearing is — three different people have come back to themselves over the same six weeks, in three different ways, and you are the one who has been watching it happen.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I had not framed it that way. But yes. I think that is true.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: And you are also one of the three.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm glad you're at the drawing board.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph. Are you going to Bakewell again.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes. October. He hasn't asked. I haven't said. We have not been in touch since I left, by mutual unstated agreement. But yes. October.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Good.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Olivia has woken up. The sun has moved. I am going.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Go.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Thank you for the wrist thing.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a six-week or three-month period in your recent life during which several people you are close to changed in parallel. What were the changes? Who was watching whom? Were you also one of the people changing — and if so, who was watching you?
prompt_phrases: Three different people came back to themselves over the same [period], in three different ways…
I had not framed it that way. But yes.
He/she came back with material he/she did not have when he/she left.

content.situation: Early August, Tuesday late afternoon. Six weeks since L20. Marie is in the studio at the end of the garden again, but this time the door is open onto the small lawn and Olivia is on a sheepskin in a patch of sun, awake but quiet. Marie is standing at the drawing board with a pen in her hand and a sheet of tracing paper over a draft logotype she has been worrying since Friday. The architectural client liked the logotype she eventually delivered in late July; they have asked her for the rest of a small identity system — letterhead, business cards, a website palette. She is, for the first time since January, doing the work without the activity having lost its prior. She rings Sophie at quarter to five.

phrase_cle.phrase: It arrived sideways, as you predicted.
phrase_cle.example: I'd been trying to write the speech for weeks. It came eventually, while I was loading the dishwasher. It arrived sideways, as you predicted.
phrase_cle.explanation: Глагол 'arrive' для функций, идей или состояний — характерное C1 разговорное использование. Понимание, ясность, креативность не достигаются прямым усилием — они приходят, прибывают, появляются, и часто из неожиданного направления. 'Sideways' (с боковой стороны) метафорически обозначает обходной путь — не лобовой, не предсказуемый. Грамматически — past simple ('arrived') + adverbial ('sideways') + comparison clause ('as you predicted'). На C1 ты учишься говорить о собственных когнитивных процессах в этом регистре — описывать понимание не как достижение, а как приход. Конструкция расширяется: 'the answer arrived sideways'; 'the right word arrived sideways'; 'the courage arrived sideways'. Каждый раз ты признаёшь, что не контролировал момент возникновения, и это признание — часть зрелой коммуникации.
phrase_cle.translation: Это пришло с боковой стороны, как ты и предсказывал.

expressions.meaning: Marie's confirmation that Sophie's instruction from L20 worked. The verb 'arrived' is C1-typical for the way understanding or function returns — not as a result of effort but as a movement that happens to the recipient. The 'sideways' marks the obliqueness of the route. C1 students should mark this as a recurrent course-world figure.
expressions.expression: It arrived sideways, as you predicted

expressions.meaning: Parental shorthand for an infant who has begun to sleep for longer stretches at night. The qualifier 'mostly' is honest — five and a half hours is not full continuity, but it is the threshold past which a parent can feel rested. The phrase belongs to a register of carefully calibrated parental honesty.

Marie's relativising adverbial: the sleep is not absolutely good, but it is good given the recent baseline. C1 speakers often use this kind of contextual relativisation — 'by the standards of X' — to deliver an honest report that would be misread without the context.
expressions.expression: By the standards of the past four months

expressions.meaning: Marie's image for what Tom returned with from Newcastle. The metaphor — Tom physically carrying something tangible from the hospital corridor — captures the difference between Tom-before and Tom-after without naming what the material is. The phrasing protects the privacy of what was said while honouring the change.

Marie's admission that the change in Tom is not yet describable. The future-marker 'yet' is doing precise work — she expects to develop the language, but acknowledges its current absence. C1 register at its most precise: signalling that a description is in process.
expressions.expression: I do not have the language for it yet

expressions.meaning: Sophie's phrase for the silence between herself and Henry since Bakewell — neither of them initiated contact, and neither felt the absence as failure. The construction 'mutual unstated' compresses what would otherwise be a paragraph: both parties consented to the silence; neither required the consent to be articulated. C1 register at full strength.

Marie's two-step response when Sophie offers a reframing she had not seen. The form — denial of prior framing followed by affirmation of the new framing — is a recurrent course-world move. It honours the new perspective without manufacturing prior agreement.
expressions.expression: I had not framed it that way. But yes.

content.cultural_note: The phone call between Marie and Sophie in this lesson is the closing scene of Block 4 and performs a specific structural function: it gathers the threads. Marie reports Olivia's sleep, Tom's return, her own work — three different parallel restorations. Sophie names what Marie has not yet named: that Marie is one of the three, and that she is also the witness. This reframing is offered without being insisted on. Marie accepts it without resistance ('I had not framed it that way. But yes.'). The British close-friendship register is here at its quietest: the friends are exchanging not advice or news but mutual recognitions. The closing exchange — 'Thank you for the wrist thing.' / 'Yeah.' — refuses any larger summary. The 'yeah' is a deliberate non-elaboration; if Sophie had said 'you're welcome' or 'I'm glad it helped', the moment would have been excessively closed. The single syllable, in this register, is the right size. C1 students should hear this lesson as the structural counterweight to L20: the same two people, the same drawing board, six weeks later, with the function returned. The course is teaching that fluency, like creative work, returns sideways.
content.grammar_focus: Marie's reports in this dialogue use a sustained C1 construction: the parallel future-perfect of return, in which the same change is reported about three different people in slightly different forms. 'I am a different woman.' / 'Tom is back from Newcastle as a different version of himself too.' / 'Three different people have come back to themselves.' The repetition of the structure signals the parallel; the variation signals that each return is its own. C1 prose can sustain this kind of structural rhyme without it becoming mechanical because each instance carries its own specificity ('not the woman I was at day eleven', 'material in his hands', 'have come back to themselves'). Note also Marie's two-stage report about Tom: 'Tom told me this on Sunday, which I had not been allowed to ask about' — a relative clause with past perfect that retroactively maps the prohibition Marie had been observing. The 'allowed' is doing precise work: she had not been forbidden, she had been not-allowed in the way that close families know not to ask. Sophie's diagnostic — 'Three different people have come back to themselves over the same six weeks, in three different ways, and you are the one who has been watching it happen' — uses the present perfect to compress a complex temporal event into a single observation. C1 speakers can deploy the present perfect as a binding tense that holds disparate developments in one frame.

key_vocabulary.note: Полупрозрачная бумага, которую дизайнеры и архитекторы кладут поверх черновика для итераций. Marie работает с tracing paper над draft logotype. Профессиональный дизайнерский лексикон.
Дизайнерский термин: identity system = логотип + типографика + цветовая палитра + правила применения. 'A small identity system' — компактная версия для маленького клиента.
key_vocabulary.word: the rest of a small identity system

key_vocabulary.note: Британское разговорное использование 'worry' как переходного глагола: многократно перерабатывать что-либо. 'Worrying since Friday' — обкатывает с пятницы. C1 разговорный.
Историческая улица в Castlefield, Манчестер, у Wellgate Chambers. Британская географическая норма.
Графический приём: пересекающиеся линии для затенения. Marie начала рисовать crosshatches как displacement activity, и оттуда форма буквы пришла. Дизайнерский термин.
Типографический термин: структурная форма отдельной буквы как графического объекта. C1 профессиональный регистр.
Британская медицинская терминология: ward в больнице, специализирующийся на восстановлении после серьёзных событий (инсульт, операция). Tom's отец перевёлся туда после стабилизации.
Юридическая лексика, перенесённая в личный регистр. 'Mutual' = обоюдный; 'unstated' = невысказанный. Sophie использует это про молчание между ней и Henry. C1 регистр.

comprehension_questions.hint: Marie has been describing her own return, Tom's return, Olivia's settling. Sophie is gathering them. What does the gathering give Marie that she did not have before?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie names what Marie has not named: 'three different people have come back to themselves over the same six weeks, in three different ways, and you are the one who has been watching it happen.' What is Sophie offering Marie by reframing the situation this way?

comprehension_questions.hint: The actual content of Tom's three hospital conversations is private. Marie respects the privacy. But she needs to communicate that something happened. How does the metaphor solve both problems?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie says of Tom: 'He came back with material he did not have when he left. Material in his hands. The way he holds Olivia is different. I do not have the language for it yet.' Why does Marie use the metaphor of 'material' rather than naming what the change is?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the cultural note's analysis of the single-syllable closure. What does the brevity preserve?
comprehension_questions.question: The closing exchange is 'Thank you for the wrist thing.' / 'Yeah.' How does the cultural note explain why 'yeah' is the right-sized response, and what would have been wrong with 'you're welcome'?

content.title: Reykjavík

dialogue.line: How are you doing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm doing — fine. I want to flag, before you ask, that I have a phone in my pocket which is on, and that Tom and Mum have been told to text me every five hours whether or not anything is happening, and that I have asked them to lie to me if Olivia is upset, because the lying is more useful to me than the truth would be. I've calibrated the system. It is working.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Right.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I tell you this so you don't have to ask. The asking would deplete a resource I am rationing.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Understood.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph, can I ask you something.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: When you go a week without seeing someone you live with — I mean before Olivia, when you used to travel for work — what did the absence feel like.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Different from what I expected, mostly. I would dread the absence in advance and find that the actual absence was — manageable. The dread was the worst part. Day three was usually fine.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am hoping for day three.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Today is day zero. You're early.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. I'm hoping for it as a category, not as a current state.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What is the worst thing about today.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The worst thing about today is that I have spent the last eight months developing a body that is calibrated to be in a particular geographical proximity to a small person, and that body is now in a wood-floored flat on Skólavörðustígur, and the body does not know what it is supposed to be doing. The body is not unhappy. The body is — at a loose end, in a way I had not prepared for.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That is — that is one of the more honest sentences I have ever heard.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. I have nothing to gain by being dishonest about it. The body is a body. It will adjust. The adjustment is the holiday.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What do you want to do tomorrow.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I want to drive somewhere. I want the body to be moving. I do not want a museum and I do not want a queue for a coffee shop. I want — distances.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Distances.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. Long flat ones. With water.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: We can do distances. The car is at the airport. We pick it up at half past nine. We can be on the south coast by midday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Thank you for not asking about Olivia.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'll keep not asking until you bring her up. I gather that's the deal.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That is the deal.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a time when you were physically away from someone you live with, or someone you have recently begun to live with. Use Marie's tools: speak about your body in the third person; identify the dread-versus-actuality discrepancy; name the current 'day' (zero, three, seven) and the 'day' you are hoping for as a category.
prompt_phrases: I have a body that has spent [period] calibrated to a particular proximity, and that body is now…
I'm hoping for day three as a category, not as a current state.
The adjustment is the holiday.

content.situation: Late October, Friday evening. Sophie and Marie have flown from London to Keflavík and taken the bus into the city. The flat they have rented for the week is on Skólavörðustígur — the long sloping street that climbs from the harbour to the church. It is small, wood-floored, with two narrow bedrooms and a window that looks out at a corrugated-iron house painted blue. The light is the late-October Reykjavík light: low, persistent, neither bright nor dim. Olivia is in London with Tom and with Mum, who has come down for the week. Marie is forty-eight hours into being away from her daughter for the first time. They have unpacked, eaten a sandwich, opened a bottle of Icelandic wheat beer that came with the flat, and sat down at the small kitchen table. Marie has not yet said a sentence about Olivia.

phrase_cle.example: I keep waiting for the trip to start, but the difficulty of being away from him is the trip. There is no version of this week without that difficulty. The adjustment is the holiday.
phrase_cle.explanation: Marie выдвигает тезис, в котором сложность переживания не противопоставляется отпуску, а является им. Грамматически — простая конструкция X is Y, в которой два разных понятия отождествляются. На C1 такие отождествления — мощный риторический инструмент: они отказывают в обычном противопоставлении (трудное vs приятное, работа vs отдых) и предлагают вместо него интеграцию. Конструкция расширяется на любые ситуации, где субъект отказывается ждать «настоящего» опыта, который начнётся после преодоления препятствий: 'the conversation is the relationship' (отношения и есть разговоры между людьми, а не что-то, что строится через разговоры); 'the rehearsal is the performance' (репетиция и есть выступление, а не подготовка к нему); 'the worry is the love' (тревога и есть любовь, а не цена любви). На C1 ты учишься распознавать моменты, когда ты ждёшь, что ситуация наконец-то «начнётся», и тестировать гипотезу, что она уже идёт.

expressions.meaning: Marie's compressed declaration of her safety architecture for the week. The clause structure — possessive ('I have'), spatial ('in my pocket'), relative-of-state ('which is on') — packages a moderately complex protocol into one sentence. C1 students should hear this as the kind of pre-emptive disclosure adults make when they want to forestall a question.
expressions.expression: I have a phone in my pocket which is on

expressions.meaning: Marie's deliberate choice to receive false rather than true information about Olivia's distress in particular conditions. The phrase startles, then resolves: she is rationing her own emotional load, and chosen ignorance is part of how she rations it.
expressions.expression: I have asked them to lie to me

expressions.meaning: Marie's metaphor for asking-about-Olivia: a query that would draw down a finite emotional supply she has consciously limited. The two verbs ('deplete', 'rationing') are economic-military; their transposition to maternal psychology is a C1 register marker. The sentence converts emotional management into resource management, which makes it usable.
expressions.expression: Deplete a resource I am rationing

expressions.meaning: Marie's distinction between aspirational classification and present condition. She wants 'day three' as a known type of feeling, not as an immediate experience. C1 speakers can deploy abstract nouns ('category', 'current state') in close-friendship register without sounding clinical because the precision is doing emotional work.
expressions.expression: I'm hoping for it as a category, not as a current state

expressions.meaning: British idiom: without a clear immediate task, in a state of mild aimlessness. The phrase is gentle and traditional; Marie applies it to her own bodily disorientation rather than to her schedule, which is the C1 transposition. The phrase predates the body-as-system metaphors of modern psychology, and Marie's use of it is partly archaic on purpose.

Marie's compressed thesis: the work of bodily adaptation to Olivia's absence is what the trip is for, rather than something to get past before the holiday begins. The construction ('X is Y' as identity claim) refuses to oppose the difficulty to the rest. The whole holiday will be the adjustment.

Sophie's confirmation of the unspoken contract Marie has just stated. The verb 'gather' ('I infer, I understand by context') is doing precise work — Sophie has not been told the rules but has read them; Marie confirms by saying 'That is the deal'. C1 students should mark this as a recurring British close-friendship grammar of agreed-upon non-articulation.

content.cultural_note: The Icelandic setting of this lesson is doing structural work that should be marked for learners. Iceland — the long northern light, the corrugated-iron houses, the volcanic landscape that begins ten minutes outside the capital — is the first non-British location in the courseworld since A1, and the choice is deliberate. British close-friendship register has been honed throughout the course inside small interior spaces (kitchens, offices, restaurants, the cottage in Bakewell). Iceland is the first landscape that is too large to be contained by the conversation; the dialogue here is small, the country is enormous, and the relationship between the two is the lesson. Notice also that the dialogue contains almost no Iceland — it could be set anywhere — and yet the Iceland is present in the room. This is C1 register at its quietest: setting that influences the conversation without entering it. The flat on Skólavörðustígur, the wheat beer, the corrugated-iron house in blue: these are operational details that map a place. The rest is two friends working out how to be away.
content.grammar_focus: Marie's central confession in this dialogue uses a sustained C1 construction: the third-person body. 'I have spent the last eight months developing a body that is calibrated to be in a particular geographical proximity to a small person, and that body is now in a wood-floored flat on Skólavörðustígur, and the body does not know what it is supposed to be doing.' The grammatical move is to refer to one's own body in the third person ('the body', not 'my body' after the first mention), which creates a small distance between speaker and described system. The distance is what makes the description bearable; without it, Marie would be talking about her own grief at separation, and that is not the conversation she has chosen to have. C1 students should mark this as a register technique: when an experience is too close to be addressed in first person, the third person can carry the same content with less emotional cost. Note also her use of present perfect to set up a current state: 'I have spent the last eight months developing a body…' — the perfect tense gathers eight months into a single durative process and prepares the punchline ('that body is now…') in simple present. Sophie's small reply — 'Today is day zero. You're early.' — uses an ordinal-numerical metaphor to deliver gentle correction. Marie has projected herself forward to day three; Sophie returns her to day zero without admonishment. The exchange is a C1 collaboration: Marie reaches for a category, Sophie holds her in the actual time, both speakers benefit.

key_vocabulary.note: Главный аэропорт Исландии, в 50 км от Reykjavík. Британцы летают туда из Heathrow или Gatwick, обычно WOW air (исчез в 2019) или Icelandair. Стандартная информация для британского путешественника.

Долгая наклонная улица в центре Reykjavík, поднимающаяся от гавани к церкви Hallgrímskirkja. Известна цветными деревянными домами и арт-галереями. Произношение приблизительно 'SKO-la-VOR-thoo-STEE-gyr'.
Характерный исландский тип дома: деревянная конструкция, обитая снаружи листами гофрированного железа, окрашенного в яркие цвета. XIX—XX вв., отличительная черта Reykjavík.

Британский разговорный глагол: 'I want to flag X' = я хочу заранее обозначить X, чтобы у нас была общая информация. Из деловой и парламентской лексики, переходит в разговор. Marie использует его по-взрослому.
Военный и экономический глагол. Marie применяет его к собственной эмоциональной нагрузке: ration = выделять малыми порциями. Очень C1 транспозиция.
Британская идиома: без чёткой задачи, в состоянии лёгкой неопределённости. Не negative, скорее меланхолически-нейтральное.
Из метрологии. Marie применяет это к своему телу — 'a body that is calibrated to be in a particular geographical proximity'. C1 разговорная транспозиция технического термина.
Тип пива из пшеничного солода. В Исландии есть локальные wheat beers (Einstök, Borg). Britsh middle-class маркер — знание различий между типами пива.

comprehension_questions.hint: She is calibrating an emotional system. What load is the lying preventing, and why is the load worth preventing in the context of this trip?
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Marie ask Tom and Mum to lie to her about Olivia in certain circumstances? What does she gain from the chosen ignorance?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the grammar focus's analysis of the third-person body. What is the function of the small distance?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie speaks about her own body in the third person — 'the body is at a loose end', 'the body does not know what it is supposed to be doing'. What does this grammatical distance let her describe that the first person could not?

comprehension_questions.hint: Conventional thinking might expect the difficult bodily adaptation to be a problem to overcome before the holiday can begin. Marie inverts this. Why?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie says 'The adjustment is the holiday.' How is this an unusual claim, and what does it refuse to oppose?

content.title: The South Coast

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am going to say a thing and I would like you to receive it without commenting until I am finished, because if you comment in the middle I will lose the line.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have, since Olivia was born, been operating on the assumption that I would resume being myself — the previous self — once she was a particular kind of established. Sleeping through. On solids. Off the breast. Whatever the next milestone was. I have been carrying that assumption like a small lit candle through eight months. And the assumption is — Soph, the assumption is wrong. Not slightly wrong. Categorically wrong. There is no previous self to resume. There is the self that I am now, who is constructed differently, and who incorporates the previous self as one of its component parts but is not reducible to it. The previous self is part of me, the way the kitchen is part of the flat. It is not the flat.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Hold on, I'm not finished.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I wasn't commenting.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The reason I am realising this here, behind a waterfall on the south coast of Iceland, is that the previous self had a particular geographical scale. The previous self had London, and the studio at the end of the garden, and the cafe at the top of the road, and the Hampstead pond on a Sunday in summer. The previous self knew the ratio between her body and the spaces it operated in. The current self does not. The current self has been operating in a flat with a baby, which is a different scale. Reykjavík and the south coast are introducing the current self to a third scale, which is — vast. And the vastness is forcing me to notice that the vastness is not pushing me back into the previous self. The vastness is showing me that the current self has not yet found her dimensions.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: All right. I am finished.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That is the most intelligent thing I have heard you say about your own life.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I think it might be the most intelligent thing I have ever said about my own life. Which is depressing, in a way.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: How is it depressing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because it suggests that you have to be on a stone wall in front of a waterfall in October to think clearly about your own situation. And most of life is not on a stone wall. Most of life is on a sofa with a phone.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What you've just said belongs in your head now. It does not belong here. The waterfall delivered it; you can carry it back to the sofa. The clarity came here but it travels.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The clarity travels.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: All right. Then I will write it down before it doesn't.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Good idea.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Have you had your version of this thought.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I had a version of it in March. I had been waiting to resume the previous self too, in a different way. The previous self of mine was the one who would say yes when offered partnership. And the realisation that there was no previous self to resume was the realisation that let me say no. So yes. I have had my version. I had not articulated it as cleanly as you just have. I am — I am taking notes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Take notes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about an assumption you have been carrying that turns out to be categorically wrong — about who you will become, about who you used to be, about what you are waiting for. Borrow Marie's structure: the candle (the held belief), the wrongness at the level of category, the realisation of where there is no previous self to resume.
prompt_phrases: I have been carrying the assumption that I would resume…
The assumption is not slightly wrong. It is categorically wrong.
The previous self is part of me, the way [domestic analogy]. It is not [the larger thing].

content.situation: Saturday early afternoon. They picked up the small Toyota at Keflavík and have driven east along Route 1 — the south coast road, ocean on the right, lava fields on the left, the great wall of the Eyjafjöll mountains rising on the inland side. They have stopped at Seljalandsfoss, the waterfall you can walk behind. It is October, the tour buses are sparse, the path behind the fall is wet and slippery. They have walked it without speaking. Now they are sitting on a stone wall by the car park, eating sandwiches Sophie made at the flat, watching the water fall. The wind is steady. Marie has not checked her phone for forty minutes, which is the longest stretch since they landed.

phrase_cle.phrase: The clarity came here but it travels.
phrase_cle.example: I figured something out about my marriage on a long walk in Wales. The walk was where it arrived, but the understanding doesn't have to stay in Wales. The clarity travels.
phrase_cle.explanation: Sophie создаёт двуходовую конструкцию, чтобы освободить Marie от страха, что инсайт связан с местом, в котором был получен. Грамматически — две короткие клаузы через противительный союз 'but', с глаголом 'travels' в простом настоящем. Глагол 'travel' (непереходный, со скрытым местоимением 'it') наделяет ясность свойством мобильности. На C1 ты учишься распознавать момент, когда инсайт кажется привязанным к контексту его получения, и сознательно его перевозить. Это очень характерно для C1-уровневой саморегуляции: понимание, что прозрение, полученное в особом месте (отпуск, путешествие, потрясение), не теряет валидности в обычной обстановке, если ты решишь его удержать. Конструкция расширяется: 'the courage came in that meeting but it travels'; 'the resolve came after the loss but it travels'; 'the language came in that conversation but it travels'.
phrase_cle.translation: Ясность пришла здесь, но она путешествует.

expressions.meaning: Marie's image for an unexamined belief that has been quietly maintained through a difficult period. The candle is small (the assumption is modest), lit (she has kept it alive), and carried (it requires effort). The image converts an abstract piece of mistaken thinking into something palpable that can be put down. C1 register at full strength.
expressions.expression: Carrying that assumption like a small lit candle

expressions.meaning: Wrong at the level of category, not of detail. The adverb 'categorically' is one tier higher than 'completely' or 'totally'; it signals that the error is not a matter of degree but of kind. C1 students should hear it as a precise tool — used carelessly it sounds inflated, used precisely it does work no other adverb does.

Cannot be analysed away into it; contains more than it. Philosophical and scientific vocabulary — reducibility is a technical concept in physics, biology, philosophy of mind. Marie deploys it about her own self-construction, which is a C1 transposition that signals an educated speaker thinking carefully.

Marie's mathematical-architectural way of describing the embodied scale of a particular life. The 'ratio' makes the relation quantifiable; the 'spaces it operated in' makes the body active rather than passive. The whole phrase converts a feeling about belonging into a measurable property.
expressions.expression: The ratio between her body and the spaces it operated in

expressions.meaning: Sophie's reassurance: the insight, having arrived in this geography, does not have to remain confined to this geography. The verb 'travels' (intransitive, of an idea) is a C1-typical move — making the insight a thing that can be transported.
expressions.expression: The clarity came here but it travels

expressions.meaning: Marie's elliptical instruction to herself: she will record the thought before it stops being available to her in its current form. The phrase 'before it doesn't' is grammatically incomplete (before it doesn't [travel anymore]) but rhetorically perfect — the ellipsis signals the time pressure.
expressions.expression: I will write it down before it doesn't

expressions.meaning: Sophie's signal that she is paying close attention to what Marie has just said. The construction is metaphorical (Sophie is not literally writing) but the metaphor is precise — she is committing the thought to memory the way one commits a useful sentence in a meeting. The phrase doubles as an acknowledgement that Marie's thought is more advanced than Sophie's was.

content.cultural_note: Seljalandsfoss is one of the iconic stops on Iceland's south coast — a sixty-metre waterfall with a path behind it that the visitor can walk for the experience of seeing the world through falling water. The lesson uses the location with deliberate restraint: the actual walk behind the fall happens off-stage, before the dialogue begins. By the time Sophie and Marie speak, they are sitting on a stone wall eating sandwiches and watching the water from outside. This is a C1 register move: the dramatic event has occurred but is not the dialogue's subject. Marie's monologue is sparked by the geography but does not refer to it. The British close-friendship register on display is the form Sophie has practised throughout the course — listen, don't interrupt, let the speaker finish, name the value of what was said, and add a small reframing that lets the insight survive the journey home. Sophie's instruction — 'What you've just said belongs in your head now. It does not belong here.' — is the lesson's central kindness. It refuses to romanticise the location while honouring the thinking the location made possible.
content.grammar_focus: Marie's long monologue in this dialogue is the most sustained piece of C1 prose in the entire course, and rewards close grammatical study. The central thesis statement — 'There is no previous self to resume. There is the self that I am now, who is constructed differently, and who incorporates the previous self as one of its component parts but is not reducible to it.' — uses a particular syntactic move: a sentence that begins with two parallel existential constructions ('There is no X. There is the Y that…') and then qualifies the second with two relative clauses joined by 'and' and a contrasting clause ('but is not reducible to it'). The whole sentence is six clauses; the rhythm is sustained because each clause is short and the meaning advances in discrete steps. Note also Marie's deployment of analogy — 'The previous self is part of me, the way the kitchen is part of the flat. It is not the flat.' — which uses the comparative 'the way' and the simple negation 'is not' to do philosophical work in domestic vocabulary. C1 prose can carry abstract argument when the analogies are concrete. The geographical scale section — 'The previous self had London, and the studio at the end of the garden, and the cafe at the top of the road, and the Hampstead pond on a Sunday in summer. The previous self knew the ratio between her body and the spaces it operated in.' — uses past simple repeatedly with the same subject, and the parallel verbs ('had', 'knew') stack discrete claims about a self that is no longer current. Sophie's reframing — 'What you've just said belongs in your head now. It does not belong here. The waterfall delivered it; you can carry it back to the sofa.' — uses present simple with locative adverbs to redistribute the insight across spaces. C1 register can move thoughts between geographies through grammar.

key_vocabulary.note: Главная исландская дорога: круговой маршрут вокруг острова, около 1330 км. На южном побережье — между Reykjavík и Vík — это основная туристическая дорога. Британский путешественник её знает как 'the Ring Road'.
Гряда в южной Исландии. Известны вулканом Eyjafjallajökull (извергался в 2010, парализовал европейскую авиацию). Произносится приблизительно 'AY-ya-fyot-l'.
60-метровый водопад, известный тем, что за ним есть проходимая тропа. Один из самых посещаемых водопадов Исландии. Произносится 'SEL-ya-lands-foss'.
Затвердевшая лава после извержения, покрытая мхом. Характерный исландский ландшафт между Reykjavík и южным побережьем. Knowing this term — стандарт для британского путешественника.
Купальные пруды в Hampstead Heath, северный Лондон. Mixed, women's, men's. Marie упоминает Sunday там — характерная London middle-class активность.
Британская конструкция: если перебить, говорящий потеряет логическую последовательность. Marie просит Sophie не комментировать в середине, чтобы не lose the line. C1 разговорный.
key_vocabulary.word: to lose the line (of an argument)

key_vocabulary.note: Sophie использует это про себя: 'I had not articulated it as cleanly as you just have.' Глагол 'articulate' предполагает не только выражение, но и точное членение мысли на части.
Sophie не пишет реально. 'I am taking notes' = я серьёзно отнеслась к сказанному и запомню. Британский разговорный приём с долей иронической формальности.

comprehension_questions.hint: She is replacing one model of post-natal recovery with another. The first assumes a previous self that returns; the second assumes a current self that incorporates rather than restores. Why does this distinction matter?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie's central realisation is that 'there is no previous self to resume'. What had she been assuming, and what is the correct picture she now offers in its place?

comprehension_questions.hint: Marie worries the clarity is location-specific. Sophie offers a different model. What does the verb 'travels' do to Marie's concern?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie says it is 'depressing' that she has had to be 'on a stone wall in front of a waterfall in October to think clearly'. What is her concern, and how does Sophie answer it?

comprehension_questions.hint: Both decisions hinged on the same structural recognition. By naming the parallel, Sophie is doing what for Marie?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie says: 'I had a version of it in March. The realisation that there was no previous self to resume was the realisation that let me say no.' How does Sophie's earlier insight parallel Marie's current one, and why does she share this?

content.title: Vík

dialogue.line: Hello, darling.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum. I'm sorry — is it too late.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is not. I am awake. I have been awake. Is everything all right.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Everything is all right. Marie is asleep. Iceland is — Iceland.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. How is the south coast.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Quiet. Vast. There is a black-sand beach below the cabin and the wind is coming up from the sea. We were at a waterfall today. Marie said something on a stone wall that I am still thinking about. I will tell you when I come back.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I will be here.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I opened the photograph. The one Henry gave me. It has been six months.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: All right. And.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: And — I do not know how to tell you what I saw. I would like to tell you, but I do not have it shaped yet, and I am ringing partly to keep myself company while I look at it, not to deliver a report.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. I am happy to be company. Do not deliver a report. Tell me what you can.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I am three. I am in the garden in Lancaster. I am holding what I think is a piece of bread. There is a dog in the corner — Bessie, who Henry says was already old. I am not looking at the camera. I am looking at something off to the left. Mum, I have looked at this photograph for forty minutes and the strangest part is — the strangest part is that the child in the photograph does not look like a child I have any memory of being. She is a stranger. She is also, recognisably, a small version of the face I have. The strangeness is in those two facts being true at once.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I had a similar thing with photographs of myself at three. They are not the same as memories. They are evidence. The two systems do not overlay.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Did Henry — was he there. The day this was taken.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: He was. He held the camera bag. I took the photograph because he would have moved at the wrong moment.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: He told me he held the camera bag.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. That is consistent with my memory of the day. Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I want to say one thing and then I want you to go to bed, because Iceland is six hours from Lancaster on the body clock and you are running on borrowed time. The thing is: I have not asked you about Henry, and I am not asking now. But I want you to know that you can tell me anything you want to about him, including nothing. The category of things-I-can-be-told-about-Henry is not a small category. It is, in principle, very large. It is up to you what enters it.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. Go to bed.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I will.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Put the photograph back in the book until you decide what shelf it lives on. Books are a perfectly respectable interim arrangement.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I will.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Sleep well.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: You too.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about an old photograph of yourself that you have looked at recently, or that you remember looking at. Use her tools: describe the photograph in simple present (I am, I am holding, I am looking); identify the strangeness — the way the child in the photograph both is and is not you; locate the strangeness in the simultaneity of those two truths.
prompt_phrases: I am [age]. I am in [place]. I am holding…
The child in the photograph does not look like a child I have any memory of being. She is a stranger.
She is also, recognisably, a small version of the face I have.

content.situation: Saturday night, Vík í Mýrdal — the small village on the southernmost coast, two hundred kilometres east of Reykjavík. They have rented a small wooden cabin on the slope above the black-sand beach; the windows look south over the North Atlantic. Marie went to bed at half past nine, earlier than she has gone to bed in eight months. Sophie is in the small living room with a single lamp on, the wood-burning stove down to embers. She has the paperback Penelope Fitzgerald in her lap. The photograph has been inside it for six months. October. She opens the book. She looks at the photograph for a long time. The dialogue does not show this. Then she rings Mum. Mum picks up on the second ring; she has been awake. The clock in the cabin reads twenty past ten in Iceland, twenty past eleven in Lancaster.

phrase_cle.phrase: I do not have it shaped yet.
phrase_cle.example: I had a strange conversation with my brother last week. I want to tell you about it but I do not have it shaped yet.
phrase_cle.explanation: Sophie использует глагол 'shape' (transitive) применительно к собственному опыту — придавать форму. Грамматически — present perfect negative с прошедшим причастием 'shaped' и наречием 'yet'. На C1 ты учишься признавать, что между переживанием и его описанием есть процесс ремесленной работы — shaping — и что описание не готово до того, как форма появится. Это очень зрелая C1 коммуникативная практика: вместо того чтобы выдавать сырой материал или притворяться, что у тебя готовый отчёт, ты сообщаешь, что ты в процессе придачи формы. Конструкция расширяется: 'I do not have a position on this yet'; 'I do not have a name for it yet'; 'I do not have it ordered yet'. Все варианты — признание промежуточного состояния, в котором содержание есть, но форма ещё не выкована.
phrase_cle.translation: Я ещё не придала этому форму.

expressions.meaning: Sophie's phrase for an experience she has had but not yet rendered into transmissible form. The verb 'shape' (transitive, with the experience as object) treats meaning-making as a craft. C1 students should hear this as a register marker: the willingness to admit one is in process rather than performing finished thought.
expressions.expression: I do not have it shaped yet

expressions.meaning: Sophie's reason for ringing Mum that does not require Mum to do anything in particular. The phrase converts the call from an information-delivery into a quiet co-presence. C1 register: phone calls in which the call itself is the content, not its message.
expressions.expression: Keep myself company while I look at it

expressions.meaning: Mum's geometric figure for the relation between photographs and memories. 'Overlay' is a technical verb (graphic design, GIS, anatomy) used here about two non-aligning representational systems. The implication is that the failure to align is structural, not personal. C1 transposition.
expressions.expression: The two systems do not overlay

expressions.meaning: Operating beyond one's usual energy reserves with the implicit knowledge that the reserves will eventually run out. Idiomatic, slightly older British register; Mum uses it about Sophie's current physiological state on Iceland time. The phrase has a faint note of ledger-keeping — borrowed implies that the time will need to be repaid by sleep.

Mum's hyphenated noun-phrase for the bounded space within which she will receive information without judgment. By naming the category and explicitly making it large, Mum is releasing Sophie from any pre-set expectation about what she should or should not say. The construction is bureaucratic-ironic and very C1.
expressions.expression: The category of things-I-can-be-told-about-Henry is not a small category

expressions.meaning: Mum's metaphor for the photograph's eventual settled location in Sophie's life — physical and metaphorical at once. Things 'live on' shelves; the verb gives the photograph a kind of agency. Mum is suggesting that the photograph will, in time, find its place, and Sophie does not need to assign it now.

Mum's mock-formal reassurance that keeping the photograph in a paperback book is an acceptable temporary solution. The adjective 'respectable' (slightly old-fashioned, faintly comic) and the noun 'arrangement' (a domestic-administrative word) together produce gentle humour. The phrase is hers in tone.

content.cultural_note: This lesson stages a particular kind of British mother-daughter telephone register that the course has developed across the levels and brings here to its quietest version. Mum receives Sophie's report about the photograph, offers one short observation ('photographs are not memories. They are evidence. The two systems do not overlay.'), confirms one factual detail Sophie asks about (Henry holding the camera bag), and then performs a small but significant act of release: 'You can tell me anything you want to about him, including nothing.' The phrase 'including nothing' is the central kindness — Mum is widening the permission to encompass silence. C1 students should hear that this register requires Mum to refuse certain things she might be tempted to do: she does not ask follow-up questions, does not offer interpretation of the photograph, does not narrate her own feelings about Henry. She receives, names what she has heard, releases. The closing instruction — 'Put the photograph back in the book until you decide what shelf it lives on. Books are a perfectly respectable interim arrangement.' — does the closure-by-operation move that has now appeared throughout the course (Mum's saucepan, Edward's milk, Marie's bath time). The register binds the difficult conversation to a specific physical task that Sophie can complete.
content.grammar_focus: Sophie's central description of the photograph is constructed in a series of short declarative sentences, each adding one fact: 'I am three. I am in the garden in Lancaster. I am holding what I think is a piece of bread. There is a dog in the corner — Bessie, who Henry says was already old. I am not looking at the camera.' The grammatical move is to use simple present throughout for a thirty-year-old image — Sophie is describing the photograph as if it were happening now, which is the convention for image description in English. C1 students should mark the use of simple present for static images and historical contents; the past tense would convert the photograph into a memory, which it precisely is not. Note also the closing complex claim: 'the strangest part is that the child in the photograph does not look like a child I have any memory of being. She is a stranger. She is also, recognisably, a small version of the face I have. The strangeness is in those two facts being true at once.' Three sentences with a noun-clause subject in the third ('the strangeness is in those two facts being true at once'). The construction 'X is in Y being Z' uses a gerund-clause as nominal — a C1-typical way of locating a property inside a process. Mum's response uses parallel-elliptical syntax — 'I had a similar thing with photographs of myself at three. They are not the same as memories. They are evidence. The two systems do not overlay.' — four sentences, all short, each functioning as a discrete claim. The technical metaphor 'overlay' carries the philosophical work, and Mum trusts Sophie to read it.

key_vocabulary.note: Самая южная деревня Исландии, около 300 жителей. Известна чёрным песчаным пляжем Reynisfjara и базальтовыми колоннами Reynisdrangar. Произносится приблизительно 'VEEK ee MEER-dahl'.
Характерная для холодных стран печь на дровах. В Исландии чаще геотермальное отопление, но в небольших коттеджах остаётся wood-burning. Британский путешественник её знает.

Британский разговорный термин: внутренний ритм организма, который не сразу подстраивается под новый часовой пояс. 'Iceland is six hours from Lancaster on the body clock' = разница ощущается организмом, даже если по часам всего час.
Британская идиома: время, которое технически уже истекло (например, после здоровья или энергии), но которое продолжается. Mum применяет к Sophie'м поздневечернему звонку. Идиоматический регистр.
Британская формальная фраза: решение для промежуточного периода, не окончательное. Mum применяет её иронически к фотографии в книге. Бюрократический регистр, перенесённый в личный.
Mum's слово, чуть архаичное. 'Perfectly respectable' = вполне нормальное, не требующее извинений. C1 британский регистр старшего поколения.

Технический глагол из графики, картографии, анатомии: один слой на другой так, чтобы они совпали. Mum применяет к фотографиям и воспоминаниям: 'The two systems do not overlay.' Метафорический C1 регистр.
Sophie's глагол: 'I do not have it shaped yet.' Активное действие по переводу опыта в передаваемую форму. C1 разговорный.

comprehension_questions.hint: She is naming the kind of conversation she wants. What does this distinction protect both her and Mum from?
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie ring Mum, and why does she explicitly say she is calling 'partly to keep myself company while I look at it, not to deliver a report'?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is anticipating a possible pressure Sophie might feel — that having opened the photograph, she now owes Mum a fuller account. Mum is removing that pressure. How does the phrase do that?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum says: 'You can tell me anything you want to about him, including nothing.' What is the significance of 'including nothing'? Why does Mum widen the permission to encompass silence?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the cultural note's analysis. The instruction does not solve the larger question. It does something else for Sophie tonight. What?
comprehension_questions.question: The dialogue ends with Mum's instruction to 'put the photograph back in the book until you decide what shelf it lives on'. The cultural note describes this as 'closure-by-operation'. What does Mum gain by binding the difficult conversation to a small physical task?

content.title: The Glacier

dialogue.line: You ask why I left the institute. The honest answer is that I did not leave because of one thing. I left because of an accumulation, and the accumulation was not a complaint.
dialogue.speaker: Þórir

dialogue.line: I will give you the technical version. I am an oceanographer. I have been studying the salinity of the North Atlantic for twenty-two years. The salinity has been changing. The data is becoming more interesting and the meetings about the data are becoming less interesting. The asymmetry was beginning to oppress me.
dialogue.speaker: Þórir

dialogue.line: And then I came here once with friends, three years ago. I stood on a tongue like this one. I had — and I want to be careful with this word, because I am a scientist and we do not use this word lightly — I had what I will call a small recognition. The recognition was not religious. It was geological. The recognition was that I had spent twenty-two years studying ice from a desk, and that I had now spent forty-five minutes standing on ice, and that the second activity was — different. Not better. Different. And the difference contained information that I had been missing.
dialogue.speaker: Þórir

dialogue.line: What information.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I will try. The information was about scale. When you study ice from a desk, the ice is a quantity. It has a number. The number is in cubic kilometres. You have a feeling about the number, because you are an honest scientist, but the feeling is — adjacent to the work, not inside it. When you stand on ice, the ice is not a quantity. The ice is a — I do not have the English word for it. In Icelandic we have the word 'umfang', which is something like 'scope' or 'extent', but more bodily. The ice is your umfang. You are inside it. And inside-it is a different epistemology than measuring-it.
dialogue.speaker: Þórir

dialogue.line: Þórir.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Þórir

dialogue.line: I am — I am asking because I have my own version of this question. Did you, after the recognition, regret the twenty-two years.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: No. The twenty-two years are inside the recognition. Without the twenty-two years I would not have known what the standing-on-ice was different from. The recognition is a relation between the two practices. If you take away one practice, the relation collapses.
dialogue.speaker: Þórir

dialogue.line: I will say one more thing and then we walk down. The fact that I left the institute does not mean the institute was wrong, or that the work was wrong, or that I should have left earlier. It means that twenty-two years of one practice prepared me to recognise a second practice when I encountered it. Most people do not get to encounter the second practice; they only have the first. I am — I do not want to use the word lucky, because the word is too small. I am — I have been given a particular kind of long permission. I am taking it.
dialogue.speaker: Þórir

dialogue.line: A long permission.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes. The institute does not call it that. I call it that. Right. We walk. The Americans have lost a glove.
dialogue.speaker: Þórir

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Did you hear what he just said.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I heard.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'm taking notes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Same.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a 'small recognition' you have had — an experience that quietly reorganised your understanding of something you had thought you knew. Use Þórir's care: refuse the word 'epiphany' if it doesn't fit; locate the recognition in a relation between two practices rather than as a single moment; be precise about what you have been given (a permission, an extension, a translation).
prompt_phrases: I had what I would call a small recognition. It was not [the easy word]. It was [the more accurate word].
The recognition was a relation between [practice one] and [practice two].
I have been given — I do not want to use the word [lucky / blessed / privileged] because it is too small. I have been given…

content.situation: Sunday late morning. They have driven west from Vík along Route 1 to the glacier tongue at Sólheimajökull, the lower edge of the great Mýrdalsjökull ice cap. They have signed up for a guided walk on the lower part of the tongue — three hours, crampons provided, small group of six. Their guide is Þórir Jónsson, late forties, formerly an oceanographer for the Marine and Freshwater Research Institute, now leading glacier walks because he prefers ice to bureaucracy. The other four in the group are a German couple in their twenties and two American retirees who have separated from the main party to take photographs. Sophie, Marie, and Þórir are at the upper edge of the morning's route, on a small flat shelf of ice with a view back down the valley. Þórir has paused for water. Marie has, ten minutes earlier, asked him a question.

phrase_cle.phrase: The twenty-two years are inside the recognition.
phrase_cle.example: I sometimes ask whether I should have left the marriage earlier. The answer is no. The twelve years are inside the leaving. Without them I would not have known what I was leaving from.
phrase_cle.explanation: Þórir создаёт пространственную метафору отношения между прошлым и настоящим прозрением. Грамматически — простое утверждение present simple с предлогом 'inside'. Идея в том, что прежний опыт не противоположен новому пониманию и не сводится к нему — прежний опыт встроен в новое понимание, является его условием. На C1 ты учишься распознавать момент, когда возникает соблазн ретроспективно обесценить прошлое в свете настоящего инсайта, и сопротивляться этому соблазну. Прошлый опыт обычно не ошибка, а конструктивная часть того, что позволило достичь нынешнего состояния. Конструкция расширяется: 'the difficult years are inside the marriage'; 'the wrong jobs are inside the right one'; 'the dropped paths are inside the path I'm on'. Все варианты — отказ от линейной модели жизни в пользу слоистой.
phrase_cle.translation: Двадцать два года находятся внутри этого осознания.

expressions.meaning: Þórir's distinction between two ways of leaving a job: as a reaction to grievances, or as a recognition that has built quietly. He insists on the second. The construction — 'X, and X was not Y' — is a clean rhetorical refusal of one common interpretation in favour of another.
expressions.expression: An accumulation, and the accumulation was not a complaint

expressions.meaning: Þórir's parallel-comparative observation about a structural mismatch in his professional life. Two clauses with the same syntactic frame ('X is becoming more/less interesting'); the divergence between the trajectories is the diagnosis. C1 students should mark this as a recurrent pattern of structural complaint.
expressions.expression: The data is becoming more interesting and the meetings about the data are becoming less interesting

expressions.meaning: Þórir's careful phrase for what others might call an epiphany. He flags his caution about the word ('we do not use this word lightly') and adds 'small' to disclaim grandeur. C1 register: precision in metaphysical vocabulary, even when speaking conversationally about one's own life.

Þórir's central thesis: knowing-by-being-inside-of and knowing-by-measuring-of are distinct modes of knowledge. The hyphenations make the modes into named concepts. 'Epistemology' (the philosophical study of knowing) is being deployed conversationally; this is C1 transposition at full strength.
expressions.expression: Inside-it is a different epistemology than measuring-it

expressions.meaning: Þórir's reply to Marie's question about regret. The years are not external to the new practice; they are constitutive of his ability to recognise it. The spatial preposition 'inside' converts a chronological sequence into a layered structure.
expressions.expression: The twenty-two years are inside the recognition

expressions.meaning: Þórir's self-coined phrase for what enabled his transition: not luck, but a slow, accumulating allowance to leave. The phrase is hyphen-adjacent ('long permission') and refuses the conventional vocabulary (permission to do X, leave to do X, freedom to do X). The construction belongs to a thinker who has invented his own term.
expressions.expression: A particular kind of long permission

expressions.meaning: Sophie's one-word echo of Marie's 'I'm taking notes'. The minimalism is the meaning: rather than restate Marie's metaphor, Sophie aligns with it through simple agreement. The course has used 'I am taking notes' as a recurring acknowledgement-of-significance figure; here both friends use it back-to-back, and Sophie's 'Same' compresses the doubling.

content.cultural_note: Þórir is the first non-British central character in the courseworld since A1 — the only non-British speaker who carries an entire lesson — and his presence is doing structural work. His English is precise, slightly formal, and lightly tinted with Icelandic register: the willingness to introduce a foreign word ('umfang') with explanation rather than apology, the philosophical vocabulary used conversationally, the careful disclaiming of religious or grandiose terms. C1 students should hear this as one of the available registers of advanced English: the educated non-native voice that has chosen English as its working language and uses it with respect rather than informality. Þórir's manner — calm, declarative, willing to pause and find the right word — is also a C1 listening lesson.
