He does not rush to fill silences; he does not soften his thesis; he closes the conversation by pivoting to an operational concern ('the Americans have lost a glove'). The closure-by-operation move that has appeared throughout the course in British registers is here performed by an Icelandic speaker, which suggests that the move is not specifically British — it is a feature of the kind of person Þórir is, regardless of national origin. The lesson's deepest cultural point is that this register transcends nation: precision, restraint, and a willingness to invent careful phrases (long permission, small recognition, inside-it / measuring-it) belong to a kind of speaker, not a kind of culture.
content.grammar_focus: Þórir's speech is a sustained example of non-native English at advanced register, and rewards study because it shows what C1 fluency looks like when the speaker is consciously translating from a different linguistic tradition. His sentences are slightly more formal than a native speaker would deploy in the same situation — 'The asymmetry was beginning to oppress me' — and slightly more philosophical in vocabulary ('epistemology', 'recognition', 'umfang'). C1 students should mark this as one mode of English fluency: the precise non-native register, often used by educated continental speakers, that uses the language's full philosophical vocabulary without the colloquial shortcuts. Note the central thesis sentence: 'Inside-it is a different epistemology than measuring-it.' Two hyphen-bound noun phrases (inside-it, measuring-it) function as substantives; the verb 'is' carries the comparison; 'epistemology' is the predicate. The construction would be a grammatical experiment in casual English; in Þórir's register it works because he has earned the right to use 'epistemology' conversationally. Note also the layered explanation of the recognition: 'The recognition was not religious. It was geological.' Two short sentences that refuse one category and assert another. C1 prose can use negation followed by assertion as a tool of definitional work — saying first what something is not is a way of locating it without overclaiming. And the closing exchange between Marie and Sophie — 'I'm taking notes.' / 'Same.' — uses a recurring course-world figure (taking notes) and minimal agreement (Same) to perform recognition without elaboration.

key_vocabulary.note: Ледниковый язык на южной оконечности Mýrdalsjökull, доступный для walking tours. Произносится приблизительно 'SOAL-hay-ma-yoh-kuhdl'. Сокращается до 'jökull' (ледник) в местной речи.
Геологический термин: продвижение ледника вниз по долине, нижняя часть. Британский путешественник его знает по educational signage.
Шипованные накладки на обувь для движения по льду. Стандартное оборудование на guided glacier walks. Произносится 'KRAM-ponz'.
Реальная исландская научная организация (Hafrannsóknastofnun). Þórir был там oceanographer перед уходом в гиды.
key_vocabulary.word: the Marine and Freshwater Research Institute

key_vocabulary.note: Концентрация соли в воде, измеряется в practical salinity units (PSU). Изменения salinity Северной Атлантики — реальный научный сюжет, связанный с климатом. Þórir изучал это.

Слово существует и в исландском, и в немецком. Þórir использует его, потому что в английском нет точного эквивалента: между 'extent', 'scope', и 'volume', с телесной коннотацией. C1 студент учится распознавать моменты, когда родной язык говорящего вмешивается в его английский.

Раздел философии, изучающий природу знания и его границы. На C1 это рабочий разговорный термин среди образованных британцев и continental европейцев. Þórir применяет к контрасту между двумя способами знать ледник.
Self-coined phrase Þórir: разрешение на изменение жизни, накапливавшееся годами. Не общеупотребительная конструкция, но Þórir её зарабатывает контекстом.

comprehension_questions.hint: He is not saying one is better than the other. Look at his careful 'Not better. Different.' What does he mean by 'different epistemology'?
comprehension_questions.question: Þórir distinguishes between knowing ice 'from a desk' and knowing it by 'standing on it'. He calls the difference 'a different epistemology'. What is he claiming about the relationship between embodied experience and abstract measurement?

comprehension_questions.hint: He is refusing a linear model in which the new replaces the old. What model does he offer instead?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie asks whether Þórir regrets the twenty-two years at the institute. He answers: 'The twenty-two years are inside the recognition.' What does the spatial metaphor 'inside' do for the relationship between past and present?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at how 'taking notes' has appeared before. The two friends are aligning. With what, and against what kind of more elaborate response?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie says 'I'm taking notes' and Sophie says 'Same'. What does this minimal exchange perform between the two friends, and how does it relate to similar moments earlier in the course?

content.title: The Letter to Henry

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I have written something. Can I read it to you. Once. Without commentary.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: 'Henry. Thank you for the open invitation in April. I had said yes for October and I am writing because I am not, in fact, going to come in October. The reason is not one I had anticipated when I accepted. The reason is that I have been doing other thinking, in other geographies, and the autumn has filled with the consequences of that thinking. Bakewell would not, this autumn, get the version of me that the visit deserves. I would prefer to come when the visit can have a clean room of my attention rather than the corner of a busy one. I am proposing March. The first weekend you can manage. If March does not work for you I will propose another date until one does. I want to be clear that this is a postponement, not a cancellation, and I want to be even clearer that I do not require you to reply to this with reassurance about the postponement. The reassurance is, in advance, accepted; the next thing I would like from you, when you are ready for it, is a date. Sophie.'
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Send it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Anything you'd change.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I would not change anything. I would, however, name what is happening in the letter, because if you do not name it for yourself you will not realise you have done it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You have written him a letter that uses his own structure back at him. The triple option (postponement, not cancellation; reassurance pre-accepted; the next thing I want is a date) is the same triple-option architecture his letter to you used in April. You are matching his register. You are also doing it without imitating him — you are doing it in your own voice, which has been calibrated by your year. You have written him the letter of an equal.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I had not noticed.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. That is why I am noticing it for you.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. Send it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: [Sophie types the letter into a text and sends it. They wait. The cabin is quiet. The rain on the window is steady. Three minutes pass. Marie has gone back to her book. Sophie is watching the screen. The reply comes.]
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. Understood. March suits. The first weekend would be the second of March; if that's too soon, the next one I can offer is the sixteenth. Either is fine with me. I will hold both. Henry.
dialogue.speaker: Henry (text)

dialogue.line: Well.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: He held both.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: He held both.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I'll take the sixteenth.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Take the sixteenth.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: He answered in the same register he wrote in. He has been writing letters to you in his head all year, on the chance you would write back. That is what the holding-both means.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Imagine writing a letter to someone you have an existing relationship with — a parent, a former colleague, a friend you have lost touch with — to postpone or reshape an arrangement you had previously made. Use Sophie's structure: identify the action (postponement, not cancellation); pre-accept any reassurance the recipient might offer; specify the next thing you want from them.
prompt_phrases: I had said yes for [original date] and I am writing because I am not, in fact, going to…
I want to be clear that this is a postponement, not a cancellation.
The reassurance is, in advance, accepted. The next thing I would like from you, when you are ready for it, is…

content.situation: Monday morning, the cabin in Vík. They are leaving for Reykjavík in the early afternoon; tonight is their last night in Iceland. The weather has turned — light rain, low cloud, the sea grey and steady. Marie is at the kitchen table reading. Sophie has, since waking at seven, been writing a letter to Henry on the back of two pages of cabin stationery. She has drafted it three times. The fourth draft is now in front of her. She wants to read it to Marie before she sends it as a text — Henry does not use email reliably and Sophie does not have his phone for voice. The text version will have to be transcribed. The British custom of reading important things aloud before sending them, which Mum taught her at sixteen, returns here.

phrase_cle.example: I asked her if she could keep my place open while I figured things out. She said yes without conditions. She held both — the staying and the leaving.
phrase_cle.explanation: Sophie повторяет фразу Henry's текста как отдельное предложение, и Marie повторяет за ней. Глагол 'hold' здесь имеет операционное значение (резервировать, удерживать в наличии — как в 'hold a reservation'), но в этом контексте приобретает эмоциональную нагрузку. Тот, кто 'holds both', не выбирает за тебя и не давит на твоё решение — он держит обе возможности открытыми, пока ты не будешь готов. На C1 ты учишься распознавать такие операционные глаголы, нагруженные эмоциональным содержанием, и использовать их с уважением к их двойному регистру. Конструкция расширяется: 'she held the door for me' (в любом смысле — буквально и метафорически); 'he held the question' (не отвечал, но и не отказывался); 'we held the silence' (не нарушали друг другу пространство для размышления).

expressions.meaning: Sophie's metaphor for the quality of focus a visit deserves: an entire room (full attention) versus a corner of a room already full of other things. The architectural metaphor for cognitive availability is C1 register; it converts an abstract scarcity into a spatial one.
expressions.expression: A clean room of my attention rather than the corner of a busy one

expressions.meaning: Sophie's pre-emptive disclaimer to spare Henry the obligation of reassuring her about the postponement. The construction — receiving the reassurance before it has been offered — converts a future expected gesture into a present completed transaction. C1 register at its most precise.
expressions.expression: The reassurance is, in advance, accepted

expressions.meaning: Marie's diagnostic for what has happened to Sophie's voice over the twelve months from L1 to L29. The verb 'calibrated' (which the course has used multiple times — about Mum's mother in L8, about Tom's mother's account in L21, about Marie's body in L25) returns here in its most condensed application.

Marie's verdict on what Sophie has written: not the letter of a daughter, not the letter of a child returning, but the letter of an equal. The construction 'the letter of an X' (where X is a relational role) is a recurring British figure for genre identification.

Sophie's quoting and confirming of Henry's promise to keep both proposed dates available until she chose. The verb 'hold' (as in 'hold a reservation') is operational; in Sophie's reuse it acquires emotional weight. The repetition of the phrase by Marie is the register's recognition of significance.

Marie's reading of what Henry's response reveals: that he has been mentally drafting communications across the silence, not pressuring Sophie but staying ready. The phrase 'on the chance' is doing precise work — Henry was not waiting, he was prepared.
expressions.expression: Writing letters to you in his head all year, on the chance you would write back

expressions.meaning: Marie's two-step response when asked for editorial input. She refuses to suggest changes while offering a naming that the writer has not seen. The construction echoes Eleanor's L23 'I would not be offended by Henry; I would not be offended by anything else either. I would not, however' — same structure of refusal-then-distinction.
expressions.expression: I would not change anything. I would, however, name what is happening

content.cultural_note: The exchange of letters in this lesson — Sophie's drafted prose followed by Henry's telegraphic text — performs one of the deepest moves in the courseworld: register-matching across generation and medium. Sophie writes a long careful letter using paper-and-stationery prose conventions; Henry replies in the compressed register of texting, but with the same precision Sophie's letter has shown. Both are operating at the same level; the medium has changed but the register has not. Marie's diagnosis — 'He has been writing letters to you in his head all year, on the chance you would write back' — is the lesson's central reading. Henry's brevity is not laziness; it is the result of having mentally composed the full version many times, so that when the actual reply is required, it arrives in finished form. This is a recognisable feature of older British correspondents who came of age with letters: their texts and emails carry the architecture of letters compressed. C1 students should hear the lesson as a literacy in modes — the same care can travel across paper, text, voice, and embodied presence, and the speaker's task is to recognise the register and meet it.
content.grammar_focus: Sophie's letter is a sustained piece of C1 prose worth reading aloud and dissecting. The opening — 'Henry. Thank you for the open invitation in April. I had said yes for October and I am writing because I am not, in fact, going to come in October.' — uses past perfect ('I had said yes') against present continuous ('I am writing') and present simple negative ('I am not going to come') to map a small chronology of commitment, current writing, and revised intention. The 'in fact' is doing British corrective work: a phrase that signals the writer is updating the previously stated position. Note the triple-option structure: 'this is a postponement, not a cancellation' / 'I do not require you to reply to this with reassurance about the postponement' / 'the next thing I would like from you, when you are ready for it, is a date'. Three sentences performing different operations: classification, release, request. This is the same architecture Henry's April letter used, redeployed by Sophie now. Marie's analytical observation — 'You have written him a letter that uses his own structure back at him' — is itself a C1 grammatical-stylistic diagnosis: she names the form, identifies the original, and credits Sophie with the redeployment without imitation. Henry's reply text — 'Understood. March suits. The first weekend would be the second of March; if that's too soon, the next one I can offer is the sixteenth. Either is fine with me. I will hold both.' — uses six short sentences, no preamble, semicolon-stacked clauses for the conditional offer, and the future simple 'I will hold both' as final commitment. The whole reply is forty-six words; in those forty-six words Henry confirms receipt, accepts the postponement, offers two specific dates, and closes the loop. This is C1 telegraphic register, and Sophie reads it correctly.

key_vocabulary.note: Не stationary (неподвижный). Stationery = paper and writing implements. В коттедже часто кладут небольшое количество для гостей. Британская домашняя норма.
Sophie 'has drafted it three times'. Глагол подразумевает многократное переписывание. Регистр: письменный, серьёзный.
Приглашение без жёсткой даты или условий. Henry дал такое в апреле. Британская конструкция.
Henry: 'March suits.' = March подходит мне. Глагол 'suit' непереходный, краткий, очень британский.
Операционное значение: keep available, not release. Henry: 'I will hold both.' = я удержу обе даты доступными. На C1 — также метафорический регистр.
Лингвистический термин: ответ на сообщение в том же стилистическом регистре, в котором оно написано. Marie диагностирует: Sophie написала Henry в его регистре.
Краткий, без артиклей и неважных слов, как старая телеграмма. Henry's text — telegraphic. C1 описательный регистр.
Marie: 'your voice has been calibrated by your year'. Год работы и переходов как инструмент настройки голоса. C1 транспозиция.

comprehension_questions.hint: Equality here is not about asserting parity but about a register that has matured to meet the original on its own terms. What has the year done that makes equality possible?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie says of Sophie's letter: 'You have written him a letter that uses his own structure back at him… You are doing it in your own voice, which has been calibrated by your year. You have written him the letter of an equal.' What is the significance of writing 'the letter of an equal' rather than the letter of a daughter?

comprehension_questions.hint: Drafted reply requires time. A reply that arrives in finished form within three minutes implies the drafting happened before the question was asked. What does this tell Sophie about what Henry has been doing across the silence?
comprehension_questions.question: Henry's reply is forty-six words long. Marie reads it as evidence that Henry 'has been writing letters to you in his head all year, on the chance you would write back'. How does compression become evidence of long preparation?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is preventing a particular kind of social labour. What labour, and why preventing it is itself a form of respect?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie's letter pre-accepts the reassurance Henry might offer about the postponement. Why does she remove this from his to-do list?

content.title: The Last Evening

dialogue.line: I will say something now and you do not need to do anything with it. It can sit on the table like the bread.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am tired in a way I have not been tired before. Not the eight-month tired. A different kind. The tired of a person who has held a small thing through a difficult winter and is now putting the small thing down for one evening. Iceland has been the evening. It has not been a holiday; it has been an evening.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Tomorrow I will pick up the small thing again. The small thing is not a burden. The small thing is my life. But the evening exists. The evening is allowed.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I have something I have been thinking about saying since the glacier.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: When Þórir said 'a particular kind of long permission', I — Marie, I have been given that, this year. By more people than I can name in one sentence. Helena. Edward. Mum. You. Carlos. Eleanor. Henry. Even Tom, in his way. I have been given a long permission, and I have not yet — I have not yet noticed that I have been given it. I have been treating it as a sequence of separate kindnesses. I think it is one thing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is one thing.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: And I do not know how to thank everyone for one thing when they each gave it to me as a separate thing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You don't. The thanking is structural. The way you live, in the next ten years, is the thanking. The fact that you are allowed not to articulate the thanks to each person is part of what they gave you.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Same goes for you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Soph. The wine is going. Should we have another.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: We should have another.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What's next.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I do not know.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The bread is good.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: The bread is very good.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Iceland.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Iceland.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Tell Sophie about a year you have lived through that has been organised, in retrospect, by 'a long permission' you did not realise you were being given. Who gave it? What did each separate kindness contribute to the larger structure? And, in answer to Marie's question — what is next?
prompt_phrases: I was given a long permission, this year, by more people than I can name in one sentence…
I had been treating it as a sequence of separate kindnesses. I think it is one thing.
The thanking is structural. The way I live, in the next ten years, is the thanking.

content.situation: Monday evening. They have driven back from Vík to Reykjavík in light rain, returned the car to the rental office near the harbour, and walked to a small fish restaurant on the old harbour front. They have a window table. Outside, the harbour is quiet, the boats settled for the night, the sky the unrepeatable late-October Reykjavík sky — a pale dark with no stars yet, the lights from the city reflecting on the water. They have ordered fish soup and bread and a single glass of white wine each. Their flight tomorrow is at half past one in the afternoon. This is the last evening of the trip. The dialogue is the quietest in the block. Neither friend is performing closure; the trip will close itself.

phrase_cle.example: We finished the project. The team has stopped working on it. The relief has settled. And then someone asks: what's next.
phrase_cle.explanation: Двусловный вопрос, который Marie произносит как утверждение (точка, не вопросительный знак). Грамматически — самое короткое возможное вопросительное предложение в английском: contraction вопросительного слова с глаголом-связкой ('what is' → 'what's') и наречие 'next'. Используется для открытия следующего этапа без давления немедленного ответа. Sophie отвечает 'I do not know.' Marie отвечает 'Good.' Это очень C1-уровневое сочетание: незнание ответа на 'что дальше' — не неудача, а правильное состояние в данный момент. На C1 ты учишься различать ситуации, когда следующий шаг должен быть ясен, и ситуации, когда отсутствие плана — само по себе позиция. Конструкция расширяется в обоих направлениях: вопросительное ('what's next?') и риторическое ('the question is what's next, but we do not have to answer it tonight'). Знаменитая в британской культуре сцена в The West Wing использует эту фразу как повторяющийся мотив — 'what's next' в режиме принятия следующей задачи; здесь Marie использует её в противоположном регистре — 'what's next' в режиме открытого незнания, которое допустимо.

expressions.meaning: Marie's image for a thing said without expectation of response. The bread is on the table; one may eat it or not. The construction converts a sentence into an object — present, available, unburdening. C1 register at its quietest.
expressions.expression: It can sit on the table like the bread

expressions.meaning: Marie's syntactic move of using 'the tired' as a noun rather than an adjective, and qualifying it with a relative clause about the person who carries it. The construction is faintly poetic — 'the tired' as a category — and carries a precision that 'the tiredness' would not.
expressions.expression: The tired of a person who has held a small thing

expressions.meaning: Marie's reframing of the trip: not a holiday but an evening, a single bracketed period of putting the daily weight down. The metaphor is hers and marks the trip without describing it. C1 students should hear the willingness to redefine a category by replacing one noun with another that does precise work.

Þórir's phrase from L28, returning here in Sophie's mouth as the figure that has organised her year. The transmission of the phrase from a stranger on a glacier to Sophie's central self-description in twenty-four hours is the kind of register-acquisition C1 students should mark.

Marie's response when Sophie says she does not know how to thank everyone. The thanks is not a series of utterances but a property of a way of living. C1 transposition: an interpersonal verb-act ('thanking') is reclassified as an architectural feature ('structural').

Sophie's three-word echo applied to Marie's situation. The course has used 'Same' as a recurring minimal acknowledgement (L28); here Sophie extends it. The phrase claims that Marie too has received the long permission, and Marie's quiet 'Yes' confirms.

Marie's two-word question that closes the lesson without closing the course. The question is asked late in the meal, after wine, after the longer thoughts have been spoken. Sophie's 'I do not know' and Marie's 'Good' constitute a refusal of premature planning — the future is not yet shaped, and that is the right state to leave it in.

content.cultural_note: The closing dialogue of C1 deserves its own kind of attention because it is not designed to close. Marie and Sophie do not summarise the year, do not enumerate what has changed, do not rehearse the gratitude in a way that would tidy it. Instead they exchange three minimal moves: Marie names the texture of her current tiredness; Sophie names the year as a single long permission; both refuse to articulate the thanks because Marie has reframed the thanking as structural. Then they order more wine. Then Marie asks 'What's next' and Sophie says 'I do not know' and Marie says 'Good'. The whole exchange takes perhaps four minutes. The cultural lesson is that British close-friendship register at its most evolved does not perform endings. It allows the year to have happened, allows it to continue happening, and orders bread. The final two words — 'Iceland.' / 'Iceland.' — are not a summary of the trip; they are an acknowledgement that the trip has had a name, and that the name has been earned. C1 students should leave this lesson knowing that fluency at the highest level often consists in the willingness to say nothing more than is needed, and in trusting that the listener has heard everything the silence carries. The course has been teaching this from L1 onwards. Here it teaches it without comment.
content.grammar_focus: This dialogue is the most syntactically minimal in Block 5, and the minimalism is itself the lesson. Marie's central admission uses a sustained metaphorical present perfect: 'I am tired in a way I have not been tired before. Not the eight-month tired. A different kind. The tired of a person who has held a small thing through a difficult winter and is now putting the small thing down for one evening.' Five sentences, each shorter than the last in a particular pattern, with the syntactic centre being 'the tired of a person who…' — a definite article + adjective-as-noun + of-genitive + relative clause. C1 prose can use the article 'the' before an adjective to convert it into a noun phrase that names a category ('the tired', 'the angry', 'the unsaid'). Note also Sophie's confession: 'I have been given that, this year. By more people than I can name in one sentence. Helena. Edward. Mum. You. Carlos. Eleanor. Henry. Even Tom, in his way.' Present perfect passive ('I have been given') followed by a fragment-list of seven proper names, each a single noun, plus 'Even Tom, in his way' as the eighth — the list is a recapitulation of the entire courseworld in nominal form. C1 register can deploy fragmentary lists as rhetorical devices when the listener will recognise each item. The final exchange — 'Iceland.' / 'Iceland.' — is two single nouns, each a complete utterance. Sophie's repetition of Marie's word performs the agreement and closes the lesson without commentary. C1 students should hear that the smallest grammatical unit (a single noun) can carry the largest meaning when both speakers know what the noun stands for.

key_vocabulary.note: Исландское fish soup — традиционное блюдо: рыба (часто треска или пикша), картофель, лук, сливки. Подаётся с тёмным ржаным хлебом. Стандартный заказ в портовом ресторане Reykjavík.
Gamla höfnin — историческая гавань Reykjavík, теперь туристический район с ресторанами и whale-watching tours. Британский путешественник там обычно проводит вечер.
Буквальное: положить груз. Метафорическое: временно перестать нести ответственность. Marie применяет к материнству: 'putting the small thing down for one evening'. Британский разговорный.
Marie: 'the thanking is structural'. Архитектурный термин (несущая конструкция), применённый к человеческому действию: благодарность не как утверждение, а как свойство образа жизни. C1 транспозиция.
Глагол с акцентом на чёткое словесное выражение, в отличие от молчаливого выражения через действия. Marie освобождает Sophie от необходимости articulate.
'The boats settled for the night' — лодки встали на ночь. Морской регистр, переносимый в обыденную лексику. Британский разговорный.
Прилагательное с морфологическим интенсивом: 'un-' + 'repeat' + '-able'. Применяется к моментам, которые невозможно воспроизвести. C1 описательный регистр.
Британский разговорный: глагол 'go' для убывания напитка. 'The wine is going. Should we have another.' Casual middle-class регистр в ресторане.

comprehension_questions.hint: A holiday implies a complete escape; an evening implies a bracketed pause within an ongoing life. Marie is not escaping. She is putting something down for a few hours. What does this distinction protect her from claiming?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie says 'Iceland has been the evening. It has not been a holiday; it has been an evening.' What is the difference between 'a holiday' and 'an evening' in her usage, and why does she insist on the second category?

comprehension_questions.hint: She borrows Þórir's phrase. The recognition is structural, not about content. Why does it require all the separate acts to have happened before the pattern becomes visible?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie says: 'I had been treating it as a sequence of separate kindnesses. I think it is one thing.' What is the 'one thing' she names, and why has it taken twelve months for her to see the year this way?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the cultural note's closing analysis. The two single-word utterances do not summarise the trip; they acknowledge that the trip has had a name. Why is naming sufficient?
comprehension_questions.question: The lesson ends with 'Iceland.' / 'Iceland.' — a single noun spoken twice. What does this exchange perform, and how does it relate to the cultural note's observation that British close-friendship register at its most evolved 'does not perform endings'?

content.title: The Witness

dialogue.line: Read it again.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I have read it twice.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Read it three times. The third reading is where the problem will surface, because the first reading was emotional and the second reading was procedural and the third reading is the one in which you notice what she has not said.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: She has not said where she was on the Tuesday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Quite. And had she been where she has, in every previous statement, asserted she was, she would, almost certainly, have mentioned it again, because witnesses returning to a story they have told before tend to repeat their fixtures. The fact that she does not mention the Tuesday is not, in itself, evidence of anything. It is, however, the kind of absence that one would expect a competent solicitor to notice and write a follow-up question about.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: And you would prefer that we be the competent solicitor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I would prefer that we be the competent barrister, since we are barristers, but yes. The principle is the same.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Edward, I want to flag something.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I have been, since Monday, slightly less attentive than usual. I have a personal thing happening this weekend that I have been carrying for three weeks at low volume, and the carrying has cost me roughly half a brain-cell in working memory. I would not have missed the Tuesday absence on a clearer week. I am telling you this not to apologise but so that you can calibrate what you ask me for between now and Monday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Two thoughts. First. Thank you for telling me, because it spares me the work of inferring it, which I would otherwise have done by Tuesday. Second. I am not going to ask you whether the personal thing is large or small or what kind of thing it is, because that is not my territory, but I will say that if you are functioning at half a brain-cell less than usual you are still functioning at approximately one and a half brain-cells more than most of my colleagues do at full strength, and I do not propose to lose sleep over the discrepancy.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Thank you, Edward.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: However.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: However.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: If the personal thing recurs — that is, if it is something that will happen again, in some form, over the coming year — I would like, when you are ready, to know roughly what shape of recurrence to expect. Not because I want to manage it. Because I want to plan around it. Diaries are easier when one knows what to leave room for.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: It will recur. Probably twice a year. I do not know yet whether the right shape is autumn-and-spring or just the spring.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. When you know, tell me. In the meantime I have left the spring in your diary as flexible. The autumn one I will believe in when I see it.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Right. The Tuesday.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: The Tuesday. I will draft a question by lunchtime and put it in front of you before three.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: By two. I am at the Crown Court at three and I would rather sign off the question before I go than after.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: By two.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: And, Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The fact that you flagged the half-brain-cell — that is the version of you the chambers hired. Keep doing it. Do not, under any circumstances, become the version that pretends to be at full capacity when she is not. That version would be more efficient on a Tuesday afternoon and considerably less useful by Easter.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Understood.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Coffee?
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I will make it. The kettle is sulking again.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It has been sulking since 2019. We accept it. It accepts us.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

pratique.scenario: Imagine you are about to enter a working week during which you will be operating at slightly less than full capacity because of something private you are carrying. Use Sophie's frame: flag the situation to whoever needs to know, name the cost in operational terms ('half a brain-cell in working memory'), specify what you are not asking for, and offer your manager or colleague the information they need to plan around you.
prompt_phrases: I want to flag something. I have been, since [time], operating at slightly less than usual…
I am telling you this not to apologise but so that you can calibrate…
If the thing recurs, I will tell you. In the meantime…

content.situation: Friday morning, early March. Sophie is now ten months into Wellgate Chambers. The office on the second floor has acquired the small accumulations of a working life — a cardigan on the back of the chair, a row of coffee cups Edward has been threatening to remove since November, a photograph on the windowsill of Olivia at one. Edward has knocked at twenty past nine carrying a witness statement and the look he wears when a case has acquired an unwelcome new layer. Sophie is preparing for a directions hearing on Monday. The case is a contested contact matter that has been running since June; the witness, the children's grandmother, has produced a statement overnight that nobody asked her to produce. Sophie has read it once. She is reading it again when Edward arrives. She is going to Bakewell tonight on the seven-twelve from Manchester Piccadilly to Sheffield, then the local to Bakewell, but neither she nor Edward is going to mention that until the working conversation has finished.

phrase_cle.phrase: I am telling you this not to apologise but so that you can calibrate.
phrase_cle.example: I have been distracted this week and I missed the Tuesday point. I am telling you this not to apologise but so that you can calibrate what you ask me for between now and Friday.
phrase_cle.explanation: Конструкция, в которой Sophie разделяет два возможных мотива признания и заявляет о втором, отсекая первый. Это очень характерная для C2 операция: говорящий заранее определяет жанр собственного высказывания, чтобы слушатель не прочитал его в неправильной рамке. Грамматически — параллельная отрицательно-утвердительная инфинитивная конструкция через 'not X but so that Y'. Первая часть — to-infinitive в роли цели ('to apologise'); вторая часть — so that + clause с модальным глаголом ('so that you can calibrate'). Обрати внимание: цели разнопорядковые. Извинение — социальный акт; калибровка — операционный. Sophie отказывается от социального жанра в пользу операционного, и формула делает это ясным. Расширения: 'I am telling you this not to complain but so that you can plan'; 'I am sharing this not to alarm you but so that we can decide together'; 'I am writing not to ask for anything but so that the record is accurate'. На C2 ты учишься не только использовать эту формулу, но и распознавать, когда твоё собственное высказывание должно её получить, чтобы быть прочитанным правильно.
phrase_cle.translation: Я говорю тебе это не чтобы извиниться, а чтобы ты могла рассчитать.

expressions.meaning: Edward's instruction that close reading of a witness statement requires not one but three passes, each performing a different function. The numbered repetition is itself a piece of professional pedagogy: he is teaching Sophie how to reread, not just telling her to do it. C1-into-C2 transposition: the rule has become so internalised it is offered without explanation.

Edward's compressed maxim about the psychology of testimony. 'Fixtures' (architectural — things permanently installed) is doing technical work: in a re-told story, the elements treated as load-bearing tend to recur, and the absence of an expected fixture is often more diagnostic than the presence of a new claim.
expressions.expression: Witnesses returning to a story they have told before tend to repeat their fixtures

expressions.meaning: Sophie's pre-emptive professional disclosure, deployed here in a specifically C2 register: she is naming a current limitation on her capacity, in advance, so that her supervisor can adjust expectations. The construction is recurrent in the courseworld (Marie used it in L25 of C1) and now appears in upper professional speech.

Sophie's metaphor for an unresolved personal matter that is quietly present without being acute. The audio metaphor (volume as intensity) converts a worry into something with a dial — and worry one can imagine turning down is worry one can also imagine carrying. C2-typical figure: domestic technology vocabulary deployed for psychological description.

Edward's signal that he recognises a private domain and is choosing not to enter it. The territorial metaphor is borrowed from political and legal English ('out of my territory', 'not in my jurisdiction') and used here as a courtesy. C2 students should hear the precision: he is not refusing curiosity, he is locating it.

Edward's dry shorthand for a contingent commitment: he will reserve diary space for the spring meeting because Sophie has confirmed it, but will not yet treat the autumn one as real because she herself has not confirmed it. The phrase is faintly biblical (doubting Thomas) and Edward uses it without pious overtone.
expressions.expression: I will believe in when I see it

content.cultural_note: Edward's office at Wellgate is a particular kind of British professional space, and a small note on its register may help. Senior barristers in chambers — particularly at smaller, less-corporate sets — work in offices that look more like university dons' rooms than law-firm interiors: bookshelves, slightly chaotic accumulations of paper, a kettle that has earned a personality. Edward's reference to the kettle as 'sulking' since 2019 belongs to a register British speakers reach for when describing inanimate objects that have, through long association, acquired the status of household members. The closing exchange — 'It has been sulking since 2019. We accept it. It accepts us.' — is doing a small piece of British comic anthropomorphism, and is also a quiet allusion to the Larkin line about acceptance ('what will survive of us is love'). Edward will not make the allusion explicit; if Sophie has heard it, that is between her and him. Note also the phrase 'we are barristers, but yes. The principle is the same' — Edward's way of correcting the technical error in Sophie's vocabulary (solicitor vs barrister, two distinct legal professions in England) without reproaching her for it. The correction is parenthetical, friendly, and immediately released. C2 register often handles correction this way: register the error, note the correction, move on. The teaching is in the not-dwelling.
content.grammar_focus: This dialogue carries one of the central C2 grammatical structures: the inverted counterfactual conditional in fully extended form. Edward says: 'And had she been where she has, in every previous statement, asserted she was, she would, almost certainly, have mentioned it again.' The opening 'had she been' is a third-conditional inverted ('if she had been' → 'had she been'); the embedded relative clause ('where she has, in every previous statement, asserted she was') runs a present perfect inside the conditional protasis; the apodosis ('she would, almost certainly, have mentioned it again') uses a parenthetically inserted adverbial ('almost certainly') between the modal and its perfect infinitive. C2 speakers can construct and parse this kind of compound conditional in real time. The rhetorical effect is that the diagnostic point — what the witness has not said — is delivered through a hypothetical reconstruction of what she would have said had her account been true. Note also Edward's parallel parenthetical structure: 'I am not going to ask you whether the personal thing is large or small or what kind of thing it is, because that is not my territory, but I will say that…' — the 'but I will say that' construction is doing C2-typical work: a long subordinate-clause structure resolved by a final assertion, with the contrast handled by 'but'. Sophie's professional disclosure uses a present perfect continuous with a quantitative metaphor: 'I have been, since Monday, slightly less attentive than usual.' The position of 'since Monday' between auxiliary and predicate is mild fronting — it gives temporal scope before the diagnostic word arrives. C2 prose tolerates this kind of mid-sentence adverbial because the listener is expected to track the syntactic frame across the interruption.

key_vocabulary.note: Слушание, на котором суд решает процедурные вопросы (расписание, доказательства, эксперты). В семейном суде Англии — рутинный этап в contested contact matter. Sophie готовится к нему на понедельник.
Семейное право: дело, в котором один родитель (или другой родственник) оспаривает порядок общения с ребёнком. Ведутся в семейном суде. Wellgate Chambers ведёт такие дела как часть family practice.
key_vocabulary.translation: оспариваемое дело о порядке общения с ребёнком

key_vocabulary.note: Суд, рассматривающий серьёзные уголовные дела в Англии. Edward — criminal silk, поэтому он часто там. В Манчестере Crown Court в Crown Square.
Морская метафора, перенесённая в аналитический регистр. Edward: 'the third reading is where the problem will surface'. Профессиональный британский.
Архитектурный термин (сантехника, электрика — то, что прикручено и не двигается), применённый Edward к структуре рассказа свидетеля. Авторская транспозиция, не общая идиома.
См. C1 L25. Профессиональный британский: 'I want to flag X' = я хочу заранее обозначить X. Sophie использует это в формальной рабочей коммуникации с Edward.
Sophie's метафора: личное дело присутствует, но не громко. На C2 такие домашние технологические образы — рабочий ресурс психологического описания.

Технический глагол (метрология), переходящий в управленческую и психологическую лексику. Recurring через C1 (L8 Mum: 'calibrated to not panic me'; L29 Marie: 'calibrated by your year'). На C2 — рабочая лексика.
Edward's afоризм. Безличное 'one' (а не 'you') — формальный британский регистр. Импликация: планирование — навык освобождения пространства, а не заполнения его.
key_vocabulary.word: diaries are easier when one knows what to leave room for
key_vocabulary.translation: ежедневники проще, когда знаешь, под что оставлять место

key_vocabulary.note: Британское комическое одушевление техники. Sulk — глагол про обиженное молчание (обычно у детей или подростков). Применённый к чайнику — типичный британский домашний приём.

comprehension_questions.hint: He himself names the three. Look at what each reading does that the previous one could not.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Edward's argument for reading a witness statement three times rather than once or twice? What does each reading add?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the difference between a social act and an operational one. What does she gain by refusing the first category?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie says she is telling Edward about her reduced capacity 'not to apologise but so that you can calibrate.' Why does she explicitly disavow the apology framing?

comprehension_questions.hint: He gives a specific prediction about cost. Look at the temporal scale of his warning.
comprehension_questions.question: Edward's closing instruction warns Sophie against 'the version that pretends to be at full capacity when she is not'. What does he claim about that version, and why does he prefer the version he hired?

content.title: Sheffield Station

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum. I am at Sheffield. I have twenty-two minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The train down ran on time, then.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Late, but the connection is generous.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is. The Bakewell train was, when I was younger, perpetually delayed in a manner that suggested it had its own ideas about time. I am told this has been corrected.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I wanted to ring. Not because there is anything to report, but because the last time I went, I rang, and not ringing this time would have been a small change of policy that I had not intended to make.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I appreciate the policy. The continuity is — useful to me. Although I would also not have minded if you had not rung. The not-minding is genuine.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I assumed.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Henry has Mark there this weekend. I think I told you in February.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You did. I had not retained the date.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, are you all right with — that. With Mark.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I had to think about that question for a moment when you first told me, because the honest answer was that I had no immediate reaction, and the no-reaction itself surprised me. I thought I would have a reaction. I did not.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mark is a person who exists in the world and who has, for reasons of his own, been part of Henry's life for a decade. None of that requires my permission. I did not give Henry permission to live the life he has been living since 2003, and he has not, to my knowledge, asked for any. The arrangement is satisfactory.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, can I ask you something else.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: On Wednesday you mentioned — you said in passing that you had been to the surgery. You said it as if it were a fact you were filing rather than reporting. I have been carrying the unspecified surgery-visit since Wednesday and I would prefer, before I get on the local, to know whether I should be carrying it or not.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. You are too good at noticing the things I had hoped were filed too compactly to register. That is — well. That is what a child becomes, if she has been listening properly, by the age of thirty-three.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Right. The honest version. I had a routine appointment about my hip, which has been complaining at me since Christmas, and the GP has referred me on for a scan. The scan is in late April. I will know what is happening at the start of May. Before May, there is nothing to know. I had not planned to mention it before May because I do not believe in distributing uncertainty, but you have, in your characteristic way, retrieved it from where I had filed it, so. There it is.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Are you in pain.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am in low-grade discomfort that is, on a bad day, slightly more than discomfort. I am not in pain in the sense that the word is generally used. I am taking paracetamol when I remember to and not taking it when I do not remember to, and the difference between the two is smaller than it ought to be, which suggests the paracetamol is more habit than help.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Thank you for giving me the honest version.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You requested the honest version. I am not yet so dishonest as to deliver the optimistic version when the operational one has been specifically asked for.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Sophie. Carry it, if you must, but carry it lightly. There is nothing to do until May. Whatever comes in May will come in May. Do not bring it to Bakewell with you. Bakewell has its own weather.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Bakewell has its own weather.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. Train.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Give them both my regards. The two of them. By name.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I will.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Off you go.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

pratique.scenario: Imagine telling someone close to you something they have been carrying — a worry, a piece of news, a question — that you would prefer they release. Use Mum's two-step: first, give them the honest version with the actual scope and timing; second, instruct them to carry it lightly and not import it into a different setting where it does not belong.
prompt_phrases: The honest version is: [scope and timing]…
I had not planned to mention it before [date] because I do not believe in distributing uncertainty…
Carry it lightly. There is nothing to do until [date]. [Other place] has its own weather.

content.situation: Friday evening, six minutes past six. Sophie has come up on the seven-twelve from Manchester Piccadilly — a misnomer, the train was running late and she has caught it at half past five — and is changing at Sheffield for the local service to Bakewell. The Sheffield platform is the cold, lit kind of platform that exists between two stations she actually has feelings about; this one is a corridor. She has twenty-two minutes. There is a bench by the third support pillar that is sheltered from the wind. She rings Mum because she has not rung her since Wednesday and because the second visit deserves the same advance disclosure the first one did. Mum picks up on the third ring; she has been folding washing.

phrase_cle.example: Don't take work-worries into Sunday lunch. Sunday lunch has its own weather.
phrase_cle.explanation: Метеорологическая метафора, в которой каждое место (физическое или социальное) имеет автономный эмоциональный климат, и говорящий обязан этот климат уважать, не привнося в него атмосферу из других мест. Грамматически — простая структура X has its own Y, где X — место или ситуация, а Y — нечто, на что обычно не распространяется владение (weather, light, rules, time). На C2 ты учишься создавать и принимать такие микро-метафоры в реальном времени: формула 'X has its own Y' — рабочий каркас. Расширения: 'family lunch has its own weather' (там работают другие правила); 'morning has its own grammar' (утренние разговоры устроены не как вечерние); 'illness has its own time' (время в больнице идёт иначе). Mum использует фразу как инструкцию о boundary-keeping: то, что Sophie несёт, не должно войти в Bakewell. На C2 это очень характерный приём: вместо прямого 'put it down' (что звучало бы наставительно) Mum предлагает образ, который Sophie может применить сама.

expressions.meaning: Sophie's faintly bureaucratic phrase for 'not-ringing-this-time'. By naming the act of not-ringing as a 'change of policy', she gives it weight it would not have had as a casual omission. The construction belongs to civil-service English ('a change in departmental policy') and is being deployed here in a daughter-mother register, which is the joke. C2 transposition.
expressions.expression: A small change of policy that I had not intended to make

expressions.meaning: Mum's diagnostic about her own emotional response to Mark's existence. The hyphenated 'no-reaction' converts an absence into a noun, which can then be the subject of 'surprised me'. C2 register: the willingness to make negative space into named content. The construction echoes Henry's 'I am not at risk from this letter' from C1 L14, applied to a different category.
expressions.expression: I had no immediate reaction, and the no-reaction itself surprised me

expressions.meaning: Mum's principle for handling not-yet-known medical information: she does not share uncertainty with people who cannot act on it, because asking them to carry it is a form of involuntary tax. The phrase is hers and characteristic; it converts a parental restraint into a stated philosophy.
expressions.expression: I do not believe in distributing uncertainty

expressions.meaning: Mum's image for information she had hoped Sophie would not notice. The metaphor is bureaucratic-archival (filing) with an embedded perceptual one (register). The whole phrase functions as a quiet compliment to Sophie's listening, though Mum delivers it in the form of a mild complaint.

Mum's metaphor for the autonomous emotional climate of a particular place. Each setting in a person's life has its own air pressure; what one is carrying elsewhere should not be imported. The phrase is a release: Mum is instructing Sophie to leave the surgery-visit at Sheffield. C2 register: the use of meteorological metaphor for psychological boundary-setting.

Mum's compressed self-diagnosis about taking paracetamol whose actual effect is small but whose ritual function is meaningful. The two-word contrast (habit vs help) is the structure; the comparative ('more X than Y') is the recurring courseworld figure for confessing what one is actually doing rather than what one tells oneself one is doing.

content.cultural_note: Mum's reluctance to share medical information before there is anything actionable to share belongs to a particular British generational register — what one might call the parental ethics of non-distribution. The principle is not stoicism; it is courtesy. To share uncertainty with people who cannot act on it is, in Mum's view, to oblige them to carry weight without the means to set it down. Younger British registers (and most American ones) tend to operate on the opposite principle — that loved ones have a right to know early, that withholding is a form of distance, that bearing things alone is a small failure of intimacy. Both are coherent positions; the lesson is not that one is correct. The lesson is that Mum's position is internally consistent, and that Sophie has read it correctly across thirty-three years of practice. Notice the closing exchange: 'Give them both my regards. The two of them. By name.' Mum is doing something quietly substantial — naming Mark as a person whose name she will recognise the use of, in a context in which the social default would be to refuse the recognition. The instruction 'by name' is the exact opposite of 'in general'. C2 students should hear the small generosity of it: Mum is not asking Henry's life to come closer, but she is permitting Sophie to refer to its current shape with full vocabulary. The phrase 'by name' will return — see if you can predict where.
content.grammar_focus: Mum's central disclosure in this dialogue is built on a sustained C2 grammatical construction: the hypothetical-narrative past in extended apposition. 'I had a routine appointment about my hip, which has been complaining at me since Christmas, and the GP has referred me on for a scan.' Past simple ('I had') for the completed event, present perfect continuous ('has been complaining') for the ongoing condition that motivated it, present perfect ('has referred') for the consequent action. Three different past-related tense constructions in one sentence, each doing distinct semantic work. C2 prose can hold this much temporal layering when the speaker has thought carefully about which moments to anchor where. Note also her use of inversion in the closing structural moves: 'I am not yet so dishonest as to deliver the optimistic version when the operational one has been specifically asked for.' The 'so X as to Y' construction is C2-typical for measured negative claims; the comparative-result frame keeps Mum's self-assessment precise. Sophie's request for the honest version uses a mild fronted construction: 'I have been carrying the unspecified surgery-visit since Wednesday and I would prefer, before I get on the local, to know whether I should be carrying it or not.' Present perfect continuous ('have been carrying'), embedded conditional with adverbial inserted ('I would prefer, before I get on the local, to know'), and an indirect question with negative tag ('whether I should be carrying it or not'). The final 'or not' is doing C2 work: it admits the possibility that the answer is no and signals Sophie's willingness to be told to put the matter down.

key_vocabulary.note: Британский разговорный глагол. В американском чаще 'call'. На C2 — стандарт. Sophie 'rang' Mum, не 'called'.
Британское разговорное: поезд со всеми остановками между крупными станциями. Sophie меняется с InterCity на the local в Sheffield для Bakewell.
В Великобритании 'surgery' имеет два значения: (1) хирургия (как и в US), (2) терапевтический приём в GP practice. Mum говорит про второе значение. C2 — знание различия.
General Practitioner. Британская система NHS: первая точка контакта, который при необходимости направляет (refers on) к специалистам. Mum референс — типичная NHS-траектория.
Медицинский британский фразовый глагол. Не просто 'refer' — 'refer on' предполагает движение в цепочке специалистов.
Медицинский британский: используется про симптомы, которые присутствуют, но не остры. Mum применяет к hip. На C2 — рабочий регистр.
Британское название (acetaminophen в US). Стандартное безрецептурное обезболивающее в Британии. Mum использует его как маркер нескормленной сильной боли.
Бюрократическая метафора: информация как документ, который убирают в нужное место. Mum применяет к собственному обращению с медицинскими фактами. C2 транспозиция.
Парная конструкция к 'file'. Sophie 'retrieved' информацию, которую Mum заархивировала. Метафорическая последовательность: file → retrieve.
key_vocabulary.word: to retrieve (something from where it was filed)
key_vocabulary.translation: извлечь (что-либо из места, куда его положили)

key_vocabulary.note: Британская формальная формула вежливости. 'Regards' — слегка старомодное; передаёт уважение без интимности. Mum использует это про Henry и Mark.

comprehension_questions.hint: She names the principle herself — 'a small change of policy that I had not intended to make'. What is the policy, and what would silently abandoning it have meant?
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie ring Mum from Sheffield, when she has nothing specific to report? What does she gain by maintaining the practice?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is making a courtesy claim, not a stoic one. What does she think she would be obliging Sophie to do, if she had told her in February?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum says she does not 'believe in distributing uncertainty'. What is her ethical principle, and how does it differ from a more contemporary instinct to share early?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at what 'by name' rules out. What would the alternative — vague or generic regards — have communicated about Mum's position?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum's closing instruction is 'Give them both my regards. The two of them. By name.' Why does she specify 'by name'?

content.title: Three at the Table

dialogue.line: The casserole is, by any objective standard, slightly overdone, but I am hoping that the company will distract from the meat.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: It is fine, Henry.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: It is fine.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You are both being polite.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: I am being moderately polite. Sophie has only been here forty minutes; I expect her politeness to last for at least another hour, after which we shall see.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Mark, can I ask — what did Henry tell you about me. Not the facts. The framing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: He told me that you are a careful person who has chosen, in the last two years, a series of unfashionable career options that are, on examination, sensible. He told me that you read more than is strictly required for your work. He told me that you are not, at present, in a relationship, and that this is — as far as he can tell — a matter of preference rather than circumstance, but that he is not in a position to verify the inference. He told me that you have a friend called Marie who is, he understands, the central relationship of your adult life so far. He told me that I should not, under any circumstances, attempt to be your brother, because we are not, he said, of an age or a history that would make that gesture credible, and the attempt would be condescending.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: That is — that is a more accurate framing than I would have managed myself.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: He has had longer to compose it.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: I should clarify that I did not deliver that as a single speech. It accumulated over several conversations, in much the same way that I have accumulated, since April, an account of Mark for you, Sophie, which I have been releasing in three letters because I did not know whether you wanted the whole thing or none of it, and the letters allowed you to choose at each interval.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: I noticed.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Sophie. Can I ask you a similar question.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What did Henry tell you about me.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: He told me that you are an exact person whose exactness has been, over time, softened by a wife who is more than a little funnier than you are. He told me that you came into his life in 2014 by being delivered, by Hilary, as one of the conditions of being with Hilary, and that you and he took roughly four years to develop a register that worked. He told me that you, like him, are a person who finds writing easier than speaking, but that you are, unlike him, capable of speaking when you have to, which makes you the more practically useful of the two of you. He told me that I should not assume, on the basis of your being his stepson, that I have any particular relationship to you, but he asked me to consider, separately from that, whether I might want to know you on your own merits, and that the question was for me to answer in my own time.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That is — that is more flattering than I would have managed myself.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: I am, in both cases, attempting to provide accurate framings rather than complimentary ones, and the accuracy may, in the eye of the framed, look like flattery, but I would ask you to consider whether the alternative — accurate framings that look unflattering — would have been more useful tonight.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: It would not.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: It would not.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Mark.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: What is the first question you wanted to ask me, before we sat down at this table.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I had three. I will give you the easiest first because the others I am not yet sure I have earned the right to ask.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Are you genuinely all right with the fact that I have been here all week, given that this is, structurally, your weekend with Henry.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: I am genuinely all right with it. I had three weeks to consider whether I would not be all right with it, and the not-being-all-right-with-it never came. I think the absence of the reaction is itself a piece of information. It tells me that I do not see this weekend as a private audience that you have intruded into. It tells me that whatever this is, between Henry and me, is large enough to accommodate other people.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Then I do not need the second question.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: What was the second question.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Whether you would like me to leave on Saturday morning rather than Sunday, so that you and Henry could have at least one full day without a third party in the house.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Stay till Sunday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I will.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: I should mention that I have, separately, prepared a small plan for tomorrow that I have not yet told either of you about, in case it transpired that the configuration of the weekend needed adjustment. The plan involves Mark and me walking up to the upper field at half past six, because there is an arrangement with Geoff Eyre about the lambs which Mark agreed to help with on Tuesday, and which neither of us had remembered to discuss with Sophie. The plan would mean that Mark and I are out of the house from six-thirty to roughly nine, and Sophie has the kitchen and the kettle and the morning to herself, after which we converge for a walk.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: That works.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Henry, you had not told me you were going to release that plan in this form. I had assumed we would discuss it in the morning.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: I had intended to discuss it in the morning. The conversation reached a juncture at which it seemed more efficient to release it now.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Right. The casserole is fine, by the way. I am no longer being moderately polite about it.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Then we have all crossed a small threshold and the wine can be opened more seriously.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

pratique.scenario: Imagine a meal at which three people sit down together for the first time — yourself, someone you know well, and someone you have never met but who has heard about you through the first person. Use the dialogue's central structure: ask the new person what framing of you they were given. Listen. Then offer to deliver, on request, the framing you were given of them.
prompt_phrases: Can I ask — what did [the third person] tell you about me. Not the facts. The framing.
He/she told me that you…
I was attempting to provide accurate framings rather than complimentary ones, and the accuracy may, in the eye of the framed, look like flattery.

content.situation: Friday evening, half past nine. The kitchen of the cottage in Bakewell. Henry has met Sophie at the station as he did in April — same waxed jacket, taller in the dusk than she had remembered him being in her head — and has driven her up. Mark has been at the cottage since the previous Saturday; he came over from Sheffield with an overnight bag and stayed for the full week, which is the longest he and Henry have done in one stretch. Henry has cooked a casserole. There is a bottle of wine open on the table that nobody is drinking very fast. Mark is forty-one — a year and four months older than Sophie — broad-shouldered, slightly stooped from years at a drafting desk. He works for the Ordnance Survey in Southampton three days a week and the rest of the week from home in Sheffield; he is married to Aoife, an Irish nurse he met in 2011, and has two children, Cara who is eight and Tom who is five. Henry has, between April and now, told Sophie all of these facts in three letters. Mark has been told, by Henry, the equivalent set about Sophie. Neither of them has met before tonight.

phrase_cle.example: Tell me what you said about me to your mother. Not the facts — she already had those. The framing.
phrase_cle.explanation: Конструкция, в которой говорящий разделяет два уровня одного описания и просит конкретно второй. На C2 ты учишься различать факты (содержимое) и рамку (организация фактов в значимое целое — что отобрано, в каком порядке, с какими акцентами, какая категория из них следует). Грамматически — две минимальные конструкции через противопоставление: отрицательная номинальная фраза 'not the facts' и утвердительная номинальная 'the framing'. Артикль 'the' в обоих случаях — определённый, потому что обе категории присутствуют в общем поле собеседников. Sophie использует эту фразу, чтобы избежать обыденного разговора о биографии Mark и попасть сразу в более интересный — как Henry его описал, какие из всего множества фактов выбрал, в каком ключе расположил. Это запрос на эпистемологию вместо данных. Расширения: 'not the words, the tone'; 'not the decision, the reasoning behind it'; 'not the answer, the way you arrived at it'. Каждая конструкция признаёт, что описание имеет минимум два уровня, и точное общение требует выбора уровня.

expressions.meaning: Henry's mock-precise hedge for a self-deprecating culinary verdict. The vocabulary of measurement (objective, standard) applied to a slightly overdone casserole produces gentle humour. C2 register: technical formality used domestically. Henry's signature register move, recurrent from C1.

Mark's compressed introduction of his own register: dry, slightly comic, willing to make a forecast about a person he has only just met. The second part ('after which we shall see') is doing the heavy lifting — it announces that he is comfortable predicting Sophie's politeness will fail, and that the failure will not be a problem.
expressions.expression: I expect her politeness to last for at least another hour, after which we shall see

expressions.meaning: Henry's distinction between two genres of description: one optimised for truth, one optimised for the recipient's self-regard. He explicitly chooses the first and notes that it can be misread as the second. C2 register: the willingness to name the genre of one's own speech in advance, to control its interpretation.
expressions.expression: An accurate framing rather than a complimentary one

expressions.meaning: Sophie's compressed account, transmitted from Henry, of how Mark entered Henry's life. The verb 'delivered' (parcel-receipt) is faintly comic; the phrase 'as one of the conditions' converts a stepson into a clause in a contract. The sentence treats a complex emotional event with operational vocabulary, which is the C2 transposition.
expressions.expression: Delivered, by Hilary, as one of the conditions of being with Hilary

expressions.meaning: Mark's principle for the timing of his other two questions. He is signalling that some questions require accumulated standing between speakers before they can be asked, and that he does not yet have it. The phrase 'earned the right' converts conversational permission into something accumulated through demonstrated trust. C2 ethical register.
expressions.expression: I am not yet sure I have earned the right to ask

expressions.meaning: Sophie's image for what the weekend is not. The royal-court vocabulary ('private audience') is being applied to a weekend visit to one's father; the absurdity of the formality is the joke, but the substance — that Sophie does not see Mark's presence as intrusion — is delivered through the deliberately oversized phrase. C2 humour.
expressions.expression: A private audience that you have intruded into

expressions.meaning: Henry's image for the moment in a meeting between strangers when the formal register can give way to a less guarded one. The architectural metaphor (threshold) is C2-typical for marking transitions in social register. The 'more seriously' applied to wine-drinking is dry — it means 'more enthusiastically', not 'more solemnly'.
expressions.expression: We have all crossed a small threshold and the wine can be opened more seriously

content.cultural_note: The structural device of this lesson — Mark and Sophie each receiving, from a third party (Henry) and through each other, a framing of themselves — is a quietly British social form that British comedy and British literature both deploy: the indirect introduction. In some cultures, two new people meet and discover each other through direct conversation. In the British register on display here, two new people first hear themselves described to each other through a mediator they both trust, and the descriptions are produced as evidence that the mediator has thought carefully about both of them. Henry's framings are doing several things at once: they assert that he has paid attention, they spare Sophie and Mark the awkward early phase of self-presentation, and they implicitly model how the two of them might describe each other in the future. Note also Mark's deferral of his other two questions ('I am not yet sure I have earned the right to ask'). The principle — that conversational permission is accumulated rather than presumed — is recognisable across many British registers but particularly characteristic of educated Northern English speech. Mark, like Henry, uses formal vocabulary in casual settings; the formality is not pretension, it is the register he was raised in. C2 students should hear that two careful speakers in this register can develop a working dynamic across one meal because the careful register is itself a shared protocol.
content.grammar_focus: This dialogue stages two parallel speeches — Mark's account of what Henry told him about Sophie, and Sophie's account of what Henry told her about Mark — that are constructed identically, with each describing the other through Henry's eyes. The grammatical move is the sustained reported-speech sequence using past simple of the reporting verb ('He told me') with present-tense embedded clauses ('that you are', 'that you read', 'that you have'). The persistence of present tense inside past-reported speech is a specific C2 register choice: it preserves the proposition as currently true, rather than backshifting it to past ('that you were a careful person'), which would imply Henry's framing was about a past Sophie. The proposition holds. C2 prose can hold this kind of selective backshift across long passages when the speaker has decided which propositions remain open. Note also Henry's inversions and parentheticals: 'I should clarify that I did not deliver that as a single speech. It accumulated over several conversations, in much the same way that I have accumulated, since April, an account of Mark for you, Sophie, which I have been releasing in three letters because I did not know whether you wanted the whole thing or none of it, and the letters allowed you to choose at each interval.' One sentence, six clauses, three nestings, with the vocative 'Sophie' inserted between commas as a parenthetical. C2 syntactic complexity. The closing exchange — 'Then we have all crossed a small threshold and the wine can be opened more seriously.' — uses the present perfect ('have crossed') with a present passive ('can be opened') joined by 'and'.
