The aspectual difference (perfective achievement / present possibility) is the rhetorical structure: the threshold is now behind them, and the next phase has just become available. C2 students should hear how tense choice maps the social geometry.

key_vocabulary.note: Британское блюдо медленного приготовления: мясо, овощи, бульон, в духовке несколько часов. Идеальное для зимнего ужина. Henry готовит его как 'безопасное' блюдо — мало рисков переварить.
key_vocabulary.translation: тушёное блюдо в керамической или чугунной посуде

key_vocabulary.note: Государственная организация, картографирующая Великобританию с 1791 года. Базируется в Southampton. Mark — картограф там; это очень специфическая британская профессия, маркер point.
Большой наклонный стол для картографов, архитекторов, инженеров. Mark стал слегка сутулым от многолетней работы за ним. Профессиональная деталь.
Британская сельская топонимия: фермы и наделы часто называются по положению ('upper field', 'lower paddock', 'top barn'). Geoff Eyre — фермер-сосед (Margaret Eyre упоминалась в C1 L13).
Ягнятся в Дербишире в конце февраля — начале марта. Sophie приехала в lambing season. Mark помогает Geoff Eyre в эти выходные.
Mark — pasynok Henry. Технически: сын Hilary от первого брака; Henry и Hilary не были женаты, но прожили вместе одиннадцать лет. Регистр: 'stepson' использует Henry, более точное слово, чем 'son'.
Произносится приблизительно 'EE-fa'. Распространённое ирландское имя. Mark женат на ирландке-медсестре.
Henry's корпоративная метафора: план как пресс-релиз. 'I had intended to discuss it in the morning.' Применяется к семейному ужину — комическое C2 переключение регистра.
Henry: 'the conversation reached a juncture'. Слово из инженерной и аналитической лексики. C2 регистр: применять формальные термины к разговору.
Геометрический и физический глагол, перенесённый в социальный регистр. Henry: 'we converge for a walk'. C2 транспозиция.
Mark's самохарактеристика: ниже, чем 'fully polite', но выше, чем 'rude'. Точная градация регистра — британское C2 умение.

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at what each level — facts versus framing — tells her. One has been available since February in Henry's letters. The other has not.
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Sophie ask Mark for 'the framing' rather than the facts? What is she gaining by asking the question this way?

comprehension_questions.hint: He is treating conversational permission as something accumulated over time rather than presumed at the outset. What does the deferral protect, and what does it offer Sophie?
comprehension_questions.question: Mark says he 'had three' questions but offers only one, deferring the others because he is 'not yet sure I have earned the right to ask.' What principle is he operating on, and how does it shape the evening's pace?

comprehension_questions.hint: He wants the configuration of the weekend to be visible to all parties simultaneously. What does the simultaneity prevent?
comprehension_questions.question: Henry's 'small plan' for Saturday morning means Sophie will have the kitchen alone. Why has he prepared this in advance, and why does he release it in front of Mark rather than telling each of them privately?

content.title: Two Henrys

dialogue.line: He has been making coffee with the same machine for sixteen years.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: It is excellent coffee.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is. The machine is, by any reasonable assessment, on its last legs, and Henry has known this for at least four of those years, but he has decided that the coffee remains acceptable and that the case for replacement has therefore not been made. I suspect the machine will be returned to the shop, posthumously, sometime around 2031.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Mark.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Last night, when you said you had three questions and I was not allowed two of them yet — what were the other two.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am still not sure I have earned the right to ask them. But I will tell you what they were, on the understanding that telling-you-what-they-were is not the same as asking-them, and you are not obliged to answer.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Understood.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The first was: have you seen — in the hours since you walked in — any version of Henry that you did not see in April. And if so, do you want to tell me which one, because I am, separately, curious whether the version of him I have known for ten years overlaps at all with the version you are now meeting.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: The second was harder. The second was: how are you, in yourself, at the second visit. Because in my experience the second visit is structurally different from the first, and the difference is the part nobody warns you about. The first visit is a question. The second visit is the beginning of an answer that you have not yet decided you want to give.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Mark.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: I will answer both. Not because you asked, because you did not, but because the questions are good ones and I have been asking the same questions of myself in lower-resolution form, and your formulations are sharper than mine. It is more useful to answer your version than to articulate my own.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: On the first. Yes. There is a Henry I am seeing this weekend that I did not see in April. The April Henry was a Henry composing himself for a stranger he had hoped, for thirty years, might one day be in his kitchen. He was, that weekend, perceptibly inside a register he had been preparing. He was — kind, exact, careful. He was also, I now see, slightly performing his own carefulness, in a way I did not, in April, have the equipment to read.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The Henry I am seeing this weekend has stopped doing that. Not because he no longer cares about the carefulness — he does — but because the carefulness has, I think, been internalised to the point where he no longer has to perform it. Yesterday at the station he was, for the first three minutes, distracted, and the distraction was a fact about Tuesday and the lambs rather than a fact about me, and the small relief of not being centrally what he was thinking about was — substantial.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: I have, in ten years, watched him do that with one other person, which was Hilary, in the second year of their being together. The carefulness moved from foreground to background. Hilary said, when it happened, that she could finally hear what was on the radio. Which I think was a joke.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Mark, can I ask — was Hilary funny.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Hilary was — yes, but in a particular way that you would have to have lived with to read. She was funny in the way certain librarians and certain Northern women of her generation are funny, which is by saying something extremely dry, in a tone that is not quite the tone she uses for serious things, and waiting for you to notice. If you noticed, she gave you nothing in return; the noticing was the gift. If you did not notice, she did not repeat herself or signal. The jokes were costly to her if they failed. She made them anyway.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Sorry. You asked a yes-or-no question and I gave you a paragraph.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: I am glad of the paragraph. Yes-or-no would have told me less.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Your second.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: On the second. I am — Mark, the honest answer is that I am at the second visit in a state I would describe as alert, by which I mean I am, at all times, aware that I am here and that being here is a thing I have chosen, and that the choosing is ongoing. I have not, yet, settled into a version of the visit in which I forget I have made a choice. I do not know whether that settling is the goal. I do not know whether the goal is rather to stay in this register, in which the visit is — visible to me as a visit. I am suspicious of arriving at a state in which Bakewell becomes invisible.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I had not thought of that.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: I think I have been operating, for ten years, on the assumption that the goal of repeated visits to Henry's kitchen is to make the kitchen invisible — to convert it from a place I notice into a place I am simply in. And you are saying — you are saying that for you the noticing might be the structure rather than the obstacle.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: I am not saying it must be. I am saying I do not yet know.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. I will revise my account. I will say to myself, when I get back to Sheffield, that there are at least two ways to do this, and one of them is mine and the other one is yours, and the difference is not — the difference is not a developmental stage. It is two different practices.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Mark.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: There is one thing I want to say before Henry comes back, and I want to say it in such a way that you can choose whether to take it as said or as a thing-I-said-once-that-need-not-be-referred-to.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am not, as you know, your brother. I am also not nothing. The category of what-I-am-to-you is, in my view, currently empty, and I think it should remain empty for at least the next two years, because the empty category is more useful than the wrong category would be. After two years, if there is a need for a name, we can find one. If there is no need, we leave the category empty and the practice continues without one. I propose this not because I want to formalise the relationship but because I want, in advance, to remove the pressure of finding a name we do not yet require.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Mark, that is — that is the most generous thing anyone has said to me about a category since Mum, ten months ago, said the category of things-I-can-be-told-about-Henry was not a small category.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Then the form has been borrowed before.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: It has.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am glad I am in good company.
dialogue.speaker: Mark

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Imagine a relationship in your life that does not yet have a name — perhaps because the relationship is new, perhaps because the available names are wrong, perhaps because the right name has not been invented. Use Mark's frame: declare the category currently empty; propose that it remain empty for a specified period; explain why the empty category is more useful than the wrong one would be.
prompt_phrases: The category of what-I-am-to-you is, in my view, currently empty…
I think it should remain empty for at least the next [time period], because the empty category is more useful than the wrong category would be.
I propose this not because I want to formalise the relationship but because I want, in advance, to remove the pressure of finding a name we do not yet require.

content.situation: Saturday morning, twenty past seven. Henry left for the upper field at half past six in his work coat and the boots he keeps by the back door for Geoff Eyre's land. Mark went with him as far as the gate, then came back inside because Geoff prefers, in lambing week, to have only one extra pair of hands at a time, and Henry is the one with the standing arrangement. The kitchen has the particular early-March early-morning quality of a Derbyshire cottage: the Aga ticking, the window over the sink showing pale grey light, the radio on at low volume tuned to a station Henry left on overnight. Mark has made coffee. Sophie has come down at twenty past seven in jeans and one of Henry's old jumpers that he left out for her on the chair by the door. Mark hands her a mug. Neither of them has decided yet how much they want to say to each other this morning. Both of them know that the morning is the one available window of the weekend in which they will be alone together. The dialogue runs forty-three minutes.

phrase_cle.phrase: The empty category is more useful than the wrong category would be.
phrase_cle.example: Don't call it a friendship yet. Don't call it a working relationship either. The empty category is more useful than the wrong category would be.
phrase_cle.explanation: Конструкция, в которой отсутствие имени для отношений или ситуации заявляется как преимущество, а не как недостаток. Это очень характерное для C2 переосмысление: молчание, неназванность, открытость не как пробел, требующий заполнения, а как самостоятельная позиция. Грамматически — сравнительная конструкция 'X is more Y than Z would be', где X — реальное (empty category), а Z — гипотетическое (wrong category) с 'would be' для контрфактического сослагательного. Mark предлагает Sophie оставить их отношения без имени два года, потому что любое имя, выбранное сейчас, навязало бы ожидания, которым отношения не должны соответствовать. Расширения: 'the unanswered question is more useful than the wrong answer would be'; 'the unbuilt structure is more useful than the wrong structure would be'; 'the unspoken assumption is more useful than the spoken-but-wrong one would be'. На C2 ты учишься удерживать пустоту как рабочий инструмент, а не как состояние, которое требует немедленного устранения.
phrase_cle.translation: Пустая категория полезнее, чем была бы неправильная.

expressions.meaning: British idiomatic for 'about to fail'. Used here about a coffee machine but in a register that anthropomorphises the appliance ('returned to the shop, posthumously') — the coffee machine has a life expectancy and a death scene. C2 students should mark the British comic anthropomorphism as a register continuous with Edward's 'sulking kettle' in L1.

Mark's mock-precise prediction that Henry will replace the coffee machine only after it has stopped working entirely, which Mark estimates will happen years from now. The triple modifier (where, how, when) compresses an affectionate diagnosis of Henry's relationship to broken objects.
expressions.expression: Returned to the shop, posthumously, sometime around 2031

expressions.meaning: Sophie's image-borrowing from photography or imaging: lower-resolution versions of her own questions have been running in the background, and Mark's formulations are higher-resolution versions of the same questions. The metaphor is precise and modern; C2 register at full strength.
expressions.expression: I have been asking the same questions of myself in lower-resolution form

expressions.meaning: Sophie's diagnostic for what changes between a first and a second visit: a quality that was being consciously enacted has become structural. The construction 'has been internalised to the point where' uses a present perfect passive followed by a result clause, and is C2-typical for describing developmental shifts.
expressions.expression: The carefulness has been internalised to the point where he no longer has to perform it

expressions.meaning: Sophie's two-part description: Henry was, in April, doing something Sophie did not have the perceptual training to detect at the time. The phrase 'have the equipment to read' converts perception into a tool one does or does not yet possess. C2 register: epistemic vocabulary at conversational ease.
expressions.expression: Slightly performing his own carefulness, in a way I did not, in April, have the equipment to read

expressions.meaning: Mark's proposal that he and Sophie occupy a relationship that currently has no name, and that they leave it nameless rather than impose a wrong name. The principle is C2: nameless relationships can be precise; named relationships must conform to expectations the name brings. The construction echoes Mum's 'category of things-I-can-be-told-about-Henry' from C1 L27, and Sophie names the echo.
expressions.expression: The empty category is more useful than the wrong category would be

expressions.meaning: Mark's acknowledgement that his rhetorical move (naming a category and proposing what fills it) has, unbeknownst to him, been used by Mum in a similar context. The phrase 'the form has been borrowed' converts originality into structural inheritance, and treats it as good news rather than a flaw.
expressions.expression: The form has been borrowed before

content.cultural_note: Mark's mode in this conversation deserves a note for learners. He is performing a register that British literature recognises but rarely names: the considered offer of a non-relationship. Most cultures and most British registers, when faced with two adults newly placed in proximity through a third person, push toward defining the relationship — calling it brotherhood, calling it friendship, calling it stepfamily — because named relationships are easier to operate. Mark refuses the move and explicitly proposes, instead, that the relationship operate without a name for two years. The proposal is generous because it spares Sophie the obligation of accepting or rejecting any of the available names; it is also generous because it leaves the relationship free to develop its own shape without having to fulfil a category. The phrase 'after two years, if there is a need for a name, we can find one' contains the further courtesy: the eventual name, if it arrives, will be theirs to invent. Note also Sophie's recognition that Mark's rhetorical form — naming the category, declaring its current contents — was used by Mum, ten months earlier, in C1 L27. Sophie's pleasure at recognising the form ('That is the most generous thing anyone has said to me about a category since Mum…') is a C2 listening achievement. She is hearing the structural similarity across two contexts and naming it. Mark accepts the inheritance ('Then the form has been borrowed before. I am glad I am in good company.'). C2 register, at its quietest, often consists of these moments of cross-conversation recognition. The course has been preparing for them since A2.
content.grammar_focus: Sophie's central description in this dialogue performs a sustained C2 grammatical operation: free indirect discourse used to render two versions of the same person across two visits. 'The April Henry was a Henry composing himself for a stranger he had hoped, for thirty years, might one day be in his kitchen.' The construction layers past simple ('was'), present continuous-as-attribute ('composing himself'), past perfect ('had hoped'), an embedded temporal phrase ('for thirty years'), modal-conditional ('might one day be'). Five tense or modal forms in one sentence, all serving the description of a single past version of a single person. C2 prose can sustain this kind of compression because the syntactic axis (Henry — composing — for — hoped — might be) holds even as the modifications proliferate. Note also the deliberate contrast between 'the April Henry' and 'the Henry I am seeing this weekend' — definite-article-plus-noun ('the April Henry') treats a temporal version as a quasi-proper-noun, which is a C2 register move borrowed from literary criticism ('the early Wittgenstein', 'the late Yeats'). Mark's offered category — 'the category of what-I-am-to-you is, in my view, currently empty, and I think it should remain empty for at least the next two years' — uses hyphenation to create a substantive ('what-I-am-to-you') that can be the subject of 'is empty', which would otherwise require a much longer noun phrase. C2 students should mark the hyphenation as a written-text technique entering speech: a way to refer to a relationship-category as a compact unit. The closing exchange — 'Then the form has been borrowed before.' / 'It has.' / 'I am glad I am in good company.' — uses three short utterances with present perfect ('has been borrowed') and present simple ('I am glad') to acknowledge inter-textual continuity within the courseworld. Sophie hears the borrowing as inheritance; Mark accepts it as good company. The grammar carries the warmth.

key_vocabulary.note: Британская кухонная плита непрерывного действия (всегда тёплая), стоит в кухнях сельских и пригородных домов среднего класса. Маркер class и lifestyle. Henry's кухня имеет Aga — характерно для его dwelling.
Период массового рождения ягнят на овцеводческих фермах в Дербишире. Конец февраля — начало марта. Geoff Eyre — фермер; Henry помогает соседу-фермеру в этот период.
Mark признаёт, что Sophie задала такой вопрос ('was Hilary funny'), а он дал параграф. C2 регистр самонаблюдения: не каждый вопрос требует длинного ответа, и говорящий это замечает.
Sophie's технологическая метафора: вопрос, существующий в её голове в менее чёткой форме, чем сформулировал Mark. C2 транспозиция.
Sophie's метафора восприятия как набора инструментов. 'I did not, in April, have the equipment to read' — у меня в апреле не было сенсорного аппарата, чтобы это распознать. C2 эпистемический регистр.
Психологический и педагогический глагол: преобразовать внешнее качество во внутреннее свойство. Sophie применяет к Henry's carefulness. C2 регистр.

Идиома: оказаться рядом с уважаемыми / достойными людьми, делая что-то, что могло бы быть voiced критически. Mark говорит: если он повторил структуру речи Mum, это знак, что он движется в правильном направлении.
Философский / классификаторский термин, который и Mum (L27 C1), и Mark (L4 C2) используют для описания неклассифицированных отношений. Recurring courseworld figure.
Mark отказывается от этой рамки в пользу 'two different practices'. C2 различение: не фазы одного процесса, а параллельные практики.
Бюрократический глагол, перенесённый в личный регистр. Mark отказывается формализовать отношения с Sophie именованием. C2 транспозиция.

comprehension_questions.hint: She names two simultaneous shifts: one in him (carefulness moved from foreground to background) and one in her (acquisition of 'the equipment to read'). Why does each shift depend on the other?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie distinguishes between 'the April Henry' and 'the Henry I am seeing this weekend'. What has changed in Henry between visits, and what change in Sophie has made the change visible?

comprehension_questions.hint: He explicitly says: 'I want, in advance, to remove the pressure of finding a name we do not yet require.' What pressure is he removing, and from whom?
comprehension_questions.question: Mark proposes that the category of what-he-is-to-Sophie 'should remain empty for at least the next two years'. What does he gain by naming the emptiness rather than waiting for a name to arrive?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the cultural note's analysis of cross-conversation recognition. What does inheriting a rhetorical form from someone else achieve, and why does Mark welcome the inheritance rather than feeling it as plagiarism?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie recognises that Mark's category-proposal echoes Mum's category-statement from ten months earlier. Why does the recognition matter to her, and how does Mark receive it?

content.title: The Thrush

dialogue.line: If you are quiet for the next thirty seconds, you will see something I have been watching since November. The fact that I have been watching it since November is a private piece of information that I am now telling you, and you may treat it as such or as a piece of natural history; I do not mind which.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: There. The laurel. Lower branch.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: I see it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Song thrush. Last year there were none in the garden. The year before, none. This one came back in November and has, by various indications, decided to stay. I have been keeping notes.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Notes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. In a small notebook that lives by the kitchen window. I record the time, the weather, what it is doing. The notebook is not for any purpose. I do not, at present, have a use for the notebook. Some people might call this a habit; I am not yet ready to call it that, because to call something a habit is to commit to its continuation, and I am, as you know, slightly cautious about commitments to continuation.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Henry.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: It is a beautiful bird.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is. The thrushes have, in the literature, been having a difficult thirty years. The numbers are down, and have been down, for reasons that are, in aggregate, the same reasons everything is down. But this one came back. The fact that one bird came back to one garden is not, statistically, very interesting. It is, however, interesting to me, and the interesting-to-me is, I have decided, a category I am willing to take seriously even if it has no claim on anyone else.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: I should say one thing, and I should say it before you have to start thinking about your train.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I would like, when you are ready and when not before, to know what you have done with the photograph I gave you. I have wanted to ask since October. I did not ask in October because I had no standing to ask in October. I am asking now because we have, between us, accumulated some standing in the intervening five months, and the question is at the lower threshold of what I think the standing entitles me to. If the question is still above the threshold, tell me, and I will retract it without prejudice.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: The question is not above the threshold. I will answer it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Take your time.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: I kept it in a paperback for six months. I opened the paperback once, in October, in Iceland, on a Saturday night when Marie was asleep. I looked at the photograph for forty minutes. I rang Mum about it. Mum said to put it back in the book until I decided what shelf it lived on. I put it back. It has been in the book since. The book is on a shelf in my flat in Manchester, between a Penelope Fitzgerald paperback that has nothing to do with you and a hardback of Bishop's letters that I had been meaning to read.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am telling you all of this because I think the operational detail is what you would have wanted to know. The emotional detail is harder and I am not, today, ready to give it, but the operational detail I can offer in full.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. The operational detail is more than I had asked for, and exactly what I would have asked for had I felt entitled to specify.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: I am, separately, glad that the photograph is between two books rather than alone. Books have what one might call a holding capacity for objects of uncertain status, and I had hoped — though I did not, at the time, articulate the hope — that the book I had pulled from the shelf for you that morning would do that work for some period.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: It has done that work.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Henry.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Can I ask you something, on the same threshold.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Was there a moment, between April and now, when you considered not writing the second letter — not writing to me at all, between visits — and decided against it. And if so, what tipped the decision.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: There were three such moments. I will not enumerate them, because the enumeration would be excessive, but I will tell you about the third, because it is the one in which the deciding-against happened.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It was a Wednesday in early June. I had finished a piece of work on the Robertson cottage roof — Geoff's neighbour, you may not know — and I had come back here for tea, and I had sat at the kitchen table, and I had thought: it has been six weeks since she went; if I do not write within the next week, the not-writing will start to be its own message; the not-writing will start to mean something I do not, in fact, mean. And I considered: do I want to send a message I do not mean, in order to spare her the obligation of a reply. And I decided: no. I would rather she carry the obligation than the false message.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: I wrote that evening. The letter was short. You replied within ten days. Each subsequent letter has been roughly the same length. The interval between letters has, as you may have noticed, been calibrated by you, not by me. I have allowed you to set the rhythm because the rhythm is — in the situation — yours to set.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: I had not noticed that. I notice it now.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Knowing that I have set up the system that way may, or may not, change how you operate it. Either is acceptable to me.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: The thrush has gone.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: I noticed.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It will be back. The pattern is roughly every forty minutes when it is feeding. The pattern is, of course, only a pattern, and may break, but the breaking would itself be information, so.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Henry.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: What are days for.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Larkin.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I do not think I can answer the question. I think the question is the answer. I think days are the units in which we propose to live, and the proposing is what days are for. I do not, today, have a more elegant formulation, and I am not embarrassed by the inelegance. The thrush is back.
dialogue.speaker: Henry

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Imagine you have a question you have wanted to ask someone for a long time, and you are now considering whether you have accumulated enough standing to ask it. Use Henry's frame: name the threshold, declare your judgment about whether the question is at, above, or below it, and offer the other person the option of declining without consequence ('without prejudice').
prompt_phrases: I would like, when you are ready and when not before, to know…
I have wanted to ask since [time]. I did not ask then because I had no standing to ask then.
I am asking now because we have, between us, accumulated some standing in the intervening [period]. If the question is still above the threshold, tell me, and I will retract it without prejudice.

content.situation: Sunday afternoon, half past one. Mark left at ten with an overnight bag and a small wave at the gate; he is back in Sheffield by now. Sophie's train from Bakewell to Sheffield leaves at three forty-three; she will catch the connection to Manchester at five and be home by six-thirty. Henry has, after lunch, taken her into the small back garden of the cottage. The garden is, this March, half-bare and half-thinking-about-it: the apple tree at the back has the first suggestion of bud, the rosemary by the path is unbothered by winter, and Henry has set out two folding chairs on the patch of paving where the morning sun falls in a useful corner. He has, in his hand, a pair of binoculars, which Sophie has not previously seen him carry. He has been watching, since November, a song thrush that returned to nest in the laurel by the fence after an absence of two years. Henry has not mentioned the thrush to Sophie before. He mentions it now.

phrase_cle.phrase: The not-writing will start to be its own message.
phrase_cle.example: If I do not call her by Friday, my not-calling will start to be its own message — a message I do not, in fact, mean.
phrase_cle.explanation: Henry формулирует фундаментальный принцип C2 коммуникации: отсутствие действия в коммуникативном поле в какой-то момент перестаёт быть нейтральным и начинает означать. Грамматически — будущее время с фазовым глаголом 'start to be' и притяжательным 'its own', где 'its' относится к самому not-writing. Hyphenated 'not-writing' превращает отсутствие действия в существительное, способное быть подлежащим. На C2 ты учишься распознавать момент, когда твоё молчание становится высказыванием, и принимать решение в отношении этого: либо ты согласен с сообщением, которое твоё молчание передаст, либо ты должен его прервать. Henry не любит коммуникативные двусмысленности и поэтому пишет даже когда не хочет, чтобы не транслировать неверное сообщение собственным молчанием. Расширения: 'the not-asking will start to be its own message'; 'the not-coming will start to be its own message'; 'the not-replying will start to be its own message'. Каждая конструкция — упражнение в распознавании того, что отсутствие коммуницирует.

expressions.meaning: Henry's verbal flag for a disclosure that has been internal until this moment. The construction does two things at once: it tells Sophie what is being shared, and it grants her the choice of how to receive it ('as such or as a piece of natural history'). C2 register: the speaker openly catalogues the genres in which his disclosure can be heard.
expressions.expression: A private piece of information that I am now telling you

expressions.meaning: Henry's understated phrasing for his reluctance to claim he will keep doing something he has so far been doing. The hyphen-adjacent compound noun ('commitments to continuation') is itself a C2 construction; the qualifier 'slightly cautious' keeps the disclosure in dry register. The phrase has the quality of a self-diagnosis that has been reached carefully.
expressions.expression: Slightly cautious about commitments to continuation

expressions.meaning: Henry's hyphenated noun-phrase ('the interesting-to-me') for the category of personally meaningful preferences whose meaningfulness does not require external validation. The structure is C2-typical: hyphenation creates a substantive that can be the subject of 'is a category'. The argument — that personal interest is sufficient justification for attention — is delivered without sentimentality.
expressions.expression: The interesting-to-me is, I have decided, a category I am willing to take seriously even if it has no claim on anyone else

expressions.meaning: Henry's legal-formal phrasing for withdrawing his question if Sophie indicates it is too much. 'Without prejudice' is a courtroom term meaning that the withdrawal does not preclude raising the matter again later, and does not affect the standing of either party. Henry uses it here as a precision tool: he wants to ensure Sophie that retraction is genuinely available.
expressions.expression: I would retract it without prejudice

expressions.meaning: Henry's geometric image for what he is allowed to ask: a height-of-question metaphor with a threshold below which questions are permitted, above which they are not. 'Standing' is the legal-derived noun (recurring from C1 L10 in Helena's mouth) for whether one has earned the right to a particular conversational move. C2 conceptual vocabulary.
expressions.expression: The lower threshold of what I think the standing entitles me to

expressions.meaning: Henry's image for what books are useful for: the storage of physical objects whose place in one's life has not yet been decided. The term 'holding capacity' is borrowed from engineering or shipping (how much something can store). The phrase 'objects of uncertain status' converts the photograph from 'thing I do not know what to do with' into a category of which there are presumably others. C2 register.
expressions.expression: A holding capacity for objects of uncertain status

expressions.meaning: Henry's diagnostic about the moment at which silence between two correspondents tips from neutral absence into communicative content. Hyphenation again ('not-writing') makes the absence into a substantive that can have a message. Henry's principle: he would rather Sophie carry the obligation of a reply than carry the false message of his silence.
expressions.expression: The not-writing will start to be its own message

expressions.meaning: Henry's signature epistemic stance: pattern is provisional, deviation is not failure but data. The construction reframes broken expectations as continued knowledge. Recurring philosophical position in his speech, here applied to a thrush's feeding schedule.
expressions.expression: The breaking would itself be information

content.cultural_note: Henry's quoting of Philip Larkin in this lesson — 'What are days for.' — without attribution, and Sophie's recognition of it without naming the title, deserves the closest C2 attention. Larkin's poem 'Days', written in 1953 and collected in The Whitsun Weddings (1964), opens with this exact question and offers no answer beyond a continuation: 'They are to be happy in / Where can we live but days?' The poem is one of the most quoted lines in twentieth-century English poetry, and any educated British reader of Henry's generation would recognise it. Henry recognises it; he says only the poet's name. Sophie also recognises it; she says only 'Yes.' Neither completes the line, neither summarises the poem, neither performs the cultural literacy. The recognition is the affection. C2 register, at the upper end of educated British conversation, often consists in this kind of unspoken recognition: a quotation surfaces, both speakers know it, and the knowing is allowed to do its work without commentary. Henry's eventual answer — 'I do not think I can answer the question. I think the question is the answer.' — is both his own thought and a small homage to Larkin's poem, which famously refuses to answer the question it asks. The closing line 'The thrush is back. / Yes.' returns the conversation to the immediate, but the immediate now contains the poem. C2 students should learn that an excellent C2 conversation is often built on shared cultural ground that does not need to be re-laid at every step. The reading list of an educated speaker becomes, over time, a private grammar shared with other educated speakers.
content.grammar_focus: Henry's central narrative reconstruction in this dialogue uses a sustained C2 grammatical move: the past perfect chain in extended sequential thinking. 'I had finished a piece of work on the Robertson cottage roof — Geoff's neighbour, you may not know — and I had come back here for tea, and I had sat at the kitchen table, and I had thought…' Four past perfect clauses chained by 'and', each marking a discrete prior action; the final 'I had thought' opens into reported speech in present tense ('it has been six weeks since she went; if I do not write within the next week, the not-writing will start to be its own message'). The shift from past perfect to present-tense reported thought is C2-precise: it preserves the immediacy of the deliberation, treating the thoughts as still currently true rather than backshifting them. Note also Henry's deployment of the inverted counterfactual: 'exactly what I would have asked for had I felt entitled to specify.' The inversion ('had I felt' instead of 'if I had felt') is the formal-literary form of the third conditional, and Henry uses it without flagging — it is the register he reaches for when describing his own past hypothetical states. C2 students should mark inverted conditionals as the upper-register native form of the standard if-clause. Sophie's direct reported speech inside a question — 'Was there a moment, between April and now, when you considered not writing the second letter — not writing to me at all, between visits — and decided against it.' — uses an interrogative with two parenthetical elaborations and a coordinated past simple ('considered… and decided'). The parentheticals slow the question to give Henry time to locate the moment. C2 register tolerates this kind of mid-question elaboration when both speakers are tracking the architecture together. The closing exchange — 'What are days for.' / 'Larkin.' / 'Yes.' — quotes the opening line of Philip Larkin's poem 'Days' without attribution. Henry recognises the source by name; Sophie confirms by minimal affirmation. C2 reading: the cultural literacy is the affection.

key_vocabulary.note: Turdus philomelos. Британская садовая певчая птица. В 1990-2010 численность сократилась на ~50%, теперь — UK Red List of Birds of Conservation Concern. Henry знает контекст; его внимание к одному дрозду — характерно для образованного британского naturalist'а его поколения.
Лавровое дерево — частое в английских садах вечнозелёное растение, дающее укрытие птицам. У Henry's кухни. Британская домашняя ботаника.
Стандартный аксессуар британского bird-watcher'а. Henry носит их с ноября — деталь, которая показывает, что он развил новый интерес после Sophie's первого визита.
Аналитическое наречие: рассматриваемое как сумма, не отдельные случаи. Henry применяет к причинам спада популяций. Профессиональный британский регистр.
Юридическая формула: заявление, отказ или предложение, сделанное без потери права делать то же самое позже. Henry применяет к гипотетическому отзыву своего вопроса. C2 транспозиция.

Юридический термин: квалификация, дающая лицу право поднять вопрос или предъявить иск. Recurring courseworld figure (Helena C1 L10, Henry C1 L13). На C2 — рабочая лексика для социальных и личных отношений.
Из правовой и журналистской лексики: отозвать публичное заявление. Henry применяет к личному вопросу — формальная transposition.

Elizabeth Bishop (1911-1979), американская поэтесса, чьи письма опубликованы в 'One Art' (1994). Sophie упоминает hardback в своей квартире — маркер её reading life. Larkin и Bishop — два центральных поэта середины XX века в англоязычной литературе.

Philip Larkin (1922-1985), британский поэт. Один из самых читаемых британских поэтов XX века. Стихотворение 'Days' (1953) — короткое (две строфы), известное, начинается строкой 'What are days for?'. Henry распознаёт цитату Sophie.
Henry: 'I will not enumerate them, because the enumeration would be excessive'. Формальный латинизированный глагол. На C2 — рабочая лексика, без саркастической пафосности.

comprehension_questions.hint: He explicitly says she may treat it 'as such or as a piece of natural history'. What pressure would a less framed disclosure have placed on her?
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Henry call the thrush 'a private piece of information' he is now sharing? What does this framing release Sophie from?

comprehension_questions.hint: He says: 'The operational detail is more than I had asked for, and exactly what I would have asked for had I felt entitled to specify.' What does this acknowledge about both Sophie's offering and his own restraint?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie tells Henry the operational detail of where the photograph is, but withholds the emotional detail. How does Henry respond to the partial answer, and what does his response signal about his expectations?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at the cultural note's discussion of unspoken recognition. What does completing the quotation, naming the poem, or summarising it cost? What does the minimal acknowledgement preserve?
comprehension_questions.question: Why does Henry quote Larkin only by surname ('Larkin'), and why does Sophie reply only 'Yes'? What kind of cultural exchange is being performed in this minimal pair?

content.title: The Train Back, Again

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. I am on the train. I have a window seat and a school party at the other end with trumpets.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Tell me one true thing.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Bakewell has its own weather, and the weather this weekend was different from April.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That is more than one true thing. That is a true thing and the entry-point to a longer description. I will accept both. Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: There are two things I am still working out, and a third thing that arrived as a sentence in my head on the platform at Sheffield, which I want to say to you before I lose the wording.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Third thing first.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I had been operating, since April, on the assumption that the goal of repeated visits to Bakewell would, eventually, be to convert the place from a place I notice into a place I am simply in. To make Bakewell invisible. And on Saturday morning Mark, in a kitchen conversation, articulated that exact assumption as his own — he said he had been doing that for ten years — and I heard myself, in response, say: I do not yet know whether the noticing might be the structure rather than the obstacle. And I have been carrying that sentence since.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That is the same sentence I had at Seljalandsfoss. In a different shape.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Is it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. Mine was: there is no previous self to resume; the current self has not yet found her dimensions; the goal is not to return but to discover what the new shape is. Yours is: the visit is not a thing I am trying to make familiar; the visit might be a thing I am supposed to keep noticing, and the noticing is the shape. The two of us are saying the same thing. We are saying that the developmental model — the model in which difficulty resolves into ease, and the resolved state is the goal — is, at least in some cases, wrong. The difficulty is not the entry-fee for ease. The difficulty might be the practice.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I had not heard the parallel.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am not surprised. The parallel would not be visible without me on the line. That is what telephones are for, on Sunday evenings, when one party is on a train.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Right. The first two things.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: First. Henry has been keeping a notebook about a song thrush since November, which is a piece of information he disclosed to me this afternoon as if it were embarrassing, and which I find — I find unexpectedly affecting. The thrush is not, for him, a metaphor for anything. The thrush is a thrush. The fact that he has been writing down its movements for four months without telling anyone, and the fact that he chose to tell me today, are, between them, evidence of a particular kind of trust whose name I do not have.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The trust is the showing. The showing is the trust. He gave you, this afternoon, the same kind of object he gave you in October — a private practice that has been kept, in his case, in a drawer or a notebook rather than in a wallet — and his giving it suggests that he has decided you are, now, the kind of person to whom such things can be shown. That is not a small judgement, on his part. That is, I would think, a structural judgement.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I had not framed it that way.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. Second thing.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Second. Mark proposed, on Saturday morning, that the category of what-he-is-to-me should remain empty for at least two years, on the basis that the empty category is more useful than the wrong category would be. He said it carefully and at length; I will not reproduce the speech. I accepted the proposal. I noticed, while accepting it, that the form of his proposal — the naming of a category and the declaration of what it should currently contain — was the form Mum had used in October about Henry. I told Mark this. Mark said: 'Then the form has been borrowed before. I am glad I am in good company.'
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Marie is, separately, glad.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Why are you glad.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because I have been quietly worried, since you told me Mark would be there, that the configuration would oblige you to perform a relationship you had not yet had time to think about. The empty-category solution is the right solution; the fact that Mark proposed it is more reassuring to me than if you had had to propose it yourself; and the fact that he proposed it in a form your mother had also used, ten months earlier, suggests that the people who have been chosen, by various paths, to be in your life share a working register. Which means you are not alone in maintaining it. Which means the labour of being you, in the next twenty years, will be slightly distributed.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Will you tell me one true thing about your week before I lose the line at Stockport.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Olivia walked seventeen consecutive steps on Tuesday in the kitchen, fell into Tom, and laughed in a way that suggested the falling was the point and the walking was the pretext, which has, since Tuesday, made me reconsider some of my own foundational assumptions about how to do things.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That is more than one true thing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am following your example. The first true thing is also the entry-point to a longer description.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I will hear the longer description on Friday. I am coming down.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Bring wine. Bring the question about noticing-as-practice. We can entertain it on the sofa.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: The clarity does, in fact, travel.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It does. Stockport in two minutes. Go and lose the line.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Imagine a phone call with someone close to you at the end of a weekend during which you have had several small recognitions. Use the dialogue's structure: offer one true thing as the entry-point; let the listener identify any parallels with your own previous insights; surface two further things and let the listener reframe them; and ask, in return, for the one true thing about the listener's week.
prompt_phrases: Tell me one true thing.
That is a true thing and the entry-point to a longer description. Go on.
I had not framed it that way.
I am following your example. The first true thing is also the entry-point to a longer description.

content.situation: Sunday evening, twenty past five. Sophie is on the train from Sheffield to Manchester, the same kind of carriage in which she rang Marie ten months ago coming back from her first Bakewell visit. The carriage is fuller than it was that April Sunday — half a school party returning from a weekend music competition in Sheffield, the back of the carriage punctuated by trumpet cases and the exhausted parents of fifteen-year-olds. Sophie has a window seat in the quiet end. The light is going. Marie rings as the train pulls out of Sheffield; Olivia, now eighteen months, is at her grandmother's for the night, and Marie has thirty unmediated minutes she is offering to Sophie. Sophie has been expecting the call and has not been expecting the call; she finds, when the phone vibrates, that she has been holding the question 'how am I' at one remove for the last four hours, and Marie is the person from whom the question can finally be asked.

phrase_cle.phrase: The difficulty is not the entry-fee for ease. The difficulty might be the practice.
phrase_cle.example: I keep waiting for the writing to get easier. Maybe it doesn't. Maybe the difficulty isn't the entry-fee for ease — maybe the difficulty is the practice.
phrase_cle.explanation: Marie формулирует фундаментальный сдвиг от линейной модели развития (сложность → лёгкость, как фазы) к структурной модели практики (сложность как постоянная форма активности). Грамматически — две короткие конструкции через противопоставление: отрицание одной модели ('not the entry-fee for ease') и осторожное утверждение альтернативной ('might be the practice'). 'Entry-fee' — экономическая метафора: цена, которую платят, чтобы попасть куда-то лучше; Marie отказывается от этой схемы. На C2 ты учишься не торопиться с переходом от трудного состояния к лёгкому: иногда трудность — не временная фаза, а сама форма деятельности, и попытка её преодолеть оказывается попыткой выйти из деятельности. Расширения: 'the uncertainty is not the price of arrival, the uncertainty is the road'; 'the asking is not the price of knowing, the asking is the knowledge'; 'the noticing is not the price of being-here, the noticing is being-here'. Каждая конструкция — отказ от трансакционной модели опыта в пользу процессуальной.
phrase_cle.translation: Сложность — не входная плата за лёгкость. Сложность, возможно, и есть практика.

expressions.meaning: Marie's reframing of Sophie's 'one true thing' answer: rather than treating it as a complete reply, she identifies it as the doorway through which a longer account can enter. The construction 'X is the entry-point to Y' is C2-typical for converting a small offering into the start of something larger without forcing the larger thing.
expressions.expression: The entry-point to a longer description

expressions.meaning: Marie's diagnosis that Sophie's articulation about Bakewell and her own articulation at Seljalandsfoss in October are the same proposition, expressed in different vocabularies. The construction makes the underlying claim visible across two different stories, which is the friend's specific contribution: pattern-recognition across the other's experiences.
expressions.expression: The same sentence in a different shape

expressions.meaning: Marie's compressed reframing of the developmental model: difficulty as a price one pays to reach the easier later state, versus difficulty as the ongoing form of the activity. The two-sentence structure (refusal of the first model, suggestion of the second) is C2-typical: name what one is rejecting, then offer the alternative.
expressions.expression: The difficulty is not the entry-fee for ease. The difficulty might be the practice.

expressions.meaning: Marie's chiasmus naming the structural identity of two acts that might be assumed to be sequential: trust does not precede showing, and showing is not the consequence of trust; they are the same act named twice. C2 register: the willingness to refuse causal sequencing in favour of structural identity.
expressions.expression: The trust is the showing. The showing is the trust.

expressions.meaning: Sophie's signal that the kind of trust Henry's disclosure represents is not in her current vocabulary as a named category. The construction allows her to refer to the thing without misnaming it, and is C2-precise: it preserves the unidentified quality rather than rounding it to the nearest available label.
expressions.expression: A particular kind of trust whose name I do not have

expressions.meaning: Marie's image for what it means that Mum and Mark share a register that supports Sophie's particular way of being. The phrase 'labour of being you' converts identity into work; 'slightly distributed' makes the work shareable. C2 register: economic vocabulary deployed for emotional infrastructure.
expressions.expression: The labour of being you, in the next twenty years, will be slightly distributed

expressions.meaning: Marie's reuse of her own verb from C1 L20 ('I cannot afford to entertain it'), here with permission granted: the question that could not be entertained then can be entertained now, in a domestic register. The verb 'entertain' (to give a question hospitality) is recurring courseworld figure.
expressions.expression: We can entertain it on the sofa

expressions.meaning: Marie's instruction-as-permission for the train to take Sophie out of phone signal. The phrase converts an inevitability ('the line will be lost at Stockport') into a small command, which is the British close-friendship register's recurring move: turning operational fact into affectionate instruction. Recurring across the courseworld.

content.cultural_note: This dialogue closes Block 1 with a particular British close-friendship move that the course has developed across the levels: the phone call as parallel diagnosis. Marie does not interpret Sophie's weekend; she places it next to her own October recognition at Seljalandsfoss and lets Sophie hear the two articulations as the same proposition expressed in different vocabularies. The intellectual labour of the call — finding the parallel, naming it, releasing it — is Marie's specific contribution to the friendship, and it is recognisable as a particular form of British intelligence: the pattern-recognition that respects each speaker's experience as primary while also seeing the shared structure underneath. Note the closing formula 'The clarity does, in fact, travel' as a near-quotation of C1 L26's 'The clarity travels'. The added 'does, in fact' is C2-precise: it converts the original assertion into a confirmed report. Sophie has tested the proposition over five months — across Iceland and Manchester and Bakewell — and is now reporting that the proposition has held. Marie's 'It does' is the appropriate two-word receipt. The instruction 'Go and lose the line' performs the closure-by-operation move that has now appeared in roughly a dozen lessons across C1 and C2: the difficult conversation is concluded by a small operational instruction that returns both speakers to the practical world. C2 students should hear the formula as one of the courseworld's reliable structural devices, and learn to deploy it themselves: when a serious conversation has done its work, conclude with a concrete next step rather than a summary. The summary risks tidying; the next step preserves the open texture.
content.grammar_focus: Marie's central diagnostic in this dialogue uses a C2 grammatical construction that rewards close attention: the parallel articulation of two different speakers' insights through structural equation. 'Mine was: there is no previous self to resume; the current self has not yet found her dimensions; the goal is not to return but to discover what the new shape is. Yours is: the visit is not a thing I am trying to make familiar; the visit might be a thing I am supposed to keep noticing, and the noticing is the shape.' Two semicolon-separated triples, each introduced by a possessive ('Mine was:' / 'Yours is:'), each containing three clauses in present tense. The grammatical parallelism enacts the conceptual parallelism. C2 prose can use this kind of architectural repetition to produce a recognition that would, in flatter syntax, be a deduction. Note also Marie's self-naming move: 'Marie is, separately, glad.' Third-person singular reference to oneself, which in many registers would sound stilted, here works because Marie has earned the comic distance through long practice in the courseworld. The third-person self-reference is C2-typical when the speaker is performing a small registered emotion she does not wish to over-claim. Sophie's question — 'Will you tell me one true thing about your week before I lose the line at Stockport.' — uses a request structure with embedded temporal anchor, the 'before' clause specifying a finite window. C2 register tolerates the temporal compression because both speakers are tracking the route. Marie's answer — 'Olivia walked seventeen consecutive steps on Tuesday in the kitchen, fell into Tom, and laughed in a way that suggested the falling was the point and the walking was the pretext' — uses past simple sequence ('walked, fell, laughed') with a final relative clause containing a 'that'-complement reporting Olivia's apparent intention. The construction 'in a way that suggested X' is C2-typical for attributing intention to an action through observed manner. The closing exchange — 'The clarity does, in fact, travel.' / 'It does.' — quotes the C1 L26 conversation with a slight grammatical addition ('does, in fact'), and Marie's two-word affirmation closes the inheritance. C2 students should hear the present-perfect-of-borrowing across one's own life: things one said before become things one is now confirming.

key_vocabulary.note: Школьная музыкальная группа возвращается с соревнования. Британская деталь поездов в воскресные вечера: school parties, обременённые инструментами и усталыми родителями.
Идиома: не напрямую, через дистанцию. Sophie 'has been holding the question how-am-I at one remove' — не сама себе задавала, не отвечала, держала на расстоянии. C2 идиоматический регистр.
Marie has 'thirty unmediated minutes' — без Olivia, без посредничества материнства. Слово из медиа-теории, перенесённое в личную лексику. C2 транспозиция.
Marie's термин для структурного сходства двух ситуаций. 'I had not heard the parallel.' Recurring courseworld figure: видение параллели — отдельный когнитивный акт.
Психологический и педагогический термин: модель, в которой состояния следуют друг за другом по фазам. Marie его называет, чтобы отвергнуть. C2 рабочая лексика.
Экономическая метафора, которую Marie применяет к опыту: difficulty as the price you pay to access ease. Затем она отказывается от этой модели.
Marie's термин для решения, которое касается базовой архитектуры отношений, а не их содержания. Henry's выбор показать дрозда — structural judgement о том, кем стала Sophie.

Recurring courseworld figure (Marie C1 L20: 'cannot afford to entertain it'). На C2 — реабилитация: 'we can entertain it on the sofa'. Глагол 'entertain' = give hospitality to a guest; применённый к вопросу — give it space without committing to act on it.
Британская железнодорожная норма. На участке Sheffield-Manchester сигнал часто пропадает в районе Стокпорта — это знают регулярные пользователи маршрута.
Marie описывает Olivia: семнадцать consecutive steps. Слово 'consecutive' — точное; не 'in a row' (разговорное). На C2 — стандарт.

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at her two formulations side by side. What model is being rejected, and what alternative is being offered? Why is naming the parallel a specifically friend-shaped contribution?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie identifies Sophie's articulation about Bakewell as 'the same sentence I had at Seljalandsfoss. In a different shape.' What is the proposition both women are articulating, and what does Marie gain by naming it as shared rather than coincidental?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is refusing the model in which trust precedes showing or in which showing produces trust. What model is she offering instead?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie says, of Henry's disclosure of the thrush notebook: 'The trust is the showing. The showing is the trust.' What does this chiasmus claim about the relationship between Henry's act and the quality his act demonstrates?

comprehension_questions.hint: The 'in fact' converts a claim into a confirmed report. What has Sophie's experience between Iceland and now demonstrated about the original sentence?
comprehension_questions.question: The dialogue ends with Sophie saying 'The clarity does, in fact, travel' — a slight grammatical revision of Marie's original assertion in C1 L26. Why does Sophie now confirm the proposition with the added 'does, in fact', and what has been tested in the intervening five months?

content.title: Tenancy

dialogue.line: Right. I have a proposition. Before I make it, I want to do three things.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: First. I want to acknowledge that your appointment-line on the diary said 'tenancy', and that you have, for at least twenty-four hours, been holding the word at one remove. I am sorry about the diary line. The diary line was a clerical decision that I had not, when it was made, intended to use as advance disclosure. I would have preferred to deliver this in a less telegraphed form. The telegraphing has happened, and I can only apologise.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Edward, the telegraphing was, in retrospect, a kindness. I have had a day to consider rather than a moment.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Second. The proposition is, in form, an offer of tenancy at Wellgate Chambers, with effect from the first of October, on the standard terms which I will leave with you in this folder. The proposition is, in substance, a question I am putting to you about whether you wish to bind yourself, at this point in your career, to this set of rooms, this group of colleagues, and this particular kind of work for what is, in all honesty, likely to be the next twenty-five years.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I distinguish form from substance because the form would suggest a binary answer and the substance does not. The substance permits, and indeed requires, a more considered reply than the form alone would extract.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Third. I want to tell you, before you reply to anything, what I would not have done. I would not have made this offer in March. I had been minded to wait until June, on the grounds that twelve months is the conventional minimum and I am, on principle, suspicious of conventional minima. The reason I have brought it forward to April is unrelated to your performance — your performance has been at the level I expected — and is, instead, related to a piece of information about my own situation that I will share with you at the appropriate stage of this conversation, but not yet. I mention the unrelatedness because I do not want you to think that the offer is a reward, or that it has been brought forward because you have, in some sense, earned an acceleration. The offer is not a reward. The offer is a working arrangement that I am proposing because, on examination, the working arrangement makes sense.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I want to say one thing before you continue.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I appreciate the three preliminary moves. I appreciate them not because they are kind — though they are — but because they make it possible for me to receive the proposition without spending the next hour decoding subtext I would otherwise be obliged to decode. The decoding would be a distraction. The absence of subtext is, in this room, the substantive courtesy.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Then I will continue without subtext.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Please.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What I am offering is the standard package. Two-thirds share of receipts on your own work after chambers expenses, with a five-year glide on the expenses formula that protects junior tenants in the early period. A vote at chambers meetings, with the customary expectation that you will not, in the first three years, vote against any motion proposed by silk unless the motion is, in your judgement, structurally wrong, in which case voting against is — encouraged. Use of the larger room on the third floor when Marsden retires in November, which by my estimate will be the first time in your career you have a window that opens in both directions.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: What I am, separately, asking is whether you have, in the eleven months since you joined, formed a view of this place that makes a twenty-five-year arrangement plausible to you. I am not asking whether you are ready to commit. I am asking whether the kind of commitment is, in principle, the kind you can imagine yourself making to a place of this sort. Were the answer no, the answer would be useful information, and would lead us to a different conversation.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: It is the kind.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I will not give you the binary answer today. I would like — by the standard formula, I think it is two weeks — to consider the substance, as you put it, rather than the form. I would also like, before I commit to anything that runs to 2052, to have one further conversation with you about the piece of information about your own situation that you mentioned. I do not require it now. I require it before I sign.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That is reasonable. The information I am withholding is a six-month sabbatical I am taking from October to March. It is not, I should clarify, in any way medical. It is a period I have been planning since Christmas to spend at the cottage in Argyll my late wife's family kept, doing — not nothing, but considerably less than I do here. The sabbatical is the reason I have brought the offer forward. I would prefer that you accept tenancy before I leave, so that the chambers has the configuration settled before my absence rather than during it.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I have not yet announced this. I will, on Friday, to the rest of chambers.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Edward.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Thank you for telling me before Friday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It would have been operationally indefensible to ask you to bind yourself to a place without telling you that the head of chambers was about to be away from it for a third of your first year as tenant. Not telling you was not an option I considered.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Two weeks from today.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Two weeks from today.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I will send the answer by email rather than ask for a meeting. The meeting would be ceremonial, and I think we have, between us, a slight mutual aversion to the ceremonial.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: We do.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Right.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The folder is yours. The window is yours from November. The vote is yours from October. The decision is yours, and remains yours, until the fourteenth at noon, after which I will assume — only because the formula requires that I assume something — that silence indicates the negative. In practice I will simply ring you on the fifteenth if I have not heard.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Edward.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I will not let it run to the fifteenth.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I had assumed.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

pratique.scenario: Imagine you are receiving a significant professional offer that requires you to commit for a long time. Use Edward's preliminary architecture: distinguish form from substance, request — or offer — a clear time window for considering, ask for any withheld information you require before signing, and decline the binary answer in favour of the considered reply.
prompt_phrases: I will not give you the binary answer today.
I distinguish form from substance because the form would suggest X and the substance does not.
Before I commit to anything that runs to [date], I require [information]. I do not require it now. I require it before I sign.

content.situation: Tuesday, late April. Six weeks since Bakewell. The grandmother witness's contradiction has, as Edward predicted, surfaced on the third reading; the case settled before the hearing, which is what tends to happen when a witness's account fails its own pressure test. Edward has been travelling — two weeks in Glasgow on a complex appeal — and is back in Manchester since Friday. He has called Sophie into his office at twenty past two on a Tuesday. The appointment is on the diary as 'tenancy' which Sophie has, since Monday, been treating as a piece of information whose meaning she does not yet have. Edward's office has the slightly recovering quality of a room someone has just returned to: papers in stacks that have not yet been re-sorted, a coat draped over the visitor's chair, the window propped at the angle Edward prefers when the radiator is too vigorous. He moves the coat. He gestures Sophie to the chair. He does not, today, offer coffee. He has brought a folder.

phrase_cle.example: What you said was, in form, a question. In substance, it was a request. I distinguish form from substance because the answer to a question is different from the answer to a request.
phrase_cle.explanation: Эта формула — рабочий C2-инструмент для коммуникации, в которой одно высказывание делает несколько разных вещей одновременно. Грамматически — простое утверждение present simple с предлогом 'from' (не 'and'): 'distinguish X from Y' предполагает, что X и Y связаны, но различны. На C2 ты учишься признавать, что многие реплики имеют двойную природу — официальную форму и фактический смысл — и что хорошая коммуникация требует ясности на обоих уровнях. Edward использует эту формулу, чтобы освободить Sophie от ловушки немедленного бинарного ответа: предложение в форме (тенант­шип, да или нет) — но в содержании это вопрос о двадцатипятилетнем выборе образа жизни, на который бинарный ответ был бы оскорблением. Расширения: 'I distinguish description from prescription'; 'I distinguish observation from accusation'; 'I distinguish disclosure from request'. Каждый раз говорящий называет два регистра одного высказывания и предупреждает слушателя, чтобы тот выбрал, на каком из них отвечать.

expressions.meaning: Edward's professional move: he separates the surface category of his communication ('an offer of tenancy') from the underlying request it represents ('a question about whether you wish to bind yourself'). The form-substance distinction is legal and philosophical vocabulary deployed conversationally; Edward uses it to shape Sophie's reply space.

Edward's diagnosis of what Sophie has been doing with the diary entry 'tenancy' since Monday: keeping the word in view but not yet engaging it as a proposition addressed to her. The phrase 'at one remove' (recurring courseworld figure, see L6) names a particular cognitive stance — adjacent without absorbed.
expressions.expression: Holding the word at one remove

expressions.meaning: Edward's use of the formal Latinate plural ('minima') to refer to industry standards he distrusts as defaults. The construction allows him to appear to respect the convention while signalling his independence from it. C2 register at full strength: the willingness to deploy an unusual plural in casual professional speech.

Sophie's two-step appreciation of Edward's pre-emptive transparency. She names what she would otherwise have to do (decode subtext), reclassifies its absence as the actual courtesy, and locates the courtesy specifically in this room. The phrase 'substantive courtesy' (as distinct from formal courtesy) is C2-precise: courtesy that does work, not courtesy that performs.
expressions.expression: The decoding would be a distraction. The absence of subtext is, in this room, the substantive courtesy.

expressions.meaning: Edward's compressed verdict on the alternative he rejected — letting Sophie commit to tenancy without disclosing his sabbatical. The phrase converts a question of professional courtesy into a question of professional structure, which is the genre Edward operates in. C2 register: ethics rendered as operational logic.

Edward's careful question structure: he is not asking for the commitment, he is asking whether the category of commitment is, abstractly, available to her. The phrase isolates the question from the answer and gives Sophie a usable formal handle. C2 modal-conditional reasoning at conversational ease.
expressions.expression: The kind of commitment is, in principle, the kind you can imagine yourself making

expressions.meaning: Edward's slightly archaic British construction for a former intention now revised. 'Minded' (past participle as adjective) belongs to formal English, often legal and parliamentary; 'I had been minded to' is the past perfect of the construction, marking the intention as previously held but not currently maintained.
expressions.expression: I had been minded to wait

expressions.meaning: Chambers vocabulary: a graduated rather than abrupt schedule of expense-share contribution for new tenants. 'Glide' is borrowed from finance and aviation; in chambers it means a soft transitional curve rather than a step function. C2 professional jargon.
expressions.expression: A glide on the expenses formula

content.cultural_note: The English barrister's tenancy is not equivalent to a partnership in a solicitors' firm or to a corporate promotion, and the cultural note here matters for any C2 learner reading legal English. Barristers in England and Wales are, formally, self-employed practitioners who share premises and administrative resources within a chambers; tenancy means a long-term seat at that table, with a share of expenses and the right to take work. It is not a salaried position and the chambers does not employ the tenant. The closest American equivalent might be a partnership in a small specialised law firm, but the structural independence is stronger in chambers. Edward's offer, accordingly, is not an offer of employment; it is an invitation to join a long-term professional cooperative on terms that will, if accepted, structure twenty or thirty years of Sophie's working life. The reference to Marsden's window is a chambers detail: senior tenants accumulate the better rooms over time, and a tenant becoming entitled to a particular office is a small marker of having arrived. Note also Edward's reference to his Argyll cottage and his 'late wife's family' — Edward has, until this lesson, been a working figure without disclosed personal context. The disclosure is small and precise: he does not say his wife died, he does not say when, he gives the geography only as a matter of where the cottage is. C2 register, at this level, often discloses by gesture rather than by speech. The fact of bereavement is communicated by the adjective 'late'; the duration is left unspecified; the relationship to the cottage is family-by-marriage rather than personally Edward's. C2 students should learn to read this kind of disclosure: a single adjective can carry a decade of context that no one is being asked to dwell on.
content.grammar_focus: Edward's central proposition uses one of the most demanding C2 grammatical constructions: the cleft sentence in compound form.
