'What I am offering is the standard package… What I am, separately, asking is whether you have, in the eleven months since you joined, formed a view of this place that makes a twenty-five-year arrangement plausible to you.' Two parallel wh-clefts ('What I am offering is X' / 'What I am, separately, asking is Y'), each followed by a long predicate that holds the substantive content. The cleft structure foregrounds Edward's two distinct speech acts (offering, asking) and signals that they are different operations even though they belong to the same conversation. C2 students should mark the wh-cleft as a precision tool for separating multiple things one is doing in a single utterance. Note also Edward's use of the inverted counterfactual at high formal register: 'Were the answer no, the answer would be useful information.' The inversion ('were the answer' instead of 'if the answer were') is the upper-formal form of the second conditional, used here to demonstrate that even the negative outcome has been considered and accepted. Edward's first preliminary move uses a sustained past-perfect construction with embedded conditional negation: 'I had not, when it was made, intended to use as advance disclosure.' The intercalated temporal clause ('when it was made') splits the past-perfect verb phrase, which is a C2 syntactic move that requires the listener to hold the predicate open across the interruption. Sophie's reply uses a combined cleft and reverse-cleft: 'The decoding would be a distraction. The absence of subtext is, in this room, the substantive courtesy.' The first sentence is plain SVO; the second is a reverse-cleft equivalent ('The absence of subtext is X' rather than 'It is the absence of subtext that is X'), with a fronted parenthetical adverbial ('in this room') that locates the substantive claim. The closing exchange — 'I will not let it run to the fifteenth.' / 'I had assumed.' — uses Sophie's future negative and Edward's past-perfect of inferred prior assumption. The grammar carries the entire texture of mutual professional respect.

key_vocabulary.note: В английской системе barristers — постоянное членство в chambers, с долей расходов и правом брать дела. Не employment, не partnership в обычном смысле. Edward предлагает Sophie именно это, не положение в firm.
key_vocabulary.translation: тенант­шип, постоянное место в адвокатской конторе

key_vocabulary.note: Recurring from L1. Edward's предсказание о the grandmother witness's contradiction surfaced на третьем чтении — реализовалось. Профессиональный британский регистр.
Литературный и драматургический термин: то, что не сказано, но подразумевается. Sophie использует его про коммуникацию Edward — он отказался от subtext, и она это ценит. C2 транспозиция.
Финансовая структура chambers: каждый barrister получает свою часть от оплаты за свою работу, минус общие chambers expenses. 'Two-thirds share' для junior tenant — стандарт.
Recurring from L19 C1. KC носят шёлковые мантии. 'A motion proposed by silk' = предложение, выдвинутое старшим барристером.
Edward's термин для решения, которое не просто нежелательно, а нарушает базовую архитектуру chambers. C2 профессиональный регистр.
Латинское заимствование: длительный перерыв в работе для отдыха, исследований, или путешествий. Чаще встречается в академической среде, но используется и юристами высокого ранга.
Гористая прибрежная область с lochs (фьордами) и небольшими прибрежными деревнями. Cottage в Argyll — типичная британская middle-class дача, часто наследуемая через семью.
Британское формальное прилагательное для умершего человека. Edward говорит 'my late wife' — констатирует факт без распространения. C2 регистр: одно прилагательное несёт целый контекст.
Sophie различает два режима встреч: один с символическим весом, другой с рабочим. Она выбирает email вместо meeting именно потому, что не хочет ceremonial. C2 регистр.

comprehension_questions.hint: He apologises for the diary line, separates form from substance, and disavows the offer-as-reward framing. What is being cleared away, and what does its clearing make possible?
comprehension_questions.question: Edward begins with three preliminary moves before making his proposition. What is each move doing, and why does he name them as preliminary?

comprehension_questions.hint: Look at what Sophie would otherwise be doing for the next hour, and what she is now able to do instead.
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie says: 'The decoding would be a distraction. The absence of subtext is, in this room, the substantive courtesy.' What does she gain from Edward's transparency, and why is the gain specifically substantive rather than merely pleasant?

comprehension_questions.hint: He could have led with the sabbatical. He didn't. Why does he treat the disclosure as part of the bargain Sophie negotiates, rather than as initial information?
comprehension_questions.question: Edward chooses to reveal his sabbatical only when Sophie asks for the withheld information. What does this sequence — withhold first, disclose on request — protect or accomplish?

content.title: Cammell Laird

dialogue.line: Soph. Are you working.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I am at the kitchen table looking at a folder. Whether that is working depends on what I do in the next ten minutes. Why.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have had a piece of news. I want to tell you it before I tell anyone else, because if I tell Tom first he will be sensible about it and I do not, at this stage, want sensible. I want — I want to hear the news in my own voice and have somebody on the other end who is not going to immediately try to be helpful.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Tell me. I will not be helpful.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: An email arrived at seven thirty-two from a partnership called Renwick and Soames. They are an architectural practice in Liverpool that does — what they do is not the point — and they are, as part of a consortium led by Renwick, the lead designers on the redevelopment of the old Cammell Laird shipyard at Birkenhead. The redevelopment is real; it has been moving for three years; the funding came through the Liverpool City Region in February. They are building a visitor centre, a research archive, and a cultural building on the site. They have been looking, for six months, for a brand and identity studio for the whole programme. They have, this morning, invited my studio to compete for the work. The competition is two stages. The first stage is open to four invited studios. The first stage is paid. The second stage is two studios. The contract, if won, runs for three years.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I am here. I am being not-helpful by saying nothing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. I will continue.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Continue.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The reason it is me — the reason my studio, which is two people including me — is on the invited list, is the architectural identity I did for Lyon and Hooke last summer. The principal at Renwick has, it transpires, been quietly watching that work since November. He has, the email says, been waiting until the procurement framework was settled before reaching out. The reaching-out has now happened. The deadline for the first-stage submission is the eighteenth of June.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Soph, the studio is not the right size for this. I am not the right size for this. The studio is one and a half people, and the contract, if won, would require me to be — I would have to be at minimum five people, possibly six, possibly more, with a project manager and an account director and a senior designer and a junior designer and a production assistant and somebody I have not yet imagined. I would have to lease an additional floor in the building. I would have to do the kind of recruitment I have, until this morning, considered theoretical.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What would you like me to do, in the next twenty minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I would like you not to advise me. I would like you not to congratulate me, because I have not won anything and I am not yet at the stage of accepting the invitation, and the congratulating would be premature. I would like you to ask me a particular kind of question that I cannot, this morning, formulate for myself.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What kind of question.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The kind that — the kind that you asked me about returning to work, in June, when I had been at the drawing board for forty minutes and made no marks. The question that reframed what I thought the problem was. I am not asking you to know in advance what the question will be. I am asking you to start asking, and the right one will, eventually, be among them.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: All right.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Question one. What is the version of you that runs a six-person studio. Have you met her.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have not met her. I am — I am suspicious of her. I do not know whether she would be a person I would want to be.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Question two. If you do not enter the competition, what happens to the version of you that you are now.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: She continues. She runs a one-and-a-half-person studio doing slightly larger logotypes than she was doing two years ago. She has more confident relationships with three architectural clients. She does not, in the next ten years, do work at the scale of Cammell Laird. The shipyard is — Soph, the shipyard is the largest brownfield regeneration in the north of England this decade. The work, if won, would be visible. It would be in the press. It would be in books. The version of me that says no to this is the version that — that decided, at some level, that the next ten years of her practice are going to be at this scale rather than another one.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Question three. Is there a version of entering that does not commit you to the larger version of yourself.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: There might be. I could enter the first stage as a two-person studio with a clear plan to scale on win, and the panel might prefer that to a studio that has scaled on speculation. The competition is judged on the work, and on the credibility of the team. Credibility does not, necessarily, require existing scale. It requires demonstrable plan-to-scale.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I notice I have asked you three questions and you have answered them at lengths that suggest you have been thinking about them this morning.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have been thinking about them since seven thirty-two. I had not asked them in this order or this form. Hearing you ask them has — it has changed which order they are in, and which order is the one I can act on.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Soph. Do you have time to tell me your folder.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I do.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I would like to hear about it. Not because I am trading. Because I have, in the last fifteen minutes, become a person whose curiosity about your folder has returned to its proper size. The size of curiosity I had this morning, before seven thirty-two, was — diminished. The seven thirty-two diminished it. Asking your three questions has restored it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: The folder is an offer of tenancy at Wellgate, with effect from October, for the next twenty-five years.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: We are, this morning, two women on the phone receiving offers of permanent membership in our respective fields, on the same Wednesday in April, for futures that run roughly to the same year.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: We are.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am going to make tea. Then I am going to think about this for the rest of the morning. We will speak on Saturday.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Saturday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. And Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Thank you for not being helpful.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It was the work I knew how to do.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Imagine someone close to you rings with significant professional news that they are not yet ready to celebrate, decide on, or be advised about. Use Sophie's restraint: name what you will not do (advise, congratulate, sensibly help), ask what would actually be useful, and offer a different kind of help when invited.
prompt_phrases: I will not be helpful.
What would you like me to do, in the next twenty minutes?
I would like to hear about it. Not because I am trading. Because [the proper-size of curiosity has returned]…

content.situation: Wednesday morning, the day after Edward's offer. Sophie has not yet told Marie. Marie rings at twenty past nine from the studio; Olivia is at nursery for the morning, three days a week now, and Wednesday is one of the days. Sophie is at her kitchen table with the tenancy folder unopened beside her laptop. Marie's voice on the line has a quality Sophie has not heard since before Olivia — a specific kind of held attention that belongs to the part of Marie that is, despite the eighteen months of motherhood, still primarily a designer. Marie is not calling about Olivia. Marie is calling about a piece of professional news that arrived in her inbox at half past seven this morning and that she has been holding, between then and now, as a thing she could not yet absorb.

phrase_cle.phrase: Thank you for not being helpful.
phrase_cle.example: I rang my brother because I needed to think out loud and not be solved. He listened and asked three questions. Thank you for not being helpful.
phrase_cle.explanation: Парадоксальная конструкция, в которой благодарность выражается за отсутствие конкретного типа поведения. Грамматически — present participle в gerund-форме после предлога 'for' с отрицательной частицей: 'not being helpful'. На C2 ты учишься различать виды поддержки, и распознавать момент, когда самая нужная поддержка — это явное удержание от того, что в обычной коммуникации считается полезным: совета, утешения, оптимистических переформулировок. Когда человек звонит, чтобы услышать собственные мысли в чужом ушах, активная помощь часто становится помехой. Marie запрашивает у Sophie именно отказ от обычного режима помощи; Sophie его обеспечивает; благодарность направлена на этот отказ, не на содержание реакции. Расширения: 'thank you for not advising me'; 'thank you for not making it easier'; 'thank you for not congratulating me yet'. Каждая конструкция — точное называние того, что было удержано, и почему это удержание ценно.

expressions.meaning: Marie's mid-sentence verb-conversion: 'sensible' (adjective) deployed as the object of 'want' makes a quality into a category of response, which Marie can refuse. The construction is C2-typical: a quality becomes a noun through its position, allowing her to identify the genre of help she does not, this morning, want.
expressions.expression: I do not, at this stage, want sensible

expressions.meaning: Marie's specific request, signalling that she has identified a particular failure mode of caring listeners — the rush to usefulness that overrides the value of being heard. The construction 'somebody who is not going to immediately try to be helpful' uses three modifiers (going to, immediately, try to) that together mark the specific behaviour she is excluding.
expressions.expression: Have somebody on the other end who is not going to immediately try to be helpful

expressions.meaning: Marie's hyphenated noun-phrase converting Renwick's act of contacting her into a substantive event. The hyphenation makes 'reaching out' (a verb-particle phrase) into a noun that can be the subject of 'has happened'. C2 register: the willingness to nominalise actions to discuss them as events.

Marie's compressed account of a category of professional activity that has, until 7:32, existed for her only as a hypothetical possibility. The verb 'considered theoretical' (rather than 'considered hypothetical' or 'considered abstract') is C2-precise: theoretical means structurally available but not currently operating, which is the exact relation Marie has had to recruitment.
expressions.expression: Recruitment I have, until this morning, considered theoretical

expressions.meaning: Marie's hyphenated technical term for a credible documented strategy to grow the studio if the contract is won, as distinct from already operating at the larger size. The phrase belongs to professional consulting and venture-capital vocabulary, redeployed here for design-studio competition. C2 transposition.

Marie's compressed framing of the parallel between her competition invitation and Sophie's tenancy offer. The phrase 'permanent membership in our respective fields' converts both offers into the same category — a long-term professional commitment to a particular kind of work. C2 register: the willingness to find structural equivalence across different domains.
expressions.expression: Two women on the phone receiving offers of permanent membership in our respective fields

expressions.meaning: Marie's spatial-volumetric metaphor for the recovery of friend-curiosity that had been temporarily reduced by her own news. The phrase 'returned to its proper size' implies that curiosity has a default volume which can be diminished and restored, and that responsible friendship requires the proper-size version. C2 register at full strength.
expressions.expression: Curiosity about your folder has returned to its proper size

expressions.meaning: Sophie's closing line, in receipt of Marie's thanks. She locates her contribution in the category of work-she-knew-how-to-do rather than in the category of friendship-as-emotional-support, which is a small but precise refusal of sentimentality. The phrase echoes Marie's L20-C1 'wrist thing' but redirected: skill is what was deployed, not feeling.
expressions.expression: It was the work I knew how to do

content.cultural_note: Cammell Laird is a real shipbuilding firm with a long history at Birkenhead, on the Wirral peninsula across the Mersey from Liverpool. Founded in 1828, it built warships, merchant vessels, and submarines for over 150 years; the yard's most famous product is HMS Achilles. The firm survives, in reduced form, today, but a substantial part of the original site has been progressively redeveloped since the early 2000s. The 'Cammell Laird redevelopment' in this lesson is fictional in its specifics but plausible in its outline: the Liverpool City Region has, in real life, funded several major regeneration programmes along the Mersey, and the architectural and identity-design competition for such a programme would, in fact, follow the structure Marie describes. The choice of this particular site is doing structural work for the courseworld: it places Marie's potential next decade in a specifically Northern English industrial-heritage context, which is the same cultural geography Henry's Bakewell occupies (the Peak District, the limestone valleys, the post-industrial and rural North). Marie's eventual decision will, by the end of Block 2, have placed her studio's trajectory in the same regional gravitational field as Sophie's chambers in Manchester. Note Sophie's closing line — 'It was the work I knew how to do.' — as a register continuous with Mum's 'It is a small skill. I've been working on it for thirty-three years' from C1 L11. Both sentences refuse the romanticisation of the kind of help they describe; both locate the help in accumulated practice rather than in inspiration. C2 students should hear these as a recurring family of phrases: skill named in compressed form, the naming itself part of the modesty.
content.grammar_focus: This dialogue is structured around a sustained C2 grammatical operation: the question-as-instrument, deployed across three numbered queries that progressively reframe Marie's situation. Each question is constructed differently. Question one — 'What is the version of you that runs a six-person studio. Have you met her.' — uses a wh-question with embedded relative clause ('the version of you that runs') followed by a present-perfect question ('have you met her'). The two clauses convert an abstract identity-question into a specific encounter-question. Question two — 'If you do not enter the competition, what happens to the version of you that you are now.' — uses first conditional with present simple in both clauses; the question is forward-looking but in real present-tense, which is C2-precise (a hypothetical future framed as a present question gets a more direct answer). Question three — 'Is there a version of entering that does not commit you to the larger version of yourself.' — uses an existential there-question with two embedded relative clauses ('a version of entering that does not commit'). The grammar refuses the binary (enter or not) by asking whether entering itself has versions. C2 students should mark the three questions as a usable pattern: one about identity, one about counterfactual continuation, one about granular alternatives. Note also Marie's central nominalisation cluster: 'the reaching-out has now happened', 'the seven thirty-two diminished it', 'recruitment I have considered theoretical'. Hyphenated and compressed phrases that convert events, times, and abstractions into discrete substantives. C2 prose can sustain this kind of nominalisation when the speaker has decided to discuss her experience as a series of named entities rather than as a flow of feelings. The closing exchange — 'Thank you for not being helpful.' / 'It was the work I knew how to do.' — uses a present participle as the object of thanks (a thing-done-by-not-doing-it) and a past simple in the response. The semantic compression is C2-typical: gratitude for restraint, reciprocated by a precise account of what was actually offered.

key_vocabulary.note: Британская судостроительная фирма в Биркенхеде, основана в 1828. Знаковое имя в индустриальной истории Северной Англии. Произносится 'KAM-uhl LAIRD'.
Через Mersey от Ливерпуля. Историческая судостроительная и промышленная зона. Сейчас в значительной части реабилитируется.
Группа фирм, объединившихся для одного проекта. Renwick — лидер консорциума, ведущего реабилитацию Cammell Laird. Профессиональный британский.
В крупных архитектурных проектах роль 'lead' предполагает координацию работы остальных команд. Renwick and Soames — lead на этом проекте.
Британская система государственных и квази-государственных закупок: формализованный процесс выбора поставщика. Marie's приглашение приходит после settling процедуры.

Британский термин: bespoke development на земле, ранее использовавшейся под промышленность. Противоположное greenfield (новое строительство на нетронутой земле). Cammell Laird — крупнейший такой проект на севере.
Marie's термин для рискованной стратегии: нанять людей до того, как контракт выигран, чтобы выглядеть готовой. Она склоняется к альтернативе: 'scale on win', грузиться после победы.
В архитектурной практике 'principal' = старший владелец-партнёр. Renwick — фамилия, имя фирмы и одновременно её principal.
Marie's клиент в B2-C1, для которого она делала identity. Recurring courseworld figure.
Полуостров и река, разделяющие Liverpool и Birkenhead. Британская географическая норма.

comprehension_questions.hint: Useful help would, in this context, override the value of hearing oneself. What does Marie need to do before she can be helped, and what would helping her now prevent?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie's opening request — 'I would like to hear the news in my own voice and have somebody on the other end who is not going to immediately try to be helpful' — names a specific failure mode of caring listeners. What is the failure, and why is it a failure given what Marie actually needs?

comprehension_questions.hint: The questions were already implicit in Marie's morning. Sophie's contribution is reordering rather than supplying. What does the reordering give Marie that her own thinking could not?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie offers three numbered questions rather than advice. What does each question accomplish, and why does Marie say 'Hearing you ask them has changed which order they are in, and which order is the one I can act on'?

comprehension_questions.hint: She uses 'proper size' as if curiosity has a default volume. What makes curiosity diminished or proper-sized in this account?
comprehension_questions.question: At the end Marie says: 'My curiosity about your folder has returned to its proper size.' What was wrong with the curiosity she had this morning before her own news, and how did the conversation restore it?

content.title: Eleanor, Eighteen Months In

dialogue.line: I have, since October, ordered the burrata for both of us at this restaurant on three occasions, including once when you were not present, and I would like to record that the burrata has held its standard. Sit down.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor, that is the longest opening sentence you have ever offered me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is. I have, in the last six months, become — I am not yet sure how to describe it. I have acquired a slight verbal looseness that I had not previously had time for. It is, I think, the consequence of having less to prove in any given conversation. I do not mind it. I am noticing it.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: It suits you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I want to tell you something. I want to tell you it before the food, because if I tell you over the food you will dribble pho on your shirt and the moment will be undignified, and I am borrowing your line from October.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The line is ratified for return-borrowing. Go.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Edward has offered me tenancy. I have until the seventh of May to reply. I am going to accept.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: This time I will congratulate you in advance. I am going to congratulate you in advance because I am, on principle, suspicious of the rule that one does not congratulate before the deed is done, and the rule has, in my view, served the wrong people. I am also going to congratulate you in advance because the deed is, in this case, internally accomplished, even if the email has not been sent. The going-to-accept is the accepting. What you do at four o'clock on Tuesday is a clerical formality.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor, that is — that is one of the more useful things anyone has said to me in twenty-four hours.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am pleased. I have been working on saying useful things, in the last eighteen months, with — variable success. The variability is, I think, decreasing.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Tell me one true thing about the last six months.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I no longer rehearse my own sentences in the shower.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: When did that stop.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Roughly January. I noticed it in February. I had been, until October, rehearsing every difficult conversation in the shower for fifteen years — so consistently that I had stopped thinking of the rehearsing as a fact about me and had started thinking of it as a fact about showers. In January I had a difficult conversation with a partner about a client, and I did not, that morning, rehearse it. I noticed I had not rehearsed it after the conversation had finished, and the conversation had gone, by my standards, very well. I tried, the next day, to deliberately not rehearse a different conversation. The deliberate non-rehearsing did not work — I rehearsed it anyway. The non-rehearsing only happens when I am not watching for it.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor, that is — that is a remarkably specific account of a developmental shift.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have been keeping notes.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: On.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: On the differences between the version of me who was waiting for partnership, the version of me who has just had it, and the version of me who is now, eighteen months later, a person who has had it. The three are not, as I had assumed, a sequence of refinements of one underlying me. They are three different working configurations. I did not anticipate that. I am, since January, somewhere between mildly interested and mildly frightened by it.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Marie said something analogous in October. About post-natal selfhood. There is no previous self to resume, she said. The current self has not yet found her dimensions. The two of you are, I think, articulating the same thing from different angles. I do not have your version of it — I am not yet eighteen months in — but I can predict, on the basis of having now heard it twice, that I will be having a comparable noticing somewhere around 2027.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I will be interested to compare notes in 2027.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: There is one more thing I want to ask you, while we are not yet eating.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Edward is taking a six-month sabbatical from October. I will be a tenant from October. I will, in my first six months as tenant, be without the one person whose judgement I rely on most, in the building I am committing to. I have not been able to decide whether this is a problem, an opportunity, or — and this is the version I am most suspicious of in myself — a gift. I would like a sentence from you about it, if you have one.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is a gift. Be suspicious of the suspicion.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: I will explain. The judgement of the senior person in any working environment is, in my experience, the single largest constraint on the development of one's own judgement. In the absence of the senior person, the junior person discovers that her judgement, which she had been treating as adjunct, is, in fact, load-bearing. The discovery cannot be made while the senior person is in the room. Edward has, I would suggest, calibrated his sabbatical with this in mind, although he would not, I think, frame it that way to you. He would frame it as the cottage in Argyll being available.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: He did frame it as the cottage in Argyll being available.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Then you have your data point. The framing is the camouflage. The structure is what I have just described.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

dialogue.line: Thank you, Eleanor.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Order the burrata. I have already ordered it. I am extending the courtesy of letting you know.
dialogue.speaker: Eleanor

pratique.scenario: Imagine catching up with a friend or colleague who is now eighteen months into a role you have just been offered. Borrow Sophie's frame: ask for one true thing about the last six months, then ask for one sentence about a specific concern you have. Be willing to receive answers as concise as those Eleanor offers.
prompt_phrases: Tell me one true thing about the last six months.
I would like a sentence from you about it, if you have one.
I will be interested to compare notes in [year].

content.situation: Friday, end of April. Sophie has come down to London for a CPR application at the Royal Courts on Thursday afternoon — a procedural matter for a chambers colleague who could not make the date — and stayed an extra night. She and Eleanor have agreed to dinner at the same Vietnamese place near her old chambers in Manchester where they sat in C1 L23, which is no longer near her chambers in any meaningful sense, but the symmetry pleased Eleanor and Sophie did not object. Eleanor has been an equity partner since October. Eighteen months in. The first nine were exhausting in the manner Helena had warned about; the next nine have, as Eleanor has reported in fragments across the year, become something more like a working life. She arrives ten minutes after Sophie. She is wearing a coat Sophie has not seen before. She looks, in a small definite way, well.

phrase_cle.example: I have decided to leave my job. I have not yet told them. The deciding-to-leave is the leaving — the email is administrative.
phrase_cle.explanation: Eleanor's формула — мощный C2-инструмент для отделения внутреннего совершения акта от внешнего его оформления. Грамматически — конструкция X is Y, где X — hyphenated nominalisation глагольной фразы 'going to accept' (внутреннее намерение, оформленное как существительное), а Y — gerund 'accepting' (сам акт). Конструкция утверждает их структурное тождество. На C2 ты учишься распознавать момент, когда решение уже принято внутренне, и понимать, что внешний акт его сообщения — это не сам выбор, а его регистрация. Это освобождает от иллюзии, что есть какой-то «настоящий» момент решения, отличный от момента, когда ты уже знаешь, что решил. Расширения: 'the deciding-to-call is the calling'; 'the saying-no-in-my-head is the saying-no'; 'the going-to-write is the writing'. Каждая конструкция признаёт, что у внутренних актов есть собственный завершённый статус, не требующий внешнего подтверждения для своей реальности.

expressions.meaning: Eleanor's diagnosis of a stylistic shift in her own speech: less compressed, more willing to let sentences unfold without efficiency pressure. The phrase converts a register change into an acquired property. C2 register: the willingness to name one's own evolving manner.

Eleanor's compressed account of what changed when she became equity: each individual exchange no longer has to demonstrate her competence. The phrase 'in any given conversation' is the key — it converts a chronic state into a per-conversation accounting, which is C2 register.
expressions.expression: Less to prove in any given conversation

expressions.meaning: Eleanor's mock-formal verb-conversion: 'ratified' (legal-formal: confirmed by authority) deployed about a casual line of speech. The construction acknowledges that Sophie has used Eleanor's October phrasing back at her, and Eleanor formally permits the borrowing. C2 humour built on the joke of applying treaty-language to friendship.

Eleanor's identity-claim across a future-progressive construction: the present mental state of intending-to-accept is, structurally, the same as the future act of accepting. The phrase echoes Marie's chiastic constructions ('the trust is the showing') and applies the same move to internal versus external acts. C2 register at full strength.

Eleanor's reduction of the email-of-acceptance to the category of paperwork. The construction 'a clerical formality' (rather than 'a formality' or 'a clerical task') is precise: it keeps the act dignified (not nothing) while declassing it (not the substantive event). C2 register: the willingness to grade the importance of acts within a single sentence.

Eleanor's architectural pair for two relations one's own judgement can have to a senior person's judgement. 'Adjunct' (subordinate, attached, supplementary) versus 'load-bearing' (structural, supporting the building). The metaphor is C2-recurring (Tom's 'humour as load-bearing wall' in C1 L2) and is here applied to professional self-conception.

Eleanor's two-step diagnostic about Edward's sabbatical: the explicit reason given (the cottage) is a screen, the actual function (creating space for Sophie's judgement to develop) is the underlying logic. The chiasmus-adjacent structure ('framing/structure', 'is the camouflage / is what I described') performs the diagnostic move it describes.
expressions.expression: The framing is the camouflage. The structure is what I have just described.

expressions.meaning: Eleanor's account of a self-management paradox: the rehearsing stopped only when she stopped attempting to stop it. The hyphenated 'non-rehearsing' makes the absence into a substantive that can be deliberate or not. C2 register: psychological self-observation rendered with the granularity of laboratory description.
expressions.expression: The deliberate non-rehearsing did not work

content.cultural_note: Eleanor's reference to 'rehearsing my own sentences in the shower' belongs to a particular register of British professional self-description: the named domestic ritual that became, over years, the symptom of professional anxiety. The shower is doing specific cultural work in this context — it is the only place in the day at which one is reliably alone, away from screens, with a small reflective surface and a thinking voice that one can use without consequences. Many British professionals of Eleanor's generation, particularly women in male-dominated fields, have, at some point in their careers, conducted entire imagined difficult conversations under shower water before delivering their actual real-world versions. The cessation of the practice is a specific marker of professional settling. Eleanor's account of the shift — that it happened only when she stopped trying to make it happen — is a small piece of self-observation that British literary culture has captured occasionally (Bennett, Drabble, Lively) but rarely as compactly. C2 students should hear that the cultural specificity is in the location (shower, not commute, not desk, not bed) and in the mechanism (rehearsal of sentences). Note also that the dialogue takes place at the same Vietnamese restaurant as C1 L23, with the same opening move (Eleanor ordering for both, declaring the burrata). The difference in register between the two visits is the cultural lesson: in October, Eleanor's news was difficult and her opening was nervously constructed; in April, eighteen months later, the opening is verbally loose, Eleanor is well, and the conversation has the quality of two equal professionals catching up rather than two friends managing an asymmetry. The restaurant is the same; everything else has changed. This is the structural counterweight the course has been preparing.
content.grammar_focus: Eleanor's central diagnostic in this dialogue uses a sustained C2 grammatical operation: the multiple-version self-description across an introduced typology. 'On the differences between the version of me who was waiting for partnership, the version of me who has just had it, and the version of me who is now, eighteen months later, a person who has had it.' Three parallel relative clauses, each modifying 'the version of me', each in a different aspectual relation to partnership: past continuous ('was waiting'), present perfect ('has just had'), present-perfect-as-state ('is now a person who has had'). The grammar performs the typology it introduces. C2 prose can sustain this kind of triple in spoken English when the speaker has sufficiently differentiated the versions in advance. Note also Eleanor's deliberate use of present perfect with negative continuous in a developmental claim: 'I no longer rehearse my own sentences in the shower' — a habitual present negation followed by 'I had been, until October, rehearsing every difficult conversation in the shower for fifteen years' — past perfect continuous with two embedded adverbials ('until October', 'for fifteen years'). The shift from a long-running practice to its cessation is mapped through aspect alone. C2 students should mark this as the grammatical signature of accurately reported personal change. Sophie's question — 'I would like a sentence from you about it, if you have one' — uses a request for a single predicate: not advice, not a paragraph, a sentence. The construction 'a sentence' (with the indefinite article) treats the desired output as a discrete unit, which Eleanor obliges. Eleanor's reply — 'It is a gift. Be suspicious of the suspicion.' — uses a copular declarative followed by an imperative with a noun-from-verb subject ('the suspicion'). Two short sentences, each doing distinct work: the first names the category, the second instructs Sophie about her relation to the previous category-attempt. C2 register: the smallest possible utterances doing the precise work required.

key_vocabulary.note: Британский процессуальный термин: формальное ходатайство в суде в рамках Гражданских процессуальных правил. Sophie приехала в Лондон представлять коллегу. Профессиональный британский.
Recurring from C1 L21. Гражданские и Апелляционные суды на Strand. Sophie там по конкретному CPR делу.
Recurring from C1 L23. Eleanor's шутка из октябрьской встречи. Sophie reactivates её, и Eleanor приветствует возврат-цитирование.
key_vocabulary.word: to dribble pho on your shirt

key_vocabulary.note: Юридический термин: придать формальную силу акту, ранее заявленному. Eleanor применяет к фразе ('the line is ratified for return-borrowing') — мок-формальный регистр C2.
Eleanor's термин: дело сделано во внутреннем мире, даже если внешне ещё не оформлено. C2 регистр: отделение внутреннего акта от его внешней регистрации.
Британский professional self-description: подготовка к трудным разговорам мысленно, обычно в одиночестве в душе. Маркер хронической профессиональной тревожности.
Hyphenated nominalisation: отсутствие действия как объект внимания. Eleanor's конструкция, очень характерная для C2.
Recurring courseworld figure (Tom L2 C1: 'humour as load-bearing wall'). Eleanor применяет к юридическому суждению Sophie.
Из университетской и грамматической лексики: то, что добавлено к основному, но не структурно. Eleanor противопоставляет 'load-bearing'.
Психологический и педагогический термин: переход между фазами развития. Sophie применяет к описанию Eleanor собственного изменения.

comprehension_questions.hint: She is naming a developmental shift through a domestic symptom. What does the shower as marker tell Sophie about Eleanor's interior architecture, and why is the marker more useful than, say, a list of cases won?
comprehension_questions.question: Eleanor offers, as her 'one true thing about the last six months', the fact that she has stopped rehearsing her sentences in the shower. Why does she choose this specific marker of change rather than a more substantive professional achievement?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is making two distinct claims: one about the rule, one about Sophie's specific situation. Look at her phrase 'the going-to-accept is the accepting' for the structural argument.
comprehension_questions.question: Eleanor congratulates Sophie 'in advance' — before Sophie has formally accepted tenancy — and defends the choice. What is her argument against the conventional rule that one does not congratulate before the deed is done?

comprehension_questions.hint: She uses an architectural pair: adjunct versus load-bearing. What discovery cannot be made while the senior person is in the room, and why?
comprehension_questions.question: Eleanor diagnoses Edward's sabbatical as 'a gift' and instructs Sophie to 'be suspicious of the suspicion'. What does she claim about the relationship between senior judgement and junior development, and why does the absence of the senior figure produce a structural opportunity?

content.title: Carlos in the Park, Again

dialogue.line: He will be down for fifty minutes. He has been raising the bar.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Fifty minutes is generous of him.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is. The generosity has been increasing. I am taking notes for the second one, because the second one will inherit the timetable Mateo is establishing, and a generous timetable is, in the household, more valuable than money.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I want to think out loud with somebody who has, in the last three years, accepted a permanent role in a particular organisation, and who has, since accepting, not had reason to regret accepting. The list of people in that category whom I trust is, I think, three. Marie, who is making her own version of the decision this month and is therefore not the right person to receive mine. Eleanor, who I saw last night, and whose answer I have already absorbed. And you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am, separately, glad to be on the list.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Edward has offered me tenancy at Wellgate. I have until the seventh of May. I am going to accept. I have not yet replied. The week I have between now and then is — Carlos, the week is not a deciding week. The week is something else. I do not yet know what.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The week is the consolidating week. Different work.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: The consolidating week.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. The deciding week was the week between Edward's offer and your knowing what you were going to say. That week, on my reading, has already passed. The week between knowing and saying is structurally different. It is the week in which you let the decision settle into you in such a way that, when you do, on Tuesday, send the email, the email is not the moment of accepting — the email is the documentation of an accepting that has already, by then, become part of how you stand at the kettle.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos, that is — that is exactly the distinction I had been failing to articulate.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I had a similar week in 2024 before signing the permanent contract. I had decided on the Wednesday. I signed on the following Wednesday. The seven days between were, at the time, slightly disorienting because I kept thinking I should be doing something with the decision, and the something kept failing to materialise. I have, since, come to think that the something I was failing to do was the consolidating, and that the consolidating is — is not an action. It is a settling-in of an act that has already happened internally.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Eleanor said it differently. She said: 'The going-to-accept is the accepting. What you do at four o'clock on Tuesday is a clerical formality.'
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Eleanor is — Eleanor is right, and I would not, at this stage of my life, want to debate her. The two formulations are not in competition. Eleanor's formulation is about the temporal location of the act. Mine is about what you do with the seven days between. They cohere.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Tell me one thing about accepting that I have not yet thought of.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: All right. The thing I will tell you — and you will, on first hearing, dismiss it as a small point, and on third hearing, two months from now, recognise as the point — is that accepting is, structurally, harder than refusing. People assume the opposite. People assume that refusing is the brave thing and accepting is the default, the path of least resistance, the easy yes. This is, in my experience, exactly wrong. Refusing has a clear shape. Accepting has no shape; or rather, accepting requires you to provide the shape yourself. When you refused partnership, the refusal had a clear edge — you said no to a thing, and the no defined what you were doing. When you accept tenancy, you say yes to a thing whose contents will, for the next twenty-five years, be defined by you. The yes is, in itself, almost contentless. The work begins after the yes, and the work is the long, granular, daily work of populating the contentless yes with whatever you actually do for the next quarter-century.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: I am taking notes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Do not, in the next twenty-five years, do what I did for the first eighteen months of my permanent role, which is to spend most of the time waiting for the contentless yes to acquire content on its own. It does not. The content only arrives if you bring it. The bringing-it is the work.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: What did you bring.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I brought, eventually, a small set of practices. I started, after the first year, refusing meetings that were not about a decision. I started writing weekly fifteen-minute summaries of what I had actually decided, addressed to myself, which I now keep in a folder I do not, weekly, reread but which I do, twice a year, reread, because the summaries function as a calibration check on what I think I am doing. I started, in the second year, refusing one thing per week — any one thing — that I had been doing by inertia. The practice has been, in aggregate, the structure of the role for me. I did not know it would be. I made it up.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos. Mateo is stirring.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mateo is. Walk a bit. The practice of walking-while-the-conversation-deepens has been refined since 2024 and is now reasonably reliable.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Carlos. One more thing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: How is Anna.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Anna is — Anna is fine. She is also, separately, tired in a way that has not yet reached the point of needing me to do anything specific about it. The tiredness is in the category of things-we-are-watching. The watching is the practice. We have, between us, in the last two and a half years, become quite good at the watching, although I would not, today, say we are reliably good at acting on what we watch. Acting on what we watch is the year-three problem.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Refill the kettle on the way back.
dialogue.speaker: Carlos

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Imagine you are about to take on a long-term commitment — a role, a contract, a relationship — and you want to think out loud with someone who has already done so. Use Sophie's frame: identify the small list of people whose experience qualifies them, locate where you currently are in the deciding-knowing-saying sequence, and ask for one thing the listener has learned about accepting that you have not yet thought of.
prompt_phrases: I want to think out loud with somebody who has, in the last [time], accepted [thing] and has, since accepting, not had reason to regret accepting…
The week between knowing and saying is structurally different. It is the week in which…
Tell me one thing about accepting that I have not yet thought of.

content.situation: Saturday morning, the day after Eleanor. Sophie has stayed in London a third night because Carlos rang on Friday afternoon and proposed that she come down to Greenwich on Saturday for a walk. Carlos has Mateo, who is now two and a quarter, for the morning while Anna is at the lab finishing a draft she has been writing since Tuesday. Mateo is in the pram, asleep with the determination two-year-olds bring to the activity. The pram is the same tank-like model from C1 L5, slightly more battered. Anna is, Carlos has mentioned in passing, fourteen weeks pregnant with their second; the news arrived in February and is, by mutual agreement, not yet for general distribution. They have walked from the station up the hill toward the Royal Observatory and have stopped on the path that runs along the upper wall, looking out over London with the river and the city flat below them. The view is, in late April, the green-into-grey-into-pale-distance view that Greenwich produces when the weather has decided to behave.

phrase_cle.phrase: Accepting requires you to provide the shape yourself.
phrase_cle.example: I said yes to the post. The yes was easy. The shape of the post — what I would actually do, day by day — was not in the yes. Accepting requires you to provide the shape yourself.
phrase_cle.explanation: Carlos's формула — фундаментальный принцип C2 регистра принятия. Грамматически — простое утверждение present simple с инфинитивной конструкцией: subject (accepting, gerund) + verb (requires) + object (you) + infinitive (to provide) + object (the shape) + reflexive (yourself). 'The shape' здесь — метафорическое: контуры, содержание, операциональная форма того, что было принято. На C2 ты учишься распознавать, что многие 'да' структурно пусты, и что ответственность за наполнение этого 'да' лежит на принявшем. Это переворачивает обыденное представление о том, что отказы требуют силы воли, а согласия лёгкие — Carlos утверждает, что обратное. Согласие открывает интервал, в котором никто, кроме принявшего, не определит, что в этом интервале произойдёт. Расширения: 'staying requires you to provide the reason yourself'; 'committing requires you to provide the meaning yourself'; 'choosing requires you to provide the trajectory yourself'. Каждая конструкция обнаруживает невидимую долю работы, которая распределена внутри акта согласия.
phrase_cle.translation: Принятие требует, чтобы ты сам обеспечил его форму.

expressions.meaning: Carlos's term for the period between knowing what one will do and formally doing it. The phrase converts an interval that might feel useless into a category of usable time. C2 register: the willingness to give a name to a phase of mental life that conventional vocabulary leaves unmarked.

Carlos's bodily image for what an internalised decision feels like once it has settled. The phrase converts an abstract psychological state into a postural one — the way a settled person holds themselves while making tea. C2 register: domestic specificity used to anchor a metaphysical claim.
expressions.expression: How you stand at the kettle

expressions.meaning: Carlos's gerund-noun phrase for the work of the consolidating week. 'Settling-in' is treated as a substantive that can be the predicate of 'is'. The construction is C2-typical: nominalisation that makes a process discussable.
expressions.expression: A settling-in of an act that has already happened internally

expressions.meaning: Carlos's term for the act of acceptance considered apart from what one actually does in the role afterward. The compound adjective 'contentless' (without content) modifies 'yes' (a noun made from an interjection). The whole phrase is C2-original and is doing the lesson's central conceptual work.

Carlos's verb-pair for the daily work of giving substance to an initially empty acceptance. 'Populating' (as in populating a database, populating a country, populating a calendar) makes the contentless yes into a space that can be filled. C2 register: technical-administrative vocabulary deployed for existential work.

Carlos's specific anti-inertia practice, named with quantity ('one'), frequency ('per week'), and qualifier ('any one'). The dash-bracketed parenthetical 'any one thing' is permissive — it signals that the choice of what to refuse is unconstrained, which is the practice's point.
expressions.expression: Refusing one thing per week — any one thing — that I had been doing by inertia

expressions.meaning: Carlos's instrumentation metaphor for what reading his own weekly summaries does for him: confirming or correcting his sense of what he is actually doing in the role. 'Calibration' is recurring courseworld figure (Mum's mother L8 C1, Marie's body L25 C1, Sophie's voice L29 C1), here applied to one's own activity-tracking.

Carlos's hyphenated noun-phrase for a category of attention shared between him and Anna. The construction makes a continuous practice into a discrete substantive that can be the contents of a category. C2 register: hyphenation as compression.

content.cultural_note: Carlos's mode in this dialogue is the British-male-mentor register at full strength, and the cultural note here concerns a specific feature of how he constructs his disclosures. He does not advise Sophie. He describes his own practice — the weekly summaries, the meeting-refusal rule, the inertia-refusal rule — and lets Sophie infer whether any of it is portable. The transmission model is the same as in C1 L22: Carlos passes on what was passed to him, modifies what he has invented, and trusts the receiver to discriminate. Note that he names his practice with administrative precision (one thing per week, fifteen-minute summaries, twice-yearly rereading) — the precision is itself part of the gift, because vague advice ('write things down sometimes') would be useless. Note also Carlos's disclosure about Anna — 'tired in a way that has not yet reached the point of needing me to do anything specific about it' — which belongs to a register of marital reporting that British-male speakers of his generation rarely make so cleanly. The phrase 'in the category of things-we-are-watching' converts the marital practice into a working operation; 'we have, between us, in the last two and a half years, become quite good at the watching' is the kind of self-assessment that the register of L22 ('the accounting is what lets the romance survive') established as available. C2 students should hear the consistency of Carlos's voice across two appearances eighteen months apart: the same registers, slightly evolved, deployed at higher density. This is what character-continuity looks like at the upper end of the course.
content.grammar_focus: Carlos's central distinction in this dialogue uses a sustained C2 grammatical operation: the structural pair across two acts that look symmetrical but are not. 'Refusing has a clear shape. Accepting has no shape; or rather, accepting requires you to provide the shape yourself. When you refused partnership, the refusal had a clear edge — you said no to a thing, and the no defined what you were doing. When you accept tenancy, you say yes to a thing whose contents will, for the next twenty-five years, be defined by you.' Three structural moves: parallel short declaratives ('Refusing has X. Accepting has Y'), parenthetical correction ('or rather'), and a temporal-comparative pair ('When you refused… When you accept'). The grammar enacts the asymmetry. C2 prose can sustain this kind of comparative architecture when the speaker is making a precise philosophical claim. Note also the deliberate aspect-shift between 'refused' (past simple, completed) and 'accept' (present simple, current and ongoing) — the past act has a clear edge, the present act is unfolding. The grammar carries the argument. Note also Carlos's use of past habitual constructions: 'I started, after the first year, refusing meetings… I started writing weekly fifteen-minute summaries… I started, in the second year, refusing one thing per week.' Three 'I started' constructions in past simple followed by gerund objects, each with a temporal location ('after the first year', a time-frame, 'in the second year'). The triple-parallel locates a developmental sequence in compressed form. C2 students should mark the construction 'I started Xing' as a compressed alternative to 'I began the practice of Xing' or 'I made it my practice to X'. Sophie's request — 'Tell me one thing about accepting that I have not yet thought of' — uses a relative clause with negative present perfect that constrains the answer. The construction is C2-precise: she is not asking for an opinion or a story, she is requesting a specific cognitive object whose qualifier is its novelty to her.

key_vocabulary.note: Carlos's термин для интервала между внутренним решением и его внешним оформлением. C2 авторская конструкция — не общая идиома, но прозрачная по контексту.
Carlos: 'let the decision settle into you'. Глагол settle — успокоиться, утрамбоваться. 'Settle into' с одушевлённым объектом — метафорическое C2 использование.
Recurring courseworld figure (Mum L11 C1: 'standing at the kettle'). Британский домашний образ для бытового внутреннего состояния. Carlos применяет к Sophie.
Carlos's составное прилагательное: содержит суффикс -less, который придаёт смысл 'без X'. 'Contentless yes' — авторская C2 конструкция.
Технический глагол (populate a database, populate a region) перенесённый в экзистенциальный регистр. Carlos применяет к работе после согласия.
Carlos описывает практику отказа от вещей, которые он делал 'by inertia'. Физический термин в социальном применении: продолжение действия по причине отсутствия сопротивления, не выбора.
Recurring courseworld figure. Из метрологии: периодическая проверка точности измерительного прибора. Carlos применяет к чтению собственных еженедельных summaries.
Carlos's термин для класса проблем, возникающих после того, как навыки наблюдения установлены, но навыки действия отстают. C2 авторская конструкция.
В Greenwich Park, на холме над Темзой. Историческое место установки Гринвичского меридиана. Британская географическая норма.
Recurring courseworld figure (C1 L26, L28). Sophie использует это в третий раз в курсе. Маркер серьёзного приёма.

comprehension_questions.hint: The first week is for choosing. The second is for what — exactly? Look at his image of how she will, by Tuesday, stand at the kettle.
comprehension_questions.question: Carlos distinguishes 'the deciding week' from 'the consolidating week'. What is the structural difference, and why does naming the second category give Sophie something she did not previously have?

comprehension_questions.hint: Refusing has a clear edge; accepting opens an interval whose content is the work. Why does the conventional view get this wrong?
comprehension_questions.question: Carlos claims that 'accepting is, structurally, harder than refusing' — the opposite of the conventional view. What is his argument, and what does he mean by 'the contentless yes'?

comprehension_questions.hint: Vague advice would be useless to Sophie. What does the precision allow her to do that a principle would not?
comprehension_questions.question: Carlos describes three specific practices he developed in his first two years of permanent role — meeting-refusal, weekly summaries, the one-refusal-per-week rule. Why does he name them with administrative precision rather than as general principles?

content.title: Marie's Two Floors

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. You have decided.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have decided. Tell me you have time.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I have time. I am at my desk. I have an email I am about to send. The email can wait twenty minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph, I am — I am going to enter the competition. I am going to enter as a two-person studio with a documented plan-to-scale. I am going to take the lease on the second floor of the building above mine, which is currently empty, and which the landlord has been wanting to let to me at a discounted rate since last summer. I am going to recruit, conditional on winning, a project manager and a senior designer. I am going to keep the studio at one and a half people through the first stage of the competition and only commit to the larger structure if we are shortlisted to the second stage. I am going to write the submission with my existing methods, which is to say slowly, with a great deal of pencil, in the studio at the end of the garden. The submission is due on the eighteenth of June. I have, between now and then, six weeks.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That is the most operationally specific account of a decision I have heard from you in five years.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. The operational specificity is, I think, one of the things the decision did to me. I had been holding the decision as if it were a feeling. Once I let it become a series of operations, the feeling, which I had been confused by, separated from the operations and became — manageable. The operations are, separately, hard. They are not, however, confusing. The confusion was in the conflation.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie, that is — that is also the most articulated thing you have said about your own thinking process in five years.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have been on a bit of a roll, since seven thirty-two on the twenty-third of April.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Soph. Tell me about the email.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: The email accepts tenancy at Wellgate, with effect from the first of October, on the standard terms. I have, after Carlos, included a paragraph about how I propose to operate during Edward's sabbatical. After Eleanor, I have not included a paragraph thanking Edward for the offer, because Eleanor pointed out, on Friday, that the conventional thanking-paragraph in such emails is, structurally, an obstacle to the working relationship that begins after the email. The thanks belongs to a different document, not yet written, and not, in any case, to be written this year.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Eleanor is, year on year, becoming more useful.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: She is.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: We are, on this Tuesday in May, two women who have spent the last two weeks producing the operational specifics of long-term professional commitments. We were, on the same Wednesday in April, two women receiving the offers. The interval between then and now is — I think it is the interval Carlos was telling you about. The consolidating interval.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie, I had been planning to tell you about the consolidating week from Carlos, and you have, by predicting it, taken the wind out of my sails.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am sorry. I will, in compensation, listen to your account of Carlos's framing as if I have not just predicted it. Tell me his version.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: His version was that the work of the consolidating week is to let the decision settle into you in such a way that, when you do the documentary act, the act is the documentation of an accepting that has already, by then, become part of how you stand at the kettle.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: How you stand at the kettle.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph, I have been standing at the kettle differently for about four days. I had not yet noticed that I was standing differently. Hearing it phrased — Carlos has, by phone, retroactively named what I have been doing in my own kitchen.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: There is one thing I want to ask before you go and the email goes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: If you do not, in June, win the first stage. If you are, on the twenty-fifth or whenever the result comes, told that the work has gone to one of the other three studios. What is the version of the next year that follows.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph, I had been preparing the answer to that question for five days, on the assumption that you, or Tom, or someone, would, before I sent the submission, ask. I am glad it is you. The answer is — the answer is that the version of the next year that follows is the version in which I have, by entering, learned that I am the kind of designer who can, when the brief arrives, scale the practice in response. The learning is durable. The learning is mine even if the contract is not. The learning is — Soph, the learning would not have arrived without entering. The losing, were it to happen, would be the second-best outcome rather than the worst. The worst outcome would be not entering and discovering, slowly over the next decade, that I had, by not entering, decided what kind of designer I was going to be.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Send the submission. Sign the lease.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Send the email.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I am about to.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Go and do it. I will, separately, sign the lease this afternoon. We will speak on Sunday.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Sunday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is a good morning to be sending these things.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It is.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Imagine ringing a close friend or partner to report a decision you have, after some weeks of deliberation, fully settled. Use Marie's structure: deliver the decision as a chain of 'going to' future actions with operational specifics; identify what changed in your own thinking that allowed the decision to consolidate; ask the listener for one question before they go; answer it as the durable form of the decision.
prompt_phrases: I have decided. I am going to X. I am going to Y. I am going to Z.
I had been holding the decision as if it were a feeling. The confusion was in the conflation.
If [hypothetical bad outcome]. The losing would be the second-best outcome rather than the worst. The worst would be not [doing the thing] and discovering…

content.situation: Tuesday morning, the seventh of May. Sophie is at her desk in chambers. The email to Edward is in her drafts folder — the message she has been writing in fragments since Saturday night, which is now in its fourth version, and which she will, before lunch, send. Marie rings at twenty past ten. The light is the particular Manchester May light: high, thin, decisive. Olivia is at nursery; Tom is at his office. Marie has made tea. Marie is, on the line, in the voice she uses when she has been thinking for forty-eight hours and has, just now, finished. Sophie hears it in three words. They have not spoken since the Wednesday news, ten days ago, although they have texted twice — short, operational, both signalling that the longer call would happen when the deciding had finished its work.

phrase_cle.phrase: I had been holding the decision as if it were a feeling.
phrase_cle.example: I had been treating the breakup as if it were a mood. Once I let it become a series of operations — telling, moving, dividing the books — the feeling separated from the operations and became manageable. The confusion was in the conflation.
phrase_cle.explanation: Marie's формула — диагностическая. Грамматически: past perfect continuous ('had been holding') + 'as if it were' (subjunctive past в conditional clause). Конструкция 'as if it were' маркирует, что обращение с объектом было неверным относительно его реальной природы. Решение имеет операционную природу — у него есть конкретные шаги, последствия, графики. Чувство — нет, чувство либо есть, либо нет, его нельзя разбить на операции. Когда Marie начала обходиться с решением как с чувством, она потеряла способность с ним работать. Преобразование решения в серию операций освободило связанное с ним чувство, чтобы оно стало само по себе, а не parazitировало на структуре, которая для него не предназначена. На C2 ты учишься распознавать category errors в собственной коммуникации с собой: трактуешь ли ты решение как чувство, или вопрос как утверждение, или приглашение как обязательство. Расширения: 'I had been treating the question as if it were an answer'; 'I had been holding the offer as if it were a demand'; 'I had been receiving the silence as if it were a verdict'. Каждая конструкция — потенциальный момент диагностики misalignment между категорией объекта и категорией обращения с ним.
phrase_cle.translation: Я держала это решение так, словно оно было чувством.

expressions.meaning: Recurring from L8: Marie's hyphenated technical term for a credible documented strategy to grow the studio if the contract is won. The phrase has, between L8 and L11, become a working object Marie uses without elaboration. C2 register: the consolidation of a piece of vocabulary between two appearances.

Marie's diagnosis of what was confusing her in the previous two weeks: she had treated the decision (which has operational specifics) as if it were a feeling (which does not have specifics), and the category-error had produced the confusion. The construction 'as if it were' (subjunctive in the conditional clause) is C2-precise: the as-if signals that the treatment was a misclassification, now corrected.
expressions.expression: I had been holding the decision as if it were a feeling

expressions.meaning: Marie's compressed three-word diagnosis of her own previous state. 'Conflation' (the merging of two distinct things into one) is the kind of analytical noun that C2 register uses to compress what would otherwise be a paragraph of explanation. The phrase echoes the structural diagnostics throughout the courseworld.
expressions.expression: The confusion was in the conflation

expressions.meaning: British informal idiom: in a sustained period of productive momentum or insight. Marie's mock-modest acknowledgement that her own articulation has been unusually sharp in the last two weeks. The phrase keeps her self-assessment in a register Sophie can receive without ceremony.
expressions.expression: On a bit of a roll

expressions.meaning: Sophie's hyphenated noun-phrase for the conventional grateful section of a tenancy-acceptance email. By naming it as a discrete unit, she can identify it as removable. C2 register: the willingness to treat conventional letter-elements as discrete components.

Marie's compressed verdict on Eleanor's evolution. The temporal phrase 'year on year' (financial-statistics language for sequential annual measurements) applied to a friend's developing usefulness is dry C2 humour. The construction echoes Eleanor's own 'rehearsing every difficult conversation in the shower for fifteen years' from L9 — both speakers are now using calibrated temporal vocabulary.
expressions.expression: Year on year, becoming more useful

expressions.meaning: British idiom: deflating someone's planned moment by anticipating them. Sophie uses it without complaint — Marie's prediction has confirmed rather than undermined the framing. C2 register: idioms used self-consciously, with the speaker registering that the idiom is being deployed.
expressions.expression: Taking the wind out of my sails

expressions.meaning: Marie's reframing of the value of the competition independent of the outcome. The participle 'by entering' specifies the means; the past simple 'learned' (in present-perfect aspect) marks the gain as durable. The whole construction converts a possibly-losing contest into a learning-secured event. C2 register at full strength: the willingness to redefine victory.
expressions.expression: By entering, learned that I am the kind of designer who can scale the practice in response

content.cultural_note: This dialogue closes the structural pair that Block 2 has been building since L8: the parallel of two professional decisions arriving on the same Wednesday in April and resolving on the same Tuesday in May. Marie and Sophie have, by mutual unstated agreement, used the two weeks identically — for what Carlos called 'the consolidating'. The phone call performs the closure of that parallel, and the closure is structural: both women send their respective documents (Sophie's email, Marie's lease) within hours of the call. C2 register, at this level of two-decade friendship, supports the kind of synchronisation the courseworld is staging without sentimentalising it. Note Marie's idiomatic admission — 'I have been on a bit of a roll' — in a register Marie has not used in this register before. The phrase belongs to a slightly looser British informal mode that Marie reaches for when she wants to signal that her recent insight-streak has been unusual but is not, she insists, evidence of any major personality change. The mock-modesty is Marie's signature. Note also the structural recurrence of the consolidating-week metaphor: Carlos delivered it to Sophie on Saturday in Greenwich; Sophie reports it to Marie on Tuesday morning; Marie applies it to her own previous four days ('I have been standing at the kettle differently for about four days'). The metaphor has, in three days, travelled from Greenwich to Manchester to Hackney, picking up new applications at each stop. C2 students should hear this as one of the courseworld's recurring techniques: a phrase enters the world and is applied across speakers and contexts as a working tool. The closing line — 'It is a good morning to be sending these things.' — is Marie's quiet ratification of the day. C2 register at full strength: the smallest possible declaration carrying the largest structural acknowledgement.
content.grammar_focus: Marie's central account in this dialogue uses a sustained C2 grammatical operation: the future-as-decided declaration through chained 'going to' constructions. 'I am going to enter the competition. I am going to enter as a two-person studio with a documented plan-to-scale. I am going to take the lease on the second floor… I am going to recruit, conditional on winning, a project manager and a senior designer. I am going to keep the studio at one and a half people through the first stage of the competition and only commit to the larger structure if we are shortlisted to the second stage. I am going to write the submission with my existing methods…' Six 'going to' constructions in sequence, each adding operational specificity. The grammatical move performs the decision: by stating future actions in the form 'I am going to X', Marie is documenting that the decisions about each operation have already been made internally, and the email Sophie will send (and the lease Marie will sign) are documentations of the same kind. C2 students should mark the chained 'going to' as the grammatical signature of a fully consolidated decision; the alternative ('I will' or 'I'll') would signal a more recent or less-settled commitment. Note also Marie's use of past perfect with subjunctive past in a conditional reframe: 'were it to happen, would be the second-best outcome rather than the worst'.
