The inverted conditional ('were it to happen' instead of 'if it were to happen') is the upper-formal form of the second conditional. C2 register reaches for inversions when the speaker is ranking hypothetical outcomes against each other. The closing exchange — 'Send the submission. Sign the lease.' / 'Send the email.' — uses parallel imperatives in three short utterances. Each speaker instructs the other in the same grammatical form, performing the symmetrical commitment. The grammar is the gesture of mutual witness.

key_vocabulary.note: Британский профессиональный термин для прохождения в финальный этап конкурса. Marie's plan-to-scale предполагает commit to the larger structure только if shortlisted на второй этап.
В архитектурных конкурсах: документированный пакет дизайн-предложений, отправляемый на оценку. Срок Marie — 18 июня. Профессиональный британский.
Британская морская идиома. Sophie использует её без жалобы — Marie предсказала Carlos's framing, и Sophie это нравится. Дружеский регистр.
key_vocabulary.word: to take the wind out of (someone's) sails

key_vocabulary.note: Британская разговорная идиома. Marie применяет к себе про последние две недели. Mock-modest саморегистрация.
key_vocabulary.word: to be on a bit of a roll

key_vocabulary.note: Из финансовой и статистической лексики: показатель, измеряемый ежегодно. Marie применяет к Eleanor's evolution. C2 транспозиция: статистический регистр в дружеском контексте.
Sophie's термин для отправки email Edward'у. Не сам акт принятия, а его внешнее оформление. C2 регистр: точное называние акта в его юридической функции.
Аналитическое существительное: ошибочное объединение двух разных категорий. Marie: 'the confusion was in the conflation'. C2 рабочая лексика.
Marie's термин для ранжирования гипотетических исходов: первый — выиграть; второй — проиграть, но получить опыт; третий (худший) — не участвовать. C2 регистр: явное ранжирование.
Recurring courseworld figure: identification through capacity. Marie применяет к собственной профессиональной идентичности.
key_vocabulary.word: to be the kind of (X) who can (Y)

key_vocabulary.note: Marie: 'Carlos has, by phone, retroactively named what I have been doing in my own kitchen'. Признание, что чужая формулировка дала имя тому, что говорящий уже делал. C2 регистр.

comprehension_questions.hint: Decisions and feelings have different structures. What did treating a decision as a feeling prevent her from doing, and what did the operational reframing release?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie says: 'I had been holding the decision as if it were a feeling. The confusion was in the conflation.' What was the category error she was making, and what changed when she let the decision become operational?

comprehension_questions.hint: Eleanor pointed out that the conventional thanking belongs to a different document. Look at what she said about the working relationship that begins after the email.
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie reports that, on Eleanor's advice, she has not included a thanking-paragraph in her acceptance email. What is Eleanor's argument against the conventional thanks, and what alternative is implied?

comprehension_questions.hint: Losing is a single bounded event with a clear shape. What is the shape of the alternative she is describing, and why does the slowness of the discovery matter?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie's account of the worst outcome — 'not entering and discovering, slowly over the next decade, that I had, by not entering, decided what kind of designer I was going to be' — names a specific failure mode. What is it, and why is it worse than losing the competition?

content.title: The Briefcase

dialogue.line: Come in. Close the door, if you would. I have been thinking about what to say to you for four days, and I have arrived at, I think, a workable form. The form involves a brief statement, a small object, and a slightly impertinent request. The order is not negotiable; I have settled it.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Edward, the orchestration is itself a piece of information. Go ahead.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The brief statement. I have, in receipt of your email of Tuesday, formally accepted your acceptance and processed the membership entry with the senior clerk this morning. From the first of October you are a tenant of Wellgate Chambers. The vote at chambers meetings is yours from then. The Marsden room is yours from November. The standard share of receipts will appear, on your statement, from the first quarter beginning the first of October. I will not, today, repeat the substance of what we discussed two weeks ago. I will only confirm that the substance has been actioned.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Thank you, Edward.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: The small object.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I would like, before I leave for Argyll on the twenty-second of September, to give you my briefcase.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Edward.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Your briefcase.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am giving it to you because it is, as you may have observed, the only piece of equipment I have used continuously for twenty-eight years, and because I do not, in the period of the sabbatical, intend to use it. It would be in a cupboard in Manchester for six months, which strikes me as a waste of a perfectly serviceable briefcase. I would prefer that it be in your hand. The transfer is not a symbol. The transfer is an operational decision. The briefcase is, separately, very good — Italian leather, made in 1997 by a firm in Florence that no longer makes briefcases, which is, in part, why I have kept it for twenty-eight years. It will outlive me, and possibly you.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Edward, I cannot — Edward, the briefcase is your briefcase. I cannot accept your briefcase.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. The briefcase is, until I hand it to you, my briefcase. The handing-it-to-you converts it into your briefcase. The conversion is not symbolic and is not, I should add, sentimental. It is a piece of office furniture moving from one person's daily use to another's. You can, when I return in March, return it to me if you have not, in the interim, become attached to it; or you can keep it if you have. I am, I should say, indifferent to the outcome. What I would object to is the briefcase sitting in a cupboard for six months.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: All right.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. I have, on the desk, transferred the contents into the manilla envelope to your left. The contents are: a fountain pen which I will retain because I have an irrational attachment to it; a pair of reading glasses which are also, today, coming with me; and a small notebook which is yours to keep or discard, as it has nothing in it but blank pages and the date 'September 1995' written on the inside cover, which is the date I bought the briefcase, and which has, I think, no further claim on either of us.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I will keep the notebook.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: The slightly impertinent request.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I would like, when I return in March, to take you to lunch and have you tell me three things you have learned in my absence that you would not have learned had I been here. The lunch is, frankly, a structuring device — I am asking for the three things, and the lunch is the container. The three things do not, today, exist; you will discover them between October and March; the lunch will be the point at which you tell me what they are. I am asking now because I want, between now and September, to refrain from giving you any more advice than is strictly necessary, on the grounds that the advice would, if delivered, become the things you would have learned in my presence, which is not the category of things I am asking for.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Edward, that is — that is a remarkable thing to ask.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is, possibly, slightly more than I have any right to ask. Hence the impertinence. You are at liberty to refuse, or to agree and discover, in March, that the three things do not exist. Either is acceptable to me.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I will come to the lunch. I will bring three things. I do not, today, know what they will be. I am, separately, going to write them down as they arrive, in the notebook.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That is a more constructive use of the notebook than I had imagined for it. It pleases me.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Edward.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: There is one thing I want to say.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: I have, between Tuesday and now, been preparing a brief paragraph of thanks which I had drafted into the email and then, on Eleanor's advice, removed. The paragraph belongs to a different document, not yet written, and not, in any case, to be written this year. I am telling you this because I want you to know that the absence of thanks in the email was not an oversight. It was a structural decision.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. I had, on reading your email on Tuesday, noticed the absence and registered it as a structural decision, and I had, separately, approved the decision. The absence was, in my reading, the substantive courtesy. The thanks would have been formal.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Right.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. It is twenty past four. The chambers is quiet. I have nothing else for you. Take the briefcase. Take the envelope. Take the notebook. Have a good weekend.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Edward.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

dialogue.line: Have a good September.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I will. The Argyll cottage has, in the past four months, become an object of quite disproportionate longing. I am ready.
dialogue.speaker: Edward

pratique.scenario: Imagine giving someone a significant object that they might be tempted to refuse on symbolic grounds. Use Edward's frame: pre-emptively reclassify the transfer as operational rather than ceremonial; allow the receiver an exit (return the object later, refuse to attach to it); name the absence of symbolism in the act so that the act can be received without weight.
prompt_phrases: The transfer is not a symbol. The transfer is an operational decision.
You can, when [time arrives], return it to me if you have not, in the interim, become attached to it; or you can keep it if you have.
I am indifferent to the outcome. What I would object to is [specific bad alternative].

content.situation: Friday, the twelfth of May. Sophie sent the email at twelve forty-two on Tuesday afternoon. Edward replied at fourteen-eleven the same day with three lines: receipt acknowledged, terms confirmed, see you Friday at four. He did not say what Friday at four was for. Sophie has, between Tuesday and now, attended to other matters. Friday at four arrives. The chambers is quiet — most of the senior tenants have left for the weekend, and the kitchen kettle has, against type, been refilled by someone other than the most junior person in the room. Edward's office door is open. He is standing at the window. On his desk, beside the usual papers, there is a leather briefcase Sophie has not, in eleven months, seen him without — the one he carries to court, the one he carries home, the one she has, on three or four occasions, watched him retrieve from the floor by his chair as he stood up. The briefcase is sitting at the centre of the desk in a way that suggests it is the subject of the meeting.

phrase_cle.example: He was leaving on the twenty-second for a long retreat. I did not say goodbye. I said: Have a good September.
phrase_cle.explanation: Sophie's формула — компрессированное прощание, в котором временной интервал (sentябрь — летний месяц перед его уходом) заменяет более привычные категории прощания. Грамматически — императив 'have' + притяжательное определение 'a good' + конкретное существительное-период. На C2 ты учишься выбирать единицу времени, к которой обращаешься, и тем самым задавать тон прощания. 'Have a good weekend' — стандарт; 'Have a good week' — рабочий регистр; 'Have a good September' — необычно, потому что за сентябрём, в данном случае, следует длительное отсутствие, и Sophie обращается именно к этому маленькому отрезку перед разлукой. Это деликатно: она не говорит 'have a good sabbatical' (что было бы преждевременно и слишком масштабно), но и не отделывается дежурным 'have a good weekend' (что было бы недостаточно). Sentябрь — последний нормальный месяц перед его уходом, и Sophie его именно так и называет. Расширения: 'have a good Tuesday' (когда ты знаешь, что у собеседника во вторник важная встреча); 'have a good summer' (когда расставание перед длительным летним перерывом); 'have a good last week' (когда собеседник заканчивает работу через неделю). Каждая конструкция — выбор временной единицы, наиболее точной для прощания.

expressions.meaning: Sophie's diagnostic: Edward's announcement that he has a three-part structure is not a preamble, it is content. The phrase 'is itself a piece of information' is C2-typical: meta-communicative recognition that the form of a communication carries meaning beyond what its substance carries.
expressions.expression: The orchestration is itself a piece of information

expressions.meaning: Edward's professional-corporate verb: 'to action' (to put into operation) used about a confirmed acceptance. The construction is faintly bureaucratic and entirely deliberate — Edward is dignifying the email-and-clerical-process by giving it the verb appropriate to a formal proceeding. C2 register: bureaucratic vocabulary as professional respect.

Edward's hyphenated nominalisation of the gift-act, with the verb 'converts' framing the transfer as an operational state-change. The whole construction refuses sentimental language about gifts: this is a conversion event, not a gesture. C2 register: technical vocabulary applied to objects with personal weight.
expressions.expression: The handing-it-to-you converts it into your briefcase

expressions.meaning: Edward's deliberate reduction of a twenty-eight-year-old briefcase to its administrative category. The construction is the kind of disinflation Edward reaches for when he wants to prevent Sophie from refusing the gift on grounds of its symbolic weight. C2 register: the willingness to underplay the personal significance of an object precisely when the object is significant.

Edward's term for the lunch he is proposing in March: the lunch itself is not the point, it is the container that gives shape to the three-things request. The phrase belongs to design and writing vocabulary; Edward redeploys it for a personal event. C2 transposition.

Edward's third-conditional construction in a hypothetical-restrictive clause: the category of things that would have been learned if the absence had not happened. The construction is C2-typical for distinguishing actual from counterfactual learning, and is the structural reason Edward refrains from giving more advice now.
expressions.expression: Things you would have learned in my presence

expressions.meaning: Edward's compressed valuation of the cottage in Argyll. 'Disproportionate' (out of scale) modifies 'longing' (a noun usually paired with romantic or transcendent objects); the combination dignifies a domestic location through ironic registers. C2 humour at full strength.
expressions.expression: An object of quite disproportionate longing

expressions.meaning: Recurring from L7. Edward picks up Sophie's earlier formulation and confirms its application to the email. The phrase has, between L7 and L12, become a working concept the two of them share. C2 register: the consolidation of a phrase into shared vocabulary across a working relationship.

content.cultural_note: Edward's three-part structure for this meeting — brief statement, small object, slightly impertinent request — is a recognisable British senior-professional architecture for difficult or weighted communications. The pre-announcement of the structure itself ('the form involves… the order is not negotiable') is a courtesy: it tells the listener what is coming, in what order, so that the listener can receive each part without trying to anticipate the whole. Edward's gift of the briefcase deserves close attention as a register move. He explicitly disclaims the symbolic dimension of the gift, refers to the briefcase as 'a piece of office furniture', and frames the transfer as an operational decision against six months in a cupboard. This is the British senior-professional version of generosity at its dryest: the actual significance of the act is precisely as large as Edward refuses to acknowledge. Sophie reads the register correctly — she initially demurs, then accepts, and when Edward offers the notebook for free disposal she keeps it. The notebook, with the date 'September 1995' on the inside cover, is the lesson's quiet historical detail. September 1995 is the month Sophie was three years old, the same month the photograph in C1 L17 was taken (November 1995, two months later). The briefcase has, in other words, accompanied Edward across the same span of time the photograph has accompanied Henry. The course is not insisting on this parallel — it is letting it sit, available to the C2 reader who notices, invisible to the reader who does not. Note also Edward's closing line: 'The Argyll cottage has, in the past four months, become an object of quite disproportionate longing. I am ready.' The two short sentences perform what they describe — Edward is, after eleven months of careful working presence, ready to leave. C2 students should hear that the proper closure to a long working relationship in this register is not a speech but a brief technical update on one's own state.
content.grammar_focus: Edward's central proposition in this dialogue uses a sustained C2 grammatical operation: the gift-as-conversion through nominalisation and hyphenation. 'The briefcase is, until I hand it to you, my briefcase. The handing-it-to-you converts it into your briefcase.' Two short sentences performing a quasi-legal transfer. The first uses present simple ('is') with a temporal-conditional clause inserted ('until I hand it to you'); the second uses a hyphenated gerund-phrase ('the handing-it-to-you') as the subject of 'converts' with a result construction ('into your briefcase'). The grammatical move is to treat the transfer as a state-change event rather than a gesture, which is C2-typical for moments that conventional registers would render through sentimental language. Edward refuses the sentimental register in favour of the technical one. Note also Edward's three-conditional account of why he is asking for the three things: 'the advice would, if delivered, become the things you would have learned in my presence, which is not the category of things I am asking for.' Modal-conditional ('would, if delivered, become'), embedded relative clause with modal-perfect ('you would have learned'), with the relative-clause continuation ('which is not the category…') extending the diagnostic. C2 prose can sustain this kind of layered conditional reasoning when the speaker has actually thought about the structural difference between presence-learning and absence-learning. Sophie's reply uses a precisely calibrated future-with-modal construction: 'I will come to the lunch. I will bring three things. I do not, today, know what they will be. I am, separately, going to write them down as they arrive, in the notebook.' Future simple ('I will come', 'I will bring'), present simple negative with temporal qualifier ('I do not, today, know'), present continuous indicating intention ('I am going to write'), and a temporal subordinate ('as they arrive'). Each tense choice is doing semantic work: the commitments are firm; the contents are unknown; the practice of recording is preparatory. C2 register at full strength: tense as instrument.

key_vocabulary.note: В английских chambers старший клерк — управляющий, отвечающий за распределение работы между barristers и за финансовые потоки. Не secretary; центральная фигура в работе chambers. Edward processed Sophie's membership entry through the senior clerk.
Британский корпоративный глагол: формально оформить и запустить процесс. Edward: 'the substance has been actioned'. C2 регистр; маркер профессионального английского.
Грубо-волокнистый коричнево-жёлтый конверт, стандартный для британского делопроизводства. На столе Edward'а — knownю британской офисной нормы.
Edward retains his fountain pen — деталь, характеризующая его поколение и регистр. Не gel pen, не biro. Британский профессиональный маркер.
Edward's самокритичная фраза о его перьевой ручке. C2 саморегистрация: говорящий называет привязанность нерациональной, чтобы предотвратить её защиту.
Edward: 'It will outlive me, and possibly you.' Метафорически про долговечность briefcase'а; буквально — про срок службы. C2 разговорный.
Из дизайна и теории письма: элемент, дающий организующую форму содержанию. Edward применяет к lunch in March. C2 транспозиция.
Британская формальная фраза, перенесённая в личный регистр. Edward: 'You are at liberty to refuse'. C2 регистр: формальная вежливость как точное называние возможности.
Edward's компрессированная самооценка про коттедж в Argyll. C2 регистр: называние собственного эмоционального состояния через категорию его величины относительно объекта.
Edward's заключительное 'I am ready'. Не перед challenge — а готов уйти, готов к sabbatical'у. Британское немногословное прощание.

comprehension_questions.hint: He is preventing Sophie from refusing the gift on certain grounds. What grounds, and what does the operational reframing make possible?
comprehension_questions.question: Edward gives Sophie his briefcase but explicitly refuses the symbolic framing of the gift. Why does he insist that 'the transfer is not a symbol' and reclassify the briefcase as 'a piece of office furniture'?

comprehension_questions.hint: He distinguishes between presence-learning and absence-learning. What is the difference, and why is the second category specifically valuable to him as a question?
comprehension_questions.question: Edward asks Sophie, in March, to tell him 'three things you have learned in my absence that you would not have learned had I been here.' Why does he frame the request this way, and what is the structural argument for refraining from giving more advice between now and September?

comprehension_questions.hint: Each option marks a different temporal scale. What does September name that the others would either inflate or shrink?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie's closing line is 'Have a good September.' Why does she choose this specific time-unit rather than 'have a good weekend', 'have a good summer', or 'have a good sabbatical'?

content.title: The Scan

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have a small piece of news that I would like to deliver in roughly four sentences, and then we can discuss whatever follows from the four sentences. Are you in a position to receive four sentences.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I am. Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The scan was done on the eleventh. The letter arrived on the twenty-third. The diagnosis is osteoarthritis of the right hip, advanced enough that the orthopaedic team has recommended a total hip replacement rather than further conservative management. The operation has been provisionally scheduled for the third of July at the Royal Lancaster Infirmary, with admission the day before. That is the four sentences. The fifth sentence, which I am offering as an addendum rather than a substantive sentence, is that I am, by the standards of the conversation, fine.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Thank you for the four sentences. I am now going to ask, in roughly the order you would expect, three questions, after which I will stop asking questions and we can discuss what is operationally useful.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That is, on procedural grounds, well structured. Proceed.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: First. Is the third of July the date you would have chosen, or is it the date the orthopaedic team has chosen and which you have accepted because it was the available one.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is the date they have offered. I have considered, and rejected, the option of asking them to move it, on the grounds that the option of asking would have produced a marginal improvement at best — perhaps a fortnight either way — and would, I think, have been an exercise in giving myself a sense of control rather than improving the substantive arrangement. I have therefore accepted the date. The acceptance is not, however, capitulation. The acceptance is — what was it Carlos said to you about accepting being structurally harder than refusing — yes, the acceptance is operational.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, you are quoting Carlos at me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am. You repeated his framing to me at Easter. The framing struck me at the time as the kind of framing one keeps in reserve for occasions one cannot, on Easter, predict. The occasion has now arrived. The framing has been usefully retrieved.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Second question.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Who, in the practical sense, is going to be at the hospital with you on the morning of the second when you are admitted, on the third when you go in, and at any point on the fourth or fifth when you wake up sufficiently to register the company.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The honest answer is that I had been planning to manage the admission and the surgery alone, on the entirely defensible grounds that I have, for the last forty-eight years of adult life, managed most things alone, and the principle has not, until today, struck me as inadequate. The principle has, in the last hour and a half since I sat down to ring you, become — let us say, less entirely defensible than it had been at three o'clock.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I will be there.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie, I want to be careful about what I am asking and what I am not asking. I am not asking you to drop work for a week. I am not asking you to be, on the fourth and fifth, in a chair by my bed reading aloud, which is the version of hospital company that I find, in advance, slightly more burdensome than the alternative of being alone. I would, however, find it useful — useful, not necessary — to have somebody at the admission on the second, somebody at the discharge whenever that turns out to be, and somebody available by phone on the fourth and fifth in case the system requires a next-of-kin signature for some procedural reason. The middle two days are, in principle, manageable.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Then I will be at the admission and the discharge, and I will be on call for the fourth and fifth. Marie has, in the last month, become a person whose phone is reliably nearby; if you would consent, I would also list her as a backup phone for any moments I am, by chambers commitment, unreachable.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I would be glad of Marie's number on the form. I have always liked Marie. She is also, separately, the only person I know who would, if rung at three in the morning by the Royal Lancaster Infirmary, conduct the conversation in such a way that the hospital would conclude they had rung the right person.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, that is the most exact description of Marie I have heard.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have had thirty years to compose it.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Third question.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Are you, in any of the ways you have not yet mentioned, frightened.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The honest answer to the question you have just asked is yes, but the version of the yes that is true today is not the version that would be useful to discuss on a telephone on a Sunday afternoon. The version that is true today is small and bearable. The version that may, between now and the third of July, become not-bearable is a different version, and if it arrives I will tell you. I would rather we discuss the larger version of the yes, if at all, in person, on Wednesday, when you come.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Wednesday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I had assumed Wednesday.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Then Wednesday it is. I will get the eleven-thirteen from Piccadilly and be at Cedar View by half past one. I will stay until Friday morning.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I will, between now and Wednesday, do the things one does to prepare a small flat for a daughter who is staying for two nights. The list is short, and largely involves the bedding.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Thank you for asking the third question rather than waiting for me to volunteer the answer.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I had been asking it of myself for the last six minutes. Asking it of you was the next step.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The crossword is yours. I have not yet started mine. Wednesday.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Wednesday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: Imagine delivering a piece of medical or other significant news to a close family member. Use Mum's structured-disclosure architecture: pre-announce the form ('a small piece of news in roughly four sentences'), ask whether the listener is ready, deliver the substantive information in numbered or counted sentences, and offer an addendum about your own state without making the addendum the topic.
prompt_phrases: I have a small piece of news that I would like to deliver in roughly [number] sentences. Are you in a position to receive [number] sentences?
That is the [number] sentences. The [next] sentence, which I am offering as an addendum rather than a substantive sentence, is that I am, by the standards of the conversation, fine.
The version of [the answer] that is true today is not the version that would be useful to discuss [on the telephone / in this setting].

content.situation: Sunday afternoon, the twenty-eighth of May. Sophie has not been to Lancaster since Easter. The scan, which she has been not-asking about since the seventh of May, has happened on the eleventh; the letter has arrived on the twenty-third. Mum has, between the twenty-third and now, been holding the letter for five days, on the principle that information of this kind, once received, ought to be allowed to settle before it is shared. Sophie is in her flat in Manchester, at the kitchen table, with a half-finished crossword and a cup of tea that has gone slightly cold. Her phone rings at twenty past three. Sophie picks up on the second ring. The afternoon is bright, the windows are open, the radio is on at low volume. By the end of the call, the radio will still be on, the windows will still be open, and Sophie will have decided that she will be in Lancaster on Wednesday, which is the next moment in the working week she can afford to give.

phrase_cle.example: I would like you to come to the meeting if you can. Useful, not necessary. I will not feel let down if you cannot.
phrase_cle.explanation: Mum's формула — фундаментальный C2-инструмент для выражения просьб без давления. Грамматически — две короткие constructions через противопоставление: положительное прилагательное ('useful') и отрицательное ('not necessary'). На C2 ты учишься различать эти две категории и явно их разделять, чтобы освободить собеседника от чувства долженствования. Когда нечто 'необходимо', отказ воспринимается как провал; когда нечто 'полезно', отказ воспринимается как нейтральный выбор. Mum использует формулу, чтобы пригласить Sophie быть на admission и discharge, не превращая просьбу в требование. Расширения: 'desirable, not required'; 'helpful, not crucial'; 'welcome, not expected'. Каждая конструкция — точная градация социального давления, и говорящий, владеющий ею, освобождает слушателя от обязанности отвечать на просьбу так, словно от ответа зависит будущее отношений. На C2 это становится автоматическим инструментом.

expressions.meaning: Mum's principle for handling diagnostic information: a deliberate interval between receipt and disclosure, during which the receiver assimilates the news privately before it enters circulation. The phrase 'ought to be allowed' uses a mild deontic modal — Mum is asserting a quiet ethical rule rather than a personal preference.
expressions.expression: Information of this kind, once received, ought to be allowed to settle

expressions.meaning: Mum's calibrated qualifier: 'fine' would, in another context, be a misleading word; here, with the qualifier, it specifies what kind of fine — adequate to the requirements of the conversation, neither minimising nor inflating. C2 register: relativised vocabulary that names its own scale.
expressions.expression: By the standards of the conversation, fine

expressions.meaning: Mum's classificatory move: she places her own report on her state in the category of supplementary information, which signals that it should not be the topic of the conversation but should be available as data. C2 register: bibliographic vocabulary applied to spoken speech.
expressions.expression: An addendum rather than a substantive sentence

expressions.meaning: Mum's two-step refusal of one reading of her acceptance in favour of another. The dash between 'is' and 'operational' marks the moment Mum looks for the right word and chooses one Carlos has, indirectly through Sophie, supplied her with. C2 register: thinking visible in syntax.
expressions.expression: The acceptance is not, however, capitulation. The acceptance is — operational.

expressions.meaning: Mum's two-word distinction at the level of category: she is asking Sophie to provide a useful presence (one that adds value when present) rather than a necessary presence (one whose absence would constitute a failure). The phrase releases Sophie from any sense that she is being summoned, while making clear what would be welcome.

Mum's diagnostic stratification of her own affirmative answer to Sophie's third question. The phrase converts a single 'yes' into multiple temporally-indexed versions, each with different conditions of usefulness. C2 register: emotional truth treated as a multi-version document.
expressions.expression: The version of the yes that is true today is not the version that would be useful to discuss on a telephone

expressions.meaning: Mum's deferred-disclosure structure: the larger version may not need discussion ('if at all'), and if it does, the medium should be in-person rather than telephonic. The phrase 'if at all' is doing precise work — it preserves the option that the matter resolves itself.
expressions.expression: I would rather we discuss the larger version of the yes, if at all, in person

expressions.meaning: Mum's mock-modest disclaimer about her precision in describing Marie. The construction echoes Mark's L3 'He has had longer to compose it' about Henry's framing of Sophie. The shared phrase is a recurring courseworld figure for the relationship between time-spent-with and quality-of-description.
expressions.expression: I have had thirty years to compose it

content.cultural_note: The British telephone register Mum deploys in this dialogue belongs to a particular generational and class-specific mode of medical disclosure that deserves close attention. Her opening — 'I have a small piece of news that I would like to deliver in roughly four sentences' — performs three functions simultaneously. It dignifies the news by structuring it; it limits the disclosure (four sentences, not a paragraph); and it gives the listener structural permission to ask follow-up questions only after the four sentences have completed. This is not the register of denial or stoicism; it is the register of administrative care. Mum is treating her own diagnosis as information that requires careful handling, both for her own emotional management and for Sophie's. The four-sentence form is not a coping device; it is a courtesy, and Sophie reads it correctly. Note Mum's casual erudition in adopting Carlos's framing ('the acceptance is operational'): she has, at Easter, registered Sophie's report of Carlos's words as a usable phrase, kept it in reserve, and now retrieves it at the precise moment it serves her. C2 register, at this generational level, often operates as a long-running borrowing economy of phrases between people who do not all know each other. Mum has never met Carlos. Carlos's words are now available to Mum because Sophie carried them. The transmission is the work of family-as-network. Note also Mum's closing remark — 'I will, between now and Wednesday, do the things one does to prepare a small flat for a daughter who is staying for two nights. The list is short, and largely involves the bedding.' — which performs the closure-by-operation move that has become one of the courseworld's reliable structural devices. The serious conversation is sealed by a small domestic task. The bedding is the affection.
content.grammar_focus: Mum's central proposition uses a sustained C2 grammatical operation: the structured-disclosure-with-numbered-sentences. 'I have a small piece of news that I would like to deliver in roughly four sentences, and then we can discuss whatever follows from the four sentences. Are you in a position to receive four sentences.' The construction does several things: it pre-announces the form, it delimits the scope, it gives Sophie an opportunity to refuse if not ready, and it establishes the fourth-sentence boundary as the discussion-trigger. C2 register: meta-communicative architecture deployed in personal speech. Note the modal-conditional in the qualifier 'roughly' — Mum signals that the precise count is approximate, which preserves naturalness. Note also Mum's use of past perfect with a hypothetical adverbial in extended apposition: 'on the entirely defensible grounds that I have, for the last forty-eight years of adult life, managed most things alone, and the principle has not, until today, struck me as inadequate. The principle has, in the last hour and a half since I sat down to ring you, become — let us say, less entirely defensible than it had been at three o'clock.' Three temporal anchors ('forty-eight years', 'until today', 'in the last hour and a half'), three different aspectual relations to the principle ('have managed', 'has not struck', 'has become'). The grammar maps the development of Mum's stance across time-scales of decreasing duration. C2 prose can sustain this kind of three-layer temporal mapping when each layer is doing distinct semantic work. Note also the exchange about the third question: Mum's deferred-disclosure formula — 'the version of the yes that is true today is not the version that would be useful to discuss on a telephone on a Sunday afternoon' — uses a definite-article-plus-noun structure ('the version of the yes') treating an emotional answer as a documented entity with multiple released versions. The construction is C2-philosophical: feeling is rendered as text with versions. Sophie's response — 'I had been asking it of myself for the last six minutes. Asking it of you was the next step.' — uses past perfect continuous with a precise temporal anchor and a gerund-as-subject. The two sentences map a small interior process and its external completion. C2 register: the willingness to make one's own private process visible as a structured sequence.

key_vocabulary.note: Дегенеративное заболевание суставов; самая частая причина hip replacement у людей старше 60. Произносится 'OSS-tee-oh-arth-RY-tis'. Британский медицинский регистр.
Операция полной замены сустава. Стандарт NHS-практики при advanced osteoarthritis. Сокращение в медицинском британском: 'THR'.
Медицинский термин: лечение без хирургического вмешательства (физиотерапия, обезболивающие, пр.). Mum's previous treatment — conservative; теперь рекомендована операция.
В NHS многопрофильная команда (хирурги, anaesthetists, специализированные медсёстры) ведёт конкретного пациента. Mum пишет от их имени.
Реальная NHS-больница в Lancaster. 'Infirmary' — старомодное британское название для major hospital. Профессиональный регистр.
Латинское заимствование, юридический и академический термин: материал, добавленный к основному документу. Mum применяет к своему предложению о собственном состоянии. C2 регистр.
Юридический и медицинский британский термин: лицо, имеющее право принимать решения за пациента в его отсутствие сознания. Sophie — Mum's next-of-kin.
Mum: 'rather than waiting for me to volunteer the answer'. Глагол означает дать информацию, не будучи об этом спрошенным. C2 регистр.
Военно-политический термин: признание поражения. Mum использует, чтобы отвергнуть это прочтение её принятия даты операции. C2 транспозиция.
Британский медицинский разговорный: pre-operative период (admission, anaesthetic check, etc.). Стандарт перед major surgery.

comprehension_questions.hint: She is doing more than warning Sophie about the news. She is shaping the conversation that will follow. What kind of conversation is enabled by the four-sentence form, and what kind is prevented?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum opens with: 'I have a small piece of news that I would like to deliver in roughly four sentences. Are you in a position to receive four sentences.' Why does she structure her disclosure this way, and what does the pre-announcement give Sophie?

comprehension_questions.hint: Capitulation implies defeat; operational acceptance implies decision. What does each framing make available, and what does each foreclose?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum distinguishes between 'capitulation' and 'operational' acceptance, and explicitly retrieves a phrase Sophie reported from Carlos. What is the structural difference she is naming, and why does it matter to her that her acceptance falls in the second category?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is making a claim about emotional truth as multi-versioned and medium-dependent. Why is the telephone unsuitable for the larger version, and what does in-person availability provide that it cannot?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum says: 'The version of the yes that is true today is not the version that would be useful to discuss on a telephone on a Sunday afternoon.' What does she mean by treating her affirmative answer as having multiple versions, and why does she defer the larger version to Wednesday in person?

content.title: Two Days Before

dialogue.line: I have not, until packing this bag, been entirely able to believe that the operation is going to happen.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I have been treating the third of July as a date on a piece of paper. I have not been treating it as a Monday on which I will be in a hospital gown with my hair tied back, breathing into a mask while a man whose name I do not yet remember inserts a needle into the back of my left hand. The bag is, this afternoon, converting the date on the piece of paper into the Monday.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I would like, if you are willing, to hear the larger version of the yes you mentioned on Sunday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: All right. The larger version of the yes is approximately this. I am not, in any meaningful sense, frightened of dying in surgery. The mortality rate for elective hip replacement in a woman of my age and general health is, by the figures I have looked up, lower than the mortality rate for crossing the road in central Lancaster, and I have been crossing the road in central Lancaster for forty-eight years without daily anxiety about it. The death possibility is, in my mind, where it ought to be: present, statistically minor, not occupying the foreground.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: What I am frightened of is something else. I am frightened of becoming, after the third of July, a particular kind of person. The kind of person whose conversational opener, for the next ten years, is the operation. The kind of person who refers to her own body as if it were a piece of equipment requiring maintenance reports. The kind of person who, having had one significant medical event, organises her remaining decade around the prospect of the next one. I have known several such persons. Some of them have been my closest friends. I do not, with respect to them, intend to become one.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Can you say more about what specifically you are frightened of, in becoming that person.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am frightened of the slow narrowing of conversational territory. The moment one becomes the operation-person, certain conversations close. People stop telling you about their own difficulties because they assume your difficulties are larger. People stop offering invitations because they assume you will not manage the stairs, or the journey, or the late hour. People stop, gradually, asking what you think about anything other than your own physiology, because the physiology has become, in their reading of you, the most current information. I have watched this happen to women I have known well. I have watched it happen by accident, against everyone's intention. I am, I think, rationally frightened of the structural inertia of the position.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, that is — that is a precise piece of social analysis.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is. I have had, since the twenty-third, twenty-five days to make it precise. The precision is, in its way, the work I have been doing while the bag has been waiting to be packed.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I will, in the next ten years, refuse to allow your hip to become your conversational opener. I will continue to ask what you think about anything other than your own physiology. I will continue to invite you to things that involve stairs, journeys, and late hours, on the assumption that you will refuse the ones you do not wish to manage and accept the ones you do, and that the refusing and accepting are operational decisions about specific occasions rather than structural statements about who you have become.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That is — that is the most useful thing anyone could have said to me this afternoon.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I would also like to say one further thing, on a slightly different axis.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I am, in the small private part of myself that does not survive being phrased aloud well, frightened of you becoming a person whose hip is her conversational opener, because that person would be slightly less of you than I currently have, and I have, in the last two years, become quite attached to the version of you I currently have, and the prospect of less of her is — is not nothing, is what I would say, if pressed.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am phrasing it, you may notice, with the phrasing-aloud-of-something-that-does-not-survive-being-phrased-aloud-well.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I noticed.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Sophie. I would like, if you are willing, to make a small mutual undertaking that may help us both with the next ten years.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Whenever, in the next ten years, you suspect that I am beginning to drift toward becoming the operation-person, you will tell me, by some small signal we agree on now, that the drifting has begun. The signal does not have to be diplomatic. It can be — let us say a single word, in the middle of the relevant conversation, that I will recognise. The word will release us both from the conversation we were drifting into.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: What is the word.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What about — 'Bakewell'.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Bakewell has its own weather, as I once mentioned. The principle generalises. When, in any conversation, the weather begins to be the wrong weather, one of us says 'Bakewell' and the other one knows. The use of the word will not, I think, embarrass us. It is sufficiently strange to be functional and sufficiently familiar to be private.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Bakewell. Agreed.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The bag has, while we have been talking, finished packing itself. I will close it. We will have another cup of tea. And then I would like to ask you to help me decide whether the slippers I have packed are the right slippers, because I bought them yesterday and have not, until this afternoon, tried them on.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: We can do slippers.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

pratique.scenario: Imagine sitting with someone close to you who is two days away from a significant medical event. They are afraid not of the event itself but of becoming a particular kind of person afterward. Use Sophie's response: ask for the larger version of the fear, listen to the social analysis without rushing to reassure, make a specific commitment about how you will continue to relate to them, and propose a small mutual signal for use in the years ahead.
prompt_phrases: I would like, if you are willing, to hear the larger version of the [fear / yes / answer]…
I will, in the next [period], refuse to allow X to become Y. I will continue to ask…
Whenever, in the next [period], you suspect that I am beginning to drift toward [the position], you will tell me, by [agreed signal]…

content.situation: Wednesday afternoon, the twenty-eighth of June. Sophie has come up on the eleven-thirteen and is at Cedar View by half past one as promised. The flat is, in late June, particularly hers — afternoon sun across the kitchen, the small geranium on the sill that Mum has been encouraging since Easter, the radio on at the volume Mum prefers when she is doing something that requires half her attention. Mum is at the kitchen table sorting a small zip-up bag. The bag is the size of a school satchel and contains, by Sophie's quick observation, a nightie, two pairs of cotton trousers, a paperback, a small notebook, a charger, a toothbrush in its own case, and a pair of slippers Sophie has not seen before. Mum has been packing for forty minutes. Sophie has put the kettle on. The kettle has boiled. Sophie has made tea. Sophie has placed a cup at Mum's elbow. Mum has continued sorting. The conversation, when it begins, begins because Mum decides it will.

phrase_cle.phrase: Operational decisions about specific occasions rather than structural statements about who you have become.
phrase_cle.example: Don't go to the party if you don't want to. The not-going is an operational decision about Friday night, not a structural statement about being someone who no longer goes to parties.
phrase_cle.explanation: Sophie's формула — мощный C2-инструмент для защиты от social drift, в котором отдельные отказы или поведенческие выборы постепенно складываются в идентичность. Грамматически — два параллельных noun phrase через противопоставление 'rather than': 'operational decisions about X' vs 'structural statements about Y'. Operational и structural — пара, восходящая к L13 и Mum's собственному употреблению ('the acceptance is operational'). На C2 ты учишься различать эти уровни в собственных и чужих действиях: что есть однократный выбор для конкретной ситуации, а что есть заявление о том, кем ты являешься. Конструкция применима к любым ситуациям, в которых ты или собеседник опасаетесь, что отдельный выбор будет прочитан как тип. Расширения: 'a single absence rather than a pattern of withdrawal'; 'a particular refusal rather than a category of refusal'; 'a temporary preference rather than a settled disposition'. На C2 эти различения становятся автоматическими и защищают от ловушек, в которые попадает социальная инерция.
phrase_cle.translation: Операционные решения о конкретных случаях, а не структурные заявления о том, кем ты стала.

expressions.meaning: Mum's hyphenated noun-phrase for a social category she is afraid of becoming: a person whose primary public identity, after a medical event, is the medical event. The construction is C2-typical: hyphenation as compression of a long descriptor into a single named role.

Mum's compressed account of the social mechanism she fears: not a single dramatic exclusion but a gradual reduction in the range of topics she will be addressed about. The phrase 'conversational territory' is C2-original — geography applied to discourse — and 'slow narrowing' specifies the temporal and structural form of the loss.
expressions.expression: The slow narrowing of conversational territory

expressions.meaning: Mum's diagnostic for why becoming the operation-person is hard to escape once the position has been accepted: the social structure around the position pushes one further into it. 'Inertia' is borrowed from physics; 'the position' is borrowed from sociology. C2 transposition: technical-academic vocabulary in personal speech.
expressions.expression: The structural inertia of the position

expressions.meaning: Sophie's two-step distinction in her promise to Mum: refusing a particular invitation does not, by itself, mean having become a refusal-person. The contrast 'operational / structural' echoes Mum's vocabulary throughout L13 and her own self-diagnosis here, and Sophie deploys it to give Mum a usable defence against the drift.
expressions.expression: Operational decisions about specific occasions rather than structural statements about who you have become

expressions.meaning: Sophie's hyphenated long-noun phrase for what she has just done: said something whose substance is partly destroyed by the act of saying it. The construction performs what it describes — it is itself an awkward verbal object, and the awkwardness is part of the point. C2 register at full strength.

Mum's formal noun-phrase for an agreement between two parties to a shared course of action. 'Undertaking' is legal and parliamentary vocabulary; 'mutual' specifies that both parties commit. The phrase converts an emotional pact into a documented arrangement, which is Mum's signature register move.

Mum's two-clause justification for choosing 'Bakewell' as the signal-word. The pair of qualities ('strange enough to work as a flag, familiar enough to mean something to both of them') maps the exact requirements of an effective coded signal. C2 register: precision in specifying selection criteria.
expressions.expression: Sufficiently strange to be functional and sufficiently familiar to be private

expressions.meaning: Mum's mock-passive construction: the bag has not actually packed itself, but Mum's sustained attention to it during the conversation has resulted in completion. The phrase 'finished packing itself' converts the agent (Mum) into the object (bag) for comic-philosophical effect. C2 humour built on agency-shift.
expressions.expression: The bag has, while we have been talking, finished packing itself

content.cultural_note: Mum's social analysis in this dialogue belongs to a particular British register that British literary culture has captured occasionally — Anita Brookner, Penelope Lively, Hilary Mantel — but rarely at this density. The register depends on a specific kind of intellectual self-observation in which the speaker treats her own social trajectory as a piece of analysable data, applying sociological vocabulary ('the structural inertia of the position', 'the slow narrowing of conversational territory') to her own life without irony or apology. Mum has, by her own admission, spent twenty-five days making the analysis precise. The precision is itself a defence against the drift she is describing — she will, by having named it, be harder to deliver into. Sophie's three-part commitment is the appropriate British close-relationship reply: not reassurance, not denial, but specific operational promises about specific behaviours. The Bakewell signal-word agreement is a register move recognisable from longstanding British family life: small coded vocabularies that develop between two people across years and that allow communication in single words. C2 students should hear this as the most evolved form of family register: a single word ('Bakewell') will, for the next ten years, do the work that would otherwise require a paragraph. The phrase 'sufficiently strange to be functional and sufficiently familiar to be private' is Mum's specification of what makes a signal-word work: it must be unusual enough that its use in the wrong context would be conspicuous (and thus reliably triggered by drift), and familiar enough that both speakers recognise it without effort. The choice of 'Bakewell' specifically is not arbitrary: it was Mum's metaphor in C2 L2 for autonomous emotional climate, and now becomes the trigger word for protecting that climate. The course is making the long memory of language concrete.
content.grammar_focus: Mum's central self-description in this dialogue is built on a sustained C2 grammatical operation: the structured-fear in repeated definite-article-plus-noun categories. 'I am frightened of becoming, after the third of July, a particular kind of person. The kind of person whose conversational opener, for the next ten years, is the operation. The kind of person who refers to her own body as if it were a piece of equipment requiring maintenance reports. The kind of person who, having had one significant medical event, organises her remaining decade around the prospect of the next one.' Three parallel relative clauses, each modifying 'the kind of person', each with a different syntactic structure inside (genitive-relative, present-tense subject relative, participial-modified subject relative). The grammatical parallelism produces taxonomic precision: Mum is naming the category by enumerating its features. C2 prose can sustain this kind of taxonomic listing in spoken English when the speaker has actually thought about the category. Note also Mum's deployment of free indirect discourse in the social-analysis paragraph: 'People stop telling you about their own difficulties because they assume your difficulties are larger. People stop offering invitations because they assume you will not manage the stairs, or the journey, or the late hour.' Three parallel 'People stop X-ing Y because they assume Z' constructions, each describing a small mechanism. The 'they assume' is the FID move — Mum is voicing the unspoken reasoning of the people she has watched, presenting it as if from inside their thinking. C2 students should mark FID as a precision tool for representing the implicit logic of social drift. Sophie's promise — 'I will, in the next ten years, refuse to allow your hip to become your conversational opener.' — uses future simple with a temporal qualifier inserted between the modal and the verb. The same construction recurs in three parallel clauses, each starting with 'I will' and each adding a specific commitment. C2 register: future-as-binding-undertaking, with the parallelism enacting the comprehensiveness of the commitment. The signal-word agreement at the end uses a sustained future-conditional architecture: 'Whenever, in the next ten years, you suspect that I am beginning to drift, you will tell me…' — the universal-temporal 'whenever' clause with embedded present continuous ('am beginning to drift') and future simple consequent. C2 grammar at its quietest: the architecture of a long-running mutual practice rendered in two clauses.

key_vocabulary.note: Стандартная одноразовая рубашка для пациентов перед операцией. Британская медицинская норма; деталь, которую Mum приводит как конкретный образ предстоящего.
Перед общим наркозом анестезиолог надевает маску с предварительным газом. Стандартная процедура; Mum описывает её точно.
Стандартное место для cannula (внутривенного катетера) перед операцией. Mum описывает с медицинской точностью; знание процедуры — часть её подготовки.
key_vocabulary.word: the back of my left hand

key_vocabulary.note: Медицинский термин: операция, запланированная заранее, не экстренная. Hip replacement обычно elective. Mum использует это слово в обсуждении mortality rates.
Из социологии и физики: тенденция системы оставаться в текущем состоянии. Mum применяет к социальной позиции 'the operation-person'. C2 транспозиция.
Mum: 'The signal does not have to be diplomatic.' Идея в том, что между близкими сигналы могут быть прямыми, без социальной обработки. C2 регистр интимности.
Юридическая и парламентская лексика: соглашение между двумя сторонами действовать определённым образом. Mum применяет к договорённости с Sophie. C2 формальный регистр.
Морская и социальная метафора: постепенно перемещаться без явного намерения. Mum опасается drifting toward becoming the operation-person. C2 разговорный.
Sophie's фраза для уровня сознания, который не выдерживает прямого выражения. C2 регистр интроспекции.
key_vocabulary.word: a small private part of myself

key_vocabulary.note: Sophie's оценка речи Mum. Конструкция превращает Mum's размышления в академический жанр. C2 регистр: достижение, опознанное по своему жанру.
key_vocabulary.word: to make a precise piece of social analysis

comprehension_questions.hint: She has identified a specific social mechanism that operates against everyone's intention. Look at her account of 'the slow narrowing of conversational territory' and what makes it inevitable rather than personal.
comprehension_questions.question: Mum says she is not frightened of dying in surgery — 'the death possibility is, in my mind, where it ought to be: present, statistically minor, not occupying the foreground.' What is she frightened of instead, and why does she say her fear is 'rationally' rather than emotionally based?

comprehension_questions.hint: Reassurance is general; operational commitments are specific. What does Mum need that reassurance would not have provided?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie's three-part response promises specific behaviours — 'I will refuse to allow your hip to become your conversational opener. I will continue to ask. I will continue to invite.' Why does she make operational commitments rather than offer reassurance?

comprehension_questions.hint: A signal needs to be unmistakable when used and meaningful when received. What would happen if the word were too ordinary, and what if it were too obscure?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum proposes 'Bakewell' as a signal-word and specifies that the word must be 'sufficiently strange to be functional and sufficiently familiar to be private'. What are these two requirements doing, and why do they need to coexist?

lessons.title_en: The Corridor Has Given Me a Draft

content.title: The Corridor Has Given Me a Draft

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. The corridor has given me a draft.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I am borrowing your line. From C — from before.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It was your line first. You said it to Tom. I have been waiting four years to hear you use it back.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: She has been gone for thirty minutes. The operation is, I think, beginning now or beginning in twenty minutes; I have not been told the precise time. I am in the corridor because I do not yet know whether I want to be in the visitors' area, where there are other people in similar configurations, or whether I want to be alone.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph. Tell me what the draft is, in case the saying-it-aloud helps.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: The draft is — Marie, the draft is that I am, this morning, more like Tom than I had expected to be. I had expected to be the daughter who shows up, who handles the paperwork, who reads aloud if asked, who does not, in any registerable way, struggle. The version of me in the corridor is a version that is, in fact, struggling, in a small but specific way. I had not thought of myself as a person who would, at any point in this process, struggle. I had thought of struggling as Mum's department. The corridor has given me — has given me the information that the struggling is mine too, which is the kind of information one can only receive in an actual corridor, on an actual morning, after the porters have, in fact, taken the actual person down.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: That is the draft. The draft is good. The draft does not need editing this morning. Read it back to me one more time, slightly more slowly, so that the saying-it-aloud finishes its work.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I am, this morning, in a small but specific way, struggling. I had not expected to. I had thought of the struggling as Mum's department. It turns out it is not only Mum's department. It is also, at six fifty-two on the third of July, mine.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The draft is filed. Now you are allowed to either drink something from the vending machine or not, sit in the visitors' area or not, ring me again at any point in the next four hours or not, and the not-doing of any of those things is also a permitted use of the morning. I am your phone for the day. I will not be far from it. Olivia has nursery and Tom has work and I have the studio to myself until three.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I am — the small thing I want to say is that I am glad you are on the line.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The other thing I want to say, which you can ignore, is that the version of you in the corridor — the slightly struggling version — is not a downgrade of the version of you who handles paperwork. It is the same version, including the bit that is, on this morning, allowed to be the bit it is. Do not, in the next eight hours, treat the struggling as a small failure of the daughter-who-shows-up. The daughter who shows up includes, on the morning of a hip replacement, a daughter who is, in a small specific way, in a corridor. The corridor is part of the showing up.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. That is — that is the most useful sentence I have received in twenty-four hours.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I had been preparing it since yesterday evening. I had hoped you would not need it. Since you do, I am pleased it was, when needed, ready.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I am going to find the visitors' area.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Find the visitors' area. Drink something. Or do not. Ring me at any point. Or do not. The morning has been organised around you, by me, in a way that requires you to do nothing in response.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Soph.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Bakewell.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am using the word for myself, not for you. I am the one who was about to start telling you it would all be fine. The Bakewell is on me.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Mum told you about Bakewell.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: She rang me on Friday evening. We had a brief and businesslike conversation in the course of which she explained the protocol and asked whether I wished to be inducted into it. I accepted the induction. The word is now mutually licensed across three speakers.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie. Mum inducted you into Bakewell.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: She did.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I am going to find the visitors' area. I am going to ring you at lunchtime.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Lunchtime. Or before. Or after. Bakewell.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Imagine standing in a difficult place — a hospital corridor, a waiting room, an airport gate — while something significant is happening that you cannot influence. Ring someone whose presence by phone is enough. Use Sophie's frame: name what the place is giving you, name the version of yourself you had expected to be, name the version that has, in fact, arrived.
prompt_phrases: The corridor has given me a draft.
I had expected to be the [role] who [does X, Y, Z]. The version of me in [place] is a version that is, in fact, [struggling / faltering / quietly afraid].
I had thought of [the difficulty] as [the other person's] department. It turns out it is also mine.

content.situation: Monday morning, the third of July. Six fifty-two. Sophie is in a corridor on the second floor of the orthopaedic wing of the Royal Lancaster Infirmary. Mum was admitted yesterday evening; the porters came at six-twenty this morning to take her to pre-op. Sophie walked beside the trolley as far as the double doors at the end of the corridor and was, by the senior nurse, kindly redirected to the visitors' area. She has been in the corridor for thirty minutes. The corridor is quiet at this hour — fluorescent lights, the small sounds of a hospital waking, two cleaners with a trolley moving away from her. Sophie has not eaten. There is a vending machine she has not yet approached. She rings Marie at six fifty-two because Marie is the person who, in her recent memory, has the closest experience of standing in a corridor while a difficult thing is happening behind a door, and Sophie wants — she is not yet sure what she wants. Marie picks up on the third ring. Marie has been awake since six.

phrase_cle.phrase: The corridor is part of the showing up.
phrase_cle.example: I am at her hospital, but I am only sitting in the corridor, not doing anything visibly useful. That is fine. The corridor is part of the showing up.
phrase_cle.explanation: Marie's формула — мощная C2-конструкция против ложной дихотомии между «правильной» формой присутствия и «слабой» формой. На многих культурных уровнях существует невысказанное представление о том, что присутствие в трудный момент должно быть бодрым, организованным, полезным; всё, что не подпадает под это, читается как недостаточное. Marie отказывается от этой иерархии: коридор — не сбой в showing up, не его более слабая версия. Коридор сам по себе — часть его. Грамматически — простое утверждение через предлог 'part of', который превращает обстоятельства в составляющие: коридор включён в категорию showing up, а не противопоставлен ей. На C2 ты учишься распознавать в себе предположения о том, что 'правильное' присутствие имеет одну форму, и сопротивляться им. Расширения: 'the silence is part of the listening'; 'the not-knowing is part of the witnessing'; 'the small failures are part of the long competence'. Каждая конструкция — отказ от исключения трудных частей опыта из его законной формы.
phrase_cle.translation: Коридор тоже входит в то, чтобы прийти.

expressions.meaning: Sophie's reuse of her own phrase from C1 L21, originally offered to Tom on the morning he left for Newcastle. The phrase has, in the four years since, sat unused in the courseworld; Sophie deploys it now back at Marie, who recognises it instantly. C2 register: the long memory of one's own phrases applied to oneself.
expressions.expression: The corridor has given me a draft

expressions.meaning: Sophie's compressed phrase for the category of activities and emotional states she had assigned to Mum within the family division of labour. The corporate-bureaucratic noun ('department') applied to a parent's emotional role is dry C2 humour; the phrase converts an unconscious assumption into a usable named concept.

Sophie's calibration of her own struggling: not a large or general distress, but a localised and definable one. The pair of qualifiers ('small' / 'specific') keeps the report precise — she is reporting an actual condition, not performing emotion.

Marie's verb-shift converting the just-spoken account into a documented entity that no longer requires further articulation. The construction echoes Mum's L2 'a fact you were filing rather than reporting' — the recurring courseworld figure of filing as the act of putting something into ordered storage.

Marie's diagnostic: the version of Sophie in the corridor is not a lesser version of the competent-daughter version; the two versions are not on a hierarchy. The phrase 'a downgrade of the version' uses software-update vocabulary, applied to identity. C2 register: technical metaphors for selfhood.
expressions.expression: Not a downgrade of the version of you who handles paperwork

expressions.meaning: Marie's reframing: showing up does not exclude the difficulty of showing up; the difficulty is included in the act. The phrase converts the daughter-who-shows-up role from a demand for performance into a category that includes its own struggle.
expressions.expression: The corridor is part of the showing up

expressions.meaning: Marie's mock-formal acknowledgement that the Bakewell signal-word has now been authorised for use among Sophie, Mum, and Marie. The phrase 'mutually licensed' is legal and copyright vocabulary; 'across three speakers' makes the agreement multilateral. C2 humour applied to a private code.

Marie's compressed acknowledgement that she has triggered the signal — she was about to drift into reassurance, and has caught herself. The construction 'X is on me' converts responsibility into a possessive claim. C2 register: ownership of one's own near-mistakes.

content.cultural_note: This dialogue carries a particular kind of British close-friendship register: the morning-of-the-event phone call in which one party is in the difficult place and the other is at a desk in a different city, doing nothing more demanding than being available. Marie has, by her own admission, prepared a sentence in advance ('I had been preparing it since yesterday evening. I had hoped you would not need it.'). The willingness to prepare a sentence for someone — and the discipline to deliver it only when needed — is a quiet British register move that depends on long practice. Sophie's reuse of her own phrase from C1 L21 — 'the corridor has given me a draft' — is a small piece of self-quotation that the courseworld has been preparing for four years. In L21 of C1, Sophie offered the phrase to Tom as a release from the anxiety of not knowing in advance who he would be at the hospital with his father. Now Sophie deploys the phrase back at herself, in a parallel hospital corridor, to a different friend. The structure is the same; the speaker has shifted; the phrase has, in the interim, become a usable family object. C2 students should notice that the courseworld's language is now operating as a kind of inheritance — Sophie is not inventing a metaphor, she is retrieving one she once invented for someone else. The closing exchange involving 'Bakewell' performs the first deployment of the signal-word agreed in L14, four days earlier. Mum has, in the interim, telephoned Marie to induct her into the protocol — an off-stage act between Friday evening and now that Sophie discovers only through Marie's casual mention. The course has, since C1, made several large emotional moves through such off-stage events; here the device is operating at full strength. Mum has organised a multi-speaker support architecture without involving Sophie in the planning, on the principle that the architecture would be more useful to Sophie if she did not have to construct it.
content.grammar_focus: Sophie's central self-description in this dialogue uses a sustained C2 grammatical operation: the multi-versioned self-account through 'I had thought of X as Y' constructions. 'I had expected to be the daughter who shows up, who handles the paperwork, who reads aloud if asked, who does not, in any registerable way, struggle. The version of me in the corridor is a version that is, in fact, struggling, in a small but specific way. I had not thought of myself as a person who would, at any point in this process, struggle. I had thought of struggling as Mum's department.' Past perfect ('had expected', 'had not thought'), present tense for current state ('is, in fact, struggling'), and the reified noun-phrase ('Mum's department') for the category Sophie had unconsciously assigned. Three parallel relative clauses ('who shows up, who handles, who reads aloud if asked, who does not struggle') describe the version Sophie expected. C2 prose can sustain this kind of multi-layer self-description when the speaker is genuinely in the process of revising her self-conception. Note also Marie's diagnostic intervention: 'The version of you in the corridor — the slightly struggling version — is not a downgrade of the version of you who handles paperwork. It is the same version, including the bit that is, on this morning, allowed to be the bit it is.' The construction 'the bit that is allowed to be the bit it is' uses a relative clause with self-referential predicate (the X that is allowed to be X), which is C2-philosophical: a thing being allowed to be itself rather than required to be a smoothed version. Note the closing exchange — 'Bakewell.' / 'Marie.' / 'I am using the word for myself, not for you.' — which deploys the agreed signal-word in its first courseworld use, immediately followed by Marie's clarification of its direction.
