The signal is meta-linguistic — it interrupts the flow to redirect it. C2 register: a single word can do the work of an entire intervention when both speakers know what it means. The dialogue's grammatical signature is the use of recurring courseworld phrases in their second or third deployment: 'the corridor will give you a draft' (L21 of C1), 'a permitted use of X' (L17 of C1), 'Bakewell' (L2 of C2). Each phrase has, by this lesson, become a working concept that requires no introduction.

key_vocabulary.note: Архитектурно-функциональная единица большой больницы: целое крыло, отведённое под одну специальность. Royal Lancaster Infirmary имеет такое крыло; Mum's операция там.
Британские больничные сотрудники, перевозящие пациентов между отделениями. Не nurses; отдельная категория. Стандарт NHS-практики.
Британское название передвижной больничной кушетки на колёсах для транспортировки пациентов. Американское — 'gurney'. C2 британский медицинский регистр.
Специальная зона больницы для родственников, ожидающих пациентов в операционной или восстановлении. Стулья, иногда автоматы с напитками, тихая обстановка.
В больницах — стандартное оборудование для родственников. Sophie замечает его, не подходит. Деталь, маркирующая физическую реальность ожидания.
Mock-formal Marie's регистр. Глагол 'induct' (формально принять в группу или практику) применённый к private codeword. C2 ироничный регистр.
Из copyright и intellectual-property лексики. Marie применяет к Bakewell-protocol, mock-формально превращая частное соглашение в юридический акт. C2 транспозиция.
Из software-vocabulary: переход к менее функциональной версии. Marie применяет к личностной категории — отрицая, что corridor-version Sophie является downgrade. C2 транспозиция.
Hyphenated nominalisation. Marie's термин: 'the saying-it-aloud finishes its work'. C2 разговорный приём — превращать процесс в существительное для обсуждения.
Sophie's редкое прилагательное: 'in any registerable way'. Из 'register' (зафиксировать). Sophie признаёт, что её struggling может не выдавать себя внешне. C2 регистр самонаблюдения.

comprehension_questions.hint: The phrase was a metaphor for the corridor doing part of the speaker's identity work. What does its application to Sophie's own corridor on this morning reveal about the metaphor's range?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie says: 'The corridor has given me a draft' — quoting her own line from four years earlier, which she had originally said to Tom. Why does the phrase travel so well from one situation to another, and what does its return tell Sophie about herself?

comprehension_questions.hint: Sophie had been treating the struggling-version as a lesser form of the competent-version. Marie does not deny the difference; she relocates it. Where does she place it instead?
comprehension_questions.question: Marie's diagnostic — 'The version of you in the corridor is not a downgrade of the version of you who handles paperwork. It is the same version, including the bit that is, on this morning, allowed to be the bit it is.' — refuses a hierarchy Sophie was assuming. What was the hierarchy, and what alternative model does Marie offer?

comprehension_questions.hint: Mum could have asked Sophie to organise this. She chose not to. What does the choice protect, and what does it provide?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum has, between Friday and now, telephoned Marie to induct her into the Bakewell-protocol — an act Sophie discovers only through Marie's mention in this lesson. What does Mum's off-stage organising of a multi-speaker support architecture reveal about her preparation for the operation?

content.title: Andrew

dialogue.line: Coffee. Bless you.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: And a pastry. The choice is between an almond croissant and something I think is a Danish but might be something more architectural.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I will take the almond. The architectural one, you should keep, on the grounds that nobody on this ward is in a position to identify it with certainty, and the uncertainty would, eventually, undermine my recovery.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: How are you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am, by the standards of the conversation, fine. I have been, since Tuesday morning, in the slow process of becoming acquainted with a body that has been altered in a way I am still negotiating with. The negotiation is, I suspect, the rest of the year. The hip itself is — early indications are that the hip itself will, in due course, be a quietly successful piece of engineering.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Good.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I want to tell you something while I have, this afternoon, the sustained attention to tell it. I am telling you because I have, in the last four days, become slightly clearer about which things I would like to have said before I forget to say them, and this is one of them. I do not propose to make a habit of bedside disclosures. I am making this one because the moment is structurally available and the moment will not, in the ordinary course of life, recur with the same clarity. Are you in a position to hear it.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Yes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The thing is about Andrew. You will remember that I mentioned Andrew, in passing, last summer, in the context of a letter you were not invited to read. I had not, then, intended to say more. I am, today, going to say slightly more. I would like you to receive what I say without expanding it into a larger conversation, on the grounds that the expansion would be the ordinary course of life and we are not, this afternoon, in the ordinary course of life.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Understood.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Andrew was a man I knew between 1988 and 1991. He was a research fellow at Lancaster — political theory, moderately well thought of — and I was a junior librarian at the university library. We had what I would now describe as a substantial relationship and what I would, at the time, have described as the relationship that I had been waiting to have. We did not, in the end, marry. He took a position at a Canadian university in 1991 and asked me to come with him. I did not go.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I did not go because — and I want to be precise about this, because the precision is what I am giving you — I did not go because I had, by 1991, accumulated a particular life in Lancaster that I was not, at that point in my development, ready to dismantle. The life included a flat I had bought, a job I had grown into, three friendships that have proved durable, and a sense of geographical settlement that I had been, until Andrew, treating as provisional. The encounter with Andrew clarified that the geographical settlement was not, in fact, provisional — it was structural. The clarification was useful. The price of the clarification was Andrew.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Andrew wrote to me three times in 1992 and twice in 1993. The letters were good letters. I wrote back twice. The second time I wrote, I told him that I would not, in any continuing way, write back, on the grounds that the writing-back was producing in him a hope I could not, in good conscience, sustain. He understood. He did not write again. I have, in the thirty-three years since, occasionally heard things about him through mutual acquaintances. He married a Canadian woman in 1996. They had two children. He is, by the most recent report, professor emeritus at his university and a person of moderate continuing achievement. I do not know whether he is well, today, but I have no reason to suspect he is not.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am telling you this not because Andrew was the love of my life — he was not — and not because the choice not to go to Canada was a tragedy — it was not. I am telling you because Andrew is the only piece of my own history I have not, at any point, told you, and I have, in the last four days, decided that the not-telling was a small administrative oversight rather than a settled position. The settled position would require ongoing maintenance, and I do not propose to spend whatever years I have ahead maintaining the silence. The silence, today, is hereby retired.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, can I ask one question.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Did Henry know about Andrew.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Henry knew that there had been someone before him. He did not know the name, the nationality, the duration, or the details. He did not, to my knowledge, ask. The not-asking was, I think, one of the kinder things he did in our early years.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Thank you for telling me.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. The instruction was to receive what I said without expanding it into a larger conversation. Receive it. Drink your coffee. Eat your architectural pastry. I will, in the next ten minutes, attempt to walk to the window and back, which is the goal the physiotherapist has set for this afternoon.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Can I help.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You can be in the chair when I sit down again. The being-there is sufficient. The walking is mine.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: All right.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Pass me the stick. The one with the rubber base. The other one is for ornamental purposes only and I have been told, by everyone with authority on the matter, not to use it.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

pratique.scenario: Imagine telling someone close to you a piece of your own history that you have, for whatever reason, never told them. Use Mum's frame: pre-announce the disclosure with limited scope; instruct the listener to receive without expanding; give the precise version once and only once; close by retiring the silence as a formal act.
prompt_phrases: I want to tell you something while I have, this [moment], the sustained attention to tell it…
I would like you to receive what I say without expanding it into a larger conversation…
The silence, today, is hereby retired.

content.situation: Friday afternoon, the seventh of July, four days after the operation. Sophie has come back up to Lancaster on the eight-thirty from Manchester; she had been planning to come on Saturday, but Mum, on Wednesday evening by phone, had said that Friday afternoon would suit better, on the grounds that she had been transferred that morning to the rehabilitation ward and was, by her own report, in possession of more sustained attention than she had had in the previous three days. The rehabilitation ward is on the third floor of the orthopaedic wing — a smaller, quieter space than the post-op recovery ward where Sophie had first found her on Tuesday afternoon. Mum is in a four-bed bay; the curtain around her bed is half-drawn. The other three beds are occupied by women between roughly seventy and ninety, two of them dozing, one reading a magazine. Mum is in the chair beside her bed, not in the bed; the physiotherapist has, that morning, encouraged her to sit. She is wearing her own dressing gown, which Sophie packed in the bag in C2 L14, and a pair of socks. The slippers are not in evidence. Sophie has brought a small flask of proper coffee and two pastries from the bakery near the hospital. The pastries are wrapped in paper. Mum looks tired, but with the particular tiredness of recovery rather than of distress — the body using its energy efficiently, somewhere internal.

phrase_cle.example: I have not told you about my first marriage. I have not, in any active sense, hidden it; I have simply not mentioned it. The not-mentioning has, by inertia, become a position. The silence, today, is hereby retired.
phrase_cle.explanation: Mum's формула — компрессированное закрытие практики не-говорения. Грамматически: subject (the silence) + adverb 'hereby' (которое в юридической английской лексике означает 'by means of this very statement', т. е. высказывание само производит действие, о котором говорит) + verb 'retired' в passive past. Конструкция превращает прекращение молчания в формальный акт, как уход в отставку. На C2 ты учишься различать молчание-как-secret (активное удержание информации) и молчание-как-administrative-oversight (пассивное не-доведение информации до внимания). Mum не призналась в подавлении; она признала отсутствие проактивности. Закрытие через 'hereby retired' — мягкое: не драматизирует прошлое не-говорение, но и не делает вид, что его не было. Расширения: 'the unspoken topic is hereby raised'; 'the open question is hereby retired'; 'the indefinite arrangement is hereby formalised'. Каждая конструкция — превращение неоформленного состояния в оформленное через один перформативный акт.

expressions.meaning: Mum's recurring qualifier: 'fine' specified by reference to the scope of the conversation, which prevents misreading. The phrase first appeared in L13 and is now established as one of Mum's verbal signatures. C2 register: relativised vocabulary as a recurring courseworld figure.
expressions.expression: By the standards of the conversation, fine

expressions.meaning: Mum's diagnostic for her current relation to her post-operative physiology: not 'recovered from' or 'damaged by' but 'in negotiation with', which implies a long bilateral process. The verb 'negotiating with' (rather than 'adjusting to') treats the body as an interlocutor. C2 register: technical-political vocabulary applied to physiology.
expressions.expression: A body that has been altered in a way I am still negotiating with

expressions.meaning: Mum's classification of a working artificial hip joint. The phrase 'quietly successful' is doing precise work — it specifies that the success is invisible, undramatic, structural; 'a piece of engineering' converts a body part into a manufactured object that can be evaluated by its category. C2 transposition.
expressions.expression: A quietly successful piece of engineering

expressions.meaning: Mum's compressed phrase for the genre of confession people make from hospital beds. The construction allows her to name and immediately disclaim membership in the genre — she will make one disclosure but does not propose to make a habit of them. C2 register: genre-naming as defence against the genre's expectations.

Mum's two-step reclassification of the silence around Andrew: not a deliberate withholding but a piece of unfinished filing. The reframing converts decades of not-telling into a category that can be addressed in ten minutes rather than into a substantive secret. C2 register: bureaucratic vocabulary as the mode of difficult disclosure.
expressions.expression: A small administrative oversight rather than a settled position

expressions.meaning: Mum's mock-legal closing of the not-telling. 'Hereby' (legal-formal: by means of this very statement) and 'retired' (taken out of active service) convert a continuing absence into a documented termination. C2 humour built on legal vocabulary applied to private experience.

Mum's two-clause distinction between two roles in the immediate task: Sophie's witnessing presence and Mum's autonomous physical work. The construction gerund-as-subject ('the being-there', 'the walking') treats both contributions as equal categories of activity. C2 register: hyphenated and gerundial nouns making relational architecture visible.
expressions.expression: The being-there is sufficient. The walking is mine.

expressions.meaning: Mum's mock-bureaucratic phrase for the wrong walking-stick. The construction belongs to museum and product-warning vocabulary ('not for human consumption', 'for display only'); Mum applies it to a domestic object she has been told not to use. C2 humour: institutional vocabulary at home.

content.cultural_note: Mum's disclosure about Andrew belongs to a particular register of British generational confession that the courseworld has been preparing for since B2, when Mum first mentioned the existence of a letter Sophie was not invited to read. The decision to disclose now — from a hospital chair, four days after major surgery, with an explicit instruction not to expand the conversation — is doing several things at once. It uses the structural availability of the moment (Mum's heightened sense of mortality and clarified attention after the operation) to release a piece of family history without giving it the weight of a deathbed scene. It hands Sophie a portable piece of biographical information that can be quietly absorbed rather than dramatically processed. And it converts what might have been treated, in a different register, as a major reveal into what Mum classifies as 'a small administrative oversight'. C2 students should hear that the genre-defiance is itself the work: by refusing to grant Andrew the status of a withheld secret, Mum prevents the disclosure from being mythologised. The quiet detail — Andrew was a Canadian-bound political theorist, the relationship lasted three years, Mum chose Lancaster over him, he wrote five letters and stopped — is delivered with the precision of an annotated entry rather than the texture of a memoir. Note the closing detail about the walking-stick — the one 'with the rubber base' versus the one 'for ornamental purposes only'. British post-operative life, particularly for older patients, often involves multiple walking-sticks acquired across decades, each with a particular function and a particular emotional weight. The ornamental one was probably a gift; the rubber-based one is the medically correct one. Mum's mock-bureaucratic language ('I have been told, by everyone with authority on the matter, not to use it') protects her dignity around the practical accommodation. The closing of a chapter on Andrew is followed immediately by the practical work of the afternoon — walking to the window. The course is staging the return to operational life as itself the proper closure of the disclosure.
content.grammar_focus: Mum's central narrative reconstruction in this dialogue uses a sustained C2 grammatical operation: the multi-tense biographical compression. 'Andrew was a man I knew between 1988 and 1991. He was a research fellow at Lancaster — political theory, moderately well thought of — and I was a junior librarian at the university library. We had what I would now describe as a substantial relationship and what I would, at the time, have described as the relationship that I had been waiting to have. We did not, in the end, marry.' Past simple ('was', 'had', 'did not marry') for the events; present continuous-as-evaluation ('I would now describe') for current view; past perfect continuous in counterfactual past ('I had been waiting to have') embedded in modal-perfect past ('would have described'). The sentence layers four temporal positions — events in 1988-1991, the speaker's view in 1988-1991, the speaker's view today, and what was being expected in the 1980s — without the listener losing track. C2 prose can sustain this kind of layered temporality when the narrative axis (the relationship in chronological order) holds the structure even as the perspectives shift. Note also Mum's deployment of the inverted past perfect with subjunctive correlative: 'I had, by 1991, accumulated a particular life in Lancaster that I was not, at that point in my development, ready to dismantle.' Past perfect ('had accumulated') with mid-sentence temporal anchor ('by 1991'), followed by past simple negative with embedded temporal qualifier ('was not, at that point in my development, ready to dismantle'). The clause structure performs the architecture it describes — Mum's life in 1991 was structured, and the description of it is also structured. Note also Mum's use of nominalised actions through hyphenation: 'the writing-back was producing in him a hope I could not, in good conscience, sustain'. The compound subject 'the writing-back' converts a habitual activity into a substantive that can be the subject of 'was producing'. C2 prose at its most economical. The closing exchange — 'You can be in the chair when I sit down again. The being-there is sufficient. The walking is mine.' — uses three short utterances each performing a discrete operation: assignment of role, classification of contribution, claim of autonomy. The grammar carries the boundary work.

key_vocabulary.note: Recurring from C1 L24. NHS-практика: после major surgery пациентов переводят в специализированное отделение для восстановления и физиотерапии. Стандарт для hip replacement.
Стандартная архитектурная единица NHS-отделений. 'Bay' — отгороженная зона с несколькими кроватями, между ними занавески. Британский медицинский регистр.
Британский эквивалент американского 'physical therapist'. Ключевая фигура в hip replacement recovery; отвечает за прогресс пациента в первые недели.
Латинский академический титул: профессор, ушедший в отставку, но сохраняющий академическое признание. Mum применяет к Andrew. Британский академический регистр.
Британское академическое звание: исследовательская позиция, обычно после докторской и до постоянного места. Andrew был research fellow в Lancaster в 1988-1991.
Mum's профессия в момент знакомства с Andrew. В University library — академический библиотекарь. C2 деталь, маркирующая её социальный круг и интеллектуальное окружение.
Британская моральная фраза: действие, совершённое в соответствии с собственным моральным суждением. Mum: 'a hope I could not, in good conscience, sustain'. Этический регистр.
Mum's оценка Andrew's карьеры: не provincial, не stellar; устойчивое профессиональное продолжение. Британский understatement.
Юридическое и формальное наречие: 'by means of this very statement'. Performative использование: высказывание само производит действие. C2 формальный регистр.
Музейная и продуктовая лексика, применённая к ходунку-палке. Mum mock-формально называет неправильную палку. C2 ироничный регистр.

comprehension_questions.hint: Settled position would require ongoing maintenance and would carry secrecy. Oversight is something one corrects in ten minutes. What does the reframing release Mum from, and what does it release Sophie from?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum classifies her decades of not-telling Sophie about Andrew as 'a small administrative oversight rather than a settled position'. What is the difference, and why does the reclassification matter to her?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is not saying Andrew was a mistake. She is saying he served a particular epistemic role. What does the relationship reveal that she could not have known otherwise, and how does she price the revelation?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum says she did not go to Canada with Andrew because 'the encounter with Andrew clarified that the geographical settlement was not, in fact, provisional — it was structural. The clarification was useful. The price of the clarification was Andrew.' What is she claiming about the function of the relationship in her own life?

comprehension_questions.hint: The disclosure was a piece of personal history Mum gave Sophie. The walking is something Mum will not give to Sophie. What is being protected, and how does the structure of the role-assignment mirror the structure of the disclosure?
comprehension_questions.question: After the disclosure Mum says: 'You can be in the chair when I sit down again. The being-there is sufficient. The walking is mine.' How does this small instruction relate to the larger conversation that has just finished?

content.title: By Post

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. You sound unusually direct on the phone this morning.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I have a small operational matter to bring to you, and I would prefer to bring it briefly rather than by gradual approach. Are you in a position to receive it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am sitting in my chair with a cup of tea. The phone is in my hand. The morning is, in all other respects, slow. Bring it.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I have, this morning, received a letter from Henry. The letter is short. It contains, in addition to a brief enquiry about how you are recovering from the operation — about which he has, somehow, been informed, possibly by Margaret Eyre's niece — a single proposal which I will quote verbatim because the wording is the substance.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Quote it.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: 'I have been thinking. Send me a date you would like me to ring your mother. I will only do it if she would welcome it. You may say no on her behalf without consulting her.'
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Read it again, Sophie. More slowly.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: 'I have been thinking. Send me a date you would like me to ring your mother. I will only do it if she would welcome it. You may say no on her behalf without consulting her.'
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: There are several things I want to do at once, and I am going to do them in series rather than in parallel because the parallel processing is, this week, beyond me.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Take your time.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: First. I want to acknowledge that Henry has, in writing this letter, demonstrated the same fundamental quality I had recognised in him in 1989 and which had survived, apparently, the intervening thirty-six years. The quality is — let us call it the quality of producing the most calibrated possible offer at the moment of producing any offer at all. The offer he has made through you is exactly the offer that would, in fact, be acceptable to me, framed in the only terms in which it would be acceptable to me. I want, separately, to register some mild astonishment that he has, in his late sixties, retained the calibration.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Second.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Second. I would like to accept the offer. Once. The accepting is not — Sophie, the accepting is not a resumption. The accepting is a single instance. I would like to have, with him, by phone, a brief conversation, and I would like the conversation to be sufficient unto itself, by which I mean that I do not, after the conversation, intend further conversations. The single instance is the whole arrangement. He should know this in advance, so that he is not, in advance or in subsequent reflection, expecting more.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Understood.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Third. I want to decide on the date now, while I have a small clear window of attention, rather than postpone the decision and accumulate the burden of having to make it. The date I would propose is — let me think — the second of August, which is approximately four weeks from now and which would coincide with the point at which I will be, by the physiotherapy timetable, walking with one stick rather than two. I would prefer to take the call as a person who can stand up at the end of it, even if I am, for the call itself, sitting.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, that is — that is a precisely judged date.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. I have, since the operation, found that my judgement on small dates has improved. I attribute this to having, for the first time in a year, slightly less to think about. The hip recovery has, somewhat counterintuitively, simplified my schedule.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Fourth thing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Fourth. I want, before this call ends, to instruct you on the form in which you reply to Henry.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Reply by post, not by phone or text, on the principle that he wrote on paper and the form should match. The reply should be brief — three or four lines — and should contain, in order: confirmation that he has been correctly informed about the operation; my acceptance of his offer; the proposed date and approximate time, which I would suggest be eleven in the morning my time on the second of August; and a single sentence in your own voice indicating that you have, in passing this on, not, in any active sense, advocated for the call. The fact that the call is happening should be visible to him as a decision of mine rather than as a recommendation of yours. Is that achievable.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: It is achievable.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Write it today, post it tomorrow, and let me know when it is in the post so that I can, between then and the second of August, do whatever interior preparation the call will require. I do not, today, know what that preparation will look like. I am confident I will recognise it when it begins.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: There is one thing I would like to ask, which you can decline to answer.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Why, after the call, do you not intend further conversations.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Because the conversation I will have with him on the second of August will be the conversation that should, in the natural course of two reasonable adults' lives, have happened sometime around 2005, and the having of it now will be, in itself, the entire correction of the omission. The correction is single-instance. Repeated correction would be the inverse problem — would create, by accumulation, exactly the structural arrangement neither of us, on the basis of available evidence, wishes to have. The single instance is the proper closure. I am, today, in a position to know the difference between proper closure and protracted continuation, and I am choosing the first.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, that is — that is also a precise piece of analysis.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have, in the last fortnight, become slightly disciplined about distinctions of this kind. The discipline is, I suspect, a temporary side-effect of the operation. I am enjoying it while it lasts.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I will write the letter today.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: The other thing in Henry's letter was a photocopy of a page from his thrush notebook. The page is from November. There are twelve lines of observation. The bird was on the laurel at half past eleven on the eighth, eating a snail at quarter to twelve, and gone by twelve-fifteen. Henry has noted at the bottom that the snail was, by his judgement, a fortunate one, which I think is a small joke.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Henry has, after thirty-five years, sent me a piece of his private practice. By way of you. Without being asked. I had not — I had not, frankly, anticipated that. I am, this morning, slightly more affected by the photocopy than I am by the rest of the letter, and I am not sure what to do with that, and I am also not sure I have to do anything with it today.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: You do not have to do anything with it today.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. Bakewell.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Bakewell.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am using the word for myself again. The drift was beginning. The drift has been arrested.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

pratique.scenario: Imagine receiving a letter on behalf of someone else — your parent, your partner, a friend — that proposes a specific, bounded, contingent action. Use Sophie's relay protocol: bring the proposal directly rather than gradually; quote the precise wording; let the recipient decide; carry the decision back in the form they specify.
prompt_phrases: I would prefer to bring it briefly rather than by gradual approach. Are you in a position to receive it?
I will quote it verbatim because the wording is the substance.
I want, before this call ends, to instruct you on the form in which you reply…

content.situation: Thursday morning, the thirteenth of July, six days after Sophie's afternoon at the rehabilitation ward. The post has arrived at Sophie's flat at twenty past nine — a thin envelope in Henry's handwriting, with a small folded pamphlet inside. Sophie is at the kitchen table with toast and a coffee. She opens the envelope. Inside there are two items: a typed letter on plain paper, signed Henry, and a photocopy of one page from a small notebook, with a date in the top corner — the eighth of November of the previous year — and twelve lines of observation about a song thrush. The thrush is the one from C2 L5. The letter is short. The course will not show the letter in full; it will show only the line that matters, which is what Henry has asked. Sophie reads the letter twice. She rings Mum at twenty past ten. Mum is at home — she was discharged on the Saturday after L16 and has been at Cedar View, with twice-weekly physiotherapy visits, since then. The conversation between Sophie and Mum, and the subsequent call between Henry and Mum, are the lesson. The call between Henry and Mum will, however, not be staged. The lesson ends before it begins.

phrase_cle.example: I would like one conversation. Not a series. One conversation, sufficient unto itself, after which we both go back to our lives.
phrase_cle.explanation: Mum's формула — компрессированная конструкция для бытия чего-либо как полного события, не требующего продолжения. Грамматически — формула из старого английского с предлогом 'unto' (= to, towards) в архаической форме. 'Sufficient unto X' означает: достаточно для нужд самого X, ничего дополнительного не требуется. Mum применяет это к проектируемому звонку с Henry: разговор должен быть законченным событием, не первым в серии, не основанием для дальнейшего общения. На C2 ты учишься распознавать ситуации, в которых единичное событие должно остаться единичным, и не позволять ему превратиться в начало последовательности через инерцию. Эта формула также эхом отзывается в Carlos's 'consolidating week' и в Mum's собственном 'I do not believe in distributing uncertainty' — это всё одна семья принципов, которая регулирует масштаб эмоциональных операций. Расширения: 'one apology, sufficient unto itself'; 'one visit, sufficient unto itself'; 'one explanation, sufficient unto itself'. Каждая конструкция защищает событие от превращения в начало повторяющейся практики, которой говорящий не желает.

expressions.meaning: Sophie's compressed phrase for the alternative method she is rejecting — easing into the topic through preliminary conversation rather than naming it directly. The construction is C2-typical: meta-communicative naming of the conversational genre being avoided.

Sophie's principle for why she will quote Henry's proposal verbatim rather than paraphrase: in this case, the precise phrasing carries the meaning, and any rewording would lose part of the offer. The phrase converts a quotation choice into a structural claim about Henry's writing.

Mum's borrowed phrase from electrical engineering and software vocabulary: tasks performed one after another rather than simultaneously. Mum applies it to her own current cognitive capacity, which post-operatively is single-threaded. C2 transposition: technical metaphor for personal state.
expressions.expression: In series rather than in parallel

expressions.meaning: Mum's compressed verdict on Henry's quality: every offer he makes is, structurally, the most precisely-tuned offer that situation allows. The phrase 'calibrated' is recurring courseworld figure (introduced about Mum's mother in C1 L8, now applied to Henry's letter-writing). C2 register at full strength.
expressions.expression: The most calibrated possible offer at the moment of producing any offer at all

expressions.meaning: Mum's archaic-literary phrase ('sufficient to its own needs', 'requiring nothing further') for what the proposed call should be: a single bounded event that does not generate obligations or expectations afterwards. The phrasing is biblical-adjacent and Mum uses it without apology. C2 register: archaic vocabulary deployed precisely.

Mum's precise distinction between two possible framings of the same fact: her acceptance of the call should appear, to Henry, as her choice rather than as Sophie's lobbying. The construction protects two things — Mum's agency in the decision, and Sophie's neutrality in the relay. C2 register: structural attribution.
expressions.expression: Decision of mine rather than recommendation of yours

expressions.meaning: Mum's diagnostic for the call: it is a delayed regular conversation rather than a special event. The phrase 'in the natural course of two reasonable adults' lives' relativises the call against an idealised baseline; 'sometime around 2005' specifies the original window. C2 register: counterfactual normality used as the standard.
expressions.expression: The conversation that should, in the natural course of two reasonable adults' lives, have happened sometime around 2005

expressions.meaning: Mum's mock-formal closure of her own near-drift toward over-affected reaction. 'Arrested' (legal: stopped by formal action) applied to a personal cognitive movement. The phrase performs the Bakewell-protocol's function — naming the drift and stopping it in the same utterance. C2 register at full strength.

content.cultural_note: This lesson uses ellipsis as its central structural device, in a way the courseworld has been preparing since C1 L29. The actual call between Henry and Mum on the second of August is not staged — it happens off-screen, between L17 and L18. The lesson stages only the relay through Sophie. C2 students should read this as a deliberate compositional choice: the most significant event of the block — the first conversation between Mum and Henry in twenty-two years — is precisely the event the course refuses to dramatise. The course's ethical position is consistent: events of this kind belong to the people having them, not to the reader. Sophie does not know the contents of the call afterward, and the course will not know them either. What we will see, in L18, is only Mum's quiet reference to it. Note also the device of the photocopied notebook page. Henry has, in addition to the letter, sent a photocopy of one page from the thrush notebook. The notebook was introduced in L5 as a private practice Henry had not, until that lesson, shared with anyone. He has now, six months later, sent a sample to Mum — through Sophie — without commentary. Mum recognises immediately that this is a piece of Henry's private practice being released into the world for the first time, and she is more affected by the photocopy than by the rest of the letter. C2 students should hear the exchange as the courseworld's quietest piece of intimacy: a man sends, through his estranged daughter, a page of bird observations to the mother of his child, and she understands without being told what the gesture means. The Bakewell-protocol triggers correctly at the close of the conversation. Mum names the drift on herself, names that she is naming it, and the conversation closes. The signal-word agreed in L14 has now operated three times across two speakers in two different conversations. The protocol is established as working. It will, the course is gently signalling, continue working for the next decade.
content.grammar_focus: Mum's central response in this dialogue uses a sustained C2 grammatical operation: the numbered-list structured-disclosure deployed at length. The four-part architecture — 'First. I want to acknowledge… Second. I would like to accept… Third. I want to decide on the date now… Fourth. I want, before this call ends, to instruct you…' — is the same structured-disclosure form Edward used in C2 L7 and C2 L12, now in Mum's mouth. C2 students should mark this as a register move that has migrated across speakers in the courseworld: a particular kind of British educated speaker uses numbered structures for important communications, and the form is portable across speakers who share the register. Note also Mum's use of inverted constructions and parenthetical inserts: 'The fact that the call is happening should be visible to him as a decision of mine rather than as a recommendation of yours. Is that achievable.' Final word ('achievable') with question intonation but no question mark — a C2 speech feature where the interrogative is delivered with falling intonation, as a kind of operational checking rather than open inquiry. Note Mum's deployment of present-perfect-with-mid-clause-temporal-anchor: 'Henry has, after thirty-five years, sent me a piece of his private practice.' The temporal anchor 'after thirty-five years' splits the present perfect verb phrase, which is C2-typical for foregrounding the temporal weight. The reverse-cleft construction — 'The wording is the substance' (rather than 'The substance is in the wording' or 'It is the wording that is the substance') — is C2-precise: it uses the simplest copular form to assert identity rather than property. Sophie's quotation-protocol — 'I will quote it verbatim because the wording is the substance' followed by the actual quotation, then 'Read it again, Sophie. More slowly.' — performs the same kind of register-matching that the courseworld has been staging since C1 L29 (Sophie's letter to Henry that matched his own structure). The closing exchange — 'Bakewell.' / 'Bakewell.' / 'I am using the word for myself again. The drift was beginning. The drift has been arrested.' — uses the signal-word in its second courseworld deployment (after L15), with explicit naming of the direction (Mum is using it on herself). The grammar of self-policing is precise: a single word, then explicit attribution.

key_vocabulary.note: Латинское заимствование: слово в слово. Используется в правовом и журналистском регистрах. Sophie применяет к цитированию Henry's формулировки. C2 регистр.
Из электротехники и компьютерных систем: serial vs parallel processing. Mum применяет к собственной когнитивной функции после операции. C2 транспозиция.
key_vocabulary.word: in series rather than in parallel

key_vocabulary.note: Recurring courseworld figure (Mum's mother L3 C1; Marie's body L25 C1; Sophie's voice L29 C1; Tom's mother's account L21 C1). На C2 — рабочая лексика.
Mum's термин для проектируемого звонка с Henry: одно событие, не первое в серии. C2 рабочая категория.
Архаическая английская конструкция с предлогом 'unto'. Регистр: библейско-литературный. Mum применяет без апологии. C2 регистр.
Из компьютерной лексики. Mum: 'the parallel processing is, this week, beyond me'. Точный технический термин в личной речи. C2 транспозиция.
Mum's фраза для того, что она должна сделать между сегодняшним днём и звонком 2 августа. Не описывает; говорит, что узнает, когда начнётся. C2 эпистемический регистр.
Юридический термин: активно поддерживать позицию. Mum инструктирует Sophie не advocate в письме Henry. Точное разделение ролей.
Recurring courseworld figure. Mum применяет к социальной конфигурации, которая возникла бы, если бы звонок Henry-Mum стал серией. C2 рабочая лексика.
Юридическое и навигационное использование 'arrest'. Mum применяет к собственному эмоциональному состоянию: 'the drift has been arrested'. C2 mock-формальный регистр.

comprehension_questions.hint: He is giving Sophie a particular kind of authority while simultaneously protecting Mum's agency. What is the authority, and what does it protect?
comprehension_questions.question: Henry's letter contains the line: 'You may say no on her behalf without consulting her.' What is the structural courtesy of this clause, and what does it allow Sophie to do that a less-precise letter would not?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is protecting against accumulation. What kind of structural arrangement would multiple conversations produce, and why does she not want it?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum says she would like 'one conversation, sufficient unto itself, by which I mean that I do not, after the conversation, intend further conversations.' What is the difference between a single instance and the start of a series, and why does Mum want Henry to know the distinction in advance?

comprehension_questions.hint: The notebook is a private practice Henry had kept for many months. The photocopy is a sample of that practice released without commentary. What is being communicated by the gesture itself, separately from anything written?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum is, by her own admission, 'slightly more affected by the photocopy than by the rest of the letter'. What does the photocopy of one page of the thrush notebook represent, and why is it more significant than the proposal of a phone call?

content.title: After

dialogue.line: I would like, if you have not already eaten, to offer you the second half of a small Battenberg cake which I am, for medical reasons, supposed to be eating less of than I am eating.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I will accept the second half of the Battenberg, on the understanding that the offer is, in itself, a medical intervention on my part.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: An interesting framing. Take it. The plates are by your foot.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I would like, before this afternoon, to tell you the parts of the conversation with Henry that are sayable. I have been considering, since Wednesday, which parts those are. I have arrived at, I think, a usable list. The list is short. I would prefer to give it now and move on, rather than carry the list as something pending.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Go ahead.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: First. The call lasted approximately thirty-two minutes. We had set, without explicitly setting, a half-hour limit, and both of us recognised the limit at roughly the right moment. Neither of us, by my reading, exceeded the limit by the act of will. The conversation finished at the moment it had finished, and we both, separately, noticed that it had finished. The mutual noticing was — well. It was a small piece of evidence that something between us still functions.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Second. We did not, in any meaningful sense, address the past. We spoke about the present, and we spoke about the present in a way that allowed the past to be present without being discussed. I am not, this afternoon, in a position to explain how that worked. I will say only that it worked, and that the working of it was the principal achievement of the call.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Third. He spoke about the thrush at greater length than I had expected, which is to say at any length at all. I had assumed, when he sent the photocopy, that the thrush was a gesture rather than a subject. The call disabused me of the assumption. The thrush is, for him, a subject. He has, since November, been observing the thrush with a degree of sustained attention that — Sophie, that is one of the several things about Henry I had forgotten in twenty-two years, and which the call has, by various small means, restored to me.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Fourth. He asked me, near the end of the call, whether I would mind if he wrote, perhaps once a year — once a year, no more, was the phrasing he used — to tell me what the thrush was doing. I said I would not mind. He said: only if you actively welcome it. I said: actively welcome is a slightly stronger formulation than I am, today, prepared to deploy. We agreed: 'consent to' rather than 'welcome'. He will, I believe, send a letter once a year, in November or thereabouts, of the kind he sent on Thursday.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, that is — that is a remarkably calibrated outcome.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is. We have, between us, some experience of negotiating the calibrations. The experience is from a long time ago and has, evidently, not been forgotten.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Is there a fifth thing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: There is a fifth thing, and the fifth thing is the one I am most uncertain about saying.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: You are, this afternoon, free to decline to say it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know. Hence the saying-it. The fifth thing is — Sophie, the fifth thing is that Henry is, today, neither a romantic figure to me nor a relative. He is something I have not, in the existing English vocabulary, found a precise term for. The closest I can come is: the father of my child. Not 'your father' from his point of view, although he has, on the basis of recent evidence, become slightly more that than he was. The father of my child, from mine. Which is a structurally real category that the language treats as if it were merely the conjunction of two other categories. It is not. It is its own category. I have, in twenty-two years, not had occasion to articulate that, because the silence had absorbed the need for the articulation.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I am articulating it now because the call has, for the moment, made the category visible to me, and I would prefer to mark its visibility while it is visible rather than wait for it to recede into the silence again, which it will.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: That is — that is the most precise thing you have ever said about him.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It may be the most precise thing I have ever said about anything. I do not, today, propose to repeat it. Once is the appropriate number of times for sentences of that quality to be said.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I have, in the last two months, been carrying — a question, which I had not realised I was carrying until just now, and which the fifth thing has answered. The question was about how to think about him. I had been operating without a category. Operating without a category is, I have learned in the last fortnight from various quarters, exhausting. You have, in one sentence, ended the operating-without.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. The category, when used, will need to be used carefully. The category is mine in the strict sense — I am the only person for whom Henry is, structurally, the father-of-my-child. He is, separately, your father, which is your category, and which you are at liberty to define in whatever terms you find most useful. I am not, by articulating mine, recommending you adopt any particular version of yours.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Understood.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right. The Battenberg. Eat it. The heron is, possibly, about to make a return appearance, and you should be in a position to evaluate, on first viewing, whether it is the same heron as the one I have been, possibly inaccurately, identifying.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Thank you.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yes. The heron is, by my count, twelve minutes overdue. We will, between us, watch the small piece of sky above the holly tree and see whether it appears.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

pratique.scenario: Imagine reporting back, to a close family member, on a difficult conversation you have had with someone whose relation to your shared family is complex. Use Mum's frame: classify which parts are sayable and which are not; deliver the sayable parts in numbered structure; conclude by, if appropriate, articulating any one piece of philosophical clarification that the conversation has produced and that you would prefer to mark in language while it is visible.
prompt_phrases: I would like to tell you the parts of the conversation with X that are sayable. I have been considering, since [time], which parts those are.
We did not, in any meaningful sense, address Y. We spoke about Z, and we spoke about Z in a way that allowed Y to be present without being discussed.
Once is the appropriate number of times for sentences of that quality to be said.

content.situation: Saturday afternoon, the fifth of August. Three days after the call between Mum and Henry, four weeks after the operation. Sophie has come up to Lancaster on the morning train. The weather has turned warm — high summer, the small back garden of Cedar View has the dry brightness Lancaster sometimes produces in the first week of August. Mum is in the garden in a folding chair, wearing the navy linen trousers and the cardigan that has been her summer uniform since 2008. She is using one walking stick. The other is, by mutual ratification, ornamental. There is a tea tray on the small table beside her. The geranium has, in Sophie's two-month absence, become entirely unreasonable in size. Mum has not, until Sophie sits down, said anything substantive about Wednesday. She has, since Sophie's arrival, talked about the geranium, the physiotherapist's surprise at her progress, and a heron that has been seen twice in the small park behind the building. The heron is, Mum reports, almost certainly the same heron, which is — and she has said this twice — interesting.

phrase_cle.phrase: A structurally real category that the language treats as if it were merely the conjunction of two other categories.
phrase_cle.example: Stepfamilies often face this. Stepmother is a structurally real category that the language treats as if it were merely the conjunction of step + mother. It is not. It is its own thing.
phrase_cle.explanation: Mum's формула — лингвистически-философский C2-инструмент для опознания и защиты категорий, для которых язык не предоставил отдельного имени, и которые поэтому сводятся к комбинации соседних категорий. Грамматически — определение через relative clause с counterfactual subjunctive ('as if it were'). 'Merely the conjunction of two other categories' — отказ от того, что отношение можно построить простым сложением. На C2 ты учишься узнавать момент, когда обыденный язык подводит, и когда роль или отношение существует структурно, но не имеет своего слова. Mum применяет это к 'father of my child' — категории, которая не есть просто 'ex-partner + father of Sophie', и не 'romantic past + biological present'. Это собственная категория с собственными свойствами. Расширения: 'former colleague who became central' (для отношений, которые перешагивают границу 'work-friend'); 'absent presence' (для людей, которые ушли, но продолжают определять текущую жизнь); 'distant intimacy' (для отношений, которые близки эмоционально, но не в действии). На C2 эта формула становится инструментом, которым ты можешь пользоваться, когда обнаруживаешь, что сводишь сложное отношение к комбинации простых.
phrase_cle.translation: Структурно реальная категория, к которой язык относится как к простому пересечению двух других.

expressions.meaning: The narrator's mock-formal classification of the second walking-stick — its ornamental status has been agreed by both Sophie and Mum without explicit conversation. The phrase 'mutual ratification' (parliamentary-treaty vocabulary) applied to a domestic object is C2 humour with substance: the joke marks that small categorisations between family members can have the weight of formal agreements.

Mum's explicit classification of the call into sayable and unsayable parts. The construction acknowledges that some of what passed between her and Henry is not for transmission, without making the unsayable a secret. C2 register: the boundary between disclosable and undisclosable as a precise distinction.
expressions.expression: The parts of the conversation with Henry that are sayable

expressions.meaning: Mum's hyphenated nominalisation for the moment when both she and Henry recognised, simultaneously, that the conversation had completed itself. The phrase converts a synchronised perception into a substantive event. C2 register: shared cognitive moments rendered as named items.

Mum's diagnostic for how the call worked: the past was not banished from the conversation, but it was not the topic. The construction 'be present without being discussed' captures a distinct mode of intersubjective time that conventional English vocabulary lacks a single word for. C2 register at full strength.
expressions.expression: Allowed the past to be present without being discussed

expressions.meaning: Recurring from L14. Mum's earlier diagnostic for what produces unwanted social trajectories now applies, in a different application, to the question of whether Henry's correspondence might develop into more than the agreed once-yearly note. The phrase has, between L14 and L18, become a working concept available for re-use.
expressions.expression: The structural inertia of the position

expressions.meaning: Henry's calibrated phrasing for what he would require Mum to be in relation to his proposed letters. Mum's response — that 'actively welcome' is stronger than she is prepared to deploy — and the negotiated landing on 'consent to' performs C2 register at its most precise: distinct words for distinct degrees of permission.

Mum's philosophical claim about 'father of my child' as a discrete relational category that English vocabulary fails to grant a name to. The phrase 'structurally real' is doing precise work — Mum is asserting the reality of the category against the language's failure to recognise it. C2 register: linguistic-philosophical claim deployed conversationally.
expressions.expression: A structurally real category that the language treats as if it were merely the conjunction of two other categories

expressions.meaning: Sophie's compressed phrase for the cognitive condition of relating to a person whose role in one's life has no name. The construction is recurring courseworld figure (Mark's L4 'empty category'; Mum's L27 C1 'category of things-I-can-be-told-about-Henry'). Sophie now uses it about her own previous state. C2 register: family of phrases applied at moment of self-recognition.

content.cultural_note: Mum's articulation of 'father of my child' as a structurally distinct category that English vocabulary fails to name belongs to a particular kind of late-twentieth-century British feminist intellectual register that the courseworld has been quietly preparing for. The register insists on the philosophical reality of relations that fall outside the names language has settled on, and it insists on the precision of distinguishing between, for example, 'ex-partner', 'father of my child', and 'absent former lover'. Mum's claim that the category 'is its own category' — that it is not reducible to the conjunction of others — is a claim that language users accustomed to a particular intellectual tradition (Spinoza, Wittgenstein, Iris Murdoch, Mary Midgley) make easily, and that is recognisable as a marker of educated British speech. C2 students should hear that the act of articulating a category-without-a-name is itself a cultural practice: a willingness to live with a precision that the available vocabulary makes effortful. Note also the structural device of the elliptical call. Henry and Mum's thirty-two-minute conversation has happened off-stage between L17 and L18; the lesson stages only Mum's report, and the report is selective. This is the second time in Block 3 that a major event has been handled by ellipsis (the operation itself in L15 was the first). C2 students should hear the consistent compositional position: events that belong to the people having them are not staged; only the conversation about the events, between people who have a right to have it, is staged. The closing — the small joint attention to the heron's possible appearance — performs the closure-by-shared-attention move that has now appeared multiple times in the courseworld. The philosophical conversation is over; the heron may or may not arrive; both women will, between them, watch the sky.
content.grammar_focus: Mum's central five-part disclosure in this dialogue uses a sustained C2 grammatical operation: the numbered architecture deployed at length, with each item performing a different syntactic operation. First item: present perfect ('has lasted approximately thirty-two minutes') with a parenthetical condition ('without explicitly setting, a half-hour limit'). Second item: parallel past simple negatives ('we did not, in any meaningful sense, address the past') with a positive parallel ('we spoke about the present, and we spoke about the present in a way that allowed'). Third item: comparative-than ('greater than I had expected') with a self-correcting phrase ('which is to say at any length at all'). Fourth item: reported speech with negotiated phrasing — 'consent to' rather than 'welcome'. Fifth item: extended philosophical disclosure with embedded counterfactual ('which the language treats as if it were'). C2 students should mark how the numbered structure, while constant, accommodates wildly different grammatical operations within each item. The form is portable; the substance varies. Note also the philosophical move in the fifth point: 'A structurally real category that the language treats as if it were merely the conjunction of two other categories.' The construction asserts ontological priority of a category over the linguistic apparatus available to name it. C2 prose can sustain this kind of meta-linguistic claim when the speaker has thought carefully about the categories in question. The 'as if it were' is subjunctive past in counterfactual clause, marking that the language's treatment is a misclassification. Note Sophie's recognition speech: 'I have, in the last two months, been carrying — a question, which I had not realised I was carrying until just now, and which the fifth thing has answered.' Present perfect continuous ('have been carrying') with mid-sentence dash and embedded relative clauses, the second of which contains a past perfect with infinitival ('I had not realised I was carrying'). The grammar performs the act of recognition by syntactically interrupting the predicate. C2 register: the willingness to break a sentence at the moment of insight and let the syntax reflect the cognitive event. The closing exchange — about the heron's possible appearance, twelve minutes overdue — performs the closure-by-operational-attention move that the courseworld has been deploying since C1 L11. The serious philosophical conversation is sealed by sustained joint attention to a small piece of sky.

key_vocabulary.note: Британский кондитерский классик: бисквитный пирог с розовыми и жёлтыми кубиками внутри, обернут в марципан. Считается несовременным; Mum с её поколения. Деталь домашнего регистра.
Recurring detail: Mum's геранн на подоконнике с Easter, теперь в саду. Британский cottage-gardening маркер.
Серая цапля — обычная для британских парков и водоёмов. Mum's внимание к heron — параллель Henry's вниманию к thrush. Намеренная courseworld-симметрия.
Mum и Henry договариваются о registre частоты писем. 'Actively welcome' — Henry's исходная формулировка; 'consent to' — Mum's предпочтение. Точная C2 калибровка.
key_vocabulary.word: to actively welcome (vs. to consent to)

key_vocabulary.note: Лингвистико-философский термин: категория, которая существует в реальности отношений, но не имеет собственного имени в языке. C2 регистр.
Логико-философский термин: пересечение множеств. Mum применяет к лингвистической ситуации. C2 транспозиция.
key_vocabulary.word: the conjunction of two other categories

key_vocabulary.note: Recurring courseworld figure (parliamentary). Narrator's mock-формальное описание status'а второй палки. C2 ироничный регистр.
Британский литературный глагол: убрать ложное представление. Mum: 'the call disabused me of the assumption'. C2 регистр.
Mum's фраза для будущего исчезновения её только что сделанной артикуляции. Recurring courseworld figure: что-либо visible сейчас, что вернётся в неоформленное состояние.
Recurring courseworld figure (Edward L12). Британская формальная фраза. Mum применяет к собственному ограничению на рекомендации Sophie.

comprehension_questions.hint: She is describing a particular intersubjective mode in which historical context is acknowledged without being made the topic. What kind of conversation does this enable, and what kind does it prevent?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum says of the call with Henry: 'We spoke about the present in a way that allowed the past to be present without being discussed.' What is the difference between addressing the past and allowing it to be present, and why does Mum say the working of this distinction was 'the principal achievement of the call'?

comprehension_questions.hint: 'Actively welcome' would commit Mum to a more positive disposition than she is prepared to claim. 'Consent to' acknowledges the receipt without claiming enthusiasm. What does each phrasing make available, and what does each foreclose?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum and Henry negotiate from 'actively welcome' to 'consent to' as the term governing his proposed yearly letters. Why does the precise word matter, and what is each speaker protecting by the choice?

comprehension_questions.hint: She is asserting that the category exists independently of its linguistic recognition. What did the silence absorb, and why does the call's restoration of visibility require, today, the articulation that was, until now, unnecessary?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum articulates 'father of my child' as 'a structurally real category that the language treats as if it were merely the conjunction of two other categories'. What is the philosophical claim, and why does she say she has, in twenty-two years, 'not had occasion to articulate that'?

content.title: The Notebook

dialogue.line: September the third, twenty twenty-six.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: I have, until this moment, not used this notebook. I had been waiting, I now realise, for a particular threshold of substance — an event distinctive enough to inaugurate the practice — and the waiting has, in the absence of such an event, become its own absence.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: I am, today, beginning the practice without the inaugurating event, on the operational principle that practices begun on Wednesdays in the second week of one's tenancy can, retrospectively, be made to look as if they had been begun on a more dignified occasion.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: Edward asked, in May, for three things I would learn in his absence that I would not have learned had he been here. The lunch is in March. I have, between now and then, six months.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: I find myself, on this first entry, considering the form rather than the substance. The form — what kind of writing this is — appears to be the question I am, in fact, working on. The substance, presumably, will arrive only after the form has been provisionally settled.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: I am aware that the writing-down of the three things may, by being written down, change them. The act of writing converts a passing observation into a documented observation, and the documentation has a different kind of life than the observation alone. The documented version is more durable, more available for retrospection, and — here is the risk — more susceptible to being treated as if it were the observation itself rather than its later transcription.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: I will, accordingly, treat each entry as a working draft rather than as a final statement. Whatever I write tonight may, by March, have been overtaken by what I have learned subsequently. I am giving myself permission, in advance, to revise.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: Tonight's entry, then, is preliminary. It is a sketch of what the three things might, in their current form, be.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: First. I am, after three days of tenancy, beginning to suspect that the change Eleanor described in April — the cessation of rehearsing sentences in the shower — has, in my own case, already begun, in advance of the formal acquisition of partnership-equivalent. I had not expected this. I had assumed the cessation would require the formal status; the cessation appears, instead, to have been triggered by the decision to accept the formal status. The decision did the work, not the status. I do not yet know whether this generalises, but I will record the observation and watch it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: Second. Edward's absence has, even after three days, produced a different quality of attention in me. When Edward was in the building, I was, without being aware of it, partly outsourcing certain judgements to him — not by asking him, but by holding the judgements pending in case I might, later, ask him. With his absence, the holding-pending has stopped. The judgements have, accordingly, had to settle themselves earlier in the process. The settling is, I find, faster than I had expected. I do not, today, conclude that this is straightforwardly good. I notice it, and I notice that I am noticing it sooner than Edward, I think, anticipated.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: Third. There is, in my mother's recent recovery, a piece of evidence I am still processing. She has, since the operation, become more articulate about distinctions of the kind that the operation, on its surface, had nothing to do with. The recovery has produced, as a side-effect, a sharpening of her capacity for analytical disclosure. I do not know whether this is the operation, or the proximity of mortality, or the simple fact of having had, for the first time in years, a sustained period of enforced reduced activity in which to think. I do not yet know what to do with this observation. I may, by March, have decided what to do with it. For now, I am noting that it is, in itself, a piece of information about how serious medical events function in the lives of the people who survive them well.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: Those are tonight's three. I am aware that they may, in March, look entirely wrong, and that the three I would write in March may have nothing in common with these three. That is permitted. The notebook is for the practice, not for the prediction.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: I will, on this first entry, also record one further thing, which is not a candidate for the lunch but which I would like to have written down somewhere private.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: Mum used a phrase, in August, that I have been carrying since. The phrase was: 'a structurally real category that the language treats as if it were merely the conjunction of two other categories'. She was talking about Henry — about what he is to her — but the formulation, separated from its specific application, has become for me a general instrument. I find myself testing other relations against it. There are several in my own life that may, on examination, turn out to be structurally real categories language has not yet supplied a single word for. I will, in subsequent entries, attempt to name some of them.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: Including, possibly, what Mark is to me. Although I am, this evening, not yet ready to attempt that one.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: Tomorrow morning I have a directions hearing at ten. The papers are ready. The kettle is, by this hour, cold, and I should refill it if I want a final cup before bed. The geranium Mum sent home with me at Easter has, against expectation, survived to September. I am taking, in this small and entirely undignified way, several small things as evidence that I am, in fact, the person who lives here.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

dialogue.line: Closing tonight's entry. Pen down. Cup of tea. Bed.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie (writing)

pratique.scenario: Imagine writing the first entry in a notebook intended to record observations over a long period — a working diary, a research log, a journal kept for someone else's eventual reading. Use Sophie's frame: pre-announce the practice's status (working drafts, revisable); identify the form-question rather than rushing to substance; write at most three observations and acknowledge their preliminary nature; close with a domestic operational note.
prompt_phrases: I have, until this moment, not used this [notebook / journal / log]…
I find myself, on this first entry, considering the form rather than the substance.
I am giving myself permission, in advance, to revise.

content.situation: Wednesday evening, the third of September. Sophie has been a tenant of Wellgate Chambers for three days. The first three days have been administratively ordinary — a clerk's induction, two routine appearances in court that she would, as a non-tenant, have done in identical form, a short conversation with Edward in the corridor on the Tuesday afternoon before he left for the day. Sophie is at the kitchen table in her flat at twenty past nine. The remains of a small supper are at the other end of the table. She has, on the table in front of her, the notebook Edward gave her in May. The notebook is small — perhaps fifteen by ten centimetres — bound in green cloth, with the date 'September 1995' in blue ink on the inside cover. The pages are blank. Sophie has a pen. She has been holding the pen for, by her own estimate, eleven minutes. The lesson stages what she now writes. The form is single-speaker — Sophie writing rather than speaking — and it represents a register of personal English the course has not, until this point, formally taught: the diary entry written in conscious knowledge that the diary will, in March, be partly read aloud to the person who gave the notebook.

phrase_cle.phrase: I find myself, on this first entry, considering the form rather than the substance.
phrase_cle.example: I sat down to write about the trip and ended up thinking about how to write about the trip. I find myself, on this first entry, considering the form rather than the substance.
phrase_cle.explanation: Sophie's формула — фундаментальная C2-конструкция письменного самонаблюдения. Грамматически — reflexive construction 'find oneself + present participle': 'I find myself doing X' создаёт лёгкую дистанцию между пишущим 'я' и наблюдаемым 'я'. Эта дистанция — ключевая особенность дневникового регистра. Она позволяет писать о своих собственных когнитивных процессах, не утверждая их, не оправдываясь за них, и не превращая их в драму. Mid-sentence temporal anchor ('on this first entry') между 'find myself' и participle — типичный для C2 приём, который смягчает потенциально бескомпромиссное самонаблюдение через временную локализацию. Распространения формулы: 'I find myself, in this conversation, defending a position I had not, this morning, intended to defend'; 'I find myself, on third reading, agreeing with the witness rather than with the prosecutor'; 'I find myself, on this trip, behaving differently from the version of me that left the house'. Каждая конструкция признаёт изменение в собственной позиции через формулу 'I find myself X-ing', которая регистрирует, не объясняя. На C2 эта формула становится автоматическим инструментом для письменного и формального устного самонаблюдения.
phrase_cle.translation: Я обнаруживаю себя, на этой первой записи, размышляющей о форме, а не о содержании.

expressions.meaning: Sophie's term for the kind of distinctive moment whose presence would justify the start of a new practice. The phrase is C2-precise: it identifies a category of event by its function (it inaugurates) rather than its content. Sophie's diagnostic that she has been waiting for one and that the waiting itself has become the absence is a small piece of self-observation that the diary-form makes available.

Sophie's recurring distinction (echoing Edward's L7 'I distinguish form from substance'). In the diary-form, however, the operation is reflexive — Sophie applies the distinction to her own writing. The phrase 'considering the form rather than the substance' converts a meta-question into a working subject of the entry itself.
expressions.expression: The form rather than the substance

expressions.meaning: Sophie's diagnostic about her own developmental change. The construction is C2-precise: two parallel agents ('the decision', 'the status') with the verb 'did the work' attributed to one and not the other. The grammatical isolation makes the developmental claim testable.
expressions.expression: The decision did the work, not the status

expressions.meaning: Sophie's hyphenated phrase for the unconscious habit of keeping decisions open in case a senior colleague might be consulted later. The verb 'holding pending' (court vocabulary for matters not yet decided) applied to her own internal cognitive process. C2 transposition.

Sophie's term for a phrase that can be applied beyond its original context — the way Mum's 'structurally real category' formulation has become, for Sophie, a tool for thinking about other relations. The construction 'general instrument' is mathematical-philosophical vocabulary applied to a piece of inherited language. C2 register at full strength.

Sophie's classification of each notebook entry: provisional, revisable, not committed-to. The phrase belongs to writing and editing vocabulary; Sophie applies it to her own self-documentation. The construction protects her from the trap of treating diary entries as fixed declarations.
expressions.expression: Working draft rather than final statement

expressions.meaning: Sophie's compressed account of what she has learned from Mum's recovery. The phrase 'function in the lives of' converts the event into a structural variable; 'the people who survive them well' specifies the relevant population. C2 register: sociological-style claim deployed in private writing.
expressions.expression: A piece of information about how serious medical events function in the lives of the people who survive them well

expressions.meaning: Sophie's domestic-philosophical closing. The flat, the kettle, the geranium, the diary itself constitute, in aggregate, evidence of inhabitation. The phrase 'in fact' is doing precise work — it acknowledges the slight strangeness of needing such evidence. C2 register: domestic objects as ontological proof.
expressions.expression: Several small things as evidence that I am, in fact, the person who lives here
