The construction recurs in the May/November distinction (Marie L25) and the structural-textual distinction (Mum L26). C2 register: family of analytic distinctions.
expressions.expression: Wanting and hoping are importantly different

expressions.meaning: Edward's structural recognition: he and Sophie have, across the six months of the sabbatical, both arrived at related insights about attentional practice — Sophie through writing, Edward through tending a wood-burning stove. The phrase 'the same workshop' (echoing Sophie's earlier 'instruments in the same workshop') marks the convergence. C2 register: structural recognition of unplanned parallel development.
expressions.expression: The two of us have, by different routes, arrived at the same workshop

content.cultural_note: The British professional lunch — particularly between two members of the Bar where one has been a senior tenant and the other has, in the interim, joined the chambers as a tenant — has a particular cultural register that this lesson is teaching by demonstration. The choice of a non-chambers venue is structurally important: chambers itself carries the working register, and a lunch held there would, by venue alone, slide back into operational discussion. A small Italian restaurant near St Ann's Square — chosen by Edward in October, six months in advance — signals that the lunch is to be conducted in a register that can accommodate substantive content without becoming a working meeting. C2 students should hear this as the British register of dignified intimacy between professional colleagues: serious enough to permit substantive exchange, casual enough to permit the wine and the small final exchange about it. Note Edward's deliberate distinction between wanting and hoping. The phrase 'wanting and hoping are importantly different' is doing precise philosophical work: wanting is the general desire for an outcome; hoping is the more specific, more vulnerable expectation that a particular outcome will, in fact, materialise. Edward's discipline of not voicing his hope until it had been retrospectively earned is the same discipline Mum exercised with the photograph in Bishop's Letters — the ethic of allowing the recipient's own activity, rather than the discloser's pre-emptive guidance, to produce the outcome. Sophie recognises the parallel ('that is the same discipline Mum had with the photograph in Bishop's Letters') and Edward, characteristically, declines to discuss the photograph but registers the comparison for a possible future lunch. C2 register: parallels named in passing without being elaborated. The wood-burning stove section is doing significant cultural work. Edward's discovery that the most useful part of the sabbatical was a heating-failure-induced fortnight of attending to a stove is itself a particular kind of British observation — the recognition that the sustained practice of attentional discipline can be developed through humble domestic activity, and that this development is structurally similar to (or 'in the same workshop as') the development Sophie has been doing through written practice. The convergence is the lesson's quietest claim. Two careful people, by different routes, have arrived at related insights about attention. Note the deliberate restraint of the lesson's closing. The exchange about the wine — 'the wine is doing what good wine does' — is the courseworld's gentlest possible descent from substantive register. Both speakers have, by twelve forty-five plus approximately ninety minutes, completed the structural work the lunch was meant to do. The remainder of the lunch will not, by the courseworld's choice, be staged. The reader leaves Sophie and Edward at the table with their wine, and the next lesson will be approximately three weeks later in Bakewell. The non-staging of the rest of the lunch is itself a register move: C2 register, at the close of the course, knows that some of the most pleasant parts of life do not need to be transcribed.
content.grammar_focus: This lesson stages the central conversation of Block 5 — the lunch Sophie has been preparing for since L19 — and the grammar of the dialogue reflects the achieved register of two speakers operating at full capacity in the C2 mode. Note the use of meta-event opening: Edward's 'I want to tell you that the lunch is, today, what I had hoped it would be in May when I gave you the briefcase, which is to say that it is happening in approximately the form I had imagined, with you in approximately the position I had hoped you would be in, and that the imagining and the hoping have, by the operation of the last ten months, been partly justified by events.' One sentence with three coordinated clauses, each operating on a different temporal-modal axis: present ('the lunch is what I had hoped'), past-perfect counterfactual ('I had hoped it would be in May'), present-perfect with mid-clause anchor ('have been partly justified by events'). The grammar performs Edward's habitual register move — the compression of complex temporal architecture into a single sustained sentence. Note Sophie's central delivery, which uses the structured-disclosure register Edward and Mum have both been deploying throughout the courseworld: 'I have, in March of twenty twenty-six, after six months of writing in the notebook you gave me in May of twenty twenty-five, two observations to deliver. The number two is, on reflection, the right number. I had, last night at half past eleven, three on the shortlist; one of the three has, between then and now, become a candidate for next time rather than for this lunch. The two I will deliver have been, since November, the two I have most kept returning to.' Four sentences performing the full architecture: temporal anchor and substantive content, evaluation of the count, account of the prior-state and shift, identification of the qualifying criterion. C2 register at the level of Edward's own sustained sentences. Note Edward's response to the second observation: 'It is the observation I had not, in May, allowed myself to predict you would arrive at, on the grounds that predicting it would have been an act of presumption on my part about a practice I was, at the time, recommending without certainty. The prediction has, by your having arrived at the observation, been retrospectively earned.' Two sentences mapping a complex epistemic-ethical architecture: the prediction was not pre-emptively voiced because doing so would have been presumption; the prediction has, by Sophie's arrival, become legitimate. Past perfect counterfactual ('would have been'), present perfect ('has been retrospectively earned'). The grammar enacts the discipline of not pre-empting and the relief of being borne out. The closing exchange about the wine — 'The wine, I have to say, is — the wine is doing what good wine does.' / 'I have, since October, been looking forward to the wine more than is strictly defensible.' — uses Sophie's mid-sentence self-correction (a register move recurring throughout the courseworld) and Edward's mock-evaluative confession ('more than is strictly defensible'). The lesson closes with the courseworld's gentlest registers: a pair of utterances about wine that do, in their casualness, the structural work of the lunch's lower register-ending. C2 register: serious work concluded by domestic pleasure.

key_vocabulary.note: Manchester central square, recurring courseworld geography. Edward's выбор небольшого Italian restaurant поблизости. Британская городская точная топография.
Британский формальный термин для restaurant owner. Edward's persona — 'the proprietor and I have known each other for some years'. C2 формальный регистр.
Recurring courseworld figure (с C2 L8). Sophie's термин для pre-finalised candidate observations. C2 рабочая лексика.
Sophie's hyphenated термин для cognitive practice change in Edward's absence. Compound modifier. C2 регистр precise self-observation.
Sophie's термин для того, что Edward's presence делал в её processing. Compound noun. Recurring courseworld figure: nominalised cognitive operations.
Британский деревенский термин: укладывание угольев перед сном так, чтобы огонь горел медленно до утра. Edward's specific Argyll detail. C2 британский сельский регистр.
Британский сельский элемент: heating system in older cottages, использует firewood. Edward's central November Argyll experience. C2 британская сельская лексика.
Edward's mock-технический глагол: применяет к собственной cognitive practice. Computer/engineering vocabulary в personal context. C2 транспозиция.
Edward's фраза для validity status of his earlier hope. Logical/temporal precision. C2 регистр.
Edward's mock-formal qualifier для anticipation of wine. Self-aware ironic register для small confession. C2 регистр.

comprehension_questions.hint: The lunch could simply have happened without comment. Edward chooses to mark it. What does the marking do for both speakers, and what does it preserve about the arrangement they made ten months earlier?
comprehension_questions.question: Edward opens by marking 'that the having-of-the-lunch is itself, on my reading, a successful outcome of an arrangement we made when the outcome was uncertain.' Why does Edward make this meta-observation before the substantive part of the lunch begins, and what does the explicit naming protect?

comprehension_questions.hint: Delivering all three would have demonstrated comprehensive preparation. Withholding the third demonstrates a different kind of preparation — knowing which material is ready and which is not. What does each demonstration teach about C2-level professional self-management?
comprehension_questions.question: Sophie has prepared three observations and chooses to deliver only two, declining the third on the grounds that 'it is, this March, not yet ready in the form it would need to be ready in.' Edward says: 'The discipline of not delivering the third is, by my reading, more useful than delivering it would have been.' What is the structural argument for restraint, and what does the discipline demonstrate about Sophie's relationship to her own material?

comprehension_questions.hint: Convergence by different routes validates the underlying claim about attention. What does the parallel arrival demonstrate about the kind of insight involved, and what does it teach about the relationship between independent practice and shared understanding?
comprehension_questions.question: Edward's account of the wood-burning stove fortnight in November and Sophie's account of writing in the notebook produce, in his closing, the recognition that 'the two of us have, by different routes, arrived at the same workshop.' What is the structural significance of two people independently reaching related insights about attentional practice, and why is the convergence more meaningful than either insight alone would have been?

content.title: Bakewell

dialogue.line: The map is, by the way, the new one. Mark sent it in February.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The map arrived without ceremony. There was a brief covering note. The note specified that the map was the up-to-date Ordnance Survey one-inch sheet for the Bakewell area, that it had been ordered through the official OS channels, and that the sender had marked, in pencil, three locations he thought might be of interest based on the brief conversation we had had at the wedding lunch. The pencil marks are, by his account, removable. The map is otherwise unannotated. The note closed with one line.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: What was the line.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: 'I hope this finds you continuing to walk well.' That was the line.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, that is — that is a notably calibrated covering note.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It was. The Beresford brothers have, between them, a particular gift for the calibrated covering note. Whether the gift is environmental or genetic is, today, an open question. Mark sent the map. He did not, by sending it, ask for anything in return. I have, accordingly, returned nothing in particular, on the principle that the calibration would have been disturbed by my returning anything that was not, by independent grounds, owed.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Did you — were you, by the receipt of the map, more affected than you had expected to be.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. I had assigned the map, in advance, to the category of operational kindness. The receipt of it was, by and large, consistent with that assignment. I was, however, briefly aware on opening the parcel that I had, between the wedding and February, been not-thinking about whether the map would, in fact, arrive. The not-thinking had, by its duration, been a small piece of practice. The arrival ended the practice. I noticed the ending. The noticing was, on my reading of myself, slightly more affecting than the map itself was.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, that is — that is a precise distinction.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It is. I have, in the last year, become slightly disciplined about distinctions of that kind. We have already established that the discipline is, I suspect, partly a side-effect of the operation. I am still enjoying it.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Sophie, you are taking the A6 rather than the A623.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I am. The A6 adds, I think, twelve minutes; the twelve minutes are, by the standards of an open Saturday, available. The A623 has the limestone gorge near Wardlow Mires, which I had thought, on balance, you might prefer to encounter on the way back when the light has changed.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie, that is a routing decision worthy of someone who has been practising routing decisions in writing for six months.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I have, in fact, been practising routing decisions in writing for six months. I would not, in September, have noticed the gorge thing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Sophie. The geranium has, by my count this morning, survived its third winter in succession.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mine has too. Three, also.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: We are, between us, running two parallel geraniums of approximately equivalent age, in two different flats, with two different routines of care, and both have made it to a third spring. I have, separately, started keeping a small note about how often I water mine, on the grounds that the question of whether the survival is the geranium or the routine is, today, of some interest to me.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, that is — Henry would, I think, find that practice familiar.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie. Henry would. The third candidate, in the November letter, was the geranium. I used it. I had said I would tell you, after Christmas, if I had used it. I am, today, telling you. The letter went in the post on the eighteenth of December. He has not, by Easter, replied. He may reply by November. He may not. The not-replying is, by the form of the agreement, also a permitted response.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Thank you for telling me on the way to Bakewell.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It seemed the right route for it.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Sophie. The hill, when we get there, I would like to attempt. The whole hill, not the half hill. The physiotherapist has, by various means, signalled that the whole hill is, by April, structurally available, provided I take it slowly. I will take it slowly. You will, I assume, be patient.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, I will be patient. I have, since approximately Easter 2025, been practising patience as one of the small ongoing instruments. The hill is, by my reading, the right occasion for the instrument.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Bakewell has, as we have previously established, its own weather. The weather today is bright. The hill, by all the indications visible to me from approximately twenty miles out, is dry. We will, between us, do approximately as well as the day allows. Whether 'as well' will turn out to be, in retrospect, a useful piece of vocabulary, is, I think, the kind of thing we will know by four o'clock.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Four o'clock.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: By four o'clock we will, between us, have walked the hill, eaten the sandwiches, drunk the tea, and looked at the photograph in the small park near the church. The looking at the photograph is the part I had, this morning at half past nine when you arrived, not yet decided whether to propose. I am, by approximately Sheffield, deciding. The proposal is now made. Whether you accept it is yours.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum, I accept it. The small park near the church. After the sandwiches.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: After the sandwiches. Right.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Sophie.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I am, this morning, in the car with my daughter, on the way to Bakewell, with a thermos and a photograph and an OS map and a recovering hip and a small recently-used signal-word and a herb garden at home that the heron has, in my absence on the trip, agreed by various small means to refrain from disturbing. I had not, in 2024, predicted that any one of those things would, by April 2026, be where it is. The aggregate is, by my reading, unexpectedly continuous with the life I had been, until then, living. The continuity, on reflection, is the part that has surprised me most.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: Mum.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Mm.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

dialogue.line: I am, this morning, driving.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I had assumed.
dialogue.speaker: Mum

pratique.scenario: Imagine driving with someone close to you to a place you have, between you, visited before — a return after an extended absence, made possible by recovery, time, or willingness. Use Mum's frame: deliver small operational pieces of news without ceremony; acknowledge what has, in the interim, become continuous; allow the journey itself to be the conversation rather than the destination.
prompt_phrases: I am, this morning, in [vehicle] with [person], on the way to [place], with [a list of small concrete items, each carrying weight].
I had not, in [past time], predicted that any one of those things would, by [present time], be where it is.
The aggregate is, by my reading, unexpectedly continuous with the life I had been, until then, living. The continuity, on reflection, is the part that has surprised me most.

content.situation: Saturday, the fourth of April. Sophie has driven up from Manchester this morning to collect Mum at half past nine. They have, by mutual agreement reached on the telephone last weekend, decided to drive to Bakewell — the first time they have done so together since Easter 2025, two years ago. Mum is, by her own assessment, structurally able to manage a Saturday in Bakewell: she has been walking, since November, with one stick rather than two on level ground, and the physiotherapist has, in March, signed off on the continuation of the recovery as 'self-managing'. The drive is approximately ninety minutes by the route Sophie has, in the previous fortnight, planned to take. The weather is, by the early-April standards of the Peak District, favourable: bright, cold, the kind of clarity that makes the limestone walls visible from a long way off. Mum is in the passenger seat with a small thermos of tea between her feet, the OS map of the Bakewell area unfolded on her lap, and a small flat parcel — the photograph from L27 in its simple frame — on the back seat in the green canvas bag that has, since 2017, been Mum's standard travel bag. Sophie has packed sandwiches.

phrase_cle.example: Don't predict the day from the morning forecast in Manchester. Bakewell has its own weather. Bring a coat regardless.
phrase_cle.explanation: Mum's формула — фундаментальная C2-конструкция, которая прошла через весь курс на уровне C2 в трёх различных регистрах. Сначала (в C2 L2) она была буквальной географической наблюдением: маленький рыночный городок в Peak District имеет микроклимат, отличающийся от Lancaster и Manchester. Затем (в C2 L14) она стала структурной метафорой для autonomous emotional climate каждого из нас, и из этой метафоры родился signal-word 'Bakewell' — слово, которое Mum и Sophie могут использовать, чтобы пометить дрейф разговора без обсуждения дрейфа. Наконец (в C2 L30) она возвращается к своему исходному буквальному значению — но теперь, после всего, что эта фраза прошла, её буквальное прочтение содержит в себе все её предыдущие применения. Грамматически — простое предложение subject-verb-object с притяжательным 'its own', которое утверждает автономность объекта. На C2 ты учишься распознавать фразы, которые проходят через эволюцию значений и в конце возвращаются к началу, неся в себе всю траекторию. Это не circular; это spiral — возвращение в ту же точку, но на другом уровне. Расширения: 'this conversation has its own weather'; 'this morning has its own weather'; 'each relationship has its own weather'. Каждая конструкция признаёт автономность системы и отказ от попыток предсказать её через external reference.

expressions.meaning: Sophie's mock-administrative qualifier: an unencumbered Saturday provides a different time-budget than a working day. The phrase 'an open Saturday' converts a free day into a structurally-named category. C2 register: temporal classification through borrowed qualifiers.
expressions.expression: By the standards of an open Saturday

expressions.meaning: Mum's hyphenated nominalisation for the discipline of not anticipating the map's arrival. The phrase belongs to the recurring courseworld family ('the not-asking', 'the not-writing', 'deliberate inactivity'). C2 register at full strength: sustained absence of action treated as substantive activity.
expressions.expression: The not-thinking had been a small piece of practice

expressions.meaning: Mum's classifying compound for Mark's category of gesture. The pair ('operational' / 'kindness') marks a gesture that is practical-functional in form (a useful map) but kind in motivation. C2 register: dual-classification of acts that combine instrumental and affective dimensions.

Mum's qualified-precision phrase for the parallel geraniums. The construction 'approximately equivalent' is C2 hedged precision: precision without false certainty. The two plants are, in different rooms with different routines, both reaching their third spring. C2 register: scientific vocabulary applied to domestic objects.

Mum's compressed epistemic question about the conditions of plant survival. The phrase 'is X or Y' (rather than 'depends on X or Y') is more economical and more precise: which one is the operative cause? C2 register: scientific philosophical question rendered as plain copular construction.
expressions.expression: Whether the survival is the geranium or the routine

expressions.meaning: Mum's compressed approval of Sophie's choice of the A6. The phrase 'the right route' (literal: this road) doubles as 'the right occasion' (the right context for telling Sophie about the December letter). C2 register: physical and conversational meanings of 'route' deliberately overlapped.

Mum's classifying observation about the morning's contents (thermos, photograph, map, hip, signal-word, herb garden). 'The aggregate' (statistical-summative vocabulary) is unexpectedly continuous with — rather than discontinuous from — Mum's previous life. C2 register: large-scale evaluative claim about a life-trajectory.
expressions.expression: The aggregate is unexpectedly continuous with the life I had been living

expressions.meaning: Mum's reverse-emphasis observation: of all the things one might have expected to surprise, the most surprising has been the continuity. The construction inverts the expected emphasis (one expects discontinuity to surprise) and locates the surprise in its opposite. C2 register: reverse-emphasis as a precise tool for unexpected truth.
expressions.expression: The continuity, on reflection, is the part that has surprised me most

content.cultural_note: This lesson is the closing lesson of the course, and its structural decisions are doing significant cultural and pedagogical work. The choice of Bakewell as the closing location — rather than Edinburgh, which had been the structural destination of every level's closing arc through B2 and C1 — is a deliberate deviation from the course's earlier pattern. Edinburgh has, throughout the course, served as the symbolic professional and personal destination toward which characters were moving. Bakewell, by contrast, is the small Peak District market town that has, since C2 L2, served as the courseworld's emotional home — the place Mum and Sophie's relationship is structurally located, the source of the signal-word, the destination of returning rather than of arriving. The course closes by going home rather than by reaching its destination. C2 students should hear this as the teaching of a particular British register of life-trajectory: not all stories end at the destination they were aimed at; some end by returning, with accumulated weight, to a place that had always, secretly, been the centre. The lesson's deliberate restraint is itself the closing pedagogical gesture. There is no climactic exchange. There is no final declaration. There is no summary of what the course has been. There is only a Saturday morning, a drive of approximately ninety minutes, an OS map, a thermos of tea, a photograph in a frame in a green canvas bag, a recovering hip, a signal-word that has been used and put away, and a geranium that has, against expectation, survived its third winter. Mum's closing inventory-sentence — 'I am, this morning, in the car with my daughter, on the way to Bakewell, with a thermos and a photograph and an OS map and a recovering hip and a small recently-used signal-word and a herb garden at home' — is the course's quietest possible declaration that the practice is, by this morning, in working order. Each item in the list is a piece of accumulated courseworld history: the thermos (recurring British domestic object), the photograph (L17 of C1, finally acquired in L27 of C2), the OS map (Mark's gesture from L24-L30), the hip (L13-L18), the signal-word (L14-L30), the herb garden (mentioned in passing throughout). The sentence is the course's accumulated lexicon rendered as a single morning's inventory. The closing exchange — 'I am, this morning, driving.' / 'I had assumed.' — refuses any larger statement. Sophie names her current activity in the simplest possible form. Mum responds with the recurring two-word phrase that has, across the course, been used by Sophie, Edward, Carlos, Marie, and now Mum to close moments of quiet mutual understanding. The course does not declare itself completed. It simply, by stopping, completes itself. C2 register, at the close of thirty lessons of accumulated practice, knows that the appropriate ending is the one that does not announce that it is the ending. The geranium has, by the way, survived its third winter. The course does, by its silence on the larger questions, allow the reader to register the smallness of this fact and the largeness of what the smallness, by this lesson, is doing.
content.grammar_focus: This final lesson uses sustained C2 grammatical operations whose individual elements have, by this stage of the course, become familiar but whose accumulated deployment in a closing register is itself the lesson's central technical achievement. Note Mum's central observation in the final paragraph of the dialogue: 'I am, this morning, in the car with my daughter, on the way to Bakewell, with a thermos and a photograph and an OS map and a recovering hip and a small recently-used signal-word and a herb garden at home that the heron has, in my absence on the trip, agreed by various small means to refrain from disturbing.' One sentence with a sustained list of seven items, each a different grammatical category (a thermos, a photograph, an OS map, a recovering hip, a small recently-used signal-word, a herb garden), held together by 'with'-prepositional construction and closed by an embedded relative clause about the heron. The grammar performs the act of inventory-as-life-account: Mum names the contents of the morning, and the contents constitute the morning. C2 register: the long sentence as a mode of taking stock. Note the deployment of the recurring 'I had assumed' in the very last line. The phrase has appeared, by this lesson, between Mum and Sophie (L13, L17, L18, L26), Sophie and Edward (L21, L29), Sophie and Carlos (L22), Sophie and Marie (L25), Marie and Sophie (L26 echo), and now closes the course. The phrase has, by accumulation, become the courseworld's most-used closing acknowledgement: a single phrase that signals quiet mutual understanding. C2 register: a phrase whose meaning lies entirely in its accumulation across the course's full arc. Note Mum's recovery of the original Bakewell phrase: 'Bakewell has, as we have previously established, its own weather.' The phrase first appeared in C2 L2 as Mum's metaphor for autonomous emotional climate; was converted in C2 L14 into the signal-word for the protocol; and now, in L30, returns to its original meaning, with the appended 'as we have previously established' marking the recovery as deliberate. The phrase has, across the C2 block, performed three distinct semantic operations and now closes by returning to its first. C2 register: the willingness to allow a phrase to evolve through use and then to return to its original sense as a marker of the course's having come full circle. Note the closing exchange: 'I am, this morning, driving.' / 'I had assumed.' Two short sentences, no further elaboration, no summary. Sophie's reply uses the present continuous to mark current activity in the simplest possible form; Mum's reply uses the recurring two-word phrase to acknowledge without ceremony. C2 register at its quietest: the course ends on the same level of restraint with which it has, throughout, been operating.

key_vocabulary.note: Recurring courseworld figure (с C2 L24, L26). Mark's подарок Mum'е, использован сегодня. C2 британский картографический регистр.
Британский медицинский термин: фаза восстановления, в которой пациент больше не требует регулярного супервижна. Physiotherapist's signed off в марте. C2 медицинский регистр.
Геологический термин для landscape feature в Peak District. Wardlow Mires — реальное место. C2 британская географическая лексика.
Маленькое местечко в Peak District с известным limestone gorge. Sophie's маршрутная заметка. Британская точная топография.
Британские major roads через Peak District. Sophie's выбор маршрута. Sophie's выбирает A6 чтобы оставить limestone gorge на обратный путь, когда свет изменится.
Mum's научная фраза для двух geraniums. C2 hedged precision.
Mum's mock-формальная атрибуция намерения цапле. Civilizing vocabulary applied to wildlife. C2 ironic personification.
Британский формальный квалификатор последовательности. Mum's: 'survived its third winter in succession'. C2 регистр precise temporal counting.
Recurring courseworld figure. Mum's термин для what physiotherapist has cleared as physically possible: 'the whole hill is structurally available'. C2 рабочая лексика.
Mum's hedged epistemic move: знать, окажется ли фраза useful 'in retrospect' — позиция, которую можно занять только after the fact. C2 регистр.

comprehension_questions.hint: Mum had told Sophie in November that she would, after Christmas, tell her if she had used the third candidate. The geranium has been a recurring object across C2 since L13. What does the choice of the geranium-as-subject tell Sophie about how Mum is, now, conducting the correspondence with Henry?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum says of the December letter: 'The third candidate, in the November letter, was the geranium. I used it.' What is the structural significance of Mum's choice of the geranium as the subject of her response to Henry, and why does she tell Sophie about the choice now, on the way to Bakewell, rather than at any other moment in the four months since the letter went?

comprehension_questions.hint: A larger closing statement would have summarised. The two-line closing simply continues. What does continuation rather than summary preserve, and what does it claim about how lives that have been lived through significant change should, in their close moments, be presented?
comprehension_questions.question: The course closes with two lines: 'I am, this morning, driving.' / 'I had assumed.' Why does the course refuse to deliver a larger closing statement — about what the course has been, about what Sophie has become, about what the relationship between Mum and Sophie now is — and what does the deliberate restraint teach about C2-level self-presentation?

comprehension_questions.hint: Each item is small. The accumulation, however, is substantive. What does the inventory-sentence make available that a more abstract summary statement would not, and how does it stage the course's accumulated weight in a single morning's contents?
comprehension_questions.question: Mum's inventory-sentence — 'a thermos and a photograph and an OS map and a recovering hip and a small recently-used signal-word and a herb garden' — names six items, each of which has accumulated courseworld history. Why does the sentence work as a closing statement, and what does it claim about the relationship between accumulated small objects and a life made visible in them?
